name: STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES

topics.title: Simple Stresses and Strains: Concept and Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple Stresses and Strains: Concept and Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Simple Stress"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Stress (σ or τ)** is defined as the **internal resistance offered by a material per unit area** when an external load is applied. It is a measure of the intensity of the internal forces acting on a hypothetical plane passing through a point within the body.

Mathematically, it is the ratio of the applied force (or internal restoring force) to the cross-sectional area over which it acts.

## 1.2 What is "Simple Strain"?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> **Strain (ε or γ)** is the **ratio of change in dimension (deformation) to the original dimension** of the body. It is a dimensionless quantity that represents the *deformation per unit length* caused by applied stress.

Strain is purely a geometric property — it describes *how much* the body has deformed, not *why*.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🎯

Imagine you are **stretching a rubber band** with your two hands:

| Observation in Rubber Band | Engineering Equivalent |
|---------------------------|------------------------|
| You pull both ends apart with force $P$ | External **Load** (Force) |
| The rubber band fights back, wanting to snap | Internal **Stress** (Resistance) |
| The rubber band becomes thinner in the middle (necking) and longer overall | **Strain** (Deformation) |
| The cross-section that resists the pull is the face you are looking at | **Cross-sectional Area** $A$ |

**Key Intuition:** Stress is what the *material feels inside*, while strain is what you *see happening outside*. They are directly proportional within the elastic limit (Hooke's Law).

## 1.4 Types of Simple Stresses

Simple stresses are classified based on the **direction of the applied force** relative to the cross-sectional area:

1. **Tensile Stress (Normal Tensile Stress):** A pulling force directed *away* from the surface, tending to elongate the body. Example: A cable holding an elevator.

2. **Compressive Stress (Normal Compressive Stress):** A pushing force directed *into* the surface, tending to shorten or crush the body. Example: A pillar supporting a building, concrete column under load.

3. **Shear Stress:** A force acting *parallel* to the surface, tending to slide one plane of material over another. Example: Rivets in a joint, scissors cutting paper, punching a hole in a metal sheet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **Tensile stress** and **Tensile strain** → taken as **POSITIVE (+)**.
> - **Compressive stress** and **Compressive strain** → taken as **NEGATIVE (−)**.
> - **Shear stress** → sign depends on the direction of the shearing couple; usually paired stresses are equal and opposite.

## 1.5 Types of Simple Strains

Corresponding to the three stress types:

1. **Linear (Normal) Strain (ε):** Change in length per unit original length along the direction of load.
   $$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

2. **Lateral Strain:** Strain produced in a direction **perpendicular** to the applied load. Always exists when longitudinal strain is produced (governed by Poisson's ratio).

3. **Shear Strain (γ):** Angular distortion produced when shear stress is applied. Measured in **radians**.
   $$\gamma = \frac{\delta x}{L} = \tan\phi \approx \phi \text{ (for small angles)}$$

4. **Volumetric Strain (εv):** Ratio of change in volume to original volume.
   $$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Internal Force & Stress

When a body is subjected to external forces, the material particles try to resist the deformation. This resistance is manifested as **internal forces** distributed over the cross-section. The intensity of this internal force per unit area is what we call **stress**.

To mathematically formalize:

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \left( \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta A} \right) = \frac{dP}{dA}$$

Where:
- $P$ → External applied force (N)
- $A$ → Cross-sectional area (m² or mm²)
- $\sigma$ → Stress (N/m² = Pa)

## 2.2 Stress Classification Logic Tree

```
                    STRESS (σ)
                        |
        ________________|________________
        |               |                |
   NORMAL STRESS    SHEAR STRESS    BEARING STRESS
   (σ = P/A)        (τ = V/A)       (σb = P/Aprojected)
        |
   ______|______
   |           |
TENSILE (+)  COMPRESSIVE (−)
```

## 2.3 Hooke's Law — The Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hooke's Law (Within Elastic Limit):**
> Stress is **directly proportional** to strain.
> $$\sigma \propto \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

Where $E$ is the **Modulus of Elasticity** (also called **Young's Modulus**), measured in **N/m² (Pa)** or **N/mm² (MPa)**.

## 2.4 The Four Elastic Constants

A material's elastic behavior is completely described by **four constants**:

| Constant | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|------------------|
| **Young's Modulus** | $E$ | $\sigma/\varepsilon$ | Resistance to *linear* deformation |
| **Shear Modulus** (Modulus of Rigidity) | $G$ or $N$ | $\tau/\gamma$ | Resistance to *angular* deformation |
| **Bulk Modulus** | $K$ | $p/\varepsilon_v$ | Resistance to *volume* change under hydrostatic pressure |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu$ or $\frac{1}{m}$ | $\frac{\text{Lateral Strain}}{\text{Longitudinal Strain}}$ | Ratio of lateral to axial strain |

## 2.5 Relationships Between Elastic Constants (High-Yield for KTU!)

> [!NOTE]
> These three formulas appear in **almost every KTU exam** — memorize them.

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

$$G = \frac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

### Key Inferences:
- If $\nu = 0.5$ (incompressible material like rubber), then $K \to \infty$ (no volume change possible).
- If $\nu = 0$, then $E = 2G$ (no lateral strain, e.g., cork).
- For most metals, $\nu$ lies between **0.25 and 0.33**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = P/A$ | N/m² (Pa) | +ve for tension, −ve for compression |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = V/A$ | N/m² (Pa) | Acts parallel to surface |
| 3 | Linear Strain | $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$ | Dimensionless | Also called longitudinal strain |
| 4 | Lateral Strain | $\varepsilon_{lat} = \nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long}$ | Dimensionless | Opposite sign to longitudinal |
| 5 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \phi = \delta x / L$ | Radians | Small angle approximation valid |
| 6 | Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Dimensionless | Sum of normal strains in 3 directions |
| 7 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | — | Valid only in elastic range |
| 8 | Shear Hooke's Law | $\tau = G\gamma$ | — | Valid only in elastic range |
| 9 | Volumetric Hooke's Law | $p = K\varepsilon_v$ | — | $p$ = hydrostatic pressure |
| 10 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat} / \varepsilon_{long}$ | Dimensionless | Negative sign indicates opposite direction |
| 11 | $E$-$G$ relation | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | — | High-yield KTU formula |
| 12 | $E$-$K$ relation | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | — | High-yield KTU formula |
| 13 | Volumetric strain (uniaxial) | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon(1 - 2\nu)$ | Dimensionless | For one-dimensional loading |
| 14 | Elongation of bar | $\delta L = PL / (AE)$ | m or mm | Standard formula for deformation |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tensile/Compressive stress analysis** → Used in designing **columns, beams, trusses, cables, and concrete pillars**.
- **Shear stress analysis** → Critical in designing **riveted joints, bolts, welds, and punching operations**.
- **Poisson's ratio** → Essential in **multi-axial stress analysis**, **pressure vessels**, and **composite material design**.
- **Bulk modulus** → Used in design of **hydraulic systems, submarine hulls, and fluid containers**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between E, G, and ν

> **Objective:** To prove that $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$.

**Step 1: Consider a square element ABCD of side $L$.**
Apply shear stress $\tau$ on its top and bottom faces. The element distorts into a rhombus shape (parallelogram). The top face displaces horizontally by $\delta x$ relative to the bottom.

**Step 2: Define the shear strain.**
By definition of shear strain (angular deformation in radians):

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta x}{L} = \tan\phi \approx \phi$$

where $\phi$ is the small angle of distortion at corner B (and D).

**Step 3: Apply shear Hooke's law.**

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot \frac{\delta x}{L} \quad \text{...(a)}$$

**Step 4: Now view the SAME deformation as a combination of tensile and compressive strains along the diagonal.**

Look at the diagonal AC of the original square. The square is stretched along diagonal AC (tension) and compressed along diagonal BD.

Length of original diagonal = $L\sqrt{2}$.

After deformation, diagonal AC becomes $L\sqrt{2} + \delta x / \sqrt{2} \cdot 2$... let us compute more carefully.

> Actually, for small shear strain $\gamma$, the new length of the stretched diagonal AC is approximately $L\sqrt{2}(1 + \gamma/2)$ and the compressed diagonal BD is $L\sqrt{2}(1 - \gamma/2)$.

**Step 5: Compute linear strain along the tensile diagonal AC.**

$$\varepsilon_{AC} = \frac{L\sqrt{2}(1 + \gamma/2) - L\sqrt{2}}{L\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\gamma}{2}$$

**Step 6: Compute linear strain along the compressive diagonal BD.**

$$\varepsilon_{BD} = \frac{L\sqrt{2}(1 - \gamma/2) - L\sqrt{2}}{L\sqrt{2}} = -\frac{\gamma}{2}$$

**Step 7: Express the same stress along diagonal AC using Hooke's law (with Poisson effect).**

The tensile stress on diagonal AC produces strain $\varepsilon_{AC}$ along AC. Simultaneously, the perpendicular direction (along BD) experiences a lateral strain equal to $-\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{AC}$.

$$\varepsilon_{AC} = \frac{\sigma_{AC}}{E} - \nu \cdot \frac{-\sigma_{BD}}{E} \quad \text{(using superposition)}$$

But on the diagonal plane, the normal stress equals the shear stress in magnitude:

$$\sigma_{AC} = \tau, \quad \sigma_{BD} = -\tau$$

**Step 8: Substitute and solve.**

$$\frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 9: Replace $\tau = G\gamma$ from Step 3.**

$$\frac{\gamma}{2} = \frac{G\gamma}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 10: Cancel $\gamma$ from both sides and rearrange.**

$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{G(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Strain Under Uni-axial Loading

**Step 1: Consider a rectangular parallelepiped of dimensions $L_x$, $L_y$, $L_z$.**

Apply uni-axial tensile stress $\sigma_x$ along the x-axis. The resulting strains are:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_y = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -\frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_z = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_x = -\frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 2: Volumetric strain is the sum of normal strains in three orthogonal directions.**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Engineering Implication:** If $\nu = 0.5$, the volumetric strain is **zero** — the material is **incompressible** (e.g., rubber, ideal fluids).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between E, K, and ν

**Step 1: Consider a cube subjected to hydrostatic pressure $p$ on all six faces.**

The hydrostatic stress in each direction is $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.

**Step 2: The volumetric strain under tri-axial stress (using Hooke's generalized form) is:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

**Step 3: Substitute the hydrostatic stress values.**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{E}(-p - p - p) = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4: By definition of Bulk Modulus.**

$$K = -\frac{p}{\varepsilon_v} = -\frac{p}{\frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Step 5: Rearrange.**

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A steel rod of length **2 m** and cross-section **30 mm × 30 mm** is subjected to an axial tensile load of **60 kN**. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and $\nu = 0.3$. Find:
> (a) Stress in the rod
> (b) Longitudinal strain
> (c) Lateral strain
> (d) Elongation of the rod
> (e) Volumetric strain

**Solution:**

**(a) Stress Calculation:**

$$A = 30 \times 30 = 900 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$P = 60 \text{ kN} = 60 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{900} = 66.67 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (MPa)}$$

**(b) Longitudinal Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{66.67}{2 \times 10^5} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(c) Lateral Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = 0.3 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(d) Elongation:**

$$\delta L = \varepsilon_{long} \times L = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \times 2000 = 0.667 \text{ mm}$$

**(e) Volumetric Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long}(1 - 2\nu) = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.6) = 1.333 \times 10^{-4}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Stresses and Strains

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MECHANICAL RESPONSE OF MATERIALS"] --> B["STRESS sigma / tau"]
    A --> C["STRAIN epsilon / gamma"]
    B --> D["NORMAL STRESS"]
    B --> E["SHEAR STRESS tau"]
    D --> F["TENSILE STRESS +ve"]
    D --> G["COMPRESSIVE STRESS -ve"]
    E --> H["DIRECT SHEAR"]
    E --> I["TORSIONAL SHEAR"]
    C --> J["LINEAR STRAIN"]
    C --> K["SHEAR STRAIN gamma"]
    C --> L["VOLUMETRIC STRAIN epsilon v"]
    J --> M["LONGITUDINAL epsilon long"]
    J --> N["LATERAL epsilon lat nu times epsilon long"]
    L --> O["epsilon x plus epsilon y plus epsilon z"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The Four Elastic Constants and Their Relations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E["YOUNGS MODULUS E"] --> R1["E equals 2G times 1 plus nu"]
    G["SHEAR MODULUS G"] --> R1
    NU["POISSONS RATIO nu"] --> R1
    E --> R2["E equals 3K times 1 minus 2nu"]
    K["BULK MODULUS K"] --> R2
    NU --> R2
    G --> R3["G equals 3K times 1 minus 2nu divided by 2 times 1 plus nu"]
    K --> R3
    NU --> R3
    E --> APP["APPLICATIONS: AXIAL LOADING, COLUMNS, CABLES"]
    G --> APP2["APPLICATIONS: TORSION, RIVETS, BOLTS"]
    K --> APP3["APPLICATIONS: HYDRAULIC PRESSURE VESSELS"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Stress-Strain Conceptual Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXT["EXTERNAL LOAD P Newton"] --> INT["INTERNAL RESTORING FORCE"]
    INT --> CSAREA["ACTING OVER CROSS SECTION A mm squared"]
    CSAREA --> STRESS["STRESS sigma equals P by A N per mm squared"]
    STRESS --> DEFORM["CAUSES DEFORMATION delta L mm"]
    DEFORM --> STRAIN["STRAIN epsilon equals delta L by L dimensionless"]
    STRESS --> ELIM["WITHIN ELASTIC LIMIT"]
    ELIM --> HOOKE["HOOKES LAW sigma equals E times epsilon"]
    HOOKE --> ELASTIC["E is the elastic constant"]
```

## 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (for examination drawing)

```
   TENSILE LOAD                 COMPRESSIVE LOAD              SHEAR LOAD
   ──→  ┌──────┐ ←──            ──→  ┌──────┐ ←──            ↓ ┌──────┐ ↓
        │ BODY │                    │ BODY │                       │ BODY │→
   ──→  └──────┘ ←──            ──→  └──────┘ ←──            ↑ └──────┘ ↑
        ←──elongates──→             ──shortens──→
   σ = P/A (+)                 σ = P/A (−)                  τ = V/A
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define the following terms with units: **(a)** Stress **(b)** Strain **(c)** Modulus of Elasticity.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(a) Stress:** The internal resistance offered by a material per unit cross-sectional area when an external load is applied. $\sigma = P/A$. Unit: **N/m² (Pa)** or **N/mm² (MPa)**.

**(b) Strain:** The ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension of the body. $\varepsilon = \delta L / L$. Unit: **Dimensionless** (no unit).

**(c) Modulus of Elasticity (E):** The ratio of longitudinal stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit, as per Hooke's law. $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$. Unit: **N/m² (Pa)** or **N/mm² (MPa)**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** State and explain **Hooke's Law**. What are its limitations?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain. $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$.

**Explanation:** When a material is loaded within its elastic limit, the internal stress produced is linearly proportional to the resulting strain, with $E$ (Young's Modulus) as the constant of proportionality.

**Limitations (any two):**
1. Valid only up to the **proportional limit** (elastic limit).
2. Applicable only for **static loading** (not for impact/dynamic loads).
3. Not valid for materials with **non-linear** stress-strain behavior (e.g., rubber, concrete).
4. Temperature must remain constant (isothermal conditions).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q3]**
> **Q3 (a)** Derive the relationship between **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Modulus of Rigidity (G)**, and **Poisson's Ratio (ν)**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q3 (b)** A mild steel bar **300 mm long**, **40 mm wide**, and **20 mm thick** is subjected to an axial compressive load of **120 kN**. Find: **(i)** Stress, **(ii)** Longitudinal strain, **(iii)** Lateral strain, **(iv)** Change in volume. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Q3(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Consider a square element of side $L$. Apply pure shear stress $\tau$ on all four faces. The element distorts into a rhombus.

**Step 2:** The shear strain is $\gamma = \delta x / L$ and by Hooke's law: $\tau = G\gamma$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** Examining the diagonals, the diagonal AC elongates (tension) and diagonal BD shortens (compression). The linear strain along AC is $\gamma/2$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 4:** Express the strain along AC using uniaxial Hooke's law with lateral contraction: $\varepsilon_{AC} = (\sigma_{AC} - \nu\sigma_{BD})/E$. Since on the diagonal, normal stress equals shear stress in magnitude, we get: $\gamma/2 = (\tau/E)(1 + \nu)$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 5:** Substitute $\tau = G\gamma$ and simplify:

$$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{G(1+\nu)}{E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)} \quad \blacksquare \text{ (2 Marks)}$$

### Model Solution for Q3(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $L = 300$ mm, $b = 40$ mm, $t = 20$ mm, $P = 120$ kN $= 120 \times 10^3$ N, $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$

**(i) Stress:** $A = 40 \times 20 = 800$ mm²

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{120 \times 10^3}{800} = 150 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)} \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{150}{2 \times 10^5} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Lateral Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \nu \cdot \varepsilon_{long} = 0.3 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} = 2.25 \times 10^{-4} \quad \text{(1.5 Marks)}$$

**(iv) Change in Volume:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_{long}(1 - 2\nu) = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.6) = 3.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V = 300 \times 40 \times 20 = 240000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\delta V = \varepsilon_v \times V = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 240000 = 72 \text{ mm}^3 \text{ (volume decreases)} \quad \text{(2.5 Marks)}$$

---

> ### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Q4]**
> **Q4 (a)** Explain the four elastic constants of a material. Derive the relationship $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q4 (b)** A metallic bar of **25 mm diameter** and **1.5 m length** is subjected to a tensile load of **80 kN**. The bar elongates by **0.6 mm** and its diameter reduces by **0.0036 mm**. Calculate: **(i)** Young's Modulus, **(ii)** Poisson's Ratio, **(iii)** Modulus of Rigidity. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Q4(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** List the four elastic constants: Young's Modulus $E$, Shear Modulus $G$, Bulk Modulus $K$, Poisson's Ratio $\nu$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 2:** Consider a cube under hydrostatic pressure $p$. Equal stresses $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$ act on all faces. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** The generalized Hooke's law gives volumetric strain: $\varepsilon_v = (1-2\nu)(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)/E = -3p(1-2\nu)/E$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 4:** By definition $K = -p/\varepsilon_v$. Substituting: $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5:** Rearranging gives the required result:

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1-2\nu)} \quad \blacksquare \text{ (1 Mark)}$$

### Model Solution for Q4(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $D = 25$ mm, $L = 1500$ mm, $P = 80$ kN $= 80 \times 10^3$ N, $\delta L = 0.6$ mm, $\delta D = 0.0036$ mm

**(i) Young's Modulus:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{490.87} = 163.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\varepsilon_{long} = \frac{0.6}{1500} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{163.0}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.075 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(3 Marks)}$$

**(ii) Poisson's Ratio:**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \frac{0.0036}{25} = 1.44 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\nu = \frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{4.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.36 \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

**(iii) Modulus of Rigidity:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{4.075 \times 10^5}{2(1+0.36)} = \frac{4.075 \times 10^5}{2.72} = 1.498 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(2 Marks)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention** — Compressive stress/strain must be marked negative or explicitly stated. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Mixing up E, G, and K formulas** — E = 2G(1+ν) and E = 3K(1−2ν) look similar; confuse the constant that connects E.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** — Keep N/mm² or MPa consistent throughout. Do not mix N/m² with N/mm² mid-solution.
> 4. **Not writing the Hooke's law assumption** — Most derivations need the explicit statement that the material is in the elastic range.
> 5. **In lateral strain sign** — Lateral strain is *opposite* in direction to longitudinal strain (this is captured by the minus sign in the definition of ν).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER ✍️

> 📌 **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Stress** = Internal force per unit area. Unit: N/mm² (MPa) or N/m² (Pa). **Tensile = +**, **Compressive = −**.

- ✅ **Strain** = Change in dimension per original dimension. **Dimensionless.** Three types: linear, shear, volumetric.

- ✅ **Hooke's Law** → $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (valid only within the **proportional/elastic limit**).

- ✅ **Shear Hooke's Law** → $\tau = G\gamma$ (γ in **radians**).

- ✅ **Volumetric Hooke's Law** → $p = K\varepsilon_v$ (p = hydrostatic pressure).

- ✅ **Poisson's Ratio** $\nu$ = (lateral strain) / (longitudinal strain). Range: 0 to 0.5. For **steel ≈ 0.3**, for **concrete ≈ 0.1–0.2**, for **rubber ≈ 0.49**.

- ✅ **Three Golden Relations (MUST MEMORIZE):**
  - $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$
  - $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$
  - $G = \dfrac{3K(1 - 2\nu)}{2(1 + \nu)}$

- ✅ **Volumetric strain under uni-axial stress** = $\varepsilon(1 - 2\nu)$.

- ✅ **Elongation of a uniform bar** = $\delta L = PL / (AE)$.

- ✅ **If $\nu = 0.5$** → material is **incompressible** (e.g., rubber); $K \to \infty$.
- ✅ **If $\nu = 0$** → no lateral strain (e.g., cork); $E = 2G$.

- ✅ **Sign of volumetric strain:** Positive for tensile hydrostatic stress, negative for compressive.

- ✅ **Examination Strategy:** Always state Hooke's law assumption, show the formula first, plug in numbers with units, and box the final answer.

- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** Do not confuse **Poisson's ratio (ν)** with the **number 1/m** — they are the same symbol used differently by different textbooks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: St. Venant's Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** St. Venant's Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**St. Venant's Principle** (also written as **Saint-Venant's Principle**) is a fundamental postulate in the **Theory of Elasticity** and **Strength of Materials**, first formally articulated by the French elasticity theorist **Jean-Claude Barré de Saint-Venant** in the year **1855** in his memoir on the torsion of prisms.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition (Rigid Form)**
> *St. Venant's Principle states that **the stress distribution produced by statically equivalent load systems (i.e., load systems having the same resultant force and moment) acting on a body is identical only at regions sufficiently far away from the points of application of the loads**. Near the loaded region, the stress distribution is generally non-uniform and depends upon the precise mode of application of the load.*

In simpler terms — if you apply a force to a structure in **two different ways** that are *statically equivalent* (same magnitude, direction, and line of action of the net force/moment), the **internal stresses near the point of application** will be very different, but the **stresses at locations sufficiently remote** from the loaded point will be **essentially the same**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Quantitative Yardstick**
> The "sufficiently far" distance is conventionally taken as **greater than or equal to the largest cross-sectional dimension (depth/width) of the member** at the loaded section. This is a heuristic (rule-of-thumb) and not a strict mathematical bound.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a long wooden plank (a cantilever beam) horizontally at its free end.

- **Scenario A:** You push the plank by pressing your **entire palm** flat against the end face. The force is distributed uniformly across the end surface.
- **Scenario B:** You push the same plank by pressing with **just one fingertip** at the corner of the end face.

Both pushes deliver the **same total force** $P$ (statically equivalent) and produce the **same bending moment** $M = P \cdot L$ at the fixed support.

🔍 **What do you observe?**

- **Near the free end (point of application):** The internal stress distribution in the plank is *wildly different* in the two cases. In Scenario B, the contact stresses under your fingertip are enormously high and highly localized. In Scenario A, the stresses are spread out evenly.
- **Near the fixed support (far away from load application):** The bending stress distribution — given by the familiar flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ — is **virtually identical** in both cases.

> [!TIP] **The Takeaway in One Line**
> *The exact way a load is applied matters locally; it does NOT matter globally.* This is the essence of St. Venant's Principle.

---

## 1.3 Why Does This Principle Matter for KTU?

In KTU's **Strength of Materials** curriculum, we routinely use simplified formulas (axial stress $\sigma = P/A$, bending stress $\sigma = My/I$, shear stress $\tau = VQ/Ib$, torsional shear $\tau = Tr/J$) that **presume a uniform or linear stress distribution** across a section. These formulas are *elementary* solutions of elasticity and are valid **only in regions where St. Venant's Principle applies** — i.e., away from points of concentrated load, abrupt geometric change, or support reaction.

> [!WARNING] **Common Student Misconception**
> KTU students often apply $\sigma = P/A$ at the **exact cross-section** where a concentrated load is applied. This is **NOT** correct. The simple formula is only valid at a distance $\geq$ the largest local cross-sectional dimension away from the load.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Convention | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold distance for principle validity | $d \geq$ largest cross-sectional dimension (e.g., depth $h$ for a beam) | $d$ |
| Force application tolerance | Line of action must coincide for static equivalence | $P$ |
| Saint-Venant decay rate (general) | Exponential decay of stress perturbation | $\sim e^{-x/\ell}$ |
| Modulus of Elasticity (for steel) | $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$ (for context, not in principle) | $E$ |
| Poisson's Ratio (for steel) | $\nu = 0.30$ (for context, not in principle) | $\nu$ |

> **Note for KTU 2024:** Numerical values of $E$ and $\nu$ are **not** part of the principle itself. They are listed only as engineering reference material; the principle is **material-independent** and purely **geometric/topological** in nature.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind St. Venant's Principle — The Mathematical Logic

To understand the principle rigorously, we must distinguish between:

1. **Equilibrium / Statics** — only demands that the *resultant* force and moment match.
2. **Compatibility / Constitutive Law** — requires the displacement field to be continuous and the stress-strain relationship to be satisfied everywhere.

When two statically equivalent load systems are applied, they satisfy (1) trivially, but they differ in the *local* stress field. However, the **equations of elasticity** (a system of **3 elliptic partial differential equations** — the Navier–Cauchy equations of equilibrium) have the property of **boundary-layer smoothing**: localized stress irregularities decay rapidly as one moves into the interior of the body.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Step 1 — Identify statically equivalent systems.**
   Two load systems $L_1$ and $L_2$ are statically equivalent if:
   $$\int_{S} \mathbf{t}^{(1)} \, dS = \int_{S} \mathbf{t}^{(2)} \, dS$$
   $$\int_{S} \mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{t}^{(1)} \, dS = \int_{S} \mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{t}^{(2)} \, dS$$
   where $\mathbf{t}^{(i)}$ is the traction vector on the loaded surface and $\mathbf{r}$ is the position vector.

2. **Step 2 — Subtract the two solutions.**
   Define the *difference* stress field: $\Delta\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ij}^{(1)} - \sigma_{ij}^{(2)}$.
   This difference field satisfies the homogeneous equilibrium equations (no body force) in the interior:
   $$\sigma_{ij,j}^{(1)} - \sigma_{ij,j}^{(2)} = 0$$

3. **Step 3 — Apply zero traction on the lateral and far boundaries.**
   On the unloaded boundaries, both solutions have zero traction, so the difference field also has zero traction there.

4. **Step 4 — Apply the principle of minimum potential energy (or uniqueness theorem).**
   By the **Kirchhoff uniqueness theorem** of linear elasticity, the difference field that vanishes at infinity and satisfies homogeneous equilibrium must be **bounded and small** away from the loaded region.

5. **Step 5 — Saint-Venant conjectured exponential decay.**
   He proposed that the difference stresses decay approximately as:
   $$\vert \Delta\sigma_{ij} \vert \sim e^{-x/\ell}$$
   where $\ell$ is a characteristic length scale (typically the smaller of the cross-sectional dimensions of the body).

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Note**
> The 1855 conjecture of exponential decay was not rigorously *proved* in full generality until the 20th century (Toupin, 1965; Knowles, 1966) for specific geometries. The KTU board expects the **statement** of the principle and its **practical consequences**, not the rigorous PDE proof.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master These — They Appear Repeatedly in KTU ESE Questions**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Validity Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uniaxial normal stress (axial loading) | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $P$ = axial force (N), $A$ = cross-section area (m²), $\sigma$ = stress (Pa) | Far from load application and end connections |
| 2 | Flexural (bending) stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{M y}{I}$ | $M$ = bending moment (N·m), $y$ = distance from NA (m), $I$ = 2nd moment of area (m⁴) | Far from concentrated loads/holes |
| 3 | Transverse shear stress | $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ | $V$ = shear force (N), $Q$ = 1st moment of area (m³), $b$ = width (m) | Far from concentrated loads |
| 4 | Torsional shear stress (circular shaft) | $\tau = \dfrac{T r}{J}$ | $T$ = torque (N·m), $r$ = radial distance (m), $J$ = polar moment (m⁴) | Far from grips, keyways, fillets |
| 5 | Stress concentration factor | $K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{nominal}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | At the point of discontinuity |
| 6 | Saint-Venant decay (qualitative) | $\Delta\sigma \propto e^{-x / \ell}$ | $x$ = distance from load, $\ell$ = characteristic length | $x \gg \ell$ |
| 7 | Threshold distance rule of thumb | $x \geq h$ (or $b$, whichever is larger) | $h$ = depth of section, $b$ = width | Engineering approximation |

> [!WARNING] **No Vertical Pipes in Tables**
> In all formula cells above, absolute value or magnitude is denoted by the word "magnitude" or written as $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ — never using the raw pipe character `|` which would break markdown parsing.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

St. Venant's Principle is the *unsung hero* of nearly every structural design formula in your KTU syllabus:

- 🏗️ **Reinforced Concrete Beams:** We use $\sigma = My/I$ even though the load is applied through a bearing plate of finite area, not as a true point load. The principle justifies the simplified analysis.
- 🌉 **Steel Bridges:** Gusset-plate connections transfer large forces over a small region; the principle tells us the far-field stresses in the connected member are unaffected by the connection's bolt pattern.
- ⚙️ **Machine Shafts:** Keyways, splines, and shoulders create local stress concentrations ($K_t$ can be 2 to 3), but the principle assures us these are *local* phenomena.
- 🔩 **Pressure Vessels:** Bolted flanges create highly non-uniform contact stresses that do not propagate into the cylindrical shell body.
- 🛩️ **Aerospace Structures:** Composite laminates with bolted joints are designed knowing that load introduction effects decay within a few ply-thicknesses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equivalence of Two Statically Equivalent Load Systems Far From the Load

We will now formally derive the consequence of St. Venant's Principle using the **complementary energy / variational approach**, which is the most common technique referenced in advanced strength-of-materials texts.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a prismatic body occupying region $V$ with boundary $\partial V$ consisting of:
- $S_t$: the portion where tractions $\mathbf{t}$ are prescribed (the loaded end)
- $S_u$: the portion where displacements $\mathbf{u}$ are prescribed (the support)

Let there be **two distinct but statically equivalent** traction fields, $\mathbf{t}^{(1)}$ and $\mathbf{t}^{(2)}$, prescribed on $S_t$. By static equivalence:
$$\int_{S_t} \mathbf{t}^{(1)} \, dS = \int_{S_t} \mathbf{t}^{(2)} \, dS = \mathbf{F}$$
$$\int_{S_t} \mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{t}^{(1)} \, dS = \int_{S_t} \mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{t}^{(2)} \, dS = \mathbf{M}$$

Both traction fields give rise to valid stress fields $\sigma_{ij}^{(1)}$ and $\sigma_{ij}^{(2)}$ inside $V$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Define the *difference* stress field and difference strain field.

$$\Delta\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_{ij}^{(1)} - \sigma_{ij}^{(2)}$$

$$\Delta\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ij}^{(1)} - \varepsilon_{ij}^{(2)}$$

**Step 2:** In the interior, both $\sigma^{(1)}$ and $\sigma^{(2)}$ satisfy equilibrium (no body force assumed for simplicity):

$$\sigma_{ij,j}^{(1)} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_{ij,j}^{(2)} = 0$$

Subtracting:

$$\Delta\sigma_{ij,j} = 0$$

**Step 3:** On the unloaded portion of the boundary $S_u$ (or any boundary where both tractions are zero), the difference traction is also zero:

$$\Delta\sigma_{ij} \, n_j = 0 \quad \text{on } S_u$$

**Step 4:** On the loaded portion $S_t$, the difference traction is generally **not zero** (this is the key):

$$\Delta\sigma_{ij} \, n_j = t_i^{(1)} - t_i^{(2)} \quad \text{on } S_t$$

**Step 5:** Both stress fields are related to their respective strain fields through Hooke's law (linear elastic, isotropic):

$$\sigma_{ij}^{(k)} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{mm}^{(k)} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}^{(k)} \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2$$

where $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are the Lamé constants. Therefore the difference field also obeys:

$$\Delta\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \Delta\varepsilon_{mm} + 2\mu \, \Delta\varepsilon_{ij}$$

**Step 6:** Consider the **complementary strain energy** of the difference field:

$$U_c = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \Delta\sigma_{ij} \, \Delta\varepsilon_{ij} \, dV$$

Substituting Hooke's law in inverse form:

$$U_c = \frac{1}{2} \int_V \frac{1}{2\mu} \left( \Delta\sigma_{ij}\Delta\sigma_{ij} - \frac{\lambda}{3\lambda + 2\mu} \Delta\sigma_{kk}^2 \right) dV$$

**Step 7:** The **Principle of Minimum Complementary Energy** (a variational principle of linear elasticity) states that among all self-equilibrated stress fields satisfying the boundary tractions, the true one minimizes $U_c$. Since $\Delta\sigma_{ij}$ is self-equilibrated (Step 2) and satisfies zero traction on $S_u$ (Step 3), and zero net traction on $S_t$ (by static equivalence), we may extend $\Delta\sigma_{ij}$ to be **identically zero** as a feasible comparison field.

**Step 8:** Therefore, $U_c \geq 0$, and the comparison field gives $U_c = 0$. By the uniqueness of the minimizer (linear elasticity uniqueness theorem):

$$\Delta\sigma_{ij} \equiv 0 \quad \text{throughout the body}$$

**Step 9 — The Caveat (Saint-Venant's modification):**

The argument in Step 8 is **exact** only if the entire surface of the body is unloaded except $S_t$. In most real engineering problems, the body is **partially loaded on multiple surfaces** (e.g., a beam with a load at one end and a support at the other). In such cases, the difference field does **not** vanish identically, but Saint-Venant showed it must **decay rapidly** with distance from the load.

The quantitative decay estimate is:

$$\lvert \Delta\sigma_{ij}(x) \rvert \leq C \cdot \lVert \mathbf{t}^{(1)} - \mathbf{t}^{(2)} \rVert_{\infty} \cdot e^{-\alpha x / \ell}$$

where:
- $C$ is a dimensionless constant (geometry-dependent, typically $O(1)$ to $O(10)$)
- $\alpha$ is a geometry-dependent decay exponent (typically $O(1)$)
- $\ell$ is a characteristic length (e.g., the smaller cross-sectional dimension)
- $x$ is the distance from the loaded region

**Step 10 — Engineering Practical Rule:**

For engineering purposes, the exponential decay is so rapid that we adopt the conservative rule of thumb:

$$x \geq h \quad \text{or} \quad x \geq b \quad (\text{whichever is greater})$$

That is, at a distance equal to the **largest cross-sectional dimension** away from the loaded section, the local perturbation has decayed to less than ~5% of its original magnitude, and elementary formulas (like $\sigma = P/A$) are valid to within engineering accuracy (typically 5–10%).

> [!TIP] **Why This Derivation Matters for KTU**
> Even though the full PDE proof is beyond the scope, KTU's Course Outcome **CO1** of Strength of Materials explicitly tests your ability to **state the principle, identify the region of validity, and apply elementary formulas in the correct region**. Memorize the "$\geq$ largest dimension" rule.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Demonstrating the Principle

**Problem (Typical KTU 2-mark style):**

> A steel bar of rectangular cross-section $40 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$ carries an axial pull of $P = 100 \text{ kN}$. The load is applied through a 20 mm-diameter hole at one end (a pin connection). At what minimum distance from the loaded end can the elementary formula $\sigma = P/A$ be used with confidence?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area:
$$A = 40 \times 20 = 800 \text{ mm}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Nominal stress (from the elementary formula):
$$\sigma_{\text{nom}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{100 \times 10^3}{800} = 125 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 125 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Largest cross-sectional dimension:
$$h_{\max} = 40 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Apply St. Venant's rule of thumb:
$$x_{\min} \geq h_{\max} = 40 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Conclusion:

The elementary formula $\sigma = P/A$ is valid only at distances $x \geq 40 \text{ mm}$ from the pin-connected end. Within this 40 mm zone, the actual stress distribution is non-uniform and depends on the pin-hole geometry (stress concentration factor $K_t$ applies here, with $K_t \approx 2.0$ to $2.5$ for a typical pin joint).

**Result:**
$$\sigma_{\text{valid}} = 125 \text{ MPa}, \quad \text{valid for } x \geq 40 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Exam Tip**
> Always state the **region of validity** explicitly in your final answer. Examiners award full marks only when the Saint-Venant justification is given, not just the formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: St. Venant's Principle in Design Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates **how St. Venant's Principle guides the engineering design workflow** from loading to stress computation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identified Load System L1] --> B[Define Geometry and Supports]
    C[Identified Load System L2] --> B
    B --> D{Statically Equivalent?}
    D -- "Yes" --> E[Compute Stress Field sigma1 from L1]
    D -- "Yes" --> F[Compute Stress Field sigma2 from L2]
    D -- "No" --> G[Treat as Different Problems]
    E --> H[Local Zone Region A within x less than h]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Stress Field sigma1 and sigma2 are GENERALLY DIFFERENT]
    E --> J[Far Zone Region B with x greater than h]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Stress Field sigma1 and sigma2 are PRACTICALLY IDENTICAL]
    I --> L[Use Full Elasticity or Stress Concentration Factors]
    K --> M[Apply Elementary Formulas sigma equals P by A or sigma equals My by I]
    L --> N[Engineering Decision Section Sizing or Reinforcement]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Design Verified]
```

> **Figure Caption:** Block flow showing how St. Venant's Principle partitions the analysis domain into a *local zone* (where detailed elasticity is required) and a *far zone* (where simplified elementary formulas suffice).

---

## 4.2 Schematic: Pin-Loaded Bar Stress Distribution

The following block diagram maps the **stress distribution along the length of a pin-loaded bar**, showing the transition from non-uniform (local) to uniform (far) stress.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pin Load Applied at End x equals 0] --> B1[Local Zone x less than h max]
    B1 --> C1[Stress Distribution: Highly Non Uniform]
    C1 --> D1[Stress Peaks at Pin Hole Contact Points]
    D1 --> E1[Stress Concentration Factor Kt applies]
    E1 --> F1[Transition Zone x approximately equals h max]
    F1 --> G1[Far Zone x greater than h max]
    G1 --> H1[Stress Distribution: Essentially Uniform]
    H1 --> I1[Elementary Formula sigma equals P by A Valid Here]
    I1 --> J1[Engineering Analysis Region]
```

> **Figure Caption:** Schematic showing the spatial evolution of the stress field along a pin-loaded tensile bar.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Decision Tree for Choosing the Right Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Given: A Cross Section with Internal Forces] --> q1{Is the section within distance h of a concentrated load or geometric discontinuity?}
    q1 -- "YES" --> local[Use Stress Concentration Factor Kt times nominal stress]
    q1 -- "NO" --> far[Use Elementary Formulas]
    far --> q2{Type of internal force?}
    q2 -- "Axial P" --> fa[sigma equals P by A]
    q2 -- "Bending M" --> fb[sigma equals My by I]
    q2 -- "Shear V" --> fc[tau equals VQ by Ib]
    q2 -- "Torsion T" --> fd[tau equals Tr by J for circular sections only]
    local --> result[Compute Maximum Stress for Design]
    fa --> result
    fb --> result
    fc --> result
    fd --> result
```

> **Figure Caption:** A KTU-style flowchart for selecting the appropriate stress formula based on Saint-Venant's regional criterion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State St. Venant's Principle. Mention the criterion used to determine the region where elementary stress formulas are valid.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** *St. Venant's Principle states that if the actual distribution of loads on a body is replaced by a statically equivalent load distribution, the stress distribution produced will be the same as the actual distribution at all points that are sufficiently far from the points of application of the loads, but may differ significantly in the vicinity of the loaded region.*

**Criterion (1 mark):** *The elementary stress formulas are valid at a distance equal to or greater than the largest cross-sectional dimension of the member away from the point of application of the load.*

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A steel rod of diameter 30 mm is loaded at one end through a threaded connection. At what distance from the threaded end can the formula $\sigma = P/A$ be safely applied?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Given:** Diameter $d = 30 \text{ mm}$, so cross-section is circular with largest dimension = 30 mm.

**By St. Venant's Principle (2 marks):**

$$x_{\min} \geq d = 30 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** *The elementary formula $\sigma = P/A$ is valid at distances $x \geq 30 \text{ mm}$ from the threaded end. Within the 30 mm zone near the threads, the stress distribution is non-uniform due to thread root stress concentrations, and the simple formula is NOT applicable.*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question is worth **14 marks** split into two sub-parts: **Part (a) for 7 marks** and **Part (b) for 7 marks**. The student answers **one** of the two choice questions.

---

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: A flat steel bar 60 mm wide and 12 mm thick is subjected to an axial pull of 90 kN. The load is applied through a U-shaped clevis at one end. Determine:**
**(i) The average stress in the section using the elementary formula.**
**(ii) The distance from the loaded end beyond which the elementary formula is accurate (apply St. Venant's Principle).**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area (1 mark):**
$$A = 60 \times 12 = 720 \text{ mm}^2 = 720 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Average stress (2 marks):**
$$\sigma_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{720 \text{ mm}^2} = 125 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 125 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Identifying the largest dimension (1 mark):**
$$h_{\max} = \max(60, 12) = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Applying St. Venant's Principle (2 marks):**

By St. Venant's Principle, the elementary formula $\sigma = P/A$ is valid only at a distance:
$$x \geq h_{\max} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

In the clevis (loaded) region ($x < 60$ mm), the actual stress is non-uniform and includes a stress concentration. The average value of 125 MPa is the *far-field* value.

**Step 5 — Final answer (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{avg}} = 125 \text{ MPa}, \quad \text{valid for } x \geq 60 \text{ mm}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: Two different load application methods are used on the same bar as in Part (a). Method 1 grips the full 60 mm width; Method 2 grips only the central 20 mm width. Both transmit the same total axial force P. Comment on the stresses at (i) the loaded section, and (ii) a section 100 mm away from the loaded end. Justify using St. Venant's Principle.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statically equivalent check (1 mark):**
Both methods transmit the same total force $P$ and no net moment — hence they are **statically equivalent**.

**Step 2 — Analysis at the loaded section $x = 0$ (3 marks):**

- **Method 1 (full width grip):** The traction is distributed uniformly over the full 60 mm width. The local stress at $x = 0$ is approximately uniform at $\sigma \approx P/A = 125$ MPa.
- **Method 2 (central 20 mm grip):** The traction is concentrated over only the central 20 mm width. The local contact stress at $x = 0$ is much higher — approximately $\sigma_{\text{local}} \approx P / (12 \times 20) = P / 240 \approx 375$ MPa (3× the Method 1 value).
- **Conclusion:** The stresses are **clearly different** at the loaded section. Method 1 gives lower peak stress; Method 2 gives higher peak stress.

**Step 3 — Analysis at section $x = 100$ mm (2 marks):**

Since $x = 100$ mm $\gg h_{\max} = 60$ mm, we are well into the *far zone*.

**By St. Venant's Principle:** The stress distribution at $x = 100$ mm is **practically identical** for both methods:
$$\sigma(x = 100 \text{ mm}) \approx 125 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{for both methods}$$

**Step 4 — Final concluding statement (1 mark):**

St. Venant's Principle confirms that the **mode of load application affects only the local stress field** (within a distance equal to the largest cross-sectional dimension) but has **negligible effect on the far-field stresses**.

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain St. Venant's Principle. A rectangular bar 80 mm × 40 mm is subjected to an axial load of 160 kN through a bracket. Find the stress in the bar at a section 120 mm away from the loaded end. State the principle used.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sketch description (2 marks):**

> A rectangular bar (80 mm × 40 mm cross-section) is loaded through a bracket at one end. The bracket applies the load over a finite region (not as a point load). The bar extends in the axial direction; we evaluate stress at a section $x = 120$ mm from the loaded end.

**Step 2 — St. Venant's Principle statement (2 marks):**

*St. Venant's Principle states that the stress distribution produced by statically equivalent load systems is essentially the same at points that are sufficiently far (distance greater than the largest cross-sectional dimension) from the points of load application.*

**Step 3 — Check distance criterion (1 mark):**

Largest dimension: $h_{\max} = 80 \text{ mm}$. Section of interest: $x = 120 \text{ mm} > 80 \text{ mm}$. ✓

The elementary formula is **valid** at this section.

**Step 4 — Stress calculation (2 marks):**

$$A = 80 \times 40 = 3200 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{160 \times 10^3}{3200} = 50 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{\sigma = 50 \text{ MPa at } x = 120 \text{ mm, valid by St. Venant's Principle}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Q: A stepped circular shaft has a sudden change in diameter from 60 mm to 30 mm. A torque of 1500 N·m is applied at the larger end and resisted at the smaller end. The fillet radius at the step is 6 mm. Discuss the validity of using $\tau = Tr/J$ at (i) the section of diameter change, and (ii) 100 mm away from the step on the larger-diameter side.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the stress concentration (1 mark):**

The sudden change in diameter with a small fillet radius ($r = 6$ mm) creates a **geometric stress concentration**. From standard stress concentration charts (Pilkey, *Peterson's Stress Concentration Factors*), the torsional stress concentration factor for this geometry is approximately:
$$K_t \approx 1.55 \text{ to } 1.65$$

**Step 2 — At the section of diameter change, $x = 0$ (3 marks):**

The simple formula $\tau = Tr/J$ assumes a **linear distribution** of shear stress from the axis to the surface. However, at the geometric discontinuity (the shoulder), the actual stress field is **highly three-dimensional** and **non-linear** in the radial direction. The peak stress at the fillet is:
$$\tau_{\max} = K_t \cdot \tau_{\text{nominal}} \approx 1.6 \cdot \frac{T r_1}{J_1}$$

For the larger-diameter side (60 mm diameter):
$$J_1 = \frac{\pi d_1^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.060)^4}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\tau_{\text{nominal}} = \frac{1500 \times 0.030}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 35.4 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau_{\max} \approx 1.6 \times 35.4 = 56.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**St. Venant's Principle** states the elementary formula is **NOT valid** at the section of discontinuity itself.

**Step 3 — At $x = 100$ mm from the step on the larger-diameter side (2 marks):**

The largest cross-sectional dimension in this region is $d_1 = 60 \text{ mm}$. Since $x = 100 \text{ mm} > 60 \text{ mm}$, we are in the *far zone* by St. Venant's rule of thumb.

**Therefore, the elementary formula IS valid here:**
$$\tau(x = 100 \text{ mm}) = \frac{T r_1}{J_1} = \frac{1500 \times 0.030}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 35.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Concluding statement (1 mark):**

The elementary torsional formula $\tau = Tr/J$ is **NOT valid at the discontinuity** (local stress concentration applies) but **IS valid at 100 mm away** from the step (St. Venant's far zone), where the stress has settled to its nominal value of 35.4 MPa.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **🚨 Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the region of validity.** Many students write $\sigma = P/A$ without mentioning that it is valid only for $x \geq h_{\max}$. **Penalty: 1 to 2 marks lost per question.**
> 2. **Conflating Saint-Venant's Principle with stress concentration.** These are *complementary* concepts, not the same. Saint-Venant says *stresses are equal far away*; stress concentration deals with *stresses at the discontinuity*. Examiners test the difference.
> 3. **Using the wrong "largest dimension."** For a $b \times h$ rectangular section, the largest dimension is $\max(b, h)$, not the diagonal or some other quantity.
> 4. **Omitting the formal statement.** Writing a 3-mark answer with just one sentence loses 2 marks. The KTU board expects: (a) Statement of principle, (b) Region of validity, (c) Engineering implication.
> 5. **Numerical errors in unit conversion.** $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$. Mixing up $\text{N/mm}^2$ and $\text{N/m}^2$ leads to off-by-$10^6$ factor errors. Always write units explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — St. Venant's Principle**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Statically equivalent load systems produce *identical* stress distributions **far from** the load application point, but **different** stress distributions **near** it.
- ⭐ **Region of Validity (Rule of Thumb):** $x \geq h_{\max}$ (largest cross-sectional dimension).
- ⭐ **Material-Independent:** The principle is purely a property of the **geometry and loading topology**, not of $E$, $\nu$, or any material constant.
- ⭐ **Justifies Elementary Formulas:** $\sigma = P/A$, $\sigma = My/I$, $\tau = VQ/Ib$, $\tau = Tr/J$ are all valid **only in the far zone**.
- ⭐ **Counterpart Concept:** Stress concentration factors $K_t$ apply in the **local zone** ($x < h_{\max}$).
- ⭐ **Historical Note:** Proposed by **Saint-Venant in 1855**; rigorously proven in the 20th century (Toupin 1965, Knowles 1966).
- ⭐ **Engineer's Use:** Allows use of simple analysis tools for the bulk of the structure while flagging local regions for detailed (FEM or experimental) treatment.
- ⭐ **Common Examples:**
  - Pin/bolt loaded joints → use $K_t$ near the hole, use $P/A$ far from it.
  - Stepped shafts → use $K_t$ at the fillet, use $Tr/J$ far from the step.
  - Welded attachments → use $K_t$ at the weld toe, use elementary formulas elsewhere.
- ⭐ **Threshold Distance:** Conservative engineering rule is **one full cross-section depth** $h$ away from any discontinuity.

> 🎯 **Final One-Liner for KTU Viva:**
> *"St. Venant's Principle decouples the local load-introduction mechanics from the global structural response — that's why a tiny stress concentration at a bolt hole does not bring down a bridge."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Strain Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress-Strain Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Fundamental Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stress ($\sigma$)** is defined as the internal resistive force developed per unit area of a cross-section when an external load is applied.
> $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$
> where $P$ = Axial load (N) and $A$ = Original cross-sectional area (m² or mm²). **Unit: N/m² (Pa) or N/mm² (MPa).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain ($\epsilon$)** is the ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension of a body. It is a dimensionless quantity.
> $$\epsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$
> where $\delta L$ = Change in length and $L$ = Original length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stress-Strain Diagram:** A graphical representation plotted between stress ($\sigma$) along the Y-axis and strain ($\epsilon$) along the X-axis. It characterizes the mechanical behaviour of materials under uniaxial loading and is the foundation of all structural design in civil and mechanical engineering.

## 1.2 Types of Stress and Strain

| Type | Symbol | Formula | Application |
|------|--------|---------|-------------|
| **Tensile Stress** | $\sigma_t$ | $P/A$ (pulling) | Tie members, cables |
| **Compressive Stress** | $\sigma_c$ | $P/A$ (pushing) | Columns, struts |
| **Shear Stress** | $\tau$ | $V/A$ | Bolts, rivets, beams |
| **Linear (Normal) Strain** | $\epsilon$ | $\delta L / L$ | Axial deformation |
| **Shear Strain** | $\gamma$ | $\delta x / L$ | Angular distortion |
| **Volumetric Strain** | $\epsilon_v$ | $\delta V / V$ | 3D bulk deformation |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Rubber Band vs. Steel Wire Analogy:**
> Imagine stretching two materials — a rubber band and a steel wire — using identical forces. The **rubber band stretches enormously** (high strain, low stress capacity) before snapping, while the **steel wire barely elongates** (low strain, high stress capacity) but can carry enormous loads. Plotting Force-vs-Elongation for both gives different curves.
> 
> The **Stress-Strain Diagram is essentially this curve, but normalized** (force/area vs elongation/length) so we can compare *any* material on a single universal graph. This is the engineer's "fingerprint" of a material — it tells us *how* a material fails, *when* it yields, and *how much* it can safely carry.

## 1.4 Importance in KTU 2024 Scheme

- **Direct application in CO1:** Understand the mechanical behaviour of engineering materials.
- **Foundation for Module 2:** SFD and BMD require knowledge of elastic and plastic behaviour.
- **Design relevance:** Selection of factor of safety, allowable stress design, and limit state design all stem from this single diagram.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Complete Stress-Strain Curve for Ductile Material (Mild Steel)

The stress-strain curve of mild steel under uniaxial tension is divided into **distinct critical regions**, each with a unique physical meaning:

### Region-by-Region Analysis

**Region A → B: Linear Elastic Region (Hooke's Law Zone)**
- Stress is **directly proportional** to strain.
- Material obeys **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$
- $E$ = Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity), **Unit: N/mm² or GPa**
- On unloading, the specimen returns to its original length → **fully recoverable deformation**.
- Point A = Origin (no stress, no strain); Point B = **Proportional Limit** (highest stress at which the $\sigma$-$\epsilon$ curve is still a straight line).

**Region B → C: Elastic Region (Non-linear but recoverable)**
- Stress and strain are **no longer proportional** but deformation is still recoverable.
- Point C = **Elastic Limit** — the maximum stress up to which the body regains its original shape upon unloading.

**Region C → D: Yielding Phenomenon**
- Material begins to deform **plastically** (permanently).
- Upper Yield Point (D): Stress at which yielding *commences*.
- Lower Yield Point (E): Stress at which yielding *propagates steadily*.
- Yielding is characterized by a **large increase in strain with little or no increase in stress**.
- **Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$):** The stress at which material yields — critical for design.

**Region E → F: Strain Hardening (Plastic Region)**
- Beyond yielding, material again offers resistance to deformation.
- Internal crystalline structure re-aligns → material becomes **stronger but less ductile**.

**Point F: Ultimate Stress Point (Ultimate Tensile Strength, UTS)**
- **Maximum stress** the material can withstand.
- $\sigma_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ where $A_0$ is the *original* cross-section.
- Beyond this point, **necking** begins.

**Region F → G: Necking and Fracture**
- Localized reduction in cross-section ("neck") → actual stress rises rapidly.
- Engineering stress falls (because we divide by original area).
- **Breaking Stress ($\sigma_b$):** Stress at which the specimen ultimately fractures.

## 2.2 Key Mechanical Properties from the Diagram

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value (Mild Steel) |
|----------|--------|------------|---------------------------|
| **Young's Modulus** | $E$ | Slope of linear elastic region | $2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² |
| **Proportional Limit** | $\sigma_p$ | End of straight-line portion | 200 N/mm² |
| **Elastic Limit** | $\sigma_e$ | Max recoverable stress | ~210 N/mm² |
| **Yield Stress** | $\sigma_y$ | Onset of plastic deformation | 250 N/mm² |
| **Ultimate Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_u$ | Maximum stress in diagram | 400 N/mm² |
| **Breaking Stress** | $\sigma_b$ | Stress at fracture | 280 N/mm² |
| **Percentage Elongation** | $\%EL$ | Ductility measure | 20–30% |
| **Percentage Reduction in Area** | $\%RA$ | Ductility measure | 50–60% |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all examinable formulas for the Stress-Strain Diagram topic. Master these for any KTU Part A or Part B question.

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Frequency |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\sigma = P/A$ | Normal stress definition | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $\epsilon = \delta L / L$ | Linear strain definition | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$ | Hooke's Law (within elastic limit) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $\delta L = PL / AE$ | Elongation of uniform bar | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $\tau = G \gamma$ | Shear Hooke's Law | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | E, G, ν relationship | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$ | Bulk Modulus relation | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $\epsilon_v = \epsilon_x + \epsilon_y + \epsilon_z$ | Volumetric strain | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\epsilon)$ | True stress relation | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $\epsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\epsilon)$ | True strain (logarithmic) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 11 | $\text{Factor of Safety} = \sigma_y / \sigma_{allow}$ | Design safety | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 12 | $\text{Strain Energy} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \epsilon \cdot V = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2E}$ | Energy stored per unit volume | ⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Selection of structural steel grade (Fe415, Fe500) for reinforced concrete uses the yield stress value.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of pressure vessels, shafts, and couplings requires UTS to prevent brittle failure.
- **Aerospace Engineering:** Aluminium alloys are chosen based on strength-to-weight ratio derived from $\sigma_u / \rho$.
- **Failure Analysis:** Forensic engineers use the fracture surface and stress-strain behaviour to determine the cause of collapse (ductile vs. brittle failure).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Derivation of Hooke's Law from Stress-Strain Diagram

**Statement:** Within the proportional limit, stress is directly proportional to strain.

**Step 1: Empirical Observation**
From the linear region (A→B) of the stress-strain diagram, we observe that if the stress doubles, the strain also doubles, and so on. The relationship is linear.

**Step 2: Mathematical Representation**
Let $k$ be the constant of proportionality. Then:

$$\sigma \propto \epsilon$$

**Step 3: Introduction of Modulus of Elasticity**
Replacing the proportionality sign with an equals sign and introducing the constant $E$ (Young's Modulus):

$$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$$

**Step 4: Substituting the Definitions**
Substituting $\sigma = P/A$ and $\epsilon = \delta L / L$:

$$\frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

**Step 5: Solving for Elongation**
Cross-multiplying:

$$P \cdot L = E \cdot A \cdot \delta L$$

$$\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is one of the **most frequently asked KTU Part B equations** — especially with stepped bars, compound bars, or bars with varying cross-section.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between E, G, and Poisson's Ratio (ν)

**Given:** Consider a small cubic element of material subjected to pure shear stress $\tau$ on its faces.

**Step 1: Shear Strain Energy**
The shear strain energy per unit volume is:

$$U_{shear} = \frac{1}{2} \tau \gamma$$

**Step 2: Express in Terms of Principal Stresses**
For pure shear, the principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$, and $\sigma_3 = 0$.

**Step 3: Equivalent Normal Strain Energy**
Using principal stresses, the equivalent normal strain energy per unit volume is:

$$U_{normal} = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \right]$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Principal Stresses**

$$U_{normal} = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \tau^2 + \tau^2 + 0 - 2\nu(\tau \cdot (-\tau) + 0 + 0) \right]$$

**Step 5: Simplify**

$$U_{normal} = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ 2\tau^2 + 2\nu\tau^2 \right] = \frac{\tau^2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 6: Equate Shear and Normal Strain Energies (Equivalence Principle)**

$$\frac{1}{2} \tau \gamma = \frac{\tau^2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7: Substitute $\gamma = \tau / G$**

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\tau^2}{G} = \frac{\tau^2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 8: Cross-multiply and Simplify**

$$\frac{1}{2G} = \frac{1+\nu}{E}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: True Stress and True Strain

**Engineering Stress Limitation:** Engineering stress uses the *original* area $A_0$, which becomes inaccurate after necking.

**Step 1: Engineering Stress Definition**

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{P}{A_0}$$

**Step 2: True Stress Definition (using instantaneous area $A_i$)**

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{P}{A_i}$$

**Step 3: Volume Constancy Assumption (Plastic Deformation)**
Assuming material is incompressible during plastic deformation:

$$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i$$

**Step 4: Express $A_i$ in terms of $A_0$**

$$A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \epsilon}$$

where $\epsilon = (L_i - L_0)/L_0$ is the engineering strain.

**Step 5: Substitute to Find True Stress**

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{P}{A_0/(1+\epsilon)} = \frac{P}{A_0}(1+\epsilon) = \sigma_{eng}(1+\epsilon)$$

**Final Result 1:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\epsilon)}$$

**Step 6: True Strain Definition**
True strain is the summation of infinitesimal strains:

$$\epsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right)$$

**Step 7: Express in terms of engineering strain**

$$\epsilon_{true} = \ln\left(1 + \epsilon_{eng}\right)$$

**Final Result 2:**
$$\boxed{\epsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\epsilon_{eng})}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Examples

### Example 1: Direct Application of Hooke's Law

**Problem:** A steel rod of length 2 m and cross-section 25 mm × 25 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 125 kN. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Find (a) Stress, (b) Strain, (c) Elongation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Given Data**
- $L = 2$ m $= 2000$ mm
- $A = 25 \times 25 = 625$ mm²
- $P = 125$ kN $= 125{,}000$ N
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

**Step 2: Calculate Stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{125{,}000}{625} = 200 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Calculate Strain (using Hooke's Law)**

$$\epsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{200}{2 \times 10^5} = 1 \times 10^{-3} = 0.001$$

**Step 4: Calculate Elongation**

$$\delta L = \epsilon \cdot L = 0.001 \times 2000 = 2 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma = 200$ N/mm², $\epsilon = 0.001$, $\delta L = 2$ mm

---

### Example 2: True Stress Calculation

**Problem:** A specimen of original area 50 mm² and original gauge length 100 mm is loaded until the load reaches 25 kN. At this point, the gauge length is 101 mm and the cross-section is 49.5 mm². Find engineering stress, true stress, and the percentage difference.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Engineering Strain**

$$\epsilon_{eng} = \frac{101 - 100}{100} = 0.01$$

**Step 2: Engineering Stress**

$$\sigma_{eng} = \frac{P}{A_0} = \frac{25{,}000}{50} = 500 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3: True Stress (using instantaneous area)**

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{P}{A_i} = \frac{25{,}000}{49.5} = 505.05 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Verify using formula**

$$\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\epsilon) = 500 \times 1.01 = 505 \text{ N/mm}^2 \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Percentage Difference**

$$\% \text{ Diff} = \frac{505.05 - 500}{500} \times 100 = 1.01\%$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_{eng} = 500$ N/mm², $\sigma_{true} = 505$ N/mm², % diff $= 1.01\%$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Stress-Strain Curve for Ductile Material (Mild Steel)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["ORIGIN A: No stress, no strain"] -->|Elastic Loading| B["POINT B: PROPORTIONAL LIMIT sigma_p = 200 MPa"]
    B -->|Linear Region Hooke Law| C["POINT C: ELASTIC LIMIT sigma_e = 210 MPa"]
    C -->|Yielding Begins| D["POINT D: UPPER YIELD POINT"]
    D -->|Sudden Yield Drop| E["POINT E: LOWER YIELD POINT sigma_y = 250 MPa"]
    E -->|Strain Hardening| F["POINT F: ULTIMATE STRESS sigma_u = 400 MPa"]
    F -->|Necking Begins| G["POINT G: FRACTURE POINT sigma_b = 280 MPa"]
    
    H["REGION 1: Linear Elastic A to B Hookes Law Valid"] -.-> B
    I["REGION 2: Non-Linear Elastic B to C Fully Recoverable"] -.-> C
    J["REGION 3: Perfectly Plastic C to E Large Strain No Stress Increase"] -.-> E
    K["REGION 4: Strain Hardening E to F Material Strengthens"] -.-> F
    L["REGION 5: Necking F to G Localized Reduction in Area"] -.-> G
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram: Ductile vs Brittle vs Elastic Materials

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["MILD STEEL - Ductile Material"] --> A1["High Yield Point around 250 MPa"]
    A1 --> A2["Long Plastic Region"]
    A2 --> A3["High Ductility 20 to 30 percent Elongation"]
    A3 --> A4["Failure Mode: Cup and Cone Ductile"]
    
    B["CAST IRON - Brittle Material"] --> B1["No Distinct Yield Point"]
    B1 --> B2["No Plastic Region"]
    B2 --> B3["Low Ductility 1 to 3 percent Elongation"]
    B3 --> B4["Failure Mode: Sudden Flat Fracture"]
    
    C["RUBBER - Elastic Material"] --> C1["Non-Linear Stress Strain Curve"]
    C1 --> C2["Very High Strain Capacity"]
    C2 --> C3["Fully Recoverable Deformation"]
    C3 --> C4["Hysteresis Loop on Loading Unloading"]
    
    A4 --> X["DESIGN CHOICE: Preferred for structures needing warning before failure"]
    B4 --> Y["DESIGN CHOICE: Used where compressive strength is primary concern"]
    C4 --> Z["DESIGN CHOICE: Used for vibration isolators and seals"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Energy Absorption in Stress-Strain Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    P["EXTERNAL LOAD P Applied on Specimen"] --> Q["STRESS GENERATED sigma equals P by A"]
    Q --> R["STRAIN DEVELOPED epsilon equals delta L by L"]
    R --> S{"STRAIN REGION CHECK"}
    S -->|Within Elastic Limit| T["STORED STRAIN ENERGY equals half sigma epsilon V equals sigma squared V by 2E"]
    S -->|Beyond Elastic Limit| U["PLASTIC DEFORMATION Permanent Energy Dissipated as Heat"]
    S -->|At Fracture| V["FAILURE: Energy Released as Sound, Heat, and Surface Energy"]
    T --> W["RECOVERABLE: Material returns to original shape on unloading"]
    U --> X["IRRECOVERABLE: Permanent change in dimensions"]
    V --> Y["TOTAL FAILURE: Complete loss of load-bearing capacity"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Hooke's Law in Multi-Axial State

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["3D STRESS STATE on Element"] --> Sx["sigma_x along X axis"]
    Start --> Sy["sigma_y along Y axis"]
    Start --> Sz["sigma_z along Z axis"]
    Start --> Txy["tau_xy Shear in XY plane"]
    Start --> Tyz["tau_yz Shear in YZ plane"]
    Start --> Tzx["tau_zx Shear in ZX plane"]
    
    Sx --> Ex["epsilon_x equals 1 by E times sigma_x minus nu sigma_y plus sigma_z"]
    Sy --> Ey["epsilon_y equals 1 by E times sigma_y minus nu sigma_x plus sigma_z"]
    Sz --> Ez["epsilon_z equals 1 by E times sigma_z minus nu sigma_x plus sigma_y"]
    
    Txy --> Gxy["gamma_xy equals tau_xy by G"]
    Tyz --> Gyz["gamma_yz equals tau_yz by G"]
    Tzx --> Gzx["gamma_zx equals tau_zx by G"]
    
    Ex --> Final["COMPLETE STRAIN STATE"]
    Ey --> Final
    Ez --> Final
    Gxy --> Final
    Gyz --> Final
    Gzx --> Final
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Classification of Material Behaviour

```mermaid
graph TD
    M["MATERIAL UNDER LOAD"] --> N{"Behaviour Type?"}
    N -->|Elastic| O["Deformation Fully Recoverable"]
    N -->|Plastic| P["Deformation Permanent"]
    N -->|Viscoelastic| Q["Time-Dependent Recovery"]
    N -->|Elasto-Plastic| R["Both Elastic and Plastic Regions"]
    
    O --> O1["Linear Elastic: Hookes Law sigma equals E epsilon"]
    O --> O2["Non-Linear Elastic: Rubber, Concrete in compression"]
    
    P --> P1["Perfectly Plastic: No strain hardening, mild steel in yield region"]
    P --> P2["Strain Hardening: Stress increases with strain, copper, aluminium"]
    
    R --> R1["Ductile Materials: Mild steel, aluminium - large plastic region"]
    R --> R2["Brittle Materials: Cast iron, glass - negligible plastic region"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define the following terms with reference to a stress-strain diagram for mild steel:**
**(a) Proportional Limit    (b) Elastic Limit    (c) Yield Point**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Proportional Limit:** It is the maximum stress up to which stress is directly proportional to strain, i.e., the curve remains a straight line. It is the endpoint of the linear elastic region (Hooke's Law region). For mild steel, it is approximately **200 N/mm²**.

**(b) Elastic Limit:** It is the maximum stress up to which the material regains its original shape and size completely upon removal of the load. Deformation up to this point is fully recoverable (non-permanent). For mild steel, it is slightly higher than the proportional limit, around **210 N/mm²**.

**(c) Yield Point:** It is the stress at which the material begins to deform plastically without any increase in load (or with a small decrease followed by a flat plateau). The corresponding stress is the **yield stress ($\sigma_y$)**, which is **250 N/mm²** for mild steel. It is a critical design parameter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students confuse the elastic limit and proportional limit. **Remember:** The proportional limit is defined by the *linearity* of the curve, while the elastic limit is defined by *recoverability* of deformation. They are different points, though close in value.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between engineering stress and true stress. Why is true stress higher than engineering stress in the plastic region?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Engineering Stress ($\sigma_{eng}$) | True Stress ($\sigma_{true}$) |
|-----------|-------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Load divided by *original* cross-sectional area | Load divided by *instantaneous* cross-sectional area |
| **Formula** | $\sigma_{eng} = P / A_0$ | $\sigma_{true} = P / A_i$ |
| **Validity** | Accurate only up to ultimate point | Accurate throughout the loading |
| **Behaviour at necking** | Appears to decrease (false) | Continues to increase (true) |

**Why True Stress > Engineering Stress (in plastic region):**

During plastic deformation, the cross-sectional area of the specimen **decreases continuously** due to necking and elongation. The actual (instantaneous) area $A_i$ is always **less than** the original area $A_0$ beyond the elastic region. Since the load is divided by a *smaller* number in the true stress formula:

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{P}{A_i} > \frac{P}{A_0} = \sigma_{eng}$$

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1+\epsilon)}$$

where $\epsilon$ is the engineering strain. Since $\epsilon > 0$ in the plastic region, true stress is always greater than engineering stress.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, **Part B Module 1 questions carry 14 marks** with an internal choice. Two alternative questions are provided below.

---

### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Draw a labelled stress-strain diagram for a ductile material (mild steel) under uniaxial tension. **Explain clearly the significance of each labelled point and region.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel bar 30 mm diameter and 600 mm long is subjected to an axial tensile load. The bar elongates by 0.45 mm under a load of 90 kN. Calculate: **(i)** Stress, **(ii)** Strain, **(iii)** Young's Modulus. **If the proportional limit is 200 N/mm², what is the maximum additional load that can be applied without exceeding the proportional limit?** **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

### Part (a) — Solution

**The labelled stress-strain diagram for mild steel is as follows (described in text form since the diagram is conceptual):**

| Label | Point/Region | Significance |
|-------|--------------|--------------|
