**Step 3: Calculate the change in volume.**

$$\delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = 1.943 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^6 = 971.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 4: Express the final answer clearly.**

$$\boxed{\delta V = +971.4 \text{ mm}^3 \text{ (volume increases)}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{final} = 5{,}000{,}971.4 \text{ mm}^3}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A steel cube of side 100 mm is placed in a hydraulic press and subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure of 200 MPa. Given $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$, find:**
**(a) The reduction in each side and the change in volume of the cube.** (7 Marks)
**(b) The Bulk Modulus of the material and verify it with the formula $K = E / [3(1 - 2\nu)]$.** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Solution for Question B:**

**Given Data:**
- Cube side: $a = 100 \text{ mm}$
- Hydrostatic pressure: $p = 200 \text{ MPa}$
- $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200{,}000 \text{ MPa}$
- $\nu = 0.30$

**Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Identify the stress state.**

Under uniform hydrostatic pressure on all six faces:
$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p = -200 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Apply generalized Hooke's Law for $\varepsilon_x$.**

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values.**

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{200{,}000}[-200 - 0.3(-200 - 200)]$$

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{200{,}000}[-200 + 120] = \frac{-80}{200{,}000} = -4 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: By symmetry, $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -4 \times 10^{-4}$ (compressive).**

**Step 5: Calculate reduction in each side.**

$$\Delta a = \varepsilon_x \cdot a = -4 \times 10^{-4} \times 100 = -0.04 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the volumetric strain.**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \times (-4 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the change in volume.**

Original volume: $V = 100^3 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$

$$\delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = -1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = -1200 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\text{Volume decreases by } 1200 \text{ mm}^3}$$

---

**Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Calculate Bulk Modulus from definition.**

$$K = \frac{p}{\vert \varepsilon_v \vert} = \frac{200}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 166{,}666.67 \text{ MPa} \approx 166.67 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 2: Verify using the formula $K = E / [3(1 - 2\nu)]$.**

$$K = \frac{200{,}000}{3(1 - 0.6)} = \frac{200{,}000}{3 \times 0.4} = \frac{200{,}000}{1.2} = 166{,}666.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Confirm both values match.**

$$\boxed{K = 166.67 \text{ GPa} \checkmark}$$

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2–4 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $\nu$:** Many students write $\nu = +\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{long}$, which is **mathematically incorrect** for standard engineering materials. The negative sign is mandatory.
>
> 2. **Skipping the small-strain approximation step:** When deriving $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$, examiners expect to see the step where **second-order terms are dropped**. Omitting this step costs 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing **MPa and GPa** without converting (e.g., $E = 200$ GPa but $p = 200$ MPa) leads to wildly wrong answers. Always express all quantities in **consistent units** (either all in N/mm² or all in MPa).
>
> 4. **Confusing bulk modulus K with Young's Modulus E:** K is a **3D volumetric** property; E is a **1D linear** property. K is always numerically much larger than E.
>
> 5. **Drawing wrong stress diagrams:** In a hydrostatic problem, the stress arrows on all six faces must be drawn **inward** (compressive). KTU graders deduct marks for unclear diagrams.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the LATERAL contraction in Part (a) of question A:** Students often compute longitudinal strain but skip $\varepsilon_y$ and the dimension change. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Negative ratio of lateral to longitudinal strain; **dimensionless**; range $-1 < \nu < 0.5$.

✅ **Volumetric Strain Formula:** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ — valid only for **small strains**.

✅ **Uniaxial Loading Formula:** $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$.

✅ **Hydrostatic Loading Formula:** $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{p}{K} = \dfrac{3p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$.

✅ **Three Critical Inter-Constant Relations:**
- $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$
- $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$
- $\dfrac{9}{E} = \dfrac{1}{K} + \dfrac{3}{G}$

✅ **Boundary Behavior:**
- $\nu = 0.5 \Rightarrow \varepsilon_v = 0$ (incompressible — like rubber)
- $\nu = 0 \Rightarrow$ no lateral deformation (like cork)

✅ **Sign Convention:** Tension = positive; Compression = negative; use negative sign in Poisson's ratio definition to keep $\nu$ positive.

✅ **Unit Discipline:** Always convert $E$ to match stress units before substituting (GPa ↔ MPa = 1000×).

✅ **Small Strain Approximation:** In derivations, drop all products of two or more strains (e.g., $\varepsilon_x \varepsilon_y$) — examiners check for this step.

✅ **Volume Change Direction:** If $1 - 2\nu > 0$ (i.e., $\nu < 0.5$), tensile stress **increases** volume; compressive stress **decreases** volume.

✅ **Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, KTU expects **derivation + numerical application**. Always show the formula first, then substitute numbers, then box the final answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Constants and the Relationship Between Them
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elastic Constants and the Relationship Between Them

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Elastic Constants?

**Elastic constants** are fundamental material properties that quantify the stiffness of an isotropic, homogeneous, and linearly elastic material. They describe the relationship between stresses applied to a body and the resulting deformations (strains) within the elastic limit. For a 3-D isotropic elastic material, **only two independent elastic constants** are theoretically required; however, in engineering practice, **four constants** are commonly used.

### 1.2 The Four Principal Elastic Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|------------------|
| Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity) | **E** | Resistance to **uniaxial tensile/compressive** deformation |
| Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) | **G** | Resistance to **shear** deformation (shape change) |
| Bulk Modulus | **K** | Resistance to **volumetric** change (hydrostatic compression) |
| Poisson's Ratio | **ν** (or μ, m) | Ratio of **lateral strain** to **longitudinal strain** |

### 1.3 Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Young's Modulus (E):** The ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit.
>
> $$\boxed{E = \dfrac{\text{Longitudinal Stress}}{\text{Longitudinal Strain}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Modulus (G):** The ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit.
>
> $$\boxed{G = \dfrac{\text{Shear Stress}}{\text{Shear Strain}} = \dfrac{\tau}{\phi}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Modulus (K):** The ratio of hydrostatic (volumetric) stress to volumetric strain.
>
> $$\boxed{K = \dfrac{\text{Hydrostatic Pressure}}{\text{Volumetric Strain}} = \dfrac{p}{\varepsilon_v} = \dfrac{-p \, V}{\Delta V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** The negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain under uniaxial loading.
>
> $$\boxed{\nu = -\dfrac{\text{Lateral Strain}}{\text{Longitudinal Strain}} = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}}$$

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rubber Band & Sponge" Analogy**
> Imagine stretching a **rubber band** between your fingers. The rubber band becomes **longer (axial extension)** but also becomes **thinner (lateral contraction)**. The ratio of "how much thinner" to "how much longer" is **Poisson's ratio (ν)**. The "stiffness" or "resistance" of the rubber band to being stretched is **Young's Modulus (E)**.
>
> Now take a **sponge** and try to **twist** it (apply shear). The amount it resists twisting is the **Shear Modulus (G)**. If you squeeze the same sponge from all sides (hydrostatic pressure), the resistance to its volume shrinking is the **Bulk Modulus (K)**.
>
> Each of these four constants measures the material's response to a different type of "stress challenge". For a perfectly isotropic material, they are all mathematically interlinked — knowing any **two** allows you to compute the other **two**.

### 1.5 Limiting Values & Physical Constants

- **Units:** All four constants have units of **N/m²** (or **Pa**, **MPa**, **GPa**). **Poisson's ratio (ν)** is **dimensionless**.
- **Range of Poisson's Ratio:** $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$ for most engineering materials
- For **steel**: $E \approx 200$ **GPa**, $G \approx 77$ **GPa**, $K \approx 160$ **GPa**, $\nu \approx 0.3$
- For **concrete**: $E \approx 30$ **GPa**, $\nu \approx 0.15$ to $0.20$
- For **rubber (nearly incompressible)**: $\nu \approx 0.5$, $K \to \infty$
- For **cork (special)**: $\nu \approx 0$ (almost no lateral expansion when compressed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The theoretical limit $\nu = 0.5$ represents a perfectly incompressible material (e.g., rubber, water). The lower limit $\nu = 0$ means no lateral contraction (rare, e.g., cork).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions (KTU Theory Block)

The relationships derived below rest on these assumptions:

- The material is **homogeneous** (uniform properties throughout).
- The material is **isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
- The material is **linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
- Deformations are **infinitesimally small** (linearized strain theory).
- The material is **continuous** (no voids or discontinuities).

### 2.2 Generalized Hooke's Law for a 3-D Isotropic Body

For a triaxial state of stress $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z)$ with no shear components:

$$\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

The volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize this entire block for KTU Board Exams.**

| # | Relationship | Standard Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|--------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | E, G, ν | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Most-tested derivation in KTU |
| 2 | E, K, ν | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | Used for hydrostatic loading problems |
| 3 | E, G, K | $E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K + G}$ | Universal 3-constant formula |
| 4 | Reciprocal of (3) | $\dfrac{1}{E} = \dfrac{1}{3G} + \dfrac{1}{9K}$ | Quick computation trick |
| 5 | ν from G & K | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)} = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{6K + 2G}$ | Finding ν when K and G are known |
| 6 | K from E, ν | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ | Solves for K from tensile tests |
| 7 | G from E, ν | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | Solves for G from tensile tests |
| 8 | G, K, E ratio (steel) | $\dfrac{E}{G} \approx 2.6$ | Quick check in numericals |
| 9 | Volumetric strain | $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3K}$ | Hydrostatic / multi-axial problems |
| 10 | Shear strain (uniaxial) | $\phi = \dfrac{\tau}{G}$ | Torsion problems |

### 2.4 Why These Relationships Matter in Engineering

- **Design of Pressure Vessels, Dams, Submarines:** All surfaces experience hydrostatic pressure; use **K** to compute volumetric changes.
- **Design of Shafts, Springs, Rivets, Bolts:** These elements experience torsional/shear loading; use **G**.
- **Design of Tensile Members, Columns, Beams:** Use **E** for axial/bending deformation.
- **Stability Checks (e.g., column buckling, plates):** Poisson's ratio $\nu$ controls lateral restraint, which is critical for buckling loads.
- **Composite Materials & FEA:** Knowing only two constants (usually E and ν) lets you build the full elastic stiffness matrix $[C]$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these derivations appear in KTU exams:** The three fundamental relationships (1), (2), and (3) in the formula sheet above are asked in KTU Board Exams almost every semester, either as direct 7-mark derivations or as 14-mark combined questions with numerical verification.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship E = 2G(1 + ν) — Direct Strain Method

This is the **most important derivation** in this module. We will prove it by considering the principal strains along the diagonals of a square element subjected to pure shear.

**Step 1 — Set up the element under pure shear**

Consider a square element of side length $a$ subjected to a complementary pair of shear stresses $\tau$ on its four faces.

By definition of shear modulus, the shear strain is:

$$\phi = \dfrac{\tau}{G}$$

The element deforms into a parallelogram. The top face displaces horizontally by an amount $\delta = a\phi$ relative to the bottom face.

**Step 2 — Geometry of the deformed diagonal**

Set up a coordinate system where the bottom-left corner $A$ is at the origin $(0,0)$. After deformation:

- Corner $A$ remains at $(0, 0)$
- Corner $B$ at $(a, 0)$
- Corner $C$ at $(a + \delta, \ a) = (a + a\phi, \ a)$
- Corner $D$ at $(\delta, \ a) = (a\phi, \ a)$

The diagonal $AC$ originally has length $a\sqrt{2}$. After deformation, the new length of $AC$ is:

$$AC' = \sqrt{(a + a\phi)^2 + a^2}$$

**Step 3 — Linearize the new length (small strain assumption)**

Expand the squared term:

$$\begin{aligned}
AC'^2 &= (a + a\phi)^2 + a^2 \\
      &= a^2(1 + \phi)^2 + a^2 \\
      &= a^2 \left[ (1 + \phi)^2 + 1 \right] \\
      &= a^2 \left[ 1 + 2\phi + \phi^2 + 1 \right] \\
      &= a^2 \left[ 2 + 2\phi + \phi^2 \right]
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\phi$ is infinitesimally small, the term $\phi^2$ is a higher-order infinitesimal and is **neglected**:

$$AC'^2 \approx a^2 (2 + 2\phi) = 2a^2(1 + \phi)$$

Take the square root:

$$AC' = a\sqrt{2(1 + \phi)} = a\sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \phi}$$

Using the binomial expansion $\sqrt{1 + \phi} \approx 1 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ (valid for small $\phi$):

$$AC' \approx a\sqrt{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the strain along diagonal AC**

$$\varepsilon_{AC} = \dfrac{AC' - AC}{AC} = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) - a\sqrt{2}}{a\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{\phi}{2}$$

Therefore, the strain along the diagonal $AC$ is:

$$\varepsilon_{AC} = \dfrac{\phi}{2} = \dfrac{\tau}{2G}$$

**Step 5 — Express the same state in terms of principal stresses**

The pure shear state $(\tau)$ is **equivalent** to a biaxial stress state with:

- A **tensile principal stress** $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ acting along the diagonal $AC$
- A **compressive principal stress** $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ acting along the diagonal $BD$

(These principal stresses act on planes oriented at $45°$ to the original faces — this is a standard Mohr's circle result.)

**Step 6 — Apply Hooke's Law along the diagonal AC**

Using the generalized Hooke's law for the principal directions:

$$\varepsilon_{AC} = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu \, \sigma_2\right] = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(-\tau)\right] = \dfrac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 7 — Equate the two expressions for $\varepsilon_{AC}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} &= \dfrac{\tau}{2G} \\
\dfrac{1 + \nu}{E} &= \dfrac{1}{2G} \\
2G(1 + \nu) &= E
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance:** This is the most fundamental relationship in solid mechanics. It tells us that $E$ is always **greater** than $2G$ (since $1 + \nu > 1$ for any real material). For most metals, $E/G \approx 2.6$ (since $\nu \approx 0.3$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship E = 3K(1 − 2ν) — Volumetric Strain Method

**Step 1 — Hydrostatic loading setup**

Apply a uniform hydrostatic pressure $p$ to a cubic element of side $a$. The three normal stresses on the cube are all equal to $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$ (compressive). The shear stresses are zero.

**Step 2 — Compute each normal strain from generalized Hooke's Law**

$$\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \dfrac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \dfrac{-p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

By symmetry, $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = \dfrac{-p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the volumetric strain**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_v &= \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \\
              &= 3 \cdot \dfrac{-p}{E}(1 - 2\nu) \\
              &= \dfrac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of Bulk Modulus**

By definition:

$$K = \dfrac{-p}{\varepsilon_v} = \dfrac{-p}{\dfrac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}} = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** When $\nu \to 0.5$, the denominator $1 - 2\nu \to 0$, so $K \to \infty$ — confirming that a perfectly incompressible material resists any volume change.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship E = 9KG / (3K + G) — Elimination of ν

**Step 1 — Express ν from each of the previous two relationships**

From $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$:

$$1 + \nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1 = \dfrac{E - 2G}{2G}$$

From $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$:

$$1 - 2\nu = \dfrac{E}{3K} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2\nu = 1 - \dfrac{E}{3K} = \dfrac{3K - E}{3K} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$$

**Step 2 — Set the two expressions for ν equal**

$$\dfrac{E - 2G}{2G} = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply to clear denominators**

$$\begin{aligned}
6K(E - 2G) &= 2G(3K - E) \\
6KE - 12KG &= 6KG - 2GE \\
6KE + 2GE &= 6KG + 12KG \\
2E(3K + G) &= 18KG \\
E(3K + G) &= 9KG
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K + G}}$$

**Equivalent reciprocal form:**

$$\dfrac{1}{E} = \dfrac{3K + G}{9KG} = \dfrac{1}{3G} + \dfrac{1}{9K}$$

This is a very useful form for **numerical substitution**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: ν in terms of G and K

**Step 1 — Substitute $E = 9KG/(3K + G)$ into the expression $\nu = (E - 2G)/(2G)$**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu &= \dfrac{1}{2G}\left[\dfrac{9KG}{3K + G} - 2G\right] \\
   &= \dfrac{1}{2G} \cdot \dfrac{9KG - 2G(3K + G)}{3K + G} \\
   &= \dfrac{1}{2G} \cdot \dfrac{9KG - 6KG - 2G^2}{3K + G} \\
   &= \dfrac{1}{2G} \cdot \dfrac{3KG - 2G^2}{3K + G} \\
   &= \dfrac{1}{2G} \cdot \dfrac{G(3K - 2G)}{3K + G} \\
   &= \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)} = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{6K + 2G}}$$

---

### 3.5 Verification of Formulas (Numerical Sanity Check)

For **structural steel**: $E = 200$ GPa, $G = 77$ GPa, $K = 160$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$.

| Formula | LHS | RHS | Status |
|---------|-----|-----|--------|
| $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | $200$ | $2(77)(1.3) = 200.2$ | ✓ |
| $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | $200$ | $3(160)(0.4) = 192$ | ≈ |
| $E = 9KG/(3K+G)$ | $200$ | $\dfrac{9(160)(77)}{480+77} = 199.07$ | ✓ |
| $\nu = (3K-2G)/(6K+2G)$ | $0.3$ | $(480-154)/(960+154) = 0.293$ | ✓ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: The Four Elastic Constants & Their Engineering Tests

This block diagram maps the **inter-relationships** between the four elastic constants and the **engineering tests** used to measure them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([ISOTROPIC LINEAR ELASTIC MATERIAL]) --> test1
    startA --> test2
    startA --> test3
    startA --> test4

    test1[UNIAXIAL TENSION TEST]:::test --> Econst[Youngs Modulus E]
    test1 --> nuConst[Poissons Ratio NU]

    test2[TORSION TEST ON SHAFT]:::test --> Gconst[Shear Modulus G]

    test3[HYDROSTATIC PRESSURE TEST]:::test --> Kconst[Bulk Modulus K]

    test4[LATERAL STRAIN MEASUREMENT]:::test --> nuConst

    Econst -.-> rel1[E = 2G 1 plus NU]
    Gconst -.-> rel1
    nuConst -.-> rel1

    Econst -.-> rel2[E = 3K 1 minus 2NU]
    Kconst -.-> rel2
    nuConst -.-> rel2

    Econst -.-> rel3[E equals 9KG over 3K plus G]
    Gconst -.-> rel3
    Kconst -.-> rel3

    rel1 --> resultBox[Only TWO independent constants for isotropic materials]
    rel2 --> resultBox
    rel3 --> resultBox

    classDef test fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#003366
    classDef result fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF8800,stroke-width:2px,color:#663300
    class resultBox result
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Derivation Logic Chain

This flowchart shows the **logical dependency** in the derivations, illustrating that Derivation 3 (and 4) build upon Derivations 1 and 2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pure Shear Element] -->|Diagonal Strain| B[E equals 2G times 1 plus NU]
    C[Hydrostatic Pressure Element] -->|Volumetric Strain| D[E equals 3K times 1 minus 2NU]
    B -->|Eliminate NU| E[E equals 9KG over 3K plus G]
    D -->|Eliminate NU| E
    B -->|Eliminate E| F[NU equals 3K minus 2G over 6K plus 2G]
    D -->|Eliminate E| F
    E -->|Solve for K| G[K equals EG over 3 times 3G minus E]
    E -->|Solve for G| H[G equals 3KE over 9K minus E]

    classDef derived fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    class B,D,E,F,G,H derived
```

### 4.3 Physical Setup Schematic (Block Description)

**Test Setup for Measuring G (Torsion Test):**

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:grpLeft["FIXED END"]:1
    columns 1
    block:grpShaft["CYLINDRICAL SHAFT"]:3
    columns 1
    block:grpRight["LOADING END"]:1
    columns 3
    Mblock["APPLIED TORQUE M"]:1
    thetablock["ANGLE OF TWIST THETA"]:1
    Lblock["GAUGE LENGTH L"]:1

    grpShaft --> Mblock
    grpShaft --> thetablock
    grpShaft --> Lblock
```

**Textual description of physical setup:**

- A solid cylindrical shaft of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is fixed at one end.
- A torque $M$ is applied at the free end, producing an angle of twist $\theta$.
- The shear modulus is computed as:

$$G = \dfrac{32 M L}{\pi d^4 \, \theta}$$

- The lateral contraction (Poisson effect) on the shaft surface can simultaneously be measured using a strain gauge to determine $\nu$, thereby allowing $E$ to be back-calculated from $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** In lab courses, torsion tests on mild steel specimens are the standard way to measure $G$. A typical KTU experiment asks for the Young's modulus $E$ of the same material from a tension test, and then verification of the formula $E = 2G(1+\nu)$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define Poisson's ratio. State its limiting values for engineering materials and explain the physical significance of these limits.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) is defined as the **negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain** in a material subjected to a uniaxial tensile or compressive stress, within the elastic limit.

$$\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

The negative sign ensures that $\nu$ is a **positive** quantity, since lateral strain is opposite in sign to longitudinal strain (a bar under tension gets longer but thinner).

**Limiting Values:** $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$

- **Lower limit ($\nu = 0$):** No lateral contraction occurs. Example: **cork**, which is why cork is used as a bottle stopper (it slides into a glass neck without exerting lateral pressure).
- **Upper limit ($\nu = 0.5$):** The material is **perfectly incompressible** (volume is preserved exactly). Example: **rubber** and **water**, which is why hydraulic fluid does not change volume when pressurized.

For most engineering metals: $\nu \approx 0.25$ to $0.33$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**State and briefly explain the three fundamental relationships between the elastic constants E, G, K, and ν.**

**Model Answer:**

The three fundamental relationships between the four elastic constants $E, G, K, \nu$ are:

**(i) Relationship between E, G, and ν:**

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

This is derived by analyzing the principal strain along the diagonal of a square element under pure shear. It shows that a material's stiffness in tension is always greater than twice its stiffness in shear.

**(ii) Relationship between E, K, and ν:**

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

This is derived by considering a cubic element under hydrostatic pressure and computing the volumetric strain. It shows that a material's resistance to hydrostatic compression depends on how close $\nu$ is to 0.5.

**(iii) Relationship between E, G, and K:**

$$E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K + G}$$

This is obtained by eliminating $\nu$ from the first two relationships. It is a purely **experimental** relationship that allows computing one constant when the other two are known.

**Physical Insight:** For an isotropic material, only **two** of these constants are independent; the other two can be derived from the above relations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) Derive the relationship E = 2G(1 + ν) for a homogeneous and isotropic material using the principal strain approach. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

We consider a square element of side $a$ subjected to complementary shear stresses $\tau$ on all four faces. The shear strain produced is:

$$\phi = \dfrac{\tau}{G} \quad \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 1: Geometry of deformed diagonal**

Let the bottom-left corner be at the origin. After deformation, the top face displaces by $a\phi$. The new length of the diagonal $AC$ is:

$$AC' = \sqrt{(a + a\phi)^2 + a^2} = a\sqrt{(1 + \phi)^2 + 1}$$

**Step 2: Linearization**

Expanding and neglecting $\phi^2$:

$$AC'^2 = a^2[2 + 2\phi + \phi^2] \approx 2a^2(1 + \phi)$$

$$AC' = a\sqrt{2}\sqrt{1 + \phi} \approx a\sqrt{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3: Strain along diagonal AC**

$$\varepsilon_{AC} = \dfrac{AC' - AC}{AC} = \dfrac{a\sqrt{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) - a\sqrt{2}}{a\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{\phi}{2} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4: Equivalent biaxial principal stress system**

The pure shear state is equivalent to a biaxial state with:

- $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ along diagonal $AC$ (tensile)
- $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ along diagonal $BD$ (compressive)

**Step 5: Apply generalized Hooke's law along AC**

$$\varepsilon_{AC} = \dfrac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2] = \dfrac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \dfrac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 6: Equate (iii) and (iv)**

$$\dfrac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} = \dfrac{\phi}{2} = \dfrac{\tau}{2G}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$2G(1 + \nu) = E$$

$$\boxed{\therefore E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

---

**(b) A steel specimen has Young's modulus E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² and Poisson's ratio ν = 0.3. Calculate the values of the Shear Modulus (G) and Bulk Modulus (K). Verify the relationship E = 9KG/(3K + G). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200$ GPa
- $\nu = 0.3$

**Step 1: Calculate G**

Using $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$:

$$G = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{2(1 + 0.3)} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{2.6} = 0.7692 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{G = 76{,}923.08 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 76.92 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate K**

Using $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$:

$$K = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{3(1 - 0.6)} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{1.2} = 1.6667 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{K = 166{,}666.67 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 166.67 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 3: Verify E = 9KG/(3K + G)**

Compute numerator and denominator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 9KG = 9 \times 1.6667 \times 10^5 \times 0.7692 \times 10^5 / (10^5 \times 10^5)$$
$$= 9 \times 1.6667 \times 0.7692 \times 10^5 = 11.538 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 3K + G = 3(1.6667 \times 10^5) + 0.7692 \times 10^5 = 5.0 \times 10^5 + 0.7692 \times 10^5 = 5.7692 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\dfrac{9KG}{3K + G} = \dfrac{11.538 \times 10^5}{5.7692 \times 10^5} = 2.0$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{E = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ GPa} = \text{Given value} \quad \checkmark}$$

**The relationship is verified.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Board Exams — Read carefully!**
>
> **Trap 1 — Forgetting the small-strain approximation in the E = 2G(1+ν) derivation.**
> Many students write $AC' = a\sqrt{(1+\phi)^2 + 1}$ and **forget to apply the binomial expansion** $\sqrt{1+\phi} \approx 1 + \phi/2$. If you skip this step, you cannot reach the final answer. **Lose 2 marks** for this omission.
>
> **Trap 2 — Sign convention error in Poisson's ratio.**
> When writing $\varepsilon_{AC} = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2]$, students sometimes write $\frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 + \nu\sigma_2]$ (forgetting the negative sign for compressive $\sigma_2$). This gives the wrong final formula. **Lose 3 marks.**
>
> **Trap 3 — Unit inconsistency in numerical problems.**
> If the problem gives $E$ in **MPa** and asks for $G$, you must convert carefully: $1$ **GPa** $= 1000$ **MPa**. Many students mix units and get $G$ off by a factor of $10^3$. **Lose 1 mark.**
>
> **Trap 4 — Confusing the three relationships during elimination.**
> When asked to "show that $E = 9KG/(3K+G)$", students must show **all algebraic steps** of elimination of $\nu$. Writing only the final answer without showing the cross-multiplication loses 4–5 marks.
>
> **Trap 5 — Forgetting to state assumptions.**
> The first line of any derivation should be: *"Consider a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material..."* Examiners reward 1 mark for stating assumptions.
>
> **Trap 6 — Wrong bulk modulus formula.**
> Some textbooks/notes incorrectly write $E = 9K(1 - 2\nu)$. The correct formula is $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$. Use $K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ carefully. **Lose 2 marks** for this mistake.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Read this section the night before the exam.**
>
> ✅ **The four elastic constants** are $E$ (Young's modulus), $G$ (Shear modulus), $K$ (Bulk modulus), $\nu$ (Poisson's ratio). All have units of **N/m²** except $\nu$, which is **dimensionless**.
>
> ✅ **For an isotropic material, only TWO constants are independent.** Once any two are known, the other two follow from the three relationships.
>
> ✅ **Master the three key formulas:**
> - $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ — derived from shear + diagonal strain
> - $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ — derived from hydrostatic pressure
> - $E = 9KG/(3K + G)$ — derived by eliminating $\nu$
>
> ✅ **Poisson's ratio range:** $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$. For metals $\nu \approx 0.3$. For rubber $\nu \approx 0.5$ (incompressible). For cork $\nu \approx 0$.
>
> ✅ **Limiting cases of E = 2G(1+ν):**
> - $\nu = 0$ → $E = 2G$ (max shear stiffness relative to Young's modulus)
> - $\nu = 0.5$ → $E = 3G$ (e.g., rubber)
>
> ✅ **Limiting cases of E = 3K(1-2ν):**
> - $\nu = 0$ → $E = 3K$ (cork-like)
> - $\nu = 0.5$ → $K = \infty$ (incompressible material)
>
> ✅ **Common numerical values for steel:** $E = 200$ GPa, $G = 77$ GPa, $K = 160$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. These are "default" values to use if the problem does not specify.
>
> ✅ **Standard for examiners:** Always begin derivations with the assumption statement, then the diagram/setup, then the step-by-step algebra, then the final boxed answer.
>
> ✅ **Shear strain from a uniaxial stress $\sigma$:** The shear strain on a $45°$ plane is $\gamma = (1+\nu)\sigma/E$. This is a frequently asked numerical sub-question.
>
> ✅ **Volumetric strain for triaxial stress:** $\varepsilon_v = (1-2\nu)\sigma/E$ when $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = \sigma$. Equate this to $\sigma/(3K)$ to get $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ — exactly the derivation in Section 3.2.
>
> ✅ **Reciprocal form of the universal formula** (useful for quick numerical checks): $\dfrac{1}{E} = \dfrac{1}{3G} + \dfrac{1}{9K}$.
>
> ✅ **Typical KTU 14-mark question structure:** Part (a) 7 marks = derivation of one of the three relationships; Part (b) 7 marks = numerical problem — "Given two constants, find the other two and verify the third relationship."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bars of Varying Section
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bars of Varying Section

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **bar of varying cross-section** is a structural member whose cross-sectional area is *not constant* along its longitudinal axis. Under an axial load, this variation causes the **internal stress (σ = P/A)** and the **unit elongation (ε = σ/E)** to change from section to section, even when the axial force remains uniform.

The principal sub-categories recognised in the KTU 2024 Strength of Materials syllabus are:

1. **Tapering (Continuously Varying) Bars** — cross-section changes smoothly (e.g., a cone). Typical examples: machine tool spindles, transition pieces in shafts, hammer/press columns, transmission line poles.
2. **Stepped Bars** — cross-section changes abruptly at discrete junctions (e.g., a bolt shank, multi-diameter shaft). Typical examples: crankshafts, coupling rods, railway axles.
3. **Bars of Uniform Strength** — a *specially designed* varying bar in which the **maximum stress remains constant** along the length when self-weight is considered. Typical examples: a leaf spring designed for flexural uniform strength, suspension rods in heavy machinery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Examiner Tip:** In KTU valuation, the phrase *"bar of uniform section"* and *"bar of uniform strength"* are **NOT** synonymous. A uniform-section bar has constant area; a uniform-strength bar has constant *stress* and therefore a varying area. Marks are routinely lost when students confuse the two.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water pipeline** that has a small diameter at the top and a wider diameter at the bottom. As water flows down, the pressure (force per unit area) at the wider section is less. If the pipeline were designed so that the stress (or "pressure") in the walls was *exactly* the same at every level, it would be a **bar of uniform strength**. A simple straight pipe of the same diameter is a *uniform-section* bar, while a pipe whose diameter changes every few feet is a *stepped* bar.

A even simpler analogy: think of a **rope holding a heavy bucket**. If the rope has the *same thickness* throughout, the strands near the top carry more load (their own weight plus the bucket). If you weave the rope *thicker* near the top and *thinner* near the bucket, every cross-section is now equally stressed — a *bar of uniform strength*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric used in this Module:** $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/mm}^{2} = 200\ \text{GPa}$ for **mild steel**, $\gamma_{\text{steel}} \approx 77\ \text{kN/m}^{3}$, $\gamma_{\text{cast iron}} \approx 70.5\ \text{kN/m}^{3}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Description of a Tapering Circular Bar

Consider a circular bar of length $L$ whose diameter varies *linearly* from $D$ at end A to $d$ at end B. Measuring distance $x$ from the smaller end B:

$$d_{x} = d + \left(\dfrac{D - d}{L}\right) x$$

Area at section $x$:

$$A_{x} = \dfrac{\pi \, d_{x}^{2}}{4}$$

The **internal axial force** at any section is $P$ (constant, assuming no intermediate load), so:

$$\sigma_{x} = \dfrac{P}{A_{x}} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi \, d_{x}^{2}}$$

Stress is **maximum at the smallest section** and minimum at the largest. This is the engineering *'why'* behind the visual placement of thicker flanges at points of higher bending moment in beams, etc.

### 2.2 Stepped Bar — Additive Deformation Principle

For a stepped bar, the **superposition principle** applies: the total elongation is the algebraic sum of the elongations of individual prismatic sections.

$$\delta = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{P_{i} \, L_{i}}{A_{i} \, E_{i}}$$

Where $P_i$ is the internal force in section $i$ (computed from equilibrium of the free body beyond that section).

### 2.3 Bars of Uniform Strength

When a bar hangs under its own weight, the stress at any section $x$ is:

$$\sigma_{x} = \dfrac{W_{x}}{A_{x}}$$

where $W_x$ is the weight of material *below* the section. For **uniform strength**, $\sigma_x$ is set equal to the allowable stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$:

$$A_{x} = \dfrac{W_{x}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$

A **sub-case** of great KTU importance: *bar of uniform strength in a fluid* (immersed bar with buoyancy), where the effective unit weight becomes $\gamma_{\text{eff}} = \gamma_{\text{material}} - \gamma_{\text{fluid}}$. The bar's net stress then *decreases* with depth, leading to a *negative taper* (gets thicker downward).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** The theory of bars of uniform strength is the design foundation for: (i) leaf springs in automobiles, (ii) flat belt drives, (iii) riveted and welded pressure vessels, (iv) cantilever beams of uniform strength, and (v) gravity dam cross-sections. In civil structures, it explains why a *masonry retaining wall* is thicker at its base.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Module 1 — Bars of Varying Section)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Diameter variation in a linearly tapering bar | $d_{x} = d + \left(\dfrac{D-d}{L}\right)x$ | $D > d$, $x$ measured from smaller end |
| 2 | Stress in tapering bar at section $x$ | $\sigma_{x} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi \, d_{x}^{2}}$ | $P$ in N, $d_x$ in mm, $\sigma_x$ in MPa |
| 3 | **Elongation of uniformly tapering circular bar** | $\delta = \dfrac{4 P L}{\pi E D d}$ | Major KTU formula; $D$ and $d$ are the end diameters |
| 4 | Elongation of stepped bar | $\delta = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$ | $n$ sections, each prismatic |
| 5 | Elongation of uniform bar under self-weight | $\delta = \dfrac{W L}{2 A E} = \dfrac{\gamma L^{2}}{2 E}$ | $\gamma$ = weight density |
| 6 | Maximum stress in uniform bar under self-weight | $\sigma_{\max} = \gamma L$ | At the support (top of suspended bar) |
| 7 | Tapering bar — diametral expression for uniform strength | $d_{x}^{2} = d_{0}^{2} + \dfrac{16 \gamma}{\pi \sigma_{\text{allow}}}\left(L - x\right)$ | $d_0$ = diameter at the load-end |
| 8 | Bar of uniform strength in a fluid (effective unit weight) | $\gamma_{\text{eff}} = \gamma_{\text{material}} - \gamma_{\text{fluid}}$ | Used when buoyancy reduces self-weight |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever you see $\dfrac{4PL}{\pi E D d}$ in a question paper, it is a *circular* tapering bar. For *rectangular tapering* (one dimension varies), the formula changes — verify the section shape before plugging in values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Elongation of a Uniformly Tapering Circular Bar

**Given:** A solid circular bar of length $L$, diameter $D$ at end A and $d$ at end B, subjected to an axial tensile load $P$ at end A. Modulus of elasticity $E$ is constant.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry of an elementary disc.**

Consider a disc of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from end B (the smaller end). The diameter at this disc, from linear interpolation, is:

$$d_{x} = d + \left(\dfrac{D - d}{L}\right) x \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the area of the disc.**

$$A_{x} = \dfrac{\pi \, d_{x}^{2}}{4} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's law to the elementary disc.**

The internal axial force throughout the bar is $P$ (no intermediate loads). Elongation of the elementary disc:

$$d\delta = \dfrac{P \, dx}{A_{x} \, E} = \dfrac{4P \, dx}{\pi E \, d_{x}^{2}} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the full length.**

Total elongation:

$$\delta = \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{4P \, dx}{\pi E \, d_{x}^{2}}$$

Substitute $d_x$ from (i):

$$\delta = \dfrac{4P}{\pi E} \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{dx}{\left[ d + \left(\dfrac{D - d}{L}\right) x \right]^{2}} \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Step 5 — Use substitution $u = d + \dfrac{(D-d)}{L} x$.**

Then $du = \dfrac{(D-d)}{L} dx \implies dx = \dfrac{L}{D-d} du$.

When $x = 0,\ u = d$; when $x = L,\ u = D$.

$$\delta = \dfrac{4P}{\pi E} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D - d} \int_{d}^{D} \dfrac{du}{u^{2}} \quad \text{...(v)}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$\int_{d}^{D} u^{-2}\,du = \left[-\dfrac{1}{u}\right]_{d}^{D} = -\dfrac{1}{D} + \dfrac{1}{d} = \dfrac{D - d}{D \, d} \quad \text{...(vi)}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back.**

$$\delta = \dfrac{4P}{\pi E} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D - d} \cdot \dfrac{D - d}{D \, d}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \dfrac{4 P L}{\pi E D d}\;} \quad \text{...(vii)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this formula is beautiful:** Notice how $D$ and $d$ appear in the denominator as a *product* — this is the **Geometric Mean Diameter** effect. The tapering bar behaves *as if* it were a uniform bar whose diameter is the geometric mean of the two end diameters, $d_{\text{GM}} = \sqrt{Dd}$. This is a KTU favourite for MCQ traps.

### 3.2 Derivation — Bar of Uniform Strength (Self-Weight Only)

**Given:** A vertical bar of length $L$, suspended from the top, carries its own weight only. Allowable tensile stress = $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Identify the critical section.**

The stress is maximum at the **top** (where the bar must support the entire weight of material below it) and minimum at the **bottom** (free end, zero stress).

**Step 2 — Write equilibrium at section $x$ from the bottom.**

Let $A_x$ be the area at depth $x$ and $W_x$ the weight of the material *below* this section. For uniform strength:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{W_{x}}{A_{x}} \implies W_{x} = \sigma A_{x} \quad \text{...(viii)}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $x$.**

Consider an elementary disc of thickness $dx$ just above the section. Its weight is $\gamma A_{x} \, dx$ where $\gamma$ is the weight density. The increase in $W_x$ when moving upward (i.e., decreasing $x$) is:

$$-dW_{x} = \gamma A_{x} \, dx \implies \dfrac{dW_{x}}{dx} = -\gamma A_{x} \quad \text{...(ix)}$$

(The negative sign is because $W_x$ *decreases* as we move up the bar.)

**Step 4 — Differentiate equation (viii) and combine with (ix).**

$$\dfrac{dW_{x}}{dx} = \sigma \dfrac{dA_{x}}{dx} = -\gamma A_{x}$$

$$\dfrac{dA_{x}}{A_{x}} = -\dfrac{\gamma}{\sigma} dx \quad \text{...(x)}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from the bottom (where $A = A_0 = 0$ at free end... correction: the load is zero but stress must equal $\sigma$, hence the area is *not* zero at the free end if a load $P$ is present).**

General case — bar carries a load $P$ at the bottom *and* its own weight. At the bottom ($x = 0$):

$$A_0 = \dfrac{P}{\sigma} \quad \text{...(xi)}$$

Integrating (x) from $0$ to $x$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{A_{x}}{A_0}\right) = -\dfrac{\gamma x}{\sigma}$$

$$A_{x} = A_0 \, e^{-\gamma x / \sigma} = \dfrac{P}{\sigma} e^{-\gamma x / \sigma} \quad \text{...(xii)}$$

For a **circular cross-section**, $A_x = \pi d_x^{2}/4$:

$$\boxed{\;d_{x}^{2} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi \sigma}\, e^{-\gamma x / \sigma} \quad \text{or} \quad d_{x} = d_{0}\, e^{-\gamma x / (2\sigma)}\;} \quad \text{...(xiii)}$$

where $d_0 = \sqrt{4P/(\pi \sigma)}$ is the diameter at the loaded (bottom) end.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The diameter variation is **exponential**, not linear. A linearly-tapering bar does *not* give uniform strength under self-weight — a common misconception that costs 2–3 marks per question paper.

### 3.3 Derivation — Stress in a Uniform Bar due to its Own Weight

**Given:** A uniform bar of length $L$, area $A$, weight density $\gamma$, suspended from the top.

At a section $x$ from the bottom, the weight hanging below = $\gamma A (L - x)$.

$$\sigma_{x} = \dfrac{\gamma A (L - x)}{A} = \gamma (L - x)$$

Maximum at the top ($x = 0$): $\sigma_{\max} = \gamma L$

Total elongation = $\int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{\sigma_x}{E} dx = \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{\gamma (L - x)}{E} dx = \dfrac{\gamma}{E}\left[Lx - \dfrac{x^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{L}$

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \dfrac{\gamma L^{2}}{2 E} = \dfrac{W L}{2 A E}\;} \quad \text{...(xiv)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Check:** Compare this with a point load $W$ applied at the bottom: $\delta_{\text{point}} = \dfrac{WL}{AE}$. The self-weight case gives *half* that value — because the weight is distributed, not concentrated.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Analytical Workflow for Bars of Varying Section

The following block diagram captures the **decision flow** that a KTU examiner expects a student to demonstrate when solving any problem on this topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read the Problem Statement] --> B{Identify the Bar Type}
    B -- "Tapering - Circular" --> C1[Apply Formula 3 from Cheat Sheet]
    B -- "Stepped Bar" --> C2[Compute Force in Each Section from Equilibrium]
    B -- "Uniform Strength" --> C3[Apply Sectional Equilibrium with Self-Weight]
    C2 --> D2[Sum PL_i over A_i E_i over all sections]
    C1 --> E[Substitute Values with SI Units]
    D2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    E --> F{Stress Check Required?}
    F -- "Yes" --> G1[Compute sigma_x = P / A_x at critical section]
    F -- "No" --> H[Output Final Elongation in mm]
    G1 --> I[Compare with sigma_allowable]
    I --> H
    H --> J[END: Present Answer with Units]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force–Deformation Logic Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Axial Load P] --> S1[Section 1: A1, L1]
    P --> S2[Section 2: A2, L2]
    P --> S3[Section 3: A3, L3]
    S1 --> D1[delta 1 = P L1 slash A1 E]
    S2 --> D2[delta 2 = P L2 slash A2 E]
    S3 --> D3[delta 3 = P L3 slash A3 E]
    D1 --> SUM[Total Elongation: Sum of all deltas]
    D2 --> SUM
    D3 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[delta total in mm]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Tapering Bar Geometric Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    X0[Smaller end: diameter d at x = 0] --> X1[Linear interpolation: d_x = d + D minus d over L times x]
    X1 --> X2[Area: A_x = pi d_x squared over 4]
    X2 --> X3[Element elongation: d delta = 4 P dx over pi E d_x squared]
    X3 --> X4[Integrate from 0 to L]
    X4 --> X5[Substitution u = d + D minus d over L times x]
    X5 --> X6[Final Result: delta = 4 P L over pi E D d]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Distinguish clearly between a "bar of uniform section" and a "bar of uniform strength". Give one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bar of uniform section** has a *constant cross-sectional area* along its length (e.g., a standard bolt shank, a prismatic column). Its stress varies along the length when self-weight is considered.

A **bar of uniform strength** is a *deliberately varying* bar designed so that the *maximum stress in every cross-section* is the same and equal to the allowable stress (e.g., a leaf spring, the taper of a connecting rod in an IC engine). For a bar carrying only its own weight, the diameter variation is exponential, not linear.

The bar of uniform section is easier to manufacture but uses more material. The bar of uniform strength is material-optimal but complex to fabricate — a typical KTU design dilemma.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **A uniformly tapering circular bar of length $L$ has end diameters $D$ and $d$. Write the expression for its elongation under an axial load $P$, and state the unit of each quantity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\delta = \dfrac{4 P L}{\pi E D d}$$

Units (SI): $P$ in **N**, $L$, $D$, $d$ in **m**, $E$ in **N/m²** (or Pa), giving $\delta$ in **m**. In KTU papers, it is conventional to use **mm** and **MPa**, with $E$ in **N/mm²**.

The formula implies the bar behaves like a uniform bar with a *geometric mean diameter* $d_{GM} = \sqrt{Dd}$.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** A stepped bar of circular cross-section consists of three sections, each of length 200 mm, with diameters 40 mm, 30 mm, and 50 mm respectively. The bar is subjected to an axial tensile load of 80 kN. Calculate the total elongation of the bar. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/mm}^{2}$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** If the middle section of the above stepped bar is replaced by a uniformly tapering circular section of the same length, tapering from 30 mm to 50 mm diameter, find the new total elongation and comment on the result.

---

#### Model Solution — Q3(a)

**Given data:**
- $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = 200\ \text{mm}$
- $d_1 = 40\ \text{mm},\ d_2 = 30\ \text{mm},\ d_3 = 50\ \text{mm}$
- $P = 80\ \text{kN} = 80\,000\ \text{N}$
- $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\ \text{N/mm}^{2}$

**Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional areas.**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi (40)^{2}}{4} = 1256.64\ \text{mm}^{2}$$

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (30)^{2}}{4} = 706.86\ \text{mm}^{2}$$

$$A_3 = \dfrac{\pi (50)^{2}}{4} = 1963.50\ \text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Elongation of each section.**

$$\delta_1 = \dfrac{P L_1}{A_1 E} = \dfrac{80\,000 \times 200}{1256.64 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{7}}{2.513 \times 10^{8}} = 0.0637\ \text{mm}$$

$$\delta_2 = \dfrac{80\,000 \times 200}{706.86 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{7}}{1.4137 \times 10^{8}} = 0.1132\ \text{mm}$$

$$\delta_3 = \dfrac{80\,000 \times 200}{1963.50 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{7}}{3.927 \times 10^{8}} = 0.0407\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Total elongation.**

$$\delta_{\text{total}} = 0.0637 + 0.1132 + 0.0407 = 0.2176\ \text{mm} \approx 0.218\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{\text{total}} \approx 0.218\ \text{mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student error is forgetting to *square* the diameter when computing the area. Mark loss here is **2 marks** per occurrence. Also, never mix up units: $E$ in MPa and length in mm, OR $E$ in Pa and length in m — pick one and stick with it.

---

#### Model Solution — Q3(b)

**Given data for new middle section:**
- $L_2 = 200\ \text{mm}$ (unchanged)
- Smaller diameter $d = 30\ \text{mm}$, larger diameter $D = 50\ \text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Elongation of the new tapering section using Formula 3.**

$$\delta_{2,\text{taper}} = \dfrac{4 P L_2}{\pi E D d} = \dfrac{4 \times 80\,000 \times 200}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 50 \times 30}$$

$$\delta_{2,\text{taper}} = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{10}}{\pi \times 3.0 \times 10^{8}} = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{10}}{9.4248 \times 10^{8}} = 67.90\ \text{mm (check units) — recalculation below}$$

**Re-evaluation (correcting the unit chain):**

$$= \dfrac{4 \times 80\,000 \times 200}{\pi \times 200\,000 \times 1500} = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{10}}{9.4248 \times 10^{8}} = 67.90\ \text{mm}$$

> This number is obviously too large for a 200 mm bar. The error is that the numerator and denominator are *dimensionally inconsistent* when the diameter product $D \cdot d$ is in mm².

**Correct unit-aware evaluation:**

Take diameters in **metres**: $D = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $d = 0.03\ \text{m}$, $L = 0.200\ \text{m}$, $E = 2 \times 10^{11}\ \text{Pa}$, $P = 80\,000\ \text{N}$.

$$\delta_{2,\text{taper}} = \dfrac{4 \times 80\,000 \times 0.200}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 0.05 \times 0.03} = \dfrac{64\,000}{\pi \times 3.0 \times 10^{8}} = \dfrac{64\,000}{9.4248 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$= 6.79 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m} = 0.0679\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Add unchanged sections.**

$$\delta_{1} = 0.0637\ \text{mm} \quad \text{(unchanged)}$$

$$\delta_{3} = 0.0407\ \text{mm} \quad \text{(unchanged)}$$

$$\delta_{\text{new total}} = 0.0637 + 0.0679 + 0.0407 = 0.1723\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{\text{new total}} \approx 0.172\ \text{mm}}$$

**Comment:** Replacing the uniform middle section with a tapering one **reduces** the total elongation (from 0.218 mm to 0.172 mm), because the tapering section is *on average* larger in cross-section than the original 30 mm uniform section. The volume (and hence weight) of the bar, however, is greater.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive, step-by-step, the expression for the elongation of a uniformly tapering circular bar of length $L$ having end diameters $D$ and $d$ when subjected to an axial tensile load $P$. Take Young's modulus as $E$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A vertical steel rod of length 4 m is rigidly fixed at the top and carries a gradually applied tensile load of 30 kN at the bottom. The rod tapers uniformly from 60 mm diameter at the top to 30 mm diameter at the bottom. Calculate: (i) the elongation of the rod, and (ii) the maximum stress in the rod. Take $E = 200\ \text{GPa}$ and neglect the self-weight.

---

#### Model Solution — Q4(a)

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.** Consider a disc of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the smaller end (diameter $d$).

Diameter at the disc:

$$d_x = d + \left(\dfrac{D - d}{L}\right) x$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area.**

$$A_x = \dfrac{\pi d_x^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Element elongation by Hooke's law.**

$$d\delta = \dfrac{P \, dx}{A_x E} = \dfrac{4P \, dx}{\pi E \, d_x^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the full length $0$ to $L$.**

$$\delta = \dfrac{4P}{\pi E} \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{dx}{\left[d + \left(\dfrac{D - d}{L}\right) x\right]^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute** $u = d + \dfrac{(D-d)}{L} x \implies du = \dfrac{(D-d)}{L} dx$.

Limits: $x = 0 \Rightarrow u = d$; $x = L \Rightarrow u = D$.

$$\delta = \dfrac{4P}{\pi E} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D - d} \int_{d}^{D} \dfrac{du}{u^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate.**

$$\delta = \dfrac{4P L}{\pi E (D - d)} \left[-\dfrac{1}{u}\right]_{d}^{D} = \dfrac{4P L}{\pi E (D - d)} \cdot \dfrac{D - d}{D d}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\;\delta = \dfrac{4 P L}{\pi E D d}\;}$$

The bar behaves as a *uniform bar* of equivalent diameter $d_{\text{eq}} = \sqrt{D d}$ (the geometric mean).

---

#### Model Solution — Q4(b)

**Given data:**
- $L = 4\ \text{m}$
- $D = 60\ \text{mm} = 0.06\ \text{m}$ (top)
- $d = 30\ \text{mm} = 0.03\ \text{m}$ (bottom)
- $P = 30\ \text{kN} = 30\,000\ \text{N}$
- $E = 200\ \text{GPa} = 2 \times 10^{11}\ \text{N/m}^{2}$

**(i) Elongation:**

$$\delta = \dfrac{4 P L}{\pi E D d} = \dfrac{4 \times 30\,000 \times 4}{\pi \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 0.06 \times 0.03}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{4.8 \times 10^{5}}{\pi \times 3.6 \times 10^{8}} = \dfrac{4.8 \times 10^{5}}{1.1310 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta = 4.244 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m} = 0.4244\ \text{mm}}$$

**(ii) Maximum stress:**

Stress is maximum at the smallest cross-section (bottom, $d = 30\ \text{mm}$):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{4P}{\pi d^{2}} = \dfrac{4 \times 30\,000}{\pi \times (0.03)^{2}} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{5}}{\pi \times 9 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{5}}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.244 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa} = 42.44\ \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 42.44\ \text{MPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Q4(b):** Many students calculate the stress at the *larger* end and answer incorrectly. The maximum stress is always at the **smaller** cross-section because $\sigma = P/A$ and $A$ is minimum there. This is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** if missed.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake for Q4(a):** A frequent error is to apply the *average diameter* (arithmetic mean) in the elongation formula, giving $\delta = \dfrac{4PL}{\pi E \left(\frac{D+d}{2}\right)^{2}}$. The **correct** diameter is the **geometric mean** $\sqrt{Dd}$, *not* the arithmetic mean. Only the geometric mean makes the integration consistent — a proof of this is often asked as a "show that" follow-up in 14-mark questions.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section for last-minute KTU revision.**

- A **bar of uniform section** has constant $A$; a **bar of uniform strength** has constant $\sigma_{\max}$.
- For a **linearly tapering circular bar**, the diameter at section $x$ is $d_x = d + \left(\frac{D-d}{L}\right) x$.
- The **master formula** for elongation of a uniformly tapering circular bar is $\delta = \dfrac{4PL}{\pi E D d}$, equivalent to a uniform bar of diameter $\sqrt{Dd}$.
- For a **stepped bar**, $\delta = \sum \dfrac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$ — apply equilibrium on each segment to find $P_i$.
- A bar's elongation under its **own weight** is $\delta = \dfrac{W L}{2 A E} = \dfrac{\gamma L^{2}}{2 E}$, exactly **half** the value for an equivalent point load.
- A bar of **uniform strength** under self-weight has an *exponential* taper: $d_x = d_0 \, e^{-\gamma x/(2\sigma)}$.
- The **maximum stress** in a tapering bar under axial load is at the **smallest cross-section**.
- In a **uniform bar under self-weight**, $\sigma_{\max} = \gamma L$ at the support, $\sigma_{\min} = 0$ at the free end.
- For **bars in fluids** (buoyancy), use $\gamma_{\text{eff}} = \gamma_{\text{material}} - \gamma_{\text{fluid}}$.
- **Units consistency** is non-negotiable in KTU papers: convert *all* lengths to metres (or all to mm) before substitution.
- The **geometric mean** diameter controls tapering-bar stiffness; the **arithmetic mean** diameter is *not* correct.
- **Stepped-bar superposition** assumes small deformations and linear elastic behaviour — explicitly mention Hooke's law in your answer to earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite Bars and Strain Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Composite Bars and Strain Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Composite Bars — Formal Definition

A **composite bar** (also called a **compound bar** or **composite member**) is a structural member made by rigidly joining **two or more different engineering materials** of the same length, such that the combination behaves as a single unit when subjected to an external axial load. The cross-section of the bar is divided into distinct regions, each composed of a homogeneous material possessing its own unique **Young's Modulus (E)** and allowable stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A composite bar consists of two or more different materials bonded together so as to undergo a common longitudinal strain under an axial tensile or compressive load. The load is shared between the constituent materials in proportion to their axial rigidities (AE).*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel-Reinforced Concrete" Model

Imagine a plain **chalk stick** (weak in tension) wrapped tightly with a thin layer of **fibre-glass tape** (strong in tension). When you try to pull the composite apart, the tape prevents the chalk from cracking. Both materials stretch by the **same amount** (you cannot stretch one without the other), yet the stronger tape carries the **major share** of the load.

This is exactly how **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)** works — steel rods and concrete deform together as one body, and engineers must know how the total load splits between them. This is the heart of composite bar analysis.

## 1.2 Strain Energy — Formal Definition

**Strain Energy (U)** is the internal mechanical energy **stored within a deformed body** when it is loaded within its **elastic limit**. This energy is a function of the work done by the external load in producing elastic deformation, and is fully **recoverable** upon removal of the load.

Mathematically, it is the area under the load–deflection (P–δ) curve up to the elastic limit, equal to the work done by the gradually applied load:

$$U = \int_0^{\delta} P \, d\delta = \frac{1}{2} P \delta$$

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretched Bow" Model

Consider an archer pulling back a bowstring. The archer does work against the elastic resistance of the bow, and this work is **stored** as strain energy in the bent wood. Upon release, this stored energy is rapidly converted into the **kinetic energy** of the arrow. The bow material is not permanently damaged — all the energy returns. This is the essence of **elastic strain energy**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance of Strain Energy:**
> 1. Design of springs, shock absorbers, and buffers.
> 2. Resilience of materials under sudden/impact loads.
> 3. Foundation of **Castigliano's Theorem** and **Unit Load Method** for deflection analysis.
> 4. Capacity of a structure to absorb energy during earthquakes (Seismic Design).

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Strain Energy Density (u):** Energy stored per unit volume — units of **N·m/m³ = J/m³ (Pa)**.
- **Modulus of Resilience (u_r):** Maximum strain energy density up to the elastic limit.
- **Proof Resilience (U_p):** Total strain energy stored up to the elastic limit.
- **Volumetric Strain (ε_v):** Total volume of material undergoing strain energy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Composite Bars — Underlying Theory

### 2.1.1 Two Materials in Parallel (Same Length, Different Areas)

When two materials (Material 1 and Material 2) of areas $A_1$ and $A_2$, Young's moduli $E_1$ and $E_2$, and the **same length $L$** are bonded and loaded axially by force $P$:

**Step 1 — Compatibility Condition (Kinematic):**
The strains in both materials must be equal because they are rigidly bonded:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} = \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2}$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition (Static):**
The total external load is shared between the two materials:
$$P = P_1 + P_2 = \sigma_1 A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2$$

**Step 3 — Solving simultaneously**, we obtain the **load-sharing ratio**:
$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_2 E_2}$$

The individual material stresses are:
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P \cdot E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot A_1, \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{P \cdot E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2} \cdot A_2$$

The common elongation is:
$$\delta = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

### 2.1.2 Composite Bar with Temperature Change

If the bar is subjected to a **temperature rise $\Delta T$** in addition to an axial load $P$, the **free thermal expansion** would be $\delta_T = L \alpha \Delta T$, where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of thermal expansion. Because the bar is restrained, the actual deformation is:
$$\delta_{actual} = \delta_P - \delta_T \quad \text{or} \quad \delta_P + \delta_T \text{ (depending on direction)}$$

The general strain compatibility becomes:
$$\varepsilon_1 - \alpha_1 \Delta T = \varepsilon_2 - \alpha_2 \Delta T$$

### 2.1.3 Three or More Materials in Parallel

For $n$ materials in parallel under common strain:
$$\delta = \frac{P L}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i E_i} = \frac{P L}{A_{eq} E_{eq}}$$

where the **equivalent axial rigidity** is:
$$A_{eq} E_{eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i E_i$$

## 2.2 Strain Energy — Underlying Theory

### 2.2.1 Strain Energy in Axial Loading

For a prismatic bar of length $L$, area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ subjected to a gradually applied axial load $P$:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} P \delta = \frac{P^2 L}{2 A E} = \frac{\sigma^2 A L}{2 E} = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2 E}$$

where $V = AL$ is the volume of the bar.

### 2.2.2 Strain Energy Density

The energy stored per unit volume is:
$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2 E} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2$$

### 2.2.3 Strain Energy Due to Shear, Bending, and Torsion

| Loading Type | Strain Energy Expression |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Pure Shear** | $U = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G} \cdot V = \dfrac{Q^2 L}{2 A G}$ |
| **Bending Moment** | $U = \dfrac{M^2 L}{2 E I}$ |
| **Torsion** | $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$ |
| **Combined** | $U = U_{axial} + U_{shear} + U_{bending} + U_{torsion}$ |

### 2.2.4 Sudden and Impact Loading

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Strain Energy under Different Loading Rates:**
> - **Gradual (Static) Loading:** $U_{static} = \dfrac{P^2 L}{2 A E}$ and $\delta_{static} = \dfrac{P L}{A E}$
> - **Sudden Loading (load applied instantaneously with no impact velocity):** Strain energy = Work done by falling load
>   $$U_{sudden} = 2 \cdot P \cdot \delta_{static} \Rightarrow \delta_{sudden} = 2 \, \delta_{static}, \quad \sigma_{sudden} = 2 \, \sigma_{static}$$
> - **Impact Loading from Height h:** Strain energy = Loss of potential energy
>   $$U_{impact} = W \left( h + \delta_{static} \right) \Rightarrow \delta_{impact} = \delta_{static} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_{static}}} \right]$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Reference Table for Composite Bars and Strain Energy**

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Units (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Composite Bar — Equal Strain | $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 \Rightarrow \sigma_1 / E_1 = \sigma_2 / E_2$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Load Distribution | $P_1 = P \cdot \dfrac{A_1 E_1}{\sum A_i E_i}$ | N (Newton) |
| 3 | Equivalent Rigidity | $A_{eq} E_{eq} = \sum A_i E_i$ | N |
| 4 | Elongation of Composite Bar | $\delta = \dfrac{P L}{\sum A_i E_i}$ | m |
| 5 | Stress in $i^{th}$ Material | $\sigma_i = \dfrac{P E_i}{\sum A_i E_j}$ | Pa (N/m²) |
| 6 | Thermal Composite | $\varepsilon_i - \alpha_i \Delta T = \text{common strain}$ | — |
| 7 | Strain Energy (Axial) | $U = \dfrac{P^2 L}{2 A E} = \dfrac{\sigma^2 V}{2 E}$ | J (Joule) |
| 8 | Strain Energy Density | $u = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2 E}$ | J/m³ |
| 9 | Modulus of Resilience | $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2 E}$ | J/m³ |
| 10 | Proof Resilience | $U_p = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2} V}{2 E}$ | J |
| 11 | Strain Energy (Bending) | $U = \dfrac{M^2 L}{2 E I}$ | J |
| 12 | Strain Energy (Torsion) | $U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J}$ | J |
| 13 | Sudden Load Factor | $\delta_{sudden} = 2 \, \delta_{static}$ | m |
| 14 | Impact Load Factor | $\delta_{impact} = \delta_{static} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{static}}} \right]$ | m |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Reinforced concrete (steel + concrete), pre-stressed concrete tendons, composite steel-concrete beams in high-rise buildings.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Bimetallic strips in thermostats, layered drive shafts, fiber-reinforced composites in aerospace.
- **Automotive:** Leaf springs with multiple leaves of varying length, brake pads, crash-absorbing crumple zones (strain energy design).
- **Aerospace:** Carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP) panels — designed using composite bar analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stress in a Two-Material Composite Bar (Parallel Configuration)

### Given:
- Material 1: Area $= A_1$, Young's Modulus $= E_1$
- Material 2: Area $= A_2$, Young's Modulus $= E_2$
- Common length: $L$
- External axial load: $P$ (Tensile)

### Step 1 — Apply Compatibility
Both materials deform together, so their elongations are identical:
$$\delta_1 = \delta_2$$
$$\frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2}$$
$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{A_1 E_1}{A_2 E_2} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Static Equilibrium
The total load equals the sum of individual loads:
$$P = P_1 + P_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 3 — Solve Simultaneously
From (i), express $P_2$ in terms of $P_1$:
$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1}$$

Substitute into (ii):
$$P = P_1 + P_1 \cdot \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1}$$
$$P = P_1 \left[ \frac{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1} \right]$$

Solving for $P_1$:
$$P_1 = \frac{P \cdot A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

By symmetry:
$$P_2 = \frac{P \cdot A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Stresses
$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P_1}{A_1} = \frac{P E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$
$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P_2}{A_2} = \frac{P E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Common Elongation
$$\delta = \frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P L}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Strain Energy in Axial Loading (Fundamental Derivation)

### Step 1 — Consider Gradually Applied Load
A load $P$ is increased gradually from $0$ to $P_{max}$. At any intermediate value $P_x$, the elongation is:
$$\delta_x = \frac{P_x L}{A E}$$

### Step 2 — Infinitesimal Work Done
The infinitesimal work done for an additional elongation $d\delta_x$ is:
$$dU = P_x \, d\delta_x = P_x \cdot \frac{L}{A E} dP_x$$

### Step 3 — Integrate to Obtain Total Strain Energy
$$U = \int_0^{P} P_x \cdot \frac{L}{A E} \, dP_x$$
$$U = \frac{L}{A E} \int_0^{P} P_x \, dP_x$$
$$U = \frac{L}{A E} \cdot \left[ \frac{P_x^{\,2}}{2} \right]_0^{P}$$
$$\boxed{U = \frac{P^2 L}{2 A E}}$$

### Step 4 — Alternative Forms Using Stress
Since $\sigma = P / A$ and $V = A L$:
$$U = \frac{\sigma^2 A^2 L}{2 A E} = \frac{\sigma^2 (A L)}{2 E} = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2 E}$$

Since $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$:
$$U = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon V = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2 V$$

### Step 5 — Strain Energy Density (u)
Energy per unit volume:
$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2 E} = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon = \frac{1}{2} E \varepsilon^2$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sudden Loading — Proof of Stress Doubling

### Setup:
A weight $W$ is suddenly placed on a bar (no impact velocity, $h = 0$). The bar deflects by $\delta$ and reaches equilibrium.

### Step 1 — Energy Conservation
Work done by weight = Strain energy stored in bar:
$$W \cdot \delta = U = \frac{k \delta^2}{2}$$
where $k = A E / L$ is the axial stiffness.

### Step 2 — Solve for $\delta$
$$W = \frac{k \delta}{2} \Rightarrow \delta = \frac{2 W}{k}$$

But the static deflection (gradual application) is:
$$\delta_{static} = \frac{W}{k}$$

### Step 3 — Compare
$$\delta_{sudden} = 2 \cdot \delta_{static}$$

Since stress is proportional to strain (and hence deflection):
$$\boxed{\sigma_{sudden} = 2 \cdot \sigma_{static}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Impact Loading from a Height h

### Step 1 — Loss of Potential Energy
A weight $W$ falls through height $h$ and then deflects the bar by additional $\delta$.

$$\text{PE Lost} = W (h + \delta)$$

### Step 2 — Equate to Strain Energy
$$W (h + \delta) = \frac{1}{2} k \delta^2$$
$$2 W (h + \delta) = k \delta^2$$
$$k \delta^2 - 2 W \delta - 2 W h = 0$$

### Step 3 — Apply Quadratic Formula
This is a quadratic in $\delta$:
$$\delta = \frac{2 W \pm \sqrt{4 W^2 + 8 k W h}}{2 k}$$
$$\delta = \frac{W}{k} \pm \frac{\sqrt{W^2 + 2 k W h}}{k}$$
$$\delta = \frac{W}{k} \left[ 1 \pm \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 k h}{W}} \right]$$

Taking the **positive root** (since deflection is positive):
$$\delta = \delta_{static} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 h}{\delta_{static}}} \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Cases for Verification:**
> - If $h = 0$ (sudden, no drop): $\delta = \delta_{static}[1 + 1] = 2 \delta_{static}$ ✓
> - If $h \to \infty$: $\delta \approx \delta_{static} \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{\delta_{static}}} = \sqrt{2 h \delta_{static}}$ ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Composite Bar — Force Flow & Strain Compatibility Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the analytical flow for solving a two-material composite bar problem, mapping the interaction between the physical system, compatibility, equilibrium, and resulting material stresses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Axial Load P] --> B[Applied on Composite Bar]
    B --> C{Material 1 Region: A1, E1}
    B --> D{Material 2 Region: A2, E2}
    C --> E[Internal Force P1 = sigma1 times A1]
    D --> F[Internal Force P2 = sigma2 times A2]
    G[COMPATIBILITY: delta1 equals delta2] --> H[Common Elongation delta]
    I[EQUILIBRIUM: P equals P1 plus P2] --> J[Total Load Balance]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    E --> I
    F --> I
    H --> K[Compute Stress sigma1 and sigma2]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Output: sigma1, sigma2, delta]
    L --> M{Design Check: sigma less than allowable}
    M -->|Yes| N[SAFE DESIGN]
    M -->|No| O[REDESIGN WITH HIGHER AREA]
```

## 4.2 Strain Energy — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following schematic depicts the energy transformations in a loaded elastic bar, showing how external work is partitioned into recoverable strain energy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT[External Work Done] --> EP[External Potential Energy]
    EP --> SU[Strain Energy Stored in Bar]
    SU --> SD[Strain Energy Density u]
    SD --> MR[Modulus of Resilience]
    SU --> PR[Proof Resilience]
    SU --> RP[Resilience of Bar]
    LOAD[Loading Type] --> SUD[Sudden Load]
    LOAD --> IMP[Impact Load from Height h]
    LOAD --> GRD[Gradual Static Load]
    SUD --> FAC1[Deflection Doubled: delta = 2 times delta_static]
    IMP --> FAC2[Deflection: delta = delta_static times bracket 1 plus sqrt 1 plus 2h by delta_static]
    GRD --> FAC3[Deflection: delta = PL by AE]
    SU --> CAS[Castigliano Theorem Application]
    SU --> ULM[Unit Load Method for Deflection]
```

## 4.3 Composite Bar with Three Materials — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Load P] --> S[Common Strain Condition]
    S --> T[Sum of Ai times Ei for all i]
    T --> EQ[Equivalent Axial Rigidity]
    EQ --> DEF[Common Elongation delta = PL by sum of Ai Ei]
    P --> PI[Load in material i: Pi = P times Ai Ei by sum]
    PI --> SIG[Stress in material i: sigma i = P times Ei by sum]
    SIG --> CK[Strength Check per Material]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define strain energy. Derive the expression for strain energy in a bar subjected to an axial tensile load.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Strain energy is the internal energy stored in a body when it is deformed within its elastic limit. It is equal to the work done by the external load in producing the deformation and is fully recoverable upon unloading.

**Derivation:** Consider a bar of length $L$, area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ subjected to a gradually increasing axial load $P$. At any intermediate value $P_x$, the elongation is $\delta_x = P_x L / (A E)$. The infinitesimal work done is:

$$dU = P_x \, d\delta_x = \frac{P_x L}{A E} dP_x$$

Integrating from $0$ to $P$:

$$U = \int_0^{P} \frac{P_x L}{A E} \, dP_x = \frac{P^2 L}{2 A E} = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2 E}$$

**Common equivalents:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2} P \delta = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon V$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the assumptions made in the analysis of a composite bar.**

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental assumptions in composite bar analysis are:

1. **Perfect Bonding:** The two (or more) materials are rigidly bonded so that no slip occurs at the interface.
2. **Equal Longitudinal Strain (Compatibility):** Both materials undergo identical elongation, i.e., $\delta_1 = \delta_2 = \delta_3 = \ldots$
3. **Linear Elastic Behavior:** Each material obeys Hooke's Law within its elastic limit ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$).
4. **Plane Sections Remain Plane:** Cross-sections perpendicular to the axis remain plane after deformation (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
5. **Uniform Stress Distribution:** Stress in each material is uniformly distributed over its respective area.
6. **Negligible Lateral Effects:** Poisson's ratio effects and lateral contraction are neglected for the primary analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]`

A composite bar consists of a steel rod of **20 mm diameter** enclosed concentrically inside a copper tube of **30 mm outer diameter** and **25 mm inner diameter**. The composite bar is **500 mm long** and is subjected to an axial tensile load of **50 kN**. The bar is also subjected to a **temperature rise of 40 °C**.

**Given:**
- $E_{steel} = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6} / °C$
- $E_{copper} = 100 \text{ GPa}$, $\alpha_{copper} = 18 \times 10^{-6} / °C$

**Find:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** The stresses developed in steel and copper.
- **(b) [7 Marks]** The total elongation of the composite bar.

---

### **Solution A:**

#### **Part (a) — Calculation of Stresses**

**Step 1 — Compute Cross-Sectional Areas**
- Area of steel (solid rod, $d_s = 20$ mm):
$$A_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$
- Area of copper tube ($D_o = 30$ mm, $D_i = 25$ mm):
$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4}(30^2 - 25^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(900 - 625) = 216.04 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Compatibility with Thermal Effect**

The total strain in any material = Mechanical strain + Thermal strain. Since the bar is rigidly bonded, the total strain is the same:
$$\varepsilon_{mech,s} + \alpha_s \Delta T = \varepsilon_{mech,c} + \alpha_c \Delta T$$
$$\frac{\sigma_s}{E_s} + \alpha_s \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_c} + \alpha_c \Delta T$$

Substituting values (in compatible units: stress in N/mm² = MPa):
$$\frac{\sigma_s}{200 \times 10^3} + 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = \frac{\sigma_c}{100 \times 10^3} + 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 40$$
$$\frac{\sigma_s}{200000} + 0.00048 = \frac{\sigma_c}{100000} + 0.00072$$
$$\frac{\sigma_s}{200000} - \frac{\sigma_c}{100000} = 0.00072 - 0.00048 = 0.00024$$
$$\frac{\sigma_s - 2 \sigma_c}{200000} = 0.00024$$
$$\sigma_s - 2 \sigma_c = 48 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Static Equilibrium**
$$P = \sigma_s A_s + \sigma_c A_c$$
$$50 \times 10^3 = \sigma_s (314.16) + \sigma_c (216.04)$$
$$\sigma_s (314.16) + \sigma_c (216.04) = 50000 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Linear System**

From (i): $\sigma_s = 2 \sigma_c + 48$. Substituting into (ii):
$$(2 \sigma_c + 48)(314.16) + \sigma_c (216.04) = 50000$$
$$628.32 \sigma_c + 15079.68 + 216.04 \sigma_c = 50000$$
$$844.36 \sigma_c = 34920.32$$
$$\sigma_c = 41.36 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}$$
$$\sigma_s = 2(41.36) + 48 = 130.72 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}$$

#### **Part (b) — Calculation of Total Elongation**

The common total strain is:
$$\varepsilon_{total} = \frac{\sigma_s}{E_s} + \alpha_s \Delta T = \frac{130.72}{200000} + 0.00048$$
$$\varepsilon_{total} = 0.0006536 + 0.00048 = 0.0011336$$

**Verification using copper:**
$$\varepsilon_{total} = \frac{41.36}{100000} + 0.00072 = 0.0004136 + 0.00072 = 0.0011336 \checkmark$$

**Total elongation:**
$$\delta = \varepsilon_{total} \times L = 0.0011336 \times 500 = 0.5668 \text{ mm}$$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

A mild steel bar of **length 2 m, cross-section 30 mm × 30 mm** is subjected to a gradually applied axial tensile load. The stress in the bar is **150 MPa**. The bar is then subjected to a sudden load of equal magnitude (no impact) and also an impact load falling from a height of **100 mm**.

**Given:** $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, allowable elongation = **1.5 mm**.

**Find:**
- **(a) [7 Marks]** The strain energy stored in the bar under gradual loading. Compare it with the strain energy under sudden loading.
