- **(b) [7 Marks]** The maximum stress and elongation under the impact loading condition.

---

### **Solution B:**

#### **Part (a) — Gradual and Sudden Loading**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area and Volume**
$$A = 30 \times 30 = 900 \text{ mm}^2 = 900 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$
$$V = A L = 900 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 = 1.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Gradual Loading: Strain Energy**
$$U_{gradual} = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2 E} = \frac{(150 \times 10^6)^2 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 200 \times 10^9}$$
$$U_{gradual} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{16} \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{11}}$$
$$U_{gradual} = \frac{4.05 \times 10^{13}}{4 \times 10^{11}} = 101.25 \text{ J}$$

**Step 3 — Static Elongation**
$$\delta_{static} = \frac{\sigma L}{E} = \frac{150 \times 10^6 \times 2}{200 \times 10^9} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Sudden Loading Analysis**
For sudden loading, $\delta_{sudden} = 2 \times \delta_{static} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3.0$ mm.
$$\sigma_{sudden} = 2 \times \sigma_{static} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

Strain energy stored:
$$U_{sudden} = \frac{\sigma_{sudden}^{\,2} V}{2 E} = \frac{(300 \times 10^6)^2 \times 1.8 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{11}}$$
$$U_{sudden} = 4 \times U_{gradual} = 4 \times 101.25 = 405 \text{ J}$$

**Comparison:** $U_{sudden} = 4 \cdot U_{gradual}$; Sudden loading quadruples the strain energy.

#### **Part (b) — Impact Loading from h = 100 mm**

**Step 1 — Maximum Stress under Impact**
$$\sigma_{impact} = \sigma_{static} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 h}{\delta_{static}}} \right]$$
$$\sigma_{impact} = 150 \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \times 100}{1.5}} \right]$$
$$\sigma_{impact} = 150 \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + 133.33} \right]$$
$$\sigma_{impact} = 150 \left[ 1 + \sqrt{134.33} \right] = 150 \left[ 1 + 11.59 \right] = 150 \times 12.59$$
$$\boxed{\sigma_{impact} \approx 1888.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Elongation under Impact**
$$\delta_{impact} = \delta_{static} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 h}{\delta_{static}}} \right]$$
$$\delta_{impact} = 1.5 \times 12.59 = 18.89 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units consistently** — MPa vs GPa vs N/m². Always use SI base units ($N$, $m$, $Pa$) in derivations.
> 2. **Sign convention error in thermal composite bars** — When temperature rises and load is tensile, thermal expansion *reduces* effective tension. A common mistake is to add $\alpha \Delta T$ directly without proper sign analysis.
> 3. **Sudden vs Impact confusion** — Sudden loading: load placed without drop ($h = 0$, factor = 2). Impact loading: weight falls from height $h$ (factor depends on $h/\delta_{static}$).
> 4. **Strain energy factor of 4 for sudden load** — Students often wrongly write $U_{sudden} = 2 \cdot U_{gradual}$ (forgetting the squared relation $U \propto \sigma^2$).
> 5. **Allowable deflection check** — In Part (b) of Question B, the impact elongation (18.89 mm) far exceeds the allowable 1.5 mm, indicating a real engineering design failure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Composite Bars and Strain Energy**

- ☐ **Composite Bar — Equal Strain Principle:** The two materials undergo the *same* longitudinal strain due to rigid bonding.
- ☐ **Load Sharing:** Load is shared in proportion to *axial rigidity* ($A E$), not area alone.
- ☐ **Key Formula:** $\delta = \dfrac{P L}{\sum A_i E_i}$ — this is the *Master Equation* for any composite bar in parallel.
- ☐ **Thermal Composite:** Always include $\alpha \Delta T$ in compatibility equation. The bar's *total* strain = mechanical strain + thermal strain.
- ☐ **Stress in $i^{th}$ Material:** $\sigma_i = \dfrac{P E_i}{\sum A_j E_j}$ — independent of $A_i$ (a powerful simplification).
- ☐ **Strain Energy Master Formula (Axial):** $U = \dfrac{P^2 L}{2 A E} = \dfrac{\sigma^2 V}{2 E} = \dfrac{1}{2} P \delta = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon V$.
- ☐ **Strain Energy Density:** $u = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2 E}$ — workhorse for impact and resilience problems.
- ☐ **Modulus of Resilience:** $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2 E}$ — area under the linear elastic portion of the stress–strain curve.
- ☐ **Proof Resilience:** $U_p = u_r \cdot V$ — total elastic strain energy capacity.
- ☐ **Sudden Loading:** $\delta = 2 \delta_{static}$, $\sigma = 2 \sigma_{static}$, $U = 4 U_{static}$.
- ☐ **Impact Loading from height h:** Use the full quadratic formula $\delta = \delta_{static} \left[ 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_{static}}} \right]$.
- ☐ **Units Discipline:** Use SI base units throughout: Force in N, Length in m, Stress in Pa = N/m², Energy in J.
- ☐ **Cross-verification trick:** Always recompute the common strain using *both* materials to verify the answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resilience - Gradual, Sudden, Impact and Shock Loadings and Its Simple Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resilience - Gradual, Sudden, Impact and Shock Loadings and Its Simple Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Resilience

> [!NOTE]
> **Strain Energy (U):** The energy stored in a body within its elastic limit when it is deformed by external forces. It is numerically equal to the work done by the external forces in deforming the body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Resilience:** The capacity of a material to absorb energy when it is loaded elastically and to release this energy when unloaded. Mathematically, **Resilience = Strain Energy stored within the elastic limit.**

The resilience up to the **elastic limit** is known as the **Proof Resilience**, and the maximum value of the strain energy that can be stored in a material up to its ultimate failure is called the **Modulus of Resilience** (strain energy per unit volume).

$$U = \int_{0}^{\delta} P \, d\delta \quad \text{or} \quad U = \int_{0}^{\sigma_y} \sigma \, d\varepsilon \times V$$

## 1.2 Real-World Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Trampoline & The Ball:**
> Imagine a **trampoline** as a structural member (like a steel bar or a spring). When a **bowling ball** (representing the load) is **slowly placed** on it, the trampoline sags gently and stores a small amount of energy — this is **Gradual Loading**.
> If you **drop the ball from a small height** (a few millimetres), the trampoline sags twice as much and the springs experience double the stress — this is **Sudden Loading**.
> If you **drop the ball from a tall building**, the impact is devastating because the energy of the fall converts into a massive shock through the trampoline — this is **Impact Loading**.
> If a **hammer hits the trampoline once and bounces off** (energy is delivered in a tiny fraction of a second), the trampoline momentarily stores and releases energy violently — this is **Shock Loading**.

In all four cases, the *resilience* (energy storage capacity) of the trampoline's springs determines whether the system survives the event or fails.

## 1.3 Classification of Loadings Based on Rate of Application

| S.No. | Type of Loading | Loading Rate | Energy Input Source |
|:----:|:----------------|:-------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | **Gradual (Static)** | Infinitely slow | Slowly applied external work |
| 2 | **Sudden** | Instantaneous, no initial velocity | Full load applied at once |
| 3 | **Impact** | High velocity from a height | Gravitational potential energy + elastic PE |
| 4 | **Shock** | Very brief impulse | Sudden release of stored energy |

## 1.4 Physical Significance in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:**
> - **Automotive:** Designing leaf springs, coil springs, and shock absorbers that must withstand impact and shock loading without permanent deformation.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Bridges must resist sudden loading from moving trains/vehicles and impact from seismic events.
> - **Mechanical Design:** Cranes, hoists, and punch presses experience sudden and impact loading; selecting the right material with adequate resilience is critical.
> - **Aerospace & Defence:** Aircraft landing gear and armour plating are designed considering impact and shock resilience.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gradual (Static) Loading

When a load **P** is applied to a bar of length **L**, area **A**, and Young's modulus **E** so gradually that it increases from **0 to P** at the end:

- The load-extension relation is linear (Hooke's Law): $\delta = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$
- The work done by the gradually applied load equals the area under the load-deflection curve (a triangle).

$$U_{\text{gradual}} = \frac{1}{2} P \cdot \delta_s = \frac{P^{2} L}{2AE}$$

where $\delta_s = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ is the **static deflection**.

**In terms of stress and volume:**

$$U_{\text{gradual}} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \times V \quad \text{where } \sigma = \frac{P}{A} \text{ and } V = A \cdot L$$

**Modulus of Resilience (u):** Strain energy per unit volume.

$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

## 2.2 Sudden Loading

When a load **P** is applied **suddenly** (full magnitude, no initial velocity) at the free end of a bar, the bar instantaneously elongates by $\delta$. Since the load is constant at **P** during elongation, the work done is:

$$W = P \cdot \delta$$

This work is entirely converted into strain energy:

$$U_{\text{sudden}} = P \cdot \delta$$

But the strain energy in terms of deformation is $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta^{2}$. Equating:

$$P \cdot \delta = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta^{2} \implies \delta = \frac{2PL}{AE} = 2\delta_s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result — Sudden Loading Multiplier:**
> - Maximum deformation: $\delta_{\max} = 2 \delta_s$
> - Maximum stress: $\sigma_{\max} = 2 \sigma_s$
> - Strain energy: $U_{\text{sudden}} = 2 U_{\text{gradual}} = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{AE}$

## 2.3 Impact Loading

A weight **P** falls from a height **h** onto a structure. Let the maximum deformation be $\delta$. Applying conservation of energy:

$$\text{Strain Energy} = \text{Work done by falling weight}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta^{2} = P(h + \delta)$$

Recognising that the static deflection $\delta_s = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$, so $P = \dfrac{AE \cdot \delta_s}{L}$. Substituting and simplifying:

$$\delta^{2} - 2\delta_s \cdot \delta - 2h\delta_s = 0$$

Solving the quadratic:

$$\delta = \delta_s \left( 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_s}} \right)$$

**Impact Factor (IF):**

$$\boxed{\text{IF} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_s}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Cases of Impact Factor:**
> - If $h = 0$ (load placed suddenly, no drop): $\text{IF} = 1 + 1 = 2$ (matches sudden loading)
> - If $h \gg \delta_s$ (load dropped from large height): $\text{IF} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{\delta_s}}$
> - Maximum stress under impact: $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_s \cdot \text{IF}$

## 2.4 Shock Loading

Shock loading is essentially a **suddenly applied load of very short duration** (an impulse). The energy is delivered so quickly that the system responds with a sudden deformation followed by oscillations. The instantaneous maximum stress and deformation follow the same principles as sudden loading:

$$\sigma_{\max} = 2\sigma_s, \quad \delta_{\max} = 2\delta_s$$

The distinction lies in the **rate of energy release** — a shock delivers a finite amount of energy in negligible time, causing oscillatory stress waves that propagate through the material.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — 80% of numerical problems are solved from these formulas.**

| S.No. | Loading Type | Max Stress $\sigma_{\max}$ | Max Deformation $\delta_{\max}$ | Strain Energy $U$ | Impact Factor |
|:-----:|:-------------|:--------------------------:|:-------------------------------:|:-----------------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | **Gradual** | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $\delta_s = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | $\dfrac{P^{2} L}{2AE}$ | $1$ |
| 2 | **Sudden** | $2\sigma_s$ | $2\delta_s$ | $\dfrac{P^{2} L}{AE}$ | $2$ |
| 3 | **Impact** (height h) | $\sigma_s \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_s}}\right)$ | $\delta_s \left(1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_s}}\right)$ | $P(h + \delta_{\max})$ | $1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_s}}$ |
| 4 | **Shock** | $2\sigma_s$ | $2\delta_s$ | $\dfrac{P^{2} L}{AE}$ | $2$ |
| 5 | **Modulus of Resilience** | — | — | $u = \dfrac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E}$ | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in practice:**
> 1. **Spring design** — A suddenly applied load on a spring causes twice the stress; springs must be designed for $2 \times$ the static stress to avoid yielding.
> 2. **Crane hooks and chains** — When a chain is used to lift a load, the load is essentially suddenly applied. Designers use a safety factor of at least 2.
> 3. **Punch presses** — The impact energy must be absorbed by the frame; impact factor is critical in frame design.
> 4. **Bridges** — A moving train on a bridge is a combination of impact and shock loading; dynamic load allowances are mandated by codes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Strain Energy Under Gradual Loading

**Setup:** Consider a prismatic bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ subjected to a gradually increasing axial load from $0$ to $P$.

**Step 1:** At any intermediate load $P'$, the elongation is:

$$\delta' = \frac{P' L}{AE}$$

**Step 2:** The work done by the load in producing an additional infinitesimal elongation $d\delta$ is:

$$dW = P' \cdot d\delta' = P' \cdot \frac{L}{AE} \cdot dP'$$

**Step 3:** Total work done (equal to strain energy stored):

$$U = \int_{0}^{P} P' \cdot \frac{L}{AE} \cdot dP' = \frac{L}{AE} \cdot \int_{0}^{P} P' \, dP' = \frac{L}{AE} \cdot \frac{P^{2}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{U_{\text{gradual}} = \frac{P^{2} L}{2AE}}$$

**Step 4:** Converting to stress and volume form. Substituting $P = \sigma A$ and $V = AL$:

$$U_{\text{gradual}} = \frac{(\sigma A)^{2} \cdot L}{2AE} = \frac{\sigma^{2} \cdot A \cdot L}{2E} = \frac{\sigma^{2} \cdot V}{2E}$$

**Step 5:** Modulus of resilience (per unit volume):

$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Stress and Deformation Under Sudden Loading

**Setup:** A load $P$ is applied **instantaneously** to the free end of a bar. The bar elongates from $0$ to $\delta_{\max}$.

**Step 1:** Since the load remains constant at $P$ during elongation, the work done is:

$$W = P \cdot \delta_{\max}$$

**Step 2:** All this work converts to strain energy. Using $U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k \cdot \delta^{2}$ where stiffness $k = \dfrac{AE}{L}$:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta_{\max}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Equating work done to strain energy:

$$P \cdot \delta_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta_{\max}^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $\delta_{\max}$ (assuming $\delta_{\max} \neq 0$):

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{2PL}{AE} = 2 \delta_s$$

**Step 5:** Maximum stress using $\sigma = \dfrac{E \cdot \delta}{L}$:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{E \cdot \delta_{\max}}{L} = \frac{E}{L} \cdot \frac{2PL}{AE} = \frac{2P}{A} = 2\sigma_s$$

**Step 6:** Strain energy at maximum deformation:

$$U_{\text{sudden}} = P \cdot \delta_{\max} = P \cdot \frac{2PL}{AE} = \frac{2P^{2} L}{2AE} = 2 U_{\text{gradual}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Sudden loading **doubles** both the stress and the deformation, and **quadruples the work input** (but only **doubles** the strain energy stored, because half the energy is dissipated as kinetic energy at the point of maximum deformation).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Impact Factor from First Principles

**Setup:** A weight $P$ falls from a height $h$ onto the end of a vertical bar fixed at the top. Let $\delta$ be the maximum instantaneous deformation.

**Step 1:** Apply conservation of energy (potential energy of fall = strain energy stored):

$$P \cdot (h + \delta) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta^{2}$$

**Step 2:** The static deformation under load $P$ alone is:

$$\delta_s = \frac{PL}{AE} \implies P = \frac{AE \cdot \delta_s}{L}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $P$ from Step 2 into Step 1:

$$\frac{AE \cdot \delta_s}{L} \cdot (h + \delta) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{AE}{L} \cdot \delta^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $\dfrac{AE}{L}$ on both sides:

$$\delta_s (h + \delta) = \frac{\delta^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange into standard quadratic form:

$$\delta^{2} - 2\delta_s \cdot \delta - 2h \delta_s = 0$$

**Step 6:** Apply the quadratic formula $\delta = \dfrac{2\delta_s \pm \sqrt{4\delta_s^{2} + 8h\delta_s}}{2}$:

$$\delta = \delta_s \pm \sqrt{\delta_s^{2} + 2h\delta_s}$$

**Step 7:** Taking the positive root (physical significance — deformation must be positive):

$$\delta_{\max} = \delta_s + \sqrt{\delta_s^{2} + 2h\delta_s} = \delta_s \left( 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_s}} \right)$$

**Step 8:** The ratio of impact deformation to static deformation is the **Impact Factor**:

$$\boxed{\text{IF} = \frac{\delta_{\max}}{\delta_s} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_s}}}$$

**Step 9:** Maximum stress under impact:

$$\sigma_{\max} = E \cdot \frac{\delta_{\max}}{L} = \frac{E}{L} \cdot \delta_s \cdot \text{IF} = \sigma_s \cdot \text{IF}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Special Case — Drop from Zero Height (Sudden Loading Recovery)

Set $h = 0$ in the impact factor formula:

$$\text{IF} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + 0} = 1 + 1 = 2$$

$$\delta_{\max} = 2\delta_s, \quad \sigma_{\max} = 2\sigma_s \quad \checkmark$$

This **confirms** that sudden loading is a special case of impact loading with $h = 0$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Approximation for Large Drop Height

When $h \gg \delta_s$, the term $\dfrac{2h}{\delta_s} \gg 1$, so:

$$\text{IF} \approx 1 + \sqrt{\frac{2h}{\delta_s}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{2h}{\delta_s}}$$

This approximation is used in **forensic engineering** and **drop-weight impact tests** where the height is several orders of magnitude larger than the static deflection.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Problem-Solving

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix as a problem-solving flowchart in the exam.**

| Given in Question | Required to Find | Formula to Apply |
|:------------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|
| $P$, $L$, $A$, $E$, gradual | $U$ | $\dfrac{P^{2} L}{2AE}$ |
| $P$, $L$, $A$, $E$, sudden | $\sigma_{\max}$, $\delta_{\max}$ | $2\sigma_s$, $2\delta_s$ |
| $P$, $h$, $\delta_s$, impact | $\text{IF}$ | $1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_s}$ |
| $\text{IF}$, $P$, $A$ | $\sigma_{\max}$ | $\sigma_s \cdot \text{IF}$ |
| $\sigma_y$, $E$, $V$ | Proof resilience | $\dfrac{\sigma_y^{2}}{2E} \cdot V$ |
| $h$, $\delta_s$ only | Approximate IF | $\sqrt{2h/\delta_s}$ |

## 3.7 Sample Numerical: KTU-Style Solved Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A steel bar of length $2 \text{ m}$, cross-section $30 \text{ mm} \times 30 \text{ mm}$, and $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$ is subjected to a sudden load of $60 \text{ kN}$. Find the maximum stress, maximum elongation, and strain energy stored.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Static stress and elongation:

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^{3}}{30 \times 30} = 66.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\delta_s = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{60 \times 10^{3} \times 2000}{900 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = 0.667 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Sudden loading multiplier = 2:

$$\sigma_{\max} = 2 \times 66.67 = 133.33 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = 2 \times 0.667 = 1.333 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Strain energy under sudden loading:

$$U = 2 \cdot \frac{P^{2} L}{2AE} = \frac{P^{2} L}{AE} = \frac{(60 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 2000}{900 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = 40{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 40 \text{ J}$$

```mermaid
    E1 --> F1["Max Stress equals sigma s"]
    E2 --> F2["Max Stress equals 2 sigma s"]

    E4 --> F4["Max Stress equals 2 sigma s"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define the following terms with suitable equations:
(a) **Strain Energy**
(b) **Modulus of Resilience**
(c) **Proof Resilience**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Strain Energy (U):** The energy stored in a body within its elastic limit due to deformation. It is equal to the work done by external forces in deforming the body.

$$U = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} \times V \quad \text{or} \quad U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2AE}$$

(b) **Modulus of Resilience (u):** The strain energy stored per unit volume of a material when it is stressed up to its elastic limit.

$$u = \frac{\text{Strain Energy}}{\text{Volume}} = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$$

(c) **Proof Resilience:** The maximum amount of strain energy that a material can absorb per unit volume **without undergoing permanent deformation** (i.e., up to the elastic limit).

$$U_{\text{proof}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{elastic limit}}^{2}}{2E} \times V$$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

A mild steel bar of length $1.5 \text{ m}$ and cross-sectional area $400 \text{ mm}^{2}$ is subjected to a gradual tensile load of $80 \text{ kN}$. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$. Calculate:
(a) The strain energy stored in the bar.
(b) The modulus of resilience.

**Model Answer:**

(a) Strain energy under gradual loading:

$$U = \frac{P^{2} L}{2AE} = \frac{(80 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 1500}{2 \times 400 \times 2 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$U = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{9} \times 1500}{1.6 \times 10^{8}} = 60{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 60 \text{ J}$$

(b) Volume of the bar: $V = A \times L = 400 \times 1500 = 6 \times 10^{5} \text{ mm}^{3}$

$$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{60{,}000}{6 \times 10^{5}} = 0.1 \text{ N·mm/mm}^{3} = 0.1 \text{ MPa}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts (7 + 7). Students must attempt **one** of the two choices provided.

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** A bar of length $3 \text{ m}$ and diameter $40 \text{ mm}$ hangs vertically from a rigid support. A load of $50 \text{ kN}$ is **suddenly applied** at the bottom. Determine:
(i) The maximum stress induced in the bar.
(ii) The maximum elongation of the bar.
(iii) The strain energy stored at the point of maximum elongation.

Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$.

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the difference between **gradual, sudden, impact, and shock loadings**. Derive the impact factor and state the special case when impact loading reduces to sudden loading.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $L = 3 \text{ m} = 3000 \text{ mm}$, $d = 40 \text{ mm}$, $P = 50 \text{ kN} = 50 \times 10^{3} \text{ N}$, $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 40^{2} = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Static stress and elongation:

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^{3}}{1256.64} = 39.79 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\delta_s = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{50 \times 10^{3} \times 3000}{1256.64 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = 0.597 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (i):** Maximum stress under sudden loading:

$$\sigma_{\max} = 2\sigma_s = 2 \times 39.79 = 79.58 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 4 (ii):** Maximum elongation:

$$\delta_{\max} = 2\delta_s = 2 \times 0.597 = 1.194 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 (iii):** Strain energy at maximum elongation:

$$U_{\text{sudden}} = P \times \delta_{\max} = 50 \times 10^{3} \times 1.194 = 59{,}700 \text{ N·mm} = 59.7 \text{ J}$$

**Alternative calculation for verification:**

$$U_{\text{sudden}} = \frac{P^{2} L}{AE} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 3000}{1256.64 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = 29.85 \text{ J} \text{ (in strain energy form)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconciliation:** Note that the work done by the load $P \cdot \delta_{\max} = 59.7 \text{ J}$ equals the **strain energy plus kinetic energy** at the point of maximum deformation. At the instant of maximum deformation (velocity = 0), all the work is in strain energy. The factor of 2 difference comes from the average force during elongation.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Sketch of the four loading types:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1["Gradual Load vs Time: Linear ramp 0 to P over time T"] --> A2["Sudden Load vs Time: Vertical jump to P at t equals 0"]
    A2 --> A3["Impact Load vs Time: P applied with velocity from height h"]
    A3 --> A4["Shock Load vs Time: Brief impulse of magnitude P over time dt"]
```

**Derivation of Impact Factor:** (See Section 3.3 for full derivation)

$$\text{IF} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_s}}$$

**Special case:** When $h = 0$, $\text{IF} = 1 + 1 = 2$, which is the impact factor for sudden loading. Hence, sudden loading is a special case of impact loading with zero drop height.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** A weight of $20 \text{ kN}$ falls from a height of $100 \text{ mm}$ on a collar attached to the lower end of a vertical brass bar $1.5 \text{ m}$ long and $30 \text{ mm}$ in diameter. The upper end of the bar is fixed. Take $E = 1 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$. Find:
(i) The maximum instantaneous stress in the bar.
(ii) The maximum instantaneous elongation.
(iii) The strain energy stored.

**(b)** A hoist rope is used to lift a load of $5 \text{ kN}$. If the rope is $8 \text{ m}$ long and has a cross-sectional area of $50 \text{ mm}^{2}$, determine the energy stored in the rope **immediately after the load is suddenly applied**. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:** $P = 20 \text{ kN} = 20 \times 10^{3} \text{ N}$, $h = 100 \text{ mm}$, $L = 1500 \text{ mm}$, $d = 30 \text{ mm}$, $E = 1 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 30^{2} = 706.86 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Static deflection under the load:

$$\delta_s = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{20 \times 10^{3} \times 1500}{706.86 \times 1 \times 10^{5}} = 0.424 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Impact factor:

$$\text{IF} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2h}{\delta_s}} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \frac{2 \times 100}{0.424}}$$

$$\text{IF} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + 471.7} = 1 + \sqrt{472.7} = 1 + 21.74 = 22.74$$

**Step 4 (i):** Maximum instantaneous stress:

$$\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_s \cdot \text{IF}$$

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{20 \times 10^{3}}{706.86} = 28.29 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{\max} = 28.29 \times 22.74 = 643.3 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 5 (ii):** Maximum elongation:

$$\delta_{\max} = \delta_s \cdot \text{IF} = 0.424 \times 22.74 = 9.64 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 (iii):** Strain energy stored at maximum elongation:

$$U = P(h + \delta_{\max}) = 20 \times 10^{3} \times (100 + 9.64) = 20 \times 10^{3} \times 109.64$$

$$U = 2.193 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm} = 2193 \text{ J} = 2.193 \text{ kJ}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $P = 5 \text{ kN}$, $L = 8 \text{ m} = 8000 \text{ mm}$, $A = 50 \text{ mm}^{2}$, $E = 2 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^{2}$, sudden loading.

**Step 1:** Static elongation:

$$\delta_s = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{5 \times 10^{3} \times 8000}{50 \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum elongation under sudden loading:

$$\delta_{\max} = 2\delta_s = 8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Energy stored immediately after sudden loading (at maximum deformation, all work = strain energy):

$$U = P \times \delta_{\max} = 5 \times 10^{3} \times 8 = 40{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 40 \text{ J}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for sudden loading** — Students often use $\delta_s$ instead of $2\delta_s$. Always check the loading type first.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency** — Keep $L$ in mm, $A$ in mm², $E$ in N/mm² to get $U$ in N·mm. Convert to Joules by dividing by 1000.
> 3. **Sign error in quadratic formula** — Always take the **positive root** for maximum deformation in impact loading.
> 4. **Confusing $h$ with $\delta_s$** — $h$ is the **height of fall** (in mm or m); $\delta_s$ is the **static deflection** under the impact load. These are not the same.
> 5. **Forgetting to compute $\delta_s$ first** — In impact problems, the impact factor cannot be evaluated without $\delta_s$. Many students skip this step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Strain Energy Formula:** $U = \dfrac{\sigma^{2} V}{2E} = \dfrac{P^{2} L}{2AE}$ (gradual loading only)
- **Modulus of Resilience:** $u = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$ — measured in $\text{N·m/m}^{3}$ or $\text{Pa}$
- **Proof Resilience:** $U_p = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{elastic limit}}^{2}}{2E} \times V$
- **Sudden Loading Multiplier:** Stress and deformation are **doubled**; strain energy is **doubled**.
- **Impact Factor Master Formula:** $\text{IF} = 1 + \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2h}{\delta_s}}$ — always compute $\delta_s$ first.
- **Special Case:** Impact with $h = 0$ gives $\text{IF} = 2$ (sudden loading).
- **Approximation for Large $h$:** $\text{IF} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2h}{\delta_s}}$ when $h \gg \delta_s$.
- **Maximum Stress Under Impact:** $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_s \cdot \text{IF}$
- **Strain Energy Under Impact:** $U = P(h + \delta_{\max})$
- **Shock Loading Behaviour:** Numerically identical to sudden loading for maximum stress and deformation; the difference is in the **rate** of energy delivery and the resulting stress-wave propagation.
- **Shear Resilience (for springs):** $u_{\text{shear}} = \dfrac{\tau^{2}}{2G}$ where $G$ is the shear modulus.
- **Exam Tip:** Always write the **units** of every intermediate quantity. This prevents calculation errors and earns full marks.
- **Design Rule of Thumb:** For sudden loading, use a **safety factor of 2** on static stress; for impact loading, use the **impact factor** as the dynamic amplification factor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composite Stresses and Strains - Two-Dimensional System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Composite Stresses and Strains - Two-Dimensional System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Two-Dimensional System of Composite Stresses** is a state of stress at a point in a loaded body where the normal stresses ($\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$) and the complementary shear stresses ($\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$) act simultaneously on orthogonal planes. The resultant stress on an inclined plane through that point is termed a **composite (or combined) stress**, and its magnitude and direction depend on the angle of the inclined plane.

The corresponding **Composite Strains** arise from this stress state, and the associated two-dimensional strain field is described by normal strains ($\epsilon_x$, $\epsilon_y$) and shear strain ($\gamma_{xy}$). Because materials obey Hooke's Law, the principal directions of stress and strain coincide in homogeneous, isotropic materials.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):**
> A material element is rarely subjected to a single uniaxial stress. Engineering components such as pressure vessel walls, dam sections, crank shafts, and welded girders experience **bi-axial** or **multi-axial** stress states. The transformation of these stresses onto an arbitrary inclined plane is fundamental to predicting failure using theories such as Maximum Normal Stress, Maximum Shear Stress, or Distortion Energy (von Mises).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are **pulling a flat rubber sheet from two opposite corners** along a diagonal direction.

- The rubber stretches along the pull direction (this is like a **tensile normal stress** $\sigma_\theta$).
- At the same time, the sheet tends to **slide along its edges** because one part of the sheet wants to move differently from the adjacent part (this is like a **shear stress** $\tau_\theta$).
- The pull you applied is the **resultant composite stress** on an inclined plane that is NOT aligned with the sheet's natural edges.

If you rotate the sheet (i.e., look at it from a different angle $\theta$), the magnitudes of normal and shear stress **change** — even though the actual physical forces on the sheet are unchanged. The mathematics of "Composite Stresses in a 2D System" is simply the **rulebook for predicting how these stresses change with rotation angle $\theta$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Insight:**
> The 2D stress state at a point is fully characterized by **three independent components**: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$. All other stress values on rotated planes can be derived from these three invariants. This is why KTU problems always begin by giving you these three values.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Quantities Used

- **Young's Modulus (E)** — units: **N/mm² or GPa**
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$ or $\frac{1}{m}$)** — dimensionless, typically **0.25 to 0.33** for metals
- **Shear Modulus (G)** — units: **N/mm² or GPa**
- **Stress** — units: **N/mm² (MPa)** or **kN/m² (kPa)**
- **Strain** — dimensionless (mm/mm)

---

### 1.4 Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Quantity | Positive Sign | Negative Sign |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Normal Stress $\sigma$ | Tensile (pulling) | Compressive (pushing) |
| Shear Stress $\tau$ | Tends to rotate element **clockwise** | Tends to rotate element **anticlockwise** |
| Normal Strain $\epsilon$ | Elongation | Contraction |
| Angle $\theta$ | Measured **counter-clockwise** from x-axis | Clockwise |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Stress Transformation Problem

Consider a 2D element subjected to $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$. We want to find the stresses acting on a plane whose **outward normal makes an angle $\theta$** (counter-clockwise) with the x-axis.

**Step 1 — Identify the Wedge:**
Cut the element by an inclined plane through the point. A triangular wedge is formed with:
- Face perpendicular to x-axis (area $dA$)
- Inclined face (area $dA / \cos\theta$)
- Face perpendicular to y-axis (area $dA \tan\theta$)

**Step 2 — Apply Equilibrium:**
Resolving forces on the wedge along the inclined plane's normal and tangential directions yields the transformation equations.

**Step 3 — Two Critical Orientations Exist:**
- **Principal Plane:** where shear stress $\tau_\theta = 0$. Normal stress here is **extreme** (max or min).
- **Plane of Maximum Shear:** where $\tau_\theta$ is **extreme**. Normal stress here equals the average of $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Principal Stresses

In design, engineers always look for the **worst-case stress** at a point. By rotating the reference axis, we can find the plane on which the normal stress is highest (principal stress $\sigma_1$) or the shear stress is highest ($\tau_{max}$). These extreme values — **NOT** the given $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$ — are used in failure criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:**
> Invariant quantities (independent of $\theta$):
> - $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$
> - $I_2 = \sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$
>
> These are the **stress invariants** and are routinely asked in KTU exams.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for Module 1. Memorize the sign and the trig function of $2\theta$ carefully — the most common KTU error is swapping $\sin$ and $\cos$ between normal and shear equations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Normal stress on inclined plane | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ | N/mm² |
| 2 | Shear stress on inclined plane | $\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$ | N/mm² |
| 3 | Principal stress (major) | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | N/mm², max |
| 4 | Principal stress (minor) | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | N/mm², min |
| 5 | Maximum shear stress | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | N/mm² |
| 6 | Average normal stress | $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{2}$ | N/mm² |
| 7 | Principal plane angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | gives $\theta_{p1}, \theta_{p2}$ (90° apart) |
| 8 | Max shear plane angle | $\tan 2\theta_s = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | gives $\theta_s$, 45° from $\theta_p$ |
| 9 | Normal strain (biaxial) | $\epsilon_x = \dfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y)$ | Hooke's law, 2D |
| 10 | Shear strain | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$, where $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Volumetric strain (2D / plane stress) | $e_v = \epsilon_x + \epsilon_y = \dfrac{1-\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ | for plane stress |
| 12 | Strain transformation | $\epsilon_\theta = \dfrac{\epsilon_x + \epsilon_y}{2} + \dfrac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta$ | analogous to stress |
| 13 | Shear strain on rotated plane | $\dfrac{\gamma_\theta}{2} = -\dfrac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\cos 2\theta$ | analogous to shear stress |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation:** In the formula table, $\sqrt{ (\cdot)^2 + (\cdot)^2 }$ always produces a **positive** value by definition. The KTU answer key accepts only the positive root for $\sigma_1$ and $\tau_{max}$.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of This System

| Field | Real-World Use |
|-------|---------------|
| Pressure Vessel Design | Finding **hoop vs. longitudinal stress** on a weld seam oriented at an angle |
| Mechanical (Shafts) | Combined bending + torsion yields composite stress; principal stress governs fatigue |
| Civil (Dams, Retaining Walls) | Biaxial stress on dam base: vertical weight + horizontal water pressure |
| Aerospace (Wing Spars) | Combined axial + bending + shear in thin-walled sections |
| Geotechnical | Principal stress orientation governs **failure planes in soil/rock** (Mohr-Coulomb) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stress Transformation Equations

**Setup:** Consider a 2D element of unit thickness. Stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ act on its faces. We cut a wedge by an inclined plane whose outward normal makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis.

**Step 1 — Define Wedge Geometry:**
Let the inclined face have area $dA$. Then:
- Face perpendicular to x-axis has area = $dA \cos\theta$
- Face perpendicular to y-axis has area = $dA \sin\theta$

**Step 2 — Identify Forces on Each Face:**

Force on face $\perp$ to x-axis (acts in +x direction, has components in x and y):
- Force_x = $\sigma_x \cdot dA\cos\theta$
- Force_y = $\tau_{xy} \cdot dA\cos\theta$ (using complementary shear, $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$)

Force on face $\perp$ to y-axis (acts in +y direction):
- Force_x = $\tau_{xy} \cdot dA\sin\theta$
- Force_y = $\sigma_y \cdot dA\sin\theta$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium of Inclined Face:**
The resultant force on the inclined face has magnitude $R \cdot dA$ where $R$ is the resultant stress. Resolve it into:
- Normal component $\sigma_\theta$ (along outward normal)
- Tangential component $\tau_\theta$ (along the plane)

**Step 4 — Sum of Forces along the Normal Direction (n):**
The unit normal vector is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Dot product of total force vector with this normal gives $\sigma_\theta \cdot dA$:

$$
\sigma_\theta \cdot dA = \left[ \sigma_x dA\cos\theta + \tau_{xy} dA\sin\theta \right] \cos\theta + \left[ \tau_{xy} dA\cos\theta + \sigma_y dA\sin\theta \right] \sin\theta
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta
$$

Apply double-angle identities:
- $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$
- $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$
- $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta &= \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2} + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \\[4pt]
\sigma_\theta &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum of Forces along the Tangential Direction (t):**
The unit tangent vector is $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$. Following the same procedure:

$$
\tau_\theta = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta)
$$

Using $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\theta$ and $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta\;}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Principal Stresses

The principal plane is defined as the orientation where $\tau_\theta = 0$. Setting the shear stress equation to zero:

$$
-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta_p = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
$$

This gives **two values of $\theta_p$ separated by 90°** — corresponding to the major and minor principal planes.

**Substitute back into the normal stress equation.** Let $\phi = 2\theta_p$. Then $\cos\phi = \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2R}$ and $\sin\phi = \dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{R}$, where $R = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cdot \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2R} + \tau_{xy}\cdot \frac{\tau_{xy}}{R} \\[4pt]
\sigma_\theta &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{1}{R}\left[ \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{4} + \tau_{xy}^2 \right] \\[4pt]
\sigma_\theta &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{R^2}{R} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + R
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}\;}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress

The shear stress $\tau_\theta$ is a sinusoidal function of $2\theta$. Its extreme values occur when $\dfrac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$, i.e., when $\cos 2\theta = 0$, giving $2\theta = 0, \pi, 2\pi$. Substituting $\cos 2\theta_s = 0$ and $\sin 2\theta_s = \pm 1$:

$$
\tau_{max} = \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \pm \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}
$$

The corresponding **normal stress on the plane of maximum shear** is:

$$
\sigma' = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \sigma_{avg}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of 2D Strain Transformation

Hooke's law in 2D plane stress gives:

$$
\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y), \quad \epsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x), \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}
$$

Since stress and strain tensors are coaxial (have the same principal directions) for isotropic materials, the strain transformation equations have an **identical form** to stress equations:

$$
\epsilon_\theta = \frac{\epsilon_x + \epsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta
$$

$$
\frac{\gamma_\theta}{2} = -\frac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\cos 2\theta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference vs. Stress:** The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ appears in the shear term of the strain equation because the engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$ is already the **total** angular distortion, whereas $\tau_{xy}$ is the shear stress itself.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mohr's Circle — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete analytical procedure for solving a 2D composite stress problem using Mohr's Circle, which is the **graphical equivalent** of the transformation equations derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy] --> B[Plot point X = sigma_x, minus tau_xy]
    A --> C[Plot point Y = sigma_y, plus tau_xy]
    B --> D[Draw diameter XY; center C = avg of sigma_x and sigma_y]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Compute radius R = half of XY length]
    E --> F[Measure angle 2theta from XY line to x-axis on circle]
    F --> G[Read sigma_theta and tau_theta at rotated point]
    G --> H[Identify principal point P1: tau = 0, sigma = sigma_1 max]
    G --> I[Identify principal point P2: tau = 0, sigma = sigma_2 min]
    G --> J[Identify max shear point S: tau = tau_max, sigma = sigma_avg]
    H --> K[OUTPUT: sigma_1, sigma_2, tau_max, theta_p, theta_s]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Verify with analytical formulas]
```

### 4.2 Stress Element Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    elemA[Element Face Right: sigma_x acts outward, tau_xy acts downward] --> rotA[Rotate element by angle theta CCW]
    elemB[Element Face Top: sigma_y acts outward, tau_yx acts rightward] --> rotA
    rotA --> rotB[New inclined face experiences sigma_theta and tau_theta]
    rotA --> rotC[Stress state fully defined by sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy]
    rotC --> rotD[Invariants: I1 = sigma_x + sigma_y, I2 = sigma_x*sigma_y minus tau_xy squared]
```

### 4.3 Principal vs. Maximum Shear Plane — Orientation Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axis[X-axis reference] --> angA[Angle theta_p1 from x-axis to principal plane sigma_1]
    axis --> angB[Angle theta_s from x-axis to max shear plane]
    angA --> note1[Theta_s = theta_p1 plus or minus 45 degrees]
    angB --> note1
    note1 --> out1[Both planes are perpendicular to each other]
    out1 --> out2[On principal plane: only normal stress sigma_1, no shear]
    out1 --> out3[On max shear plane: tau_max and sigma_avg = sigma_1 plus sigma_2 divided by 2]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The two principal planes are always **perpendicular to each other** ($\theta_{p2} = \theta_{p1} + 90°$). The two max-shear planes are also perpendicular to each other and lie at **45° from the principal planes**. This geometric relationship is testable directly using Mohr's circle without any computation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define the terms **principal planes** and **principal stresses** in a two-dimensional stress system. Why are they important in design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Principal Planes (1 mark):** Principal planes are the two mutually perpendicular planes at a point in a stressed body on which the **shear stress is zero** and the **normal stress attains its extreme (maximum or minimum) values**.
- **Principal Stresses (1 mark):** The normal stresses acting on these principal planes are called principal stresses, denoted $\sigma_1$ (major) and $\sigma_2$ (minor), with $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$.
- **Importance in Design (1 mark):** They are critical because most **failure theories** (Maximum Normal Stress Theory, Maximum Shear Stress Theory, and Distortion Energy Theory) use principal stress values — not the given $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$ — to predict yielding or fracture. Finding principal stresses allows the engineer to identify the **worst-case loading direction** at every point in a structure.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

Distinguish between **principal planes** and **planes of maximum shear stress** in a 2D stress system. What is the angular relationship between them?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Principal Plane | Plane of Maximum Shear |
|---------|----------------|------------------------|
| Shear stress on plane | $\tau = 0$ | $\tau = \tau_{max}$ (extreme value) |
| Normal stress on plane | $\sigma = \sigma_1$ or $\sigma_2$ (extreme) | $\sigma = \sigma_{avg} = \tfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ |
| Governing equation | $\tan 2\theta_p = \tfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | $\tan 2\theta_s = -\tfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ |

**Angular relationship (1 mark):** The principal planes and the planes of maximum shear stress are oriented at exactly **45° to each other** (i.e., $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Main Exam, Modified]*

At a point in a structural steel member, the stresses on two perpendicular planes are:
- $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa (tensile)
- $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa (compressive)
- $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa (positive sense as per KTU sign convention)

Take **E = 200 GPa** and **Poisson's ratio $\nu$ = 0.3**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the magnitudes and orientations of the **principal stresses** and the **maximum shear stress** at the point.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Using the principal stresses, compute the **principal strains** and the **maximum shear strain** in the material.

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Principal and Maximum Shear Stresses:**

Given: $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Center of Mohr's circle (Average stress):**

$$
\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = \frac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Radius of Mohr's circle:**

$$
R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{(60)^2 + (30)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} = 67.082 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Principal stresses:**

$$
\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R = 20 + 67.082 = 87.082 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

$$
\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R = 20 - 67.082 = -47.082 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Step 4 — Principal plane orientation:**

$$
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - (-40)} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5
$$

$$
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.565°
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{p1} = 13.28°}, \quad \boxed{\theta_{p2} = 13.28° + 90° = 103.28°}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{max} = R = 67.082 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Max-shear plane orientation:**

$$
\theta_s = \theta_{p1} + 45° = 13.28° + 45° = 58.28°
$$

On this plane, the normal stress is $\sigma_{avg} = 20$ MPa.

---

**Part (b) — Principal and Maximum Shear Strains:**

**Step 1 — Compute the shear modulus G:**

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{200{,}000}{2(1+0.3)} = \frac{200{,}000}{2.6} = 76{,}923.08 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Principal strains (apply Hooke's law with $\sigma_2$ as the minor principal):**

$$
\epsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu\sigma_2) = \frac{1}{200{,}000}\left(87.082 - 0.3 \times (-47.082)\right)
$$

$$
\epsilon_1 = \frac{1}{200{,}000}(87.082 + 14.1246) = \frac{101.2066}{200{,}000} = 5.06 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\epsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_2 - \nu\sigma_1) = \frac{1}{200{,}000}\left(-47.082 - 0.3 \times 87.082\right)
$$

$$
\epsilon_2 = \frac{1}{200{,}000}(-47.082 - 26.1246) = \frac{-73.2066}{200{,}000} = -3.66 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum shear strain:**

On the plane of maximum shear stress, the **principal stresses act as orthogonal normal stresses** to that plane (i.e., $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ on its faces). Therefore:

$$
\gamma_{max} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{G} = \frac{67.082}{76{,}923.08} = 8.72 \times 10^{-4}
$$

(Equivalently, in terms of principal strains: $\gamma_{max} = \epsilon_1 - \epsilon_2 = 5.06 \times 10^{-4} - (-3.66 \times 10^{-4}) = 8.72 \times 10^{-4}$, which verifies the result.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary, Adapted]*

A point in a brass component is subjected to the following 2D stress state:
- $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa (tensile)
- $\sigma_y = 20$ MPa (tensile)
- $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa

For this material, **E = 100 GPa** and **$\nu$ = 0.32**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the **normal and shear stresses** on a plane whose normal is inclined at $\theta = 30°$ counter-clockwise from the x-axis. Also calculate the resultant stress on this plane.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **strains** $\epsilon_x$, $\epsilon_y$, and $\gamma_{xy}$ in the original x-y coordinate system and then determine the **strain on the inclined plane** $\epsilon_\theta$ at $\theta = 30°$.

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stresses on the Inclined Plane:**

Given: $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 20$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa, $\theta = 30°$.

Therefore $2\theta = 60°$, with:
- $\cos 2\theta = \cos 60° = 0.5$
- $\sin 2\theta = \sin 60° = 0.8660$

**Step 1 — Normal stress on inclined plane:**

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = \frac{60 + 20}{2} + \frac{60 - 20}{2}(0.5) + 25(0.8660)
$$

$$
\sigma_\theta = 40 + 10 + 21.65 = 71.65 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

**Step 2 — Shear stress on inclined plane:**

$$
\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
$$

$$
\tau_\theta = -20(0.8660) + 25(0.5) = -17.32 + 12.5 = -4.82 \text{ MPa}
$$

The negative sign indicates the shear tends to rotate the element in the **opposite** sense to the assumed positive direction.

**Step 3 — Resultant stress on the inclined plane:**

$$
R = \sqrt{\sigma_\theta^2 + \tau_\theta^2} = \sqrt{(71.65)^2 + (-4.82)^2}
$$

$$
R = \sqrt{5133.72 + 23.23} = \sqrt{5156.95} = 71.81 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Orientation of resultant stress (angle $\phi$ from plane normal):**

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{\vert\tau_\theta\vert}{\sigma_\theta} = \frac{4.82}{71.65} = 0.0673 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = 3.85°
$$

The resultant is nearly **normal to the inclined plane**, as expected when $\sigma_\theta$ dominates.

---

**Part (b) — Strains in the Original and Rotated Coordinates:**

**Step 1 — Shear modulus:**

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{100{,}000}{2(1+0.32)} = \frac{100{,}000}{2.64} = 37{,}878.79 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Normal strains $\epsilon_x$ and $\epsilon_y$:**

$$
\epsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y) = \frac{1}{100{,}000}(60 - 0.32 \times 20)
$$

$$
\epsilon_x = \frac{60 - 6.4}{100{,}000} = \frac{53.6}{100{,}000} = 5.36 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\epsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x) = \frac{1}{100{,}000}(20 - 0.32 \times 60)
$$

$$
\epsilon_y = \frac{20 - 19.2}{100{,}000} = \frac{0.8}{100{,}000} = 0.08 \times 10^{-4} = 8.0 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 3 — Shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$:**

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{25}{37{,}878.79} = 6.6 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Normal strain on the inclined plane (strain transformation):**

$$
\epsilon_\theta = \frac{\epsilon_x + \epsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\epsilon_x - \epsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta
$$

$$
\epsilon_\theta = \frac{5.36 \times 10^{-4} + 0.08 \times 10^{-4}}{2} + \frac{5.36 \times 10^{-4} - 0.08 \times 10^{-4}}{2}(0.5) + \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-4}}{2}(0.8660)
$$

$$
\epsilon_\theta = 2.72 \times 10^{-4} + 1.32 \times 10^{-4} + 2.858 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\epsilon_\theta = 6.898 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign of $\sigma_y$:** If the problem says "compressive," you **MUST** enter it as a negative number (e.g., -40 MPa, not 40 MPa). Forgetting this flips the signs of $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$. This is the **#1 mark-deduction error** in KTU valuation.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\theta$ and $2\theta$:** The transformation equations use $\cos 2\theta$ and $\sin 2\theta$, but the principal angle is $\theta_p$. Many students solve for $\theta_p$ but then plug $\theta_p$ into $\cos 2\theta_p$ without first computing $2\theta_p$.
>
> 3. **Two principal angles, not one:** $\tan 2\theta_p$ gives a periodic solution. You must report **both** $\theta_{p1}$ and $\theta_{p2} = \theta_{p1} + 90°$. Omitting the second is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Strain equation factor of ½:** Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in front of $\gamma_{xy}$ in the strain transformation equation gives a wrong $\epsilon_\theta$. Always derive it fresh from Hooke's law if unsure.
>
> 5. **Units in final answer:** Principal stress must have units **MPa** or **N/mm²** stated explicitly. Numerical answer without units gets 0.5 marks deducted.
>
> 6. **Volumetric strain vs. area strain:** The 2D volumetric strain under plane stress is $\tfrac{1-\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$, **not** $\tfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$. The factor $(1-\nu)$ comes from the out-of-plane strain $\epsilon_z = -\tfrac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision for Exam Day:**
>
> - 2D composite stress at a point is fully defined by **3 independent components**: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$.
> - **Normal stress on inclined plane:** $\sigma_\theta = \tfrac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} + \tfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$.
> - **Shear stress on inclined plane:** $\tau_\theta = -\tfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$.
> - **Principal stresses:** $\sigma_{1,2} = \tfrac{\sigma_x+\sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
> - **Principal plane angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = \tfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$.
> - **Max shear stress:** $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\tfrac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \tfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$.
> - **Max shear plane:** rotated **45° from the principal planes**, and on it $\sigma = \sigma_{avg}$.
> - **Invariants:** $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ and $I_2 = \sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2 = \sigma_1 \sigma_2$.
> - **Strain transformation** has the same form as stress, but $\gamma_{xy}/2$ replaces $\tau_{xy}$.
> - **Hooke's law in 2D plane stress:** $\epsilon_x = \tfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y)$, $\epsilon_y = \tfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x)$, $\gamma_{xy} = \tfrac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$.
> - **Sign convention:** Tensile $\sigma$ and counter-clockwise $\theta$ are **positive**.
> - **Always** report $\theta$ in degrees (KTU convention) and stress in **MPa** unless asked otherwise.
> - **Mohr's circle** is the graphical tool: the center is at $\sigma_{avg}$, the radius is $\tau_{max}$, and rotating $2\theta$ on the circle equals rotating $\theta$ on the element.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress at a Point on a Plane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Stress at a Point on a Plane # 📘 Module 1 — Stress at a Point on a Plane ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Stress at a Point — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > The **stress at a point** on a plane is defined as the *internal resistive force per unit area* acting on that infinitesimal plane passing through the point. Since a plane through a point can be oriented in *infinitely many directions*, the stress component (both normal $\sigma_n$ and tangential $\tau$) **varies with the orientation of the plane**. The complete stress state at a point requires specification of stress on three mutually perpendicular planes. A point in a loaded body is subjected to a **general three-dimensional state of stress** consisting of **nine stress components** (three normal: $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ and six shear: $\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yx}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zy}, \tau_{zx}, \tau_{xz}$), with the symmetry of the stress tensor ($\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$) reducing the independent components to **six**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > *"Stress at a point on a plane — normal and tangential stresses on inclined planes, principal stresses, and maximum shear stress."* ### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **tightly packed cube of wet sand** in your palm. Now imagine pressing it from the top with your thumb. - The force from your thumb pushes downward. - Inside the sand cube, particles push *sideways* on each other (not just vertically). - If you were to slice this cube *diagonally* with an imaginary knife, the force you'd see on the knife's surface depends on the **angle of your slice**. - A vertical cut → mostly horizontal push (shear). - A 45° cut → a mix of push and slide. This is exactly the situation inside a structural member! The **orientation of the imaginary cut plane** determines the magnitude and nature (normal vs. shear) of the stress. Engineers must therefore know how to compute these stresses on **any arbitrary inclined plane** to find the *most dangerous* orientation — the plane most likely to fail. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Quantities & Standard Units (SI):** > - **Normal Stress** ($\sigma$) → Force per unit area perpendicular to the plane → Units: **N/m² (Pa)** or **MPa = 10⁶ Pa = N/mm²** > - **Shear Stress** ($\tau$) → Force per unit area parallel to the plane → Units: **MPa** or **N/mm²** > - **Stress Tensor Components** → Represented as a **3 × 3 symmetric matrix** (second-order tensor). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🧠 Theoretical Breakdown — How Stress Transforms with Plane Orientation Consider a **two-dimensional state of stress** at a point (this is the most commonly tested KTU scenario): 1. **Given state**: A point has known stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ acting on the faces of a small element aligned with the $x$–$y$ axes. 2. **The Question**: What are the stresses ($\sigma_n$ and $\tau$) on a plane whose **outward normal makes an angle $\theta$** (measured counter-clockwise from the $x$-axis) with the original $x$-face? 3. **The Method (Free-Body Approach)**: - Isolate a *wedge* (triangular element) of the original body, bounded by the inclined plane. - Apply equilibrium equations: $\Sigma F_x = 0$, $\Sigma F_y = 0$. - Resolve all force vectors into components *normal* and *tangential* to the inclined plane. - Divide forces by the inclined area to get stresses. 4. **The Result**: Two elegant equations (the **stress transformation equations**) relate $(\sigma_n, \tau)$ to $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy})$ and the angle $\theta$. 5. **Special Orientations**: - **Principal Planes**: Where shear stress vanishes ($\tau = 0$). The normal stresses here are the **principal stresses** $\sigma_1$ (max) and $\sigma_2$ (min). - **Maximum Shear Plane**: Where $\tau$ is greatest. Normal stress here is the average: $\sigma_{avg} = (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/2$. ### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Normal stress on inclined plane | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ | MPa or N/mm² | | 2 | Shear stress on inclined plane | $\tau = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$ | MPa or N/mm² | | 3 | Average normal stress | $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | Independent of $\theta$ | | 4 | Radius of Mohr's Circle | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Same units as stress | | 5 | Principal Stresses (max & min) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Always: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ | | 6 | Principal Plane Angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Gives **two values** $90°$ apart | | 7 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Occurs at $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$ | | 8 | Normal stress on max-shear plane | $\sigma' = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \sigma_{avg}$ | Always equals the center | | 9 | Invariant of Stress Tensor | $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (sum is constant in 2D) | Useful cross-check | | 10 | Stress Tensor Matrix (2D) | $\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y \end{bmatrix}$ | Symmetric → real eigenvalues | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** > - **Tensile normal stress** → **Positive** (+). > - **Compressive normal stress** → **Negative** (−). > - **Shear stress** is **positive** if it tends to rotate the element **counter-clockwise** (CCW). ### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |-------|-------------| | **Pressure Vessels** | Hoop & longitudinal stresses in cylindrical/tank walls; principal stresses help locate failure-prone regions. | | **Bridges & Trusses** | Identifying inclined planes where combined bending + shear creates dangerous stresses. | | **Machine Components** | Shafts under combined bending & torsion — finding principal stresses for fatigue analysis. | | **Geotechnical Engineering** | Soil & rock slopes — principal stresses determine failure planes (Mohr-Coulomb theory). | | **Aerospace** | Wing spars and fuselage — multi-axial stress state under combined aerodynamic + inertial loads. | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🧮 Derivation 1: Normal and Shear Stress on an Inclined Plane #### Setup Consider a 2D element of unit thickness ($t = 1$) with known stresses: - $\sigma_x$ acting on the right face (positive → tension) - $\sigma_y$ acting on the top face (positive → tension) - $\tau_{xy}$ acting on the right face in the +y direction; $\tau_{yx}$ acting on the top face in the +x direction (complementary, $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$). We isolate a **wedge** by slicing along a plane whose *outward normal* makes an angle $\theta$ (CCW from the $x$-axis). #### Geometry of the Wedge - Let the inclined face have area $dA$. - Then the vertical face (perpendicular to $x$) has area $dA \cos\theta$. - The horizontal face (perpendicular to $y$) has area $dA \sin\theta$. Forces on each face (per unit thickness): - On vertical face: $\sigma_x \cdot dA \cos\theta$ (normal, in +x) and $\tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cos\theta$ (shear, in +y). - On horizontal face: $\sigma_y \cdot dA \sin\theta$ (normal, in +y) and $\tau_{xy} \cdot dA \sin\theta$ (shear, in +x). - On inclined face: $\sigma_n \cdot dA$ (normal, outward) and $\tau \cdot dA$ (tangential, chosen CCW). #### Equilibrium Equations **Step 1:** Sum of forces along the *normal* direction to the inclined plane = 0. The unit normal direction makes angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis. So the unit normal is $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Projection of all forces onto the normal: $$ \sigma_n \, dA = (\sigma_x \, dA\cos\theta)\cos\theta + (\tau_{xy} \, dA\cos\theta)\sin\theta + (\sigma_y \, dA\sin\theta)\sin\theta + (\tau_{xy} \, dA\sin\theta)\cos\theta $$ **Step 2:** Divide both sides by $dA$ and simplify: $$ \sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy} \sin\theta \cos\theta $$ **Step 3:** Apply double-angle identities: - $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2}$ - $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$ - $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$ Substitute: $$ \sigma_n = \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1 + \cos 2\theta}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2} + 2\tau_{xy} \cdot \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} $$ Grouping the constant terms and the $2\theta$ terms: $$ \sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta $$ ✅ **This is the KTU formula for normal stress on an inclined plane.** **Step 4:** Sum of forces along the *tangential* direction (perpendicular to normal, i.e., at angle $\theta + 90°$) = 0. Tangential unit vector: $(-\sin\theta, \cos\theta)$. Projection of all forces onto tangent: $$ \tau \, dA = -(\sigma_x \, dA\cos\theta)\sin\theta + (\tau_{xy} \, dA\cos\theta)\cos\theta + (\sigma_y \, dA\sin\theta)\cos\theta - (\tau_{xy} \, dA\sin\theta)\sin\theta $$ **Step 5:** Simplify using identities: $$ \tau = -\sigma_x \sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin\theta\cos\theta - \tau_{xy}\sin^2\theta $$ Apply $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$, $\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta = \cos 2\theta$: $$ \tau = -\sigma_x \cdot \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} - 0 $$ Rearranging: $$ \tau = \frac{\sigma_y - \sigma_x}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta $$ Or equivalently: $$ \tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta $$ ✅ **This is the KTU formula for shear stress on an inclined plane.** > [!NOTE] > **Why these signs?** > The negative sign in front of $\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}$ appears because of the standard CCW-positive shear convention. Some textbooks write it with the opposite sign convention — always cross-check using a **physical sketch** in the exam. --- ### 🧮 Derivation 2: Principal Stresses #### Concept **Principal stresses** are the *extreme* (maximum and minimum) normal stresses at a point. They occur on planes where the shear stress $\tau = 0$ (the **principal planes**). #### Method Differentiate $\sigma_n$ with respect to $\theta$ and set it to zero (to find extrema of $\sigma_n$). **Step 1:** Differentiate: $$ \frac{d\sigma_n}{d\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cdot 2\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cdot 2\cos 2\theta $$ Simplify: $$ \frac{d\sigma_n}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta $$ **Step 2:** Set to zero: $$ (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin 2\theta_p = 2\tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta_p $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate $\tan 2\theta_p$: $$ \tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} $$ This equation yields **two values of $\theta_p$ separated by 90°** — these are the principal plane orientations. **Step 4:** Substitute $\sin 2\theta_p$ and $\cos 2\theta_p$ back into the $\sigma_n$ equation. Using the identity: $\cos 2\theta_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2 2\theta_p}}$ and $\sin 2\theta_p = \dfrac{\tan 2\theta_p}{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2 2\theta_p}}$, after considerable algebraic simplification (or via Mohr's circle geometrically): $$ \sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ ✅ **This is the KTU formula for principal stresses.** By convention: $\sigma_1$ is the **larger** (algebraically maximum) and $\sigma_2$ is the **smaller** (algebraically minimum) value. --- ### 🧮 Derivation 3: Maximum Shear Stress #### Method Differentiate $\tau$ with respect to $\theta$ and set to zero. **Step 1:** Differentiate: $$ \frac{d\tau}{d\theta} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cdot 2\cos 2\theta - \tau_{xy}\cdot 2\sin 2\theta = 0 $$ **Step 2:** Simplify: $$ -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta_s - 2\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta_s = 0 $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange: $$ \tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} $$ **Step 4:** Compare with $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$. Multiplying the two: $$ \tan 2\theta_s \cdot \tan 2\theta_p = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} \cdot \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = -1 $$ So **$\theta_s$ and $\theta_p$ differ by exactly $45°$** (since $\tan A \cdot \tan B = -1 \Rightarrow A - B = 90°$). **Step 5:** Substitute $\sin 2\theta_s$ and $\cos 2\theta_s$ into the $\tau$ equation to get $\tau_{max}$: $$ \tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ But also note that $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = 2R$ (where $R$ is the radius of Mohr's circle), so: $$ \tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} $$ **Step 6:** The *normal stress* on the plane of maximum shear is: $$ \sigma_n \Big|_{\tau = \tau_{max}} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \sigma_{avg} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** > When asked to find stresses on a specific plane, *always*: > 1. Draw the wedge diagram. > 2. Mark the angle $\theta$ **clearly** (measured from the $x$-face to the normal of the inclined plane). > 3. Substitute values — **never** by-eye-estimate. > 4. Verify with $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (invariance). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 📊 Diagram A: Wedge Element for Stress Transformation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph WEDGE[Inclined Wedge Element of Unit Thickness] direction TB A1["Vertical Face
Area = dA times cos theta
Stress: sigma_x, tau_xy"] A2["Horizontal Face
Area = dA times sin theta
Stress: sigma_y, tau_xy"] A3["Inclined Face
Area = dA
Stress: sigma_n, tau
Normal at angle theta to x axis"] A1 --- A2 A2 --- A3 A1 --- A3 end F1["Force on Vertical Face: sigma_x dA cos theta i + tau_xy dA cos theta j"] F2["Force on Horizontal Face: tau_xy dA sin theta i + sigma_y dA sin theta j"] F3["Force on Inclined Face: sigma_n dA n_hat + tau dA t_hat"] F1 --> A1 F2 --> A2 F3 --> A3 EQS["Apply Equilibrium
Sigma F_n equals zero and Sigma F_t equals zero"] EQS --> RES["Result: sigma_n equation and tau equation"] ``` ### 📊 Diagram B: Functional Architecture of Stress Transformation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT DATA] I1["Given sigma_x"] I2["Given sigma_y"] I3["Given tau_xy"] I4["Given angle theta"] end subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATION ENGINE] C1["Compute Center C
C equals sigma_x plus sigma_y over 2"] C2["Compute Radius R
R equals sqrt of ((sigma_x minus sigma_y over 2) squared plus tau_xy squared)"] C3["Compute sigma_n using
double angle formula"] C4["Compute tau using
double angle formula"] C5["Find Principal Stress
sigma1, sigma2 equals C plus or minus R"] C6["Find Max Shear
tau_max equals R"] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT RESULTS] O1["Normal Stress sigma_n on inclined plane"] O2["Shear Stress tau on inclined plane"] O3["Principal Stresses sigma1 and sigma2"] O4["Principal Plane Angle theta_p"] O5["Max Shear Stress tau_max at theta_s"] end I1 --> C1 I2 --> C1 I1 --> C2 I2 --> C2 I3 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 I3 --> C3 C1 --> C4 C2 --> C4 I3 --> C4 I4 --> C3 I4 --> C4 C1 --> C5 C2 --> C5 C1 --> C6 C2 --> O1 C3 --> O1 C3 --> O2 C4 --> O2 C5 --> O3 I1 --> C5 I2 --> C5 C5 --> O4 C6 --> O5 ``` ### 📐 Diagram C: Stress State on a 2D Element (Block Representation) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SIGMAX[Right Face] S1["Normal Stress sigma_x outward"] S2["Shear Stress tau_xy downward"] end subgraph SIGMAY[Top Face] S3["Normal Stress sigma_y outward"] S4["Shear Stress tau_yx rightward"] end subgraph BOTFACE[Bottom Face] S5["Normal Stress sigma_y inward"] S6["Shear Stress tau_yx leftward"] end subgraph LEFTFACE[Left Face] S7["Normal Stress sigma_x inward"] S8["Shear Stress tau_xy upward"] end NOTE["Equilibrium Note: tau_xy equals tau_yx
Complementary Shear Stress Principle"] NOTE --- SIGMAX NOTE --- SIGMAY ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** > - The **wedge diagram** shows how the original rectangular element is sliced along an inclined plane, isolating the triangular wedge on which equilibrium is applied. > - The **functional flow** mirrors the KTU problem-solving pipeline: input given values → compute center & radius → find required stresses. > - The **stress state block diagram** enforces the symmetry rule $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, which is the foundation of the stress tensor's symmetry. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define principal stresses and principal planes. How are they determined mathematically?** **Model Answer:** - **Principal stresses** are the maximum and minimum normal stresses occurring at a point in a loaded body. - **Principal planes** are the orientations of planes on which these principal stresses act. On these planes, the **shear stress is zero**. - **Mathematically**, they are obtained by differentiating the normal-stress-on-inclined-plane equation with respect to $\theta$ and equating to zero, yielding: $$ \tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} $$ Substituting back gives the principal stress values: $$ \sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **State the expression for normal stress on an inclined plane and explain the significance of the term $\sigma_{avg}$.** **Model Answer:** The expression for normal stress on an inclined plane whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with the $x$-axis is: $$ \sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta $$ The term $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ represents the **average normal stress** at the point, which is **independent of the plane orientation**. Physically, it is the normal stress that acts on the planes of **maximum shear stress**. It is also the **center** of Mohr's circle of stress. --- ### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]** At a point in a stressed material, the stresses on two mutually perpendicular planes are: - $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa (tensile), $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa (CCW positive). **Find:** **(a)** The normal and shear stresses on a plane whose normal makes $30°$ with the $x$-axis. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The magnitudes and directions of the principal stresses and the maximum shear stress at the point. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution:** **Given:** $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa, $\theta = 30°$. **(a) Stresses on Inclined Plane:** Apply the transformation equation: $$ \sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta $$ Compute intermediate values: - $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{80 + 40}{2} = 60$ MPa - $\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} = \dfrac{80 - 40}{2} = 20$ MPa - $2\theta = 60°$ → $\cos 60° = 0.5$, $\sin 60° = 0.866$ Substitute: $$ \sigma_n = 60 + 20 \cdot 0.5 + 30 \cdot 0.866 $$ $$ \sigma_n = 60 + 10 + 25.98 = 95.98 \text{ MPa} \approx 96 \text{ MPa (tensile)} $$ Now for shear stress: $$ \tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta $$ $$ \tau = -20 \cdot 0.866 + 30 \cdot 0.5 $$ $$ \tau = -17.32 + 15 = -2.32 \text{ MPa} $$ The **negative sign** indicates the shear stress acts in the **clockwise (CW) direction** (opposite to the assumed CCW-positive convention). **Answer (a):** $\sigma_n \approx 96$ MPa (tensile), $\tau \approx 2.32$ MPa (clockwise). --- **(b) Principal Stresses and Maximum Shear Stress:** Step 1 — Principal plane angle: $$ \tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \cdot 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5 $$ $$ 2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31° $$ $$ \theta_{p1} = 28.155° \quad \text{(for } \sigma_1\text{)}, \quad \theta_{p2} = \theta_{p1} + 90° = 118.155° \text{ (for } \sigma_2\text{)} $$ Step 2 — Principal stress magnitudes: $$ \sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x -
