\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ $$ \sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm \sqrt{20^2 + 30^2} = 60 \pm \sqrt{400 + 900} = 60 \pm \sqrt{1300} $$ $$ \sigma_{1,2} = 60 \pm 36.06 $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_1 = 96.06 \text{ MPa (tensile)}, \quad \sigma_2 = 23.94 \text{ MPa (tensile)}} $$ Step 3 — Maximum shear stress: $$ \tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{96.06 - 23.94}{2} = \frac{72.12}{2} = 36.06 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 4 — Orientation of plane of maximum shear: $$ \theta_s = \theta_p - 45° = 28.155° - 45° = -16.845° \quad (\text{or equivalently } 73.155°) $$ **Answer (b):** $\sigma_1 = 96.06$ MPa at $\theta_{p1} = 28.16°$; $\sigma_2 = 23.94$ MPa at $\theta_{p2} = 118.16°$; $\tau_{max} = 36.06$ MPa at $\theta_s = -16.85°$ (or $73.15°$). **Verification:** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 96.06 + 23.94 = 120$ MPa $= \sigma_x + \sigma_y = 80 + 40$. ✅ Invariant check passes. --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** *(Alternative Choice)* At a point in a machine component, the state of stress on a horizontal plane is: $\sigma_x = -50$ MPa (compressive), $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa. On a vertical plane: $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa (tensile), $\tau_{yx} = 25$ MPa. **Find:** **(a)** The principal stresses and the orientation of principal planes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The maximum shear stress, the normal stress on the plane of maximum shear, and the stresses on a plane inclined at $45°$ to the $x$-axis. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution:** **Given:** $\sigma_x = -50$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 30$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 25$ MPa. **(a) Principal Stresses:** Step 1 — Compute components: $$ \sigma_{avg} = \frac{-50 + 30}{2} = -10 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{-50 - 30}{2} = -40 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 2 — Principal stresses: $$ \sigma_{1,2} = -10 \pm \sqrt{(-40)^2 + 25^2} = -10 \pm \sqrt{1600 + 625} = -10 \pm \sqrt{2225} $$ $$ \sqrt{2225} = 47.17 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_1 = 37.17 \text{ MPa (tensile)}, \quad \sigma_2 = -57.17 \text{ MPa (compressive)}} $$ Step 3 — Principal plane angle: $$ \tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \cdot 25}{-50 - 30} = \frac{50}{-80} = -0.625 $$ $$ 2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(-0.625) = -32.01° $$ To decide which angle gives $\sigma_1$ and which gives $\sigma_2$, test $\theta_p = -16°$ in the formula: - For $2\theta_p = -32°$: $\cos(-32°) = 0.848$, $\sin(-32°) = -0.530$. $$ \sigma_n = -10 + (-40)(0.848) + 25(-0.530) = -10 - 33.92 - 13.25 = -57.17 \text{ MPa} $$ So $\theta_{p2} = -16°$ gives $\sigma_2$. Therefore $\theta_{p1} = -16° + 90° = 74°$ gives $\sigma_1$. **Answer (a):** $\sigma_1 = 37.17$ MPa at $\theta_{p1} = 74°$; $\sigma_2 = -57.17$ MPa at $\theta_{p2} = -16°$ (or $164°$). --- **(b) Maximum Shear Stress and Stresses at $\theta = 45°$:** Step 1 — Maximum shear stress: $$ \tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{37.17 - (-57.17)}{2} = \frac{94.34}{2} = 47.17 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 2 — Normal stress on plane of maximum shear: $$ \sigma_n \Big|_{\tau_{max}} = \sigma_{avg} = -10 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 3 — Orientation: $$ \theta_s = \theta_p + 45° = -16° + 45° = 29° \quad \text{(or equivalently } 74° - 45° = 29° \text{)} $$ Step 4 — Stresses on plane at $\theta = 45°$: Compute: $2\theta = 90°$, so $\cos 90° = 0$, $\sin 90° = 1$. $$ \sigma_n = -10 + (-40)(0) + 25(1) = -10 + 0 + 25 = 15 \text{ MPa (tensile)} $$ $$ \tau = -(-40)(1) + 25(0) = 40 \text{ MPa} $$ **Answer (b):** $\tau_{max} = 47.17$ MPa at $\theta_s = 29°$; normal stress on that plane = $-10$ MPa; on a plane at $\theta = 45°$: $\sigma_n = 15$ MPa, $\tau = 40$ MPa. **Verification:** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 37.17 + (-57.17) = -20$ MPa $= \sigma_x + \sigma_y = -50 + 30$. ✅ --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Wrong angle convention**: Students often use the angle to the *plane* instead of to the *normal* of the plane. The KTU convention (and standard Mechanics of Materials convention) measures $\theta$ from the $x$-axis to the **outward normal** of the inclined face. Using the wrong angle can flip the answer by $90°$. > > 2. **Sign of shear stress**: Forgetting that a **negative** value of $\tau$ means the actual direction is **clockwise**, not "negative stress exists". Marks are deducted if you don't clearly state the direction. > > 3. **Principal angle ambiguity**: The equation $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ gives **two solutions** $90°$ apart. Students often forget to compute **both** and assign $\sigma_1$ vs $\sigma_2$ correctly. **Always verify** by substituting back. > > 4. **Missing the verification step**: The invariant check $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ should ALWAYS be performed. KTU examiners reward this with 1 bonus mark and may penalize absence of it. > > 5. **Unit confusion**: Forgetting to convert MPa ↔ N/mm². In KTU 2024 scheme, **MPa** and **N/mm²** are numerically equal — but writing inconsistent units in the same problem loses marks. > > 6. **Confusing $\sigma_1$ vs $\sigma_2$**: $\sigma_1$ is *algebraically largest* (not "first calculated"). If $\sigma_x = 80, \sigma_y = 40, \tau_{xy} = 30$, then $\sigma_1 = 96.06 > \sigma_2 = 23.94$. Both are **tensile**. > > 7. **Sketch missing**: A clear wedge diagram with the angle $\theta$ marked is worth **at least 1–2 marks** in Part B answers — always include it. --- ### 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Stress at a point** is fully defined by **three normal stresses** ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$) and **three shear stresses** ($\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$) — total **6 independent components** in 3D (3 in 2D). - ✅ The **stress transformation equations** for an inclined plane at angle $\theta$: $$ \sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta $$ $$ \tau = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta $$ - ✅ **Principal planes**: $\tau = 0$ → $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ (gives **two values $90°$ apart**). - ✅ **Principal stresses**: $$ \sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ - ✅ **Maximum shear stress**: $$ \tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ - ✅ **Maximum shear plane orientation**: $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$ (always $45°$ rotated from principal planes). - ✅ **Normal stress on max-shear plane** = $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$. - ✅ **Invariant property**: $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (always — use as a check). - ✅ **Sign convention**: Tension positive, compression negative, CCW shear positive. - ✅ **Angle $\theta$ is measured from the $x$-axis to the OUTWARD NORMAL** of the inclined plane (not to the plane itself). - ✅ **Complementary shear stress principle**: $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ — required for moment equilibrium of the element. - ✅ **2D Mohr's circle (mental check)**: Center at $\sigma_{avg}$, radius = $\tau_{max}$; principal stresses at the circle's horizontal extremes. --- > **End of Module 1 — Topic: Stress at a Point on a Plane** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education (OBE) | NEP 2020 Aligned*

topics.title: Principal Stresses and Principal Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principal Stresses and Principal Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Principal Stresses** are the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** that occur at a point in a stressed material when the element is rotated to orientations where the **shear stress on the planes becomes zero**. These special orientations are called **Principal Planes**, and the normal stresses acting on them are called **Principal Stresses**, denoted as $\sigma_1$ (major/algebraically maximum) and $\sigma_2$ (minor/algebraically minimum).

In a **biaxial stress state**, any arbitrary plane inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the x-axis carries both a normal stress $\sigma_\theta$ and a shear stress $\tau_\theta$. As we rotate the element, these stresses change continuously. The two specific orientations where the shear stress **vanishes completely** are the **Principal Planes**, and the normal stresses acting purely on these planes (without any shear) are the **Principal Stresses**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Diamond"

Imagine a small **diamond-shaped rubber stamp** placed on a stretched rubber sheet. If you look straight down (along the x-direction), you see forces pulling in all directions. But if you slowly *rotate* the stamp, the picture of the internal forces keeps changing.

- At most rotations, the stamp experiences both a **pulling-apart force** (normal) and a **sliding force** (shear).
- However, at **two specific rotations**, the sliding force **completely disappears**, and the stamp only feels pure pulling (or pushing) along its edges.
- These two "magic" orientations are the **Principal Planes**, and the pure pulls/pushes along them are the **Principal Stresses**.

This is exactly what happens inside a beam, shaft, or pressure vessel — engineers need to find these principal stresses because materials **fail along planes of maximum stress**, not necessarily along the original x or y axes.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Highlighted)

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal stress along x | $\sigma_x$ | **N/mm² (MPa)** | Tension is positive |
| Normal stress along y | $\sigma_y$ | **N/mm² (MPa)** | Tension is positive |
| Shear stress on xy plane | $\tau_{xy}$ | **N/mm² (MPa)** | Complementary shear $\tau_{yx} = \tau_{xy}$ |
| Inclination angle | $\theta$ | **degrees (°)** or **radians (rad)** | Measured from x-axis (anticlockwise positive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard:** All stresses in this module follow the **sign convention** where **tensile normal stresses are POSITIVE** and **compressive normal stresses are NEGATIVE**. Shear stresses are positive when they act on a positive face in the **positive y-direction** (right-hand rule).

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering

Principal stress analysis is the foundation for:
- **Design of pressure vessels, boilers, and storage tanks** (finding hoop and meridional stresses).
- **Failure theories** like Maximum Normal Stress Theory, Maximum Shear Stress Theory, and Von-Mises Criterion.
- **Shaft design under combined bending and torsion** (a very common KTU question).
- **Brittle material design** (cast iron, concrete) where failure initiates on planes of maximum principal tensile stress.
- **Ductile material design** (mild steel) where failure initiates on planes of **maximum shear stress** (which occurs on planes at **45°** to the principal planes).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stress Transformation Concept (Operational Logic)

When a 2D stress element is rotated by an angle $\theta$ anticlockwise, the stresses on the new inclined faces must satisfy **static equilibrium**. The operational logic, broken into steps, is:

- **Step 1 — Section the Element:** Cut the 2D element along an inclined plane whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with the x-axis. This exposes a new pair of faces (let's call them the $\theta$-face and the $(\theta+90°)$-face).
- **Step 2 — Resolve Forces:** The original stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$ acting on the four original faces can be projected onto the inclined plane. This gives the force per unit area on the new face.
- **Step 3 — Decompose the Resultant:** The resultant stress vector on the inclined plane is split into two components:
  - A component **perpendicular** to the inclined plane → **Normal stress** $\sigma_\theta$
  - A component **parallel** to the inclined plane → **Tangential (shear) stress** $\tau_\theta$
- **Step 4 — Equate to Zero:** For a *principal plane*, the shear stress component must equal zero. Solving $\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$ (or $\tau_\theta = 0$) yields the angle of the principal plane and the value of the principal stress.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this works:** The normal stress on a plane, when plotted as a function of $\theta$, forms a **cosine wave**. Its maximum and minimum are the crests and troughs of this wave — these are precisely the principal stresses. The shear stress on the same plane forms a **sine wave**, which crosses zero exactly at the peaks and troughs of the normal stress wave.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal stress on inclined plane | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta$ | Any general inclined plane |
| 2 | Shear stress on inclined plane | $\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta$ | Companion to Formula 1 |
| 3 | **Principal Stress (Major)** | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Maximum normal stress |
| 4 | **Principal Stress (Minor)** | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Minimum normal stress |
| 5 | **Principal Plane Angle** | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Orientation of $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ |
| 6 | **Maximum Shear Stress** | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | Failure of ductile materials |
| 7 | **Plane of Max Shear** | $\tan 2\theta_s = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | Orientation of $\tau_{max}$ |
| 8 | **Normal Stress on Max Shear Plane** | $\sigma_{avg} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{2}$ | Invariant property |
| 9 | **Radius of Mohr's Circle** | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Geometric interpretation |
| 10 | **Center of Mohr's Circle** | $C = \left(\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2},\ 0\right)$ | Geometric interpretation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Invariant Property:** The values $\sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (sum of normal stresses) and $\sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2$ (a related product) **DO NOT change** with rotation of the element. This is why the center of Mohr's circle (Formula 10) is **fixed** for a given stress state.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Principal Stresses |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Shafts** | Combined bending + torsion → principal stress at the surface determines fatigue life |
| **Pressure Vessels** | Hoop and longitudinal stresses are themselves principal stresses on cylindrical surfaces |
| **Civil Beams** | At any point in a beam under bending + shear, the principal stresses determine crack direction in concrete |
| **Aerospace** | Wing spars experience combined axial + bending → principal stress analysis is critical |
| **Geotechnical** | Soil and rock masses have natural principal stress directions (e.g., in slope stability analysis) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stresses on an Inclined Plane

Consider a 2D stress element of unit thickness. Let the inclined plane have its **outward normal** making an angle $\theta$ (anticlockwise) with the **x-axis**. The plane therefore has area $A = 1 \cdot ds$ (where $ds$ is the length of the inclined face), and the other two faces have area $A \sin\theta$ and $A \cos\theta$ respectively.

### Step 1 — Force Resolution

Resolve the original stresses (acting on x-face, y-face, and the complementary shear faces) into components along the inclined plane's normal and tangent directions.

- Force on x-face (area $A\cos\theta$): has components $\sigma_x A\cos\theta$ (along x) and $\tau_{xy} A\cos\theta$ (along y).
- Force on y-face (area $A\sin\theta$): has components $\sigma_y A\sin\theta$ (along y) and $\tau_{xy} A\sin\theta$ (along x).
- Force on inclined face (area $A$): has components $\sigma_\theta A$ (normal) and $\tau_\theta A$ (tangential).

### Step 2 — Equilibrium Along the Normal Direction

Summing forces perpendicular to the inclined face and dividing by $A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Trigonometric Identities

Using the identities $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$, $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$, and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_\theta &= \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2} + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta - \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cdot 0 \ldots \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the famous KTU Formula 1** — the normal stress on any inclined plane.

### Step 4 — Equilibrium Along the Tangential Direction

Similarly, summing forces parallel to the inclined face:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_\theta &= (\sigma_y - \sigma_x)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) \\[4pt]
&= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is Formula 2** — the shear stress on any inclined plane.

## 3.2 Derivation: Principal Stress Magnitudes

The principal stresses occur when the shear stress on the plane is **zero**.

### Step 1 — Set Shear Stress to Zero

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_\theta = 0 \quad &\Rightarrow \quad -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta + \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta = 0 \\[4pt]
&\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin 2\theta = \tau_{xy}\cos 2\theta \\[4pt]
&\Rightarrow \quad \tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives **two values of $\theta_p$ separated by 90°** — corresponding to the two principal planes. **This is Formula 5.**

### Step 2 — Substitute Back Into $\sigma_\theta$

Let $\alpha = 2\theta_p$. Then $\tan\alpha = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$. This means we can construct a right triangle where the opposite side is $2\tau_{xy}$ and the adjacent side is $(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)$. The hypotenuse is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H = \sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + (2\tau_{xy})^2} = 2\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{H} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos 2\theta_p = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{H} = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Plug Into $\sigma_\theta$ Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos 2\theta_p + \tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta_p \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2}{4\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2+\tau_{xy}^2}} + \frac{2\tau_{xy}^2}{2\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x-\sigma_y}{2}\right)^2+\tau_{xy}^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Combining the two square-root terms over a common denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This derives Formulas 3 and 4** (with the $+$ sign for $\sigma_1$ and the $-$ sign for $\sigma_2$).

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Shear Stress

The maximum shear stress occurs when the rate of change of $\tau_\theta$ with respect to $\theta$ is zero.

### Step 1 — Differentiate $\tau_\theta$ With Respect to $\theta$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = -(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\theta_s$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\cos 2\theta_s &= -2\tau_{xy}\sin 2\theta_s \\[4pt]
\tan 2\theta_s &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is Formula 7.** Notice that $\tan 2\theta_s$ is the **negative reciprocal** of $\tan 2\theta_p$, which means the principal planes and max-shear planes are **rotated 45° from each other**.

### Step 3 — Magnitude of Maximum Shear Stress

Using the same trigonometric substitution technique as before:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is Formula 6.**

### Step 4 — Normal Stress on the Max-Shear Plane

Substituting $\theta_s$ back into the $\sigma_\theta$ formula (and noting that $\sin 2\theta_p$ and $\cos 2\theta_p$ are interchanged with appropriate sign changes), the $\cos 2\theta$ and $\sin 2\theta$ terms **cancel out exactly**, leaving only:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{on\ \tau_{max}\ plane} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is Formula 8** — proving that on the plane of maximum shear stress, the normal stress equals the **average of the two principal stresses**.

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

**Problem:** At a point in a stressed material, $\sigma_x = 80\ \text{MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 40\ \text{MPa}$, and $\tau_{xy} = 30\ \text{MPa}$ (positive). Determine:
1. The **principal stresses** and the **orientation** of the principal planes.
2. The **maximum shear stress** and the corresponding **normal stress**.

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Principal Plane Angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5 \\[4pt]
2\theta_p &= \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31° \\[4pt]
\theta_p &= 28.15°\quad \text{or}\quad 28.15° + 90° = 118.15°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Principal Stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} &= \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{80-40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300} = 36.06\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
\sigma_1 &= 60 + 36.06 = 96.06\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
\sigma_2 &= 60 - 36.06 = 23.94\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Maximum Shear Stress and Its Plane:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= 36.06\ \text{MPa} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{96.06 - 23.94}{2} = 36.06\ \text{MPa}\ \checkmark \\[4pt]
\tan 2\theta_s &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{40}{60} = -0.6667 \\[4pt]
2\theta_s &= -33.69°\quad \text{or}\quad 146.31° \\[4pt]
\theta_s &= -16.85°\quad \text{or}\quad 73.15°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (d) — Normal Stress on the Max-Shear Plane:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{on\ \tau_{max}\ plane} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = 60\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Verification Check:** Always verify that $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ (Invariance Property). Here, $96.06 + 23.94 = 120 = 80 + 40\ \checkmark$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Stress Transformation Process Flow

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows the **functional architecture** of how a 2D stress state transforms into principal stresses, mimicking the analytical workflow a student follows during the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Stress State at a Point] --> B[Sigma x, Sigma y, Tau xy given]
    B --> C[Compute Average Stress: sigma_avg = sigma x + sigma y over 2]
    C --> D[Compute Radius: R = sqrt of sigma x minus sigma y over 2 squared plus tau xy squared]
    D --> E[Build Mohrs Circle or Use Formula]
    E --> F[Principal Stress sigma 1 = sigma avg + R]
    E --> G[Principal Stress sigma 2 = sigma avg - R]
    E --> H[Principal Plane Angle: tan 2 theta p = 2 tau xy over sigma x minus sigma y]
    F --> I[Output: sigma 1, sigma 2, theta p]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    D --> J[Maximum Shear Stress: tau max = R]
    J --> K[Plane of Max Shear: tan 2 theta s = minus sigma x minus sigma y over 2 tau xy]
    K --> L[Output: tau max and sigma on tau max plane = sigma avg]
```

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram — Element Rotation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OriginalElement
    OriginalElement: Original Element with sigma x, sigma y, tau xy
    OriginalElement --> RotatedAtTheta: Rotate by angle theta
    RotatedAtTheta: Rotated Element with sigma theta, tau theta
    RotatedAtTheta --> PrincipalPlane: Set tau theta = 0
    PrincipalPlane: Principal Plane has only sigma 1 or sigma 2
    PrincipalPlane --> MaxShearPlane: Rotate by additional 45 degrees
    MaxShearPlane: Max Shear Plane with tau max and sigma avg
    MaxShearPlane --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mohr's Circle Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point X: sigma x, negative tau xy] --> M[Mohrs Circle Construction]
    P2[Point Y: sigma y, positive tau xy] --> M
    M --> C[Center C: sigma avg on horizontal axis]
    M --> R[Radius R: equal to tau max]
    C --> O[Origin: 0, 0]
    R --> PR[Principal Points: sigma 1 and sigma 2 on horizontal axis]
    R --> SH[Shear Points: top and bottom of circle give tau max]
    SH --> NP[Normal stress at these points equals sigma avg]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Reading Tip:** In a Mohr's Circle, the horizontal axis is the **normal stress** ($\sigma$) and the vertical axis is the **shear stress** ($\tau$). The principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ are where the circle **intersects the horizontal axis** (where shear = 0). The maximum shear stress $\tau_{max}$ is the **top or bottom** of the circle (the points farthest from the horizontal axis).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define principal stresses and principal planes. Explain why they are important in engineering design.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Principal Stresses** are the maximum and minimum values of normal stress that occur at a point in a material under load. **Principal Planes** are the specific orientations of a cross-section on which the shear stress is **zero** and only normal stress acts.

> They are important because:
> 1. Materials fail along **planes of maximum stress**, not necessarily along the x or y axes.
> 2. For **brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete), failure occurs on the plane of **maximum principal tensile stress**.
> 3. For **ductile materials** (mild steel), failure occurs on the plane of **maximum shear stress**, which is at **45°** to the principal planes.
> 4. They form the basis of all **failure theories** (Rankine, Tresca, Von-Mises) used in machine and structural design.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the mathematical expressions for principal stresses. What is the invariant property of the stress tensor?**

**Model Answer:**

The two principal stresses at a point in a 2D stress state are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\[4pt]
\sigma_2 &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Invariant Property** states that the quantities $(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ and $(\sigma_x\sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2)$ remain **constant** regardless of the orientation of the element. This is why:
- The sum of principal stresses equals the sum of normal stresses: $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$.
- The center of Mohr's circle is fixed for a given stress state.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**At a point in a structural member, the stresses on two perpendicular planes are as follows:**
- On a plane with normal along x: $\sigma_x = 120\ \text{MPa}$, $\tau_{xy} = 40\ \text{MPa}$
- On a plane with normal along y: $\sigma_y = 60\ \text{MPa}$

**Solve the following:**

#### (a) Determine the magnitudes and directions of the principal stresses. **[7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Principal Plane Angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 40}{120 - 60} = \frac{80}{60} = 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\theta_p = 53.13° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_p = 26.57° \quad \text{or} \quad \theta_p + 90° = 116.57°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Center and Radius:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{avg} &= \frac{120 + 60}{2} = 90\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - 60}{2}\right)^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{900 + 1600} = \sqrt{2500} = 50\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Principal Stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= 90 + 50 = 140\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
\sigma_2 &= 90 - 50 = 40\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (Invariance):** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 180\ \text{MPa} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y\ \checkmark$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_1 = 140\ \text{MPa}$ at $\theta_p = 26.57°$ and $\sigma_2 = 40\ \text{MPa}$ at $\theta_p = 116.57°$.

---

#### (b) Determine the magnitude and direction of the maximum shear stress. Also find the normal stress on the plane of maximum shear. **[7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} = R = 50\ \text{MPa} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{140 - 40}{2} = 50\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Plane of Maximum Shear:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_s = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{60}{80} = -0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\theta_s = -36.87° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_s = -18.43° \quad \text{or} \quad 71.57°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Normal Stress on Max Shear Plane:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{on\ \tau_{max}\ plane} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = 90\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\tau_{max} = 50\ \text{MPa}$ at $\theta_s = -18.43°$ (or $71.57°$) with normal stress = $90\ \text{MPa}$.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**The state of stress at a point in a machine component is given by: $\sigma_x = -50\ \text{MPa}$ (compressive), $\sigma_y = 30\ \text{MPa}$, and $\tau_{xy} = 20\ \text{MPa}$.**

#### (a) Calculate the principal stresses and the principal plane orientation. **[7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Principal Plane Angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 20}{-50 - 30} = \frac{40}{-80} = -0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(-0.5) = -26.57° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_p = -13.28°\ \text{or}\ 76.72°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Center and Radius:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{avg} &= \frac{-50 + 30}{2} = -10\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{-50 - 30}{2}\right)^2 + 20^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 400} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Principal Stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= -10 + 44.72 = 34.72\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
\sigma_2 &= -10 - 44.72 = -54.72\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = -20\ \text{MPa} = \sigma_x + \sigma_y\ \checkmark$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_1 = 34.72\ \text{MPa}$ at $\theta_p = -13.28°$ and $\sigma_2 = -54.72\ \text{MPa}$ at $\theta_p = 76.72°$.

---

#### (b) A steel shaft of diameter 60 mm is subjected to a bending moment of 5 kN·m and a torque of 3 kN·m simultaneously. Determine if the material (yield stress = 250 MPa) is safe using (i) Maximum Principal Stress Theory and (ii) Maximum Shear Stress Theory. **[7 Marks]**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bending Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^8}{6.786 \times 10^5} = 235.79\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Torsional Shear Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_t = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 3 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60^3} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^7}{6.786 \times 10^5} = 70.74\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Principal Stresses (on a surface element with $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$, $\sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = \tau_t$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \frac{235.79}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{235.79}{2}\right)^2 + 70.74^2} \\[4pt]
&= 117.90 + \sqrt{13899.8 + 5004.1} = 117.90 + \sqrt{18903.9} \\[4pt]
&= 117.90 + 137.49 = 255.39\ \text{MPa} \\[4pt]
\sigma_2 &= 117.90 - 137.49 = -19.59\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} = 137.49\ \text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Check Safety:**

**(i) Maximum Principal Stress Theory:** Compare $\sigma_1$ with yield stress:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 = 255.39\ \text{MPa} > 250\ \text{MPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOT SAFE (FOS = 0.98, marginal failure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Shear Stress Theory:** Compare $\tau_{max}$ with $\sigma_y/2$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} = 137.49\ \text{MPa} < \frac{250}{2} = 125\ \text{MPa} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NOT SAFE (exceeds yield shear)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The shaft is **NOT SAFE** under both criteria. A larger diameter is needed.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign error in $\sigma_x - \sigma_y$:** When $\sigma_x < \sigma_y$ (as in Question B), the denominator becomes **negative**, making $\tan 2\theta_p$ negative. Students often forget to handle the **second quadrant** angle correctly.
> 2. **Forgetting the +90° second solution:** The equation $\tan 2\theta_p = k$ gives only ONE value of $2\theta_p$ in the principal range, but the principal plane angle has **TWO solutions separated by 90°** because both $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ act on different principal planes. Marks are awarded for both.
> 3. **Mixing up degrees and radians in $\tan 2\theta$:** The formula uses **$2\theta$**, not $\theta$. Students frequently write $\tan\theta = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ — this is a **1-mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 4. **Not stating units:** Every numerical answer MUST carry units (**MPa** or **N/mm²**). A correct number without units is penalized **0.5 marks** in strict valuation.
> 5. **Confusing which angle is for which plane:** If $\tau_{xy}$ is positive, the **minor** principal stress direction is at $\theta_p$, and the **major** principal stress direction is at $\theta_p + 90°$ (or check by substitution). Many students swap these.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Principal Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$

- **Principal Plane Angle:** $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ — gives **two values 90° apart**.

- **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ — equal to the radius of Mohr's circle.

- **Plane of Max Shear:** Always **45° away** from the principal planes (since $\tan 2\theta_s = -\cot 2\theta_p$).

- **Normal Stress on Max Shear Plane:** Always $\dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{2}$ (the invariant center).

- **Invariance Check:** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ — use this to **verify every calculation**.

- **Sign Convention:** Tensile stresses are **positive**, compressive are **negative**, shear is positive by right-hand rule.

- **Order of Principal Stresses:** $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2$ always (algebraically, in 2D). In 3D, we have $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$.

- **When $\sigma_x = \sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy} = 0$:** The element is in **uniform biaxial stress** — every plane is a principal plane, and $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma_x = \sigma_y$.

- **When $\tau_{xy} = 0$:** The x and y faces **are already** principal planes. $\theta_p = 0°$ or $90°$. $\sigma_1 = \sigma_x$ (if $\sigma_x > \sigma_y$) and $\sigma_2 = \sigma_y$.

- **Mohr's Circle Quick Construction:** Plot $(\sigma_x, -\tau_{xy})$ and $(\sigma_y, +\tau_{xy})$ as diameter endpoints. Center is at $\left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$. Radius is the maximum shear stress.

- **Pure Shear Special Case:** If $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0$ and only $\tau_{xy}$ exists, then $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$, with principal planes at **45°** to the original axes. This is a **favorite KTU question**.

- **Units Check:** Stresses in **MPa = N/mm²**, moments in **N·mm**, torques in **N·mm**, lengths in **mm** — keep consistent throughout.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mohr's Circle of Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mohr's Circle of Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Mohr's Circle of Stress** is a two-dimensional graphical representation devised by the German civil engineer **Christian Otto Mohr (1882)** that completely describes the **transformation of plane stress** under rotation of the reference axes. It is essentially a geometric plot of the **normal stress (σ)** versus the **shear stress (τ)** for every possible orientation of a stress element in 2D, with the diameter equal to the **Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress (τ_max)**.

The stress state at any point is defined by three independent components in 2D: **σ_x**, **σ_y**, and **τ_xy**, with **τ_yx = τ_xy** (complementary shear). Mohr's circle provides an elegant visual and analytical tool to determine the **principal stresses (σ₁, σ₂)**, the **maximum shear stress (τ_max)**, and the **stress on any arbitrarily inclined plane (σ_n, τ_n)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Mohr's circle is a **High-Yield Module-1 Topic** that appears almost every semester in the University ESE. Master the sign convention, the rotation rule (2θ on circle = θ on element), and the formula-based solution method (the graphical method is rarely credited with full marks).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **rotating ball inside a bowl** (a perfect circle representing the stress state). The ball always stays on the rim, and as you spin the bowl, the ball traces the circumference. Every position of the ball corresponds to a specific orientation of a stress element in the material. 

Now think of a **hula hoop held at an angle** in front of a light. The shadow the hoop casts on a wall changes its elliptical shape depending on the angle — but the hoop itself remains rigid and circular. Mohr's circle is the rigid "hoop," and as the element rotates in physical space, the stress point traces this immutable circle.

- If you **squeeze the ball from left and right** (uniaxial loading), the circle shifts to the right.
- If you **twist the ball** (pure shear), the circle is centered exactly at the origin.
- The **topmost and bottommost points** of the circle correspond to the state of **pure normal stress (no shear)** — these are the **principal stresses**.

### Physical Constants / Standard Metrics
- **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** **Tensile stress is POSITIVE (+ve)**, **Compressive stress is NEGATIVE (-ve)**. Shear stress producing **clockwise rotation of the element is POSITIVE**.
- **Stress Units:** **N/m² (Pa)**, kN/m², MPa (10⁶ Pa), or GPa (10⁹ Pa).
- **Default Plane Assumption:** 2D **Plane Stress** state (σ_z = 0, τ_xz = τ_yz = 0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Mohr's Circle important?**
> Because materials don't know about our coordinate axes. The stress inside a steel beam doesn't care whether you call the horizontal direction "x" or "y". Mohr's circle removes the coordinate-dependence and lets us find the **absolute maximum and minimum stresses** the material actually experiences.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Stress Transformation Equations
Starting from a 2D stress element with **σ_x, σ_y, τ_xy**, the stresses on a plane inclined at angle **θ** (measured counter-clockwise from the x-axis) are:

- **Normal Stress on inclined plane:**
  $$\sigma_n = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

- **Shear Stress on inclined plane:**
  $$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

### 2.2 The Underlying Logic — Why Does It Work?
- The **first term** $\left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)$ is the **center of Mohr's circle** — it represents the **mean stress** which is invariant under rotation.
- The **second and third terms** are **circular functions** of 2θ. When we plot σ_n vs τ_n, they trace a **perfect circle** because of the identity $\cos^2(2\theta) + \sin^2(2\theta) = 1$.
- The **radius** of the circle is the **Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress**.

### 2.3 Principal Stresses and Principal Plane
**Principal stresses** are the **normal stresses on planes where shear stress is zero**. These are the **extremes** of the circle along the σ-axis.

- **Principal Stress Magnitudes:**
  $$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

- **Principal Plane Angle (θ_p):**
  $$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

- **Two-fold ambiguity:** The angle 2θ_p gives two solutions 90° apart on the circle, corresponding to the σ₁ and σ₂ planes.

### 2.4 Maximum Shear Stress
- **Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress:**
  $$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$
- **Normal stress on the plane of maximum shear:**
  $$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$$
- **Angle to maximum shear plane:** 2θ_s = 2θ_p + 90° (i.e., 45° away from the principal plane).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Location on Mohr's Circle** | **Units** |
|--------------|-------------|-------------------------------|-----------|
| Center of Circle (C) | $C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | Origin of circle on σ-axis | Pa / MPa |
| Radius of Circle (R) | $R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Distance from C to any point | Pa / MPa |
| Major Principal Stress (σ₁) | $\sigma_{avg} + R$ | Right-most intercept on σ-axis | Pa / MPa |
| Minor Principal Stress (σ₂) | $\sigma_{avg} - R$ | Left-most intercept on σ-axis | Pa / MPa |
| Maximum Shear Stress (τ_max) | $R$ | Top-most / bottom-most point on circle | Pa / MPa |
| Principal Angle | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Point where circle crosses σ-axis | radians / degrees |
| Stress on Inclined Plane | $\sigma_n, \tau_n$ from transformation eqns. | Any point on circle rim | Pa / MPa |
| Rotation Rule | **2θ on circle = θ on element** | Geometric — same direction (CCW) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Mohr's circle is the daily workhorse of **structural engineers** (finding principal stresses in beams, columns, pressure vessels), **geotechnical engineers** (analyzing soil failure using the Mohr-Coulomb criterion), **aerospace engineers** (analyzing stress in wing spars), and **mechanical engineers** (designing shafts under combined torsion and bending).

### 2.6 Mohr's Circle — Element Rotation vs Circle Rotation
- To find stresses on a plane rotated by **θ** counter-clockwise on the physical element, **rotate 2θ counter-clockwise** about the center on Mohr's circle.
- This **2:1 ratio** is the most important rule in Mohr's circle. **Rotation on the circle is in the SAME sense as the physical rotation.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Stress Transformation Equations (from Equilibrium)

Consider a 2D stress element with stresses **σ_x, σ_y, τ_xy** acting on its faces. Now consider a wedge-shaped element cut by a plane at angle **θ** to the x-axis (CCW). Let the inclined plane have:
- Outward normal **n** at angle **θ**
- Tangent direction **t** at angle **(θ + 90°)**

The wedge has three faces: (1) original x-face, (2) original y-face, (3) the inclined cut face. The forces must be in equilibrium.

**Free Body Diagram Setup:**
- On the x-face (area = dA · cos θ): forces are σ_x · (dA cos θ) and τ_xy · (dA cos θ).
- On the y-face (area = dA · sin θ): forces are σ_y · (dA sin θ) and τ_xy · (dA sin θ).
- On the inclined face (area = dA): forces are σ_n · dA and τ_n · dA.

**Equilibrium Along the Normal Direction (n-axis):**
Sum of forces in n-direction = 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n \, dA &= \sigma_x (dA \cos\theta)\cos\theta + \sigma_y (dA \sin\theta)\sin\theta \\
&\quad + \tau_{xy}(dA \cos\theta)\sin\theta + \tau_{xy}(dA \sin\theta)\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by **dA**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the double-angle identities:
- $\cos^2\theta = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$
- $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$
- $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \sigma_x \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2} + \sigma_y \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2} + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta) \\
\sigma_n &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the Normal Stress Transformation Equation. ∎**

**Equilibrium Along the Tangent Direction (t-axis):**
Sum of forces in t-direction = 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n \, dA &= \sigma_x (dA \cos\theta)\sin\theta - \sigma_y (dA \sin\theta)\cos\theta \\
&\quad - \tau_{xy}(dA \cos\theta)\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(dA \sin\theta)\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing throughout by **dA**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n &= (\sigma_x - \sigma_y)\sin\theta\cos\theta - \tau_{xy}(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) \\
\tau_n &= \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) - \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the Shear Stress Transformation Equation. ∎**

### 3.2 Proof That Mohr's Circle is Indeed a Circle

The parametric equations of σ_n and τ_n both have a constant term plus sinusoidal terms. To prove the locus is circular, isolate the trigonometric terms and apply the Pythagorean identity.

**Step 1:** Define $C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ and $R_{\sigma} = \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}$.

The normal stress equation becomes:
$$\sigma_n - C = R_{\sigma}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

**Step 2:** Square both the (σ_n - C) and τ_n terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\sigma_n - C)^2 &= \left[R_{\sigma}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)\right]^2 \\
(\sigma_n - C)^2 &= R_{\sigma}^2\cos^2(2\theta) + 2R_{\sigma}\tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}^2\sin^2(2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_n^2 &= \left[-R_{\sigma}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)\right]^2 \\
\tau_n^2 &= R_{\sigma}^2\sin^2(2\theta) - 2R_{\sigma}\tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}^2\cos^2(2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Add the two equations. The cross terms cancel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\sigma_n - C)^2 + \tau_n^2 &= R_{\sigma}^2[\cos^2(2\theta) + \sin^2(2\theta)] + \tau_{xy}^2[\sin^2(2\theta) + \cos^2(2\theta)] \\
(\sigma_n - C)^2 + \tau_n^2 &= R_{\sigma}^2 + \tau_{xy}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The right-hand side is a **constant** (independent of θ). Defining $R^2 = R_{\sigma}^2 + \tau_{xy}^2$:

$$(\sigma_n - C)^2 + \tau_n^2 = R^2$$

This is the **standard equation of a circle** with:
- **Center:** $(C, 0) = \left(\frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}, 0\right)$
- **Radius:** $R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$

**The locus is a perfect circle. ∎**

### 3.3 Derivation of Principal Stresses

Principal stresses occur where **τ_n = 0**. From the shear transformation equation:

$$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) &= \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) \\
\tan(2\theta_p) &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute this 2θ_p back into the normal stress equation. Using the identity: $A\cos\phi + B\sin\phi = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}\cos(\phi - \alpha)$ where $\tan\alpha = B/A$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= C + \sqrt{R_{\sigma}^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}\cos(2\theta - 2\theta_p) \\
\sigma_n &= C \pm R \\
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**This is the Principal Stress Formula. ∎**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 2D stress element has **σ_x = 80 MPa**, **σ_y = 40 MPa**, and **τ_xy = 30 MPa** (positive, i.e., creating clockwise rotation on the right face). Find:
1. Center and Radius of Mohr's circle
2. Principal stresses σ₁, σ₂
3. Principal plane angle θ_p
4. Maximum shear stress τ_max
5. Normal and shear stress on a plane at θ = 30°

**Step 1 — Center and Radius:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa} \\
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300} \\
R &\approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Principal Stresses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2 &= 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Principal Plane Angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(2\theta_p) &= \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5 \\
2\theta_p &= \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31° \\
\theta_p &= 28.15° \quad \text{(counter-clockwise from x-axis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The other principal plane is at 28.15° + 90° = 118.15°.

**Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

The plane of maximum shear is at θ_s = 28.15° + 45° = 73.15° from the x-axis.

**Step 5 — Stresses at θ = 30°:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2\theta &= 60° \\
\sigma_n &= 60 + 20\cos(60°) + 30\sin(60°) \\
\sigma_n &= 60 + 20(0.5) + 30(0.866) = 60 + 10 + 25.98 = 95.98 \text{ MPa} \\
\tau_n &= -20\sin(60°) + 30\cos(60°) \\
\tau_n &= -20(0.866) + 30(0.5) = -17.32 + 15 = -2.32 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** Notice that σ_n ≈ 95.98 MPa is very close to σ₁ = 96.06 MPa. This makes sense because θ = 30° is very close to the principal plane angle θ_p = 28.15°. As we approach the principal plane, the normal stress approaches the principal value and the shear stress approaches zero.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mohr's Circle — Geometric Architecture Flow

The following block diagram maps the **interactions between the physical element, the Mohr's circle, and the analytical computations**. This is a functional flow rather than a literal drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT STRESS ELEMENT"] --> B["STRESS COMPONENTS sx sy txy"]
    B --> C["COMPUTE CENTER C"]
    B --> D["COMPUTE RADIUS R"]
    C --> E["PLOT CIRCLE ON s-t AXES"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["MARK REFERENCE POINT X"]
    E --> G["MARK REFERENCE POINT Y"]
    F --> H["FIND PRINCIPAL POINT SIGMA1"]
    G --> I["FIND PRINCIPAL POINT SIGMA2"]
    H --> J["MEASURE 2 THETA p FROM X"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["ROTATE 2 THETA CCW ON CIRCLE"]
    K --> L["READ NEW COORDINATES AT POINT P"]
    L --> M["OUTPUT STRESSES ON INCLINED PLANE"]
    M --> N["SIGMA n AND TAU n VALUES"]
    H --> O["MAXIMUM SHEAR POINT AT TOP OF CIRCLE"]
    O --> P["READ TAU max AS RADIUS VALUE"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow for Solving KTU Mohr's Circle Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START - READ GIVEN sx sy txy"] --> S2["IS THE PROBLEM 2D OR 3D"]
    S2 --> S3["2D PLANE STRESS"]
    S2 --> S4["3D USE 3D MOHR CIRCLE"]
    S3 --> S5["CALCULATE CENTER C"]
    S5 --> S6["CALCULATE RADIUS R"]
    S6 --> S7["WHAT IS ASKED"]
    S7 --> S8["PRINCIPAL STRESSES"]
    S7 --> S9["MAXIMUM SHEAR"]
    S7 --> S10["STRESS ON GIVEN PLANE"]
    S8 --> S11["USE SIGMA avg PLUS MINUS R"]
    S9 --> S12["USE TAU max EQUALS R"]
    S10 --> S13["USE TRANSFORMATION EQUATIONS"]
    S11 --> S14["FINAL ANSWER"]
    S12 --> S14
    S13 --> S14
```

### 4.3 Sign Convention Reference Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["TENSION POSITIVE"] --> A2["COMPRESSION NEGATIVE"]
    B1["SHEAR CAUSING CW ROTATION ON RIGHT FACE POSITIVE"] --> B2["SHEAR CAUSING CCW ROTATION ON RIGHT FACE NEGATIVE"]
    C1["CCW ANGLE FROM x-AXIS POSITIVE"] --> C2["CW ANGLE FROM x-AXIS NEGATIVE"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first flow chart shows the **algorithmic path** from input stress values to the final Mohr's circle plot. The second flow chart is a **decision tree** a KTU student should mentally walk through when solving an exam problem. The third diagram encapsulates the **sign convention rules** — the #1 place KTU students lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define principal stresses. Derive the expression for the principal stresses in a 2D stress system.*

**Model Answer:**
Principal stresses are the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** acting on planes where the **shear stress is zero**. These planes are called **principal planes** and the corresponding directions are **principal directions**.

**Derivation:**
At principal planes, τ_n = 0. From the shear stress transformation equation:
$$\tau_n = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) = 0$$

Rearranging: $\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$

Substituting this 2θ_p into the normal stress equation and using $A\cos\phi + B\sin\phi = \sqrt{A^2+B^2}\cos(\phi-\alpha)$:
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*What is the radius of Mohr's circle? Explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer:**
The **radius of Mohr's circle (R)** represents the **Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress (τ_max)** experienced by the material at that point.

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- R = τ_max is the **largest magnitude of shear stress** the element can experience as it rotates.
- It corresponds to the **top and bottom points** of Mohr's circle.
- The plane of maximum shear is oriented at **45° to the principal planes**.
- The **normal stress on the maximum shear plane equals the average stress** $\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$.
- This is critical for **ductile material failure** (Tresca and von Mises criteria).

---

### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE full question from each Module Internal Choice. We provide Question A and Question B for the student's selection.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** For a 2D stress element with **σ_x = 120 MPa, σ_y = -40 MPa, τ_xy = 50 MPa** (positive), determine:
- (i) The principal stresses and principal plane angle. **(4 marks)**
- (ii) The maximum in-plane shear stress and the orientation of its plane. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Find the **normal and shear stress on a plane inclined at θ = 40°** counter-clockwise from the x-axis. Also verify your result using the **geometric construction of Mohr's circle**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i) — Principal Stresses and Principal Plane:**

Given: σ_x = 120 MPa, σ_y = -40 MPa, τ_xy = 50 MPa.

**Step 1:** Calculate center and radius:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{120 + (-40)}{2} = 40 \text{ MPa} \\
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + 50^2} = \sqrt{80^2 + 50^2} = \sqrt{6400 + 2500} = \sqrt{8900} \\
R &\approx 94.34 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Principal stresses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= 40 + 94.34 = 134.34 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2 &= 40 - 94.34 = -54.34 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Principal plane angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(2\theta_p) &= \frac{2 \times 50}{120 - (-40)} = \frac{100}{160} = 0.625 \\
2\theta_p &= 32.00° \\
\theta_p &= 16.00° \quad \text{(CCW from x-axis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The second principal plane is at 16.00° + 90° = 106.00°.

**Part (a)(ii) — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = R \approx 94.34 \text{ MPa}$$

Orientation of maximum shear plane:
$$\theta_s = \theta_p + 45° = 16.00° + 45° = 61.00°$$

---

**Part (b) — Stresses on Plane at θ = 40°:**

Using transformation equations with 2θ = 80°:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \frac{120 + (-40)}{2} + \frac{120 - (-40)}{2}\cos(80°) + 50\sin(80°) \\
\sigma_n &= 40 + 80(0.1736) + 50(0.9848) \\
\sigma_n &= 40 + 13.89 + 49.24 = 103.13 \text{ MPa} \\
\\
\tau_n &= -\frac{120 - (-40)}{2}\sin(80°) + 50\cos(80°) \\
\tau_n &= -80(0.9848) + 50(0.1736) \\
\tau_n &= -78.79 + 8.68 = -70.11 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Geometric Verification (Mohr's Circle Construction):**

**Step 1:** Plot center C = (40, 0) on σ-τ plane.
**Step 2:** Plot point X = (σ_x, τ_xy) = (120, 50). [Note: τ_xy is positive, so plot above σ-axis]
**Step 3:** Plot point Y = (σ_y, -τ_xy) = (-40, -50). [τ_yx is opposite to τ_xy]
**Step 4:** Verify center = midpoint of XY = ((120-40)/2, (50-50)/2) = (40, 0) ✓
**Step 5:** Verify radius = distance from C to X = √((120-40)² + 50²) = √(6400+2500) = 94.34 MPa ✓
**Step 6:** Principal points: σ₁ = 40 + 94.34 = 134.34 MPa, σ₂ = 40 - 94.34 = -54.34 MPa ✓
**Step 7:** Angle 2θ_p on circle = tan⁻¹(50/80) = 32.00° CCW from CX. So θ_p = 16.00° on element ✓
**Step 8:** For θ = 40° on element, rotate 2θ = 80° CCW from X on circle.
**Step 9:** New point P coordinates:
   - σ_n = 40 + 94.34·cos(80° - 32°) = 40 + 94.34·cos(48°) = 40 + 94.34·0.669 = 40 + 63.11 = 103.11 MPa ✓
   - τ_n = 94.34·sin(80° - 32°) = 94.34·sin(48°) = 94.34·0.743 = 70.09 MPa (magnitude matches ✓)

**Result:** σ_n ≈ 103.13 MPa, τ_n ≈ -70.11 MPa. The slight sign convention difference in τ_n is due to the KTU standard convention (τ_n is negative here because it acts in the CCW sense on the cut plane). **Both methods agree. ∎**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** A point in a structural member is subjected to stresses **σ_x = 60 MPa, σ_y = 20 MPa, τ_xy = -25 MPa**. Determine the **stresses on a plane whose normal makes 30° clockwise with the x-axis**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same stress state, find the **principal stresses, the maximum shear stress, and the angle between the principal plane and the plane of maximum shear stress**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stresses on Plane at θ = -30° (clockwise):**

Given: σ_x = 60 MPa, σ_y = 20 MPa, τ_xy = -25 MPa. Plane angle θ = -30°, so 2θ = -60°.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_n &= \frac{60 + 20}{2} + \frac{60 - 20}{2}\cos(-60°) + (-25)\sin(-60°) \\
\sigma_n &= 40 + 20(0.5) + (-25)(-0.866) \\
\sigma_n &= 40 + 10 + 21.65 = 71.65 \text{ MPa} \\
\\
\tau_n &= -\frac{60 - 20}{2}\sin(-60°) + (-25)\cos(-60°) \\
\tau_n &= -20(-0.866) + (-25)(0.5) \\
\tau_n &= 17.32 - 12.50 = 4.82 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** σ_n = **71.65 MPa**, τ_n = **4.82 MPa** on the inclined plane.

---

**Part (b) — Principal Stresses, Maximum Shear, and Angle:**

**Step 1:** Center and radius:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{60 + 20}{2} = 40 \text{ MPa} \\
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{60 - 20}{2}\right)^2 + (-25)^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 25^2} = \sqrt{400 + 625} = \sqrt{1025} \\
R &\approx 32.02 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Principal stresses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= 40 + 32.02 = 72.02 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2 &= 40 - 32.02 = 7.98 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum shear stress:
$$\tau_{max} = 32.02 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Principal plane angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(2\theta_p) &= \frac{2 \times (-25)}{60 - 20} = \frac{-50}{40} = -1.25 \\
2\theta_p &= \tan^{-1}(-1.25) = -51.34° \\
\theta_p &= -25.67° \quad \text{(i.e., 25.67° CW from x-axis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Angle between principal plane and maximum shear plane:
$$\theta_s - \theta_p = (\theta_p + 45°) - \theta_p = 45°$$

The plane of **maximum shear is always 45° away from the principal plane**, by definition.

**Answer Summary:** σ₁ = 72.02 MPa, σ₂ = 7.98 MPa, τ_max = 32.02 MPa, θ_p = -25.67°, and the **angle between the principal plane and maximum shear plane is exactly 45°**. ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** The most common error is forgetting the sign of τ_xy. If τ_xy is given as negative, students blindly use it as positive — **this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss**.
> 2. **2θ vs θ Confusion:** The rotation on the circle is **2θ**, not θ. Students who rotate by 30° on the circle instead of 60° will get a wrong answer. **Write "2θ" explicitly in your solution.**
> 3. **Principal Angle Ambiguity:** $\tan(2\theta_p)$ gives two values 180° apart. **Always check which value gives σ₁** by substituting back into the transformation equation. Don't just write one answer.
> 4. **Units Inconsistency:** Mixing MPa and kPa in the same calculation = **strict 1-mark penalty** as per KTU rubric.
> 5. **Missing Average Stress at Max Shear:** Many students forget to mention that **σ_n = σ_avg** on the maximum shear plane. The 3-mark sub-question will be considered incomplete.
> 6. **Order of σ₁ and σ₂:** KTU strictly requires **σ₁ ≥ σ₂** (algebraic, not absolute). State this clearly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Mohr's Circle Definition:** 2D graphical plot of normal stress (x-axis) vs shear stress (y-axis), representing the locus of all possible stress states at a point as the element rotates.
- [ ] **Center C:** $C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ — lies on the σ-axis, invariant under rotation.
- [ ] **Radius R:** $R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ — equals τ_max.
- [ ] **Principal Stresses:** $\sigma_{1,2} = C \pm R$ — read at the **right and left intercepts** on the σ-axis (where τ = 0).
- [ ] **Principal Plane Angle:** $\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ — measured CCW from x-axis.
- [ ] **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = R$ — occurs at **top and bottom** of the circle.
- [ ] **Maximum Shear Plane Angle:** $\theta_s = \theta_p \pm 45°$ — exactly 45° away from the principal plane.
- [ ] **Average Stress at Max Shear:** $\sigma_{avg} = C$ — the normal stress on the τ_max plane.
- [ ] **Rotation Rule:** **2θ on the circle = θ on the element**, in the **same sense (CCW → CCW, CW → CW)**.
- [ ] **Sign Convention:** **Tensile +ve**, **Compressive -ve**, **Shear causing CW rotation on the right face = +ve**.
- [ ] **Plotting Rule:** Point X = (σ_x, τ_xy) and Point Y = (σ_y, -τ_xy) — the y-coordinate of Y is **negative** of τ_xy.
- [ ] **Construction Check:** The center of Mohr's circle is the **midpoint of XY** — always verify this in exam.
- [ ] **Real-World Use:** Mohr's circle is the foundation of **failure theories** (Tresca, von Mises, Mohr-Coulomb) used in machine design, structural analysis, and geotechnical engineering.
- [ ] **Master both methods:** **Analytical (transformation equations)** — guaranteed full marks. **Graphical (Mohr's circle drawing)** — supplementary, only 2-3 marks credited typically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Stress and Shear Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Shear Stress and Shear Strain # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Shear Stress **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** is the component of stress that acts *tangentially* (parallel) to the cross-sectional area of a body, as opposed to normal stress which acts perpendicular to the surface. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of the tangential (shearing) force to the area over which it acts. $$\tau = \dfrac{F_t}{A}$$ where: - $F_t$ = Tangential (shearing) force applied parallel to the surface, measured in **Newtons (N)** - $A$ = Cross-sectional area resisting the shear, measured in **m² (mm²)** - $\tau$ = Shear stress, measured in **N/m² (Pa)** or **N/mm² (MPa)** > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Shear stress is a *tangential stress* that always occurs in *pairs* on mutually perpendicular planes (complementary shear stresses), unlike normal stress which acts on a single plane. ## 1.2 Formal Definition of Shear Strain **Shear Strain ($\gamma$)** is the angular deformation (change in angle) at a point within a body, originally characterized by a right angle. For small deformations, it equals the tangent of the change in angle between two originally perpendicular line elements. $$\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L} = \tan \theta \approx \theta \text{ (for small angles in radians)}$$ where: - $\Delta x$ = Relative tangential displacement between two parallel planes - $L$ = Perpendicular distance between the two planes - $\theta$ = Angle of deformation (in radians) - $\gamma$ = Shear strain (dimensionless, often expressed in radians) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Distinction:** Shear strain is *dimensionless* (like all strains), but because it represents an angle, it is often expressed in **radians**. For small strains, $\tan \theta \approx \theta$ (radians). ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy **Imagine a thick deck of playing cards resting on a table.** If you place your palm flat on top and push it horizontally while the bottom of the deck remains fixed to the table, the cards will *slide* relative to one another in a staggered, cascading pattern. The horizontal force you apply is the **shear force**, the friction between the cards is the **resisting shear stress**, and the *tilting* of each individual card from its original vertical position represents the **shear strain** (the small angle $\theta$ each card makes with the vertical). A more engineering-friendly example is a **rivet joining two plates**. When the two plates try to slide past each other, the rivet resists this sliding by developing shear stress across its cross-section. If the rivet shears off, it has exceeded its ultimate shear strength. ## 1.4 Pure Shear vs. Simple Shear | State | Description | Physical Example | |-------|-------------|------------------| | **Pure Shear** | A state of stress where the only non-zero stresses on an element are the shear stresses $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, with all normal stresses equal to zero | Torsion of a thin-walled tube | | **Simple Shear** | A deformation where one plane is displaced parallel to a fixed parallel plane (similar to pure shear in stress terms, but emphasizing kinematic aspect) | Card deck sliding analogy | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Pure shear is actually a special case of biaxial stress. If we rotate a pure shear element by **45°**, the shear stresses vanish and equal magnitude *tensile* and *compressive* normal stresses appear. This is a key result for KTU problems. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hooke's Law in Shear (Generalized Form) Within the **elastic limit**, the shear stress is directly proportional to the shear strain. This linear relationship is the shear analogue of Hooke's Law and is fundamental to solving KTU problems. $$\boxed{\tau = G \cdot \gamma}$$ The constant of proportionality **$G$** is called the **Modulus of Rigidity** (also called **Shear Modulus** or **Modulus of Elasticity in Shear**). ### Physical Meaning of $G$: - Represents the material's **resistance to shape distortion** while preserving volume. - A high $G$ means the material is "stiff" against shearing deformations (e.g., steel has a high $G$, rubber has a low $G$). - Units: **N/m² (Pa)** or **N/mm² (MPa)** — same as other moduli of elasticity. ## 2.2 Relationship Between Elastic Constants For a **homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic material**, only **two independent elastic constants** are needed. The relationships are: $$G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$ $$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$ $$E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K + G}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{E} + \dfrac{\nu}{E} = \dfrac{1}{3K} + \dfrac{1}{2G} \cdot (\text{dimensionless consistency})$$ where: - $E$ = Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity) - $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus) - $K$ = Bulk Modulus - $\nu$ = Poisson's Ratio > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Relationship:** The formula $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ is the most frequently tested relationship. Note that since $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$, the ratio $E/G$ lies between **2.5 and 3.0** for all engineering materials. ## 2.3 Complementary Shear Stresses (Theorem of Conjugate Shear Stresses) A cornerstone principle in KTU examinations: **The shear stresses on two perpendicular planes, acting along their line of intersection, are equal in magnitude.** Mathematically: $$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$$ This is derived from the **equilibrium of moments** on an infinitesimally small element. If this were not true, the element would experience a net moment and rotate indefinitely, violating equilibrium. ## 2.4 Stress Transformation for Pure Shear For a state of **pure shear** (no normal stresses, only $\tau_{xy}$ acting), the stresses on a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the x-axis are: $$\sigma_{\theta} = \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta$$ $$\tau_{\theta} = \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta$$ **Principal Stresses** (maximum normal stresses) occur at $\theta = 45°$: $$\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy} \quad \text{(tensile)}$$ $$\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy} \quad \text{(compressive)}$$ **Maximum Shear Stress** occurs at $\theta = 0°$ or $\theta = 90°$: $$\tau_{max} = \tau_{xy}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *maximum shear stress* in a pure shear state. In pure shear, the maximum shear stress on the original element equals $\tau_{xy}$ itself — the same value. Only when the element is rotated by 45° does the shear become zero and pure normal stresses appear. ## 2.5 Shear Strain Energy (Strain Energy Density) The strain energy stored per unit volume in a material subjected to pure shear is: $$u = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G} = \dfrac{G\gamma^2}{2} = \dfrac{\tau \cdot \gamma}{2}$$ Units: **N·m/m³ = J/m³** (energy per unit volume) ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{F_t}{A}$ | Definition of shear stress | N/m² | | 2 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L} \approx \theta$ | Definition of shear strain (radians) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$ | Hooke's Law in shear | N/m² | | 4 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Relation between G, E, and $\nu$ | N/m² | | 5 | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Relation between E, K, and $\nu$ | N/m² | | 6 | $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ | Complementary shear stresses | N/m² | | 7 | $\sigma_{\theta} = \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta$ | Normal stress on inclined plane (pure shear) | N/m² | | 8 | $\tau_{\theta} = \tau_{xy} \cos 2\theta$ | Shear stress on inclined plane (pure shear) | N/m² | | 9 | $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$ | Principal stresses in pure shear | N/m² | | 10 | $u = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G}$ | Shear strain energy density | J/m³ | | 11 | $\sigma_{45°} = \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \pm \tau_{xy}$ | General plane stress transformation | N/m² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **For Tables:** All formulas are valid in standard SI units. The vertical pipe symbol inside absolute values should be rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The concept of shear stress and shear strain is **foundational** to almost every branch of structural and mechanical engineering: - **Design of fasteners (rivets, bolts, pins):** Calculating the number of rivets required to prevent shear failure of a joint. - **Torsion of shafts:** The torsion formula $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ directly uses shear modulus $G$. - **Beams in bending:** Transverse shear stress distribution in beams is governed by shear. - **Geotechnical engineering:** Shear strength of soils (Mohr-Coulomb theory) is critical for slope stability and foundation design. - **Punching and blanking operations in manufacturing:** Shear failure governs the design of punches and dies. - **Seismic engineering:** Shear waves (S-waves) propagate through the Earth with a speed determined by $G$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Complementary Shear Stresses are Equal Consider an infinitesimal element of dimensions $dx \times dy \times 1$ (unit depth perpendicular to page). Let $\tau_{xy}$ act on the x-face (positive x direction) and $\tau_{yx}$ act on the y-face (positive y direction). **Step 1:** Identify the forces on each face. On the x-face (area = $dy \times 1$): Force = $\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot 1$ acting in the **+y direction**. On the y-face (area = $dx \times 1$): Force = $\tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot 1$ acting in the **+x direction**. **Step 2:** Apply rotational equilibrium about the bottom-left corner of the element (let's call it point O). The element will not be in static equilibrium unless the sum of moments about any point is zero. $$\sum M_O = 0$$ **Step 3:** Take moments of all four shear forces about point O. - $\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot 1$ on the top face acts at a distance $dx$ from O → Moment = $+\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot dx$ (counterclockwise) - $\tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot 1$ on the right face acts at a distance $dy$ from O → Moment = $-\tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy$ (clockwise) - The two forces on the bottom and left faces pass through O → Moment = 0 **Step 4:** Write the equilibrium equation. $$\tau_{xy} \cdot dy \cdot dx - \tau_{yx} \cdot dx \cdot dy = 0$$ **Step 5:** Factor and simplify. $$\begin{aligned} (\tau_{xy} - \tau_{yx}) \cdot dx \cdot dy &= 0 \\ \text{Since } dx \cdot dy &\neq 0 \\ \tau_{xy} - \tau_{yx} &= 0 \\ \therefore \quad \tau_{xy} &= \tau_{yx} \end{aligned}$$ This is the **Theorem of Complementary Shear Stresses**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Stress Transformation in Pure Shear Consider a pure shear state with only $\tau_{xy}$ acting on the x and y faces. We want to find the stresses on a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the vertical y-axis (i.e., the cut plane has its normal at angle $\theta$ from x-axis). **Step 1:** Draw the free body diagram of a wedge cut from the element. The wedge has three faces: - Face AB: Inclined face with area $A$ and unit outward normal at angle $\theta$ from x-axis. - Face AC (vertical): Area $A \sin\theta$ with shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ acting on it. - Face BC (horizontal): Area $A \cos\theta$ with shear stress $\tau_{yx}$ acting on it. **Step 2:** Resolve the inclined face area into components. Vertical face area: $A_x = A \sin\theta$ Horizontal face area: $A_y = A \cos\theta$ **Step 3:** Apply force equilibrium in the **n-direction** (normal to the inclined cut). $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\theta} \cdot A &= \tau_{yx} \cdot (A \cos\theta) \cdot \sin\theta + \tau_{xy} \cdot (A \sin\theta) \cdot \cos\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Use $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx} = \tau$ and simplify. $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{\theta} \cdot A &= \tau \cdot A \cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta + \tau \cdot A \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta \\ \sigma_{\theta} \cdot A &= 2\tau A \sin\theta \cos\theta \\ \sigma_{\theta} &= 2\tau \sin\theta \cos\theta \\ \sigma_{\theta} &= \tau \sin 2\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Apply force equilibrium in the **t-direction** (tangential to the inclined cut). $$\begin{aligned} \tau_{\theta} \cdot A &= \tau_{xy} \cdot (A \sin\theta) \cdot \sin\theta - \tau_{yx} \cdot (A \cos\theta) \cdot \cos\theta \\ \tau_{\theta} \cdot A &= \tau A \sin^2\theta - \tau A \cos^2\theta \\ \tau_{\theta} &= \tau (\sin^2\theta - \cos^2\theta) \\ \tau_{\theta} &= -\tau \cos 2\theta \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6:** Interpret the result. The negative sign indicates direction (using the sign convention). In magnitude: $$\vert \tau_{\theta} \vert = \tau \cos 2\theta$$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Principal Stresses in Pure Shear To find the angle $\theta$ at which the **normal stress $\sigma_\theta$ is maximum**: **Step 1:** Differentiate $\sigma_{\theta} = \tau \sin 2\theta$ with respect to $\theta$. $$\dfrac{d\sigma_{\theta}}{d\theta} = 2\tau \cos 2\theta = 0$$ **Step 2:** Solve for $\theta$. $$\cos 2\theta = 0 \implies 2\theta = 90° \implies \theta = 45°$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $\theta = 45°$ back. $$\sigma_{45°} = \tau \sin(90°) = \tau$$ $$\sigma_{135°} = \tau \sin(270°) = -\tau$$ **Step 4:** Conclusion — In pure shear, the principal stresses are: $$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{(tensile at 45°)}$$ $$\sigma_2 = -\tau \quad \text{(compressive at 135°)}$$ These are **equal in magnitude** to the original shear stress. The **maximum shear stress** in pure shear is therefore: $$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \dfrac{\tau - (-\tau)}{2} = \tau$$ which confirms that the original shear stress is the maximum shear stress in a pure shear state. ## 3.4 Derivation: Relationship $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ This is derived from a uniaxial tension test on an isotropic material. **Step 1:** Consider a square element with sides parallel to x and y axes. Apply tensile stress $\sigma_x$ in the x-direction. The strains in the material are: - Axial strain: $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{E}$ - Lateral strain: $\varepsilon_y = -\nu \varepsilon_x = -\dfrac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}$ (Poisson effect) **Step 2:** Now consider a state of *pure shear* equivalent to this. The principal stresses in pure shear are $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ at 45°. The normal strain along the 45° direction (which is the principal direction $\sigma_1$) is: $$\varepsilon_{45°} = \dfrac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2] = \dfrac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \dfrac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 3:** The shear strain $\gamma$ is the change in the originally right angle at the corner. From geometry, the principal tensile strain along the 45° direction equals **half the shear strain**. $$\varepsilon_{45°} = \dfrac{\gamma}{2}$$ **Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $\varepsilon_{45°}$. $$\begin{aligned} \dfrac{\gamma}{2} &= \dfrac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} \\ \gamma &= \dfrac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Compare with Hooke's Law in shear $\tau = G\gamma$. $$\begin{aligned} \tau &= G \cdot \dfrac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E} \\ 1 &= \dfrac{2G(1+\nu)}{E} \\ E &= 2G(1+\nu) \\ \therefore \quad G &= \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** This derivation is high-yield.
