Many KTU 2024 Scheme questions ask students to *derive* this relationship from first principles, not just state it. ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Model Problem for KTU) **Problem:** A steel specimen has the following properties: - Young's Modulus, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm² - Poisson's Ratio, $\nu = 0.30$ A shear force of $50$ kN is applied to a specimen of cross-section $25$ mm $\times$ 25 mm. **Find:** (a) Shear stress, (b) Shear strain, (c) Modulus of Rigidity. **Solution:** **Part (a): Shear Stress** $$\begin{aligned} \tau &= \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{25 \text{ mm} \times 25 \text{ mm}} \\ \tau &= \dfrac{50000}{625} \\ \tau &= 80 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 80 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b): Modulus of Rigidity** $$\begin{aligned} G &= \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2(1+0.30)} \\ G &= \dfrac{200000}{2.6} \\ G &= 76923.08 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 76.92 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (c): Shear Strain** $$\begin{aligned} \gamma &= \dfrac{\tau}{G} = \dfrac{80}{76923.08} \\ \gamma &= 1.04 \times 10^{-3} \text{ radians} \end{aligned}$$ **Conversion to degrees** (for conceptual understanding): $$\gamma = 1.04 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{180°}{\pi} \approx 0.0596°$$ # 4. The second diagram illustrates the principal stress transformation in pure shear when the element is rotated. The third diagram shows the analytical workflow for solving a typical KTU numerical problem on this topic. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define shear stress and shear strain. State Hooke's law in shear. **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 (Model)]** **Model Answer:** **Shear Stress:** The tangential (or shearing) stress developed on a surface when a force acts parallel to it. It is defined as the ratio of the tangential force to the area of the surface on which it acts. $$\tau = \dfrac{F_t}{A} \quad \text{(Units: N/m}^2 \text{ or Pa)}$$ **Shear Strain:** The angular deformation at a point, defined as the change in angle (in radians) between two originally perpendicular line elements. $$\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L} = \tan\theta \approx \theta \quad \text{(radians, dimensionless)}$$ **Hooke's Law in Shear:** Within the elastic limit, shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain. The constant of proportionality is the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)**. $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$ --- ### Question 2: What are complementary shear stresses? Why must they be equal? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 (Model)]** **Model Answer:** **Complementary Shear Stresses** are the pair of shear stresses that act on *mutually perpendicular planes* of an element, *along their line of intersection*. They are denoted as $\tau_{xy}$ (on the plane normal to x) and $\tau_{yx}$ (on the plane normal to y). **Why they must be equal:** From the principle of **moment equilibrium**, an infinitesimal element cannot be in static equilibrium unless the sum of moments about any point is zero. For the shear forces to balance in rotation: $$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$$ If they were unequal, the element would experience a net moment and rotate indefinitely, which is physically impossible for a static element in equilibrium. --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A: Shear Stress Distribution and Principal Stresses **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]** **Question:** A circular shaft of diameter **60 mm** is subjected to a torque of **2 kN·m**. The material has $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and $\nu = 0.28$. **(a)** Determine the maximum shear stress in the shaft and the shear strain at the surface. Also find the Modulus of Rigidity. (7 marks) **(b)** Find the magnitudes and directions of the principal stresses at a point on the surface of the shaft. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **Step 1:** Calculate the polar moment of inertia $J$ of the circular cross-section. $$\begin{aligned} J &= \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi \times (60)^4}{32} \\ J &= \dfrac{\pi \times 12960000}{32} \\ J &= 1.272 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the maximum shear stress using the torsion formula. $$\begin{aligned} \tau_{max} &= \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}) \times 30 \text{ mm}}{1.272 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4} \\ \tau_{max} &= \dfrac{6 \times 10^7}{1.272 \times 10^6} \\ \tau_{max} &= 47.17 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 47.17 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate the Modulus of Rigidity. $$\begin{aligned} G &= \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \dfrac{2.1 \times 10^5}{2(1+0.28)} \\ G &= \dfrac{210000}{2.56} \\ G &= 82031.25 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 82.03 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate the shear strain at the surface. $$\begin{aligned} \gamma &= \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{G} = \dfrac{47.17}{82031.25} \\ \gamma &= 5.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ radians} \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b):** **Step 1:** The surface of the shaft is in a state of **pure shear** with $\tau_{xy} = 47.17$ MPa. **Step 2:** The principal stresses in pure shear are: $$\begin{aligned} \sigma_1 &= +\tau_{max} = +47.17 \text{ MPa (tensile)} \\ \sigma_2 &= -\tau_{max} = -47.17 \text{ MPa (compressive)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Direction of principal stresses — The principal planes are at **45°** to the shaft axis. - $\sigma_1$ acts at **+45°** to the axis (helical tension line). - $\sigma_2$ acts at **135°** (-45°) to the axis (helical compression line). **Step 4:** Maximum shear stress equals: $$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \dfrac{47.17 - (-47.17)}{2} = 47.17 \text{ MPa}$$ This is the original surface shear stress. --- ### Question B (Internal Choice): Strain Energy in Pure Shear and Elastic Constants **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]** **Question:** A cylindrical specimen of **mild steel** is subjected to a state of pure shear with shear stress $\tau = 60$ MPa. The material has $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa and $\nu = 0.30$. **(a)** Calculate the shear strain, the Modulus of Rigidity, and the strain energy density stored in the material. (7 marks) **(b)** If the same material is subjected to a uniaxial tensile stress of **120 MPa**, find the resulting strains in the longitudinal and lateral directions, and the volumetric strain. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a):** **Step 1:** Calculate the Modulus of Rigidity. $$\begin{aligned} G &= \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2(1+0.30)} \\ G &= \dfrac{200000}{2.6} \\ G &= 76923.08 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Calculate shear strain using Hooke's law in shear. $$\begin{aligned} \gamma &= \dfrac{\tau}{G} = \dfrac{60}{76923.08} \\ \gamma &= 7.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ radians} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate strain energy density. $$\begin{aligned} u &= \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G} = \dfrac{(60)^2}{2 \times 76923.08} \\ u &= \dfrac{3600}{153846.16} \\ u &= 0.0234 \text{ N·mm/mm}^3 = 0.0234 \text{ J/cm}^3 \\ u &= 23.4 \text{ kJ/m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b):** **Step 1:** For uniaxial tension $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$. Longitudinal strain: $$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon_x &= \dfrac{\sigma_x}{E} = \dfrac{120}{2.0 \times 10^5} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned}$$ Lateral strain (Poisson effect): $$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z &= -\nu \varepsilon_x = -0.30 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \\ &= -1.8 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2:** Volumetric strain is the sum of normal strains. $$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon_v &= \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z \\ \varepsilon_v &= 6.0 \times 10^{-4} - 1.8 \times 10^{-4} - 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \\ \varepsilon_v &= 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** Verification using Bulk Modulus. $$\begin{aligned} K &= \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \dfrac{2.0 \times 10^5}{3(1-0.6)} = \dfrac{200000}{1.2} = 166666.67 \text{ MPa} \\ \varepsilon_v &= \dfrac{\sigma_{mean}}{K} = \dfrac{120/3}{166666.67} = 2.4 \times 10^{-4} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit Consistency:** Always convert forces to **Newtons (N)** and dimensions to **mm** (or m) consistently. Mixing units (e.g., kN with mm) is a 1-mark deduction. > 2. **Radians vs Degrees:** Shear strain must be expressed in **radians** (dimensionless). Don't convert to degrees unless explicitly asked. > 3. **Sign Convention:** Tensile stresses/strains are positive; compressive are negative. Shear stress sign depends on the chosen convention. Be consistent. > 4. **Pure Shear vs. General Shear:** In a *general* 2D stress state with both $\sigma_x, \sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy}$ present, the principal stress formula is **different** from pure shear. Use the standard formula $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$. > 5. **Small Angle Approximation:** The approximation $\tan \theta \approx \theta$ is valid only for small angles. State this assumption when solving. > 6. **Skipping Derivation Steps:** KTU 2024 Scheme demands step-by-step derivations. Skipping algebraic steps in a 14-mark question costs at least 2-3 marks. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following are the absolute essentials to remember for KTU exams on this topic:** - ✅ **Shear Stress Definition:** $\tau = \dfrac{F_t}{A}$ — tangential force per unit area. - ✅ **Shear Strain Definition:** $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L} \approx \theta$ (radians) — angular deformation. - ✅ **Hooke's Law in Shear:** $\tau = G \gamma$ — fundamental linear elastic relationship. - ✅ **Modulus of Rigidity (G):** Material's resistance to *shape change* (preserving volume). - ✅ **Master Formula:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ — most important elastic constant relation. - ✅ **Complementary Shear Stresses:** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ — derived from moment equilibrium. - ✅ **Pure Shear Transformation:** - $\sigma_{\theta} = \tau \sin 2\theta$ - $\tau_{\theta} = \tau \cos 2\theta$ - ✅ **Principal Stresses in Pure Shear:** $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ at $\theta = 45°$ and $\theta = 135°$. - ✅ **Maximum Shear Stress in Pure Shear:** Equals the original $\tau_{xy}$ — does not change. - ✅ **Strain Energy Density:** $u = \dfrac{\tau^2}{2G} = \dfrac{G\gamma^2}{2} = \dfrac{\tau \gamma}{2}$. - ✅ **E/G Range:** $2.5 \le E/G \le 3.0$ for all real materials (since $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$). - ✅ **Range of Poisson's Ratio:** $0 \le \nu \le 0.5$ (theoretically). - ✅ **Real-world Applications:** Rivet/bolt design, torsion of shafts, soil mechanics, seismic waves, punching operations. - ✅ **Important Assumption:** $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ for small strains — must be stated. - ✅ **Unit Check:** $G$ and $E$ have units of pressure (Pa, MPa, or N/mm²). - ✅ **For pure shear on an inclined plane, always remember the 45° rule** — the maximum normal stress equals the shear stress magnitude.

topics.title: Modulus of Rigidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modulus of Rigidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modulus of Rigidity (G)**, also known as the **Shear Modulus** or **Modulus of Elasticity in Shear**, is the mechanical property of a material that quantifies its resistance to **shape distortion** when subjected to a tangential (shear) force, while the volume of the material is essentially preserved.

Mathematically, it is the ratio of **shear stress ($\tau$)** to the corresponding **shear strain ($\gamma$)** within the elastic limit of the material, as dictated by **Hooke's Law in Shear**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAGT302 - Module 2):**
> Modulus of Rigidity is the cornerstone property that links a material's **torsional response** to its **axial/elongation response**. It is mandatory for the design of all rotating shafts, helical springs, and laterally loaded beams.

### Governing Relationship (Hooke's Law in Shear)

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m² or Pa)
- $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity (N/m² or Pa)
- $\gamma$ = Shear strain (radians — dimensionless)

Therefore, the formal definition resolves to:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma} \quad \left[ \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ or Pa} \right]$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Physical Intuition

Imagine you have a **rubber eraser** and a **steel cube** sitting on a table. You place your hand flat on the top of each and push horizontally (parallel to the table surface).

- The **rubber eraser** tilts dramatically — its top surface slides sideways relative to the bottom. Its shape is heavily distorted.
- The **steel cube** barely moves — it strongly resists the sliding motion and retains its rectangular shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> The **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** is a numerical measure of *how stubbornly a material refuses to "slip" or "skew" when you apply a tangential force*.
> - A **high value of G** (e.g., **Steel: ~79 GPa**) means the material is very **stiff in shear** — it twists or distorts very little under torque.
> - A **low value of G** (e.g., **Rubber: ~0.0003 GPa**) means the material is very **compliant in shear** — it twists easily.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Typical Values

The standard SI unit of Modulus of Rigidity is **Pascal (Pa)**. Because typical engineering values are large, we use **Gigapascals (GPa)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Values of G (Modulus of Rigidity) for Common Engineering Materials**

| Material | Modulus of Rigidity (G) in GPa | Modulus of Rigidity (G) in N/mm² |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Steel (Mild) | **79.3 GPa** | **79,300 N/mm²** |
| Cast Iron | **40 – 50 GPa** | **40,000 – 50,000 N/mm²** |
| Aluminium Alloy | **25 – 28 GPa** | **25,000 – 28,000 N/mm²** |
| Copper | **44 GPa** | **44,000 N/mm²** |
| Brass | **36 – 40 GPa** | **36,000 – 40,000 N/mm²** |
| Concrete | **10 – 17 GPa** | **10,000 – 17,000 N/mm²** |
| Rubber (Vulcanized) | **0.0003 GPa** | **0.3 N/mm²** |
| Timber (along grain) | **0.5 – 1.0 GPa** | **500 – 1,000 N/mm²** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Fact:** For most isotropic metals, the Modulus of Rigidity (G) is roughly **37.5 %** of Young's Modulus (E). For example, if $E_{steel} = 200$ GPa, then $G_{steel} \approx 76$ GPa.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Shear Stress, Shear Strain, and the Shear Hooke's Law

To fully grasp Modulus of Rigidity, we must first isolate its two parent quantities: **Shear Stress** and **Shear Strain**.

### Step 1 — Shear Stress ($\tau$)

When a force acts **tangentially (parallel)** to a surface, it does not push or pull perpendicularly. Instead, it tries to "scrape" the surface sideways. The intensity of this tangential force per unit area is called **Shear Stress**.

$$\tau = \frac{F_t}{A}$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = Tangential (shear) force in Newtons
- $A$ = Area over which the force acts in m²

### Step 2 — Shear Strain ($\gamma$)

When shear stress is applied to a rectangular element, the element deforms into a **parallelogram**. The angle of distortion (measured in radians) is called the **Shear Strain**.

$$\gamma = \tan \theta \approx \theta \quad \text{(for small deformations)}$$

Where:
- $\theta$ = Angular deformation in radians
- $\gamma$ is **dimensionless** (radians)

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction (often asked in KTU exams):**
> - **Normal strain ($\epsilon$)** = change in **length** ÷ original length
> - **Shear strain ($\gamma$)** = change in **shape** (angular skew)
> Modulus of Rigidity (G) controls **shape change**, NOT length change.

### Step 3 — Hooke's Law in Shear

Within the **elastic limit**, shear strain is linearly proportional to shear stress. The constant of proportionality is the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)**.

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

## 2.2 Relationship with Young's Modulus (E) and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)

For an **isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic material**, the three fundamental elastic constants (E, G, $\nu$) are inter-related by a critical formula that KTU examiners love to test:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}}$$

Rearranged forms (very important for KTU problems):

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

$$\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Impossible Triangle" of Elastic Constants:**
> Given any two of (E, G, $\nu$), you can always compute the third. This is a high-frequency 3-mark and 7-mark KTU question.

## 2.3 Pure Shear and the Shear Element

Consider a small square element of side `dx × dy`. If shear stresses $\tau$ are applied on all four faces (in complementary pairs to satisfy equilibrium), the element deforms into a rhombus. The engineering shear strain $\gamma$ is the change in angle at the corners:

$$\gamma = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}$$

This **pure shear state** is exactly what occurs on the surface of a shaft under torsion, on the web of an I-beam under transverse load, and on the cross-section of a bolt under tightening.

## 2.4 Torsion of a Circular Shaft — Application of G

The most common engineering application of G is in **torsion**. For a solid circular shaft of length L, radius r, subjected to torque T, the angle of twist $\theta$ is governed by G:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

Where:
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- $T$ = Applied torque (N·m)
- $L$ = Length of shaft (m)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist (radians)

The **shear stress at radius r** is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Modulus of Rigidity Formula Sheet**

| S. No. | Formula / Expression | Description / Use Case |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $G = \tau \div \gamma$ | Basic definition — Hooke's Law in shear |
| 2 | $G = E \div \bigl[2(1 + \nu)\bigr]$ | Relation between E, G, and $\nu$ |
| 3 | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Rearranged form |
| 4 | $\nu = E \div 2G - 1$ | To find Poisson's ratio |
| 5 | $\theta = T L \div (G J)$ | Angle of twist of a circular shaft |
| 6 | $T \div J = \tau \div r = G \theta \div L$ | Torsion equation (full form) |
| 7 | $G = T L \div (J \theta)$ | Rearranged torsion equation to find G experimentally |
| 8 | $K_{torsion} = G J \div L$ | Torsional stiffness of a shaft |
| 9 | $U = \tau^2 \div (2G) \cdot \text{Vol}$ | Strain energy density in pure shear |
| 10 | $\gamma_{max} = \tau_{max} \div G$ | Maximum shear strain in torsion |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Modulus of Rigidity is indispensable across multiple branches of mechanical and civil engineering:

- **Drive Shafts & Axles** (Automobile, Turbines): Designed to transmit torque without excessive twist.
- **Helical Springs**: The spring constant $K$ of a helical spring depends directly on G.
- **Bolts, Rivets, Pins (Fasteners)**: Designed against **shear failure**, governed by $\tau$ and hence G.
- **Building Frames under Lateral Load**: Shear walls and bracings rely on G for lateral stiffness.
- **Machine Tool Spindles**: Must resist torsional vibration; G dictates the natural frequency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship $G = E \div [2(1 + \nu)]$

This is the single most important derivation for the KTU Module 2 examination.

### Starting Point — Consider a Square Element under Pure Shear

Let a unit square element of side `1 × 1` be subjected to complementary shear stresses $\tau$ on all four faces.

The engineering shear strain produced is:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

The element deforms into a rhombus. The principal planes (planes of maximum normal stress) are oriented at **45°** to the original faces.

### Step 1 — Resolve the Shear Stresses into Normal Stresses on a 45° Plane

Using **stress transformation equations** for a 2D stress state with only shear components ($\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0$, $\tau_{xy} = \tau$):

For a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the x-axis, the normal stress is:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2} \cos 2\theta + \tau_{xy} \sin 2\theta$$

Substituting $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0$ and $\tau_{xy} = \tau$:

$$\sigma_\theta = 0 + 0 + \tau \sin 2\theta$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate at $\theta = 45°$ (Principal Plane)

At $\theta = 45°$ (the plane of principal stress):

$$\sigma_{45} = \tau \sin(90°) = \tau \cdot 1 = \tau$$

On the **perpendicular plane** ($\theta = -45°$):

$$\sigma_{-45} = \tau \sin(-90°) = -\tau$$

So the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Resulting Strains via Young's Modulus and Poisson's Ratio

Using **generalized Hooke's Law** for the principal directions:

$$\epsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2\bigr) = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\tau - \nu(-\tau)\bigr) = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\epsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1\bigr) = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(-\tau - \nu \tau\bigr) = \frac{-\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 4 — Relate the Principal Strains to the Shear Strain

The original right angle of the element (90°) has been altered. The new angle becomes $(90° - \gamma)$ or $(90° + \gamma)$, depending on orientation.

By geometry of deformation (or strain transformation), the engineering shear strain is:

$$\gamma = \epsilon_1 - \epsilon_2$$

Substituting the values obtained in Step 3:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} - \left[\frac{-\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}\right] = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Modulus of Rigidity (G)

Rearranging the shear Hooke's law $\tau = G \gamma$:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

Equating the two expressions for $\gamma$:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

Canceling $\tau$ from both sides and cross-multiplying:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Check:** For steel, $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu \approx 0.3$. Then $G = 200 \div (2 \times 1.3) = 76.9$ GPa, which matches the typical value of $\approx 79$ GPa within engineering tolerance. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Angle of Twist of a Circular Shaft

Consider a solid circular shaft of length $L$ and radius $R$ fixed at one end, with torque $T$ applied at the free end.

### Step 1 — Shear Strain at the Outer Surface

A line originally drawn on the surface parallel to the axis twists through an angle $\phi$. The arc length moved by the outer fibre is $R \phi$, while the original length is $L$. So:

$$\gamma = \frac{R \phi}{L}$$

### Step 2 — Shear Stress via Hooke's Law in Shear

$$\tau = G \gamma = \frac{G R \phi}{L}$$

### Step 3 — Total Torque from Shear Stress Distribution

The shear stress varies linearly with radius: $\tau = (G \phi \div L) \cdot r$. The torque is the integral of $\tau \cdot r$ over the cross-section:

$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \int_0^R \frac{G \phi}{L} r^2 \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr$$

$$T = \frac{2\pi G \phi}{L} \int_0^R r^3 \, dr = \frac{2\pi G \phi}{L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi G \phi R^4}{2L}$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Polar Moment of Inertia

For a solid circular section: $J = \pi R^4 \div 2$. Substituting:

$$T = \frac{G \phi J}{L}$$

### Step 5 — Final Torsion Equation

$$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \phi}{L}}$$

And solving for the angle of twist:

$$\boxed{\phi = \frac{T L}{G J}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Finding G Given E and $\nu$

**Problem:** A specimen of ductile iron has Young's Modulus $E = 170$ GPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.27$. Determine the Modulus of Rigidity.

**Solution:**

Using the relation $G = E \div [2(1 + \nu)]$:

$$G = \frac{170}{2(1 + 0.27)} = \frac{170}{2 \times 1.27} = \frac{170}{2.54}$$

$$\boxed{G = 66.93 \text{ GPa}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Modulus of Rigidity Concept Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Tangential Force F] --> B[Shear Stress tau on Element Face]
    B --> C[Angular Distortion theta of Element]
    C --> D[Shear Strain gamma equals theta in radians]
    D --> E{Within Elastic Limit ?}
    E -- YES --> F[Hookes Law in Shear tau equals G gamma]
    F --> G[Modulus of Rigidity G]
    E -- NO --> H[Plastic Shear Failure]
    G --> I[Related to E and nu by G equals E over 2 times 1 plus nu]
    G --> J[Used in Torsion phi equals T L over G J]
    G --> K[Used in Spring Design K equals G d to power 4 over 8 D cubed N cubed]
    I --> L[Three Elastic Constants E G nu]
    J --> M[Shaft Design and Drive Train]
    K --> N[Helical Spring Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Flow — Torsional Loading of a Shaft

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    loadA[External Torque T applied at free end] --> loadB[Shear Stress tau develops on cross section]
    loadB --> loadC[Shear Strain gamma equals tau over G]
    loadC --> loadD[Angular Twist phi accumulates along length L]
    loadD --> loadE[Total angle of twist phi equals T L over G J]
    loadE --> loadF{Design Check}
    loadF -- phi exceeds limit --> loadG[Increase G or J]
    loadF -- phi within limit --> loadH[Shaft is Safe in Torsion]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Block — E vs. G vs. K

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    modA[Young Modulus E] --> descA[Resistance to Axial Elongation or Compression]
    modA --> unitA[Unit N per m squared]
    modB[Modulus of Rigidity G] --> descB[Resistance to Shape Distortion or Shear]
    modB --> unitB[Unit N per m squared]
    modC[Bulk Modulus K] --> descC[Resistance to Volume Change under Hydrostatic Pressure]
    modC --> unitC[Unit N per m squared]
    modA --> rel[All Three are Inter related]
    modB --> rel
    modC --> rel
    rel --> eqn[E equals 2G times 1 plus nu equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu]
```

## 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Pure Shear Element Deformation

```
    BEFORE DEFORMATION              AFTER SHEAR
    +---------+                     +---------+
    |         |                     |       / |
    |   o-----+---> x               |   o-/---+---> x
    |         |                     |     /   |
    |         |                     |    /    |
    +---------+                     +---+-----+
          |                                |
          v                                v
          y                                y

    The right angle at the corner 'o'        The angle becomes (90 deg - gamma)
    becomes (90 deg - gamma).                where gamma is the shear strain.
    gamma = tau / G  -->  G = tau / gamma
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Modulus of Rigidity. State its SI unit. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

**Modulus of Rigidity (G)** is defined as the ratio of **shear stress ($\tau$)** to the corresponding **shear strain ($\gamma$)** within the elastic limit of the material.

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

**SI Unit:** Newton per square meter ($\text{N/m}^2$) or **Pascal (Pa)**. In practice, values are expressed in **Gigapascals (GPa)** or $\text{N/mm}^2$.

> [!NOTE]
> It is also called the **Shear Modulus** or **Modulus of Elasticity in Shear**. It quantifies the material's resistance to **shape distortion** (not length change).

### Question 2: The Young's Modulus of a material is 210 GPa and its Poisson's ratio is 0.30. Find its Modulus of Rigidity. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

Given: $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$

Using the standard relation:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

$$G = \frac{210}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{210}{2 \times 1.30} = \frac{210}{2.60}$$

$$\boxed{G = 80.77 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus (E), Modulus of Rigidity (G), and Poisson's ratio ($\nu$) for a homogeneous isotropic material. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A solid circular steel shaft of diameter 60 mm and length 1.5 m is subjected to a torque of 2 kN·m. If the Modulus of Rigidity of the steel is 80 GPa, determine: (i) the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft, and (ii) the angle of twist in degrees. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1:** Consider a unit square element subjected to pure shear stress $\tau$ on all four faces. The complementary shear stress condition gives an engineering shear strain:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

**Step 2:** Resolve the shear into normal stresses on planes at 45° to the original faces. The principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

**Step 3:** Apply generalized Hooke's Law in the principal directions:

$$\epsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2\bigr) = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\epsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\bigl(\sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1\bigr) = \frac{-\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** The engineering shear strain equals the difference of principal strains:

$$\gamma = \epsilon_1 - \epsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5:** Equating with $\gamma = \tau \div G$ and simplifying:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 60$ mm $\Rightarrow$ Radius $r = 30$ mm
- Length $L = 1.5$ m $= 1500$ mm
- Torque $T = 2$ kN·m $= 2 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- $G = 80$ GPa $= 80{,}000$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Compute Polar Moment of Inertia (J):**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 12{,}960{,}000}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{40{,}715{,}040}{32} = 1{,}272{,}345 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Shear Stress (Torsion Equation):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 30}{1{,}272{,}345}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 47.15 \text{ N/mm}^2 \; (= 47.15 \text{ MPa})}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Twist:**

$$\phi = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{2 \times 10^6 \times 1500}{80{,}000 \times 1{,}272{,}345}$$

$$\phi = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{1.0179 \times 10^{11}} = 0.02947 \text{ radians}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\phi_{deg} = 0.02947 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = \boxed{1.689°}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define shear stress and shear strain. With a neat sketch, explain the deformation of a rectangular element under pure shear and derive the expression for shear modulus. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A torque of 5 kN·m is transmitted by a hollow circular shaft of external diameter 80 mm and internal diameter 50 mm. If the Modulus of Rigidity of the shaft material is 84 GPa, calculate: (i) the maximum shear stress induced, and (ii) the angle of twist per metre length of the shaft. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Definitions:**

- **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** The tangential force per unit area acting on a surface. It tries to slide one plane over another.
- **Shear Strain ($\gamma$):** The angular distortion (in radians) of an element subjected to shear stress.

**Sketch & Deformation:** A unit square element ABCD subjected to complementary shear stresses $\tau$ on all four faces distorts into a parallelogram A'B'C'D'. The original right angle at each corner is reduced by the angle $\gamma$.

**Derivation of Shear Modulus:**

Using the relationship derived earlier (refer to Section 3.1 of these notes):

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:**
- $T = 5$ kN·m $= 5 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- Outer diameter $D = 80$ mm $\Rightarrow$ Outer radius $R = 40$ mm
- Inner diameter $d_i = 50$ mm $\Rightarrow$ Inner radius $r_i = 25$ mm
- $G = 84$ GPa $= 84{,}000$ N/mm²
- $L = 1$ m $= 1000$ mm

**Step 1 — Polar Moment of Inertia for Hollow Section:**

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}\bigl(D^4 - d_i^4\bigr) = \frac{\pi}{32}\bigl[(80)^4 - (50)^4\bigr]$$

$$J = \frac{\pi}{32}\bigl[40{,}960{,}000 - 6{,}250{,}000\bigr] = \frac{\pi}{32} \times 34{,}710{,}000$$

$$J = \frac{109{,}039{,}368}{32} = 3{,}407{,}480 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 40}{3{,}407{,}480}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 58.70 \text{ N/mm}^2 \; (= 58.70 \text{ MPa})}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Twist per Metre:**

$$\phi \text{ per m} = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} = \frac{5 \times 10^6 \times 1000}{84{,}000 \times 3{,}407{,}480}$$

$$\phi = \frac{5 \times 10^9}{2.862 \times 10^{11}} = 0.01747 \text{ rad/m}$$

Converting to degrees:

$$\phi = 0.01747 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = \boxed{1.001°/\text{m}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Modulus of Rigidity Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** A frequent mistake is mixing units — using $T$ in N·m and $J$ in mm⁴ in the same equation. Always convert to a **consistent unit system** (N·mm and mm⁴) before computation.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Polar Moment of Inertia Formula:** For a **hollow shaft**, students often write $J = \pi d^4 \div 32$ (solid formula). The correct form is $J = \pi (D^4 - d_i^4) \div 32$. This single error costs **3 marks** in a 14-mark question.
>
> 3. **Confusing Modulus of Rigidity (G) with Young's Modulus (E):** E controls **axial elongation**, G controls **angular twist**. KTU examiners specifically test whether students apply the right constant to the right problem.
>
> 4. **Radian vs. Degree Error:** In torsion problems, the formula $\phi = TL \div (GJ)$ gives $\phi$ in **radians**, not degrees. Always explicitly state the unit and convert when required.
>
> 5. **Elastic Limit Violation:** The relation $\tau = G\gamma$ is valid **only within the elastic limit**. Applying it to plastic deformation is a conceptual error worth 2 marks in derivation questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modulus of Rigidity — Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Definition:** $G = \tau \div \gamma$ — ratio of shear stress to shear strain within the elastic limit. Unit: **N/m² (Pa)**, practically **GPa** or **N/mm²**.

- **Hooke's Law in Shear:** $\tau = G \gamma$ — linear, valid only up to the **proportional (elastic) limit**.

- **Master Formula:** $G = E \div [2(1 + \nu)]$ — the **bridge formula** linking all three elastic constants. This is the **single most asked formula** in KTU Module 2.

- **Torsion Equation (Full Form):** $T \div J = \tau \div r = G \phi \div L$ — memorize this as one inseparable line; it is heavily tested.

- **Angle of Twist:** $\phi = T L \div (G J)$ — used to find unknown G experimentally or to find permissible torque.

- **Polar Moment of Inertia:**
    - Solid shaft: $J = \pi d^4 \div 32$
    - Hollow shaft: $J = \pi (D^4 - d_i^4) \div 32$

- **Typical G Values:** Steel $\approx 79$ GPa, Aluminium $\approx 27$ GPa, Cast Iron $\approx 45$ GPa, Rubber $\approx 0.0003$ GPa. (Always quote in **GPa** unless asked otherwise.)

- **Applications:** Torsion of shafts, helical springs, bolted/riveted joints, shear walls in buildings, machine tool spindles.

- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never confuse $G$ (shape resistance) with $E$ (length resistance) and $K$ (volume resistance). Each applies to a different mode of deformation.

- **Dimensional Check:** $G$ has units of stress ($\text{N/m}^2$) since $\gamma$ is dimensionless. Always verify this in MCQs.

- **High-Yield Practice Topics for KTU 2024 ESE:**
    1. Numerical on $G = E \div [2(1 + \nu)]$.
    2. Numerical on $\phi = T L \div (G J)$ for both solid and hollow shafts.
    3. Derivation of the $E$-$G$-$\nu$ relationship from a pure shear element.
    4. Experimental determination of G (torsion test on a circular bar).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Shear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Simple Shear

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Shear (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> **Simple shear** is a state of deformation in which a body is subjected to a pair of equal, opposite, and parallel forces acting tangentially to the cross-section, causing the shape of the body to distort without any change in volume. The stress produced is called **Shear Stress (τ)** and the corresponding deformation per unit length is the **Shear Strain (γ)**.

When a force **P** is applied tangentially to a body of cross-sectional area **A**, the resulting **shear stress** is given by:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A}$$

The deformation produced is a relative displacement **δ** between two parallel faces separated by a perpendicular distance **L**. This defines **shear strain** as a dimensionless quantity:

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{L}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **deck of playing cards** lying on a table. If you place your hand flat on the top card and slide it forward while holding the bottom card stationary, each card slides relative to the one below it. The deck changes from a neat rectangle into a **slanted parallelogram**.

- The **force** you apply is **tangential** (parallel to the surface), not perpendicular.
- The **shape changes**, but the **overall size and volume** essentially remain the same.
- This sliding distortion is exactly **simple shear**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples of Simple Shear:**
> - **Rivets and bolts** in a lap joint carrying transverse loads.
> - **Punching a hole** in a metal sheet — the sheared material experiences pure shear.
> - **Cardboard cutter/scissors blades** applying shear at the cutting edge.
> - **Soil mechanics** — lateral sliding of earth layers.
> - **Torsion of circular shafts** (every cross-section is in a state of pure shear).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Units (SI) | Typical Range for Engineering Materials |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Force | P or V | **N** (Newton) | — |
| Shear Stress | τ (tau) | **N/m² = Pa (Pascal)** | 10⁶ – 10⁹ Pa for metals |
| Shear Strain | γ (gamma) | **radians** (dimensionless) | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻² |
| Shear Modulus | G | **N/m² = Pa** | Steel: ~79 GPa; Aluminum: ~26 GPa |
| Shear Deformation | δ | **m (metre)** | Microscopic in elastic range |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanics of Simple Shear — Structured Logic Steps

#### Step 1: Identification of Shear Loading
- Forces act **parallel** to the surface (not perpendicular as in normal stress).
- Two equal, opposite, and parallel forces form a **couple in the plane of the surface**.
- No net translation; only **distortion** occurs.

#### Step 2: Internal Shear Stress Distribution
- On the cross-section: **τ = P/A**.
- For a **uniform** shear distribution, the assumption holds only when the section is short and the material behaves uniformly.
- For non-uniform cases (e.g., circular shafts under torsion), τ varies linearly from the axis to the outer fibre.

#### Step 3: Shear Strain (γ)
- Measured in **radians** since it is the tangent of the angle of distortion.
- For small deformations, **tan(γ) ≈ γ** (in radians).

#### Step 4: Hooke's Law in Shear
- Within the **elastic limit**, shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain.
- The constant of proportionality is the **Modulus of Rigidity** (also called **Shear Modulus**), denoted **G**.

$$\tau = G \gamma$$

#### Step 5: Complementary Shear Stresses (Theorem of Conjugate Stresses)
- **Why?** Equilibrium of an infinitesimal element requires that shear stresses on perpendicular planes must be **equal in magnitude** and **opposite in direction** to maintain rotational equilibrium.
- **Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem** confirms that τ_xy = τ_yx.

#### Step 6: Strain Energy Stored Due to Shear
- Volume of the element experiences shear strain γ.
- Strain energy per unit volume = **½ × τ × γ**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Shear Stress | P = tangential force (N); A = area (m²); τ (Pa) |
| 2 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\delta}{L}$ | Shear Strain (rad) | δ = lateral displacement (m); L = perpendicular distance (m) |
| 3 | $\tau = G\,\gamma$ | Hooke's Law in Shear | G = Modulus of Rigidity (Pa); γ (rad) |
| 4 | $\delta = \dfrac{P\,L}{A\,G}$ | Total Shear Deformation | Same units as above |
| 5 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} \tau \gamma \cdot V$ | Shear Strain Energy | V = Volume of element (m³); U (Joules) |
| 6 | $u = \dfrac{\tau^{2}}{2G}$ | Shear Strain Energy Density | u (J/m³) |
| 7 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\mu)}$ | Relation between E, G, ν | E = Young's Modulus; μ = Poisson's Ratio |
| 8 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\mu)}$ | Bulk Modulus | K (Pa) |
| 9 | $\dfrac{E}{9K} + \dfrac{3G}{E} = 1$ | Identity linking E, G, K | Dimensionless relation |
| 10 | $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ | Complementary Shear | Equality of conjugate shears |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

- **Mechanical Design of Fasteners**: Bolts, rivets, and pins are sized so that τ < τ_allowable. The **shear strength** of materials directly governs joint design.
- **Torsion of Shafts**: Every point on the cross-section of a shaft under torque is in a state of **pure shear**, making this theory central to **power transmission** design (drive shafts, axles).
- **Sheet Metal Forming**: Shear is the dominant mechanism in **punching, blanking, and shearing operations**.
- **Civil Structures**: Shear walls, shear connectors in composite construction, and punching shear in flat slabs.
- **Geotechnical Engineering**: Soil failure under shear governs **bearing capacity** and **slope stability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Insight:**
> The question bank in **PCAGT302** commonly tests the **relationship between E, G, and K** and the calculation of **shear deformation in riveted/bolted joints**. Memorize the formula $G = E / 2(1+\mu)$ — it is the most repeated formula in Module 2!

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between E, G, and Poisson's Ratio (μ)

#### Step 1: Setup — Consider a Square Element Under Pure Shear
Consider an elemental square of side **a** subjected to complementary shear stresses **τ** on all four faces.

**The principal stresses** in this element are obtained by rotating the axes by **45°**. The two principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{(tensile)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = -\tau \quad \text{(compressive)}$$

#### Step 2: Compute Principal Strains Using Generalized Hooke's Law

For a 2-D state of stress in the principal directions:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \mu \sigma_2\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_2 - \mu \sigma_1\right]$$

Substituting $\sigma_1 = \tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \mu(-\tau)\right] = \frac{\tau}{E}(1+\mu)$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[-\tau - \mu(\tau)\right] = \frac{-\tau}{E}(1+\mu)$$

#### Step 3: Compute Total Shear Strain γ
The engineering shear strain γ is the **difference** between the principal strains (one tensile, one compressive), because the diagonal of the square stretches while the other diagonal contracts:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$$

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{E}(1+\mu) - \left[\frac{-\tau}{E}(1+\mu)\right]$$

$$\gamma = \frac{2\tau(1+\mu)}{E}$$

#### Step 4: Equate with Hooke's Law in Shear
From the shear Hooke's Law:

$$\tau = G \gamma \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

Setting the two expressions for γ equal:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1+\mu)}{E}$$

Cancelling τ on both sides:

$$\frac{1}{G} = \frac{2(1+\mu)}{E}$$

Therefore, the fundamental relation is:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\mu)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Shear Deformation in a Riveted Joint

A lap joint with a rivet of diameter **d** and plate thickness **t**. The rivet experiences single shear when the plates overlap.

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of rivet resisting shear:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}$$

**Step 2:** Shear stress on the rivet:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Shear deformation of the rivet under load P (length of rivet ≈ plate thickness t):

$$\delta = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot G} = \frac{P \cdot t}{(\pi d^{2}/4) \cdot G}$$

$$\delta = \frac{4 P t}{\pi d^{2} G}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Shear Strain Energy

Consider an element subjected to pure shear stress **τ**. The shear strain is **γ = τ/G**.

**Work done per unit volume** = average stress × strain = (½) × τ × γ:

$$u = \frac{1}{2} \tau \gamma = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G}$$

**Total strain energy** in a volume V:

$$U = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G} \cdot V$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Property Values

| Material | Young's Modulus E (GPa) | Poisson's Ratio μ | Shear Modulus G = E/[2(1+μ)] (GPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Steel** | 200 | 0.30 | 76.92 |
| **Aluminum** | 70 | 0.33 | 26.31 |
| **Copper** | 110 | 0.34 | 41.04 |
| **Cast Iron** | 100 | 0.25 | 40.00 |
| **Concrete** | 30 | 0.20 | 12.50 |
| **Rubber** | 0.01 | 0.45 | 0.0034 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick numerical check for Steel:** $G = 200 / [2(1 + 0.3)] = 200/2.6 \approx 76.92$ GPa. Matches the standard KTU textbook value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Simple Shear Element

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of an element under simple shear, illustrating how the four shear stresses interact through equilibrium and how deformation relates to energy storage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["APPLIED TANGENTIAL FORCE P"] --> node2["CROSS SECTION AREA A"]
    node2 --> node3["SHEAR STRESS tau = P by A"]
    node3 --> node4["MODULUS OF RIGIDITY G"]
    node4 --> node5["SHEAR STRAIN gamma = tau by G"]
    node5 --> node6["DEFORMATION delta = gamma times L"]
    node3 --> node7["COMPLEMENTARY SHEAR tau_xy = tau_yx"]
    node7 --> node5
    node5 --> node8["STRAIN ENERGY DENSITY u = tau squared by 2G"]
    node8 --> node9["TOTAL SHEAR STRAIN ENERGY U = u times V"]
    node6 --> node10["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
    node9 --> node10["OUTPUT BLOCK"]
```

### 4.2 Concept Map: From Loading to Material Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Simple Shear Loading] --> B[Shear Stress tau]
    B --> C[Shear Strain gamma]
    C --> D[Modulus of Rigidity G]
    B --> E[Strain Energy U]
    D --> F[Relation with E and mu]
    F --> G[Bulk Modulus K]
    F --> H[Young's Modulus E]
    F --> I[Poisson's Ratio mu]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description (Text-Based Rendering)

For a square element of side **a** under pure shear (representative figure):

```
  τ (top face, →→→ direction)
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  ↘                ↗│  ↑ γ (angle of distortion)
  │   ↘            ↗  │
  │    ↘          ↗   │
  │     ●          ●  │
  │    ↗          ↘   │
  │   ↗            ↘  │
  │  ↗                ↘│
  └────────────────────┘
  ↑ bottom face ←←← direction
       ↑ τ (right face, ↑ direction)
       → τ (left face, ↓ direction)
```

**Key features visible:**
- All four faces carry shear stresses **τ** (with complementary directions).
- The element distorts from a square into a **rhombus**.
- The angle of distortion at each corner is **γ** radians.
- Diagonal AC stretches; diagonal BD contracts — these become the **principal stress directions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid cannot accurately render vector arrows on quadrilaterals in a physically faithful way, so the schematic above uses ASCII art for clarity. The block-level functional architecture in the Mermaid diagrams conveys the underlying physical logic with full accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define shear stress and shear strain. State Hooke's law in shear.**

**Model Answer:**

**Shear Stress (τ):** It is the intensity of force acting tangentially (parallel) to the cross-section of a body. It is given by:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A}$$

where P = tangential force and A = area of the resisting surface. Unit: **N/m² (Pa)**.

**Shear Strain (γ):** It is the ratio of change in shape (lateral displacement δ) to the perpendicular distance L over which the displacement occurs:

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{L}$$

Unit: **radians** (dimensionless).

**Hooke's Law in Shear:** Within the elastic limit, shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain:

$$\tau \propto \gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau = G\,\gamma$$

where **G** is the **Modulus of Rigidity** (Shear Modulus).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the theorem of complementary shear stresses.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** *If a body is subjected to a shear stress τ_xy on one plane, then an equal shear stress τ_yx is automatically induced on a perpendicular plane, both acting along or across the common edge in complementary directions such that rotational equilibrium is satisfied.*

**Proof:** Consider an infinitesimal element of size **dx × dy × 1** (unit thickness). The shear stress on the top face is **τ_xy**, producing a horizontal force τ_xy · dx. The shear stress on the right face is **τ_yx**, producing a vertical force τ_yx · dy.

For **rotational equilibrium** of the element (sum of moments = 0), and equating moments about the bottom-left corner:

$$\sum M = \tau_{xy}(dx)(1)(dy) - \tau_{yx}(dy)(1)(dx) = 0$$

Since these two forces form a couple and the only way to balance moments is:

$$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$$

Hence, **complementary shear stresses are equal in magnitude** (Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Note:** As per the PCAGT302 2024 scheme, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts: (a) theory/numerical and (b) derivation/application.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Modulus of Rigidity (G)**, and **Poisson's Ratio (μ)** for a homogeneous, isotropic material.

**(b)** For a steel shaft of diameter **40 mm**, transmitting a torque of **1.2 kN·m**, find:
1. The maximum shear stress induced.
2. The angle of twist per metre length, if **G = 80 GPa**.

**Model Solution (a):**

This derivation has been exhaustively shown in **Section 3.1** above. The final result is:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\mu)}}$$

(7 marks — full derivation with clear principal stress diagram and Hooke's law application)

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Diameter d = 40 mm = 0.04 m → Radius R = 20 mm = 0.02 m
- Torque T = 1.2 kN·m = 1200 N·m
- G = 80 GPa = 80 × 10⁹ Pa
- Length L = 1 m

**Step 1: Polar moment of inertia**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.04)^{4}}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 2: Maximum shear stress (at outer surface, r = R)**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{1200 \times 0.02}{2.513 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{24}{2.513 \times 10^{-7}} = 95.49 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 95.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3: Angle of twist per metre**

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{J \cdot G} = \frac{1200}{(2.513 \times 10^{-7})(80 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{1200}{20.106} = 59.68 \text{ rad/m}$$

Converting to degrees: $59.68 \times (180/\pi) = 3419.7°/\text{m}$

$$\boxed{\frac{\theta}{L} \approx 59.68 \text{ radians/m} \approx 3420°/\text{m}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A rivet of **20 mm diameter** joins two plates of **12 mm thickness** each in a **double-shear lap joint**. If the rivet is subjected to a load of **60 kN**, find the average shear stress induced. The shear modulus of the rivet material is **80 GPa**. Also determine the **shear deformation** of the rivet.

**(b)** Explain **strain energy due to shear** with a neat sketch and derive the expression for strain energy density.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:**
- Diameter d = 20 mm = 0.02 m
- Plate thickness t = 12 mm = 0.012 m (each plate)
- Load P = 60 kN = 60,000 N
- G = 80 GPa = 80 × 10⁹ Pa
- **Double shear** (rivet has two shear planes).

**Step 1: Cross-sectional area of rivet**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.02)^{2}}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Shear stress (double shear → 2 planes share the load)**

$$\tau = \frac{P}{2A} = \frac{60{,}000}{2 \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\tau = \frac{60{,}000}{6.283 \times 10^{-4}} = 95.49 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 95.5 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 3: Shear strain γ**

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{95.49 \times 10^{6}}{80 \times 10^{9}} = 1.194 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4: Shear deformation δ**

$$\delta = \gamma \cdot t = 1.194 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.012 = 1.43 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 14.3 \, \mu m \text{ (micrometres)}}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

Refer to **Section 3.3** above for the complete derivation.

**Summary of derivation:**

- For an element under pure shear τ with shear strain γ, the work done per unit volume (strain energy density) is:

$$u = \frac{1}{2}\,\tau\,\gamma$$

- Using $\gamma = \tau / G$:

$$\boxed{u = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G}}$$

- For total volume V:

$$U = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G} \cdot V$$

**Sketch:** A square element with shear stresses τ on all four faces, distorting into a rhombus. Strain energy is stored due to this angular distortion (no volumetric change).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in double shear:** In double-shear riveted joints, students often compute τ = P/A instead of τ = P/(2A). This loses **3 out of 7 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Wrong formula for G:** Confusing $G = E/[2(1+\mu)]$ with $G = E/[2(1-\mu)]$ — the **minus** sign instead of **plus** is a very common error. The correct one has **+μ**.
> 3. **Units of γ:** Writing γ in degrees instead of radians will lose marks. Always convert: γ (rad) = γ (deg) × π/180.
> 4. **In torsion problems:** Using **J = πd³/16** (polar section modulus) instead of **J = πd⁴/32** (polar moment of inertia) is a frequent mistake when computing angle of twist.
> 5. **Forgetting to verify elastic limit:** When given a load and asked for deformation, ensure the shear stress remains within the elastic limit (τ < τ_allowable) — otherwise Hooke's Law is invalid.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Simple Shear**

- ✅ **Shear stress** $\tau = P/A$ — tangential force per unit area; unit = Pa (N/m²).
- ✅ **Shear strain** $\gamma = \delta/L$ — dimensionless; unit = rad.
- ✅ **Hooke's Law in Shear:** $\tau = G \gamma$ where **G** = Modulus of Rigidity.
- ✅ **Complementary Shear Theorem:** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ (Maxwell-Betti Reciprocal).
- ✅ **Key Material Relation:** $\boxed{G = E/[2(1+\mu)]}$ — must be memorized.
- ✅ **Bulk Modulus:** $K = E/[3(1-2\mu)]$.
- ✅ **Identity:** $\dfrac{E}{9K} + \dfrac{3G}{E} = 1$.
- ✅ **Strain Energy Density in Shear:** $u = \tau^{2}/(2G)$.
- ✅ **Torsion is Pure Shear:** Every cross-section of a circular shaft under torque is in pure shear; $\tau_{max} = TR/J$ at the outer fibre.
- ✅ **Single vs Double Shear:** Single shear → one shear plane (τ = P/A); Double shear → two shear planes (τ = P/2A).
- ✅ **Typical G value for steel:** ~77 GPa (use 80 GPa in KTU problems unless stated otherwise).
- ✅ **For pure shear state, principal stresses are equal and opposite:** $\sigma_1 = +\tau$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ at 45° to the original planes.
- ✅ **Pure shear does not change volume** (it is a constant-volume deformation, neglecting thermal effects).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Punching Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Punching Shear**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Punching Shear** is a localized, brittle failure mechanism that occurs in a structural element (typically a flat slab, raft foundation, or pile cap) when a concentrated load — such as that transferred from a column — is applied over a small contact area. The shear stress developed on a vertical surface (called the *critical perimeter* or *critical section*) located at a distance of **d/2** from the periphery of the loaded area causes the column to appear to "punch" through the slab along a truncated pyramid (or cone) shaped failure surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAGT302 Module 2):**
> "Punching shear is the failure of a slab or foundation under the action of a concentrated load, characterized by a localized shear failure surface inclined at approximately 45° around the periphery of the loaded area. The critical section for design is taken at a perimeter of `d/2` from the face of the column, where `d` is the effective depth of the slab."

In the equation form, the **nominal punching shear stress** is expressed as:

$$\tau_v = \frac{V}{b_0 \cdot d}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Factored (design) shear force (in N or kN)
- $b_0$ = Critical perimeter of the section (in mm or m)
- $d$ = Effective depth of the slab (in mm or m)
- $\tau_v$ = Nominal punching shear stress (in N/mm² or MPa)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Real-World" Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a thick metal rod straight down onto a slab of wet clay or a piece of cheese on a table.

- The **column** is the metal rod (small, concentrated contact).
- The **slab** is the clay or cheese (larger, supporting surface).
- When you press hard, the rod does **not** bend the cheese uniformly — instead, a chunk of cheese directly below the rod gets *punched out* and pushed downwards.
- The **side walls** of the punched-out chunk (an inverted pyramid) is exactly the **critical section** where shear failure occurs.
- The failure surface is **not** a straight cylinder but a **truncated cone/pyramid** with sides inclined at about **45°**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:**
> Punching shear is a **3D** phenomenon — it occurs in two perpendicular directions simultaneously, forming a closed failure perimeter around the column. This is why we calculate a *perimeter* $b_0$, not a single shear plane like in beam shear.

## 1.3 Where Does Punching Shear Occur in Practice?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Locations in Real Structures:**
> 1. **Flat slab – Column junction** (most common in KTU problems)
> 2. **Raft/Pile cap foundation – Column junction**
> 3. **Footing – Column junction** (isolated/spread footings)
> 4. **Drop panel edges** in flat slabs with capitals
> 5. **Beam – Slab junctions** at highly loaded points

## 1.4 The Two Design Philosophies

| Approach | Description | KTU Preference |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Without Shear Reinforcement** | Slab thickness is increased so that $\tau_v \leq \tau_c$ (design shear strength of concrete) | Preferred for typical KTU problems |
| **With Shear Reinforcement** | Shear studs/bars are provided to enhance capacity | Advanced KTU elective topics |

> [!NOTE]
> As per **IS 456:2000 (Cl. 31.6)** and the KTU 2024 syllabus, the critical section is taken at `d/2` from the face of the column for slabs and footings, with the corners of the critical perimeter effectively "rounded" to a smooth curve (in practice, calculations assume the corners are cut at 45° between the straight portions).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Punching Shear Failure

Let us break down *why* and *how* punching shear develops:

- **Step 1 — Load Transfer Initiation:** A column carries an axial load $P$ which it tries to transfer into the supporting slab. The contact area is small (column cross-section $a \times b$).

- **Step 2 — Stress Concentration:** The compressive stress beneath the column is very high: $\sigma = P/(a \cdot b)$. This stress must "spread out" into the slab to reach equilibrium.

- **Step 3 — Inclined Compression Struts:** The load spreads along inclined struts (inclined at ~45°) — these are analogous to the **diagonal compression struts in a strut-and-tie model**.

- **Step 4 — Tensile Ring Formation:** As the inclined struts push outward and downward, they create a **circumferential tensile stress** (a "hoop tension") in the plane of the slab, *just above* the critical perimeter.

- **Step 5 — Shear Failure Surface:** When the tensile stresses exceed the tensile strength of concrete, a crack forms. This crack propagates along a **truncated cone/pyramid** with its top at the column base and bottom at the critical perimeter (at `d/2` from the column face).

- **Step 6 — Brittle Failure:** Unlike flexural failure (ductile, with warning), punching shear failure is **sudden, brittle, and catastrophic** — the column literally punches through the slab.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `d/2`?**
> The `d/2` distance is derived from the **45° inclined failure plane** assumption. The crack travels a horizontal distance of `d/2` for a vertical drop of `d` (the effective depth), giving a slope of `tan(45°) = 1`, hence the `d/2` perimeter offset.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter / Formula** | **Expression** | **Unit / Notes** |
|-------|------------------------|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | Nominal Punching Shear Stress | $\tau_v = \dfrac{V}{b_0 \cdot d}$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 2 | Critical Perimeter (Rectangular Column, Interior) | $b_0 = 2\left[(a+d) + (b+d)\right] = 2(a+b+2d)$ | mm |
| 3 | Critical Perimeter (Rectangular Column, Edge) | $b_0 = 2a + b + 2d$ (slab edge parallel to long side) | mm |
| 4 | Critical Perimeter (Rectangular Column, Corner) | $b_0 = a + b + 2d$ | mm |
| 5 | Critical Perimeter (Circular Column, Interior) | $b_0 = \pi (D + d)$ | mm |
| 6 | Effective Depth | $d = h - \text{cover} - \frac{\phi}{2}$ | mm |
| 7 | Design Shear Strength of Concrete | $\tau_c$ (from IS 456 Table 19) | N/mm² |
| 8 | Permissible Punching Shear Stress (Slab) | $\tau_{c,\text{per}} = k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c \leq \tau_{c,\text{max}}$ | N/mm² |
| 9 | Column Side Ratio Factor | $k = 0.5 + \beta_c \leq 1.0$ (for $\beta_c \leq 2.0$); $k = 1.0$ (for $\beta_c > 2.0$) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Depth Modification Factor | $s = 1.0$ for $h \leq 150$ mm; $s = 0.75$ for $h \geq 300$ mm; linear interpolation for $150 < h < 300$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Maximum Allowable Shear Stress | $\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 0.25 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ for slabs (IS 456 Table 20) | N/mm² |
| 12 | Shear Capacity of Section | $V_c = k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c \cdot b_0 \cdot d$ | N |
| 13 | Factored Shear Force at Critical Section | $V = P_u - (\text{Load on area outside critical perimeter})$ | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where $\beta_c$ is the ratio of the longer side to the shorter side of the column cross-section. The factor $k$ accounts for the concentration of shear at column corners in rectangular sections.**

## 2.3 The Critical Perimeter — Geometric Visualization

For an **interior rectangular column** of dimensions $a \times b$ (where $a$ = long side, $b$ = short side), the critical perimeter is constructed as follows:

- Draw a line parallel to each column face at a perpendicular distance of `d/2`.
- Round the corners — each corner is taken as a quarter-circle of radius `d/2` (effectively "rounded corners").
- For numerical calculation in KTU exams, this rounded perimeter is **simplified** by:
  - Two long sides: each of length $(a + d)$
  - Two short sides: each of length $(b + d)$
  - **Total:** $b_0 = 2(a + d) + 2(b + d) = 2(a + b + 2d)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we ignore the rounded corners in calculations?**
> The rounding has a **negligible effect** on the total perimeter length (less than 2% error) and greatly simplifies computation. KTU examiners expect the simplified rectangular form unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway Bridge Decks:** Heavy wheel loads on bridge decks are checked for punching shear, especially under tandem/truck axles.
- **Raft Foundations:** Column loads punching through raft foundations are critical design checks in high-rise buildings.
- **Pile Caps:** Multiple piles supporting a column must be designed so that the column does not punch through the pile cap.
- **Flat Slab Buildings:** A dominant proportion of KTU Module 2 problems deal with **flat slab – interior column junctions**.
- **Industrial Machine Foundations:** Concentrated dynamic loads (e.g., turbines, presses) create punching shear risks.

## 2.5 The Two Safety Criteria

A slab section is **safe against punching shear** if **both** conditions are satisfied:

1. **Stress Check (Serviceability / Strength):**
   $$\tau_v \leq k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c$$
   (i.e., applied stress $\leq$ permissible stress of concrete)

2. **Maximum Shear Stress Limit (Brittle Failure Prevention):**
   $$\tau_v \leq \tau_{c,\text{max}} = 0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Exams:** Students often forget to apply the **maximum shear stress limit** check. The section may pass the first check but fail the second if the slab is too thin — this is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nominal Punching Shear Stress Formula

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider an interior column of dimensions $a \times b$ supporting a flat slab of effective depth $d$. The critical section is a vertical surface formed at a horizontal distance of `d/2` from the periphery of the column, going through the full depth of the slab.

**Assumptions:**
1. Failure occurs along a vertical surface at `d/2` from the column face (per IS 456 / KTU convention).
2. The shear stress is **uniformly distributed** over the critical perimeter area ($b_0 \cdot d$).
3. The column transmits a **factored axial load** $P_u$ to the slab.

### 3.1.2 The Derivation

**Step 1:** The total vertical force on the critical perimeter surface equals the net upward load trying to "push" the column through the slab. For a simplified KTU-level analysis, we take this as the **full factored column load** $P_u$ (a more refined analysis subtracts the upward pressure acting on the area outside the critical perimeter, but KTU Module 2 typically uses the simplified version):

$$V = P_u$$

**Step 2:** The critical perimeter for an interior rectangular column is the sum of the four straight sides (with rounded corners accounted for implicitly):

$$\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= (a + d) + (b + d) + (a + d) + (b + d) \\
b_0 &= 2(a + d) + 2(b + d) \\
b_0 &= 2(a + b) + 4d \\
b_0 &= 2(a + b + 2d)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** The area of the critical shear surface (the vertical face on which shear acts) is:

$$A_{cs} = b_0 \cdot d$$

**Step 4:** By the fundamental definition of **shear stress** (force per unit area):

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_v &= \frac{V}{A_{cs}} \\
\tau_v &= \frac{V}{b_0 \cdot d}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the master equation used in **every** KTU punching shear problem.

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A flat slab of effective depth $d = 200$ mm is supported by an interior rectangular column of size $300 \text{ mm} \times 450 \text{ mm}$. The factored load on the column is $P_u = 1200$ kN. Calculate the nominal punching shear stress.

**Given:**
- $a = 450$ mm (long side)
- $b = 300$ mm (short side)
- $d = 200$ mm
- $V = P_u = 1200 \text{ kN} = 1200 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the critical perimeter $b_0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= 2(a + b + 2d) \\
b_0 &= 2(450 + 300 + 2 \times 200) \\
b_0 &= 2(450 + 300 + 400) \\
b_0 &= 2(1150) \\
b_0 &= 2300 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the critical shear area:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{cs} &= b_0 \cdot d \\
A_{cs} &= 2300 \times 200 \\
A_{cs} &= 460{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the nominal punching shear stress:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_v &= \frac{V}{b_0 \cdot d} \\
\tau_v &= \frac{1200 \times 10^3}{460{,}000} \\
\tau_v &= 2.6087 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\tau_v \approx 2.61$ N/mm²

**Step 4 (Safety Check):** For $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm² and $p_t = 0.5\%$ (assumed), $\tau_c \approx 0.49$ N/mm² (from IS 456 Table 19). Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta_c &= \frac{a}{b} = \frac{450}{300} = 1.5 \\
k &= 0.5 + \beta_c = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.0 \quad \text{(capped at 1.0)} \\
k &= 1.0 \\
s &= 1.0 \quad (\text{since } h \leq 150 \text{ mm assumed, or use linear interpolation}) \\
\tau_{c,\text{per}} &= k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c = 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.49 = 0.49 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\tau_v = 2.61$ N/mm² $\gg \tau_{c,\text{per}} = 0.49$ N/mm², **the slab fails in punching shear**. Either increase slab depth, use a drop panel, or provide shear reinforcement.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Column Side Ratio Factor $k$

**Physical Basis:** For square columns, shear is uniformly distributed on all four faces of the critical perimeter. For very elongated rectangular columns, shear concentrates on the two short faces. The factor $k$ accounts for this.

**Empirical Basis (from tests, codified in IS 456):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\beta_c &= \frac{\text{Long side of column}}{\text{Short side of column}} = \frac{a}{b} \\
k &= 0.5 + \beta_c \quad \text{when } \beta_c \leq 2.0 \\
k &= 1.0 \quad \text{when } \beta_c > 2.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Reasoning:** For a square column ($\beta_c = 1.0$): $k = 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5$ — but $k$ is capped at 1.0, so $k = 1.0$. This means square columns get full design strength. For a column with $\beta_c = 2.0$: $k = 0.5 + 2.0 = 2.5$ — capped at 1.0. For a column with $\beta_c = 1.5$: $k = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.0$ — capped at 1.0.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive observation:** Since $k$ is capped at 1.0, the factor $k$ only becomes *less* than 1.0 when $\beta_c < 0.5$, i.e., when the column is much wider than it is deep (extremely rare). For most practical columns, $k = 1.0$ in KTU problems. The factor becomes *important* only for $\beta_c < 1.0$ — but here, $a$ and $b$ are always defined with $a \geq b$, so $\beta_c \geq 1.0$ always. **This means $k = 1.0$ in practically every KTU problem.**

## 3.3 Maximum Shear Stress Limit — Derivation Check

The maximum shear stress limit prevents **brittle crushing** of concrete at the critical section. From **IS 456 Cl. 40.2.3** and **Table 20**, the limit is:

$$\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 0.25 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

This is derived from the **principal stress criterion**: shear failure occurs when the principal tensile stress equals the tensile strength of concrete. Using Mohr's circle for uniaxial compression converted to shear:

$$f_t = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}} \quad \text{(tensile strength of concrete)}$$

For pure shear, the principal tensile stress equals the shear stress. Setting this equal to $f_t$ and applying a safety factor of 2 gives approximately $0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.

**Values Table:**

| $f_{ck}$ (N/mm²) | $\tau_{c,\text{max}}$ (N/mm²) |
|------------------|-------------------------------|
| 20 | 1.12 |
| 25 | 1.25 |
| 30 | 1.37 |
| 35 | 1.48 |
| 40 | 1.58 |

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Punching Shear Design Flowchart

| **Step** | **Action** | **Output** |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Identify column location (interior / edge / corner) | Determines critical perimeter formula |
| 2 | Compute $d = h - \text{clear cover} - \phi/2$ | Effective depth in mm |
| 3 | Compute $b_0$ using the correct formula | Critical perimeter in mm |
| 4 | Compute $V$ (factored load minus upward pressure outside critical perimeter, if applicable) | Design shear force in N |
| 5 | Compute $\tau_v = V/(b_0 \cdot d)$ | Nominal shear stress in N/mm² |
| 6 | Compute $\beta_c$, $k$, and $s$ | Modification factors (dimensionless) |
| 7 | Look up $\tau_c$ from IS 456 Table 19 (based on $p_t$ and $f_{ck}$) | Design shear strength in N/mm² |
| 8 | Compute $\tau_{c,\text{per}} = k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c$ | Permissible shear stress in N/mm² |
| 9 | **Check 1:** Is $\tau_v \leq \tau_{c,\text{per}}$? | Strength criterion |
| 10 | **Check 2:** Is $\tau_v \leq 0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}}$? | Maximum limit criterion |
| 11 | If both checks pass → **SAFE** | Design is adequate |
| 12 | If either check fails → Increase $d$ / provide shear reinforcement / add drop panel | Redesign |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matrix is gold for KTU exams:**
> The matrix provides a *systematic* 12-step procedure. If you follow it in your answer sheet, the examiner can award **method marks** even if your final numerical answer has a small error — this is a standard KTU valuation practice.

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Punching Shear Failure Geometry

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Column of Size a x b"] --> B["Apply Factored Load Pu at Column Top"]
    B --> C["Concentrated Stress Develops Below Column Base"]
    C --> D["Load Spreads Along 45 Degree Inclined Struts"]
    D --> E["Tensile Hoop Stress Forms in Slab Plane"]
    E --> F["Critical Perimeter Forms at d/2 from Column Face"]
    F --> G["Truncated Pyramid Failure Surface Develops"]
    G --> H{"Shear Stress Exceeds Concrete Strength?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Brittle Punching Shear Failure - Column Punches Through Slab"]
    H -- No --> J["Slab Section is SAFE"]
    I --> K["Remedial Action: Increase d OR Provide Shear Reinforcement OR Add Drop Panel"]
    K --> L["Re-evaluate with New Geometry"]
    L --> F
    J --> M["Continue with Flexural Design Checks"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — KTU Punching Shear Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Given Column Size, Slab Depth, Loads, fck, pt"] --> S2["Calculate Effective Depth d"]
    S2 --> S3["Identify Column Location: Interior, Edge, or Corner"]
    S3 --> S4["Choose Correct Critical Perimeter Formula"]
    S4 --> S5["Calculate Critical Perimeter b0"]
    S5 --> S6["Calculate Factored Shear Force V = Pu"]
    S6 --> S7["Compute Nominal Shear Stress tau_v = V / b0 x d"]
    S7 --> S8["Compute beta_c = a / b"]
    S8 --> S9["Compute k = 0.5 + beta_c, capped at 1.0"]
    S9 --> S10["Compute s based on slab thickness h"]
    S10 --> S11["Look up tau_c from IS 456 Table 19"]
    S11 --> S12["Compute Permissible Stress tau_c_per = k x s x tau_c"]
    S12 --> C1{"Is tau_v less than or equal to tau_c_per?"}
    C1 -- No --> F1["FAIL: Increase d or Add Shear Reinforcement"]
    C1 -- Yes --> C2{"Is tau_v less than or equal to 0.25 x sqrt fck?"}
    C2 -- No --> F2["FAIL: Concrete Crushing Risk - Increase d"]
    C2 -- Yes --> PASS["PASS: Section is SAFE against Punching Shear"]
    F1 --> S2
    F2 --> S2
```
