# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define punching shear. Where does it occur in reinforced concrete structures?**

**Model Answer:**

Punching shear is a localized shear failure that occurs in a structural member (typically a slab) when a concentrated load is applied over a small area, causing the loaded element (e.g., column) to "punch" through the supporting member along a truncated pyramid failure surface.

**Occurrence locations:**
1. Flat slab at the column-slab junction (most common)
2. Raft foundations at column junctions
3. Pile caps under columns
4. Footings supporting columns
5. Beam-column junctions under heavy concentrated loads

The critical section for design is taken at a perimeter of `d/2` from the face of the column, where `d` is the effective depth of the slab.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**State the formula for nominal punching shear stress. What is the significance of the `d/2` distance for locating the critical section?**

**Model Answer:**

The nominal punching shear stress is given by:

$$\tau_v = \frac{V}{b_0 \cdot d}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Factored shear force
- $b_0$ = Critical perimeter at `d/2` from column face
- $d$ = Effective depth of the slab

**Significance of `d/2` distance:**

The `d/2` distance is based on the assumption that the failure surface is inclined at approximately 45° to the horizontal. For a vertical drop equal to the effective depth $d$, the horizontal projection of the inclined surface is `d/2` (using the trigonometric relationship $\tan(45°) = 1$, hence horizontal offset = vertical drop / 1 = d/2). This locates the critical perimeter where shear stress is maximum and where failure is most likely to initiate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is divided into sub-parts. KTU provides internal choice — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are presented below as alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024]`

A flat slab floor system has the following details at an interior column location:
- Column size: $400 \text{ mm} \times 600 \text{ mm}$ (long side parallel to slab edge)
- Slab overall depth: $h = 250$ mm
- Effective cover: 30 mm; bar diameter $\phi = 16$ mm
- Factored column load: $P_u = 1800$ kN
- Concrete grade: M25 ($f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm²)
- Steel percentage: $p_t = 0.75\%$
- Slab depth (overall): $h = 250$ mm

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Check whether the slab is safe against punching shear at the interior column.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** If the slab is not safe, suggest suitable remedial measures with calculations.

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### **Part (a): Punching Shear Safety Check**

**Step 1 — Effective Depth:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= h - \text{effective cover} \\
d &= 250 - 30 \\
d &= 220 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Critical Perimeter for Interior Column:**

For an interior rectangular column:
$$\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= 2(a + b + 2d) \\
b_0 &= 2(600 + 400 + 2 \times 220) \\
b_0 &= 2(600 + 400 + 440) \\
b_0 &= 2(1440) \\
b_0 &= 2880 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Nominal Punching Shear Stress:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_v &= \frac{V}{b_0 \cdot d} \\
\tau_v &= \frac{1800 \times 10^3}{2880 \times 220} \\
\tau_v &= \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{633{,}600} \\
\tau_v &= 2.841 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Permissible Shear Stress:**

**Column side ratio factor:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta_c &= \frac{600}{400} = 1.5 \\
k &= 0.5 + \beta_c = 0.5 + 1.5 = 2.0 \quad \text{(capped at 1.0)} \\
k &= 1.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Depth modification factor** (for $h = 250$ mm, linearly interpolated between 150 mm and 300 mm):
$$\begin{aligned}
s &= 1.0 - \frac{(250 - 150)}{(300 - 150)} \times (1.0 - 0.75) \\
s &= 1.0 - \frac{100}{150} \times 0.25 \\
s &= 1.0 - 0.1667 \\
s &= 0.8333
\end{aligned}$$

**Design shear strength** (from IS 456 Table 19, for $p_t = 0.75\%$ and $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm²):
$$\tau_c \approx 0.60 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (interpolated from table values)}$$

**Permissible shear stress:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{c,\text{per}} &= k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c \\
\tau_{c,\text{per}} &= 1.0 \times 0.8333 \times 0.60 \\
\tau_{c,\text{per}} &= 0.500 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Check 1: Strength Criterion**

$$\tau_v = 2.841 \text{ N/mm}^2 \gg \tau_{c,\text{per}} = 0.500 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**❌ FAILS the strength check.**

**Step 6 — Check 2: Maximum Shear Stress Limit**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{c,\text{max}} &= 0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}} \\
\tau_{c,\text{max}} &= 0.25\sqrt{25} \\
\tau_{c,\text{max}} &= 0.25 \times 5 \\
\tau_{c,\text{max}} &= 1.25 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\tau_v = 2.841 \text{ N/mm}^2 \gg \tau_{c,\text{max}} = 1.25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**❌ FAILS the maximum limit check.**

**Conclusion for Part (a):** The slab is **NOT SAFE** against punching shear. Both checks fail.

---

#### **Part (b): Remedial Measures**

**Option 1: Provide a Drop Panel**

A drop panel of effective depth $d_d$ below the slab will increase the effective depth at the column. Let us find the required effective depth $d_{\text{req}}$.

**Required effective depth (ignoring depth factor for new design):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_v &\leq \tau_{c,\text{per}} \\
\frac{V}{b_0 \cdot d_{\text{req}}} &\leq k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c \\
\frac{1800 \times 10^3}{2(600 + 400 + 2 d_{\text{req}}) \cdot d_{\text{req}}} &\leq 1.0 \times 0.8333 \times 0.60 \\
\frac{1800 \times 10^3}{2(1000 + 2 d_{\text{req}}) \cdot d_{\text{req}}} &\leq 0.500
\end{aligned}$$

**Cross-multiplying:**
$$\begin{aligned}
1800 \times 10^3 &\leq 0.500 \times 2 \times (1000 + 2 d_{\text{req}}) \cdot d_{\text{req}} \\
1800 \times 10^3 &\leq (1000 + 2 d_{\text{req}}) \cdot d_{\text{req}} \\
1800 \times 10^3 &\leq 1000 d_{\text{req}} + 2 d_{\text{req}}^2 \\
2 d_{\text{req}}^2 + 1000 d_{\text{req}} - 1{,}800{,}000 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Solving the quadratic equation $2 d^2 + 1000 d - 1{,}800{,}000 = 0$:**

Using the quadratic formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{req}} &= \frac{-1000 \pm \sqrt{1000^2 - 4 \times 2 \times (-1{,}800{,}000)}}{2 \times 2} \\
d_{\text{req}} &= \frac{-1000 \pm \sqrt{1{,}000{,}000 + 14{,}400{,}000}}{4} \\
d_{\text{req}} &= \frac{-1000 \pm \sqrt{15{,}400{,}000}}{4} \\
d_{\text{req}} &= \frac{-1000 \pm 3924.95}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

Taking the positive root:
$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{req}} &= \frac{2924.95}{4} \\
d_{\text{req}} &\approx 731 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

This is impractically large, indicating that **simply increasing slab depth is not economical**.

**Option 2: Provide Shear Reinforcement (Shear Studs)** — KTU Advanced Topic

Shear reinforcement in the form of **vertical stirrups or studs** placed through the slab depth near the column can resist punching shear. The capacity becomes:

$$V_{cs} = V_c + V_{s,\text{shear}}$$

where $V_{s,\text{shear}}$ is the contribution of shear reinforcement (designed as per IS 456 Cl. 31.6 and detailed in advanced design courses).

**Option 3: Provide Column Capital / Drop Panel — KTU Recommended**

A **drop panel** (thickened portion of slab around the column) increases $d$ locally and is the most common solution in KTU Module 2 problems.

**Recommended Action:** Provide a **drop panel of 100 mm depth** below the slab, making the total effective depth at the column:

$$d_{\text{new}} = 220 + 100 = 320 \text{ mm} \text{ (use 100 mm drop)}$$

**Re-verify with $d = 320$ mm:**

$$\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= 2(600 + 400 + 2 \times 320) = 2(1640) = 3280 \text{ mm} \\
\tau_v &= \frac{1800 \times 10^3}{3280 \times 320} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{1{,}049{,}600} = 1.715 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

This is still $> 0.500$ N/mm², so even 100 mm drop is insufficient. **Try a 200 mm drop panel:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{\text{new}} &= 220 + 200 = 420 \text{ mm} \\
b_0 &= 2(600 + 400 + 2 \times 420) = 2(1840) = 3680 \text{ mm} \\
\tau_v &= \frac{1800 \times 10^3}{3680 \times 420} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{1{,}545{,}600} = 1.165 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

For $h = 450$ mm (250 slab + 200 drop): $s$ is interpolated; even if $s = 0.75$, $\tau_{c,\text{per}} = 1.0 \times 0.75 \times 0.60 = 0.45$ N/mm², still failing.

**Conclusion for Part (b):** Both options (shear reinforcement and drop panel) must be considered together; a combination of a **drop panel + shear reinforcement** is the practical design solution. For KTU Module 2, the **drop panel of depth ≥ 250 mm** is the recommended primary remedial measure.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Alternative]`

An isolated square footing supports a 400 mm × 400 mm column carrying a factored load of $P_u = 900$ kN. The footing has an effective depth of $d = 450$ mm and is made of M30 concrete with steel percentage $p_t = 0.5\%$.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Check the footing for punching shear failure.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A shear key (trapezoidal projection below the column) is proposed to enhance capacity. The shear key has a depth of 200 mm. Verify if this is sufficient.

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### **Part (a): Punching Shear Check for Footing**

**Given:**
- Column size: $a = b = 400$ mm
- Effective depth: $d = 450$ mm
- Factored load: $P_u = 900$ kN = $900 \times 10^3$ N
- $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm², $p_t = 0.5\%$

**Step 1 — Critical Perimeter:**

For interior column with $a = b = 400$ mm (square):
$$\begin{aligned}
b_0 &= 2(a + b + 2d) \\
b_0 &= 2(400 + 400 + 2 \times 450) \\
b_0 &= 2(400 + 400 + 900) \\
b_0 &= 2(1700) \\
b_0 &= 3400 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Nominal Shear Stress:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_v &= \frac{V}{b_0 \cdot d} \\
\tau_v &= \frac{900 \times 10^3}{3400 \times 450} \\
\tau_v &= \frac{900{,}000}{1{,}530{,}000} \\
\tau_v &= 0.588 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Permissible Shear Stress:**

**Column side ratio:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\beta_c &= \frac{400}{400} = 1.0 \\
k &= 0.5 + 1.0 = 1.5 \quad \text{(capped at 1.0)} \\
k &= 1.0
\end{aligned}$$

**Depth factor** (for footing, $h$ is usually > 300 mm): $s = 0.75$

**Design shear strength** (from IS 456 Table 19, for $p_t = 0.5\%$ and $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm²): $\tau_c \approx 0.51$ N/mm²

**Permissible stress:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{c,\text{per}} &= k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c \\
\tau_{c,\text{per}} &= 1.0 \times 0.75 \times 0.51 \\
\tau_{c,\text{per}} &= 0.3825 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Check 1: Strength**

$$\tau_v = 0.588 \text{ N/mm}^2 > \tau_{c,\text{per}} = 0.3825 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**❌ FAILS strength check.**

**Step 5 — Check 2: Maximum Limit**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{c,\text{max}} &= 0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}} \\
\tau_{c,\text{max}} &= 0.25\sqrt{30} \\
\tau_{c,\text{max}} &= 0.25 \times 5.477 \\
\tau_{c,\text{max}} &= 1.369 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\tau_v = 0.588 < 1.369 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{✓ Passes maximum limit check}$$

**Conclusion for Part (a):** Slab is **NOT SAFE** (fails strength check but passes maximum limit).

---

#### **Part (b): Shear Key Verification**

With a 200 mm shear key, the **new effective depth** increases to:

$$d_{\text{new}} = 450 + 200 = 650 \text{ mm}$$

**Recompute critical perimeter:**
$$\begin{aligned}
b_{0,\text{new}} &= 2(400 + 400 + 2 \times 650) \\
b_{0,\text{new}} &= 2(400 + 400 + 1300) \\
b_{0,\text{new}} &= 2(2100) \\
b_{0,\text{new}} &= 4200 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Recompute nominal shear stress:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{v,\text{new}} &= \frac{900 \times 10^3}{4200 \times 650} \\
\tau_{v,\text{new}} &= \frac{900{,}000}{2{,}730{,}000} \\
\tau_{v,\text{new}} &= 0.330 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Recompute permissible stress** (assume $h_{\text{new}} > 300$ mm, so $s = 0.75$):
$$\tau_{c,\text{per}} = 1.0 \times 0.75 \times 0.51 = 0.3825 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Comparison:**
$$\tau_{v,\text{new}} = 0.330 \text{ N/mm}^2 < \tau_{c,\text{per}} = 0.3825 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{✓ SAFE}$$

**Conclusion for Part (b):** The 200 mm shear key is **sufficient** to make the footing safe against punching shear.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `d/2` offset:** Writing the critical perimeter as $2(a+b)$ instead of $2(a+b+2d)$. This single error loses **3 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the maximum stress check ($\tau_{c,\text{max}}$):** Even if the strength check passes, the maximum limit check is a **mandatory second check**. Skipping it loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not using depth factor $s$:** Forgetting to include $s = 0.75$ for thick slabs/footings gives a non-conservative (unsafe) answer and loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $V$ in kN and $b_0 \cdot d$ in mm² gives incorrect $\tau_v$ in units of kN/mm² (off by 1000×). Always convert $V$ to **N** before computing.
> 5. **Capping $k$ at 1.0:** Writing $k = 2.0$ for a column with $\beta_c = 1.5$ (instead of capping at 1.0) gives a non-conservative answer. Lose **1 mark**.
> 6. **Wrong critical perimeter for edge/corner columns:** Using the interior formula for an edge column. Lose **2-3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Punching shear** is a localized, brittle failure at concentrated load points (e.g., column-slab junctions).
- [x] **Critical section** is taken at `d/2` from the column face (per IS 456 / KTU 2024).
- [x] **Master equation:** $\tau_v = V / (b_0 \cdot d)$.
- [x] **Critical perimeter** for interior column: $b_0 = 2(a + b + 2d)$.
- [x] **Critical perimeter** for edge column: $b_0 = 2a + b + 2d$ (long side parallel to edge).
- [x] **Critical perimeter** for corner column: $b_0 = a + b + 2d$.
- [x] **Critical perimeter** for circular column (diameter $D$): $b_0 = \pi(D + d)$.
- [x] **Two safety checks** are mandatory: (1) $\tau_v \leq k \cdot s \cdot \tau_c$ and (2) $\tau_v \leq 0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
- [x] **Column side ratio factor:** $k = \min(0.5 + \beta_c, 1.0)$ where $\beta_c = a/b \geq 1.0$.
- [x] **Depth factor:** $s = 1.0$ for $h \leq 150$ mm, $s = 0.75$ for $h \geq 300$ mm, linear interpolation in between.
- [x] **$\tau_c$** is obtained from IS 456 Table 19 (interpolation needed between given $p_t$ and $f_{ck}$ values).
- [x] **$\tau_{c,\text{max}}$** values for common grades: M20 → 1.12, M25 → 1.25, M30 → 1.37, M35 → 1.48 N/mm².
- [x] **Failure is brittle** — unlike flexure, there is no warning. This is why punching shear checks are *critical* design steps.
- [x] **Remedial measures** (in order of preference): (1) Drop panel, (2) Column capital, (3) Shear reinforcement (studs/stirrups), (4) Increase slab depth.
- [x] **Unit conversion:** $V$ must be in **N** (multiply kN by $10^3$); $b_0$ and $d$ in **mm**; $\tau_v$ in **N/mm²** = MPa.
- [x] **Effective depth** $d = h - \text{effective cover} = h - (\text{clear cover} + \phi/2)$.
- [x] **Never confuse** punching shear (vertical shear) with one-way shear (in beams) — the failure geometry and critical section locations are fundamentally different.
- [x] **Knee-jerk rule of thumb for KTU:** If the column dimensions are small relative to slab depth, punching shear is the controlling design check; if columns are large (e.g., 600×600 mm) and slabs thin (e.g., 200 mm), one-way shear may be the controlling check.

---

> **END OF NOTES — KTU PCAGT302 Module 2: Punching Shear**
> *These notes are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, IS 456:2000, and standard Board valuation patterns. Use the formula sheet, decision matrix, and worked examples for guaranteed marks.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beams - Different Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Beams - Different Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beam (KTU Definition):** A structural member which is **subjected to transverse loading** (loads acting perpendicular to its longitudinal axis) and which develops **bending moment** and **shear force** as the principal internal reactions is called a **beam**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, beams are broadly classified as **one-dimensional flexural members** whose length $L$ is significantly greater than its cross-sectional dimensions ($L \gg h, b$). They transfer transverse loads to the supports through a combination of **bending moment ($M$)** and **transverse shear force ($V$)**.

The general governing equation for elastic flexure of beams (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory) is:

$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity ($\text{N/mm}^2$)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area about the Neutral Axis ($\text{mm}^4$)
- $y$ = Deflection of the beam at distance $x$ ($\text{mm}$)
- $M(x)$ = Bending moment as a function of $x$ ($\text{N·mm}$)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Diving Board**
> Imagine a **diving board** at a swimming pool. The board is fixed at one end (your feet stand on the free end). When you walk towards the free end, the board bends downward. It carries a **transverse load** (your weight acting vertically) but resists it by developing internal **bending stresses**. This is a **cantilever beam** in action.
>
> Now imagine a **wooden plank** resting on two bricks at either end, and you stand in the middle. The plank bends, and the bricks push back upward. This is a **simply supported beam**.

In essence, **every beam in any building, bridge, or machine component is simply fighting gravity** by developing internal bending resistance. The type of support decides *how much* it bends and *where* the maximum stress occurs.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Highlighted)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Standard Unit (FPS) |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Length | $L$ | $\text{m}$ | $\text{ft}$ |
| Load | $W$ or $P$ | $\text{kN}$ | $\text{lb}$ |
| Bending Moment | $M$ | $\text{kN·m}$ | $\text{lb·ft}$ |
| Shear Force | $V$ | $\text{kN}$ | $\text{lb}$ |
| Deflection | $\delta, y$ | $\text{mm}$ | $\text{in}$ |
| Flexural Rigidity | $EI$ | $\text{N·mm}^2$ | $\text{lb·in}^2$ |
| Slope | $\theta$ | $\text{radians}$ | $\text{radians}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** Downward loads and downward deflections are taken as **positive**. Sagging bending moments (concave upwards) are **positive**. Hogging moments (concave downwards) are **negative**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants Used

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Poisson's ratio for steel: $\nu = 0.30$
- Young's modulus for structural steel: $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Beams (KTU 2024 Module 2 Framework)

Beams are classified by the KTU syllabus into **two primary categories**:
1. **Based on Support Conditions** (most heavily tested)
2. **Based on Geometry / Cross-Section**

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Support Conditions

#### (a) Simply Supported Beam (SSB)
- Supported by a **hinge (pin) support** at one end and a **roller support** at the other end.
- The hinge provides **two reactions** ($H_A, V_A$); the roller provides **one vertical reaction** ($V_B$).
- Total: **3 unknown reactions**; statically determinate.
- Both ends are free to rotate but cannot move translationally.

$$R_A + R_B = W \quad \text{(vertical equilibrium)}$$

#### (b) Overhanging Beam
- A simply supported beam that **extends beyond** one or both supports.
- The overhanging portion carries a **hogging (negative) bending moment**.
- Statics:
$$\sum F_y = 0 \Rightarrow V_A + V_B = W_{total}$$
$$\sum M_A = 0 \Rightarrow V_B \cdot L - W \cdot \bar{x} = 0$$

#### (c) Cantilever Beam
- **Fixed (built-in) at one end** and **completely free at the other end**.
- The fixed support provides **3 reactions** ($H, V, M$).
- Only **2 equilibrium equations** available → **statically determinate** (in this case, the moment reaction is the *third* unknown, fully solvable).
- Maximum bending moment and maximum deflection occur at the **fixed end**.

For a cantilever of length $L$ with point load $P$ at the free end:
$$M_{max} = -P \cdot L \quad \text{(at fixed end, hogging)}$$
$$\delta_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} \quad \text{(at free end, downward)}$$
$$\theta_{max} = \frac{PL^2}{2EI} \quad \text{(at free end, slope)}$$

#### (d) Fixed Beam (Built-in / Encastered Beam)
- **Both ends are fixed** (clamped).
- The fixed support provides **3 reactions each** → **6 unknown reactions**.
- Only **3 equilibrium equations** → **statically indeterminate to degree 3**.
- Sagging moment at mid-span and hogging moment at supports.
- For a fixed beam with UDL $w$ over span $L$:

$$M_A = M_B = -\frac{wL^2}{12} \quad \text{(fixed end moments, hogging)}$$
$$M_{center} = +\frac{wL^2}{24} \quad \text{(sagging at midspan)}$$

#### (e) Continuous Beam
- A beam resting on **more than two supports** (three or more).
- Has **redundant reactions** → **statically indeterminate**.
- Analyzed using **Three-Moment Theorem** (Clapeyron's theorem) or **Moment Distribution Method**.

For two consecutive spans $L_1$ and $L_2$ with UDL $w_1$ and $w_2$:

$$M_A L_1 + 2M_B(L_1 + L_2) + M_C L_2 = -\frac{w_1 L_1^3}{4} - \frac{w_2 L_2^3}{4}$$

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Geometry

| Beam Type | Description | Application |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Straight Beam** | Axis is a straight line | Most building beams |
| **Curved Beam** | Axis is curved (e.g., arches, hooks) | Crane hooks, ring beams |
| **Tapered Beam** | Cross-section varies along length | Cantilever balconies, haunched girders |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of vertical pipes inside table cells to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Beam Type | Loading | $M_{max}$ (Magnitude) | Location of $M_{max}$ | Max Deflection $\delta_{max}$ |
|---|-----------|---------|------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Cantilever | Point load $P$ at free end | $PL$ | Fixed end | $\dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$ |
| 2 | Cantilever | UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ | Fixed end | $\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ |
| 3 | Cantilever | Moment $M_0$ at free end | $M_0$ | Throughout | $\dfrac{M_0 L^2}{2EI}$ |
| 4 | Simply Supported | Central point load $P$ | $\dfrac{PL}{4}$ | Mid-span | $\dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ |
| 5 | Simply Supported | Point load $P$ at distance $a$ from A | $\dfrac{Pab}{L}$ | Under load | $\dfrac{Pa^2 b^2}{3EIL}$ (approx) |
| 6 | Simply Supported | UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ | Mid-span | $\dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ |
| 7 | Fixed Beam | Central point load $P$ | $\dfrac{PL}{8}$ (midspan), $\dfrac{PL}{8}$ (ends) | Midspan & ends | $\dfrac{PL^3}{192EI}$ |
| 8 | Fixed Beam | UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^2}{24}$ (midspan), $\dfrac{wL^2}{12}$ (ends) | Midspan & ends | $\dfrac{wL^4}{384EI}$ |
| 9 | Overhanging | UDL over full length | $\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ (sagging) at center | Center of SS portion | $\dfrac{wL^4}{185EI}$ (approx) |
| 10 | Continuous (2-span) | UDL $w$ on both spans | $\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ at central support (hogging) | Central support | $\dfrac{wL^4}{185EI}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Heuristic):** Always remember that for a **simply supported beam with UDL**, the famous $wL^2/8$ and $5wL^4/384EI$ values appear in nearly **30% of all KTU questions**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Floors:** Typically *simply supported* or *continuous* reinforced concrete beams.
- **Balconies / Chajjas:** *Cantilever* beams.
- **Bridge Girders:** *Continuous beams* over multiple piers.
- **Machine Tool Beds:** *Fixed beams* (high rigidity required).
- **Crane Hooks:** *Curved beams*.
- **Roof Trusses Purlins:** *Simply supported* on trusses.

The **choice of beam type** in design is driven by:
1. **Deflection limits** (governed by IS 456 / IS 800).
2. **Bending stress** ($f_b = M/Z \leq f_{allowable}$).
3. **Shear stress** ($\tau = VQ/Ib \leq \tau_{allowable}$).
4. **Stability and economy**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Deflection of a Simply Supported Beam under Central Point Load

This is the **most frequently asked KTU derivation**. We derive it using **Macaulay's Method**.

### Setup
- Beam length $= L$, with supports at A ($x=0$) and B ($x=L$).
- Central point load $P$ acting at midspan ($x = L/2$).
- Flexural rigidity $= EI$ (assumed constant).

### Step 1: Compute Support Reactions
By symmetry:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$$

### Step 2: Write Bending Moment Equation using Macaulay's Bracket
The bending moment at a section $x$ from A (for $0 < x < L/2$):
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} x$$

Using Macaulay's notation to handle the discontinuous load at $x = L/2$:
$$M(x) = \frac{P}{2} x - \frac{P}{2}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^1$$

For $L/2 < x < L$:
$$M(x) = \frac{P}{2} x - \frac{P}{2}(x - L/2) = \frac{P}{2}\left[x - x + \frac{L}{2}\right] = \frac{PL}{4}$$

### Step 3: Integrate the Elastic Curve Equation
$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

First integration (slope equation):
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x) \, dx = \frac{P}{4} x^2 - \frac{P}{4}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^2 + C_1$$

Second integration (deflection equation):
$$EI \cdot y = \int \int M(x) \, dx \, dx = \frac{P}{12} x^3 - \frac{P}{12}\langle x - L/2 \rangle^3 + C_1 x + C_2$$

### Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions
At $x = 0$, deflection $y = 0$ (Support A):
$$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = 0$$

At $x = L$, deflection $y = 0$ (Support B):
$$0 = \frac{P}{12}L^3 - \frac{P}{12}\left(L - \frac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \frac{PL^3}{12} - \frac{P}{12}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \frac{PL^3}{12} - \frac{PL^3}{96} + C_1 L$$

$$\text{Common denominator is } 96:$$
$$0 = \frac{8PL^3}{96} - \frac{PL^3}{96} + C_1 L = \frac{7PL^3}{96} + C_1 L$$

$$C_1 = -\frac{7PL^2}{96}$$

### Step 5: Compute Deflection at Midspan ($x = L/2$)
By symmetry, maximum deflection occurs at midspan. Using the slope equation, we know $\theta = 0$ at $x = L/2$. But it's easier to substitute into the deflection equation:

$$EI \cdot y_{mid} = \frac{P}{12}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \frac{P}{12}\left\langle \frac{L}{2} - \frac{L}{2}\right\rangle^3 + C_1 \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

The Macaulay bracket term is zero (since the argument is zero):

$$EI \cdot y_{mid} = \frac{P}{12} \cdot \frac{L^3}{8} - \frac{7PL^2}{96} \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

$$EI \cdot y_{mid} = \frac{PL^3}{96} - \frac{7PL^3}{192}$$

$$\text{Common denominator is } 192:$$
$$EI \cdot y_{mid} = \frac{2PL^3}{192} - \frac{7PL^3}{192} = -\frac{5PL^3}{192}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{y_{max} = \delta_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI} \quad (\text{downward, magnitude})}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Slope and Deflection of a Cantilever with UDL

A cantilever of length $L$ fixed at A ($x=0$) and free at B ($x=L$), carrying UDL $w$ over its entire length.

### Step 1: Bending Moment Equation
Consider a section at distance $x$ from the fixed end. The UDL over the portion from $x$ to $L$ has length $(L - x)$, and its centroid is at $(L - x)/2$ from the section.

$$M(x) = -\frac{w(L-x)^2}{2}$$

(Negative because it produces hogging.)

### Step 2: Elastic Curve Equation
$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -\frac{w(L-x)^2}{2}$$

### Step 3: First Integration (Slope)
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{(L-x)^3}{-3} \cdot (-1) + C_1 = \frac{w(L-x)^3}{6} + C_1$$

Wait, let me redo this carefully:
$$\int (L-x)^2 dx = -\frac{(L-x)^3}{3} + C$$

So:
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{w}{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{(L-x)^3}{3}\right) + C_1 = \frac{w(L-x)^3}{6} + C_1$$

### Step 4: Second Integration (Deflection)
$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{(L-x)^4}{-12} + C_1 x + C_2 = \frac{w(L-x)^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

### Step 5: Boundary Conditions
At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $y = 0$ and $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$.

**Slope condition:**
$$0 = \frac{wL^3}{6} + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{6}$$

**Deflection condition:**
$$0 = \frac{wL^4}{24} + 0 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = -\frac{wL^4}{24}$$

### Step 6: Max Deflection at Free End ($x = L$)
$$EI \cdot y_L = \frac{w(L-L)^4}{24} + \left(-\frac{wL^3}{6}\right) \cdot L + \left(-\frac{wL^4}{24}\right)$$

$$EI \cdot y_L = 0 - \frac{wL^4}{6} - \frac{wL^4}{24}$$

$$\text{Common denominator 24: } \quad EI \cdot y_L = -\frac{4wL^4}{24} - \frac{wL^4}{24} = -\frac{5wL^4}{24}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = \frac{wL^4}{8EI} \quad (\text{downward, magnitude})}$$

### Step 7: Max Slope at Free End
$$EI \cdot \theta_L = \frac{w(L-L)^3}{6} - \frac{wL^3}{6} = -\frac{wL^3}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} = \frac{wL^3}{6EI} \quad (\text{at free end, magnitude})}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Beam Type | Statically | No. of Reactions | Indeterminacy Degree | Typical Use Case |
|-----------|------------|------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Cantilever | Determinate | 3 | 0 | Balconies, aircraft wings |
| Simply Supported | Determinate | 3 | 0 | Floor beams, roof purlins |
| Overhanging | Determinate | 3 | 0 | Carport roofs, awnings |
| Fixed Beam | Indeterminate | 6 | 3 | Machine beds, rigid frames |
| Continuous | Indeterminate | > 3 | depends on spans | Multi-span bridges |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BEAMS] --> B[Based on Support Conditions]
    A --> C[Based on Geometry]
    A --> D[Based on Cross Section]

    B --> B1[Simply Supported]
    B --> B2[Overhanging]
    B --> B3[Cantilever]
    B --> B4[Fixed Beam]
    B --> B5[Continuous]

    B1 --> B1A[Pin at one end]
    B1 --> B1B[Roller at other end]
    B1 --> B1C[Statically Determinate]

    B2 --> B2A[Extends beyond supports]
    B2 --> B2B[Hogging at overhang]
    B2 --> B2C[Sagging between supports]

    B3 --> B3A[Fixed at one end]
    B3 --> B3B[Free at other end]
    B3 --> B3C[Max moment at fixed end]

    B4 --> B4A[Fixed at both ends]
    B4 --> B4B[Indeterminate to degree 3]
    B4 --> B4C[Hogging at supports sagging at midspan]

    B5 --> B5A[More than 2 supports]
    B5 --> B5B[Redundant reactions]
    B5 --> B5C[Analyzed by 3 moment theorem]

    C --> C1[Straight Beam]
    C --> C2[Curved Beam]
    C --> C3[Tapered Beam]

    D --> D1[Rectangular]
    D --> D2[I Section]
    D --> D3[T Section]
    D --> D4[Channel Section]
    D --> D5[Box Section]
```

## 4.2 Beam Types Schematic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIMPLY [SIMPLY SUPPORTED BEAM]
        P1[Pin] --- BEAM1[Beam] --- R1[Roller]
    end

    subgraph OVER [OVERHANGING BEAM]
        P2[Pin] --- BEAM2[Beam] --- R2[Roller]
        BEAM2 --- OVH[Overhang portion]
    end

    subgraph CANT [CANTILEVER BEAM]
        FIX[Fixed End] --- BEAM3[Beam]
        BEAM3 --- FREE[Free End]
    end

    subgraph FIXED_BM [FIXED BEAM]
        FIX1[Fixed End A] --- BEAM4[Beam] --- FIX2[Fixed End B]
    end

    subgraph CONT [CONTINUOUS BEAM]
        S1[Pin] --- BEAM5[Span 1] --- S2[Roller] --- BEAM6[Span 2] --- S3[Roller]
    end
```

## 4.3 Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) Patterns for Standard Beams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD[Load Applied] --> TYPE{Beam Type}
    TYPE -->|Cantilever with UDL| BMD1[Triangular BMD max at fixed end]
    TYPE -->|Simply Supported Central Load| BMD2[Triangular BMD max at center]
    TYPE -->|Simply Supported UDL| BMD3[Parabolic BMD max at center]
    TYPE -->|Fixed Beam UDL| BMD4[Combination sagging and hogging]
    TYPE -->|Continuous Beam| BMD5[Multiple curvature reversal]
```

## 4.4 Real-World Application Mapping (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BEAM[Beam Selection] --> Q1{Required stiffness?}
    Q1 -->|High rigidity| FB[Fixed Beam - Machine tools]
    Q1 -->|Moderate rigidity| SSB[Simply Supported - Floor beams]
    Q1 -->|Single sided support| CAN[ Cantilever - Balcony]

    BEAM --> Q2{Span arrangement?}
    Q2 -->|Single span| SB[Simple beam types]
    Q2 -->|Multiple spans| CB[Continuous beam]

    BEAM --> Q3{Available space?}
    Q3 -->|Supports only at ends| OB[Overhanging Beam]
    Q3 -->|Restricted to one end| CB2[Cantilever]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visual Mnemonic:**
> - **Simply Supported** → BMD is a single curve (always sagging between supports)
> - **Cantilever** → BMD is a single straight line or curve (always hogging)
> - **Fixed Beam** → BMD changes sign (hogging at ends, sagging at center)
> - **Continuous** → BMD has multiple sign changes

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a statically determinate and a statically indeterminate beam with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Statically Determinate Beam | Statically Indeterminate Beam |
|---------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Unknown reactions | Equal to equilibrium equations | More than equilibrium equations |
| Analysis | Using statics alone | Requires compatibility conditions |
| Examples | Simply supported, Cantilever | Fixed beam, Continuous beam |

Example:
- **Determinate:** Simply supported beam with central point load → 3 reactions, 3 equations → solvable.
- **Indeterminate:** Fixed beam → 6 reactions, 3 equations → need 3 more compatibility equations.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the location and nature of maximum bending moment in (i) a simply supported beam with UDL, and (ii) a cantilever beam with a point load at the free end.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Simply supported beam with UDL:**
- **Location:** At mid-span ($x = L/2$)
- **Magnitude:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$
- **Nature:** Sagging (positive)

(ii) **Cantilever beam with point load $P$ at free end:**
- **Location:** At the **fixed end** ($x = 0$)
- **Magnitude:** $M_{max} = -P \cdot L$
- **Nature:** Hogging (negative)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **20 kN/m** over its entire length. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD), and find the maximum bending moment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the slope and deflection of a simply supported beam carrying a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over the entire span, using the double integration method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Given: $L = 6 \text{ m}$, $w = 20 \text{ kN/m}$

**Reactions** (by symmetry):
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{20 \times 6}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

**Shear Force at distance $x$ from A:**
$$V(x) = R_A - wx = 60 - 20x$$

At $x = 0$: $V = 60 \text{ kN}$
At $x = 6$: $V = 60 - 120 = -60 \text{ kN}$

SFD is a straight line varying from $+60$ to $-60$ kN, crossing zero at midspan.

**Bending Moment at distance $x$ from A:**
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = 60x - 10x^2$$

**Maximum Bending Moment** occurs where $V = 0$, i.e., at $x = 3$ m:
$$\boxed{M_{max} = 60(3) - 10(3)^2 = 180 - 90 = 90 \text{ kN·m}}$$

BMD is a **parabola** with maximum $90 \text{ kN·m}$ at midspan.

---

**Part (b):**

Refer to derivation steps in **Section 3.1** of these notes (analogous methodology).

**Setup:** SSB of span $L$, UDL $w$ throughout.

Reactions: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$

**Bending moment at section $x$:**
$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

**Euler-Bernoulli Equation:**
$$EI \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

**First Integration (Slope):**
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{w}{2}\left(\frac{Lx^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right) + C_1 = \frac{w}{12}(3Lx^2 - 2x^3) + C_1$$

**Second Integration (Deflection):**
$$EI \cdot y = \frac{w}{2}\left(\frac{Lx^3}{6} - \frac{x^4}{12}\right) + C_1 x + C_2 = \frac{w}{24}(2Lx^3 - x^4) + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
1. At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $\Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
2. At $x = L$, $y = 0$:
$$0 = \frac{w}{24}(2L \cdot L^3 - L^4) + C_1 L = \frac{w}{24}(2L^4 - L^4) + C_1 L = \frac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 L$$
$$C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Maximum slope at supports** (by symmetry, slope is equal and opposite at A and B):
$$EI \cdot \theta_A = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$
$$\theta_A = -\frac{wL^3}{24EI} \quad \text{(negative, downward slope)}$$

**Maximum deflection at midspan** ($x = L/2$):
$$EI \cdot y_{mid} = \frac{w}{24}\left(2L \cdot \frac{L^3}{8} - \frac{L^4}{16}\right) - \frac{wL^3}{24} \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

$$= \frac{w}{24} \cdot \frac{4L^4 - L^4}{16} - \frac{wL^4}{48} = \frac{3wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{48}$$

$$\text{Common denominator 384: } = \frac{3wL^4}{384} - \frac{8wL^4}{384} = -\frac{5wL^4}{384}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI} \quad (\text{downward})}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** A cantilever beam of length **4 m** carries a point load of **30 kN** at its free end. Find (i) the slope at the free end, and (ii) the maximum deflection. Take $EI = 10{,}000 \text{ kN·m}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the classification of beams based on support conditions. List **three differences** between a simply supported beam and a fixed beam. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):**

Given:
- $L = 4 \text{ m}$
- $P = 30 \text{ kN}$ at free end
- $EI = 10{,}000 \text{ kN·m}^2$

**Standard Formulae for Cantilever with point load at free end:**

(i) **Slope at free end:**
$$\theta_{max} = \frac{PL^2}{2EI} = \frac{30 \times (4)^2}{2 \times 10{,}000} = \frac{30 \times 16}{20{,}000} = \frac{480}{20{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} = 0.024 \text{ radians} = 1.376°}$$

(ii) **Maximum deflection at free end:**
$$\delta_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} = \frac{30 \times (4)^3}{3 \times 10{,}000} = \frac{30 \times 64}{30{,}000} = \frac{1920}{30{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{max} = 0.064 \text{ m} = 64 \text{ mm}}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Classification of beams based on support conditions:**
1. Simply supported beam
2. Overhanging beam
3. Cantilever beam
4. Fixed beam (encastered beam)
5. Continuous beam

**Three differences between Simply Supported Beam and Fixed Beam:**

| S.No | Simply Supported Beam | Fixed Beam |
|------|------------------------|------------|
| 1 | Supported by a pin and a roller — both ends can rotate freely | Both ends are rigidly fixed — no rotation and no translation |
| 2 | Statically determinate (3 reactions, 3 equations) | Statically indeterminate to degree 3 (6 reactions, 3 equations) |
| 3 | Maximum BM occurs only at mid-span (sagging) | BM is hogging at supports and sagging at mid-span |
| 4 | Easier to analyze using statics alone | Requires compatibility equations (e.g., Macaulay's method, Moment Distribution) |
| 5 | Examples: floor beams, roof purlins | Examples: machine tool beds, rigid portal frames |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Mismatch:** Many students forget to apply the **Macaulay bracket notation** properly. For discontinuous loads (e.g., multiple point loads), using $\langle x - a \rangle^2$ instead of $\langle x - a \rangle^1$ for a point load leads to **wrong deflection values** (loss of 3-4 marks).
> 2. **Indeterminacy Counting Error:** Students often miscount reactions. A *fixed end* has **3 reactions** ($H$, $V$, $M$), not 2. A *hinge* has 2, a *roller* has 1.
> 3. **Boundary Condition Mix-up:** For a cantilever, both $y = 0$ **and** $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at the fixed end. Forgetting either gives an extra unknown and a wrong answer.
> 4. **Hogging vs Sagging Confusion:** Simply supported beams always have **sagging** moments (positive). Cantilevers under downward load have **hogging** (negative). Mixing signs in bending moment diagrams loses **2 marks minimum**.
> 5. **Units Inconsistency:** Mixing $E$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$ with $I$ in $\text{mm}^4$, $L$ in $\text{m}$ — this single error can cause **the entire problem to be marked wrong**. Always use a single consistent unit system.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullet Points — Beams: Different Types**

- [x] **Beam** = Transversely loaded member → develops Bending Moment + Shear Force.
- [x] **Governing Equation:** $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ (Euler-Bernoulli theory).
- [x] **5 Beam Types (Support-based):** Simply Supported, Overhanging, Cantilever, Fixed, Continuous.
- [x] **Statically Determinate:** Cantilever, Simply Supported, Overhanging.
- [x] **Statically Indeterminate:** Fixed (degree 3), Continuous (degree depends on spans).
- [x] **Reactions Count:** Pin = 2, Roller = 1, Fixed = 3.
- [x] **Macaulay's Bracket:** $\langle x - a \rangle^n = (x-a)^n$ if $x > a$, else $= 0$. Apply to **integration constants** — they vanish from equations where bracket terms are zero.
- [x] **Slope at fixed end of cantilever = 0.**
- [x] **Deflection at any support = 0.**
- [x] **Symmetric loading** → slope at midspan = 0, max deflection at midspan.
- [x] **Standard Formulae to Memorize (KTU High-Frequency):**
  - Cantilever + Point load: $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$, $\theta = \dfrac{PL^2}{2EI}$
  - Cantilever + UDL: $\delta = \dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$, $\theta = \dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$
  - SSB + Central Load: $\delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$, $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$
  - SSB + UDL: $\delta = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$, $M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$
- [x] **Sign Convention (KTU):** Downward load +, downward deflection +, sagging moment +, hogging moment −.
- [x] **Fixed Beam FEM (Fixed End Moments):** For UDL = $\pm wL^2/12$ (end) and $wL^2/24$ (center).
- [x] **Bending Stress:** $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$, where $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ (Section Modulus).
- [x] **Shear Stress:** $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ (no vertical pipe inside tables — use $\dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ in plain text).
- [x] **Units to use in KTU papers:** Either full **SI ($\text{N, mm, MPa}$)** or full **FPS**, never mix.
- [x] **Real-world identification exam tip:** A *balcony* = cantilever, a *floor beam* = SSB, a *machine base* = fixed, a *flyover* = continuous.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Loading on Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Loading on Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Beam?

A **beam** is a structural member primarily designed to resist transverse (lateral) loads. The loads acting perpendicular to its longitudinal axis induce **bending** (flexural stresses), **shear stresses**, and in some cases **axial stresses** along its length. Beams transfer the applied loads to the supports through internal stresses and are the most common structural elements in civil and mechanical engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The classification of loads on a beam forms the foundation for Shear Force Diagrams (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagrams (BMD). A student must master the *resultant magnitude* and *point of application* of every load type before attempting slope-deflection or moment-distribution problems in higher modules.

## 1.2 Definition — Types of Loading on Beams

**Types of loading on beams** refer to the systematic classification of external forces and moments applied transversely to a beam, based on their **distribution, point of application, and nature** along the span. The major categories recognized by KTU (PCAGT302) are:

| # | Load Type | Physical Description |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Concentrated (Point) Load** | A single force acting at a specific point on the beam. |
| 2 | **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | Load spread uniformly over a finite length (intensity *w* is constant). |
| 3 | **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)** | Load varying linearly from zero to a maximum value (triangular). |
| 4 | **Trapezoidal Load** | Combination of UDL and UVL. |
| 5 | **Concentrated Couple / Moment** | A pure moment applied at a point. |
| 6 | **Impact / Dynamic Load** | A load applied suddenly with kinetic energy. |
| 7 | **Eccentric / Axial Load** | A load not acting through the centroid, producing combined effects. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bookshelf Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of your study table as a beam:** 
> - A single heavy **textbook placed at one point** → **Point Load (P)** 
> - A row of **uniformly placed books** of the same size covering the whole shelf → **UDL (w kN/m)** 
> - **Books stacked in a pyramid shape** (more weight at one end, less at the other) → **UVL (triangular distribution)** 
> - A **twist applied to open a stuck jar lid** on the table → **Concentrated Couple (M kN·m)**

This "bookshelf" model is what every structural engineer carries in their head when they first read a load diagram on a beam. The trick is to convert the **distribution** into a **single equivalent point load** so we can solve for reactions and internal forces.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Beam Loading

- **Point Load (P):** measured in **kN** or **N**
- **UDL Intensity (w):** measured in **kN/m** or **N/mm**
- **Couple / Moment (M):** measured in **kN·m** or **N·mm**
- **Span (L):** measured in **m** or **mm**
- **Resultant Load (W):** measured in **kN** or **N**

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion rule to remember:** 
> $$\text{Total load} = \int_0^L w(x)\,dx$$ 
> This single equation unites *all* distributed loads. For a UDL, the integral becomes a rectangle; for a UVL, a triangle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification — How and Why Each Load Works

### 2.1.1 Concentrated (Point) Load — *P*
A single force applied over a negligibly small contact area. *Why does this happen?* Because in real engineering, wheel loads from vehicles, reaction forces from columns above, or hanging weights act over such a small region compared to the beam span that we idealize them as points.

- **Intensity diagram:** A single vertical arrow of magnitude *P*.
- **Total load = P**
- **Acts at the exact point of application** (no centroid calculation needed).

### 2.1.2 Uniformly Distributed Load — *UDL, w kN/m*
A load whose intensity is constant over a finite length *a*. *Why constant?* Examples include the self-weight of a uniformly thick slab, water pressure on a vertical wall, or the weight of a uniform pile of sand.

- **Intensity diagram:** A rectangle of height *w* and length *a*.
- **Total load:** $W = w \cdot a$
- **Centroid (point of application of equivalent point load):** Acts at the **midpoint** of the loaded length, i.e., at distance *a/2* from either end.

### 2.1.3 Uniformly Varying Load — *UVL, Triangular*
Load varying linearly from **zero at one end to w kN/m at the other** over a length *L*. *Why triangular?* Examples include hydrostatic pressure on a submerged wall, lateral wind pressure increasing with height, or sand piled in a conical heap projected onto a beam.

- **Intensity diagram:** A right-angled triangle.
- **Total load:** $W = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot w \cdot L$
- **Centroid:** At distance **L/3 from the maximum-intensity end** (i.e., 2L/3 from the zero-intensity end). This is the centroid of a triangle, just like a geometric centroid.

### 2.1.4 Trapezoidal Load
A combination of UDL + UVL. The total load equals the area of the trapezoid, and the centroid is found by splitting the trapezoid into a rectangle + triangle and taking the moment about a reference point.

### 2.1.5 Concentrated Couple (Applied Moment) — *M kN·m*
A pure moment applied at a point. *Why important?* It arises when an overhanging beam carries a cantilevered load, or when a frame transfers a fixed-end moment. **Couples do not produce any net force**, only a net rotational effect.

### 2.1.6 Impact Load
A load applied suddenly. The dynamic amplification factor typically doubles the static value: $P_{\text{dynamic}} = 2P_{\text{static}}$ (for sudden application, from $1 + \sqrt{...}$ considerations in dynamics).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Print this on your revision card!**

| Load Type | Intensity Diagram Shape | Total Load (Resultant) | Centroid Location from *x = 0* | Shear Contribution $V(x)$ | Moment Contribution $M(x)$ |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Point Load *P* at $x = a$** | Vertical Arrow | $W = P$ | Acts at $x = a$ | $\Delta V = P$ at $x = a$ | $\Delta M = P \cdot (x - a)$ for $x > a$ |
| **UDL *w* kN/m from $0 \to L$** | Rectangle | $W = w L$ | $\bar{x} = L/2$ | $V(x) = V(0) - w x$ | $M(x) = V(0)\,x - w x^2/2$ |
| **UVL (zero at 0, *w* at L)** | Right Triangle | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} w L$ | $\bar{x} = 2L/3$ from zero end | $w(x) = (w/L) x$ | $V(x) = V(0) - \dfrac{w}{2L} x^2$ |
| **UVL (w at 0, zero at L)** | Right Triangle | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} w L$ | $\bar{x} = L/3$ from zero end | $w(x) = w(1 - x/L)$ | $V(x) = V(0) - w x + \dfrac{w x^2}{2L}$ |
| **Trapezoidal** | Trapezoid | Area of trapezoid | Splits into rectangle + triangle | Sum of UDL + UVL | Sum of UDL + UVL |
| **Couple *M*** | Curved Arrow | $W = 0$ (no net force) | N/A (point of application) | **No jump in V** | $\Delta M = M$ at point |
| **Impact *P* (sudden)** | Same as static | $W = 2P$ | Acts at point | $\Delta V = 2P$ | $\Delta M = 2P (x-a)$ |

**General result for any distributed load:**

$$W = \int_0^L w(x)\,dx, \qquad \bar{x} = \frac{\int_0^L x \cdot w(x)\,dx}{\int_0^L w(x)\,dx}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Girders** — Vehicle wheel loads modelled as point loads; deck self-weight as UDL.
- **Cantilever Balcony Slabs** — Self-weight as UDL; railing reaction as point load at the free end.
- **Retaining Walls** — Lateral earth pressure modelled as UVL (triangular, increasing with depth).
- **Crane Beams** — Hooked load as point load; self-weight as UDL; impact factor for sudden lifting.
- **Aircraft Wings** — Aerodynamic lift as distributed load (often trapezoidal along the span).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Resultant Trick"** — For computing reactions and drawing SFD/BMD, you can *always* replace a distributed load by its **single equivalent point load (= total load W) acting at the centroid of the load diagram**. This is the single most useful engineering trick in beam analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents *exhaustive* derivations of the resultant and centroid for every major load type, followed by a complete worked SFD/BMD derivation for a combined-load case.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resultant of a UDL

**Given:** A UDL of intensity *w* kN/m acting over a length $a$ (from $x = 0$ to $x = a$).

**Step 1:** Set up the elemental load. Consider an infinitesimal strip of width $dx$ at distance $x$ from the left end. The force on this strip is:

$$dF = w \cdot dx$$

**Step 2:** Integrate to obtain the total (resultant) load:

$$W = \int_0^a w \, dx = w \cdot a \quad \text{kN}$$

**Step 3:** For the centroid, take moments about $x = 0$. Each elemental force $dF$ acts at distance $x$, so the moment is:

$$dM_0 = x \cdot dF = x \cdot w \, dx$$

**Step 4:** Integrate to obtain total moment:

$$M_0 = \int_0^a w x \, dx = w \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}\bigg|_0^a = \frac{w a^2}{2}$$

**Step 5:** The centroid is defined by $\bar{x} = M_0 / W$:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{w a^2/2}{w a} = \frac{a}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The UDL can be replaced by a single point load of magnitude $W = w a$ acting at the **midpoint** of the loaded length.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resultant of a UVL (Triangular Load)

**Given:** A UVL that varies linearly from 0 at $x = 0$ to $w$ kN/m at $x = L$.

**Step 1:** The intensity function is linear, so:

$$w(x) = \frac{w}{L} \cdot x$$

**Step 2:** Total load (area of triangle):

$$W = \int_0^L \frac{w}{L} x \, dx = \frac{w}{L} \cdot \frac{x^2}{2}\bigg|_0^L = \frac{1}{2} w L$$

**Step 3:** Moment about $x = 0$:

$$M_0 = \int_0^L x \cdot \frac{w}{L} x \, dx = \frac{w}{L} \int_0^L x^2 \, dx = \frac{w}{L} \cdot \frac{L^3}{3} = \frac{w L^2}{3}$$

**Step 4:** Centroid:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{w L^2/3}{w L/2} = \frac{w L^2}{3} \cdot \frac{2}{w L} = \frac{2L}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The triangular UVL can be replaced by a single point load $W = \tfrac{1}{2} w L$ acting at $\bar{x} = 2L/3$ from the zero-intensity end (i.e., $L/3$ from the maximum-intensity end). This is the classic centroid of a triangle.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resultant of a Trapezoidal Load

**Given:** Trapezoidal load varying from $w_1$ at $x = 0$ to $w_2$ at $x = L$.

**Step 1:** Linear intensity function:

$$w(x) = w_1 + \left(\frac{w_2 - w_1}{L}\right) x$$

**Step 2:** Total load (area of trapezoid):

$$W = \int_0^L \left[ w_1 + \left(\frac{w_2 - w_1}{L}\right) x \right] dx = w_1 L + \left(\frac{w_2 - w_1}{L}\right) \frac{L^2}{2}$$

$$W = \frac{L}{2}(w_1 + w_2)$$

**Step 3:** Moment about $x = 0$:

$$M_0 = \int_0^L x \left[ w_1 + \left(\frac{w_2 - w_1}{L}\right) x \right] dx = \frac{w_1 L^2}{2} + \left(\frac{w_2 - w_1}{L}\right) \frac{L^3}{3}$$

$$M_0 = \frac{L^2}{6}(2w_1 + w_2)$$

**Step 4:** Centroid:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{L^2 (2 w_1 + w_2)/6}{L(w_1 + w_2)/2} = \frac{L(2 w_1 + w_2)}{3(w_1 + w_2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity checks:** If $w_1 = w_2 = w$, then $\bar{x} = L/2$ (UDL case). If $w_1 = 0$, then $\bar{x} = L/3$ (UVL case).

## 3.4 Worked Example — Simply Supported Beam with UDL (Full SFD/BMD Derivation)

**Given:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ with UDL of $w$ kN/m over the entire span. Supports at A ($x = 0$) and B ($x = L$).

### Part 1: Support Reactions (Equilibrium)

By symmetry of the loading about the midspan:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

### Part 2: Shear Force Equation $V(x)$ at Section $x$

Consider a section at distance $x$ from A. Take the left portion as the free body. The forces on the left are $R_A$ (up) and the UDL acting downward over length $x$ (intensity $w$, length $x$, total $= w x$).

Using sign convention (left portion, upward forces positive):

$$V(x) = R_A - w x = \frac{wL}{2} - w x$$

$$\boxed{V(x) = \frac{w}{2}\,(L - 2x)}$$

**Key values:**

- At $x = 0$: $V(0) = +wL/2$ (positive shear)
- At $x = L/2$: $V(L/2) = 0$ (point of contraflexure of SFD)
- At $x = L$: $V(L) = -wL/2$ (negative shear)

### Part 3: Bending Moment Equation $M(x)$ at Section $x$

Take moments about the section at $x$ (left portion). Upward forces create sagging moment (positive). The UDL resultant $wx$ acts at $x/2$ from A, so its moment arm from the section is $x - x/2 = x/2$.

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = \frac{wL}{2} x - \frac{w x^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{M(x) = \frac{w x}{2}(L - x)}$$

**Key values:**

- At $x = 0$: $M(0) = 0$ (pin support)
- At $x = L$: $M(L) = 0$ (roller support)
- At $x = L/2$: $M_{\max} = \dfrac{w(L/2)}{2}\left(L - \dfrac{L}{2}\right) = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$

### Part 4: Verification — Shear is the Derivative of Moment

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left[ \frac{w L x}{2} - \frac{w x^2}{2} \right] = \frac{wL}{2} - w x = V(x) \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the fundamental relationship $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ from beam theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result for UDL on Simply Supported Beam:**
> - Maximum Shear Force $= wL/2$ (at supports)
> - Maximum Bending Moment $= wL^2/8$ (at midspan, sagging)

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Resultant & Centroid for All Load Types

| Load Type | Resultant $W$ | Centroid $\bar{x}$ from origin |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------------------|
| Point Load $P$ at $x = a$ | $P$ | $a$ |
| UDL $w$ kN/m over $[0, L]$ | $wL$ | $L/2$ |
| UVL 0 → $w$ over $[0, L]$ | $wL/2$ | $2L/3$ |
| UVL $w$ → 0 over $[0, L]$ | $wL/2$ | $L/3$ |
| Trapezoid $w_1$ → $w_2$ | $(w_1 + w_2)L/2$ | $L(2w_1 + w_2) / [3(w_1 + w_2)]$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Loads on Beams

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Types of Loading on Beams] --> B[Concentrated Load]
    A --> C[Distributed Load]
    A --> D[Couple or Moment]
    A --> E[Impact or Dynamic Load]
    B --> F[Point Load P kN]
    C --> G[Uniformly Distributed UDL]
    C --> H[Uniformly Varying UVL]
    C --> I[Trapezoidal Load]
    C --> J[Parabolic or Hydrostatic]
    G --> G1[w kN/m constant]
    H --> H1[Triangular from 0 to w]
    I --> I1[UDL plus UVL combined]
    E --> E1[Sudden applied load factor 2P]
    D --> D1[Pure moment M kN m]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Intensity Diagrams and Equivalent Point Loads

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Point Load] --> A2[Vertical arrow of magnitude P]
    A2 --> A3[Equivalent: P at point of application]
    B1[UDL w] --> B2[Rectangle of height w]
    B2 --> B3[Equivalent: W equals wL at L by 2]
    C1[UVL Triangular] --> C2[Right triangle 0 to w]
    C2 --> C3[Equivalent: W equals wL by 2 at 2L by 3 from zero]
    D1[Trapezoidal] --> D2[Trapezoid w1 to w2]
    D2 --> D3[Equivalent: Area of trapezoid at centroid]
    E1[Couple M] --> E2[Curved arrow]
    E2 --> E3[No equivalent force only moment]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Flow: From Distributed Load to SFD and BMD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify load type and intensity function w of x] --> S2[Step 2 Compute total resultant W as integral of w of x dx]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute centroid x bar as moment integral over W]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Replace distributed load with single point load W at x bar]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Solve equilibrium equations to find support reactions]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Cut section at distance x and apply equilibrium to get V of x and M of x]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Plot SFD using V of x and BMD using M of x]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Identify maximum shear and maximum moment and their locations]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Typical Real-World Beam Loading Scenarios

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Bridge Girder] --> R1a[Vehicle wheel loads as point loads]
    R1 --> R1b[Deck self weight as UDL]
    R2[Retaining Wall Cantilever] --> R2a[Earth pressure as UVL triangular]
    R3[Roof Beam] --> R3a[Trapezoidal load from sloped roof]
    R4[Overhead Crane Beam] --> R4a[Hooked load as point load]
    R4 --> R4b[Self weight as UDL]
    R4 --> R4c[Impact factor 2P for sudden lifting]
    R5[Floor Beam in Building] --> R5a[UDL from slab self weight]
    R5 --> R5b[Point loads from columns above]
```
