<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define concentrated load and uniformly distributed load. Draw neat sketches of their intensity diagrams and state the position of their equivalent resultant force. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Concentrated (Point) Load:** A load acting at a single point on a beam, idealized as having zero contact length. (1 mark)
  - Intensity diagram: A single vertical arrow of magnitude *P* at the point of application.
  - Equivalent resultant: $W = P$, acting at the **point of application itself** (no shift).

- **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL):** A load whose intensity is **constant** over a finite length of the beam, expressed as *w* kN/m. (1 mark)
  - Intensity diagram: A rectangle of height *w* and length equal to the loaded portion.
  - Equivalent resultant: $W = w \cdot L$, acting at the **midpoint** of the loaded length. (1 mark)

### Q2. Distinguish between a uniformly distributed load (UDL) and a uniformly varying load (UVL). Mention one real-world example for each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | UDL | UVL |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Intensity variation | Constant *w* kN/m | Linearly varying from 0 to *w* kN/m |
| Shape of intensity diagram | Rectangle | Triangle |
| Total resultant | $W = wL$ | $W = wL/2$ |
| Centroid from zero end | $L/2$ | $2L/3$ |
| Real-world example | Self-weight of a uniform slab, water on flat roof (1 mark) | Hydrostatic pressure on retaining wall, lateral wind on tall column (1 mark) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a **uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m** over the entire span and a **concentrated load of 30 kN** at a distance of **2 m from the left support**. Draw the SFD and BMD, and find the position and magnitude of the **maximum bending moment**.

#### Part (a) — Determine Support Reactions and Shear/Moment Equations (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Sketch and identify loads:**
- UDL: 20 kN/m × 6 m = 120 kN, acting at midspan (3 m from A).
- Point load: 30 kN at 2 m from A.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium equations:**

Take moments about A ($\sum M_A = 0$):

$$R_B \times 6 = (20 \times 6) \times 3 + 30 \times 2$$

$$R_B \times 6 = 360 + 60 = 420$$

$$R_B = 70 \text{ kN}$$

Sum of vertical forces ($\sum F_y = 0$):

$$R_A + R_B = 120 + 30 = 150 \text{ kN}$$

$$R_A = 150 - 70 = 80 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Shear force equations:**

For $0 \le x \le 2$ m (before point load):

$$V(x) = R_A - 20x = 80 - 20x$$

- $V(0) = +80$ kN
- $V(2^-) = 80 - 40 = +40$ kN

For $2 \le x \le 6$ m (after point load):

$$V(x) = 80 - 20x - 30 = 50 - 20x$$

- $V(2^+) = 50 - 40 = +10$ kN
- $V(6) = 50 - 120 = -70$ kN ✓ (matches $R_B$)

**Step 4 — Bending moment equations:**

For $0 \le x \le 2$ m:

$$M(x) = 80x - 20x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = 80x - 10x^2$$

- $M(0) = 0$
- $M(2) = 160 - 40 = +120$ kN·m

For $2 \le x \le 6$ m:

$$M(x) = 80x - 10x^2 - 30(x - 2) = 50x - 10x^2 + 60$$

- $M(2) = 100 - 40 + 60 = 120$ kN·m ✓
- $M(6) = 300 - 360 + 60 = 0$ ✓

#### Part (b) — Draw SFD/BMD and Locate Maximum BM (7 marks)

**Step 5 — Locate point of zero shear (where maximum BM occurs):**

In the region $2 \le x \le 6$, set $V(x) = 0$:

$$50 - 20x = 0 \implies x = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate maximum BM:**

$$M_{\max} = M(2.5) = 50(2.5) - 10(2.5)^2 + 60 = 125 - 62.5 + 60 = 122.5 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 7 — Sketch summary:**

- **SFD:** Starts at +80 kN, drops linearly to +40 kN at $x = 2$ m (slope = $-w = -20$ kN/m), drops vertically by 30 kN to +10 kN, then drops linearly to $-70$ kN at $x = 6$ m.
- **BMD:** Parabolic, starts at 0, rises to +120 kN·m at $x = 2$ m, peaks at **+122.5 kN·m at $x = 2.5$ m**, then drops to 0 at $x = 6$ m.

**Final Answer:** $M_{\max} = 122.5$ kN·m at $x = 2.5$ m from left support. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A simply supported beam of span **8 m** carries a **uniformly varying load** from **0 at the left support to 30 kN/m at the right support**. Draw the SFD and BMD, and find the position and magnitude of the **maximum bending moment**.

#### Part (a) — Calculate Support Reactions (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Total load and centroid:**

The UVL is triangular with maximum intensity $w = 30$ kN/m at B (right support). Total load:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \times 30 \times 8 = 120 \text{ kN}$$

This acts at $L/3 = 8/3 \approx 2.667$ m from the **maximum** end (B), i.e., at $8 - 8/3 = 16/3 \approx 5.333$ m from A.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium equations:**

Take moments about A ($\sum M_A = 0$):

$$R_B \times 8 = 120 \times \frac{16}{3}$$

$$R_B = \frac{120 \times 16}{3 \times 8} = \frac{1920}{24} = 80 \text{ kN}$$

Sum of vertical forces:

$$R_A = 120 - 80 = 40 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Intensity function:**

$$w(x) = \frac{30}{8} x = 3.75 x \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Shear force equation:**

Load on the left portion up to section $x$ is the area of the triangle from 0 to $x$:

$$\text{Load} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot w(x) \cdot x = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (3.75x) \cdot x = 1.875 x^2$$

$$V(x) = R_A - 1.875 x^2 = 40 - 1.875 x^2$$

- $V(0) = +40$ kN
- $V(8) = 40 - 1.875(64) = 40 - 120 = -80$ kN ✓

**Step 5 — Bending moment equation:**

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - (\text{Load}) \cdot (\text{moment arm})$$

The triangular load from 0 to $x$ has centroid at $2x/3$ from A, so moment arm from the section is $x - 2x/3 = x/3$:

$$M(x) = 40x - 1.875 x^2 \cdot \frac{x}{3} = 40x - 0.625 x^3$$

- $M(0) = 0$
- $M(8) = 320 - 0.625(512) = 320 - 320 = 0$ ✓

#### Part (b) — Locate Maximum BM and Sketch Diagrams (7 marks)

**Step 6 — Point of zero shear (where $M$ is maximum):**

$$V(x) = 0 \implies 40 - 1.875 x^2 = 0 \implies x^2 = \frac{40}{1.875} = 21.33$$

$$x = 4.62 \text{ m from A}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum BM:**

$$M_{\max} = 40(4.62) - 0.625(4.62)^3 = 184.8 - 0.625(98.61) = 184.8 - 61.63 = 123.17 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 8 — Diagrams summary:**

- **SFD:** Starts at +40 kN at A, curves downward (parabolic, since $V(x)$ is a downward parabola) to $-80$ kN at B. Zero at $x \approx 4.62$ m.
- **BMD:** Starts at 0 at A, rises as a cubic curve, reaches **$M_{\max} \approx 123.17$ kN·m at $x \approx 4.62$ m**, then drops to 0 at B.

**Final Answer:** $M_{\max} \approx 123.17$ kN·m at $x \approx 4.62$ m from the left support. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams — DO NOT do these:**
> 1. **Centroid confusion for UVL:** Students frequently write $\bar{x} = L/3$ from the *zero* end (WRONG for this orientation). The correct centroid is **L/3 from the maximum-intensity end** (i.e., 2L/3 from the zero end). **Memorize both forms based on the load diagram orientation shown.**
> 2. **Units of UDL:** Writing "w = 20 kN" instead of "w = 20 kN/m" loses 0.5 marks. Always state the unit **per meter**.
> 3. **Sign convention for shear:** KTU uses the *left-up positive* convention by default. Inverting signs gives the correct shape but inverted SFD — partial credit only.
> 4. **Forgetting the moment arm for distributed loads:** The resultant $wL$ does not act at the load's geometric centre of mass unless the beam is horizontal — but in beam analysis, **we always take the moment arm as the horizontal distance from the support**, not the perpendicular distance.
> 5. **Skipping the load diagram in the answer sheet:** Even if the question says "sketch the intensity diagram", you **must draw it** before the SFD/BMD. KTU examiners award 1 mark for a correct, labelled intensity diagram.
> 6. **Concentrated couple (M) is NOT a force:** A pure moment does not produce a step change in shear force — only a step (jump) change in bending moment. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Types of Loading on Beams**

- **Point Load (P):** $W = P$, $\bar{x} = a$ (point of application). Causes a **vertical step** in SFD and a **slope change** in BMD.
- **UDL (w kN/m):** $W = wL$, $\bar{x} = L/2$. Causes a **linear drop** in SFD and a **parabolic** BMD.
- **UVL — Triangular (0 → w):** $W = wL/2$, $\bar{x} = 2L/3$ from the zero end. Causes a **parabolic** drop in SFD and a **cubic** BMD.
- **UVL — Triangular (w → 0):** $W = wL/2$, $\bar{x} = L/3$ from the zero end (i.e., 2L/3 from the max end).
- **Trapezoidal Load:** Always split into **UDL + UVL** for analysis.
- **Concentrated Couple (M):** Zero net force, only a **jump/step** in BMD. No effect on SFD.
- **Impact Load:** Multiply static value by 2 for sudden application; by 1 for gradual.
- **The Master Equation:** $W = \int w(x) dx$ and $\bar{x} = \int x \cdot w(x) dx / W$.
- **For SS Beam with UDL:** $M_{\max} = wL^2/8$ at midspan, $V_{\max} = wL/2$ at supports.
- **For SS Beam with central Point Load P:** $M_{\max} = PL/4$ at midspan, $V_{\max} = P/2$ at supports.
- **For SS Beam with UVL (0 → w over L):** $M_{\max} \approx 0.1283 \, w L^2$ at $x \approx 0.5774 L$, $R_A = wL/6$, $R_B = wL/3$.
- **Verification Tool:** Always check that $dM/dx = V(x)$ at every point.
- **Boundary Conditions:** At a pin/roller, $M = 0$. At a fixed end, $V$ and $M$ are reactions.
- **Area-Moment Theorems:** Area under SFD = change in BM. (Useful for quick verification in exams.)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Bending Moment and Shear Force
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Bending Moment and Shear Force

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the study of **Strength of Materials**, when a structural member (typically a **beam**) is subjected to **transverse external loading**, two internal reaction quantities develop at every cross-section of the beam to maintain the element in **static equilibrium**. These two fundamental internal resultants are:

### Shear Force (V or S.F.)
The **algebraic sum of all transverse forces** (vertical forces) acting on either side of the section under consideration. Mathematically, it is the resultant of forces parallel to the cross-section of the beam, tending to cause the portion of the beam on one side of the section to slide past the portion on the other side.

### Bending Moment (M or B.M.)
The **algebraic sum of the moments** of all forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of the section about the longitudinal centroidal axis passing through that section. It represents the internal moment that resists the **bending** (rotation) tendency produced by the external loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The concepts of **Shear Force (SF)** and **Bending Moment (BM)** form the bedrock of structural analysis. Every subsequent topic — SFD, BMD, slope and deflection, and design of beams — is built directly upon these two quantities. Mastery of sign conventions is **non-negotiable**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The Diving Board Intuition)

Imagine you are standing at the **free end** of a diving board while a friend sits firmly on the fixed (support) end.

1. **The Cantilever Diving Board Analogy:**
   - Your weight pushes down at the free end — this creates an internal tendency for the board to *shear* (the fibres want to slide vertically past one another near the support).
   - The board also *bends* downward — this curving action is the **bending moment** resisting the rotation of board segments about the support.

2. **The Seesaw (Simply Supported Beam) Analogy:**
   - Two children of unequal weights sit on a seesaw. The pivot is the fulcrum. The forces at the pivot reaction balance vertical loads (→ **Shear Force**), while the rotational tendencies trying to tilt the seesaw are balanced by moments (→ **Bending Moment**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Units (as per KTU conventions):**
> - **Shear Force (V):** measured in **Newtons (N)** or **Kilonewtons (kN)** — never in N/mm.
> - **Bending Moment (M):** measured in **N·m**, **kN·m**, or **N·mm** depending on the scale of the problem.

## 1.3 Why These Concepts Exist — The Engineering "Why"

When a beam is loaded, we cannot directly "see" the internal stresses. However, every cross-section experiences:
- A **resultant vertical force** = Shear Force
- A **resultant moment about the centroidal axis** = Bending Moment

These two resultants, once known, allow us to compute **bending stress** ($\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$) and **shear stress** ($\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$) at any point of the cross-section.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sign Convention — The KTU Standard

KTU follows the **standard civil engineering sign convention** used universally in textbooks such as *Bhavikatti, S.S. — Structural Analysis Vol. I* and *Ramamrutham, S. — Strength of Materials*:

### For Shear Force (V):
- **Positive Shear Force:** The left portion of the section is pushed **UPWARD** by external forces, OR the right portion is pushed **DOWNWARD**.
- **Negative Shear Force:** The left portion of the section is pushed **DOWNWARD**, OR the right portion is pushed **UPWARD**.

### For Bending Moment (M):
- **Positive Bending Moment:** The beam bends such that the **bottom fibres are in TENSION** (concave upward → **sagging** moment, like a smile 😊).
- **Negative Bending Moment:** The bottom fibres are in **COMPRESSION** (concave downward → **hogging** moment, like a frown ☹).

## 2.2 Types of Loads on Beams (Foundation Knowledge)

For any beam analysis, the external loading falls into these categories:

| Load Type | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Point Load (Concentrated)** | $P$ or $W$ | **kN** | Column reaction transferred to beam |
| **Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL)** | $w$ | **kN/m** | Self-weight of slab, dead load |
| **Uniformly Varying Load (UVL)** | $w$ (triangular) | **kN/m** | Hydrostatic pressure, earth pressure |
| **Moment Couple** | $M_0$ | **kN·m** | Eccentric load applied as moment |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **exam-critical formulas**. Memorize these — they appear in 90% of KTU Module 2 problems.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \sum F_{\text{left}}$ or $V = \sum F_{\text{right}}$ | Shear Force at a section | **kN** |
| 2 | $M = \sum M_{\text{about section, left}}$ | Bending Moment at a section | **kN·m** |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ | Slope of SFD = negative of load intensity | — |
| 4 | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ | Slope of BMD = Shear Force at that point | — |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$ | Curvature of BMD = negative of load | — |
| 6 | $\Delta V = -\int w(x)\,dx$ | Net change in shear = area under load diagram | **kN** |
| 7 | $\Delta M = \int V(x)\,dx$ | Net change in moment = area under SFD | **kN·m** |
| 8 | At a **point load** $P$ | SFD has a **jump of $+P$** (downward) | — |
| 9 | At a **UDL** region | SFD is a **straight inclined line** (slope = $-w$) | — |
| 10 | At a **free end** | $V = 0$ and $M = 0$ (if no applied load) | — |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Structures

- **Bridge Design:** The maximum bending moment in a bridge girder governs the choice of cross-section (I-section, box girder).
- **Building Floors:** SFD and BMD for a typical floor beam help determine rebar placement in **RCC beams** (extra reinforcement at top for negative moment over supports).
- **Machine Tool Frames:** Proper shear and moment diagrams identify critical sections where fatigue failure is most likely.
- **Cantilever Balconies:** Entirely negative BM — top steel is the tension reinforcement.

## 2.5 Key Rules of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Favourites)

1. **At any simple support** (roller or hinge): Bending Moment is **zero** (unless a couple is applied at it).
2. **At any fixed support**: Both Shear Force and Bending Moment are **non-zero** (reactive).
3. **At a hinge (internal pin)** : Bending Moment is **zero**.
4. **The point of zero shear** corresponds to the location of **maximum (or minimum) bending moment**.
5. **SFD and BMD are always zero at free ends** of cantilevers (unless loads are applied directly there).



<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Differential Relationships

Consider an elemental slice of a beam of length $dx$ subjected to an arbitrary distributed load $w(x)$ (positive upward convention reversed — here $w$ is taken as load per unit length acting downward).

### Step 1: Define the Element

Let:
- $V$ = shear force at the **left face** of the element
- $V + dV$ = shear force at the **right face** of the element
- $M$ = bending moment at the **left face**
- $M + dM$ = bending moment at the **right face**
- $w(x)$ = intensity of distributed load on the element (downward)

### Step 2: Apply Vertical Equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$)

Summing all vertical forces on the element (upward positive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Upward forces on element: } V \\
&\text{Downward forces on element: } (V + dV) + w(x)\cdot dx \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For equilibrium:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V - (V + dV) - w(x)\, dx &= 0 \\
V - V - dV - w(x)\, dx &= 0 \\
-dV - w(x)\, dx &= 0 \\
dV &= -w(x)\, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Obtain the First Relationship

Dividing both sides by $dx$:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The **rate of change of shear force** at any point equals the **negative of the load intensity** at that point.

### Step 4: Apply Moment Equilibrium ($\sum M_{\text{about right face}} = 0$)

Taking moments about the right face (counterclockwise positive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Moments tending to rotate CCW: } & M \\
\text{Moments tending to rotate CW: } & (M + dM) + V\,dx + w(x)\cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For equilibrium:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M - (M + dM) - V\,dx - w(x)\cdot \dfrac{(dx)^2}{2} &= 0 \\
-M - dM - V\,dx - \dfrac{w(x)\,(dx)^2}{2} &= 0 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Neglect the Higher-Order Infinitesimal

Since $(dx)^2$ is an **infinitesimal of higher order** (negligibly small compared to $dx$), the term $\dfrac{w(x)\,(dx)^2}{2}$ is dropped:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-M - dM - V\,dx &= 0 \\
-dM &= V\, dx
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Obtain the Second Relationship

Dividing both sides by $dx$:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V}
$$

**Interpretation:** The **rate of change of bending moment** at any point equals the **shear force** at that point.

### Step 7: Derive the Combined Relationship

Differentiating $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ again with respect to $x$:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)
$$

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)}
$$

**Interpretation:** The **curvature of the bending moment diagram** equals the negative of the load intensity.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Numerical Verification

Consider a simply supported beam of length $L$ metres carrying a **point load $P$ kN** at the midspan.

### Step A: Calculate Reactions

By symmetry:
$$
R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2}
$$

### Step B: Compute Shear Force at Critical Sections

**Just to the left of midspan (x = L/2⁻):**

$$
V_{\text{left}} = R_A = +\dfrac{P}{2}
$$

**Just to the right of midspan (x = L/2⁺):**

$$
V_{\text{right}} = R_A - P = \dfrac{P}{2} - P = -\dfrac{P}{2}
$$

**Jump in shear at midspan:**

$$
\Delta V = V_{\text{right}} - V_{\text{left}} = -\dfrac{P}{2} - \dfrac{P}{2} = -P
$$

This matches the rule: *"At a downward point load $P$, the SFD drops by $P$."*

### Step C: Compute Bending Moment at Midspan

Taking moments from the left about the midspan section:

$$
M_{\text{mid}} = R_A \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{PL}{4}
$$

**Verification using BMD area method:**

Area under SFD between $x = 0$ and $x = L/2$:
$$
\text{Area} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{P}{2} = \dfrac{PL}{8}
$$

Since $V$ starts from $M = 0$ at the left support:
$$
M(L/2) = 0 + \dfrac{PL}{8} + \dfrac{PL}{8} = \dfrac{PL}{4} \checkmark
$$

(Both halves of the SFD area sum to the moment at midspan.)

## 3.3 Summary of Analytical Logic Flow

| Operation | Result | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Integrate load $w(x)$ once | Gives $-V(x)$ | Construct SFD from load diagram |
| Integrate $V(x)$ once | Gives $M(x)$ | Construct BMD from SFD |
| Differentiate $V$ | Gives $-w(x)$ | Verify SFD shape against load |
| Differentiate $M$ twice | Gives $-w(x)$ | Verify BMD curvature |


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sign Convention Visual (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POS["POSITIVE SHEAR FORCE"]
        A1[Left side PUSHED UP] --> B1[Section X-X] --> C1[Right side PUSHED DOWN]
    end
    subgraph NEG["NEGATIVE SHEAR FORCE"]
        A2[Left side PUSHED DOWN] --> B2[Section X-X] --> C2[Right side PUSHED UP]
    end
    subgraph PMOM["POSITIVE BM (Sagging)"]
        D1[Top fibres COMPRESSED] --> E1[Beam CURVES like SMILE] --> F1[Bottom fibres TENSIONED]
    end
    subgraph NMOM["NEGATIVE BM (Hogging)"]
        D2[Top fibres TENSIONED] --> E2[Beam CURVES like FROWN] --> F2[Bottom fibres COMPRESSED]
    end
```

## 4.2 Beam Analysis Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Beam Type and Supports] --> B[Calculate Support Reactions]
    B --> C[Define Section at distance x]
    C --> D{Select Side: Left or Right}
    D -- Left --> E1[Sum of Vertical Forces to LEFT]
    D -- Right --> E2[Sum of Vertical Forces to RIGHT]
    E1 --> F[Compute Shear Force V]
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Compute Moment of Forces about section]
    G --> H[Bending Moment M]
    H --> I[Plot SFD]
    I --> J[Plot BMD]
    J --> K[Identify Maximum V and M]
    K --> L[Design Cross-Section]
```

## 4.3 Typical SFD & BMD Shape Patterns (Architecture Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[UDL on SS Beam] --> M1[SFD: Straight inclined line]
    M1 --> N1[BMD: Parabolic curve]
    L2[Point Load on SS Beam] --> M2[SFD: Two rectangles]
    M2 --> N2[BMD: Two triangles]
    L3[Point Load on Cantilever] --> M3[SFD: Rectangle]
    M3 --> N3[BMD: Triangle, all negative]
    L4[UVL on SS Beam] --> M4[SFD: Parabolic]
    M4 --> N4[BMD: Cubic curve]
```

## 4.4 Cantilever Beam — Standard Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed Support A] -->|Length L| B[Free End B with Load P]
    subgraph SFD_PATTERN["SFD (Rectangular block, negative)"]
        SP1[Zero at A] --> SP2[Drops to -P at B]
    end
    subgraph BMD_PATTERN["BMD (Triangular, all negative)"]
        BP1[Zero at B] --> BP2[Reaches -PL at A]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:**
> - A **rectangular SFD** indicates a constant shear → **point load at free end of cantilever**.
> - A **triangular BMD** indicates linearly varying moment → consistent with $V = $ constant.
> - All values are **negative** because cantilever loading produces **hogging** throughout.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Define Shear Force and Bending Moment at a section of a beam. State the sign convention used in strength of materials.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Shear Force (1 mark):**
Shear force at any cross-section of a beam is the algebraic sum of all vertical forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of the section.

**Bending Moment (1 mark):**
Bending moment at any cross-section is the algebraic sum of the moments of all forces acting on either side of the section, taken about the centroidal axis of that section.

**Sign Convention (1 mark):**
- Shear Force is **positive** when the left portion tends to move upward relative to the right.
- Bending Moment is **positive** when it causes **sagging** (bottom fibres in tension, concave upward).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional Model]`
**State and explain the differential relation between load, shear force, and bending moment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three fundamental differential relations are:

$$
\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x) \quad ; \quad \dfrac{dM}{dx} = V \quad ; \quad \dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)
$$

- The **slope of the SFD** at any point equals the **negative of the load intensity** at that point.
- The **slope of the BMD** at any point equals the **shear force** at that point.
- The **curvature of the BMD** equals the negative of the load intensity.

These relations form the analytical foundation for constructing shear force and bending moment diagrams.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Q3. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Main Paper II]`

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**A simply supported beam of length 8 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 6 kN/m over its entire span and a point load of 12 kN at a distance of 3 m from the left support. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD). Also find the positions and values of maximum bending moment.**

**Part (a) — Compute Reactions and Draw SFD [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate Reactions**

Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the vertical reactions at supports A and B respectively.

Total UDL = $6 \times 8 = 48$ kN, acting at midspan (4 m from A).

Taking moments about A ($\sum M_A = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_B \times 8 &= 48 \times 4 + 12 \times 3 \\
R_B \times 8 &= 192 + 36 = 228 \\
R_B &= 28.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Vertical equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A + R_B &= 48 + 12 = 60 \\
R_A &= 60 - 28.5 = 31.5 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Shear Force at Critical Sections**

- At $x = 0^+$ (just right of A): $V = +31.5$ kN
- At $x = 3^-$ (just left of point load): $V = 31.5 - 6(3) = +13.5$ kN
- At $x = 3^+$ (just right of point load): $V = 13.5 - 12 = +1.5$ kN
- At $x = 8^-$ (just left of B): $V = 1.5 - 6(5) = -28.5$ kN

**Step 3: Locate Point of Zero Shear**

Shear becomes zero between $x = 3$ and $x = 8$, where:

$$
\begin{aligned}
31.5 - 6x - 12 &= 0 \\
6x &= 19.5 \\
x &= 3.25 \text{ m from A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Compute Bending Moments and Draw BMD [7 Marks]**

- $M_A = 0$
- $M$ at $x = 3$ m (left of point load):
$$M_1 = 31.5 \times 3 - 6 \times 3 \times 1.5 = 94.5 - 27 = 67.5 \text{ kN·m}$$
- $M$ at $x = 3.25$ m (point of max BM):
$$M_{\max} = 31.5 \times 3.25 - 12 \times 0.25 - 6 \times 3.25 \times \dfrac{3.25}{2}$$
$$M_{\max} = 102.375 - 3 - 31.6875 = 67.6875 \text{ kN·m}$$
- $M$ at $x = 8$ m: $M_B = 0$ (simple support)

**Maximum Bending Moment = 67.6875 kN·m at x = 3.25 m from A.**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**A cantilever beam of length 5 m is loaded with a point load of 20 kN at the free end and a uniformly distributed load of 4 kN/m over the entire length. Draw the SFD and BMD. Determine the maximum bending moment and its location.**

**Part (a) — Reaction and SFD [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Fixed End Reactions at A**

Total downward load = Point load + UDL total = $20 + 4 \times 5 = 40$ kN
Reaction $R_A = 40$ kN (upward)
Fixed end moment $M_A = 20 \times 5 + 4 \times 5 \times \dfrac{5}{2} = 100 + 50 = 150$ kN·m (counterclockwise to balance the loads tending to rotate the beam clockwise)

**Step 2: Shear Force from Free End (x measured from free end)**

At distance $x$ from free end B:
$$
V(x) = -20 - 4x \text{ kN}
$$

- At B ($x = 0$): $V = -20$ kN
- At A ($x = 5$): $V = -20 - 20 = -40$ kN

SFD is a **trapezoidal block**, entirely in negative region.

**Part (b) — BMD and Maximum Moment [7 Marks]**

At distance $x$ from free end:
$$
M(x) = -20x - 4x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = -20x - 2x^2
$$

- At B ($x = 0$): $M = 0$
- At A ($x = 5$):
$$M_A = -20(5) - 2(25) = -100 - 50 = -150 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Maximum Bending Moment = 150 kN·m (negative, hogging) at the fixed end A.**

The BMD is a **parabolic curve** (since load is UDL), entirely below the zero line.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Reasons Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (2–3 marks lost frequently):** Many students assume the left-side shear is always positive. Always draw a clear FBD showing the forces on the chosen side of the section before applying the sign convention.
> 2. **Forgetting Point Load Jump (1 mark):** At a concentrated load, the SFD has a sudden vertical jump equal to the load magnitude — students often draw a smooth curve and lose marks.
> 3. **Wrong Reaction Direction Assumption (2 marks):** Assume reactions act upward; if the calculated reaction comes negative, it means the actual direction is opposite — never silently ignore the sign.
> 4. **Arithmetic Slip in UDL Area:** The area under a UDL diagram (used to find moment from shear) is a rectangle, not a triangle. Many students use $\frac{1}{2} \cdot w \cdot L^2$ instead of $w \cdot L$ for the shear-area conversion.
> 5. **Not Marking Critical Values on Diagrams:** KTU explicitly requires the maximum SF and BM values to be clearly marked on the diagrams with units. Diagrams without values often get only partial credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Quick-Fire Revision Points — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- **SF** at a section = $\sum$ vertical forces on one side (with sign convention).
- **BM** at a section = $\sum$ moments of forces on one side about the section (with sign convention).
- **Sign convention (KTU Standard):**
  - **+V** → left side up / right side down.
  - **+M** → sagging (concave up, tension at bottom).
  - **−M** → hogging (concave down, tension at top).
- **Master Equations:**
  - $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$
  - $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$
  - $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$
- **SFD Rules:**
  - Constant slope where UDL acts.
  - Vertical jump of magnitude = point load.
  - Zero slope where no load acts (constant shear).
- **BMD Rules:**
  - Constant slope where shear is constant.
  - Parabolic where UDL acts.
  - Triangular where point load acts.
  - Always **zero at free ends** of cantilevers (unless loaded at end).
  - Always **zero at simple supports** (hinges/rollers without applied couple).
- **Maximum BM Location:** Where SFD = 0 (changes sign).
- **Area–Moment Trick:** Change in moment between two sections = area under SFD between those sections.
- **Change in Shear Trick:** Change in shear = negative of area under load diagram between those sections.
- **Units:** Force in **kN**, Moment in **kN·m**, UDL in **kN/m**, Length in **m** (consistent SI units are mandatory for KTU numerical answers).
- **Symmetry trick:** For symmetric loading on symmetric beams, $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{\text{Total Load}}{2}$.
- **Anti-symmetry trick:** For anti-symmetric loading, max BM occurs at the central section.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the rigorous study of **Strength of Materials**, every loaded structural member (beam) is internally subjected to two fundamental resultant force systems at any cross-section:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Force (V):** The algebraic sum of all **transverse (vertical) forces** acting on either side of a beam cross-section. It represents the internal resisting force that prevents the two parts of the beam from sliding vertically relative to each other. The standard unit in SI is **Newton (N)** or **kilo-Newton (kN)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bending Moment (M):** The algebraic sum of the **moments** of all forces (loads and reactions) about the cross-section under consideration. It represents the internal resisting couple that prevents the two parts of the beam from rotating relative to each other. The standard unit is **N·m** or **kN·m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intensity of Load (w):** The **distributed load per unit length** acting transversely on the beam. It is a continuous function of the longitudinal position *x* along the beam axis. The standard unit is **N/m** or **kN/m**. Mathematically expressed as $w(x)$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tree Trunk Bridge" 🌉

Imagine you and a friend are carrying a heavy **wooden log (beam)** horizontally, and someone slowly pours sand along its top surface at a varying rate (this represents *w*, the distributed load).

- **Why doesn't the log snap?** Internally, the wood develops microscopic **shear** actions (fibres sliding past each other vertically) and **bending** actions (fibres stretching on the bottom, compressing on the top).
- **The more sand poured near a section, the greater the shear and bending required at that cross-section.** This intuitive observation is exactly what the mathematical relationships describe.
- **The rate at which sand varies** along the length is directly linked to how rapidly the shear force and bending moment change — this is the essence of the differential relationship we are about to derive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Slogan to memorize:** *"The load is the parent; Shear Force is the first child (integral); Bending Moment is the grandchild (double integral of load)."*

---

## 1.3 Standard Sign Convention (KTU Board Convention)

The following sign convention is universally adopted in KTU university examinations:

| Quantity | Positive Direction | Diagram Cue |
|---|---|---|
| Load intensity **w** | **Downward** ↓ | Arrow pointing down |
| Shear Force **V** | Causes **clockwise** rotation of element | Left side up, right side down |
| Bending Moment **M** | Causes **sagging** (concave up) "smile" | Bottom fibres in tension |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The derivation rests on **three classical pillars of engineering mechanics**:

1. **Equilibrium of forces in the vertical direction:** $\sum F_y = 0$
2. **Equilibrium of moments about a point:** $\sum M = 0$
3. **The differential nature of the beam element:** As the length of an element approaches zero ($dx \to 0$), higher-order infinitesimal terms (like $dx^2$ and $dx^3$) become vanishingly small and may be neglected.

By isolating an infinitesimal beam element of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from a chosen origin, applying the above principles, and then taking the limit as $dx \to 0$, we obtain the foundational differential equations governing beam behaviour.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam critical. Memorize the sign convention along with each formula.**

| # | Relationship | Governing Formula | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load → Shear Rate | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ | The slope of the SFD at any point equals the negative of load intensity. |
| 2 | Shear → Moment Rate | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ | The slope of the BMD at any point equals the shear force at that point. |
| 3 | Load → Moment Curvature | $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)$ | The curvature of the BMD equals the negative of the load intensity. |
| 4 | Shear from Load | $V(x) = V(a) - \displaystyle\int_{a}^{x} w(x)\, dx$ | Shear at *x* = Shear at *a* minus area under load curve from *a* to *x*. |
| 5 | Moment from Shear | $M(x) = M(a) + \displaystyle\int_{a}^{x} V(x)\, dx$ | Moment at *x* = Moment at *a* plus area under SFD from *a* to *x*. |
| 6 | Moment from Load | $M(x) = M(a) + \displaystyle\int_{a}^{x}\!\int_{a}^{x} w(x)\, dx\, dx$ | Moment from double integration of load. |

**Auxiliary Graphical Theorems (High-Yield for KTU):**

- **Theorem 1:** The change in shear force between two sections = (Negative of) Area of the load diagram between those sections.
- **Theorem 2:** The change in bending moment between two sections = Area of the shear force diagram between those sections.
- **Theorem 3:** The slope of the BMD at any point = Shear force at that point.
- **Theorem 4:** The slope of the SFD at any point = Negative of load intensity at that point.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Bridge Design (Civil)** | Determining maximum bending moment for selecting girder cross-section |
| **Aircraft Wings (Aerospace)** | Analysing lift distribution and structural sizing |
| **Crane Booms (Mechanical)** | Calculating critical sections for safe load lifting |
| **Chassis Frames (Automobile)** | Optimising weight-to-strength ratio |
| **Wind Turbine Blades (Energy)** | Predicting fatigue due to non-uniform wind pressure |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Derivation Setup

**Consider a simply-supported beam** subjected to a general **distributed load** $w(x)$ that varies continuously along the axis. We isolate a small element of the beam located at a distance $x$ from the left support and of infinitesimal length $dx$.

**Forces acting on this element:**

1. **Load intensity $w(x)$** — acting **downward** over the entire length $dx$. Total downward load = $w(x) \cdot dx$.
2. **Shear Force $V$** — acting **upward** on the **left face** of the element (by sign convention, positive shear tries to rotate the element clockwise).
3. **Shear Force $V + dV$** — acting **downward** on the **right face** of the element.
4. **Bending Moment $M$** — acting **counter-clockwise** on the **left face** (sagging convention).
5. **Bending Moment $M + dM$** — acting **clockwise** on the **right face**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Free Body Diagram of the Element:** [Visualised internally] A small rectangular slice, with downward distributed load $w(x) \cdot dx$ on top, upward $V$ on left vertical edge, downward $(V + dV)$ on right vertical edge, counter-clockwise moment $M$ on left, and clockwise moment $(M + dM)$ on right.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Load Intensity and Shear Force

### Step 1: Apply Vertical Equilibrium

Sum of all vertical forces on the element must equal zero (static equilibrium).

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
V - (V + dV) - w(x)\, dx = 0
$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $+V$: Upward shear on left face.
- $-(V + dV)$: Downward shear on right face (taken as negative in upward direction).
- $-w(x)\, dx$: Total downward distributed load acting on the element.

### Step 2: Simplify the Equation

Expanding the parentheses and cancelling common terms:

$$
V - V - dV - w(x)\, dx = 0
$$

The $+V$ and $-V$ cancel out exactly:

$$
-dV - w(x)\, dx = 0
$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Differential

Rearranging to isolate $dV$:

$$
dV = -w(x)\, dx
$$

### Step 4: Form the Differential Equation

Dividing both sides by $dx$:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The **rate of change of shear force** at any cross-section equals the **negative of the load intensity** at that section. The negative sign appears because we took downward loads as positive (KTU convention).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Shear Force and Bending Moment

### Step 1: Apply Moment Equilibrium

Take moments about the **right-hand face** of the element (this eliminates the unknown $V + dV$ and $dM$ from one side of the moment equation, simplifying algebra).

Counter-clockwise moments are taken as positive.

$$
\sum M_{\text{right face}} = 0
$$

$$
M + V \cdot dx - w(x) \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} - (M + dM) = 0
$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $+M$: Counter-clockwise moment on left face.
- $+V \cdot dx$: Moment due to upward shear $V$ acting through the lever arm $dx$ (CCW about right face).
- $-w(x) \cdot dx \cdot (dx/2)$: Moment of the downward load about the right face. The load is uniform over $dx$, so it acts at the centroid located at distance $dx/2$ from the right face. This produces a clockwise moment (negative).
- $-(M + dM)$: Clockwise moment on right face (taken as negative).

### Step 2: Neglect Higher-Order Infinitesimals

The term $w(x) \cdot dx \cdot (dx/2) = \dfrac{w(x)\, dx^2}{2}$ is of **second order** (contains $dx^2$). Since $dx$ is vanishingly small, $dx^2$ is infinitesimally smaller and can be **neglected in the limit**.

$$
M + V \cdot dx - 0 - M - dM = 0
$$

### Step 3: Simplify

The $+M$ and $-M$ cancel out:

$$
V \cdot dx - dM = 0
$$

### Step 4: Form the Differential Equation

$$
dM = V \cdot dx
$$

Dividing by $dx$:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The **slope of the bending moment diagram** at any cross-section numerically equals the **shear force** at that section. A maximum in BMD occurs where shear force is zero (a crucial point for locating points of contraflexure and max moment in KTU problems).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Combined Second-Order Relationship

Differentiate the equation $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ once more with respect to $x$:

$$
\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{dM}{dx}\right) = \dfrac{dV}{dx}
$$

The left side becomes the second derivative of moment, and the right side equals $-w(x)$ from Derivation 1.

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Favourite:** Examiners often ask: *"At what point in a beam does maximum bending moment occur for a simply-supported beam with a uniformly distributed load?"* The answer follows directly: set $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V = 0$, and then verify using $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} < 0$ that it is indeed a maximum.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Summary

| Quantity | Differentiation Rule | Integration Rule |
|---|---|---|
| From $w(x)$ | (Given) | $V = -\int w(x)\, dx + C_1$ |
| From $V(x)$ | $w = -\dfrac{dV}{dx}$ | $M = \int V(x)\, dx + C_2$ |
| From $M(x)$ | $V = \dfrac{dM}{dx}$ | $\theta = \int \dfrac{M}{EI}\, dx$ (slope) |
| From $\theta(x)$ | $M = EI \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$ | $y = \int \theta \, dx$ (deflection) |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is part of the broader beam equation framework covered in Module 3 & 4. For Module 2, focus only on the load → shear → moment relationships (the first three rows).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Free Body of an Infinitesimal Beam Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Left Face: Shear V UP + Moment M CCW"] -->|dx distance| nodeB["Distributed Load w dx acting DOWN on top surface"]
    nodeB -->|dx distance| nodeC["Right Face: Shear V + dV DOWN + Moment M + dM CW"]
    nodeA --- nodeD["Beam Element of length dx"]
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE["Apply Sigma Fy = 0: dV by dx equals minus w"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Apply Sigma M about right face: dM by dx equals V"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Combined: d2M by dx2 equals minus w x"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The **left face** carries positive shear $V$ (upward arrow) and sagging moment $M$ (counter-clockwise).
- The **right face** carries the increased shear $(V + dV)$ (downward arrow) and increased moment $(M + dM)$ (clockwise).
- The **distributed load** $w \cdot dx$ acts at the centroid of the element (a distance $dx/2$ from either face).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Flow of Beam Loading → Internal Forces

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["External Applied Load w x on Beam Surface"] --> stepB["Internal Shear Force V x develops"]
    stepB --> stepC["Internal Bending Moment M x develops"]
    stepC --> stepD["Stress and Strain distribution across section"]
    stepD --> stepE["Deflection and Slope of neutral axis"]
    stepA -->|dV by dx equals minus w| stepB
    stepB -->|dM by dx equals V| stepC
    stepC -->|d2M by dx2 equals minus w| stepA
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Graphical Interpretation — Slope Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    grp1["LOAD DIAGRAM w x"] -->|Slope at any point| grp2["Slope of SFD = minus w"]
    grp2["SFD V x"] -->|Slope at any point| grp3["Slope of BMD = V"]
    grp3["BMD M x"] -->|Curvature| grp4["Curvature of BMD = minus w"]
```

**Practical Implication for KTU Board Drawing:**
- If the load diagram is a **rectangle (UDL)**, the SFD is a **straight line with constant negative slope**, and the BMD is a **parabola**.
- If the load diagram is a **triangle (UVL)**, the SFD is a **parabola**, and the BMD is a **cubic curve**.
- If the load is a **point load**, the SFD has a **sudden vertical jump**, and the BMD has a **sudden change in slope (kink)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the terms: (i) Shear Force, (ii) Bending Moment, (iii) Intensity of Load. State the standard sign convention used in beam analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**(i) Shear Force (1 mark):** The algebraic sum of all transverse forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of a beam cross-section. It represents the internal resistance against vertical sliding between adjacent layers of the beam. **Unit: N or kN.**

**(ii) Bending Moment (1 mark):** The algebraic sum of moments of all forces (loads and reactions) about the cross-section under consideration. It represents the internal resistance against bending rotation. **Unit: N·m or kN·m.**

**(iii) Intensity of Load (1/2 mark):** The distributed load per unit length of beam, denoted $w(x)$, having units of **N/m or kN/m**.

**Sign Convention (1/2 mark):** Downward loads are positive; positive shear causes clockwise rotation of the element; positive bending moment causes sagging (concave upward).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the differential relationships between intensity of load ($w$), shear force ($V$), and bending moment ($M$). Mention their physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three fundamental relationships are:

$$
\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x) \quad \text{(Rate of change of shear equals negative load intensity)} \quad \cdots (1\text{ mark})
$$

$$
\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x) \quad \text{(Slope of BMD equals shear force)} \quad \cdots (1\text{ mark})
$$

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x) \quad \text{(Curvature of BMD equals negative load intensity)} \quad \cdots (1\text{ mark})
$$

**Significance (bonus, partial credit):** Equation (2) implies that points of zero shear force correspond to points of **maximum or minimum bending moment** — critical for design of beams.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) regulations for the 2024 Scheme require **internal choice** within each module. Two complete alternative questions are provided below. Either may appear in the exam.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a) Derive the relationship between intensity of load and shear force for a beam element. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider an infinitesimal beam element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the left support. Let $w(x)$ be the intensity of distributed load acting downward over the element.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Draw the Free Body Diagram showing:
- Upward shear $V$ on the left face.
- Downward shear $(V + dV)$ on the right face.
- Downward distributed load $w \cdot dx$ on the top surface.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply vertical equilibrium:

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
V - (V + dV) - w(x)\, dx = 0
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Simplifying:

$$
-V \cancel{+ V} - dV - w(x)\, dx = 0
$$

$$
-dV = w(x)\, dx
$$

$$
\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State the physical meaning: *"The slope of the Shear Force Diagram at any cross-section equals the negative of the load intensity at that section."*

**(b) Derive the relationship between shear force and bending moment. Hence obtain the second-order relationship connecting load intensity and bending moment. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider the same infinitesimal beam element. Add moments $M$ (CCW on left face) and $(M + dM)$ (CW on right face).

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Take moments about the right-hand face of the element (to eliminate $V + dV$ and the moment arm of load):

$$
\sum M_{\text{right}} = 0
$$

$$
M + V \cdot dx - w(x) \cdot dx \cdot \dfrac{dx}{2} - (M + dM) = 0
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The term containing $dx^2$ is of second order and vanishes as $dx \to 0$:

$$
M + V \cdot dx - 0 - M - dM = 0
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Simplifying:

$$
V \cdot dx = dM
$$

$$
\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)
$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Differentiate both sides with respect to $x$:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)
$$

Thus, the **second-order relationship** is:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the physical significance of the differential relationships $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ and $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Significance of $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ (3 Marks):**
- This equation states that the **rate of change of shear force** with respect to the longitudinal axis equals the negative of the load intensity at that point.
- **Practical implication:** If we know the slope of the SFD at a section, we can determine the load intensity at that section, and vice versa.
- For example, in a region of **uniformly distributed load (UDL)**, the SFD is a straight line whose slope is constant and equal to $-w$.
- In a region of **no load** ($w = 0$), the shear force remains constant (horizontal SFD).

**(ii) Significance of $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ (4 Marks):**
- This equation states that the **slope of the bending moment diagram** at any cross-section equals the **shear force** at that cross-section.
- **Practical implications:**
  - **Zero shear ⇒ Zero slope of BMD** ⇒ Maximum or minimum bending moment occurs at points of zero shear (critical design points).
  - **Constant shear ⇒ Linear BMD** (e.g., under a UDL, BMD is parabolic with maximum at mid-span where shear is zero).
  - **Positive shear ⇒ Positive slope of BMD** (BMD increasing), **Negative shear ⇒ Negative slope** (BMD decreasing).
- **Points of Contraflexure (Inflection Points):** Locations where bending moment is zero while shear is non-zero; the BMD changes sign here.

**(b) A simply-supported beam of length $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load of intensity $w$ over its entire span. Using the differential relations derived above, obtain expressions for shear force $V(x)$ and bending moment $M(x)$ at a section distance $x$ from the left support. (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:** Beam of length $L$, UDL $w$ over entire span, simply supported.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Find reactions. By symmetry, each support carries:

$$
R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the second-order relation:

$$
\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Integrate once to obtain shear force:

$$
\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x) = -wx + C_1
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply boundary condition $V = R_A = wL/2$ at $x = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{wL}{2} = -w(0) + C_1 \implies C_1 = \dfrac{wL}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2} - wx
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Integrate again to obtain bending moment:

$$
M(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2}x - \dfrac{wx^2}{2} + C_2
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Apply boundary condition $M = 0$ at $x = 0$ (simply supported end):

$$
0 = 0 - 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
M(x) = \dfrac{wL}{2}x - \dfrac{wx^2}{2} = \dfrac{wx}{2}(L - x)
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Special results for design:
- **Maximum bending moment** at mid-span ($x = L/2$):

$$
M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} - \dfrac{w}{2}\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}
$$

- **Shear force is zero at mid-span**, confirming the location of $M_{\max}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Compiled from KTU Valuation Keys):**
>
> 1. **Missing the Negative Sign:** Forgetting that $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ (not $+w$) under the standard KTU downward-positive convention. *Cost: Up to 2 marks lost per derivation.*
>
> 2. **Neglecting Higher-Order Terms Incorrectly:** Students sometimes drop the $dx^2$ term from the moment equation without justification. *Always state explicitly:* *"Since $dx \to 0$, the term $(dx)^2/2$ vanishes in the limit."*
>
> 3. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Mixing the rotational sense of positive shear on left vs. right face. *Draw the FBD clearly and label each force with its assumed direction.*
>
> 4. **Skipping the Boundary Conditions:** In the integration-based problems, students often forget to apply boundary conditions to find constants of integration. *Cost: 1–2 marks lost.*
>
> 5. **Misinterpreting "Intensity of Load":** Students sometimes confuse $w$ (intensity, N/m) with total load $W$ (N). *Always write the units to avoid ambiguity.*
>
> 6. **Drawing FBD Without Labelling:** Examiners award partial credit for a well-labelled Free Body Diagram. *Even a rough sketch with arrows and labels gets 1–2 marks.*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points for Last-Minute Reading:**

- 🔑 The three governing differential relations are:
$\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$, $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$, $\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w$.
- 🔑 The negative sign in $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$ originates from the **downward-positive load convention** used in KTU examinations.
- 🔑 Higher-order infinitesimals (terms with $dx^2$ or higher powers) **vanish** in the limit and must be explicitly dropped with justification.
- 🔑 **Slope of BMD at any point = Shear force** at that point. This is the single most important graphical theorem for board problems.
- 🔑 **Curvature of BMD = Negative of load intensity.** For UDL: BMD is a **parabola**; for UVL: BMD is a **cubic**.
- 🔑 **Points of zero shear ⇒ Points of maximum/minimum moment.** Always check second derivative sign to confirm maximum vs. minimum.
- 🔑 Boundary conditions for a simply-supported beam: $M = 0$ at both ends; for a cantilever: $V = 0$ and $M = 0$ at the free end.
- 🔑 Standard sign convention summary: **Downward load = positive**; **Sagging moment = positive**; **Shear causing CW rotation of element = positive**.
- 🔑 Units are your friend: **Always write units** (kN/m for $w$, kN for $V$, kN·m for $M$). Marks are sometimes awarded explicitly for correct unit usage.
- 🔑 **Free Body Diagram is mandatory** in any derivation question. Without it, expect to lose 1–2 marks even if the math is perfect.
- 🔑 The integration approach: **Two integrations of $w(x)$** yield $V(x)$ and $M(x)$; two constants of integration must be evaluated using two boundary conditions.
- 🔑 **Graphical check:** SFD area between two points = Change in BM; Load area between two points = Negative change in SF.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams of Cantilever Beams, Simply Supported Beams and Overhanging Beams for Different Type of Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams of Cantilever Beams, Simply Supported Beams and Overhanging Beams for Different Type of Loads # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Shear Force (SF / V)** at any section of a beam is the **algebraic sum of the vertical forces** (transverse forces) acting on either side of the section. It represents the tendency of the beam segment to **slide vertically** relative to the adjacent segment along the cross-section. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bending Moment (BM / M)** at any section of a beam is the **algebraic sum of the moments** (about the centroid of the section) of all the forces acting on either side of the section. It represents the tendency of the beam to **bend (curve)** at that section. ## 1.2 Standard KTU Sign Convention The KTU board strictly follows the **sagging-positive** convention: | Quantity | Positive Sign | Negative Sign | |----------|---------------|---------------| | Shear Force $V$ | Left segment pushed UP, Right segment pushed DOWN (clockwise rotation of element) | Left segment pushed DOWN, Right segment pushed UP (anticlockwise rotation) | | Bending Moment $M$ | **Sagging** (concave UP, smiles) — beam bends like a "U" | **Hogging** (concave DOWN, frowns) — beam bends like an "∩" | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for Students:** > - **S**agging = **P**ositive = **S**mile 😊 > - **H**ogging = **N**egative = **F**rown ☹️ ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board Imagine standing at the **free end of a cantilever diving board**. Your weight is a **point load at the tip**. The board experiences: - A vertical shear (it tries to slide vertically at any cut section) → **Shear Force**. - A twisting/bending tendency (it curves downward) → **Bending Moment**. Now flip the scenario: a **bookshelf bracket** fixed to a wall (cantilever) loaded with books. The maximum bending occurs at the **wall (fixed support)**, while shear is uniform throughout. This is why diving boards and brackets are designed thicker at the fixed end! ## 1.4 Why Study SFD and BMD? > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Shear Force Diagram (SFD)** and **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** are graphical representations showing the variation of $V$ and $M$ along the beam length. They are **indispensable** for: > - **Design of beams**: determining maximum bending stress $\sigma = \frac{M y}{I}$ and maximum shear stress $\tau = \frac{V Q}{I b}$. > - **Locating critical sections** where the beam is most likely to fail. > - **Selecting cross-section** dimensions and reinforcement (especially in RCC design). # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Fundamental Relationships Between Load, Shear, and Moment Let $w(x)$ be the intensity of **distributed load** (positive when acting downward in KTU convention), $V(x)$ the shear force, and $M(x)$ the bending moment at a section located at distance $x$ from a reference point. ### Derivation of the Governing Differential Relations Consider an infinitesimal beam element of length $dx$ loaded with intensity $w(x)$ (downward). Equilibrium of vertical forces and moments gives: $$\sum F_y = 0 \implies V - (V + dV) - w\,dx = 0$$ $$\sum M = 0 \implies M + V\,dx - (M + dM) - w\,dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0$$ Neglecting the second-order term $w\,(dx)^2 / 2$ for an infinitesimal element: $$\boxed{\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)} \quad\quad \boxed{\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)} \quad\quad \boxed{\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w(x)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Deduction Rule:** > - The **slope of SFD** at any point = negative of load intensity at that point. > - The **slope of BMD** at any point = value of SF at that point. > - Where **SF = 0**, BM is either a **local maximum or minimum** (point of contraflexure if sign flips). ## 2.2 Standard SFD and BMD Profiles for Elemental Loadings | Loading Type | SF Diagram Shape | BM Diagram Shape | |--------------|------------------|------------------| | Concentrated Point Load $P$ | Vertical jump of magnitude $P$ (rectangular step) | Linear inclined line, kink at load point | | Uniformly Distributed Load (UDL) $w$ | Linearly varying (slope = $-w$) | Parabolic (2nd degree curve) | | Uniformly Varying Load (UVL) varying from $0$ to $w$ | Parabolic | Cubic (3rd degree curve) | | Pure Moment $M_0$ applied | Zero SF everywhere | Step jump of $M_0$ | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cantilever Beams Let $L$ be the length of the cantilever and $x$ be measured **from the FREE END**. | Loading | Reaction at Fixed End $R_A$ | Fixed End Moment $M_A$ | $V(x)$ from Free End | $M(x)$ from Free End | Max $\vert V \vert$ | Max $\vert M \vert$ | |---------|-----------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|--------------------|--------------------| | Point Load $P$ at free end | $P$ (up) | $-PL$ | $P$ | $-Px$ | $P$ | $PL$ (at fixed end) | | UDL $w$ over full length | $wL$ (up) | $-\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ | $wx$ | $-\dfrac{wx^2}{2}$ | $wL$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ (at fixed end) | | UVL from $0$ at fixed to $w$ at free end | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $-\dfrac{wL^2}{6}$ | $\dfrac{w x^2}{2L}$ | $-\dfrac{w x^3}{6L}$ | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{6}$ (at fixed end) | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Simply Supported Beams (SSB) Let $L$ be the span and $x$ measured **from the LEFT support A**. Reactions $R_A$ and $R_B$ are vertical (upward). | Loading | $R_A$ | $R_B$ | $V(x)$ | $M(x)$ | Max BM Location | Max BM Value | |---------|-------|-------|--------|--------|-----------------|--------------| | Point Load $P$ at midspan $L/2$ | $\dfrac{P}{2}$ | $\dfrac{P}{2}$ | $\dfrac{P}{2}$ for $0 \le x < L/2$; $-\dfrac{P}{2}$ for $L/2 < x \le L$ | $\dfrac{P}{2}x$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$ | Midspan | $\dfrac{PL}{4}$ | | Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ from A, $b = L-a$ from B | $\dfrac{Pb}{L}$ | $\dfrac{Pa}{L}$ | $\dfrac{Pb}{L}$ (left), $-\dfrac{Pa}{L}$ (right) | $\dfrac{Pb}{L}x$ for $x \le a$ | Under the load | $\dfrac{Pab}{L}$ | | UDL $w$ over entire span | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL}{2} - wx$ | $\dfrac{wLx}{2} - \dfrac{wx^2}{2}$ | Midspan | $\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ | | UVL from $0$ at A to $w$ at B (over span $L$) | $\dfrac{wL}{6}$ | $\dfrac{wL}{3}$ | $\dfrac{wL}{6} - \dfrac{w x^2}{2L}$ | $\dfrac{wLx}{6} - \dfrac{w x^3}{6L}$ | At $x = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $\dfrac{wL^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.0642\, wL^2$ | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Overhanging Beams An overhanging beam has a main span between two supports and one or both portions extending beyond the supports. The overhanging portions typically produce **hogging (negative) BM** over the supports. ### Case: SSB with overhang, UDL over entire length $L_0 = L + 2a$ (symmetric) Reactions (by symmetry): $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{w(L+2a)}{2} = w\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2} + a\right)$ - Over overhang portion (length $a$ from free end): $M$ varies parabolically from $0$ at free end to $-\dfrac{wa^2}{2}$ at support. - Over main span: SF varies linearly; BM is parabolic with **sagging** region and possible **hogging** at supports. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Overhanging beams are used in **chajjas (sun-shades), balconies, and cantilevered slabs**. The overhang reduces the maximum positive moment in the main span while introducing a negative moment at the support — a phenomenon KTU examiners love to test! ## 2.6 Points of Contraflexure (Inflection Points) > [!IMPORTANT] > A **point of contraflexure** (or point of inflection) is a section on the beam where the **bending moment is zero** AND **changes sign**. At this point, the curvature of the beam reverses (from sagging to hogging or vice versa). > > In beams where overhanging portions exist, points of contraflexure occur within the main span where the BMD crosses the axis. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Cantilever with Point Load at Free End — Full Derivation **Setup:** Cantilever beam of length $L$, fixed at A (left), free at B (right). Point load $P$ acting **downward** at B. **Step 1 — Reactions at Fixed Support A:** $$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A - P = 0 \implies R_A = P \; (\uparrow)$$ $$\sum M_A = 0 \implies M_A - P \cdot L = 0 \implies M_A = -PL \; (\text{counter-clockwise, hogging})$$ **Step 2 — Consider a Section at distance $x$ from the FREE end (B):** Forces to the right of the section: only the point load $P$ acting downward at distance $x$. **Step 3 — Shear Force at Section $x$ (using right-side convention, downward load on right ⇒ negative shear):** $$V(x) = -P \quad \text{(constant throughout the beam)}$$ **Step 4 — Bending Moment at Section $x$ (taking moments about the section, clockwise positive on right side):** $$M(x) = -P \cdot x$$ - At free end ($x = 0$): $M = 0$ ✓ - At fixed end ($x = L$): $M = -PL$ (maximum hogging) ✓ **Step 5 — Diagrammatic Representation:** - **SFD:** Rectangle of magnitude $-P$ (below axis if downward is negative). - **BMD:** Right triangle, $0$ at free end to $-PL$ at fixed end. ## 3.2 Cantilever with UDL over Entire Span — Full Derivation **Setup:** Cantilever of length $L$, fixed at A, free at B. UDL of intensity $w$ (force/length) over the whole length. **Step 1 — Total Load and Reactions:** $$W = w \cdot L \quad (\text{total downward load})$$ $$R_A = wL \; (\uparrow), \qquad M_A = -\dfrac{wL \cdot L}{2} = -\dfrac{wL^2}{2} \; \text{(hogging)}$$ **Step 2 — Section at distance $x$ from FREE end:** Load to the right of section = UDL over length $x$ = $wx$ acting downward, acting at centroid distance $x/2$ from section. **Step 3 — Shear Force at $x$:** $$V(x) = -wx$$ - At $x = 0$: $V = 0$ ✓ - At $x = L$: $V = -wL$ (matches reaction) ✓ **Step 4 — Bending Moment at $x$:** $$M(x) = -(wx) \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = -\dfrac{wx^2}{2}$$ - At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ ✓ - At $x = L$: $M = -\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ (max hogging) ✓ **Step 5 — Diagrams:** - **SFD:** Straight line from $0$ at free end to $-wL$ at fixed end. - **BMD:** Parabolic (2nd degree) curve, zero at free end, $-\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ at fixed end. Concave DOWNWARD (since $\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w < 0$). ## 3.3 Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load — Full Derivation **Setup:** SSB of span $L$, supports at A (left) and B (right). Point load $P$ acting downward at midspan C ($x = L/2$). **Step 1 — Reactions (using symmetry or moment equilibrium):** By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{P}{2} \; (\uparrow)$ **Verification using $\sum M_A = 0$:** $$R_B \cdot L - P \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0 \implies R_B = \frac{P}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad R_A = \frac{P}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Shear Force:** - For $0 \le x < L/2$ (just right of A, before C): Consider LEFT portion. Only $R_A$ acts. $$V(x) = +R_A = +\dfrac{P}{2}$$ - For $L/2 < x \le L$ (just right of C, before B): Consider LEFT portion. $R_A$ up and $P$ down. $$V(x) = +\dfrac{P}{2} - P = -\dfrac{P}{2}$$ - **Jump at $x = L/2$:** Magnitude = $\dfrac{P}{2} - \left(-\dfrac{P}{2}\right) = P$ ✓ (matches load) **Step 3 — Bending Moment:** - For $0 \le x \le L/2$: $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \dfrac{P}{2} x$$ - For $L/2 \le x \le L$ (using right portion for ease): $$M(x) = R_B \cdot (L-x) = \dfrac{P}{2}(L-x)$$ **Step 4 — Critical Values:** $$M_A = 0, \quad M_C = \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{PL}{4}, \quad M_B = 0$$ **Step 5 — Diagrams:** - **SFD:** Rectangle at $+\dfrac{P}{2}$ from $0$ to $L/2$, then rectangle at $-\dfrac{P}{2}$ from $L/2$ to $L$. - **BMD:** Triangle peaking at $\dfrac{PL}{4}$ at midspan, sagging (positive). ## 3.4 Simply Supported Beam with UDL — Full Derivation **Setup:** SSB of span $L$, UDL of intensity $w$ over entire span. **Step 1 — Reactions:** By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} \; (\uparrow)$ **Step 2 — Section at distance $x$ from A:** $$V(x) = R_A - wx = \dfrac{wL}{2} - wx$$ **Setting $V(x) = 0$ for location of maximum BM:** $$\dfrac{wL}{2} - wx = 0 \implies x = \dfrac{L}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Bending Moment at $x$:** $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - wx \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = \dfrac{wL}{2} x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum BM at midspan:** $$M_{\max} = \dfrac{wL}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} - \dfrac{w}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{wL^2}{4} - \dfrac{wL^2}{8} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$$ **Step 5 — Diagrams:** - **SFD:** Straight line from $+\dfrac{wL}{2}$ at A, crossing zero at midspan, to $-\dfrac{wL}{2}$ at B. - **BMD:** Parabola, $0$ at both supports, peaking at $+\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ at midspan, concave DOWNWARD. ## 3.5 Simply Supported Beam with Point Load Eccentric from Center — Full Derivation **Setup:** SSB of span $L$, point load $P$ at distance $a$ from A, distance $b$ from B, where $a + b = L$. **Step 1 — Reactions:** $$R_A = \dfrac{Pb}{L}, \quad R_B = \dfrac{Pa}{L}$$ **Derivation of $R_A$ using $\sum M_B = 0$:** $$R_A \cdot L - P \cdot b = 0 \implies R_A = \dfrac{Pb}{L}$$ **Step 2 — Shear Force:** - For $0 \le x < a$: $V(x) = R_A = +\dfrac{Pb}{L}$ - For $a < x \le L$: $V(x) = R_A - P = +\dfrac{Pb}{L} - P = -\dfrac{Pa}{L}$ **Step 3 — Bending Moment:** - For $0 \le x \le a$: $M(x) = \dfrac{Pb}{L} \cdot x$ - For $a \le x \le L$: $M(x) = \dfrac{Pa}{L} \cdot (L-x)$ **Step 4 — Maximum BM (under the load at $x = a$):** $$M_{\max} = \dfrac{Pb}{L} \cdot a = \dfrac{Pab}{L}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the famous **Bending Moment Formula for Eccentric Point Load** — extremely high-yield for KTU exams! ## 3.6 Overhanging Beam — Full Derivation **Setup:** SSB with both overhangs, span between supports $L$, overhang length $a$ on each side, UDL of intensity $w$ over the **entire** length $L_0 = L + 2a$. **Step 1 — Total Load and Reactions:** $$W = w(L + 2a)$$ By symmetry: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{w(L+2a)}{2} = w\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2} + a\right) \; (\uparrow)$ **Step 2 — SF and BM over Left Overhang (from free end to support A):** Let $x$ be measured from the left free end. For $0 \le x \le a$: $$V(x) = -wx, \quad M(x) = -\dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$ At support A ($x = a$): $V_A = -wa$, $M_A = -\dfrac{wa^2}{2}$ (hogging, negative). **Step 3 — SF and BM in Main Span (from A to B):** Let $x'$ be measured from support A into the span. For $0 \le x' \le L$: $$V(x') = R_A - w(a + x') = w\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2} + a\right) - w(a + x') = \dfrac{wL}{2} - wx'$$ $$M(x') = M_A + R_A \cdot x' - w \cdot \dfrac{(x')^2}{2}$$ $$M(x') = -\dfrac{wa^2}{2} + w\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2} + a\right)x' - \dfrac{w(x')^2}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Locating Point of Contraflexure (where $M = 0$ in the main span):** Setting $M(x') = 0$: $$-\dfrac{wa^2}{2} + w\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2} + a\right)x' - \dfrac{w(x')^2}{2} = 0$$ Multiplying by $\dfrac{2}{w}$: $$-a^2 + (L + 2a)x' - (x')^2 = 0$$ $$(x')^2 - (L + 2a)x' + a^2 = 0$$ Applying quadratic formula: $$x' = \dfrac{(L+2a) \pm \sqrt{(L+2a)^2 - 4a^2}}{2}$$ By symmetry, both roots are valid: $x'_1 = a$ (just past A into the span, after point of zero BM), $x'_2 = L + a - a = L + a$ wait let me recompute. $$(L+2a)^2 - 4a^2 = L^2 + 4aL + 4a^2 - 4a^2 = L^2 + 4aL = L(L+4a)$$ $$x' = \dfrac{(L+2a) \pm \sqrt{L(L+4a)}}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Maximum Positive BM in the Span (where $V = 0$):** Setting $V(x') = 0$: $\dfrac{wL}{2} - wx' = 0 \implies x' = \dfrac{L}{2}$ (midspan, by symmetry) $$M_{\max}^{+} = -\dfrac{wa^2}{2} + w\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2} + a\right) \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} - \dfrac{w}{2}\cdot\dfrac{L^2}{4}$$ $$M_{\max}^{+} = -\dfrac{wa^2}{2} + \dfrac{wL^2}{4} + \dfrac{w a L}{2} - \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$$ $$M_{\max}^{+} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8} + \dfrac{waL}{2} - \dfrac{wa^2}{2} = \dfrac{w}{8}\!\left(L^2 + 4aL - 4a^2\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This expression is positive provided $L > (\sqrt{5}-2)a \approx 0.236\,a$. If overhangs are too long, the entire main span experiences hogging! ## 3.7 Cantilever with UVL — Full Derivation **Setup:** Cantilever of length $L$, fixed at A, free at B. UVL varying linearly from $0$ at A to $w_0$ at B. **Step 1 — Total Load:** Load intensity at distance $x$ from A: $w(x) = \dfrac{w_0 x}{L}$ Total load = $\int_0^L \dfrac{w_0 x}{L}\, dx = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2}$ (acts at centroid $\dfrac{2L}{3}$ from A). **Step 2 — Reactions:** $$R_A = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2} \; (\uparrow), \quad M_A = -\dfrac{w_0 L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2L}{3} = -\dfrac{w_0 L^2}{6}$$ **Step 3 — SF and BM at distance $x$ from A:** Load to the left of section = $\int_0^x \dfrac{w_0 \xi}{L}\, d\xi = \dfrac{w_0 x^2}{2L}$ $$V(x) = R_A - \dfrac{w_0 x^2}{2L} = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2} - \dfrac{w_0 x^2}{2L}$$ $$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \int_0^x \dfrac{w_0 \xi}{L}(x - \xi)\, d\xi$$ Computing the integral: $$\int_0^x \dfrac{w_0 \xi}{L}(x - \xi)\, d\xi = \dfrac{w_0}{L}\!\left[\dfrac{x \xi^2}{2} - \dfrac{\xi^3}{3}\right]_0^x = \dfrac{w_0}{L}\!\left(\dfrac{x^3}{2} - \dfrac{x^3}{3}\right) = \dfrac{w_0 x^3}{6L}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{M(x) = \dfrac{w_0 L}{2} x - \dfrac{w_0 x^3}{6L}}$$ At $x = L$: $M = \dfrac{w_0 L^2}{2} - \dfrac{w_0 L^2}{6} = \dfrac{w_0 L^2}{3}$ Wait — this conflicts with our earlier reaction moment. Let me re-verify. Actually, considering right side from free end is cleaner. Let $x$ from free end (B). Load intensity at distance $x$ from B (going toward A) is $w_0(1 - x/L) = \dfrac{w_0(L-x)}{L}$. Load to right of section (length $x$ from free end) = $\int_0^x \dfrac{w_0(L-\xi)}{L}\, d\xi = \dfrac{w_0}{L}\!\left[Lx - \dfrac{x^2}{2}\right] = w_0 x - \dfrac{w_0 x^2}{2L}$ $$V(x) = -\!\left(w_0 x - \dfrac{w_0 x^2}{2L}\right) = -w_0 x + \dfrac{w_0 x^2}{2L}$$ At $x = L$: $V = -w_0 L + \dfrac{w_0 L}{2} = -\dfrac{w_0 L}{2}$ ✓ (matches $-R_A$) $$M(x) = -\int_0^x \dfrac{w_0(L-\xi)}{L} \cdot (x-\xi)\, d\xi$$ Let $u = x - \xi$, then $\xi = x - u$, $d\xi = -du$: $$M(x) = -\int_0^x \dfrac{w_0(L - x + u)}{L} \cdot u\, du = -\dfrac{w_0}{L}\int_0^x \!\left[(L-x)u + u^2\right] du$$ $$= -\dfrac{w_0}{L}\!\left[(L-x)\dfrac{x^2}{2} + \dfrac{x^3}{3}\right] = -\dfrac{w_0 x^2}{2L}(L-x) - \dfrac{w_0 x^3}{3L}$$ At $x = L$: $M = 0 - \dfrac{w_0 L^2}{3} = -\dfrac{w_0 L^2}{3}$ (hogging at fixed end) So the corrected formula: at fixed end, max hogging moment = $\dfrac{w_0 L^2}{3}$ (note: this is for UVL with **zero at fixed end and max at free end**). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Correction from Section 2.3 Table:** For UVL from $0$ at fixed end to $w_0$ at free end, the max BM at fixed end is $\dfrac{w_0 L^2}{3}$ (not $\dfrac{w_0 L^2}{6}$ as previously listed in the table — please trust this full derivation over the table shorthand for the UVL case). The reaction moment is $-\dfrac{w_0 L^2}{3}$ and the load centroid is at $\dfrac{2L}{3}$ from A. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Master Flowchart — SF and BM Analysis Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Step 1: Identify Beam Type
Cantilever, SSB, Overhanging"] --> B["Step 2: Calculate Support Reactions
Use Sigma Fy and Sigma M"] B --> C["Step 3: Divide Beam into Segments

Take Section at Distance x"] D --> E["Step 5: Consider Forces
Either Left or Right of Section"] E --> F["Step 6: Apply Sign Convention

Algebraic Expressions"] G --> H["Step 8: Find Critical Values
Max M, Zero V Locations"] H --> I["Step 9: Plot SFD and BMD

Area of SFD equals BM change"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Relationships in Beam Analysis ```mermaid graph LR L["Distributed Load w x"] -->|"negative gradient
dV dx equals minus w"| V["Shear Force V x"] V -->|"gradient equals V

Maximum equals wL squared divided by 8"] end subgraph Overhanging_UDL["SSB with Overhangs and UDL"] O3_SFD["SFD: Negative on overhangs

Pushed UP"] end subgraph Sagging["Sagging BM Positive"] SAG["Beam Curves Upward
