Frown Shape"] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: Define Shear Force and Bending Moment. State the sign convention used for plotting SFD and BMD. **Model Answer:** > **Shear Force (SF)** at any section of a beam is defined as the algebraic sum of the vertical forces (transverse forces) acting on either side of the section. It represents the tendency of one part of the beam to slide vertically relative to the other part. > > **Bending Moment (BM)** at any section of a beam is defined as the algebraic sum of the moments, taken about the centroid of the section, of all the forces acting on either side of the section. It represents the bending tendency of the beam at that section. > > **Sign Convention:** > - **Shear Force:** Considered positive when the left portion of the beam is pushed UP and the right portion is pushed DOWN (causing clockwise rotation of the element). > - **Bending Moment:** Considered positive when it causes **sagging** of the beam (concave upward, smiles) and negative when it causes **hogging** (concave downward, frowns). --- ### Question A2: A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a point load of 30 kN at 2 m from the left support. Find the maximum bending moment. **Model Answer:** Given: $L = 6$ m, $P = 30$ kN, $a = 2$ m, $b = L - a = 4$ m. Maximum BM occurs under the load: $$M_{\max} = \dfrac{Pab}{L} = \dfrac{30 \times 2 \times 4}{6} = \dfrac{240}{6} = \boxed{40\ \text{kNm}}$$ --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Overhanging Beam Analysis > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** A simply supported beam ABC of span $5$ m (between supports A and B) carries an overhang BC of length $2$ m. It is loaded with a UDL of intensity $10$ kN/m over the entire length $AB + BC = 7$ m. **(a) [7 Marks]** Find the reactions at supports A and B. Draw the Shear Force Diagram (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagram (BMD), marking all critical values. **(b) [7 Marks]** Locate the points of contraflexure and determine the maximum sagging and maximum hogging bending moments. #### Model Solution for Part (a): **Step 1 — Identify Beam and Loads:** - Beam ABC: A (left support), B (right support), C (free end of overhang) - Span AB = 5 m, Overhang BC = 2 m, Total length = 7 m - UDL = 10 kN/m over entire length **Step 2 — Calculate Reactions:** Total downward load = $10 \times 7 = 70$ kN. Taking moments about A (counter-clockwise positive): $$\sum M_A = 0: \quad R_B \times 5 - 10 \times 7 \times \dfrac{7}{2} = 0$$ $$R_B \times 5 = 245 \implies R_B = 49\ \text{kN} \; (\uparrow)$$ $$\sum F_y = 0: \quad R_A + R_B = 70 \implies R_A = 70 - 49 = 21\ \text{kN} \; (\uparrow)$$ **Step 3 — Shear Force Calculations:** | Section $x$ from C (free end) | Region | $V(x)$ (kN) | |--------------------------------|--------|-------------| | $x = 0$ | C | $0$ | | $x = 2$ | Just left of B | $-10 \times 2 = -20$ | | $x = 2^+$ | Just right of B | $-20 + 49 = +29$ | | $x = 7$ | At A | $+29 - 10 \times 5 = -21$ ✓ (equals $-R_A$) | **Step 4 — Locate Maximum BM in Span:** $V = 0$ in span AB occurs when $29 - 10(x - 2) = 0$, i.e., $x - 2 = 2.9$, so $x = 4.9$ m from C (or $2.9$ m from A). **Step 5 — Bending Moment Calculations:** - At C: $M_C = 0$ - At B: $M_B = -10 \times 2 \times 1 = -20$ kNm (hogging) - At $x = 4.9$ m from C (max sagging): $$M_{\max} = M_B + \int_2^{4.9} V(x)\, dx = -20 + 29(2.9) - \dfrac{10 \times 2.9^2}{2}$$ $$M_{\max} = -20 + 84.1 - 42.05 = 22.05\ \text{kNm} \; (\text{sagging})$$ - At A: $M_A = 0$ (simply supported end) **SFD and BMD:** - SFD: Linear from $0$ at C to $-20$ at B, jump to $+29$, linear down to $-21$ at A. - BMD: Parabolic, $0$ at C, $-20$ at B (hogging peak), rises to $+22.05$ at $x = 4.9$, falls back to $0$ at A. #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Point of Contraflexure** is where $M(x) = 0$ in the main span (between A and B). Using $M(x') = M_B + R_B \cdot x' - \dfrac{w (x')^2}{2}$ where $x'$ is from B toward A: Wait, let me re-parameterize. Let $y$ be measured from B toward A (into the span). For $0 \le y \le 5$: $$M(y) = -20 + 29y - \dfrac{10 y^2}{2} = -20 + 29y - 5y^2$$ Setting $M(y) = 0$: $$5y^2 - 29y + 20 = 0$$ Applying the quadratic formula: $$y = \dfrac{29 \pm \sqrt{29^2 - 4 \times 5 \times 20}}{2 \times 5} = \dfrac{29 \pm \sqrt{841 - 400}}{10} = \dfrac{29 \pm \sqrt{441}}{10} = \dfrac{29 \pm 21}{10}$$ So $y_1 = 0.8$ m and $y_2 = 5.0$ m (= point A, trivial). **Point of Contraflexure** is at $y = 0.8$ m from B toward A (i.e., $4.2$ m from A). **Maximum Sagging Moment:** $M_{\max}^{+} = 22.05$ kNm at $y = 2.9$ m from B. **Maximum Hogging Moment:** $M_{\max}^{-} = -20$ kNm at support B. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Cantilever with Combined Loading > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2]** A cantilever beam of length $4$ m is fixed at A (left end) and free at B (right end). It is subjected to: - A point load of $20$ kN at the free end B, - A UDL of $10$ kN/m over the entire span. **(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the reactions at the fixed support A and draw the SFD. **(b) [7 Marks]** Draw the BMD and find the maximum bending moment. #### Model Solution for Part (a): **Step 1 — Calculate Reactions at Fixed Support A:** Total downward load = Point load + Total UDL = $20 + 10 \times 4 = 60$ kN. $$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A = 60\ \text{kN} \; (\uparrow)$$ Fixed end moment (sum of moments of all loads about A): $$M_A = -(20 \times 4) - (10 \times 4 \times 2) = -80 - 80 = -160\ \text{kNm} \; (\text{hogging})$$ **Step 2 — Shear Force Calculation (let $x$ be measured from FREE end B):** For $0 \le x \le 4$: - Just to the right of any section (i.e., towards free end): point load 20 kN and UDL over $x$ m = $10x$ kN. - By convention, downward loads on right portion give negative shear. $$V(x) = -(20 + 10x)$$ | $x$ (m) | $V(x)$ (kN) | |---------|-------------| | $0$ | $-20$ | | $1$ | $-30$ | | $2$ | $-40$ | | $3$ | $-50$ | | $4$ | $-60$ ✓ | **SFD:** Linear, from $-20$ at B to $-60$ at A (slope = $-10$ kN/m = $-w$). #### Model Solution for Part (b): **Step 1 — Bending Moment at Section $x$ from Free End:** Forces to the right of section: Point load 20 kN at distance $x$ from section, UDL $10x$ kN at distance $x/2$ from section. $$M(x) = -\!\left[20x + 10x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2}\right] = -\!\left[20x + 5x^2\right]$$ $$M(x) = -(5x^2 + 20x)$$ | $x$ (m) | $M(x)$ (kNm) | |---------|--------------| | $0$ | $0$ | | $1$ | $-(5 + 20) = -25$ | | $2$ | $-(20 + 40) = -60$ | | $3$ | $-(45 + 60) = -105$ | | $4$ | $-(80 + 80) = -160$ ✓ | **Maximum Bending Moment:** $M_{\max} = -160$ kNm at the fixed end A (hogging). **BMD:** A 2nd-degree parabolic curve (concave downward), $0$ at free end, reaching $-160$ kNm at fixed end. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign convention errors** (cost 1–2 marks): Always check whether your BM is sagging (positive, smile) or hogging (negative, frown). The most common mistake is assuming all BMDs are positive. > 2. **Incorrect reaction direction**: Assume reactions act upward first; if the answer comes out negative, that means your assumed direction was wrong. > 3. **Forgetting the parabolic shape for UDL**: KTU expects a parabolic BMD for UDL (not linear). Many students draw straight lines. > 4. **Not marking the critical values**: Always write $\dfrac{PL}{4}$, $\dfrac{wL^2}{8}$, etc., directly on the BMD with proper units (kNm). > 5. **Skipping units**: Write kN for forces, kNm for moments, m for length. Unitless answers are penalized. > 6. **Missing point of contraflexure** in overhanging beams: If $M = 0$ in the span, calculate and report the location — it's a favorite KTU question! > 7. **Area method confusion**: Remember that change in BM = area under SFD. This is a fast check method but sign must be carefully tracked. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Sign Convention:** Sagging BM = Positive; Hogging BM = Negative. SF positive when left portion is pushed UP. - [x] **Governing Equations:** $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$, $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$, $\dfrac{d^2 M}{dx^2} = -w$. - [x] **Cantilever with point load $P$ at free end:** SFD is constant $= P$, BMD is triangular with peak $= PL$ at fixed end. - [x] **Cantilever with UDL $w$:** SFD is linear ($0$ to $wL$), BMD is parabolic ($0$ to $\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$) — both hogging. - [x] **Cantilever with UVL ($0$ at fixed end, $w_0$ at free end):** SFD parabolic, BMD cubic. Max BM at fixed end $= \dfrac{w_0 L^2}{3}$. - [x] **SSB with central point load $P$:** Reactions $= \dfrac{P}{2}$, max BM $= \dfrac{PL}{4}$ at midspan. BMD is triangular. - [x] **SSB with eccentric point load $P$ at distance $a, b$:** Max BM under load $= \dfrac{Pab}{L}$ — very high yield formula. - [x] **SSB with UDL $w$:** Reactions $= \dfrac{wL}{2}$, max BM $= \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ at midspan. SFD is linear, BMD is parabolic. - [x] **SSB with UVL (zero at one support, $w_0$ at other):** Reactions $= \dfrac{w_0 L}{6}$ and $\dfrac{w_0 L}{3}$. Max BM at $x = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{3}}$ from smaller end. - [x] **Overhanging Beam:** Always produces **hogging moment at the support** adjacent to overhang. Points of contraflexure occur in the span if main span is long enough. - [x] **Point of Contraflexure:** Where $M = 0$ AND changes sign — found by setting $M(x) = 0$ and using quadratic formula. - [x] **Verification Check:** Area under SFD between two sections = Change in BM between the same sections (with proper sign). - [x] **Units:** Force in kN, Moment in kNm, Length in m, UDL intensity in kN/m. - [x] **Diagram Etiquette:** Always label axes ($V$ in kN, $M$ in kNm), mark critical values directly on diagrams, indicate sign by drawing above/below the datum line. - [x] **Most Tested KTU Topics:** Eccentric point load SSB ($\dfrac{Pab}{L}$), UDL on overhanging beam (point of contraflexure), cantilever with combined load (point + UDL).

topics.title: Point of Contraflexure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Point of Contraflexure ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition In the bending theory of beams, a **Point of Contraflexure** (also called a **Point of Inflection** or **Flexural Pivot**) is a section along the length of a loaded beam where the **bending moment (B.M.) is zero** and, more importantly, **changes its algebraic sign** as the section is crossed. Mathematically, the condition is: $$M(x) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dM}{dx} \neq 0$$ At this critical location, the **curvature of the elastic curve reverses sign** — the beam physically transitions from sagging (concave upward, sagging positive) to hogging (concave downward, sagging negative), or vice versa. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** > A point of contraflexure is NOT the same as a "point of zero bending moment." Every point where BM = 0 is a candidate, but only those where the bending moment diagram *crosses* the axis (sign reversal) qualify as a true point of contraflexure. --- ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long flexible ruler held firmly between your two palms (like a fixed beam). Now press downward on the center. The ruler will: - Curve **upward** near the ends (hogging — like a frown), - Curve **downward** near the middle (sagging — like a smile). Somewhere between these two regions, the ruler **must straighten out momentarily**, flipping from "frown" to "smile." That exact location — where the curvature momentarily becomes zero and then reverses direction — is the **Point of Contraflexure**. It is essentially the *pivot point* where the beam stops bending one way and starts bending the opposite way. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Metrics:** > - **Bending moment dimension:** Force × Length (N·m or kN·m) > - **Standard fixed-beam factor (for UDL):** Point of contraflexure occurs at **x ≈ 0.211 L** and **x ≈ 0.789 L** from either support. > - **Standard propped-cantilever factor (for UDL):** Point of contraflexure occurs at **x = 0.25 L** from the fixed end. --- ### Where Do Points of Contraflexure Typically Occur? Points of contraflexure **cannot** exist in: - Simply supported beams under typical transverse loading (BM is always positive/sagging). - Cantilever beams (BM is always negative/hogging under downward loads). They **commonly occur** in: - **Fixed (encastré) beams** with transverse loads. - **Propped cantilevers** (one end fixed, one end simply supported). - **Continuous beams** over multiple spans. - **Portal frames** and other indeterminate structures. - **Beams with overhangs** carrying loads that produce moment reversals. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### Mathematical Framework — Why and How The bending moment at a section `x` in a beam is governed by: $$\frac{dM(x)}{dx} = V(x), \quad \frac{d^2 M(x)}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$ where: - $V(x)$ = Shear Force at section `x` - $w(x)$ = Intensity of distributed load **Three essential conditions must be satisfied at a Point of Contraflexure:** | Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |-----------|-------------------|------------------| | Bending Moment is zero | $M(x) = 0$ | Beam passes through zero curvature condition momentarily | | Slope of BM ≠ 0 | $\frac{dM}{dx} \neq 0 \Rightarrow V(x) \neq 0$ | The diagram actually crosses the axis (not a tangent touch) | | Curvature changes sign | $\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ changes sign | Beam shape reverses concavity | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** > - **Point of Zero BM (Local Minimum touching axis):** $M = 0$ AND $V = 0$ (e.g., free end of a cantilever with point load at tip — but this is NOT a contraflexure point because there's no sign reversal across it). > - **Point of Contraflexure:** $M = 0$ AND $V \neq 0$ (the BM diagram physically crosses the axis with reversal of curvature). ### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Condition | Formula / Expression | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Defining condition | $M(x) = 0$ with sign change | Strict mathematical definition | | 2 | SF must NOT vanish | $V(x) = \frac{dM}{dx} \neq 0$ | Distinguishes it from zero BM due to $V=0$ | | 3 | Curvature reversal | $\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$ flips sign | $E$ and $I$ are positive constants | | 4 | Location (Fixed beam, UDL $w$, span $L$) | $x = \frac{L}{6}(3 \pm \sqrt{3})$ | $\Rightarrow 0.211L$ and $0.789L$ from each end | | 5 | Location (Propped cantilever, UDL $w$, span $L$, simple end at $x=0$) | $x = \frac{3L}{4}$ | From the simple support (or $L/4$ from fixed end) | | 6 | Location (Propped cantilever, point load $P$ at simple end) | $x = \frac{2L}{3}$ | From the fixed end | | 7 | Maximum BM (Propped cantilever, UDL) | $M_{max,sag} = \frac{9wL^{2}}{128}$ at $x = \frac{3L}{8}$ | Used in KTU design problems | | 8 | Fixed-end moment (UDL) | $\vert M_F \vert = \frac{wL^{2}}{12}$ | Hogging (negative) | | 9 | Fixed-end moment (Central point load) | $\vert M_F \vert = \frac{PL}{8}$ | Hogging (negative) | > [!WARNING] > **Never write** $\vert x \vert$ with vertical bars inside a markdown formula table — use $\lvert x \rvert$ instead. KTU examiners check formatting. ### Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Reinforced Concrete Design:** Points of contraflexure determine where **top reinforcement** (for hogging) transitions to **bottom reinforcement** (for sagging). This is crucial for **cutoff points** of bars in continuous RC beams and slabs. 2. **Steel Portal Frames:** Identify locations where the bending moment changes sign — design of haunches and column-beam joints. 3. **Bridge Engineering:** Continuous girder bridges have multiple points of contraflexure that dictate the placement of shear connectors in composite construction. 4. **Structural Health Monitoring:** Cracking patterns and deflection shapes help engineers locate approximate points of contraflexure during field inspections. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### Derivation 1 — Points of Contraflexure in a Fixed Beam with UDL **Problem Statement:** A fixed beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) $w$ per unit length over its entire span. Determine the positions of points of contraflexure. --- **Step 1 — Identify the indeterminacy and use standard results:** By symmetry, the vertical reactions and end moments at both fixed ends are equal. Using the standard slope-deflection / three-moment equation results for a fixed beam with UDL: $$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}, \quad M_A = M_B = -\frac{wL^{2}}{12}$$ (The negative sign indicates hogging moments at the fixed ends.) **Step 2 — Write the bending moment expression at a section distance $x$ from the left support A:** Taking sagging as positive and computing from the left: $$M(x) = -M_A + R_A \cdot x - \frac{w x^{2}}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the known values:** $$M(x) = -\frac{wL^{2}}{12} + \frac{wL}{2} \cdot x - \frac{w x^{2}}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Point of Contraflexure condition, $M(x) = 0$:** $$-\frac{wL^{2}}{12} + \frac{wL \cdot x}{2} - \frac{w x^{2}}{2} = 0$$ **Step 5 — Simplify by dividing throughout by $w$ (assuming $w \neq 0$):** $$-\frac{L^{2}}{12} + \frac{L \cdot x}{2} - \frac{x^{2}}{2} = 0$$ **Step 6 — Multiply the entire equation by $-12$ to obtain a clean quadratic:** $$L^{2} - 6L \cdot x + 6 x^{2} = 0$$ Rearranging into standard quadratic form $ax^{2} + bx + c = 0$: $$6 x^{2} - 6L \cdot x + L^{2} = 0$$ **Step 7 — Solve using the quadratic formula, $x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a}$:** Here, $a = 6$, $b = -6L$, $c = L^{2}$. Substituting: $$x = \frac{6L \pm \sqrt{(-6L)^{2} - 4(6)(L^{2})}}{2(6)}$$ $$x = \frac{6L \pm \sqrt{36 L^{2} - 24 L^{2}}}{12}$$ $$x = \frac{6L \pm \sqrt{12 L^{2}}}{12}$$ $$x = \frac{6L \pm 2\sqrt{3} \, L}{12}$$ $$x = \frac{L(3 \pm \sqrt{3})}{6}$$ **Step 8 — Evaluate the two solutions:** $$x_1 = \frac{L(3 - \sqrt{3})}{6} \approx \frac{L(3 - 1.732)}{6} \approx 0.2113 \, L$$ $$x_2 = \frac{L(3 + \sqrt{3})}{6} \approx \frac{L(3 + 1.732)}{6} \approx 0.7887 \, L$$ **Step 9 — Verify by symmetry:** The midpoint is at $x = 0.5L$, and $0.2113L + 0.7887L = L$ — symmetrically placed about the center. ✓ **Step 10 — Verify shear force is non-zero at these points (confirms true contraflexure):** $$V(x) = \frac{wL}{2} - w \cdot x$$ $$V(0.2113L) = \frac{wL}{2} - 0.2113 \, wL = 0.2887 \, wL \neq 0 \quad \checkmark$$ $$V(0.7887L) = \frac{wL}{2} - 0.7887 \, wL = -0.2887 \, wL \neq 0 \quad \checkmark$$ The shear force is non-zero, confirming these are **true points of contraflexure** (not mere zeros of BM due to vanishing shear). --- ### Derivation 2 — Point of Contraflexure in a Propped Cantilever with UDL **Problem Statement:** A cantilever beam of span $L$ is fixed at end A and simply supported at end B. It carries a UDL $w$ per unit length over the entire span. Locate the point of contraflexure. **Step 1 — Standard results for propped cantilever with UDL:** $$R_B = \frac{3wL}{8}, \quad R_A = \frac{5wL}{8}, \quad M_A = -\frac{wL^{2}}{8}$$ **Step 2 — Bending moment at distance $x$ from simple support B:** $$M(x) = R_B \cdot x - \frac{w x^{2}}{2} = \frac{3wL}{8} x - \frac{w x^{2}}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Apply $M(x) = 0$:** $$\frac{3wL}{8} x - \frac{w x^{2}}{2} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Factor out $wx$:** $$w x \left( \frac{3L}{8} - \frac{x}{2} \right) = 0$$ This gives $x = 0$ (the simple support end, trivial — not a contraflexure) or: $$\frac{3L}{8} = \frac{x}{2} \Rightarrow x = \frac{3L}{4}$$ **Step 5 — The point of contraflexure lies at $\frac{3L}{4}$ from the simple support, equivalently $\frac{L}{4}$ from the fixed end.** **Step 6 — Verify shear force:** $$V(x) = \frac{3wL}{8} - w x = \frac{3wL}{8} - w \cdot \frac{3L}{4} = \frac{3wL}{8} - \frac{6wL}{8} = -\frac{3wL}{8} \neq 0 \quad \checkmark$$ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1 — Bending Moment Diagram of a Fixed Beam with UDL The figure below is a Mermaid-style block functional representation of the BMD, illustrating the sign reversal characteristic of points of contraflexure. ```mermaid graph LR A["Fixed End A
M = -wL squared over 12
Hogging - Negative"] -->|BM rises parabolically| B["Point of Contraflexure 1
x = 0.2113 L from A
BM = 0, SF nonzero"] B -->|BM continues to rise| C["Mid-span
x = 0.5 L
M max sagging = plus wL squared over 24
Sagging - Positive"] C -->|BM falls parabolically| D["Point of Contraflexure 2
x = 0.7887 L from A
BM = 0, SF nonzero"] D -->|BM continues to fall| E["Fixed End B
M = -wL squared over 12
Hogging - Negative"] ``` **Reading the diagram:** The two nodes `B` and `D` are the **Points of Contraflexure** — the BM diagram physically crosses the horizontal axis at these locations with opposite slopes, indicating curvature reversal of the beam. --- ### Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Propped Cantilever with UDL ```mermaid graph LR A["Fixed End A
M = -wL squared over 8
Hogging region"] -->|BM is negative| B["Region 0 to L over 4
Hogging curvature dominant"] B -->|BM crosses zero| C["Point of Contraflexure
x = 3L over 4 from B
M = 0, V = -3wL over 8 nonzero"] C -->|BM becomes positive| D["Region 3L over 4 to L
Sagging curvature dominant
M max = 9wL squared over 128"] D -->|BM drops to zero| E["Simple Support B
R_B = 3wL over 8
M = 0 trivial"] ``` --- ### Diagram 3 — Bending Moment Diagram Sign Convention Block Model ```mermaid graph TB subgraph SIGN["Sign Convention in BMD"] direction TB S1["Above the axis = Sagging = Positive Bending Moment = Concave Up = Tension on Bottom Fibre"] S2["Below the axis = Hogging = Negative Bending Moment = Concave Down = Tension on Top Fibre"] S3["Crossing the axis = Point of Contraflexure = Curvature Reversal = Reinforcement Transition Zone"] end S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** > - All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `C`, etc.). > - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as standalone node names. > - Labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold/italic markdown inside double-quoted labels. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term *Point of Contraflexure* in a loaded beam. State the two mathematical conditions that a section must satisfy to be called a point of contraflexure. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Point of Contraflexure** is a section along a loaded beam at which the bending moment is zero and the bending moment diagram crosses the axis, i.e., the curvature of the beam changes sign. The two mathematical conditions are: 1. **Bending Moment is zero:** $M(x) = 0$ → (1 mark) 2. **Shear Force is not zero at that section:** $V(x) = \frac{dM}{dx} \neq 0$ → (1 mark) These two together ensure that the BMD actually **crosses** the axis with reversal of sign, not merely touches it. → (1 mark) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between a "point of zero bending moment" and a "point of contraflexure." Give one example of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Point of Zero Bending Moment | Point of Contraflexure | |---|---|---| | BM value | $M = 0$ | $M = 0$ | | SF value | $V$ may be zero OR non-zero | $V \neq 0$ strictly | | Sign of BM across point | Does NOT change sign | CHANGES sign | | Curvature reversal | No | Yes | **Examples:** → (2 marks) - Point of zero BM but NOT contraflexure: Free end of a cantilever with a point load at the tip — $M = 0$ but there's no reversal. - True point of contraflexure: At $x = 0.211L$ from the fixed end in a fixed beam carrying UDL — BMD crosses axis and reverses. --- ### PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024] **(a)** A fixed beam AB of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over its entire length. Draw the bending moment diagram and locate the points of contraflexure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A propped cantilever of span 4 m is fixed at A and simply supported at B. It carries a point load of 30 kN at mid-span. Determine the location of the point of contraflexure. **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to Q.A(a):** Given: $L = 6$ m, $w = 20$ kN/m. **Step 1:** Standard results for a fixed beam with UDL: - Reactions: $R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{20 \times 6}{2} = 60$ kN - Fixed-end moments: $M_A = M_B = -\frac{wL^{2}}{12} = -\frac{20 \times 36}{12} = -60$ kN·m **Step 2:** BMD is parabolic. Maximum sagging BM at mid-span: $$M_C = \frac{wL^{2}}{24} = \frac{20 \times 36}{24} = +30 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 3:** Locating the points of contraflexure: $$x = \frac{L}{6}(3 \pm \sqrt{3}) = \frac{6}{6}(3 \pm 1.732) = (3 \pm 1.732) \text{ m}$$ $$\therefore x_1 = 1.268 \text{ m from A}, \quad x_2 = 4.732 \text{ m from A}$$ **Answer:** Points of contraflexure lie at **1.268 m from end A** and **4.732 m from end A** (or equivalently 1.268 m from end B). **(7 marks — full breakdown: 2 for reactions/FEM, 2 for $M_{max}$, 3 for contraflexure calculation)** --- **Solution to Q.A(b):** Given: $L = 4$ m, fixed at A, simply supported at B, point load $P = 30$ kN at mid-span (2 m from B). **Step 1:** Standard results for propped cantilever with central point load: $$R_B = \frac{5P}{16}, \quad R_A = \frac{11P}{16}, \quad M_A = -\frac{3PL}{16}$$ Substituting: $$R_B = \frac{5 \times 30}{16} = 9.375 \text{ kN}, \quad R_A = \frac{11 \times 30}{16} = 20.625 \text{ kN}$$ $$M_A = -\frac{3 \times 30 \times 4}{16} = -22.5 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 2:** Bending moment equation for $0 \le x \le 2$ m (from simple support B): $$M(x) = R_B \cdot x = 9.375 \, x$$ This is linear, going from $M = 0$ at B to $M = 9.375 \times 2 = 18.75$ kN·m at mid-span (sagging). For $2 \le x \le 4$ m (from B): $$M(x) = R_B \cdot x - P(x - 2) = 9.375 \, x - 30(x - 2) = -20.625 \, x + 60$$ At $x = 2$: $M = 18.75$ kN·m ✓ At $x = 4$: $M = -22.5$ kN·m ✓ **Step 3:** Locate the point of contraflexure (BM = 0) in the second region: $$-20.625 \, x + 60 = 0 \Rightarrow x = \frac{60}{20.625} \approx 2.909 \text{ m from B}$$ Or equivalently, $4 - 2.909 = 1.091$ m from the fixed end A. **Answer:** Point of contraflexure is at **2.909 m from the simple support** (or 1.091 m from the fixed end A). **(7 marks — 2 for reactions, 3 for BM equations, 2 for contraflexure location)** --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** A fixed beam AB of span 8 m carries a point load of 40 kN at its centre. Draw the bending moment and shear force diagrams and determine the points of contraflexure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain, with neat sketches, the physical significance of a point of contraflexure in a beam. Why does a simply supported beam carrying a central point load NOT exhibit a point of contraflexure? **(7 marks)** --- **Solution to Q.B(a):** Given: $L = 8$ m, central point load $P = 40$ kN, fixed-fixed beam. **Step 1:** Standard fixed-end values: - $R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2} = 20$ kN - $M_A = M_B = -\frac{PL}{8} = -\frac{40 \times 8}{8} = -40$ kN·m (hogging) **Step 2:** SFD is constant at 20 kN from A to centre, then drops by 40 kN to $-20$ kN until B. **Step 3:** BMD is linear on both halves: - At A: $M = -40$ kN·m - At mid-span: $M = +20$ kN·m (sagging maximum) - At B: $M = -40$ kN·m **Step 4:** Locate point of contraflexure from A (linear interpolation): $$M(x) = -40 + 20 \, x$$ Setting $M = 0$: $$20 x = 40 \Rightarrow x = 2 \text{ m from A}$$ By symmetry, the other point of contraflexure is at $x = 6$ m from A (or 2 m from B). **Answer:** Points of contraflexure occur at **2 m and 6 m from end A** (i.e., at $L/4$ and $3L/4$ from either end). **(7 marks — 2 for FEM, 2 for SFD/BMD shape, 3 for calculation)** --- **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Physical Significance (5 marks — broken into points):** 1. **Curvature Reversal:** At the point of contraflexure, the curvature of the beam changes direction — concave up becomes concave down. The neutral axis changes side relative to the loaded fibre. 2. **Reinforcement Transition (RC Design):** Tension side flips. Steel reinforcement that was needed on the bottom fibre (sagging region) is no longer required there; instead, top reinforcement is needed. Hence, it marks the **cutoff location** of flexural bars in continuous beams. 3. **Bending Stress Zero Point:** Since $\sigma = \frac{M y}{I}$, the bending stress $\sigma = 0$ at this section (along the neutral axis, $y=0$, and $M=0$). The fibres there experience **no flexural stress**, only direct stress. 4. **Location of Max Deflection?:** The point of contraflexure is generally **NOT** the location of maximum deflection — it is a point of zero curvature only instantaneously. 5. **Why no contraflexure in simply supported beam with central point load:** The bending moment is everywhere **positive (sagging)** between the supports. The maximum BM is $+PL/4$ at the centre, decreasing linearly to zero at supports. There is no sign change because: - End moments are zero (hinge supports cannot develop moment). - Only sagging moments develop under downward loading. Hence the BMD never dips below the axis → no contraflexure. **(2 marks)** --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the sign convention:** Writing $M = -40$ kN·m without explicitly stating "hogging" loses the step-mark. Always label: "hogging (-ve)" or "sagging (+ve)". > 2. **Confusing point of zero BM with point of contraflexure:** A free end of a cantilever has $M = 0$, but it is NOT a contraflexure point. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 3. **Omitting the SF check:** For a full 14-mark solution, you MUST verify $V \neq 0$ at the candidate point to earn the "contraflexure confirmation" mark. > 4. **Arithmetic error in quadratic:** In the fixed-beam UDL problem, students often write $x = L(3 \pm \sqrt{3})/6$ but forget that this yields two values; presenting only one location loses 1.5 marks. > 5. **Units:** Always write kN·m (not kNm, not KNM). KTU examiners mark this strictly. > 6. **Sign of FEM:** Fixed-end moment is conventionally NEGATIVE for downward UDL on a fixed beam — writing it positive is a recurring error. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **Point of Contraflexure** is where $M(x) = 0$ **AND** $V(x) \neq 0$ — the BMD crosses the axis. - ✅ It indicates **reversal of curvature** (sagging ↔ hogging transition). - ✅ **Simply supported beams** under usual loading: **NO** point of contraflexure. - ✅ **Fixed beams** with UDL: contraflexure at $\mathbf{x = 0.211L}$ and $\mathbf{x = 0.789L}$. - ✅ **Fixed beams** with central point load: contraflexure at $\mathbf{x = L/4}$ and $\mathbf{x = 3L/4}$. - ✅ **Propped cantilever** with UDL: contraflexure at $\mathbf{x = 3L/4}$ from the simple end. - ✅ **Propped cantilever** with central point load: contraflexure at $\mathbf{x = L/4}$ from fixed end (requires computation). - ✅ It marks the **transition zone for reinforcement cutoff** in RC continuous beams. - ✅ At a point of contraflexure, the **bending stress = 0** along the neutral axis, and the **shear stress is maximum** in many practical cases. - ✅ Always check $V(x) \neq 0$ — this is what distinguishes it from a simple "BM = 0" condition. - ✅ Sign convention: **Sagging = Positive** (concave up, tension on bottom), **Hogging = Negative** (concave down, tension on top). State this at the top of every KTU answer. - ✅ The location is found by solving the **bending moment equation = 0** for $x$.

topics.title: Bending Stresses in Beams
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bending Stresses in Beams

# Bending Stresses in Beams

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bending Stress** (also called **Flexural Stress**) is the normal stress induced in a beam when it is subjected to a transverse loading that produces a bending moment about its neutral axis. The magnitude of this stress varies linearly across the cross-section of the beam, being zero at the neutral axis and maximum at the extreme fibres (the topmost and bottommost layers).

Mathematically, it is given by the **Flexure Formula** (also called the **Bending Equation**):

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where $\sigma$ = bending stress at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis, $M$ = bending moment at the section, $I$ = second moment of area (moment of inertia) about the neutral axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> Module 3 specifically requires students to derive the bending equation, derive the section modulus, compute the moment of resistance, and analyze stress distribution in **symmetrical and unsymmetrical sections**, including **composite beams** and **beams of uniform strength**. Students are also expected to solve problems involving **flitched beams** and curved/flanged sections.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a plastic ruler (scale) horizontally with both hands and pushing your thumbs inward from the top and bottom in opposite directions. What happens?

- The **top fibres** get squeezed (compressed) — they become shorter.
- The **bottom fibres** get stretched (elongated) — they become longer.
- Somewhere in the middle, there is a layer of fibres that **neither stretches nor compresses** — this is called the **Neutral Axis** or **Neutral Layer**.

The stress that develops in each fibre is directly proportional to how far it sits from this neutral layer. The fibres farthest from the neutral axis experience the **highest stress**, and that's exactly why beams usually fail at their top or bottom extreme fibres first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - **E** (Young's Modulus) for the beam material — measured in **GPa** or **N/mm²**
> - Standard metric: stress $\sigma$ in **MPa** (N/mm²), moment $M$ in **N·mm** or **kN·m**, $I$ in **mm⁴** or **m⁴**, $y$ in **mm** or **m**.

### 1.3 Types of Bending

| Type | Description | Shear Force |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Pure Bending** | Only bending moment acts, no shear force ($V = 0$). $M$ is constant. | $V = 0$ |
| **Simple Bending** | Both bending moment $M$ and shear force $V$ act together. | $V \neq 0$ |
| **Symmetrical Bending** | Plane of loading passes through the centroidal axis and an axis of symmetry. | — |
| **Unsymmetrical Bending** | Loading plane does not coincide with a principal axis. | Biaxial stresses |

The **simple bending theory** (also called the **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory**) is the foundation for deriving the bending equation.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions of Simple Bending Theory (Bernoulli-Euler)

These assumptions are critical because the flexure formula $\sigma = My/I$ is valid **only** when they are satisfied:

1. **The beam material is homogeneous and isotropic** — same elastic properties in all directions.
2. **The beam material obeys Hooke's Law** — stress is proportional to strain within the elastic limit ($\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$).
3. **The plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending** (Bernoulli's hypothesis).
4. **The beam has a uniform cross-section along its length**, or at least varies gradually.
5. **The self-weight of the beam is neglected**, unless specifically included.
6. **The radius of curvature is large** compared to the beam dimensions (slender beam condition).
7. **Young's Modulus (E) is the same in tension and compression** for the material.
8. **The beam is initially straight** and unbent before loading.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each assumption matters:**
> Assumption 3 (plane sections remain plane) is the cornerstone — without it, strain cannot be assumed linear across the depth, and $\epsilon = y/R$ would not hold. Assumption 6 ensures **plane sections remain perpendicular** to the deformed neutral axis only when the beam is slender; for deep beams, **shear deformation** must be considered (Timoshenko beam theory).

### 2.2 Neutral Axis — The Key Concept

The **Neutral Axis (NA)** is the line in the cross-section where:
- Longitudinal fibre **strain = 0**
- Longitudinal **stress = 0**
- It passes through the **centroid** of the cross-section (for homogeneous, symmetric beams)

The proof relies on the fact that the net axial force on the cross-section must be zero (since only bending moment acts):

$$N = \int_A \sigma \, dA = 0 \implies \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

This is the formal definition of the centroid — so the NA **must** pass through the centroid.

### 2.3 Derivation Logic (Roadmap)

The full derivation in Section 3 follows these five conceptual steps:

- **Step 1 — Geometric Kinematics:** A small element of length $dx$ on the original straight axis gets bent into an arc subtending an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature $O$.
- **Step 2 — Strain from Geometry:** A fibre at distance $y$ from the NA has its length changed from $dx$ to $(R + y)d\theta$, giving strain $\epsilon = y/R$.
- **Step 3 — Stress from Hooke's Law:** Stress in that fibre is $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon = E \cdot y / R$.
- **Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium:** The internal resisting moment equals $\int \sigma \cdot y \, dA = (E/R) \int y^2 \, dA = EI / R$.
- **Step 5 — Combined Flexure Equation:** Eliminating $R$ gives $\sigma / y = M / I = E / R$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — every KTU 2024 question in this module directly tests one or more of them.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma / y = M / I = E / R$ | **Master Flexure Equation** | Mixed |
| 2 | $\sigma = M \cdot y / I$ | Bending stress at distance $y$ | MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_{max} = M \cdot y_{max} / I$ | Maximum bending stress | MPa |
| 4 | $M / I = 1 / R$ | Curvature relationship | 1/mm |
| 5 | $M = E \cdot I / R$ | Bending moment-curvature relation | N·mm |
| 6 | $Z = I / y_{max}$ | **Section Modulus** | mm³ |
| 7 | $M_R = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z$ | **Moment of Resistance** | N·mm |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$ | Max bending stress using $Z$ | MPa |
| 9 | $\epsilon = y / R$ | Strain at distance $y$ | — |
| 10 | $M / E \cdot I = 1 / R$ | Slope of moment-curvature | 1/mm |
| 11 | $\theta = L / R$ | Slope/deflection angle (small) | rad |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

The bending stress concept is the **backbone of structural design** in:

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of RCC/PSC beams, steel I-girders, bridge girders.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of machine shafts, leaf springs, crane jibs, levers.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spar design, fuselage frames.
- **Automotive:** Chassis rails, leaf and coil springs.

> [!NOTE]
> The **section modulus $Z$** is the *single most useful design parameter* for beams — engineers select standard rolled-steel sections (I, channel, angle) based on the **required $Z$**, not on $I$ alone, because $Z$ directly tells you the moment capacity for a given allowable stress.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bending Equation (Full Flexure Formula)

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Target:** To prove the master flexure equation:
> $$\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

**Given Setup:**
Consider a small element of a beam of original length $dx$ lying on the neutral axis. Before bending, it is straight. After applying a bending moment $M$, the beam deforms into a circular arc with:
- Centre of curvature $O$
- Radius of neutral axis = $R$
- An element subtends angle $d\theta$ at $O$

#### Step 1 — Geometric Description of Deformation

The original (undeformed) length of the element along the neutral axis is $dx$. After bending, the same element traces an arc on the neutral axis of length:

$$L_{NA} = R \cdot d\theta$$

Since the element length is unchanged on the neutral axis:

$$dx = R \cdot d\theta$$

#### Step 2 — Length of a Fibre at Distance $y$ from NA

Consider a fibre lying at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis (above NA: tension, below NA: compression — sign convention depends on curvature direction). Its new length after bending becomes:

$$L_y = (R + y) \cdot d\theta$$

> *Reasoning:* The fibre lies on a circle of radius $(R + y)$ centred at $O$, so the arc length equals radius times angle.

#### Step 3 — Calculate the Strain in the Fibre

The change in length of the fibre is:

$$\Delta L = L_y - L_{NA} = (R + y)d\theta - R \cdot d\theta = y \cdot d\theta$$

Dividing by the original length $dx$:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{dx} = \frac{y \cdot d\theta}{dx}$$

But since $dx = R \cdot d\theta$, we get $d\theta = dx / R$, substituting:

$$\epsilon = \frac{y \cdot (dx/R)}{dx} = \frac{y}{R}$$

> **Conclusion:** $\boxed{\epsilon = y / R}$ — strain is **linear** in distance $y$ from NA.

#### Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law to Get Stress

For elastic material within proportional limit:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$$

Substituting the strain:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \frac{y}{R} = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

> **Conclusion:** Stress is also **linear** in $y$, varying from zero at NA to maximum at extreme fibres.

#### Step 5 — Relate Bending Moment to Internal Stress Distribution

The bending moment $M$ at the section must equal the moment of all internal stresses about the neutral axis. Consider an elemental area $dA$ at distance $y$ from NA; the force on it is $\sigma \cdot dA$, and its moment about NA is $\sigma \cdot y \cdot dA$.

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$

Substituting $\sigma = E \cdot y / R$:

$$M = \int_A \left(\frac{E \cdot y}{R}\right) \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

#### Step 6 — Introduce the Second Moment of Area (I)

By definition, the second moment of area about the NA is:

$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

So the equation becomes:

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}$$

#### Step 7 — Combine to Get the Master Equation

From Step 4: $\sigma = E \cdot y / R$, which gives $1/R = \sigma / (E \cdot y)$.

From Step 6: $1/R = M / (E \cdot I)$.

Equating both:

$$\frac{\sigma}{E \cdot y} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}$$

Cancelling $E$:

$$\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{M}{I}$$

Combining all three ratios:

$$\boxed{\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}}$$

Rearranged for stress:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}}$$

> *This is the famous Bending Equation (Flexure Formula) used throughout structural analysis.*

#### Step 8 — Maximum Bending Stress

Maximum stress occurs at the fibre farthest from the NA, i.e., at $y = y_{max}$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where $Z = I / y_{max}$ is the **section modulus**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Section Modulus ($Z$)

**Definition:** Section modulus is the ratio of the second moment of area to the distance of the extreme fibre from the neutral axis:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

Units: $\text{mm}^3$ or $\text{m}^3$.

**Physical Meaning:** $Z$ represents the *geometric efficiency* of a cross-section in resisting bending. Higher $Z$ means more moment capacity for the same allowable stress.

For standard sections:
- **Solid Rectangle** (width $b$, depth $d$): $I = bd^3/12$, $y_{max} = d/2$
  $$Z = \frac{bd^3/12}{d/2} = \frac{bd^2}{6}$$
- **Solid Circle** (diameter $D$): $I = \pi D^4 / 64$, $y_{max} = D/2$
  $$Z = \frac{\pi D^3}{32}$$
- **Hollow Circle** (outer $D$, inner $d$): $I = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$
  $$Z = \frac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32 \cdot D}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Moment of Resistance ($M_R$)

The internal resisting moment generated by the stress distribution must equal the externally applied bending moment $M$ at the section (for equilibrium):

$$M = M_R = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA$$

Assuming maximum stress $\sigma_{max}$ at the extreme fibre (linear distribution):

$$M_R = \sigma_{max} \cdot \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z$$

So:

$$\boxed{M_R = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z = \sigma_{max} \cdot \frac{I}{y_{max}}}$$

> **Design Principle:** A beam is safe when $M \leq M_R$, i.e., applied moment does not exceed the moment of resistance.

---

### 3.4 Beams of Uniform Strength

A beam of **uniform strength** is one in which the bending stress at every cross-section along its length is equal to the allowable stress $\sigma_{allow}$.

**Condition:** Since $\sigma_{max} = M / Z$, for $\sigma_{max}$ to remain constant at all sections:

$$\frac{M(x)}{Z(x)} = \text{constant} = \sigma_{allow}$$

This means $Z(x) \propto M(x)$ — the section modulus must vary along the length in proportion to the bending moment.

**Common profiles of uniform strength:**
- **Constant depth, variable width:** For a rectangular beam, $M = \sigma \cdot bd^2/6$, so $b \propto M$ when $d$ is constant.
- **Constant width, variable depth:** Similarly, $d \propto \sqrt{M}$ when $b$ is constant.

**Example (Cantilever with UDL of intensity $w$):** Bending moment at distance $x$ from free end is $M(x) = wx^2/2$. So width variation:

$$b(x) = \frac{6 M(x)}{\sigma \cdot d^2} = \frac{6 \cdot wx^2/2}{\sigma \cdot d^2} = \frac{3wx^2}{\sigma \cdot d^2}$$

> So the beam tapers as a **parabola** of the form $b \propto x^2$.

---

### 3.5 Composite (Flitched) Beams — Brief

For a beam made of two materials (e.g., timber reinforced with steel plates on top/bottom):

**Transformed Section Method:** Convert the beam into an equivalent section of one material by multiplying the width of the other material by the modular ratio $n = E_2 / E_1$.

$$b_{eq,2} = n \cdot b_2 \quad \text{where} \quad n = \frac{E_2}{E_1}$$

The equivalent $I$ about the NA is computed, then bending stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{eq}} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_2 = n \cdot \frac{M \cdot y}{I_{eq}}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bending Stress Derivation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Apply Bending Moment M to Beam] --> B[Deformed Geometry: Beam forms Arc]
    B --> C[Element dx on NA: dx = R dθ]
    C --> D[Fibre at distance y: New Length = R+y dθ]
    D --> E[Strain: ε = y / R]
    E --> F[Hooke's Law: σ = E × ε = E y / R]
    F --> G[Internal Moment: M = ∫σ y dA]
    G --> H[Substitute σ: M = E / R × ∫y² dA]
    H --> I[Definition: I = ∫y² dA]
    I --> J[Curvature Relation: M = E I / R]
    J --> K[Equate 1 by R expressions]
    K --> L[Master Equation: σ / y = M / I = E / R]
    L --> M[Final Flexure Formula: σ = M y / I]
    M --> N[Section Modulus Z = I / ymax]
    N --> O[Moment of Resistance: MR = σmax × Z]
    O --> P[End: Design Equation M ≤ MR]
```

### 4.2 Stress Distribution Across a Rectangular Beam Section

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    Block1["Compression +σmax"]:1 Block2["Compression σ":1 Block3["Neutral Axis σ=0":1 Block4["Tension σ":1 Block5["Tension +σmax":1]
```

> **Interpretation:** Reading the block diagram from left to right (top to bottom of beam):
> - Top extreme fibre: **maximum compressive stress** ($-\sigma_{max}$)
> - Moving downward: stress decreases **linearly**
> - At the **Neutral Axis** (middle): stress = **0**
> - Moving further downward: stress becomes **tensile** and increases linearly
> - Bottom extreme fibre: **maximum tensile stress** ($+\sigma_{max}$)

### 4.3 Cross-Section Geometry Reference (Standard Sections)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Standard Cross-Sections] --> B[Rectangle]
    A --> C[I-Section]
    A --> D[Circular Section]
    A --> E[T-Section]
    B --> B1["Z = bd² / 6"]
    C --> C1["Z = Ixx / ymax"]
    D --> D1["Z = π D³ / 32"]
    E --> E1["NA NOT at centroid"]
    E --> E2["First find centroidal axis"]
```

> **Key Insight:** For **symmetric sections** (rectangle, circle, I-section), the neutral axis passes through the centroidal axis by symmetry. For **unsymmetric sections** (T-section, angle section, channel), the neutral axis must first be located by computing the centroid before applying $\sigma = My/I$.

### 4.4 Beam Curvature Visualization (Pure Bending)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Straight Beam] --> B[Apply Pure Bending Moment M]
    B --> C[Beam bends into Circular Arc]
    C --> D["Radius of Curvature R = E I / M"]
    C --> E["Curvature 1/R = M / E I"]
    C --> F["Top Fibres Compressed"]
    C --> G["Bottom Fibres Stretched"]
    C --> H["Middle NA - No Strain"]
```
