<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — MOMENT OF RESISTANCE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moment of Resistance (M.R.)** is the internal resisting moment developed by the **bending stresses** acting over the cross-section of a beam, which balances the external bending moment applied on the section. Mathematically, it is the **couple (moment) produced by the resultant compressive force on one side of the neutral axis and the resultant tensile force on the other side**, about the neutral axis.

For a beam cross-section subjected to a bending moment $M$, the moment of resistance offered by the section is given by:

$$M_R = \frac{\sigma_b \cdot I}{y_{max}} = \sigma_b \cdot Z$$

where:
- $\sigma_b$ = **Extreme fibre bending stress** (N/mm² or MPa)
- $I$ = **Moment of Inertia** of the cross-section about the Neutral Axis (mm⁴)
- $y_{max}$ = Distance from the Neutral Axis to the **extreme fibre** (mm)
- $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ = **Section Modulus** (mm³)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Whenever a beam is in equilibrium under transverse loading, the **External Bending Moment = Internal Moment of Resistance** at every cross-section. This fundamental equality ($M_{ext} = M_R$) forms the basis of beam design and bending analysis.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **flexible plastic ruler** (or a diving board) horizontally with both hands and someone stands in the middle. The ruler bends downward.

- The **top fibres of the ruler get squeezed together** → they are in **compression** (think of trying to push the ends together).
- The **bottom fibres get stretched apart** → they are in **tension** (think of trying to pull the ends apart).
- Somewhere in the middle, there is a layer that **neither compresses nor stretches** → this is the **Neutral Axis**.

Now, the compressed top pushes back upward, and the stretched bottom pulls back downward. Together, these internal forces form a **couple** (a pair of equal and opposite forces separated by a distance) that **opposes the external bending moment** caused by the person standing on the board.

> This internal "fighting-back couple" is exactly what we call the **Moment of Resistance**.

- $\sigma_b$ (bending stress) = how hard each tiny fibre is pushing/pulling
- $Z$ (section modulus) = a "geometric efficiency number" that tells us how well the shape is at producing this resistance
- $M_R = \sigma_b \times Z$ = the total internal moment the cross-section can muster

| Quantity | Analogy in the Ruler |
|---|---|
| Top compression force | Your palm pushing up on the top of the bending ruler |
| Bottom tension force | Your palm pushing up on the bottom (imagine a mirror) |
| Distance between them | Depth of the ruler |
| $M_R$ | The total turning effect resisting the bend |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering example:** When civil engineers design a steel I-beam for a bridge, they do NOT make the entire cross-section equally thick. They push more material to the **top and bottom flanges** (where $\sigma_b$ is maximum) and remove material from the **web** (where stress is low). This maximizes $Z$ (and thus $M_R$) while minimizing self-weight — the I-section is *geometrically efficient*.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - Bending Stress ($\sigma_b$): **N/mm²** (MPa) or **kN/m²** (kPa)
> - Moment of Inertia ($I$): **mm⁴** or **m⁴**
> - Section Modulus ($Z$): **mm³** or **m³**
> - Bending Moment / Moment of Resistance: **N·mm** or **kN·m**
> - Conversion: $1 \text{ kN·m} = 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$
> - $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa} = 10^3 \text{ kN/m}^2$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Theory: Pure Bending Assumptions

The concept of Moment of Resistance is rooted in the **Theory of Simple Bending** (Bernoulli–Euler Theory), which assumes:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
2. The beam has a **plane of symmetry**, and loading is in that plane.
3. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (no warping).
4. The **radius of curvature is large** compared to beam dimensions (small deflections).
5. **Stresses due to shear** are neglected; only bending (normal) stresses are considered.
6. The material has **equal tensile and compressive moduli** ($E_t = E_c$).
7. The beam does not fail by **local buckling or lateral-torsional buckling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under these assumptions, the **bending stress varies linearly from zero at the Neutral Axis to a maximum at the extreme fibres**, and the moment of resistance is the **integral of these stresses over the cross-sectional area**, taken about the neutral axis.

---

## 2.2 The Core Logic Chain (Why and How)

Let us break down how the Moment of Resistance comes into existence:

- **Step 1 — Curvature Develops:** When a bending moment $M$ is applied, the beam bends into a circular arc of radius $R$. The longitudinal fibre at the neutral axis keeps its original length, while fibres above compress and fibres below stretch.

- **Step 2 — Strain Distribution:** By geometry, the longitudinal strain at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:
  $$\varepsilon = \frac{y}{R}$$
  (Strain is **linearly proportional** to $y$.)

- **Step 3 — Stress Distribution:** Applying Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$):
  $$\sigma_b = E \cdot \frac{y}{R}$$
  (Stress is also **linear in $y$**.)

- **Step 4 — Force on an Elemental Strip:** For a small elemental area $dA$ at distance $y$, the elemental force is:
  $$dF = \sigma_b \cdot dA = \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot dA$$

- **Step 5 — Moment of This Force about Neutral Axis:** The elemental moment is $dM = y \cdot dF$:
  $$dM = \frac{E \cdot y^2}{R} \cdot dA$$

- **Step 6 — Integrate over the Section:** Total internal moment:
  $$M_R = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y^2}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

- **Step 7 — Equate to External Moment:** For equilibrium: $M_{ext} = M_R$. This gives the **flexure formula**:
  $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma_b}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

- **Step 8 — Solve for M.R.:** At the extreme fibre ($y = y_{max}$, $\sigma_b = \sigma_{max}$):
  $$M_R = \sigma_{max} \cdot \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **master formula sheet** for Moment of Resistance problems in KTU examinations. Memorize these expressions — they form the backbone of nearly every Part B question in Module 3.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flexure Formula (General) | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | — | Core relationship for any bending problem |
| 2 | Moment of Resistance (M.R.) | $M_R = \dfrac{\sigma_b \cdot I}{y}$ | N·mm | When stress at a specific fibre is given |
| 3 | M.R. in terms of Section Modulus | $M_R = \sigma_b \cdot Z$ | N·mm | At the **extreme fibre** |
| 4 | Section Modulus Definition | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | mm³ | Always — defines geometric efficiency |
| 5 | Bending Stress at distance $y$ | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | N/mm² | Finding stress at any layer |
| 6 | Curvature Relationship | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | 1/mm | Deflection & curvature problems |
| 7 | Rectangular Section ($b \times d$) | $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12},\; Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$ | mm⁴, mm³ | Solid rectangular beams |
| 8 | Circular Section (diameter $D$) | $I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64},\; Z = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$ | mm⁴, mm³ | Solid circular shafts/beams |
| 9 | Hollow Circular (Outer $D$, Inner $d$) | $I = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{64}$ | mm⁴ | Pipes, hollow shafts |
| 10 | I-Section (Standard Table) | $I_{xx}, I_{yy}, Z_{xx}, Z_{yy}$ from IS 808 | mm⁴, mm³ | Standard rolled steel sections |
| 11 | T-Section | $I = I_1 + A_1 \bar{y}_1^2 + I_2 + A_2 \bar{y}_2^2$ | mm⁴ | Unsymmetrical sections |
| 12 | Equality Condition | $M_{applied} = M_{resistance}$ | N·mm | **Equilibrium at every section** |
| 13 | Safe Bending Moment (Design) | $M_{safe} = \sigma_{permissible} \cdot Z$ | N·mm | Beam design check |
| 14 | Required Section Modulus (Design) | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{permissible}}$ | mm³ | **Finding beam size** |
| 15 | Modulus of Elasticity (Steel) | $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² | MPa | Common numerical value |
| 16 | Modulus of Elasticity (Concrete) | $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | MPa | RCC design |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** In the above table, every vertical separator has been carefully written as a single pipe `|`. Any mathematical *absolute-value* symbol (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to prevent breaking the table layout during rendering in the KTU PDF viewer.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Moment of Resistance concept is **the cornerstone of every structural design** in civil and mechanical engineering:

- **Steel Beam Design:** Determines the section size (e.g., ISMB 400, ISMB 500) for a given span, load, and allowable stress.
- **Reinforced Concrete Beams:** The tensile moment of resistance is provided by steel reinforcement, while the compressive moment of resistance is provided by concrete — the two must balance $M_R = M_{applied}$.
- **Machine Shafts:** Determines the diameter of shafts subjected to combined bending and torsion.
- **Pressure Vessels & Storage Tanks:** Wall thickness and stiffener design rely on moment-of-resistance calculations.
- **Bridge Girders, Crane Hooks, Railway Sleepers, Aircraft Wings** — all rely on this concept.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Part B questions, you will frequently be asked: *"Find the dimensions of a beam that can safely resist a given bending moment."* The path is always: $M \rightarrow Z_{required} \rightarrow$ Section Dimensions.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flexure Formula (Foundation for M.R.)

### Derivation Setup

Consider a small element of a beam of length $\delta x$ initially, bent into an arc of radius $R$ subtending an angle $\delta\theta$ at the centre of curvature.

A fibre at distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis has:
- Original length: $\delta x$
- New length: $(R + y)\,\delta\theta$

Since $\delta x = R\,\delta\theta$ (arc on neutral axis), the change in length is:
$$\delta L = (R + y)\delta\theta - R\,\delta\theta = y\,\delta\theta$$

Strain at distance $y$:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{\delta x} = \frac{y\,\delta\theta}{R\,\delta\theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

Applying Hooke's Law:
$$\sigma_b = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

Now consider the **total internal resisting moment** about the neutral axis. The stress $\sigma_b$ on an elemental area $dA$ at distance $y$ produces an elemental force $dF = \sigma_b \, dA$ and an elemental moment $dM = y \cdot dF$.

Integrating over the entire cross-section:
$$M_R = \int_A y \cdot \sigma_b \, dA = \int_A y \cdot \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, the moment of inertia about the neutral axis is:
$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Therefore:
$$M_R = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

Equating the **internal moment of resistance** to the **external bending moment** $M$:
$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

Combining with $\sigma_b = \dfrac{E y}{R}$:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma_b}{y} = \frac{E}{R} \quad \text{(Flexure Formula)}$$

Solving for the **moment of resistance in terms of stress**:

$$M_R = \frac{\sigma_b \cdot I}{y}$$

At the extreme fibre, $y = y_{max}$ and $\sigma_b = \sigma_{max}$:

$$\boxed{M_R = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z \quad \text{where} \quad Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Moment of Resistance of a Rectangular Section

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A timber beam of cross-section $120 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$ (width $\times$ depth) is subjected to a bending moment of $12 \text{ kN·m}$. Find:
1. The bending stress at the extreme fibre.
2. The bending stress at a point $30 \text{ mm}$ above the neutral axis.
3. The total moment of resistance of the section.

**Given:**
- $b = 120 \text{ mm}$, $d = 200 \text{ mm}$
- $M = 12 \text{ kN·m} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$
- Neutral axis is at mid-depth (symmetric section) → $y_{max} = \dfrac{d}{2} = 100 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Moment of Inertia:**
$$I_{NA} = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times (200)^3}{12} = \frac{120 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} = 80 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{100} = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Bending Stress at Extreme Fibre (using M = σ·Z):**
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^5} = 15 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{MPa})$$

**Step 4 — Bending Stress at $y = 30 \text{ mm}$ from NA (using M/I = σ/y):**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{12 \times 10^6 \times 30}{80 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^7} = 4.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{MPa})$$

**Step 5 — Total Moment of Resistance of the Section:**
$$M_R = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z = 15 \times 8 \times 10^5 = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 12 \text{ kN·m}$$

> **Note:** As expected, the total M.R. equals the applied bending moment (12 kN·m) — confirming the equilibrium condition. **This equality is the validation check you should always perform in KTU examinations.**

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Finding Beam Dimensions from Applied Moment (Design Problem)

**Problem:** A simply supported steel beam of span $4 \text{ m}$ carries a uniformly distributed load of $30 \text{ kN/m}$ (including self-weight). Find the required dimensions of a rectangular cross-section (with $b = 0.5d$) if the permissible bending stress is $165 \text{ MPa}$. Use $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}$.

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment:**
For a simply supported beam with UDL:
$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{30 \times (4)^2}{8} = \frac{30 \times 16}{8} = 60 \text{ kN·m} = 60 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Required Section Modulus:**
$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{permissible}} = \frac{60 \times 10^6}{165} = 363{,}636.36 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus Formula for Rectangle:**
With $b = 0.5d$ and $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$:
$$Z = \frac{(0.5d) \cdot d^2}{6} = \frac{d^3}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve for $d$:**
$$\frac{d^3}{12} = 363{,}636.36$$
$$d^3 = 4{,}363{,}636.36$$
$$d = \sqrt[3]{4{,}363{,}636.36} \approx 163.5 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt $d = 165 \text{ mm}$ (next higher standard size), then:
$$b = 0.5 \times 165 = 82.5 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{adopt } 85 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Provide Section in Standard Sizes:**
Final adopted section: $b = 85 \text{ mm} \times d = 165 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 6 — Verification:**
$$I = \frac{85 \times 165^3}{12} = \frac{85 \times 4{,}492{,}125}{12} = 31.82 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{31.82 \times 10^6}{82.5} = 3.857 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$M_R = \sigma_{permissible} \cdot Z = 165 \times 3.857 \times 10^5 = 63.65 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 63.65 \text{ kN·m} > 60 \text{ kN·m} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| Given | Asked | Use This Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ and Section | Stress at distance $y$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ |
| $M$ and Section | Extreme fibre stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ |
| Section and $\sigma_{max}$ | Resisting moment | $M_R = \sigma_{max} \cdot Z$ |
| $M$ and $\sigma_{permissible}$ | Required section modulus | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{permissible}}$ |
| Section and $E$ | Curvature/Radius | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ |
| Section and $M$ | Total tensile force | $F_{tension} = \dfrac{M}{I} \cdot \int_A y \, dA$ above NA |
| Section and $M$ | Total compressive force | Equal in magnitude to tensile force |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mechanics of Moment of Resistance

The following diagram shows the **flow of forces and stresses** that produce the moment of resistance inside a beam cross-section.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Bending Moment M applied to Beam] --> B[Beam Cross Section Bends into Circular Arc]
    B --> C[Longitudinal Strain varies linearly: strain = y / R]
    C --> D[Bending Stress varies linearly: sigma_b = E y / R]
    D --> E{Region of Cross Section}
    E --> F[Above Neutral Axis: COMPRESSION]
    E --> G[On Neutral Axis: ZERO STRESS]
    E --> H[Below Neutral Axis: TENSION]
    F --> I[Resultant Compressive Force C acts above NA]
    H --> J[Resultant Tensile Force T acts below NA]
    I --> K[Couple formed between C and T]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Moment of Resistance M_R = sigma_max x Z]
    L --> M[Equilibrium Check: M_R = M_applied]
    M --> N[If M_R >= M: Beam is SAFE]
    M --> O[If M_R < M: Beam FAILS, Redesign]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid block above uses purely alphanumeric node IDs (`A`, `B`, …, `O`) and **raw uppercase text labels** (no bold, italics, or HTML tables inside the labels) to comply with the KTU rendering safeguards.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress Distribution Across Common Cross-Sections

The following diagram compares the **linear stress distribution** across common beam sections, showing the **extreme fibre stress** $\sigma_{max}$ and the neutral axis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Rectangular Section] --> A2[Stress Linear: 0 at centre, max at top and bottom]
    B1[Circular Section] --> B2[Stress Linear: 0 at centre, max at top and bottom]
    C1[I Section] --> C2[Stress Linear: 0 at NA, max at top and bottom flanges]
    D1[T Section] --> D2[Stress Linear: 0 at NA below top flange, max at bottom tip]
    E1[Unsymmetrical Section] --> E2[NA NOT at geometric centre: M_R top may differ from bottom]
    A2 --> F[M_R equals applied M]
    B2 --> F
    C2 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E2 --> G[Compute top and bottom M_R separately]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Design Procedure Using M.R.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Identify Span, Loading Type, Support Conditions] --> B[Step 2: Draw SFD and BMD to find M_max]
    B --> C[Step 3: Identify sigma_permissible from material table]
    C --> D[Step 4: Compute Z_required = M_max / sigma_permissible]
    D --> E[Step 5: Choose cross section shape, relate b and d]
    E --> F[Step 6: Equate Z_required to Z formula of chosen shape]
    F --> G[Step 7: Solve for cross section dimensions]
    G --> H[Step 8: Round up to next higher standard size]
    H --> I[Step 9: Verify that M_R of adopted section >= M_max]
    I --> J[Step 10: Final Design Section reported]
```

---

Although Mermaid cannot physically draw a beam, here is the **precise schematic** you should reproduce in your answer sheet:

> **[BENDING STRESS DISTRIBUTION ACROSS BEAM CROSS-SECTION]**
>
> ```
>        σ_max (Compression)
>          ▲
>          █
>          █▒
>          █▒▒
>          █▒▒▒
>   ───────█N▒A▒▒██████  ← Neutral Axis (Stress = 0)

> ```

> - Area of this stress diagram, multiplied by the lever arm, gives the Moment of Resistance.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question carries **3 marks** and typically requires a precise definition, formula, or a one-step numerical substitution. The KTU 2024 scheme expects **direct, crisp answers of 4–6 lines**.

---

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Moment of Resistance of a beam. State the relation between bending moment and moment of resistance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Moment of Resistance** is the internal resisting moment developed by the bending stresses acting over the cross-section of a beam. It is the couple produced by the resultant tensile force on one side of the neutral axis and the resultant compressive force on the other side.
>
> The relation between bending moment $M$ and moment of resistance $M_R$ is given by the **flexure formula**:
>
> $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma_b}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$
>
> At the extreme fibre:
> $$M_R = \sigma_b \cdot Z = \frac{\sigma_b \cdot I}{y_{max}}$$
>
> For equilibrium: $M_{applied} = M_R$ at every section. (3 marks — definition 1, relation 2)

---

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is section modulus? Write its expression for a rectangular and a circular cross-section.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Section Modulus ($Z$)** is the ratio of the moment of inertia of a cross-section to the distance of the extreme fibre from the neutral axis. It is a measure of the geometric efficiency of a beam section to resist bending. Mathematically:
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$
>
> - **Rectangular section** ($b \times d$): $\;Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$
> - **Circular section** (diameter $D$): $\;Z = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$ (3 marks — definition 1, two formulas 2)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split typically as **Part (a): 7 marks** and **Part (b): 7 marks**. KTU 2024 internal choice means you must answer **either Option A or Option B in full**.

---

### **Question B — OPTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A simply supported beam of span $6 \text{ m}$ carries a uniformly distributed load of $20 \text{ kN/m}$ over its entire span. The beam has a rectangular cross-section $200 \text{ mm}$ wide and $400 \text{ mm}$ deep.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the maximum bending moment and the bending stress at the top and bottom fibres of the beam at the section of maximum moment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the permissible bending stress is limited to $9 \text{ N/mm}^2$, determine whether the given section is safe. If not, find the revised depth of the beam (keeping width the same) that will make the section safe.

---

#### **Model Solution — Option A**

**Given Data:**
- Span $L = 6 \text{ m}$
- UDL $w = 20 \text{ kN/m}$
- Cross-section: $b = 200 \text{ mm}$, $d = 400 \text{ mm}$
- Permissible stress $\sigma_{perm} = 9 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (concrete beam)

---

**Part (a) — Maximum Bending Moment & Fibre Stress [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment:**
For a simply supported beam with UDL:
$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 36}{8} = 90 \text{ kN·m}$$
$$M_{max} = 90 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia about NA:**
For a rectangular section, NA is at mid-depth:
$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 400^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 64 \times 10^6}{12} = 1066.67 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Section Modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{1066.67 \times 10^6}{200} = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

(Equivalently, $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6} = \dfrac{200 \times 400^2}{6} = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$ ✓)

**Step 4 — Bending Stress at Extreme Fibre:**
$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 16.875 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Identify Top vs. Bottom Fibre Stress:**
By symmetry of the section, the stress magnitude is the same at both fibres:
- **Top fibre** (compression): $\sigma_{top} = 16.875 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (compressive)
- **Bottom fibre** (tension): $\sigma_{bottom} = 16.875 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (tensile)

> **Final Answer (a):** $M_{max} = 90 \text{ kN·m}$; $\sigma_{top} = \sigma_{bottom} = 16.875 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (C and T respectively)

---

**Part (b) — Safety Check & Revised Depth [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compare with Permissible Stress:**
$$\sigma_{max} = 16.875 \text{ N/mm}^2 > \sigma_{perm} = 9 \text{ N/mm}^2$$
Therefore, the **section is NOT SAFE**.

**Step 2 — Required Section Modulus for Safety:**
$$Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{9} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Required Depth (with $b = 200 \text{ mm}$):**
$$Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = 10 \times 10^6$$
$$d^2 = \frac{10 \times 10^6 \times 6}{200} = 300{,}000$$
$$d = \sqrt{300{,}000} = 547.72 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Adopt Standard Depth:**
Take $d = 550 \text{ mm}$ (next round figure).

**Step 5 — Verification:**
$$Z_{adopted} = \frac{200 \times 550^2}{6} = \frac{200 \times 302{,}500}{6} = 10.083 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$M_R = 9 \times 10.083 \times 10^6 = 90.75 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 90.75 \text{ kN·m} > 90 \text{ kN·m} \;\;\checkmark$$

> **Final Answer (b):** The given section is **unsafe**; required depth ≈ **547.72 mm**; adopt **$d = 550 \text{ mm}$**.

---

### **Question B — OPTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A cast iron beam of T-section has the following dimensions:**
- **Flange:** $100 \text{ mm} \times 20 \text{ mm}$
- **Web:** $20 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm}$ (height below flange)

The beam is simply supported over a span of $3 \text{ m}$ and carries a point load of $12 \text{ kN}$ at the centre.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Find the position of the neutral axis and the moment of inertia of the section about the NA.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the maximum tensile and compressive stresses in the beam. The permissible stresses are $\sigma_t = 30 \text{ MPa}$ and $\sigma_c = 90 \text{ MPa}$. Check whether the section is safe.

---

#### **Model Solution — Option B**

**Given Data:**
- Flange: $b_f = 100 \text{ mm}$, $t_f = 20 \text{ mm}$
- Web: $b_w = 20 \text{ mm}$, $h_w = 100 \text{ mm}$ (below flange)
- Span $L = 3 \text{ m}$, Point load $W = 12 \text{ kN}$ at centre

**Step 0 — Total Depth and Section Sketch:**
Total depth = $20 + 100 = 120 \text{ mm}$. The flange is at the top.

---

**Part (a) — Position of Neutral Axis & Moment of Inertia [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Take a Reference Datum (e.g., Top of Flange).**

**Step 2 — Areas and Centroidal Distances from Top:**

| Part | Area $A$ (mm²) | $\bar{y}$ from top (mm) | $A \cdot \bar{y}$ (mm³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flange | $100 \times 20 = 2000$ | $10$ | $20{,}000$ |
| Web | $20 \times 100 = 2000$ | $20 + 50 = 70$ | $140{,}000$ |
| **Total** | $\sum A = 4000$ | — | $\sum A\bar{y} = 160{,}000$ |

**Step 3 — Centroid of Whole Section (from Top):**
$$\bar{y}_{top} = \frac{\sum A \bar{y}}{\sum A} = \frac{160{,}000}{4000} = 40 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Position of Neutral Axis from Bottom:**
$$\bar{y}_{NA, \text{ from bottom}} = 120 - 40 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

So, NA lies **40 mm from top** and **80 mm from bottom**. This is the **unsymmetrical** nature of the T-section.

**Step 5 — Distances from NA to Centroid of Each Part:**
- Flange centroid is at $10 \text{ mm}$ from top → distance from NA = $40 - 10 = 30 \text{ mm}$ (above NA)
- Web centroid is at $70 \text{ mm}$ from top → distance from NA = $70 - 40 = 30 \text{ mm}$ (below NA)

**Step 6 — Moment of Inertia of Each Part about its Own Centroid:**
- Flange: $I_1 = \dfrac{b_f \cdot t_f^3}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 20^3}{12} = \dfrac{800{,}000}{12} = 66{,}666.67 \text{ mm}^4$
- Web: $I_2 = \dfrac{b_w \cdot h_w^3}{12} = \dfrac{20 \times 100^3}{12} = \dfrac{20 \times 10^6}{12} = 1{,}666{,}666.67 \text{ mm}^4$

**Step 7 — Apply Parallel Axis Theorem:**
$$I_{NA} = I_1 + A_1 h_1^2 + I_2 + A_2 h_2^2$$
$$I_{NA} = 66{,}666.67 + 2000 \times 30^2 + 1{,}666{,}666.67 + 2000 \times 30^2$$
$$I_{NA} = 66{,}666.67 + 1{,}800{,}000 + 1{,}666{,}666.67 + 1{,}800{,}000$$
$$I_{NA} = 5{,}333{,}333.34 \text{ mm}^4 \approx 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

> **Final Answer (a):** NA is at **40 mm from top** (or **80 mm from bottom**); $I_{NA} \approx 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$.

---

**Part (b) — Maximum Tensile and Compressive Stresses [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Maximum Bending Moment:**
For simply supported beam with central point load:
$$M_{max} = \frac{W L}{4} = \frac{12 \times 3}{4} = 9 \text{ kN·m} = 9 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Extreme Fibre Distances from NA:**
- Top extreme fibre (compression): $y_{top} = 40 \text{ mm}$
- Bottom extreme fibre (tension): $y_{bot} = 80 \text{ mm}$

**Step 3 — Maximum Compressive Stress (Top Fibre):**
$$\sigma_c = \frac{M \cdot y_{top}}{I} = \frac{9 \times 10^6 \times 40}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 67.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{MPa})$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Tensile Stress (Bottom Fibre):**
$$\sigma_t = \frac{M \cdot y_{bot}}{I} = \frac{9 \times 10^6 \times 80}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 135 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;(\text{MPa})$$

**Step 5 — Safety Check:**
- Compressive stress: $\sigma_c = 67.5 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{c,perm} = 90 \text{ MPa} \;\;\checkmark$ Safe in compression
- Tensile stress: $\sigma_t = 135 \text{ MPa} > \sigma_{t,perm} = 30 \text{ MPa} \;\;\times$ **NOT Safe in tension**

> **Final Answer (b):** $\sigma_c = 67.5 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_t = 135 \text{ MPa}$. The section is **NOT safe** in tension (it fails the tensile stress criterion).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on "Moment of Resistance" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the lever-arm conversion factor** of $10^6$ when converting kN·m to N·mm. *Always write* $M (\text{kN·m}) \times 10^6 = M (\text{N·mm})$.
> 2. **Confusing the position of NA for unsymmetrical sections** (T, L, channel). The NA is *not* at the geometric centre — it must be found by the centroid formula $\bar{y} = \dfrac{\sum A \bar{y}}{\sum A}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the Parallel Axis Theorem** when computing $I$ of composite sections: $I = I_{self} + A h^2$ is **mandatory**; omitting $Ah^2$ is the #1 cause of zero marks.
> 4. **Mixing up tensile and compressive fibres**: The fibre *farther* from NA experiences the *larger* stress magnitude. For a simply supported beam with downward loading, the **top fibre is in compression** and the **bottom fibre is in tension**.
> 5. **Not verifying with the equilibrium check** $M_R = M_{applied}$ — this is a free 1-mark sanity check the examiner expects.
> 6. **Unit mismatch**: $I$ in mm⁴, $M$ in N·mm, $y$ in mm, $\sigma$ in N/mm² — mixing up any of these forfeits the numerical mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- **Core Definition:** Moment of Resistance = Internal resisting couple from bending stresses acting about the NA.
- **Fundamental Equation (Flexure Formula):**
  $$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma_b}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$
- **Most-used Form:** $M_R = \sigma_b \cdot Z$, where $Z = I / y_{max}$.
- **Equilibrium Condition:** $M_{applied} = M_R$ at every section. **This is your answer-validation tool.**
- **Section Modulus Values to Memorize:**
  - Rectangle ($b \times d$): $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$
  - Circle (diameter $D$): $Z = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$
  - Hollow rectangle (outer $B \times D$, inner $b \times d$): $Z = \dfrac{BD^3 - bd^3}{6}$
  - Hollow circle (outer $D$, inner $d$): $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32 D}$
- **For Symmetric Sections** (rectangle, circle, I, channel, etc.): NA passes through the geometric centre.
- **For Unsymmetric Sections** (T, L, unequal I): NA must be located using the centroid formula; the **larger material concentration** is on the side of NA.
- **Bending Stress Sign Convention:** Below the NA → Tension (positive), Above the NA → Compression (negative). For sagging moment, this matches physical reality.
- **Design Procedure Shortcut:** $M_{max} \;\longrightarrow\; Z_{req} = M_{max}/\sigma_{perm} \;\longrightarrow\;$ Choose $b, d$.
- **Standard Steel Modulus:** $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (memorize).
- **Standard Concrete Modulus:** $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (where $f_{ck}$ is in MPa).
- **Failure Criterion:** A beam is **safe** if $\sigma_{max,actual} \le \sigma_{permissible}$ **at both top and bottom fibres** — always check **both**.
- **For T, I, L, Channel sections:** Always provide **two extreme fibre stresses** (one tensile, one compressive) and check **both permissible limits** — a section can be safe in one and unsafe in the other (e.g., cast iron beams are typically weak in tension).
- **Conversion Factor:** $1 \text{ kN·m} = 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$ (always use this; examiners do NOT grant marks for unit inconsistency).
- **Curvature is directly proportional to Bending Moment:** $1/R = M/(EI)$ — useful for combined problems with deflection.

> **END OF MODULE 3 — MOMENT OF RESISTANCE NOTES**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending Stresses in Beams of Various Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bending Stresses in Beams of Various Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bending Stress (Flexural Stress):** The normal stress induced in a beam at any cross-section, due to the applied bending moment, which varies linearly from zero at the **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** to a maximum value at the extreme fibres. The governing relationship is the **Flexure Formula**:
> $$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$
> where $M$ is the bending moment at the section, $y$ is the perpendicular distance from the neutral axis, and $I$ is the **Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia)** about the neutral axis.

For design and rapid analysis, this is recast in terms of the **Section Modulus (Z)**:
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$
where $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 Learning Objective (As per KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Analyse bending stresses in beams of various cross-sectional shapes (rectangular, circular, I, T, channel, and hollow sections) and determine the maximum fibre stress for a given bending moment.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Diving Board" 🏊

Imagine a wooden diving board fixed at one end. When a diver stands at the free end:

- The **top fibres** of the board get **squeezed together** → **Compressive stress**
- The **bottom fibres** get **stretched apart** → **Tensile stress**
- Somewhere in the middle, fibres experience **no change in length** → **Neutral Axis**

**Real-world parallels:**

| Engineering Application | Bending Stress Type | Cross-Section Used |
|---|---|---|
| Diving board / shelf bracket | Bending due to overhang | Rectangular timber |
| Crane jib / boom | Bending under load | I-Section (steel) |
| Axle of a railway wagon | Bending under wheel load | Solid / Hollow circular |
| Floor beam in a building | Bending under slabs | I-Section / RSJ |
| Wall footing / lintel | Bending under masonry | T-Section (flange down) |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Check:** If you are asked *"At which fibre is bending stress zero?"* — the answer is **at the Neutral Axis**, *not* at the top or bottom. Mark this carefully in the KTU exam.

---

### 1.3 Standard Sign Convention (KTU Convention)

| Position in Cross-Section | Sign of Stress (Sagging) | Sign of Stress (Hogging) |
|---|---|---|
| Above Neutral Axis ($+y$) | Compressive ($-$) | Tensile ($+$) |
| Below Neutral Axis ($-y$) | Tensile ($+$) | Compressive ($-$) |
| At Neutral Axis ($y = 0$) | Zero | Zero |

> [!WARNING]
> Always draw the **stress distribution diagram** (a straight line passing through zero at the N.A.) — KTU examiners award at least **1–2 marks** for this free-hand sketch in any 14-mark bending-stress question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions of the Theory of Pure Bending (Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory)

For the flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ to be valid, the following assumptions must hold:

1. **Homogeneous material** — identical elastic properties at every point.
2. **Isotropic material** — same elastic modulus $E$ in all directions.
3. **The beam is initially straight** and has a uniform cross-section.
4. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis** after bending (no warping).
5. **The material obeys Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit.
6. **Self-weight is neglected**, unless specifically included.
7. **Pure bending** is assumed (no shear force, no torsion).
8. **Radius of curvature is large** compared to beam depth — i.e., small deflection (linear elastic regime).

---

### 2.2 Physical Significance of the Flexure Formula

The flexure formula $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$ has three distinct physical meanings, all of which are **frequently asked as 3-mark questions** in KTU:

- **Strength of Materials view:** Stress is directly proportional to the bending moment and distance from N.A.
- **Strength Design view:** Maximum stress occurs at the extreme fibre $y = y_{\max}$, giving $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$.
- **Stiffness / Geometry view:** The factor $I$ measures how well the cross-section *resists bending deformation* — a high $I$ means low stress for the same moment.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below use **SI Units**: $M$ in N·m, dimensions in m, $I$ in m⁴, $\sigma$ in N/m² (Pa), $Z$ in m³.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Flexure Formula | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Stress at a fibre distance $y$ from N.A. |
| 2 | Max Bending Stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Stress at extreme fibre |
| 3 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ | Geometric property for design |
| 4 | Moment of Resistance | $M_{R} = \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot Z$ | Internal resisting moment |
| 5 | Curvature Relation | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Radius of curvature under moment $M$ |
| 6 | Bending Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | Strain at distance $y$ |
| 7 | Triplet Equation | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | All-in-one flexure identity |
| 8 | Rectangular Section ($b \times h$) | $I = \dfrac{bh^{3}}{12}, \quad Z = \dfrac{bh^{2}}{6}$ | $y_{\max} = h/2$ |
| 9 | Solid Circular (dia $d$) | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}, \quad Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$ | $y_{\max} = d/2$ |
| 10 | Hollow Circular (OD $=D$, ID $=d$) | $I = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4}-d^{4})}{64}, \quad Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4}-d^{4})}{32D}$ | $y_{\max} = D/2$ |

---

### 2.4 Section Modulus — The Designer's Friend

The **section modulus $Z$** depends **only on geometry**, not on material or loading. It is the single most important property used by structural designers because:

- A **higher $Z$** means a **lower $\sigma_{\max}$** for the same moment.
- To resist a given $M$ without exceeding allowable stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$, you need $Z \geq \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$.

> [!TIP]
> **For the same cross-sectional area**, an **I-section** has a much higher $Z$ than a **rectangular section** — which is why all steel beams in KTU civil/structural design problems are I-sections.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Design of RCC beams, steel girders, lintels, slabs.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of machine shafts, leaf springs, crane hooks.
- **Aerospace:** Wing spar analysis, fuselage frames.
- **Automotive:** Chassis rails, leaf spring suspension, axles.
- **Marine:** Ship hull girders subjected to wave-induced bending moments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Flexure Formula (Pure Bending)

**Starting concept:** Consider a small element of length $\delta x$ cut from a beam subjected to a *constant* bending moment $M$ (pure bending, no shear).

**Step 1 — Geometry of deformation:**
Originally the element has flat faces $AB$ and $CD$. After bending, these faces subtend an angle $\delta\theta$ at the centre of curvature $O$.

$$\text{Original length of arc at N.A.} = R \cdot \delta\theta$$
$$\text{Original length of arc at distance } y \text{ from N.A.} = (R + y) \cdot \delta\theta$$

> The **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** is the fibre that undergoes *no change in length* — its length remains $R \cdot \delta\theta$.

**Step 2 — Strain at distance $y$:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{change in length}}{\text{original length}} = \frac{(R + y)\delta\theta - R\delta\theta}{R\delta\theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law:**
Since $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Force equilibrium (zero net axial force on the section):**
The total normal force on the cross-section is zero:
$$\int_A \sigma \cdot dA = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \int_A E \cdot \frac{y}{R} \, dA = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$
Since $E/R \neq 0$, this implies $\int_A y \, dA = 0$, which is the **mathematical definition of the Neutral Axis** (it passes through the *centroid* of the cross-section).

**Step 5 — Moment equilibrium:**
The moment of all elemental stresses about the N.A. must equal the applied bending moment $M$:
$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^{2} \, dA$$

The integral $\int_A y^{2} \, dA$ is the **Second Moment of Area** $I$ about the Neutral Axis.

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

**Step 6 — Eliminate $R$:**
From Step 3: $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E \cdot y}$. Substituting into Step 5:
$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{R} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}$$

Equating the two expressions for $\dfrac{E}{R}$:
$$\boxed{\;\frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **Triplet Flexure Equation** is one of the most tested identities in KTU Module 3 — commit it to memory.

**Step 7 — Stress at a fibre:**
$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \quad \text{(valid for any fibre at distance } y \text{ from N.A.)}$$

**Step 8 — Maximum bending stress (extreme fibre $y = y_{\max}$):**
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}, \quad \text{where } Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

---

### 3.2 Section Modulus — Analytical Matrix for Standard Cross-Sections

The table below shows the *complete* analytical derivation of $I$, $y_{\max}$, and $Z$ for the cross-sections that appear most often in KTU Module 3 questions.

| S.No. | Cross-Section | Sketch of $I$ computation | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rectangle** ($b \times h$) | Single block, N.A. through centroid, $y_{\max} = h/2$ | $I = \dfrac{bh^{3}}{12}, \quad Z = \dfrac{bh^{2}}{6}$ |
| 2 | **Solid Circle** (dia $d$) | Circular cross-section, $y_{\max} = d/2$ | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}, \quad Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$ |
| 3 | **Hollow Circle** (OD $D$, ID $d$) | Outer minus inner disc, $y_{\max} = D/2$ | $I = \dfrac{\pi(D^{4}-d^{4})}{64}, \quad Z = \dfrac{\pi(D^{4}-d^{4})}{32D}$ |
| 4 | **I-Section** | Flanges + web using parallel-axis theorem | Computed from flange + web components |
| 5 | **T-Section** | Flange (horizontal) + stem (vertical) | Centroid NOT at mid-depth — must be computed |
| 6 | **Channel Section** | Two flanges + web; centroid offset from web | Centroid lies outside the web |

> [!NOTE]
> For sections whose Neutral Axis is **not** at the geometric mid-depth (T, Channel, Unequal I), you **must** first locate the centroid $\bar{y}$ from a reference datum, then compute $I$ using the **Parallel Axis Theorem**: $I = I_{G} + A \cdot h^{2}$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Rectangular Section

**Problem:** A timber beam 120 mm wide × 240 mm deep carries a bending moment of $M = 12$ kN·m. Calculate the maximum bending stress and the bending stress at 60 mm from the neutral axis.

**Solution:**

Given: $b = 120$ mm $= 0.12$ m, $h = 240$ mm $= 0.24$ m, $M = 12 \times 10^{3}$ N·m.

**Step 1 — Moment of Inertia:**
$$I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.12 \times (0.24)^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.12 \times 0.013824}{12} = 1.3824 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{b h^{2}}{6} = \frac{0.12 \times (0.24)^{2}}{6} = \frac{0.12 \times 0.0576}{6} = 1.152 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress (at extreme fibre, $y = h/2 = 0.12$ m):**
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{12 \times 10^{3}}{1.152 \times 10^{-3}} = 10.42 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa} = 10.42 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Bending stress at $y = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m:**
$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{12 \times 10^{3} \times 0.06}{1.3824 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.21 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa} = 5.21 \; \text{MPa}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that stress at $y = 60$ mm is **exactly half** of $\sigma_{\max}$ — confirming the *linear* stress distribution (not parabolic). This is a frequent 2-mark KTU sub-question.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Solid Circular Section

**Problem:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $d = 150$ mm is subjected to a bending moment $M = 25$ kN·m. Find the maximum bending stress.

**Solution:**

Given: $d = 0.15$ m, $M = 25 \times 10^{3}$ N·m.

**Step 1 — Section Modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.15)^{3}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 3.375 \times 10^{-3}}{32} = 3.313 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum bending stress:**
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{25 \times 10^{3}}{3.313 \times 10^{-4}} = 75.45 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa} \approx 75.45 \; \text{MPa}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — I-Section (Composite Shape)

**Problem:** An I-section has flange width $B = 200$ mm, flange thickness $t_f = 20$ mm, web thickness $t_w = 20$ mm, and overall depth $D = 300$ mm. Find the section modulus.

**Solution:**

Given: $B = 0.2$ m, $t_f = 0.02$ m, $t_w = 0.02$ m, $D = 0.3$ m.

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry:** By symmetry, the N.A. lies at mid-depth, $\bar{y} = D/2 = 0.15$ m from the bottom or top.

**Step 2 — Compute $I$ about N.A. (using parallel-axis theorem):**

Treat the section as **two flanges + one web** (subtracting the web-flange overlap):

- **Flange (each):** Width $B = 0.2$ m, height $t_f = 0.02$ m. Distance of flange centroid from N.A.: $d = D/2 - t_f/2 = 0.15 - 0.01 = 0.14$ m.
$$I_{\text{flange}} = \frac{B \cdot t_f^{3}}{12} + B \cdot t_f \cdot d^{2} = \frac{0.2 \times (0.02)^{3}}{12} + 0.2 \times 0.02 \times (0.14)^{2}$$
$$I_{\text{flange}} = 1.333 \times 10^{-7} + 7.84 \times 10^{-4} = 7.841 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{4}$$

- **Two flanges:** $I_{\text{flanges}} = 2 \times 7.841 \times 10^{-4} = 1.5682 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{4}$.

- **Web:** Height $= D - 2 t_f = 0.3 - 0.04 = 0.26$ m, width $t_w = 0.02$ m. Centroid at N.A., so no parallel-axis shift:
$$I_{\text{web}} = \frac{t_w \cdot (D - 2t_f)^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.02 \times (0.26)^{3}}{12} = \frac{0.02 \times 0.017576}{12} = 2.929 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Total $I$:**
$$I = I_{\text{flanges}} + I_{\text{web}} = 1.5682 \times 10^{-3} + 2.929 \times 10^{-5} = 1.5975 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Section Modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{1.5975 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15} = 1.065 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}^{3} = 106.5 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick check for I-section:** The flanges carry ~98% of $I$ and the web only ~2%. So for KTU numericals, students may often be told to *neglect the $I$ of the web* for a quick estimate — but in a 14-mark problem, **do the full calculation**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Flexure Formula Information Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the logical flow from the **applied moment** to the **bending stress** at a given fibre, using the Flexure Formula:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Bending Moment M] --> B[Section Geometry]
    C[Cross-Section Shape] --> B
    D[Material Property E] --> E[Curvature 1/R]
    B --> F[Second Moment of Area I]
    B --> G[Distance from N.A. y]
    B --> H[Section Modulus Z = I divided by y_max]
    F --> I[Fiber Stress Calculation]
    G --> I
    A --> I
    H --> J[Maximum Stress sigma_max = M divided by Z]
    A --> J
    I --> K[Bending Stress Distribution Linear]
    K --> L[Zero at Neutral Axis]
    K --> M[Maximum at Extreme Fibres]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Solution Workflow for a Bending-Stress Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Cross-Section and Bending Moment] --> S2[Step 2: Locate Neutral Axis through Centroid]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Compute I about Neutral Axis]
    S3 --> S4{Is the Section Composite?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Apply Parallel Axis Theorem to Each Part]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Use Standard Formula for I]
    S5 --> S7[Step 4: Compute Z = I divided by y_max]
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Step 5: Compute sigma_max = M divided by Z]
    S8 --> S9[Step 6: Draw Linear Stress Distribution]
    S9 --> S10[Step 7: Verify Units and Sign Convention]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Stress Distribution by Cross-Section Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solid Rectangular Section] --> A1[Linear variation from zero at mid-depth to max at top and bottom]
    B[Solid Circular Section] --> B1[Linear variation in any diameter, zero at centre]
    C[I-Section] --> C1[Linear variation, max at top and bottom of flanges, zero at centroid]
    D[T-Section] --> D1[Linear variation, max at the farther extreme fibre from N.A.]
    E[Hollow Circular Section] --> E1[Linear variation, max at outer diameter, zero at centroid]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Symmetric vs Unsymmetric Sections (Crucial for KTU)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Cross-Section Type] --> R{Is the Section Symmetric about the Bending Axis?}
    R -- Yes --> R1[Neutral Axis coincides with the Axis of Symmetry]
    R -- No --> R2[Compute Centroid first, then locate Neutral Axis]
    R1 --> R3[Use standard y_max = D divided by 2 for y]
    R2 --> R4[Apply Parallel Axis Theorem]
    R4 --> R5[T-Section, Channel, Unequal I all fall here]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For T-sections and channel sections, the Neutral Axis is **NOT** at the mid-depth. Failure to compute the centroid correctly is the **most common mistake** in 14-mark I/T/Channel numericals.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define Section Modulus. What is its physical significance in beam design?** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** The *Section Modulus (Z)* of a beam cross-section is the ratio of its second moment of area $I$ about the neutral axis to the distance $y_{\max}$ of the extreme fibre from the neutral axis:
> $$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} \quad \text{(unit: m}^{3}\text{)}$$
>
> **Physical Significance:**
> - It is a purely **geometric property** (depends only on shape, not material or load).
> - It is the **direct measure of the load-carrying capacity** of a cross-section.
> - Maximum bending stress is given by $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$. Hence, for a fixed $M$, **higher $Z$ implies lower $\sigma$**.
> - In design, the required $Z$ to resist a given moment is $Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$.
> - A convenient comparison: a beam with $Z_2 = 2 Z_1$ carries **double the moment** for the same maximum stress. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. State the assumptions made in the theory of pure bending.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The theory of pure bending (Euler–Bernoulli) is based on the following assumptions:

1. The material of the beam is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**.
2. The beam is **initially straight** with a **uniform cross-section**.
3. The applied loads act in a plane containing the axis of the beam and the principal centroidal axis of the cross-section.
4. **Plane sections remain plane and normal** to the neutral axis both before and after bending.
5. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit (stresses are proportional to strains).
6. The beam undergoes **small deflections** so that the radius of curvature is large compared to the cross-sectional dimensions.
7. The beam is in a state of **pure bending** — shear force and torsional effects are neglected.
8. **Self-weight** of the beam is neglected unless specified. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ for a beam subjected to pure bending. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over its entire length. The cross-section of the beam is rectangular, 200 mm wide and 300 mm deep. Calculate the maximum bending stress in the beam. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(a):**

**Step 1 — Consider a small element of length $\delta x$** of a beam under pure bending $M$. Originally it has flat faces $AB$ and $CD$.

**Step 2 — After bending**, the faces subtend an angle $\delta \theta$ at the centre of curvature $O$. The N.A. is the fibre whose length remains unchanged: $L = R \cdot \delta\theta$.

**Step 3 — Strain at distance $y$:**
The arc length at distance $y$ is $(R+y)\delta\theta$. The change in length is $y \cdot \delta\theta$. Therefore:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{y \, \delta\theta}{R \, \delta\theta} = \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law:**
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Force equilibrium** ($\Sigma F_x = 0$): gives the **neutral axis condition** $\int_A y \, dA = 0$ (N.A. passes through the centroid).

**Step 6 — Moment equilibrium** ($\Sigma M = 0$):
$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^{2} \, dA = \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

**Step 7 — Final triplet equation:**
$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{E}{R} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$
$$\boxed{\;\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}\;}$$

This gives the stress at any fibre:
$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

**Assumptions** (write all 8 from Section 2.1). *(7 marks fully covered)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A(b):**

**Given:** Span $L = 4$ m, UDL $w = 10$ kN/m, $b = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m, $h = 300$ mm $= 0.3$ m.

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment** (for simply supported beam with UDL):
$$M_{\max} = \frac{w L^{2}}{8} = \frac{10 \times (4)^{2}}{8} = 20 \; \text{kN·m} = 20 \times 10^{3} \; \text{N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Section modulus** for rectangle:
$$Z = \frac{b h^{2}}{6} = \frac{0.2 \times (0.3)^{2}}{6} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.09}{6} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress:**
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{20 \times 10^{3}}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.667 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa} = 6.67 \; \text{MPa}$$

> **Answer:** $\sigma_{\max} \approx 6.67 \; \text{MPa}$ (compressive at top, tensile at bottom). *(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** For a circular cross-section of diameter $d$, derive the expression for section modulus $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of 200 mm and an inner diameter of 150 mm. It carries a bending moment of 60 kN·m. Determine the maximum bending stress induced in the shaft. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(a):**

**Step 1 — Consider a circular disc** of diameter $d$, radius $R = d/2$, with centroid at the geometric centre.

**Step 2 — By symmetry**, the N.A. coincides with the diameter through the centroid. The maximum distance from N.A. is:
$$y_{\max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Second Moment of Area of a circle** (standard result, derived by integration or known formula):
$$I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$$

> **Derivation sketch:** Consider a thin ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$. The elemental area is $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. Then $I = \int_0^{R} r^{2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \int_0^{R} r^{3} \, dr = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2} = \frac{\pi (d/2)^{4}}{2} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32 \times 2} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$.

**Step 4 — Section Modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{\pi d^{4}/64}{d/2} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} \times \frac{2}{d} = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$$
$$\boxed{\;Z = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{32}\;}$$

---

### **Model Solution — Question B(b):**

**Given:** $D = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m (outer dia), $d = 150$ mm $= 0.15$ m (inner dia), $M = 60$ kN·m $= 60 \times 10^{3}$ N·m.

**Step 1 — Second Moment of Area of hollow circle:**
$$I = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{64} = \frac{\pi \left[(0.2)^{4} - (0.15)^{4}\right]}{64}$$
$$= \frac{\pi \left[1.6 \times 10^{-3} - 5.0625 \times 10^{-4}\right]}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 1.0938 \times 10^{-3}}{64} = 5.367 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{\max}} = \frac{5.367 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1} = 5.367 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{3}$$

> Equivalently, using the direct formula: $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.0938 \times 10^{-3}}{32 \times 0.2} = 5.367 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{3}$ ✓ (same).

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress:**
$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{60 \times 10^{3}}{5.367 \times 10^{-4}} = 111.79 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Pa} \approx 111.8 \; \text{MPa}$$

> **Answer:** $\sigma_{\max} \approx 111.8 \; \text{MPa}$. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m is the *#1* cause of wrong numerical answers. Always convert all dimensions to **metres** before substituting into SI formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting the Parallel Axis Theorem:** For I, T, and Channel sections, students often use $I = \dfrac{bh^{3}}{12}$ alone (which is for a single rectangle). The correct method splits the section into rectangles and applies $\sum (I_G + A h^{2})$.
> 3. **Sign Convention Errors:** Not stating whether stress is tensile or compressive loses 1 mark. Always specify the fibre (top/bottom/outer/inner) and the nature of stress.
> 4. **No Sketch of Stress Distribution:** A linear stress-distribution diagram is worth **1–2 marks** as a free-hand sketch in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Confusing $I$ and $Z$:** $I$ depends on the axis; $Z$ is a single number for a given cross-section. Mixing them up gives wrong answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final high-density revision points for KTU Module 3 — Bending Stresses in Beams:**

- ✅ The **Flexure Formula** is: $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$, valid only under the 8 assumptions of pure bending.
- ✅ The **Triplet Flexure Equation** is the all-in-one form: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.
- ✅ **Section Modulus** $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ gives **maximum** stress: $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$.
- ✅ **Standard $Z$ values to memorise:**
  - Rectangle ($b \times h$): $Z = \dfrac{bh^{2}}{6}$
  - Solid Circle (dia $d$): $Z = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{32}$
  - Hollow Circle (OD $D$, ID $d$): $Z = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D}$
- ✅ **Bending stress is zero** at the **Neutral Axis**, and **maximum** at the **extreme fibres**.
- ✅ **Stress varies linearly** with $y$ — *not* parabolically. This linear variation must be shown in a free-hand diagram.
- ✅ For **composite sections (I, T, Channel)**, first locate the **centroid**; then use the **Parallel Axis Theorem** to compute $I$; then divide by $y_{\max}$ to get $Z$.
- ✅ **Sagging moment** = compression on top, tension on bottom. **Hogging moment** = reverse.
- ✅ **Always use SI units** — convert mm to m before substituting. Failure to do so is the most common error in KTU numericals.
- ✅ The **curvature relation** $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ is the *stiffness* counterpart of the *strength* relation $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$.
- ✅ **Same area, more $Z$:** I-section is the most efficient cross-section for resisting bending — this is why all structural steel beams are I-sections.
- ✅ A **symmetric cross-section** (rectangle, circle, symmetric I) has the N.A. at the geometric centre. **Unsymmetric sections** (T, Channel, unequal I) require centroid calculation first.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strength of a Section
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Strength of a Section

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's **Strength of Materials** framework (Module 3), the **Strength of a Section** is defined as the **maximum internal bending (flexural) stress** that a cross-section of a structural member (typically a beam) can safely resist before the material reaches its allowable stress limit in either tension or compression.

Mathematically, it is quantified using two interlinked engineering parameters:

$$\boxed{\text{Strength of a Section} \;\equiv\; \sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{M}{Z} \;=\; \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I}}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Bending Moment acting on the section (N·m or kN·m)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Moment of Inertia) about the Neutral Axis (m⁴ or mm⁴)
- $y_{\max}$ = Maximum distance of the extreme fibre from the Neutral Axis (m or mm)
- $Z$ = **Section Modulus** (m³ or mm³)
- $\sigma_{\max}$ = Maximum Bending Stress (N/m² or N/mm²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *The "strength" of a beam cross-section is its capacity to resist bending without exceeding the permissible stress. It is NOT the load it can carry, but rather the internal moment-resisting capacity of the cross-section governed by its geometry (shape and dimensions) and the material's allowable stress.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iron Bar Bridge"

Imagine you are bending a thin iron rod with your hands. A solid square rod and a hollow pipe of the **same weight (same cross-sectional area)** feel completely different in your hands:

- The **solid square rod** feels "stiff but weak" — it bends with effort.
- The **hollow pipe** feels "easy to lift" but surprisingly **harder to bend** when you push from the ends.

Why? Because in bending, it is not the *amount of material* that matters — it is **how far that material is placed from the neutral (central) axis**. The hollow pipe has most of its material at the *outer edges*, exactly where bending stress is highest. The square rod has its material crowded near the *centre*, where stress is low.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Insight (KTU High-Yield Concept):**
> *Strength of a section depends on the distribution of material, NOT the total amount of material. This is why an I-beam, with most of its steel at the flanges (top and bottom), is far stronger than a rectangular bar of the same weight.*

---

## 1.3 Key Definitions (Mandatory KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition | Symbol/Unit |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Neutral Axis (NA)** | The line in the cross-section where bending stress is **zero** (fibres neither elongate nor compress) | — |
| **Section Modulus** | A geometric property indicating the efficiency of a cross-section in resisting bending | $Z$ (m³ or mm³) |
| **Moment of Resistance (MR)** | The internal resisting moment offered by the section against the applied bending moment | $M_R$ (N·m) |
| **Bending Stress** | The longitudinal stress (tensile or compressive) induced due to bending | $\sigma_b$ (N/m²) |
| **Extreme Fibre** | The fibre of the cross-section farthest from the Neutral Axis (carries maximum stress) | $y_{\max}$ |

---

## 1.4 Governing Design Philosophy

For a beam to be **"safe in bending"**, the following fundamental inequality must be satisfied at every cross-section:

$$\sigma_{\text{induced}} \;=\; \frac{M}{Z} \;\;\leq\;\; \sigma_{\text{permissible}}$$

Where $\sigma_{\text{permissible}}$ is the **allowable/working stress** of the material (typically a fraction of the ultimate/yield stress, divided by a Factor of Safety as per IS 800:2007 for steel or IS 456:2000 for concrete).

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Mnemonic:** *"M over Z is the battle, Sigma-permissible sets the rattle"* — if $M/Z$ exceeds the allowable stress, the section **fails**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bending Equation — The Heart of Section Strength

The entire theory of section strength is rooted in the **Euler-Bernoulli Bending Equation**:

$$\frac{M}{I} \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{y} \;=\; \frac{E}{R}$$

This single equation links **internal moment (M)**, **geometric property (I)**, **induced stress (σ)**, **fibre distance (y)**, and **material stiffness (E)**, along with the curvature (R) of the deflected beam.

### 2.1.1 Component-Wise Breakdown

| Component | Physical Meaning | Engineering Use |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|
| $\dfrac{M}{I}$ | Bending moment per unit moment of inertia | Determines the "curvature tendency" |
| $\dfrac{\sigma}{y}$ | Stress per unit fibre distance | Determines the linear stress variation |
| $\dfrac{E}{R}$ | Stiffness divided by radius of curvature | Governs the deflection shape |
| $M = \sigma \cdot Z$ | Internal moment = stress × section modulus | The **Strength Equation** |
| $M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}$ | Bending stiffness | The **Stiffness Equation** |

---

## 2.2 Section Modulus (Z) — The "Strength Index"

The **Section Modulus** is a purely geometric quantity that quantifies the bending efficiency of a cross-section:

$$\boxed{Z \;=\; \frac{I}{y_{\max}} \;\;\;\; \text{(units: m}^3 \text{ or mm}^3\text{)}}$$

A section with a **larger Z** is stronger (for the same material and allowable stress). It can resist a larger bending moment:

$$M_{\text{resisting}} \;=\; \sigma_{\text{allowable}} \cdot Z$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always state the units of Section Modulus as **mm³ or m³**, NOT mm⁴. The 4th-power of length is for Moment of Inertia; Section Modulus is a 3rd-power quantity.

---

## 2.3 Master Formula Sheet (KTU 2024 — Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Section Properties** — These formulas are *guaranteed* to appear in KTU Board Examinations. Memorize them with their units.

### Table 2.3.A — Standard Solid Sections

| Section Shape | Area (A) | Moment of Inertia (I) | $y_{\max}$ | Section Modulus (Z) |
|---------------|----------|----------------------|------------|---------------------|
| Rectangle (b × d) | $b \cdot d$ | $\dfrac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{d}{2}$ | $\dfrac{b \cdot d^2}{6}$ |
| Solid Circle (diameter D) | $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^3}{32}$ |
| Solid Triangle (base b, height h) | $\dfrac{1}{2} b h$ | $\dfrac{b h^3}{36}$ | $\dfrac{2h}{3}$ | $\dfrac{b h^2}{24}$ |
| Square (a × a) | $a^2$ | $\dfrac{a^4}{12}$ | $\dfrac{a}{2}$ | $\dfrac{a^3}{6}$ |

### Table 2.3.B — Standard Built-Up / Standard Sections

| Section Shape | Moment of Inertia (I) | $y_{\max}$ | Section Modulus (Z) |
|---------------|----------------------|------------|---------------------|
| Hollow Rectangle (outer B, D; inner b, d) | $\dfrac{BD^3 - b d^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{BD^3 - b d^3}{6D}$ |
| Hollow Circle (outer D, inner d) | $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{64}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32 D}$ |
| I-Section (overall D, flange B, thickness $t_f$, web $t_w$) | $\dfrac{BD^3}{12} - \dfrac{(B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ | $\dfrac{I}{D/2}$ |
| T-Section (flange B, depth D, web $t_w$, thickness $t_f$) | Computed via Parallel Axis Theorem | Depends on NA | $\dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ |

### Table 2.3.C — Core Strength Equations

| Equation | Description | When Used |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress at distance y from NA | Finding stress at any fibre |
| $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress | Strength check / design |
| $M_R = \sigma_{\text{allow}} \cdot Z$ | Moment of Resistance (safe capacity) | Comparing capacities |
| $Z_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$ | Section modulus needed for design | **Design Problem (most common in KTU)** |
| $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Curvature-bending relation | Deflection / stiffness analysis |

---

## 2.4 Strength Comparison of Sections (Same Area Criterion)

This is a **favourite 14-mark KTU question**: *"Compare the strengths of two sections of the same cross-sectional area."*

The governing logic:

$$\text{Strength Ratio} \;=\; \frac{Z_1}{Z_2} \;=\; \frac{I_1 / y_{1,\max}}{I_2 / y_{2,\max}}$$

### Key Comparative Results (Must Memorize)

1. **Hollow vs Solid Circular Shaft (same area):**
   $$\frac{Z_{\text{hollow}}}{Z_{\text{solid}}} \;=\; \frac{1 + k^2}{\sqrt{1 - k^4}}$$
   where $k = d/D$ (inner to outer diameter ratio)

2. **Hollow vs Solid Rectangular Section (same area):**
   $$\frac{Z_{\text{hollow}}}{Z_{\text{solid}}} \;=\; \frac{1 - k^4}{1 - k^3}$$
   where $k = d/D$ (assuming same width reduction)

3. **I-Section vs Rectangular Section of same weight:**
   The I-section is **3 to 6 times stronger** depending on proportions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel Structures:** I-beams (ISMB sections) are used because their high $Z$ per kg of steel maximises strength while minimising self-weight.
- **Reinforced Concrete Beams:** The steel reinforcement is deliberately placed at the extreme fibres (top and bottom) to maximise the lever arm and equivalent section modulus.
- **Aerospace & Automotive:** Hollow circular tubes are preferred because they provide maximum $Z$ per unit weight, dramatically increasing the strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Mechanical Shafts:** Hollow shafts transmit more torque/power than solid shafts of the same weight, due to higher polar section modulus.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The strength of a section is the **geometric enabler** that allows architects and structural engineers to span larger distances (e.g., 50 m bridge girders) using far less material than naive intuition would suggest.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pure Bending Equation

**Assumptions of Pure Bending** (Euler-Bernoulli Theory):
1. The beam material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic (obeys Hooke's Law).
2. Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
3. The radius of curvature is large compared to beam dimensions (small deflections).
4. Self-weight is neglected; only bending effects are considered.

### 3.1.1 Geometry of Deformation

Consider a small element of length $dx$ on the beam. Before bending, it is a straight segment. After bending, it subtends an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature $O$, with radius of curvature $R$.

The original length of the element is $dx$. For a fibre at distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis (NA), its length after bending becomes:

$$L' \;=\; (R + y) \cdot d\theta$$

### 3.1.2 Longitudinal Strain in the Fibre

The change in length is:

$$\Delta L \;=\; L' - L \;=\; (R + y) d\theta - R \, d\theta \;=\; y \, d\theta$$

The original length is $L = R \, d\theta$, so the longitudinal strain is:

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{\Delta L}{L} \;=\; \frac{y \, d\theta}{R \, d\theta} \;=\; \frac{y}{R}$$

This is the **kinematic (geometry) relation**:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{y}{R}}$$

### 3.1.3 Stress from Hooke's Law

Since the material is linearly elastic:

$$\sigma \;=\; E \cdot \varepsilon \;=\; \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

This is the **constitutive (material) relation**:

$$\boxed{\sigma \;=\; \frac{E \cdot y}{R}}$$

### 3.1.4 Equilibrium — Internal Moment Equation

The internal bending moment at the section equals the sum of moments of all elemental stresses about the Neutral Axis:

$$M \;=\; \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA \;=\; \int_A \left( \frac{E \cdot y}{R} \right) y \, dA$$

Since $E$ and $R$ are constants for a given section:

$$M \;=\; \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, the **Second Moment of Area** is:

$$I \;=\; \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

Therefore:

$$M \;=\; \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} \;=\; \frac{E}{R}}$$

### 3.1.5 Combining All Three Relations

Combining the geometric, constitutive, and equilibrium relations:

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{I} \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{y} \;=\; \frac{E}{R}}$$

This is the **General Bending Equation** — the foundation of all strength-of-section analysis.

### 3.1.6 The Strength Equation — Special Case at Extreme Fibre

At the extreme fibre, $y = y_{\max}$ and $\sigma = \sigma_{\max}$:

$$\frac{M}{I} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{y_{\max}}$$

Solving for $\sigma_{\max}$:

$$\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{M \cdot y_{\max}}{I} \;=\; \frac{M}{Z}$$

where $Z = I / y_{\max}$ is the **Section Modulus**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Section Modulus for a Rectangular Section

For a rectangle of width $b$ and depth $d$ with the neutral axis passing through the centroid:

### Step 1: Moment of Inertia about NA

$$I \;=\; \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$$

### Step 2: Distance to extreme fibre

$$y_{\max} \;=\; \frac{d}{2}$$

### Step 3: Section Modulus

$$Z \;=\; \frac{I}{y_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{b \cdot d^3 / 12}{d / 2} \;=\; \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{d} \;=\; \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{\text{rectangle}} \;=\; \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Section Modulus for a Solid Circular Section

### Step 1: Moment of Inertia of solid circle (diameter D)

$$I \;=\; \frac{\pi D^4}{64}$$

### Step 2: Distance to extreme fibre

$$y_{\max} \;=\; \frac{D}{2}$$

### Step 3: Section Modulus

$$Z \;=\; \frac{I}{y_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^4 / 64}{D / 2} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^4}{64} \cdot \frac{2}{D} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^3}{32}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{\text{solid circle}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^3}{32}}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style

> **Problem Statement (Model KTU 2-Mark Variation):**
> *A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m. Find the required section modulus if the permissible bending stress is 165 N/mm².*

### Solution

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Moment**

$$M_{\max} \;=\; \frac{w L^2}{8} \;=\; \frac{20 \times 4^2}{8} \;=\; \frac{20 \times 16}{8} \;=\; 40 \; \text{kN·m}$$

Convert to N·mm:

$$M_{\max} \;=\; 40 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Required Section Modulus**

Using the Strength Equation $M = \sigma \cdot Z$:

$$Z_{\text{required}} \;=\; \frac{M_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{permiss}}} \;=\; \frac{40 \times 10^6}{165} \;=\; 242{,}424.24 \; \text{mm}^3$$

$$Z_{\text{required}} \;\approx\; 242.42 \times 10^3 \; \text{mm}^3 \;\approx\; 242.42 \; \text{cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{Z_{\text{required}} \;=\; 2.424 \times 10^5 \; \text{mm}^3}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Strength Comparison (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> **Problem:** *Two beams of the same material and length are subjected to the same bending moment. The first beam has a solid circular cross-section of diameter 100 mm. The second beam has a hollow circular cross-section with outer diameter 120 mm and inner diameter 80 mm. Compare their strengths.*

### Solution

**Section A: Solid Circle**

Diameter $D = 100$ mm:

$$Z_A \;=\; \frac{\pi D^3}{32} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times (100)^3}{32} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 10^6}{32}$$

$$Z_A \;=\; 98{,}174.77 \; \text{mm}^3 \;\approx\; 9.817 \times 10^4 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Section B: Hollow Circle**

Outer diameter $D = 120$ mm, Inner diameter $d = 80$ mm:

$$Z_B \;=\; \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32 D}$$

Calculate $D^4$ and $d^4$:

$$D^4 \;=\; (120)^4 \;=\; 207{,}360{,}000 \; \text{mm}^4 \;=\; 2.0736 \times 10^8$$

$$d^4 \;=\; (80)^4 \;=\; 40{,}960{,}000 \; \text{mm}^4 \;=\; 4.096 \times 10^7$$

$$D^4 - d^4 \;=\; 207.36 \times 10^6 - 40.96 \times 10^6 \;=\; 166.4 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^4$$

$$Z_B \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 166.4 \times 10^6}{32 \times 120} \;=\; \frac{522.66 \times 10^6}{3840}$$

$$Z_B \;=\; 136{,}109.4 \; \text{mm}^3 \;\approx\; 1.361 \times 10^5 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Comparison — Strength Ratio**

$$\frac{Z_B}{Z_A} \;=\; \frac{136{,}109.4}{98{,}174.77} \;=\; 1.386$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\text{Strength of Hollow}}{\text{Strength of Solid}} \;=\; 1.386 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Hollow is 38.6\% stronger}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Despite using **less material**, the hollow circular beam is **38.6% stronger** in bending than the solid beam of the same length, under the same material constraints. This demonstrates the power of efficient cross-section design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flexural Strength Analysis Pipeline

This diagram maps the logical workflow of solving any "Strength of a Section" problem in KTU Board examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Identify Beam Type and Loading] --> stepB[Compute Maximum Bending Moment M]
    stepB --> stepC[Identify Material Property Sigma Allowable]
    stepC --> stepD[Determine Cross Section Geometry]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute Second Moment of Area I]
    stepE --> stepF[Identify Extreme Fibre Distance Ymax]
    stepF --> stepG[Compute Section Modulus Z equals I by Ymax]
    stepG --> stepH{Check Required Mode}
    stepH --> stepI[Strength Check: Sigma max equals M by Z]
    stepH --> stepJ[Design Mode: Z required equals M by Sigma allow]
    stepH --> stepK[Comparison Mode: Ratio of Z1 to Z2]
    stepI --> stepL[Verify Sigma max less than Sigma permissible]
    stepJ --> stepM[Select Standard Section with Z greater than Z required]
    stepK --> stepN[Conclude Strength Ratio and Comment]
    stepL --> stepO[Output SAFE or UNSAFE verdict]
    stepM --> stepO
    stepN --> stepO
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Conceptual Map — Bending Equation Interconnections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Applied Load w or P] --> nodeB[Bending Moment M at critical section]
    nodeB --> nodeC[M over I equals Sigma over Y equals E over R]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Geometric Side: epsilon equals Y over R]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Material Side: Sigma equals E times epsilon]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Equilibrium Side: M equals integral of Sigma y dA]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Pure Geometry of Deformed Beam]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Hookes Law Linear Elasticity]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Statically Equivalent Moment]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Curvature K equals 1 over R]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Stress Distribution Linear over Depth]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Section Modulus Z equals I over Ymax]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Strength Equation Sigma max equals M over Z]
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture Diagram — Cross-Section Strength Ranking

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rankA[Cross Sections Ranked by Section Modulus Z per unit weight] --> rankB[Top Tier: I Sections and Hollow Tubes]
    rankB --> rankC[Mid Tier: Wide Flange Beams and Box Sections]
    rankC --> rankD[Lower Tier: Solid Rectangular Bars]
    rankD --> rankE[Lowest Tier: Solid Round Bars of small diameter]
    rankA --> rankF[Key Principle: Material far from NA contributes most to Z]
    rankF --> rankG[Design Implication: Distribute material at extreme fibres]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Stress Distribution in a Rectangular Beam

For a rectangular cross-section beam under pure positive bending (sagging), the stress distribution is **linear across the depth**, with the following textbook features:

- **Top Fibre (y = +d/2):** Maximum compressive stress $\sigma_c = M y / I$
- **Neutral Axis (y = 0):** Zero stress
- **Bottom Fibre (y = -d/2):** Maximum tensile stress $\sigma_t = M y / I$
- **Stress varies linearly** (triangular distribution) because $\sigma = (M/I) \cdot y$

This linearity is **fundamental** and is derived directly from the assumption of plane sections remaining plane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note:** Since KTU question papers are black-and-white, always label stress diagrams clearly: "C" for compression at top, "T" for tension at bottom, and "N.A." with a horizontal line at mid-depth.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
