# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
> *Define the term "Section Modulus" and state its physical significance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Section Modulus is defined as the ratio of the Second Moment of Area (I) to the distance of the extreme fibre ($y_{\max}$) from the Neutral Axis.

$$Z \;=\; \frac{I}{y_{\max}}$$

**Units (1 Mark):** It is expressed in mm³ or m³ (cubic units of length).

**Physical Significance (1 Mark):** Section modulus is a **purely geometric property** that quantifies the bending efficiency of a cross-section. It directly indicates the **moment-resisting capacity** of the section for a given material: $M = \sigma \cdot Z$. A larger Z means the section can resist a higher bending moment without exceeding the allowable stress.

---

### Question A.2
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> *A beam of rectangular cross-section 80 mm wide and 120 mm deep is subjected to a bending moment of 12 kN·m. Find the maximum bending stress induced in the beam.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Step 1: Section Modulus (1 Mark)**

$$Z \;=\; \frac{b d^2}{6} \;=\; \frac{80 \times (120)^2}{6} \;=\; \frac{80 \times 14400}{6} \;=\; 192{,}000 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 2: Convert Moment to N·mm (1 Mark)**

$$M \;=\; 12 \; \text{kN·m} \;=\; 12 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Stress (1 Mark)**

$$\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{M}{Z} \;=\; \frac{12 \times 10^6}{192{,}000} \;=\; 62.5 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \;=\; 62.5 \; \text{MPa}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B.A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Pattern based on Dec 2024 Syllabus]**
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *Derive the bending equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$, stating the assumptions clearly.*
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *A cast iron beam of I-section has the following dimensions:*
> - *Overall depth = 200 mm*
> - *Flange width = 150 mm*
> - *Flange thickness = 25 mm*
> - *Web thickness = 20 mm*
>
> *If the permissible stresses in tension and compression are 30 N/mm² and 90 N/mm² respectively, determine the moment of resistance of the beam. Take the NA as the centroidal axis.*

---

### Model Solution — Question B.A

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Bending Equation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law.
2. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to NA after bending.
3. Radius of curvature is large; deflections are small.
4. Self-weight is neglected.

**Derivation Steps (6 Marks):**

**Step 1: Strain at distance y from NA (Geometric relation)**

Consider an element of original length $dx$. After bending, it subtends angle $d\theta$ at radius $R$. The fibre at distance $y$ has new length $(R+y) d\theta$. Original length is $R d\theta$.

$$\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{\Delta L}{L} \;=\; \frac{(R+y)d\theta - R d\theta}{R d\theta} \;=\; \frac{y}{R}$$

**Step 2: Stress from Hooke's Law (Material relation)**

$$\sigma \;=\; E \cdot \varepsilon \;=\; \frac{E \cdot y}{R}$$

**Step 3: Moment from Equilibrium (Static relation)**

$$M \;=\; \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA \;=\; \frac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA \;=\; \frac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{M}{I} \;=\; \frac{E}{R}$$

Combining with Step 2:

$$\frac{M}{I} \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{y} \;=\; \frac{E}{R} \;\;\;\; \blacksquare$$

---

#### Part (b) — Moment of Resistance of I-Section (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Calculate Moment of Inertia (3 Marks)**

Using standard I-section formula:

$$I \;=\; \frac{B D^3}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w)(D - 2t_f)^3}{12}$$

Substituting: $B = 150$ mm, $D = 200$ mm, $t_w = 20$ mm, $t_f = 25$ mm:

$$\text{Term 1} \;=\; \frac{150 \times 200^3}{12} \;=\; \frac{150 \times 8 \times 10^6}{12} \;=\; 100 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^4$$

Inner rectangle dimensions: width $= B - t_w = 150 - 20 = 130$ mm; depth $= D - 2t_f = 200 - 50 = 150$ mm.

$$\text{Term 2} \;=\; \frac{130 \times 150^3}{12} \;=\; \frac{130 \times 3.375 \times 10^6}{12} \;=\; 36.5625 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^4$$

$$I \;=\; 100 \times 10^6 - 36.5625 \times 10^6 \;=\; 63.4375 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^4$$

$$I \;=\; 6.344 \times 10^7 \; \text{mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Section Modulus (1 Mark)**

Since NA is at mid-depth:

$$y_{\max} \;=\; \frac{D}{2} \;=\; \frac{200}{2} \;=\; 100 \; \text{mm}$$

$$Z \;=\; \frac{I}{y_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{63.4375 \times 10^6}{100} \;=\; 6.344 \times 10^5 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 3: Moment of Resistance (3 Marks)**

For unsymmetric allowable stress (different $\sigma_t$ and $\sigma_c$), the NA coincides with the centroid only if the section is **symmetric**. For a symmetric I-section:

$$M_R \;=\; \sigma_t \cdot Z_t \;=\; \sigma_c \cdot Z_c$$

Since $Z_t = Z_c = Z$ (symmetric section), take the **smaller** permissible stress for safety:

$$\sigma_{\text{governing}} \;=\; \min(\sigma_t, \sigma_c) \;=\; 30 \; \text{N/mm}^2 \;\;\text{(tension governs for cast iron)}$$

$$M_R \;=\; 30 \times 6.344 \times 10^5 \;=\; 1.903 \times 10^7 \; \text{N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_R \;=\; 19.03 \; \text{kN·m}}$$

---

### Question B.B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Pattern]**
>
> **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** *A rectangular beam 120 mm wide and 240 mm deep is subjected to a bending moment of 50 kN·m. Determine:*
> - *(i) The maximum bending stress in the beam*
> - *(ii) The bending stress at a fibre 40 mm from the top of the beam*
>
> **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** *Compare the strengths of a solid circular column of 100 mm diameter and a hollow circular column of 80 mm internal diameter and 120 mm external diameter, both made of the same material and subjected to the same allowable stress.*

---

### Model Solution — Question B.B

#### Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Section Modulus (2 Marks)**

$$Z \;=\; \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6} \;=\; \frac{120 \times 240^2}{6} \;=\; \frac{120 \times 57600}{6} \;=\; 1{,}152{,}000 \; \text{mm}^3$$

$$Z \;=\; 1.152 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Step 2: Moment in N·mm (1 Mark)**

$$M \;=\; 50 \times 10^6 \; \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3: Maximum bending stress (2 Marks)**

$$\sigma_{\max} \;=\; \frac{M}{Z} \;=\; \frac{50 \times 10^6}{1.152 \times 10^6} \;=\; 43.40 \; \text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{\max} \;=\; 43.4 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Step 4: Bending stress at 40 mm from top (2 Marks)**

The distance from NA = $\frac{d}{2} - 40 = 120 - 40 = 80$ mm.

$$\sigma_y \;=\; \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \;=\; \frac{M}{I/y} \;=\; \frac{M}{Z} \cdot \frac{y}{y_{\max}}$$

$$\sigma_y \;=\; 43.40 \times \frac{80}{120} \;=\; 28.93 \; \text{N/mm}^2 \;\;\text{(compressive, near top)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 43.4 \; \text{MPa}, \quad \sigma_{y=80} = 28.93 \; \text{MPa}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Strength Comparison (7 Marks)

**Solid Circle (D = 100 mm):**

$$Z_{\text{solid}} \;=\; \frac{\pi D^3}{32} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 100^3}{32} \;=\; 98{,}175 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Hollow Circle (D = 120 mm, d = 80 mm):**

$$Z_{\text{hollow}} \;=\; \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32 D}$$

$$D^4 = 120^4 = 207.36 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^4$$

$$d^4 = 80^4 = 40.96 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^4$$

$$D^4 - d^4 = 166.4 \times 10^6 \; \text{mm}^4$$

$$Z_{\text{hollow}} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 166.4 \times 10^6}{32 \times 120} \;=\; \frac{522.66 \times 10^6}{3840} \;=\; 136{,}109 \; \text{mm}^3$$

**Strength Ratio:**

$$\frac{Z_{\text{hollow}}}{Z_{\text{solid}}} \;=\; \frac{136{,}109}{98{,}175} \;=\; 1.386$$

**Cross-sectional area comparison (for comment):**

$$A_{\text{solid}} \;=\; \frac{\pi \times 100^2}{4} \;=\; 7854 \; \text{mm}^2$$

$$A_{\text{hollow}} \;=\; \frac{\pi (120^2 - 80^2)}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi (14400 - 6400)}{4} \;=\; 6283 \; \text{mm}^2$$

$$\text{Area ratio} \;=\; \frac{6283}{7854} \;=\; 0.80 \;\; (\text{20\% less material})$$

$$\boxed{\text{Hollow is 38.6\% stronger while using 20\% less material}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (Loses 1–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert kN·m to N·mm before computing $Z$. Always write: $M (\text{kN·m}) \times 10^6 = M (\text{N·mm})$.
>
> 2. **Confusing $Z$ with $I$ (Loses 1 mark):** Section Modulus $Z$ has units of **mm³** (3rd power). Moment of Inertia $I$ has units of **mm⁴** (4th power). Examiners verify this in the very first line.
>
> 3. **NA Confusion in Unsymmetric Sections (Loses 2–3 marks):** For T-section or unequal I-section, the NA is **NOT** at the mid-depth. You must compute the centroid first, then $y_{\max}$ is measured from the centroid (NOT from the geometric center).
>
> 4. **Omitting the Bending Equation Derivation Assumptions (Loses 1 mark):** The assumptions (Hooke's Law, plane sections, small deflections) carry dedicated marks in KTU paper valuation.
>
> 5. **Wrong Selection of Allowable Stress:** For **cast iron** (weaker in tension), $\sigma_t$ governs. For **mild steel**, both are typically taken equal. Always state which one governs.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety in design questions:** KTU design problems frequently specify $\sigma_{\text{working}}$, not $\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}$. Do not mix them up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final High-Density Revision Sheet — Strength of a Section (Module 3)**

### Core Conceptual Points
- Strength of a section depends on **section modulus (Z)**, not on cross-sectional area alone.
- Section Modulus $Z = I / y_{\max}$ is the **geometric strength index** of a cross-section.
- The **Bending Equation** $M/I = \sigma/y = E/R$ unifies geometry, material, and equilibrium.
- A section fails in bending when $\sigma_{\max} = M/Z \geq \sigma_{\text{permissible}}$.

### Must-Memorize Standard Formulas
- Rectangle: $Z = bd^2/6$
- Solid Circle: $Z = \pi D^3/32$
- Hollow Circle: $Z = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/(32D)$
- Hollow Rectangle: $Z = (BD^3 - bd^3)/(6D)$
- I-Section: Use $I = BD^3/12 - (B-t_w)(D-2t_f)^3/12$, then $Z = I/(D/2)$

### Key Design Inequalities
- **Strength Check:** $\dfrac{M}{Z} \leq \sigma_{\text{allow}}$
- **Design (find dimensions):** $Z_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$
- **Stiffness (separate):** $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{E}{R}$

### Strategic Engineering Insights
- Material placed **farther** from the NA contributes **more** to strength.
- Hollow sections are **stronger** than solid sections of equal weight.
- I-sections are preferred over rectangular sections due to high Z/weight ratio.
- For unsymmetric permissible stress (cast iron), the **weaker value** governs design.

### Quick Conversion Table
| Quantity | Conversion |
|----------|-----------|
| 1 kN·m | $10^6$ N·mm |
| 1 N/mm² | 1 MPa |
| 1 cm³ | $10^3$ mm³ |
| 1 m³ | $10^9$ mm³ |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Strategy:** In the 14-mark design question, write down the strength equation $M = \sigma Z$ first, then compute $Z_{\text{required}}$, and only then select a standard section from IS handbook. This 3-step structure fetches full marks every time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Stress in Beams - Derivation of the Equation for Shear Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shear Stress in Beams - Derivation of the Equation for Shear Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shear Stress in Beams** refers to the intensity of internal tangential (shearing) force distributed across the cross-section of a beam subjected to transverse loading. While bending stress (flexural stress) acts **normal** to the cross-section, shear stress acts **parallel** to the cross-section along its plane.

For a beam subjected to a transverse shear force $V$, the shear stress $\tau$ at any fiber located at a vertical distance $y$ from the **Neutral Axis (N.A.)** is given by the classical **Shear Stress Formula (also called Zhuravskii's Formula or Jourawski's Equation)**:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress intensity at the fiber (N/mm² or MPa)
- $V$ = Transverse shear force acting at the section (N)
- $Q$ = First Moment of Area of the portion of the cross-section lying **beyond** the fiber (about the N.A.) = $A' \cdot \bar{y}$ (mm³)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area of the entire cross-section about the N.A. (mm⁴)
- $b$ = Width of the section at the fiber level (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The derivation assumes a beam of *uniform cross-section* loaded in its *plane of symmetry*, with stresses within the *elastic limit* and considering *complementary shear* (equal horizontal and vertical shear stresses on orthogonal planes).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stack of Books" Intuition

Imagine a thick textbook lying flat on a table. Now push the top cover horizontally while holding the bottom cover fixed.

What happens? The pages **slide** over one another. The top page moves more than the middle pages, which move more than the bottom page.

This is exactly what happens inside a loaded beam:
- The **upper fibers** experience one horizontal force direction,
- The **lower fibers** experience the opposite direction,
- The **neutral axis** fibers experience **zero longitudinal change**,
- The horizontal sliding between adjacent layers creates **horizontal shear stress**.

If this horizontal sliding is non-uniform across the depth, the layers will also tend to slide **vertically** (vertical shear), which is what causes shear failure in short, deep beams (like in reinforced concrete deep beams).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight:** Shear stress in beams is **zero at the top and bottom surfaces** (free surfaces cannot resist shear), and **maximum at the Neutral Axis** for symmetrical sections. This is why short, deep beams tend to fail along a diagonal starting near the neutral axis — known as **shear failure**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of the Derivation

The shear stress equation $\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$ is derived under the following **assumptions** (commonly asked as 3-mark questions in KTU exams):

1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** (linear elastic).
2. The cross-section of the beam is **uniform** (prismatic) along its length.
3. Loading is applied in the **plane of symmetry** of the cross-section, so bending occurs without twisting.
4. Plane sections remain **plane and normal to the neutral axis** *before* loading (Bernoulli-Euler hypothesis) — but the derivation of horizontal shear requires considering that these sections **warp** slightly.
5. Stresses are within the **elastic limit** of the material.
6. The complementary shear stress acting on a horizontal plane equals the transverse shear stress on the vertical plane (**Principle of Complementary Shear / Conjugate Shear Stress Theorem**).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Meaning / Use** | **Units** |
|:------------|:------------------|:----------|
| $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$ | Shear stress at a fiber at distance $y$ from N.A. | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}$ | First moment of area beyond the fiber about N.A. | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{\max}}{I \cdot b_{\min}}$ | Maximum shear stress (for sections with non-uniform width) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A}$ (Rectangular section) | Max. shear stress for rectangular section = $1.5 \times$ avg. | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A}$ (Circular section) | Max. shear stress for solid circular section = $1.333 \times$ avg. | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{2V}{A}$ (Triangular section) | Max. shear stress for triangular section = $2 \times$ avg. | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{V \cdot A\bar{y}_{\text{NA}}}{I \cdot t}$ (I-section) | Max. shear stress at N.A. of I-section ($t$ = web thickness) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ | Relation between B.M. and S.F. (used in derivation) | — |
| $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Flexural (bending) stress at distance $y$ | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Never confuse $Q$ (first moment of area, in mm³) with shear force $V$ (in N) or bending moment $M$ (in N·mm). Always compute $Q$ using $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}$ where $\bar{y}$ is the distance from the **N.A. to the centroid** of the area $A'$ lying **above (or below)** the fiber being considered.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility — Where This Equation is Used

- **Design of steel I-beams, channels, and built-up girders** — Shear stress governs web thickness design.
- **Reinforced Concrete Beam Design** — Shear reinforcement (stirrups) is designed based on shear stress distribution.
- **Timber Beam Design** — Horizontal shear failure along the grain is a common failure mode.
- **Wooden Flitched Beams** — Differential horizontal shear between the components must be checked.
- **Mechanics of Shafts and Machine Frames** — Load-bearing structural analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation of the Shear Stress Equation

### 3.1 Setup and Free-Body Diagram

Consider a beam of **uniform cross-section** subjected to a transverse loading that produces:
- A shear force $V$ at section $x$
- A bending moment $M$ at section $x$

At a section $x + dx$, the corresponding values become:
- Shear force: $V + dV$
- Bending moment: $M + dM$

Now, isolate a small element of length $dx$ between sections $x$ and $x + dx$. On this element, draw a horizontal plane at a distance $y_1$ from the **Neutral Axis (N.A.)**.

We will analyze the **equilibrium of the lower portion** of the beam lying *below* this horizontal plane (from $y = y_1$ down to the bottom fiber).

### 3.2 Identification of Forces Acting on the Lower Portion

The forces acting on the lower isolated portion are:

**Force 1 — Normal (Bending) Force on the Left Face (at section $x$):**
This is the integral of bending stress over the area $A'$ (the area below the horizontal plane):
$$F_{\text{left}} = \int_{A'} \sigma \, dA = \int_{A'} \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \, dA$$

**Force 2 — Normal (Bending) Force on the Right Face (at section $x + dx$):**
$$F_{\text{right}} = \int_{A'} \sigma' \, dA = \int_{A'} \frac{(M + dM) \cdot y}{I} \, dA$$

**Force 3 — Horizontal Shear Force on the Top Face (along the cut plane):**
This is the unknown we want to find. If $\tau$ is the horizontal shear stress at the fiber at distance $y_1$, then:
$$F_{\text{horizontal}} = \tau \cdot b \cdot dx$$

where $b$ is the width of the beam at the level $y = y_1$, and $b \cdot dx$ is the area of the top horizontal face.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Algebraic Derivation

For equilibrium of the lower portion, the sum of horizontal forces must equal zero. Taking the rightward direction as positive:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies F_{\text{right}} - F_{\text{left}} - F_{\text{horizontal}} = 0$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\int_{A'} \frac{(M + dM) \cdot y}{I} \, dA - \int_{A'} \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \, dA - \tau \cdot b \cdot dx = 0$$

Since $I$ is constant for the section, factor out $\frac{1}{I}$:

$$\frac{1}{I} \int_{A'} (M + dM - M) \cdot y \, dA - \tau \cdot b \cdot dx = 0$$

$$\frac{1}{I} \int_{A'} dM \cdot y \, dA - \tau \cdot b \cdot dx = 0$$

Since $dM$ is constant for the cross-section, take it out:

$$\frac{dM}{I} \int_{A'} y \, dA - \tau \cdot b \cdot dx = 0$$

Now, the integral $\int_{A'} y \, dA$ is, by definition, the **First Moment of Area** of the portion $A'$ about the Neutral Axis:

$$\int_{A'} y \, dA = A' \cdot \bar{y} = Q$$

where $\bar{y}$ is the distance from the N.A. to the centroid of the area $A'$.

Substituting $Q$:

$$\frac{dM \cdot Q}{I} - \tau \cdot b \cdot dx = 0$$

Rearrange:

$$\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I}$$

$$\tau = \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I \cdot b \cdot dx}$$

### 3.4 Applying the Shear-Moment Differential Relation

From the theory of bending, the relationship between shear force and bending moment is:

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V \implies dM = V \cdot dx$$

Substituting $dM = V \cdot dx$ into our expression:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot dx \cdot Q}{I \cdot b \cdot dx}$$

The $dx$ terms cancel:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}}$$

This is the **final shear stress equation** for beams.

### 3.5 Summary of Logical Steps

1. Isolate a beam element of length $dx$.
2. Consider the equilibrium of the portion of the beam above (or below) a horizontal plane at distance $y_1$ from N.A.
3. Bending stresses on the two vertical faces give unequal normal forces because of change in bending moment.
4. This imbalance is balanced by the horizontal shear force on the cut horizontal plane.
5. Equating forces yields $\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = \dfrac{dM \cdot Q}{I}$.
6. Using $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$, we obtain $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Free-Body Diagram of Beam Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Beam[Beam Element of Length dx]
        direction LR
        A1[Section x: M and V acting on left face] --> A2[Bending stress sigma = My/I acting on left face over area A prime]
        A3[Section x plus dx: M plus dM and V plus dV acting on right face] --> A4[Bending stress sigma prime = M plus dM times y over I acting on right face over area A prime]
        A5[Horizontal plane at y1 from N.A.] --> A6[Shear stress tau acting on top face area b times dx]
    end

    F1[Force left equals integral of My over I dA equals MQ over I]
    F2[Force right equals integral of M plus dM times y over I dA equals M plus dM times Q over I]
    F3[Force horizontal equals tau times b times dx]

    A2 --> F1
    A4 --> F2
    A6 --> F3

    F2 --> E1[Subtract F1 and F3 from F2 and set sum equals 0]
    F1 --> E1
    F3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Apply dM by dx equals V]
    E2 --> E3[Final Result: tau equals VQ over Ib]

    style E3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shear Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Inputs: V, I, Cross-section Geometry] --> CalcQ[Compute Q = A prime times y bar for chosen fiber]
    CalcQ --> CalcB[Identify width b at fiber level]
    CalcB --> Apply[Apply formula tau = VQ over Ib]
    Apply --> Output[Shear stress tau at fiber level y1]

    Output --> Val1[For Rectangular section: tau max at NA equals 1.5 times V over A]
    Output --> Val2[For I section: tau max in web at NA equals V A y bar NA over I t]
    Output --> Val3[For Circular section: tau max equals 4V over 3A]
    Output --> Val4[Shear stress is zero at top and bottom fibers]

    style Apply fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Beam Element with Forces

```
              ↑ y
              │ ──────────────────────────  Top Fiber
              │       (σ = My/I = max compressive)
              │
              │      ┌──────────────────┐
              │      │                  │ ← Bending stress σ on left face
              │      │  Lower portion   │   acts over area A'
              │      │  (below plane)  │
              │      │                  │ ← Horizontal shear τ acts on top face
   ───────────┼──────┴──────────────────┴──── Neutral Axis (N.A.)
              │                  │
              │      Upper       │
              │      portion     │
              │                  │
              │      ┌───────────┐
              │      │           │
              │      │           │ ← Bending stress σ' on right face
              │      │           │   over area A'
              │      └───────────┘
              │
              ▼
              (Bottom fiber: σ = max tensile)

   Section x ◄──── dx ────► Section x + dx
   M, V              M + dM, V + dV
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**State and explain the assumptions made in the derivation of the shear stress formula in beams.**

**Model Answer:**

The derivation of $\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$ is based on the following assumptions:

1. **Homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic material:** Material properties are uniform in all directions and obey Hooke's law.
2. **Uniform cross-section:** The beam is prismatic along its length.
3. **Loading in the plane of symmetry:** Prevents twisting and ensures pure bending without lateral buckling.
4. **Plane sections remain plane:** The Bernoulli-Euler assumption holds true.
5. **Principle of complementary shear:** Horizontal shear stress on a horizontal plane equals the vertical (transverse) shear stress on a vertical plane at that point.
6. **Stresses within the elastic limit:** No yielding occurs, ensuring linear stress distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Hint:** Writing fewer than 4 assumptions may cost marks. Always list 5–6 for full 3 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define the term "First Moment of Area" as used in the shear stress formula. What are its units?**

**Model Answer:**

The **First Moment of Area** ($Q$) of a portion of a cross-section about the Neutral Axis (N.A.) is the product of the area of that portion and the perpendicular distance from the centroid of that portion to the N.A.

$$Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}$$

where $A'$ = area of the portion, and $\bar{y}$ = distance from the N.A. to the centroid of $A'$.

**Units of $Q$:** $\text{mm}^3$ (or $\text{m}^3$).

In the shear stress formula, $Q$ represents the first moment of area of the section **above (or below)** the horizontal plane at which the shear stress is being calculated, taken about the N.A.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the equation for shear stress distribution across the rectangular cross-section of a beam. Hence show that the maximum shear stress in a rectangular section is $\dfrac{3V}{2A}$.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular beam of width $b = 100\,\text{mm}$ and depth $d = 200\,\text{mm}$ carries a shear force of $V = 40\,\text{kN}$. Sketch the shear stress distribution across the depth and determine the shear stress at a depth of $50\,\text{mm}$ from the neutral axis.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

##### **Part (a) — Derivation for Rectangular Section**

For a rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $d$, with N.A. at mid-depth:

**Step 1:** Consider a fiber at distance $y$ from the N.A. The area of the portion **above** the fiber:
$$A' = b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)$$

**Step 2:** Distance from N.A. to centroid of $A'$:
$$\bar{y} = y + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} + y\right)$$

**Step 3:** First moment of area:
$$Q = A' \cdot \bar{y} = b\left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} + y\right)$$

$$Q = \frac{b}{2}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right]$$

**Step 4:** Second moment of area for rectangle:
$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$$

**Step 5:** Apply shear stress formula:
$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} = \frac{V \cdot \dfrac{b}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right]}{\dfrac{b \cdot d^3}{12} \cdot b}$$

Simplify:
$$\tau = \frac{6V}{b \cdot d^3}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 - y^2\right] = \frac{6V}{b d^3}\left[\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right]$$

**Step 6:** Maximum shear stress occurs at $y = 0$ (Neutral Axis):
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{6V}{b d^3} \cdot \frac{d^2}{4} = \frac{6V}{4bd} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = \frac{3V}{2A}$$

where $A = b \cdot d$ is the area of the cross-section. Hence proved.

Shear stress at top and bottom fibers ($y = \pm d/2$) is **zero**, confirming the parabolic distribution.

---

##### **Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution**

**Given:**
- $b = 100\,\text{mm}$
- $d = 200\,\text{mm}$
- $V = 40\,\text{kN} = 40 \times 10^3\,\text{N}$
- Find $\tau$ at $y = 50\,\text{mm}$ from N.A.

**Solution:**

Using the formula derived in Part (a):

$$\tau = \frac{6V}{b d^3}\left[\frac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right]$$

Substitute values:

$$\tau = \frac{6 \times 40 \times 10^3}{100 \times (200)^3} \left[\frac{(200)^2}{4} - (50)^2\right]$$

$$\tau = \frac{240 \times 10^3}{100 \times 8 \times 10^6} \left[10000 - 2500\right]$$

$$\tau = \frac{240 \times 10^3}{8 \times 10^8} \times 7500$$

$$\tau = 3 \times 10^{-4} \times 7500 = 2.25\,\text{N/mm}^2$$

**Maximum shear stress at N.A.** ($y = 0$):
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = \frac{3 \times 40 \times 10^3}{2 \times 100 \times 200} = \frac{120 \times 10^3}{40 \times 10^3} = 3\,\text{N/mm}^2$$

**Shear Stress Distribution Sketch (Parabolic):**

```
   τ = 0  ←──────────────────────────────────→  τ = 0  (top/bottom fiber)
        \                                     /
         \                                   /
          \                                 /
   τ=2.25  ──────────────●─────────────────  ← at y = 50 mm
                          \               /
                           \             /
                            \           /
                             \         /
                              \       /
   τmax = 3.0  ────────────────●─────────────  ← at N.A. (y = 0)
                               │
                          Neutral Axis
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** A simply supported beam of span $L = 4\,\text{m}$ carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of $w = 20\,\text{kN/m}$ over its entire span. The beam has a T-section with flange width $b_f = 150\,\text{mm}$, flange thickness $t_f = 50\,\text{mm}$, web depth $d_w = 200\,\text{mm}$, and web thickness $t_w = 50\,\text{mm}$. Calculate the maximum shear stress at the Neutral Axis.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the shear stress formula, briefly explain with a sketch the shear stress distribution across the depth of an **I-section**. Why is the maximum shear stress located at the Neutral Axis?

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

##### **Part (a) — T-Section Maximum Shear Stress**

**Step 1: Calculate Reactions**
For a simply supported beam with UDL: $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{20 \times 4}{2} = 40\,\text{kN}$

**Maximum Shear Force** at supports: $V_{\max} = 40\,\text{kN}$

**Step 2: Locate the Neutral Axis**
Take the **top of the flange** as reference:

| Part | Area (mm²) | $y$ from top (mm) | $A \cdot y$ (mm³) |
|:-----|:-----------|:------------------|:-------------------|
| Flange | $150 \times 50 = 7500$ | $25$ | $187,500$ |
| Web | $50 \times 200 = 10000$ | $50 + 100 = 150$ | $1,500,000$ |
| **Total** | $A = 17500$ | | $\sum Ay = 1,687,500$ |

$$\bar{y}_{\text{top}} = \frac{1,687,500}{17,500} = 96.43\,\text{mm}$$

Neutral axis location from top = $96.43\,\text{mm}$.

Distance of N.A. from top of flange: $y_{\text{NA,top}} = 96.43\,\text{mm}$
Distance of N.A. from top of web: $96.43 - 50 = 46.43\,\text{mm}$
Distance of N.A. from bottom: $250 - 96.43 = 153.57\,\text{mm}$

**Step 3: Calculate Second Moment of Area $I$**

For flange (about its own centroidal axis, then using parallel axis):
$$I_{\text{flange}} = \frac{b_f \cdot t_f^3}{12} + A_f \cdot d_f^2 = \frac{150 \times 50^3}{12} + 7500 \times (96.43 - 25)^2$$
$$I_{\text{flange}} = 1,562,500 + 7500 \times (71.43)^2 = 1,562,500 + 38,261,250 = 39,823,750\,\text{mm}^4$$

For web:
$$I_{\text{web}} = \frac{t_w \cdot d_w^3}{12} + A_w \cdot d_w'^2 = \frac{50 \times 200^3}{12} + 10000 \times (150 - 96.43)^2$$
$$I_{\text{web}} = 33,333,333 + 10000 \times (53.57)^2 = 33,333,333 + 28,697,449 = 62,030,782\,\text{mm}^4$$

$$I_{\text{total}} = 39,823,750 + 62,030,782 = 101,854,532\,\text{mm}^4 \approx 101.85 \times 10^6\,\text{mm}^4$$

**Step 4: Calculate $Q$ at Neutral Axis (entire area above N.A.)**

Area above N.A. = Flange + Web portion above N.A.
- Web portion above N.A.: height = $46.43\,\text{mm}$, width = $50\,\text{mm}$, area = $2321.5\,\text{mm}^2$
- Centroid of web portion above N.A. from N.A.: $46.43/2 = 23.21\,\text{mm}$
- Centroid of flange from N.A.: $96.43 - 25 = 71.43\,\text{mm}$

$$Q_{\text{NA}} = (7500 \times 71.43) + (2321.5 \times 23.21)$$
$$Q_{\text{NA}} = 535,725 + 53,889 = 589,614\,\text{mm}^3 \approx 5.896 \times 10^5\,\text{mm}^3$$

**Step 5: Apply Shear Stress Formula**
Web thickness at N.A.: $b = t_w = 50\,\text{mm}$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{V_{\max} \cdot Q_{\text{NA}}}{I \cdot b} = \frac{40 \times 10^3 \times 589,614}{101.85 \times 10^6 \times 50}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{23.585 \times 10^9}{5092.5 \times 10^6} = 4.63\,\text{N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} \approx 4.63\,\text{MPa}}$$

---

##### **Part (b) — I-Section Shear Stress Distribution**

**Explanation:**

In an I-section (or any section with flanges and a web), the shear stress distribution is **non-uniform**:

- **In the flanges:** Shear stress is **horizontal** (acting along the flange), with maximum at the junction with the web and zero at the outer edges.
- **In the web:** Shear stress is **vertical** (transverse), with maximum at the Neutral Axis and reducing to zero at top and bottom.

**Why is $\tau_{\max}$ at the N.A.?**

For the formula $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$:

- $V$ and $I$ are **constants** at a given section.
- $b$ is **smallest in the web** (especially near the N.A. for I-sections), making $\tau$ **large**.
- $Q$ is **maximum when we consider the entire area above (or below) the N.A.**, because more area contributes to $Q$.

Therefore, $Q/b$ is maximum at the N.A., giving $\tau_{\max}$ at the N.A.

**Sketch of Shear Stress Distribution in I-Section:**

```
           Flange            Flange
   ┌────────────────┐    ┌────────────────┐
   │                │    │                │
   │  τ = 0  →·····●·····→  τ = small     │  ← Flange shear
   │                │    │                │     (horizontal)
   ├────────────────┤    ├────────────────┤
   │                │    │                │
   │      τ        ↑│    │↑       τ       │
   │     small     ↑│    │↑      medium   │  ← Web shear
   │               ↑│    │↑               │     (vertical)
   │               ↑│    │↑               │
   │ ──●───────●───↑●────●↑─────────●─── │  ← N.A. (τ = max)
   │               ↓│    │↓               │
   │      τ        ↓│    │↓       τ       │
   │     small     ↓│    │↓      medium   │
   │                │    │                │
   ├────────────────┤    ├────────────────┤
   │                │    │                │
   │  τ = small     │    │·····●····· τ = 0│
   │                │    │                │
   └────────────────┘    └────────────────┘
         Web thickness = t (small, so τ large)
```

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $Q$ with the shear force $V$ or bending moment $M$.** $Q$ is the first moment of **area**, not force.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute $A'$ and $\bar{y}$ correctly.** Many students use the entire area instead of the area *above/below* the fiber.
> 3. **Using wrong units.** $Q$ must be in $\text{mm}^3$, $I$ in $\text{mm}^4$, $b$ in $\text{mm}$, $V$ in $\text{N}$ to get $\tau$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa).
> 4. **Applying the formula at the top or bottom fiber.** Shear stress is **zero** at extreme fibers — don't apply the formula there.
> 5. **Skipping the assumption statement** in derivations. KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for listing assumptions.
> 6. **Not sketching the shear stress distribution** when asked — KTU requires this for full marks in 14-mark problems.
> 7. **Forgetting to apply $dM/dx = V$** in the derivation. This is the key final step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Shear stress equation:** $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$
- **First Moment of Area:** $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}$, where $\bar{y}$ is measured from the N.A.
- **Assumptions:** Homogeneous, isotropic, elastic material; uniform cross-section; symmetric loading; plane sections remain plane; complementary shear principle.
- **Shear stress is zero at the extreme top and bottom fibers** (free surfaces).
- **Shear stress is maximum at the Neutral Axis** for symmetric sections like rectangle, circle, I-section.
- **Rectangular section:** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \times \tau_{\text{avg}}$
- **Solid circular section:** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A} = 1.333 \times \tau_{\text{avg}}$
- **Triangular section:** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{2V}{A} = 2 \times \tau_{\text{avg}}$ (at $h/2$ from base, not at base!)
- **I-section:** Shear is mostly carried by the web; flanges primarily resist bending.
- **Key relation used in derivation:** $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$
- **Distribution shape:** Parabolic for rectangle, ellipse-like for circle, parabolic-with-jumps for I-section.
- **Always state assumptions** before deriving — earns easy marks in KTU exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Stresses in Beams of Various Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shear Stresses in Beams of Various Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Shear stress in a beam** is the intensity of internal force per unit area acting tangentially to a cross-section, arising from the transverse (vertical) shear force $V$ applied to the beam. At any fiber located at a distance $y$ from the **Neutral Axis (NA)**, the transverse shear stress is given by the classical **Zhuravskii (or Jourawski) formula**:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Transverse (vertical) shear stress at the fiber level (N/mm² or MPa)
- $V$ = Total transverse shear force acting on the cross-section (N)
- $Q$ = First moment of area of the portion of the cross-section *above* (or below) the fiber, about the Neutral Axis (mm³)
- $I$ = Second moment of area (Moment of Inertia) of the entire cross-section about the Neutral Axis (mm⁴)
- $b$ = Width of the cross-section at the fiber level under consideration (mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAGT302 – Module 3):**
> The derivation assumes: (i) plane sections remain plane, (ii) shear stress is uniformly distributed across the width $b$, and (iii) complementary shear stresses exist on horizontal planes (equal in magnitude, mutually perpendicular).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thick **book lying flat on a table**, with your palm pressing down on the top cover. If you slide the top cover sideways relative to the bottom pages, the pages will "shear" against each other. Each page experiences a tiny tangential (horizontal) force from the pages above and below it.

In a beam under transverse load:
- The **fibers of the beam behave exactly like the pages of the book**.
- Fibers near the **neutral axis** slide the most relative to their neighbors → **maximum shear stress**.
- Fibers at the **top and bottom surfaces** do not slide relative to their neighbors → **zero shear stress** (they are pure bending fibers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Bending stress ($\sigma$) is maximum at the extreme fibers, while shear stress ($\tau$) is maximum at the Neutral Axis. This **inverse distribution** is a recurring KTU viva/favourite concept.

## 1.3 Assumptions Underlying the Formula

1. **Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's law valid).
2. **Beam is prismatic** (constant cross-section along length).
3. **Plane sections before bending remain plane after bending** (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
4. **Shear stress is uniformly distributed across the width** $b$ at a given fiber.
5. **Complementary shear stress** on horizontal planes equals vertical shear stress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation Logic – Step by Step (Conceptual Walk-through)

To derive the shear stress formula, consider an elemental portion of a beam of length $dx$:

1. **Cut two transverse sections** a distance $dx$ apart. The shear force on the left face is $V$ and on the right face is $V + dV$.
2. **Consider a horizontal fiber** at distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis. The bending moment on the left face is $M$ and on the right face is $M + dM$.
3. **Bending stresses** on the left and right faces of the fiber are:
   - Left face: $\sigma_1 = \frac{M y}{I}$
   - Right face: $\sigma_2 = \frac{(M + dM) y}{I}$
4. **The unbalanced horizontal force** on the fiber must be resisted by a **shear force** $dF$ acting on the bottom face of the fiber (length $dx$, width $b$).
5. Applying **equilibrium of horizontal forces** on the fiber element yields the Zhuravskii equation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units (SI) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shear stress at a fiber** | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | Zhuravskii/Jourawski formula |
| 2 | **First moment of area (Q)** | $Q = A \bar{y}$ | mm³ | $A$ = area above/below fiber; $\bar{y}$ = distance of centroid of that area from NA |
| 3 | **Average shear stress** | $\tau_{avg} = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | MPa | Used for preliminary design of wooden/mild-steel beams |
| 4 | **Maximum shear stress (Rectangular section)** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \tau_{avg}$ | MPa | Occurs at NA, where $b = $ full width |
| 5 | **Max shear stress (Circular section)** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4V}{3A}$ | MPa | Occurs at NA |
| 6 | **Max shear stress (Thin-walled tube, radius $R$, thickness $t$)** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{2V}{A}$ | MPa | $A \approx 2\pi R t$ |
| 7 | **Max shear stress (I-section: web governs)** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V}{t_w \cdot d_w}$ | MPa | $d_w$ ≈ web depth between flanges |
| 8 | **Bending stress at extreme fiber** | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{M y_{max}}{I}$ | MPa | For comparison with $\tau_{max}$ |
| 9 | **Shear centre distance (Channel section)** | $e = \dfrac{b^2 h^2 t}{4 I_x}$ (approx., for thin-walled channel) | mm | Distance from web centre to shear centre |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for KTU Valuation:** Always express **Q in mm³** when $V$ is in N and $I$ in mm⁴ so that $\tau$ comes out in **N/mm² = MPa**. Mixing units (cm, m) is a **favourite mark-deduction trap**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of steel I-beams (girders)**: Web thickness $t_w$ is sized such that $\tau_{max} \le \tau_{allowable}$ for the steel grade.
- **Wooden beams**: Shear failure (horizontal splitting along grain) governs short, deep beams.
- **Reinforced Concrete Beams**: Vertical stirrups are provided to resist diagonal shear stresses.
- **Machine shafts & axles**: Torsion + transverse shear combined analysis uses this formula as a building block.
- **Aircraft spars & thin-walled structures**: Shear flow $q = \tau \cdot b = \dfrac{VQ}{I}$ becomes critical for skin-stringer design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Zhuravskii Shear Formula

Consider an elemental slice of a beam of length $dx$ cut by two transverse sections. Let $V$ be the shear force and $M$ the bending moment on the left face.

**Step 1:** Bending stress on the left face at distance $y$ from the NA is

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{M \, y}{I}$$

**Step 2:** Bending stress on the right face is

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{(M + dM) \, y}{I}$$

**Step 3:** Consider a small horizontal fiber of area $dA$ at distance $y$ from NA, with width $b$ and length $dx$. The unbalanced horizontal force on this fiber's left and right faces must equal the shear force on its bottom face.

Unbalanced horizontal force:

$$dF = (\sigma_2 - \sigma_1) \, dA = \frac{dM \cdot y \cdot dA}{I}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over the area of the cross-section *above* the fiber (from $y$ to $y_{max}$):

$$F = \int \frac{dM \cdot y}{I} \, dA = \frac{dM}{I} \int y \, dA = \frac{dM}{I} \cdot Q$$

where $Q = \int y \, dA$ is the first moment of area above the fiber about the NA.

**Step 5:** This force $F$ acts on the horizontal plane (bottom of the fiber area) of area $b \cdot dx$. So shear stress on this plane is

$$\tau = \frac{F}{b \, dx} = \frac{dM}{dx} \cdot \frac{Q}{I \cdot b}$$

**Step 6:** From equilibrium of the beam slice, $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$ (relation between shear force and rate of change of bending moment).

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \, Q}{I \, b}}$$

This is the **Zhuravskii shear stress formula**.

## 3.2 Shear Stress Distribution – Rectangular Section

Consider a rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $d$. The Neutral Axis passes through the centroid.

**Step 1:** Moment of inertia about NA:

$$I = \frac{b \, d^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 2:** At a fiber distance $y$ from NA, the area above is a rectangle of width $b$ and height $(d/2 - y)$.

Area above fiber:

$$A' = b \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)$$

**Step 3:** Centroid of this area is at distance $\bar{y}$ from NA:

$$\bar{y} = y + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} + y\right)$$

**Step 4:** First moment of area above fiber:

$$Q = A' \bar{y} = b\left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d}{2} + y\right) = \frac{b}{2}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

**Step 5:** Shear stress at fiber $y$:

$$\tau = \frac{V \, Q}{I \, b} = \frac{V \cdot \dfrac{b}{2}\left[\left(\dfrac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]}{\dfrac{b d^{3}}{12} \cdot b} = \frac{6 V}{b d^{3}}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

**Step 6:** Maximum at NA ($y = 0$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{6V}{b d^{3}} \cdot \frac{d^{2}}{4} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = \frac{3V}{2A}$$

Hence, for a rectangular section, **$\tau_{max} = 1.5 \, \tau_{avg}$**.

**Step 7:** Minimum at extreme fibers ($y = \pm d/2$):

$$\tau_{min} = 0$$

**Distribution shape:** **Parabolic**, varying from $\tau_{max} = 1.5 \tau_{avg}$ at NA to $0$ at the top and bottom surfaces.

## 3.3 Shear Stress Distribution – Solid Circular Section

For a solid circular section of diameter $d$, the analysis is performed using the chord width at any fiber.

**Step 1:** At a fiber distance $y$ from NA, the chord width is:

$$b = 2\sqrt{r^{2} - y^{2}}, \quad \text{where } r = d/2$$

**Step 2:** First moment of area of the semicircle above the fiber is obtained by integration. The standard result for the entire semicircle is:

$$Q_{NA} = \frac{2 r^{3}}{3}$$

**Step 3:** Maximum shear stress (at NA, where $b = 2r = d$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I \cdot b} = \frac{V \cdot \dfrac{2r^{3}}{3}}{\dfrac{\pi r^{4}}{4} \cdot 2r} = \frac{4V}{3\pi r^{2}} = \frac{4V}{3A}$$

Hence, for a solid circular section, **$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4}{3}\tau_{avg} \approx 1.33\,\tau_{avg}$**.

**Step 4:** Shear stress is **zero** at top and bottom ($y = \pm r$, where $b = 0$).

**Distribution shape:** **Parabolic in vertical direction**, but with cusps at top and bottom due to the curved profile.

## 3.4 Shear Stress Distribution – I-Section (Standard Symmetric)

For a symmetric I-section with:
- Flange width $B$, flange thickness $t_f$
- Web thickness $t_w$, web depth (between flanges) $d_w$
- Overall depth $D = d_w + 2 t_f$

**Step 1:** Calculate $I$ for the entire section about NA.

**Step 2:** Shear stress in the **flange**: The width changes. At a fiber $y$ in the flange (above NA), the area above is a small rectangle. The expression becomes complex; for thin flanges, we approximate:

$$\tau_{flange} \approx \frac{V}{I} \cdot \frac{B}{2}\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

Flange shear stress is **very small** (almost negligible).

**Step 3:** Shear stress in the **web** (at a fiber in the web, distance $y$ from NA):

The area above the fiber consists of the full flange + a portion of the web.

**Step 4:** Maximum shear stress occurs in the web at the Neutral Axis:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I \cdot t_w}$$

where $Q_{NA} = B \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\dfrac{D - t_f}{2}\right) + t_w \cdot \dfrac{d_w}{2} \cdot \dfrac{d_w}{4}$

**Step 5:** **Simplified maximum shear stress formula (web-dominated assumption):**

$$\tau_{max} \approx \frac{V}{t_w \cdot d_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Designer's Note:** In a typical I-beam, **90–98% of the shear force is carried by the web**. Flanges primarily resist bending. This is why we often approximate $\tau_{max}$ using only the web dimensions.

## 3.5 Shear Stress Distribution – T-Section

For a T-section (flange on top, web below):
- Flange width $B$, flange thickness $t_f$
- Web depth $d_w$, web thickness $t_w$
- Locate NA first: $\bar{y}$ from the top.

**Step 1:** Since the section is unsymmetric, the Neutral Axis is **not at the geometric center**. Compute $\bar{y}$ from the top:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{A_f \cdot \bar{y}_f + A_w \cdot \bar{y}_w}{A_f + A_w}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum shear stress occurs at the Neutral Axis (where $Q$ is maximum and $b = t_w$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I_{NA} \cdot t_w}$$

**Step 3:** A sharp **jump in shear stress** occurs at the flange-web junction (because width changes abruptly from $B$ to $t_w$). The stress in the flange is much smaller than in the web because $b$ is much larger.

## 3.6 Summary Comparison Table – $\tau_{max}$ for Standard Sections

| Cross-Section | $\tau_{max}$ (in terms of $V$ and $A$) | Shape factor $\dfrac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}}$ | Location of $\tau_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangle ($b \times d$) | $\dfrac{3V}{2A}$ | $1.50$ | Neutral Axis |
| Solid Circle | $\dfrac{4V}{3A}$ | $1.33$ | Neutral Axis |
| Thin-walled Circular Tube | $\dfrac{2V}{A}$ | $2.00$ | Neutral Axis |
| Triangular (apex up) | $\dfrac{3V}{A}$ | $3.00$ | At $h/2$ from apex (3/4 of height from apex) |
| Standard I-section (web-only approx.) | $\dfrac{V}{t_w d_w}$ | Depends on section | Neutral Axis, in web |
| T-section | $\dfrac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I_{NA} \cdot t_w}$ | Variable | Neutral Axis, in web |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shear Stress Distribution Block Diagram (Rectangular vs Circular vs I-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Beam under transverse shear V] --> node1[Identify cross-section geometry]
    node1 --> node2[Compute I about NA]
    node1 --> node3[Compute Q at fiber y]
    node1 --> node4[Identify width b at fiber y]
    node2 --> node5[Apply Zhuravskii formula tau = VQ divided by I times b]
    node3 --> node5
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6{Which section?}
    node6 --> rec[Rectangular: parabolic, max 1.5 tau_avg at NA]
    node6 --> cir[Solid Circular: parabolic, max 1.33 tau_avg at NA]
    node6 --> isec[I-Section: max in web at NA, very low in flanges]
    node6 --> tsec[T-Section: max at NA in web, jump at flange-web junction]
    node6 --> tube[Thin Hollow Tube: max 2 tau_avg at NA]
    rec --> node7[Plot tau vs y curve]
    cir --> node7
    isec --> node7
    tsec --> node7
    tube --> node7
    node7 --> endA[End: Identify tau_max and tau_min for design]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Shear Stress Flow in a Beam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[External Loading]
        loadA[Transverse Load P]
        loadB[Support Reactions R]
    end
    subgraph A2[Internal Force Resolution]
        forceA[Shear Force V at section]
        forceB[Bending Moment M at section]
    end
    subgraph A3[Stress Computation]
        compA[Bending Stress sigma = My divided by I]
        compB[Shear Stress tau = VQ divided by Ib]
    end
    subgraph A4[Section Behaviour]
        outA[Top fiber: sigma max, tau = 0]
        outB[NA fiber: sigma = 0, tau max]
        outC[Extreme bottom: sigma max, tau = 0]
    end
    loadA --> forceA
    loadA --> forceB
    forceA --> compB
    forceB --> compA
    compA --> outA
    compA --> outC
    compB --> outB
    outA --> res[Resultant Stress State]
    outB --> res
    outC --> res
```

## 4.3 Visual Mapping of $\tau$ Distribution Across Section Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[Rectangular: b x d] --> r1[Parabolic curve, peaks at NA, value 3V over 2A]
    s2[Solid Circular: diameter d] --> r2[Parabolic with cusps, peaks at NA, value 4V over 3A]
    s3[Thin Hollow Tube: radius R, thickness t] --> r3[Approx uniform at NA, peaks at NA, value 2V over A]
    s4[I-Section: flanges plus web] --> r4[Negligible in flanges, parabolic high in web, peaks at NA]
    s5[T-Section: flange plus web] --> r5[Small in flange, jumps up sharply at flange-web junction, peaks at NA in web]
    s6[Triangular Section: apex up] --> r6[Zero at apex, max at three-quarters of height, value 3V over A]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic – Stepwise Procedure for Solving a Shear Stress Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[Step 1: Read problem and identify V, section geometry] --> p2[Step 2: Locate Neutral Axis by computing y_bar]
    p2 --> p3[Step 3: Compute I about NA using parallel axis theorem if composite]
    p3 --> p4[Step 4: For required fiber, compute Q = A times y_centroid]
    p4 --> p5[Step 5: Identify width b at fiber level]
    p5 --> p6[Step 6: Apply tau = V times Q divided by I times b]
    p6 --> p7[Step 7: Compute tau_max at NA and tau_min at extreme fibers]
    p7 --> p8[Step 8: Compare with allowable tau_allow for the material]
    p8 --> p9[End: Check safe or unsafe, then design accordingly]
```
