<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State and explain the Zhuravskii formula for shear stress in beams. Mention the assumptions involved.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Zhuravskii (Jourawski) formula for transverse shear stress in a beam at a fiber located at distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis is:

$$\tau = \frac{V \, Q}{I \, b}$$

where $V$ is the transverse shear force, $Q$ is the first moment of area of the portion of the cross-section above (or below) the fiber about the NA, $I$ is the second moment of area of the entire cross-section about the NA, and $b$ is the width at the fiber level.

**Assumptions (1 mark each):**
1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
2. The beam is initially straight and prismatic with constant cross-section.
3. Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
4. Shear stress is uniformly distributed across the width $b$ at the fiber level.
5. Complementary shear stresses exist on horizontal planes.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
For a rectangular beam of width $b$ and depth $d$, derive the ratio of maximum shear stress to average shear stress.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Shear stress at a fiber distance $y$ from NA:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{2I}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

**Step 2:** At NA ($y = 0$):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V}{2I} \cdot \frac{d^{2}}{4} = \frac{V \, d^{2}}{8 I}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $I = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \, d^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{b d^{3}} = \frac{3V}{2 b d} = \frac{3V}{2A}$$

**Step 4:** Average shear stress:

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{V}{A}$$

**Step 5:** Ratio:

$$\boxed{\frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

A simply supported beam of span **4 m** carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of **30 kN/m** over its entire span. The beam has a **rectangular cross-section of 200 mm width and 400 mm depth**.

**(a)** Determine the maximum shear force and draw the shear force diagram. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the maximum shear stress in the beam and its location. State the ratio of $\tau_{max}$ to $\tau_{avg}$. **(7 marks)**

**(c)** At what distance from the neutral axis will the shear stress be equal to **half the maximum shear stress**? **(4 marks)**

---

#### Solution

**Given:** $L = 4$ m, $w = 30$ kN/m, $b = 200$ mm, $d = 400$ mm.

**Part (a) — Maximum Shear Force:**

For a simply supported beam under UDL, reactions at both ends are equal:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{w L}{2} = \frac{30 \times 4}{2} = 60 \, \text{kN}$$

Maximum shear force occurs at the supports:

$$\boxed{V_{max} = 60 \, \text{kN}}$$

The SFD is linear, decreasing from $+60$ kN at A to $-60$ kN at B, crossing zero at midspan.

**Part (b) — Maximum Shear Stress:**

For rectangular section:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3 V_{max}}{2 A} = \frac{3 \times 60 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 200 \times 400}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{180000}{160000} = 1.125 \, \text{MPa}$$

Location: at the Neutral Axis, i.e., at $y = 0$ from NA (or 200 mm from top/bottom).

Ratio:

$$\frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5$$

**Part (c) — Distance from NA where $\tau = 0.5 \, \tau_{max}$:**

Shear stress at distance $y$ from NA:

$$\tau = \frac{6V}{b d^{3}}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right]$$

Setting $\tau = 0.5 \, \tau_{max}$:

$$\frac{6V}{b d^{3}}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - y^{2}\right] = 0.5 \times \frac{3V}{2bd}$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{6}{b d^{3}}\left[\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right] = \frac{1.5}{2 b d}$$

$$\frac{6}{d^{2}}\left[\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right] = 0.75$$

$$\frac{6 d^{2}}{4 d^{2}} - \frac{6 y^{2}}{d^{2}} = 0.75$$

$$1.5 - \frac{6 y^{2}}{d^{2}} = 0.75$$

$$\frac{6 y^{2}}{d^{2}} = 0.75$$

$$y^{2} = \frac{0.75 \, d^{2}}{6} = \frac{d^{2}}{8}$$

$$y = \frac{d}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{400}{2\sqrt{2}} = 100\sqrt{2} \approx 141.42 \, \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{y \approx 141.4 \, \text{mm from Neutral Axis}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]**

An I-section beam has the following dimensions:
- Overall flange width: $B = 200$ mm
- Flange thickness: $t_f = 20$ mm
- Web thickness: $t_w = 20$ mm
- Web depth (between flanges): $d_w = 260$ mm
- Overall depth: $D = 300$ mm

The beam carries a maximum shear force of **$V = 150$ kN**.

**(a)** Calculate the moment of inertia $I$ of the cross-section about the Neutral Axis. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the maximum shear stress in the beam (occurring at NA in the web). **(6 marks)**

**(c)** Calculate the shear stress at the **flange-web junction** and comment on the variation. **(4 marks)**

---

#### Solution

**Part (a) — Moment of Inertia:**

Using the standard formula (outer rectangle minus cut-outs), or by summing parts about NA (NA at center by symmetry):

$$I = \frac{B D^{3}}{12} - \frac{(B - t_w) d_w^{3}}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{200 \times 300^{3}}{12} - \frac{(200 - 20) \times 260^{3}}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{200 \times 27 \times 10^{6}}{12} - \frac{180 \times 17.576 \times 10^{6}}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{9}}{12} - \frac{3.1637 \times 10^{9}}{12}$$

Wait — recomputing carefully:

$$I = \frac{200 \times (300)^3}{12} - \frac{180 \times (260)^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{200 \times 27{,}000{,}000}{12} - \frac{180 \times 17{,}576{,}000}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{5{,}400{,}000{,}000}{12} - \frac{3{,}163{,}680{,}000}{12}$$

$$I = 450{,}000{,}000 - 263{,}640{,}000$$

$$\boxed{I = 186{,}360{,}000 \, \text{mm}^{4} = 1.8636 \times 10^{8} \, \text{mm}^{4}}$$

**Part (b) — Maximum Shear Stress (at NA in web):**

First compute $Q$ at NA = first moment of area above NA about NA:

$Q_{NA} =$ (Flange contribution) + (Half-web contribution)

**Flange contribution:**
$A_f = B \times t_f = 200 \times 20 = 4000 \, \text{mm}^{2}$
$\bar{y}_f = \dfrac{D}{2} - \dfrac{t_f}{2} = 150 - 10 = 140 \, \text{mm}$
$Q_f = 4000 \times 140 = 560{,}000 \, \text{mm}^{3}$

**Half-web contribution:**
$A_{hw} = t_w \times \dfrac{d_w}{2} = 20 \times 130 = 2600 \, \text{mm}^{2}$
$\bar{y}_{hw} = \dfrac{d_w}{4} = \dfrac{260}{4} = 65 \, \text{mm}$
$Q_{hw} = 2600 \times 65 = 169{,}000 \, \text{mm}^{3}$

**Total:**
$Q_{NA} = 560{,}000 + 169{,}000 = 729{,}000 \, \text{mm}^{3}$

Maximum shear stress:
$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I \cdot t_w} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^{3} \times 729{,}000}{1.8636 \times 10^{8} \times 20}$

$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1.0935 \times 10^{11}}{3.7272 \times 10^{9}}$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 29.34 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Part (c) — Shear Stress at Flange-Web Junction:**

At the junction, $y = d_w/2 = 130$ mm from NA. The area above is just the flange.

$Q_{junc} = A_f \times \bar{y}_f = 4000 \times 140 = 560{,}000 \, \text{mm}^{3}$

Shear stress at junction in the web:
$\tau_{junc} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{junc}}{I \cdot t_w} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^{3} \times 560{,}000}{1.8636 \times 10^{8} \times 20}$

$\tau_{junc} = \dfrac{8.4 \times 10^{10}}{3.7272 \times 10^{9}}$

$$\boxed{\tau_{junc} \approx 22.54 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**Comment:** The shear stress jumps from approximately **22.5 MPa** (just above the junction, in the flange) to this value in the web. The reduction across the flange-web interface is small in absolute terms, but the relative jump is significant because the flange width $B = 200$ mm is 10 times the web width $t_w = 20$ mm. Therefore, in the flange (just above the junction), the stress is:

$\tau_{flange,top} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{junc}}{I \cdot B} = \dfrac{150 \times 10^{3} \times 560{,}000}{1.8636 \times 10^{8} \times 200}$

$\tau_{flange,top} = \dfrac{8.4 \times 10^{10}}{3.7272 \times 10^{10}} \approx 2.25 \, \text{MPa}$

This is much smaller than the 22.5 MPa in the web — confirming that **the web carries ~10× more shear stress than the flange at the same Q value**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Shear Stress Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing cm and mm (e.g., $I$ in cm⁴, $V$ in kN). Always convert to a single unit system: **mm for length, N for force, MPa for stress**.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute NA first** for unsymmetric sections (T, channel, Z). Mistaking the centroid as mid-height leads to wrong $Q$ and wrong $I$.
> 3. **Using full depth $D$ instead of $d_w$** when checking web shear in I-sections. The web depth is *between* the flanges, not the overall depth.
> 4. **Neglecting parallel axis theorem** when computing $I$ for composite sections (e.g., I-beam built from rectangles). Each rectangle's own centroid may not lie on NA.
> 5. **Confusing "first moment of area above the fiber" with "area × distance from extreme fiber"**. $Q$ is always measured from the **Neutral Axis**, not from the extreme top/bottom.
> 6. **Forgetting the shear stress formula applies only to prismatic beams** with constant $b$ at the fiber. For abruptly varying widths (T-section, I-section), apply the formula at *each* fiber individually.
> 7. **Reporting only numerical answer** without stating the location of $\tau_{max}$ — KTU examiners deduct up to 1 mark for omitting the "at NA, in web" qualifier.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Formula**: $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{Ib}$ — Zhuravskii's equation is the single most tested formula in this module.
- **Inverse Relationship with Width**: Shear stress is inversely proportional to width $b$ at the fiber. Narrow regions (webs) carry much more shear than wide regions (flanges).
- **Shear Stress Distribution Shape**:
  - **Rectangular** → Parabolic, $\tau_{max} = 1.5\,\tau_{avg}$ at NA.
  - **Solid Circular** → Parabolic with cusps, $\tau_{max} \approx 1.33\,\tau_{avg}$ at NA.
  - **Thin Hollow Tube** → Approximately uniform near NA, $\tau_{max} \approx 2\,\tau_{avg}$.
  - **Triangular** → Zero at apex, max at $3h/4$ from apex, $\tau_{max} = 3\,\tau_{avg}$.
  - **I-Section** → Negligible in flanges, very high in web at NA.
  - **T-Section** → Discontinuous jump at flange-web junction; max at NA in web.
- **Always Locate NA First** — This is the single most important preliminary step for unsymmetric sections.
- **$Q$ is maximum at NA** for all sections (because $A'$ is maximum and $\bar{y}$ is not yet zero).
- **$\tau_{max}$ occurs at NA, $\sigma_{max}$ at extreme fibers** — these are the two golden design checks for beams.
- **Shear Flow** ($q = \tau \cdot b = \dfrac{VQ}{I}$) is the basis of thin-walled section design — although KTU primarily tests the stress version, be aware of the flow version for advanced problems.
- **Practical Design Insight**: For preliminary sizing of a beam, assume $\tau_{max} \approx \tau_{avg}$ and then multiply by the appropriate shape factor (1.5 for rectangle, 1.33 for circle, etc.) to get the actual maximum.
- **Common Viva Question**: *"Why is shear stress zero at the top and bottom surfaces of a beam?"* — Because the shear force $V$ is transmitted through the cross-section, and the *surface* has no material above/below it to provide a complementary shear stress. Bending stress $\sigma$ is the only stress at the extreme fibers.
- **Quick-Reference Shape Factors**:
  - Rectangle: **1.5**
  - Solid Circle: **1.33** (or $4/3$)
  - Thin Hollow Tube: **2.0**
  - Triangular (apex up): **3.0**
- **Always mention the location** of $\tau_{max}$ in the answer — at the **Neutral Axis** (unless the section is triangular, where it is at $3h/4$ from the apex).
- **Two-minute shortcut for KTU exams**: If asked only for $\tau_{max}$ at NA, compute $Q_{NA}$ by splitting the section into rectangles, computing $A_i \bar{y}_i$ for each part above NA, summing them, and applying $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V Q_{NA}}{I \, b_{NA}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torsion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Torsion** is the twisting of a straight bar (shaft) about its longitudinal axis when subjected to equal and opposite **torques** (twisting moments) acting in planes perpendicular to the axis of the bar. The internal resisting moment on any cross-section is equal to the applied external torque.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 3 of PCAGT302), torsion analysis is restricted to **uniform circular cross-sections** (both solid and hollow), assuming the material obeys **Hooke's Law** and the shaft is subjected to **pure torsion** (no bending or axial loading).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a long cylindrical steel rod with both hands and twisting the two ends in **opposite directions** — like wringing out a wet towel. The fibres on the surface of the rod, originally running parallel to the axis, will now form **small helices** (spiral lines).

- The **tighter the spiral**, the greater the twist per unit length.
- This is *exactly* what happens inside a car's drive shaft, a drill bit, or a ship's propeller shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Torsion generates **shear stresses only** (no normal stresses) on the cross-section. The maximum shear stress occurs at the **outer surface** of the shaft, where the fibres undergo the largest deformation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) | Typical Range in Practice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Torque (Twisting Moment) | $T$ | **N·m** | $10$ to $10^6$ N·m |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | **Pa** ($= \text{N/m}^2$) or **MPa** | $20$–$300$ MPa (mild steel) |
| Modulus of Rigidity | $G$ | **Pa** or **MPa** | $G = 80$ GPa (steel), $G = 27$ GPa (aluminium) |
| Angle of Twist | $\theta$ | **radians (rad)** | $0$ to $0.1$ rad typical |
| Length of Shaft | $L$ | **m** | depends on geometry |
| Polar Moment of Inertia | $J$ | **m⁴** | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-3}$ m⁴ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Always state the value of $G$ as $80 \text{ GPa}$ for mild steel unless a different material is specified in the problem statement.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Assumptions in Pure Torsion (Saint-Venant's Theory)

The torsion equation is derived under the following **valid assumptions**, which KTU examiners expect you to enumerate:

1. The shaft is **circular in cross-section** (solid or hollow) and **uniform** along its length.
2. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
3. The cross-sections **remain plane and circular** after twisting (no warping) — known as *circular shafts assumption*.
4. The cross-sections **rotate as rigid discs** about the longitudinal axis (no in-plane distortion).
5. Radii remain **straight and undistorted** — they only rotate relative to each other.
6. The twist is **uniform** along the length (constant $T$ and constant cross-section).

### 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Concept

Consider a shaft of diameter $d$ and length $L$ fixed at one end and subjected to torque $T$ at the other end.

- Due to twist, a point $P$ on the outer surface moves to a new position $P'$ on the helix.
- The arc length $\widehat{PP'} = r\theta = (d/2)\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle of twist in **radians**.
- The shear strain $\gamma$ is the angle between the radius and the tangent to the helix (angle of inclination):

$$
\gamma = \frac{r\theta}{L} = \frac{(d/2)\theta}{L}
$$

- By **Hooke's Law in shear**: $\tau = G\gamma$, so:

$$
\tau = G \cdot \frac{r\theta}{L}
$$

- For equilibrium, the resisting torque $T$ on the cross-section is the integral of $\tau \cdot dA \cdot r$ over the area:

$$
T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \int_A \frac{G\theta \cdot r^2}{L} \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L}\int_A r^2 \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot J
$$

- Combining gives the **Torsion Equation**:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}}
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for the Torsion module. Memorize the relationships in the rightmost column — KTU problems almost always involve cross-checking two of the three quantities.

| # | Formula | Expression | Description / Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Torsion Equation** | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | Master equation for all torsion problems |
| 2 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | For solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ |
| 3 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow) | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$ | $D$ = outer diameter, $d$ = inner diameter |
| 4 | Polar Section Modulus (Solid) | $Z_{p} = \dfrac{J}{r} = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{16}$ | Used to compute max shear stress: $\tau_{\max} = T / Z_{p}$ |
| 5 | Polar Section Modulus (Hollow) | $Z_{p} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{16\,D}$ | Ratio of $J$ to outer radius $R = D/2$ |
| 6 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Occurs at outer surface ($r = d/2$) |
| 7 | Angle of Twist | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | In **radians**; convert to degrees if required |
| 8 | Twist per unit length | $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G J}$ | Useful for variable cross-section shafts |
| 9 | Strain Energy in Torsion | $U = \dfrac{T^{2}\,L}{2\,G\,J}$ | Total elastic energy stored due to twist |
| 10 | Power Transmitted | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | $N$ = RPM, $T$ = torque in N·m, $P$ in Watts |
| 11 | Equivalent Diameter (Solid) | $d_{eq} = D \left[1 - k^{4}\right]^{1/4}$ | Hollow to solid, where $k = d/D$ (ratio of diameters) |
| 12 | Strength Ratio (Hollow/Solid) | $\dfrac{\tau_{hollow}}{\tau_{solid}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - k^{4}}$ | Same weight comparison |
| 13 | Stiffness Ratio (Hollow/Solid) | $\dfrac{\theta_{hollow}}{\theta_{solid}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - k^{4}}$ | Same weight comparison |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive drive shafts**: Carry torque from gearbox to differential.
- **Marine propeller shafts**: Transmit large torques across long spans in ships.
- **Drill bits and boring bars**: Subjected to combined torsion + bending.
- **Turbine shafts**: In power plants, carry torque at very high RPMs.
- **Helical and torsion springs**: Energy storage in vehicle suspensions, watches, and machinery.

> [!TIP]
> **Why hollow shafts?** For the **same weight**, a hollow shaft carries a much greater torque than a solid shaft because material near the centre carries very little stress. Hollow shafts are universally used in helicopters, bicycles, and aircraft drive systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Torsion Equation

**Setup:** Consider a uniform solid circular shaft of diameter $d$, length $L$, fixed at one end $A$ and free at the other end $B$. Apply a torque $T$ at end $B$ about the longitudinal axis.

**Step 1 — Geometry of Twist:**

A line element on the surface originally parallel to the axis of the shaft (vertical) rotates by a small angle $\gamma$ to become inclined. For a small element of length $dx$, the angle of twist is $d\theta$, so the radius of length $r$ sweeps an arc of length $r \cdot d\theta$ at the free end.

**Step 2 — Shear Strain:**

The shear strain $\gamma$ in the material is given by the ratio of the arc displacement to the length of the element:

$$
\gamma = \frac{r \, d\theta}{dx}
$$

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law in Shear:**

Since the material is elastic and isotropic, the shear stress is proportional to shear strain:

$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma = G \cdot \frac{r \, d\theta}{dx}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{\tau}{r} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium of the Cross-Section:**

The torque $T$ applied externally must be resisted by shear stresses distributed over the cross-section. The contribution of an elemental area $dA$ at radius $r$ is $\tau \cdot dA \cdot r$. Integrating over the whole area:

$$
T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA
$$

Substitute $\tau$ from Step 3:

$$
T = \int_A \left(G \cdot r \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}\right) \cdot r \, dA = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx} \int_A r^{2} \, dA
$$

**Step 5 — Define Polar Moment of Inertia:**

The integral $\int_A r^2 \, dA$ is, by definition, the **polar moment of inertia** $J$ of the cross-section about the longitudinal axis:

$$
J = \int_A r^{2} \, dA
$$

Therefore:

$$
T = G \cdot J \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}
$$

**Step 6 — Combine the Two Relations:**

From Step 3: $\dfrac{\tau}{r} = G \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$

From Step 5: $\dfrac{T}{J} = G \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{dx}$

Equating the two:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = G \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dx}}
$$

For a **uniform** shaft under a **constant** torque, $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{\theta}{L}$:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

> **Result:** This is the celebrated torsion equation. The first equality gives the **shear stress** at any radius; the second gives the **angle of twist** of the free end relative to the fixed end.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of $J$ for a Solid Circular Section

Consider a circle of radius $R = d/2$. Using an **elemental ring** of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$:

$$
dA = 2\pi r \, dr
$$

The elemental polar moment:

$$
dJ = r^{2} \, dA = 2\pi r^{3} \, dr
$$

Integrating from $0$ to $R$:

$$
J = \int_0^R 2\pi r^{3} \, dr = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_0^R = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of $J$ for a Hollow Circular Section

For a hollow shaft with outer radius $R = D/2$ and inner radius $r_i = d/2$:

$$
J = \int_{r_i}^{R} 2\pi r^{3} \, dr = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{r_i}^{R} = \frac{\pi (R^{4} - r_i^{4})}{2} = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}
$$

---

### 3.4 Maximum Shear Stress Derivation

From the torsion equation: $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$

Maximum shear stress occurs at the **outer surface**, $r = R$:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{T}{J/R} = \frac{T}{Z_p}
$$

For a solid shaft:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T}{\pi d^{3} / 16} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}
$$

---

### 3.5 Strain Energy in Torsion

The strain energy stored in a differential element $dx$ is:

$$
dU = \frac{1}{2} T \, d\theta
$$

Using $d\theta = \dfrac{T \, dx}{G J}$:

$$
dU = \frac{1}{2} T \cdot \frac{T \, dx}{G J} = \frac{T^{2}}{2 G J} \, dx
$$

Integrating over the length $L$:

$$
\boxed{U = \frac{T^{2} L}{2 G J}}
$$

> **Equivalence check:** This is analogous to the axial strain energy $U = \dfrac{P^2 L}{2 A E}$, with $T$ replacing $P$, $J$ replacing $A$, and $G$ replacing $E$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Torsion Problem

The following Mermaid flowchart maps out the **decision logic** an engineer follows when solving a torsion problem. This replaces a physical drawing with a clear procedural flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Torque T, Length L, Diameter d, Material G] --> B{Is the shaft solid or hollow?}
    B --> C[Solid Section]
    B --> D[Hollow Section]
    C --> E[Compute J = πd^4 / 32]
    D --> F[Compute J = π D^4 - d^4 / 32]
    E --> G[Compute Zp = πd^3 / 16]
    F --> H[Compute Zp = π D^4 - d^4 / 16D]
    G --> I[Compute τ_max = T / Zp]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute θ = T L / G J]
    J --> K[Convert θ to degrees if needed]
    K --> L[Check τ_max ≤ τ_permissible]
    L --> M{OK or Design Iteration?}
    M --> N[Pass: Safe Design]
    M --> O[Fail: Increase d or Use Hollow]
    O --> B
```

### 4.2 Stress & Strain Distribution Across a Circular Cross-Section

The following Mermaid block represents the **conceptual state** of stress and strain at different radial positions on a cross-section of a twisted shaft.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Centre: r = 0] --> Q1[τ = 0, γ = 0]
    P2[Mid radius: r = d/4] --> Q2[τ = τ_max / 2, γ = γ_max / 2]
    P3[Outer surface: r = d/2] --> Q3[τ = τ_max, γ = γ_max]
    Q1 --> R[Linearly increasing shear]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Torque Transmission in a Shaft System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Motor] --> A2[Coupling]
    A2 --> A3[Shaft Segment 1: L1, d1, J1]
    A3 --> A4[Pulley or Gear]
    A4 --> A5[Shaft Segment 2: L2, d2, J2]
    A5 --> A6[Load: T_out]
    A3 -.Carries T1.-> A4
    A5 -.Carries T2.-> A6
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** In a stepped shaft, the **torque is the same** in every segment (in the absence of intermediate loads). The **angle of twist** adds up: $\theta_{\text{total}} = \sum_i \dfrac{T_i L_i}{G J_i}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State the torsion equation and explain the meaning of each term. Why is the shear stress maximum at the outer surface of a solid shaft?*

**Model Answer:**

The torsion equation is:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}
$$

where:
- $T$ = applied torque (N·m)
- $J$ = polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- $\tau$ = shear stress at radius $r$ (Pa)
- $r$ = radial distance from the centre (m)
- $G$ = modulus of rigidity (Pa)
- $\theta$ = angle of twist in radians
- $L$ = length of the shaft (m)

**Why shear stress is maximum at the outer surface:**

From the torsion equation, $\tau = \dfrac{T r}{J}$. Since $T$ and $J$ are constants for a given cross-section, $\tau$ is directly proportional to $r$. Therefore, $\tau$ is maximum when $r$ is maximum, i.e., at the outer surface ($r = d/2$). The centre has zero shear stress and zero shear strain — this is why **material near the centre is underutilized**, and hollow shafts are more efficient.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Distinguish between a solid shaft and a hollow shaft of the same weight and material. Which one transmits greater torque, and why?*

**Model Answer:**

For a hollow shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$, and a solid shaft of equivalent diameter $d_{eq}$, the comparison is done at **equal cross-sectional area** (i.e., equal weight per unit length).

| Property | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft (for same weight) |
|---|---|---|
| Polar Moment of Inertia | $J_s = \pi d_{eq}^{4} / 32$ | $J_h = \pi(D^{4} - d^{4})/32 > J_s$ |
| Torque for same $\tau_{\max}$ | $T_s$ | $T_h = T_s / (1 - k^{4})$, where $k = d/D$ |
| Weight per unit length | $W_s$ | $W_h = W_s$ (by design) |

A **hollow shaft transmits a greater torque** than a solid shaft of the same weight because material is **redistributed** away from the centre (where stress is low) towards the outer fibres (where stress is high). The strength ratio is:

$$
\frac{T_h}{T_s} = \frac{1}{1 - k^{4}} > 1
$$

This is why hollow shafts are preferred in **automotive drive shafts, helicopter rotors, and aircraft structures**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Section B]`

A solid circular shaft of diameter $60$ mm transmits $30$ kW at $180$ rpm. Take $G = 80$ GPa. Find:
**(a)** The torque transmitted. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The maximum shear stress induced in the shaft material and the angle of twist per metre length. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Given:**
- Power $P = 30$ kW $= 30{,}000$ W
- Speed $N = 180$ rpm
- Diameter $d = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m

**Step 1:** Use the power–torque relation:

$$
P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $T$:

$$
T = \frac{60 \, P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 30{,}000}{2\pi \times 180}
$$

$$
T = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{360\pi} = \frac{5000}{\pi} \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 1591.55 \text{ N·m}}
$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Step 1:** Polar moment of inertia for a solid shaft:

$$
J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^{4}}{32}
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 4.05 \times 10^{-7}}{1}
$$

$$
J = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

**Step 2:** Polar section modulus:

$$
Z_p = \frac{\pi d^{3}}{16} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^{3}}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}}{16} = 4.241 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum shear stress at $r = d/2 = 0.03$ m:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{1591.55}{4.241 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\max} \approx 37.52 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 4:** Angle of twist per metre length (i.e., per $L = 1$ m):

$$
\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G J} = \frac{1591.55}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{1591.55}{101760} = 0.01564 \text{ rad/m}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\boxed{\frac{\theta}{L} \approx 0.896^{\circ} \text{ per metre}}
$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Section B]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of $80$ mm and an inner diameter of $50$ mm. It transmits $150$ kW at $250$ rpm. The maximum permissible shear stress is $60$ MPa, and $G = 80$ GPa.
**(a)** Check whether the shaft is safe in shear. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Find the angle of twist in degrees over a length of $2$ m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Given:**
- Outer diameter $D = 80$ mm $= 0.08$ m
- Inner diameter $d = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m
- Power $P = 150$ kW $= 150{,}000$ W
- Speed $N = 250$ rpm
- $\tau_{\text{permissible}} = 60$ MPa $= 60 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $G = 80$ GPa

**Step 1:** Calculate the torque:

$$
T = \frac{60 \, P}{2\pi N} = \frac{60 \times 150{,}000}{2\pi \times 250} = \frac{9{,}000{,}000}{500\pi} = \frac{18{,}000}{\pi}
$$

$$
T \approx 5729.58 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the polar moment of inertia:

$$
J = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32} = \frac{\pi ((0.08)^{4} - (0.05)^{4})}{32}
$$

$$
D^{4} = (0.08)^{4} = 4.096 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{4}
$$
$$
d^{4} = (0.05)^{4} = 6.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi (4.096 \times 10^{-5} - 6.25 \times 10^{-6})}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 3.471 \times 10^{-5}}{32}
$$

$$
J \approx 3.407 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum shear stress at $r = D/2 = 0.04$ m:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{5729.58 \times 0.04}{3.407 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{229.18}{3.407 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \approx 67.27 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4:** Compare with permissible stress:

$$
\tau_{\max} = 67.27 \text{ MPa} > \tau_{\text{permissible}} = 60 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{The shaft is NOT safe in shear. It is overstressed by } \approx 12.1\%}
$$

> **Practical Recommendation:** Either increase the outer diameter, reduce the inner diameter, or select a higher-grade material.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Step 1:** Use the torsion equation for angle of twist:

$$
\theta = \frac{T L}{G J}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $L = 2$ m, $T = 5729.58$ N·m, $G = 80 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $J = 3.407 \times 10^{-6}$ m⁴:

$$
\theta = \frac{5729.58 \times 2}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 3.407 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{11{,}459.16}{272{,}560}
$$

$$
\theta \approx 0.04204 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to degrees:

$$
\theta = 0.04204 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.04204 \times 57.2958
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 2.409^{\circ} \text{ over 2 m length}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Mixing up $N$ (rpm) with $\omega$ (rad/s) in the power formula. Use $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ directly with $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm to m:** $d = 60$ mm must be entered as $0.06$ m in SI formulas. **This is the #1 source of silly mistakes in KTU exams.**
> 3. **Degrees vs. Radians:** $\theta$ in $\dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ is in **radians**, not degrees. Always convert at the end if the question asks for degrees.
> 4. **Confusing $J$ and $I$:** $J$ is the *polar* moment (about longitudinal axis). $I$ is the *area moment* (about transverse axis). For a circle, $J = 2I_x = 2I_y = \pi d^4 / 32$.
> 5. **Hollow vs Solid in Comparisons:** When the problem says "for the same weight," it means **equal cross-sectional area**, not equal diameter.
> 6. **Forgetting the $r$ factor:** When computing $\tau$ at some *specific* radius, do not assume it is always at the outer surface — re-read the question.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

- **Torsion Equation (Master Formula):**

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

- **Polar Moment of Inertia:**
  - Solid: $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$
  - Hollow: $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$

- **Polar Section Modulus (Polar Modulus):**
  - Solid: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^{3}}{16}$
  - Hollow: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{16\,D}$

- **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ (at $r_{\max}$)

- **Angle of Twist:** $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ — answer in **radians** (convert to degrees by $\times 180/\pi$)

- **Power Equation:** $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ where $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m, $P$ in Watts

- **Strain Energy:** $U = \dfrac{T^{2} L}{2 G J}$

- **Hollow vs. Solid (Same Weight):**
  - Strength ratio: $\dfrac{1}{1 - k^{4}}$ where $k = d/D$
  - Stiffness ratio: same as above

- **Sign Convention:** Positive torque is conventionally applied such that its vector points **outward from the cross-section** (right-hand rule with thumb along axis, fingers curling in the direction of twist).

- **Units Quick Reference:**
  - $T$: N·m or kN·m
  - $J$: m⁴ or mm⁴
  - $\tau$: Pa or MPa
  - $G$: Pa or GPa (steel $\approx 80$ GPa)
  - $\theta$: rad (multiply by $57.2958$ for degrees)

- **Shear stress distribution is LINEAR** from $0$ at the centre to $\tau_{\max}$ at the outer surface. Always draw this in your KTU answer scripts to earn full marks.

- **Combined Loading (Preview for higher semesters):** When a shaft carries *both* bending moment $M$ and torque $T$, the **equivalent torque** concept uses $T_e = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$ and the **equivalent bending moment** uses $M_e = \dfrac{1}{2}(M + \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}})$. (Not in KTU Module 3, but a useful extension.)

---

*End of Module 3 — Torsion Notes*

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (shaft) about its longitudinal axis when subjected to equal and opposite torques (twisting moments) acting on parallel cross-sectional planes. The torsion of a circular shaft is a classic problem in **Strength of Materials** governed by Saint-Venant's semi-inverse method of analysis, which assumes a linear, axisymmetric warping of the cross-section.

For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter **D** and a **hollow circular shaft** with outer diameter **D** and inner diameter **d**, the fundamental torsion equation relating torque, shear stress, and angle of twist is universally expressed as:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

Where the terms are defined rigorously under KTU 2024 Scheme notation.

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **T** | Applied Torque | N·m |
| **J** | Polar Moment of Inertia | m⁴ |
| **τ** | Shear Stress at radius r | N/m² (Pa) |
| **r** | Radial distance from center | m |
| **G** | Modulus of Rigidity | N/m² (Pa) |
| **θ** | Angle of Twist | radians (rad) |
| **L** | Length of the shaft | m |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a wet cloth being wrung out.** When you twist a towel with both hands, the fibers inside shear against each other to accommodate the rotation. The resistance you feel is exactly what we call **torque**, and the internal shearing action in the fibers is the **shear stress** that the material experiences. A solid shaft is like a thick towel; a hollow shaft is like a tube — it is lighter but surprisingly difficult to twist because material is concentrated at the outside, where the "twisting action" is strongest.

In engineering practice, torsion of circular shafts is the governing design criterion for:
- **Drive shafts** of automobiles and trucks
- **Propeller shafts** of ships and aircraft
- **Rotating axles** in industrial machinery, turbines, and electric motors
- **Drill rods** and **transmission line shafts** in mining equipment

The reason circular cross-sections are overwhelmingly used is that they do **not warp** (remain plane) under pure torsion, making the stress analysis exact and elegant, unlike non-circular sections which require complex warping functions (membrane analogy / Prandtl's stress function).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Almost **90% of all power transmission in industry** uses circular shafts because of this clean, warping-free behavior under torsion.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Circular Shaft Torsion

For the classical torsion formula to be valid, the following assumptions must hold. The KTU board examiner expects these to be **memorized verbatim** for full marks.

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit.
2. The cross-section of the shaft is **circular** (solid or hollow) and remains **circular and plane** after twisting — there is no warping.
3. Cross-sections **rotate as rigid discs** about the longitudinal axis, so any cross-section rotates by an amount proportional to its distance from the fixed end.
4. The **radius of the shaft remains straight and undistorted** during twisting.
5. The **distance between any two cross-sections remains constant**; only relative rotation occurs.
6. Shafts are **initially straight** and the loading is **pure torsion** (no bending, axial, or transverse shear).
7. The shaft is subjected to a **constant torque** (uniform torsion) unless otherwise specified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the cross-section remain plane?** Because the geometry of a circle is rotationally symmetric — rotating a circle about its center by any angle produces an identical shape. This is the deep mathematical reason why the assumed displacement field works perfectly for circular sections.

## 2.2 Torsion Equation — The Master Relationship

The three-way torsion equation encapsulates **strength** (τ), **stiffness** (θ), and **geometry** (J) in a single elegant form:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

This single equation actually contains **three independent formulas** that students can extract by cross-multiplication:

### (a) Strength Formula (Maximum Shear Stress)

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r_{\max}}{J} = \frac{T}{Z_p}
$$

Where $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{r_{\max}}$ is the **Polar Section Modulus** (also called Torsional Section Modulus).

### (b) Stiffness Formula (Angle of Twist)

$$
\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}
$$

### (c) Deformation Compatibility

$$
\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU 2024 Exams:** Memorize this table column-wise. The examiner will often give numerical values and expect you to recall the correct formula and unit conversion. **Pressure units** in the exam are usually **N/mm² (MPa)** — not Pa. Always check and convert!

| # | Parameter | Solid Circular Shaft | Hollow Circular Shaft | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Polar Moment of Inertia $J$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$ | mm⁴ or m⁴ |
| 2 | Polar Section Modulus $Z_p$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{16}$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{16}\cdot\dfrac{D^{4}-d^{4}}{D}$ | mm³ or m³ |
| 3 | Outer Fiber Shear Stress $\tau_{\max}$ | $\dfrac{16T}{\pi D^{3}}$ | $\dfrac{16T \cdot D}{\pi(D^{4}-d^{4})}$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 4 | Inner Fiber Shear Stress (hollow) $\tau_{\min}$ | — | $\dfrac{16T \cdot d}{\pi(D^{4}-d^{4})}$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 5 | Angle of Twist $\theta$ | $\dfrac{32 T L}{\pi D^{4} G}$ | $\dfrac{32 T L}{\pi G (D^{4}-d^{4})}$ | radians |
| 6 | Power Transmitted $P$ | $\dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ where N is RPM | Same as solid | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power–Torque–Speed Relationship:** A frequently asked question type in KTU is to convert between power (kW), torque (N·m), and rotational speed (rpm). Master this:
> 
> $$
> P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T = \frac{60\,P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{9.549\,P}{N}
> $$
> 
> where **P** is in Watts, **N** in rpm, and **T** in N·m.

## 2.4 Hollow vs. Solid Shaft — The Strength-to-Weight Argument

> [!TIP]
> **Why do engineers prefer hollow shafts?** The shear stress in torsion is **maximum at the outer fiber** and **zero at the center** (because $\tau \propto r$). The central core of a solid shaft carries almost no stress! Removing the central material (making it hollow) **saves weight** while **preserving strength**, as long as the outer diameter is kept the same.

The strength advantage of a hollow shaft over a solid shaft of the **same weight per unit length** is captured by the ratio:

$$
\frac{\tau_{\text{solid}}}{\tau_{\text{hollow}}} = \left(1 - k^{4}\right)
$$

where $k = \dfrac{d}{D}$ is the **diameter ratio**. For $k = 0.5$, the hollow shaft is approximately **6.67 times stronger** than a solid shaft of the same weight — a staggering efficiency gain.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Torsion Equation (Saint-Venant's Theory)

The complete derivation proceeds in **three explicit phases**: kinematics (assumed displacement field), constitutive law (Hooke's Law in shear), and equilibrium (stress integration).

### Phase 1: Kinematic Assumption — Displacement Field

Let a point P in the cross-section have cylindrical coordinates $(r, \phi, x)$ with the shaft axis along $x$. Saint-Venant assumed the displacement components are:

$$
u_{x} = 0, \qquad u_{r} = 0, \qquad u_{\phi} = r \cdot \alpha(x)
$$

where $\alpha(x)$ is the **angle of twist per unit length** (twist rate), a function of $x$ only. In physical terms, a point at radius $r$ moves tangentially by an arc length $r \cdot \alpha(x) \, dx$.

### Phase 2: Shear Strain (from displacement derivatives)

The only non-zero shear strain in cylindrical coordinates is the shear in the $x$–$\phi$ plane:

$$
\gamma_{x\phi} = r \cdot \frac{d\alpha}{dx} = \frac{r \cdot \theta}{L}
$$

where $\theta$ is the **total angle of twist** over length $L$, so $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dx} = \dfrac{\theta}{L}$ is a constant for uniform torsion.

### Phase 3: Hooke's Law in Shear

For a linearly elastic material, the shear stress is directly proportional to shear strain:

$$
\tau_{x\phi} = G \cdot \gamma_{x\phi} = G \cdot r \cdot \frac{\theta}{L}
$$

This shows that **shear stress varies linearly with radial distance** — zero at the center, maximum at the outer fiber.

### Phase 4: Equilibrium — Definition of Torque

The internal torque $T$ at any cross-section is obtained by integrating the shear stress over the area, with each elemental area $dA$ contributing a moment arm $r$:

$$
T = \int_{A} \tau_{x\phi} \cdot r \, dA = \int_{A} G \cdot r \cdot \frac{\theta}{L} \cdot r \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_{A} r^{2}\, dA
$$

By definition, $\displaystyle J = \int_{A} r^{2}\, dA$ is the **polar moment of inertia** of the cross-section. Substituting:

$$
T = \frac{G \theta J}{L} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}
$$

### Phase 5: Combining with Stress Equation

From Phase 3: $\tau_{x\phi} = G \cdot r \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{L}$. Eliminating $\dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ using the previous result:

$$
\tau_{x\phi} = r \cdot \frac{T}{J} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r}
$$

Combining the two main relations yields the **Master Torsion Equation**:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Polar Moment of Inertia

### For Solid Circular Shaft (Diameter D)

$$
J = \int_{A} r^{2}\, dA = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{D/2} r^{2} \cdot r \, dr \, d\phi
$$

The full evaluation step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi \int_{0}^{D/2} r^{3}\, dr \\[6pt]
  &= \left[\phi\right]_{0}^{2\pi} \cdot \left[\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{D/2} \\[6pt]
  &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{(D/2)^{4}}{4} \\[6pt]
  &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{D^{4}}{64} \\[6pt]
  &= \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32}
\end{aligned}
$$

### For Hollow Circular Shaft (Outer D, Inner d)

The inner material is excluded, so we subtract its polar moment from the full disc:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} - \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** Notice that the polar moment of inertia uses $D^4$ (fourth power), whereas the area moment of inertia $I$ (used in bending) uses $D^4/64$. The key difference is $J = 2I$ for a solid circle — this relationship is true **only for circular cross-sections** and is a high-yield shortcut for KTU problems involving combined bending and torsion.

## 3.3 Derivation of Power Transmission Equation

The power transmitted by a rotating shaft is the rate of doing work. In one full revolution, the torque $T$ does work equal to $T \times 2\pi$ (work = force × distance = torque × angular displacement in radians). If the shaft rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the revolutions per second are $N/60$, so:

$$
P = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \times T = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \text{ Watts}
$$

Or in the more memorable form:

$$
P \,(\text{kW}) = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} = \frac{N T}{9550} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T \,(\text{N·m}) = \frac{9550 \cdot P\,(\text{kW})}{N\,(\text{rpm})}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this 5-step framework for every torsion problem** in the KTU exam. Examiners award step marks generously when you follow a structured approach.

| Step | Action | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1** | Identify given data; convert all units to **N, mm, MPa, N·m, rad** | Standardized quantities |
| **2** | Draw a **torque diagram** along the shaft length | Locate critical section |
| **3** | Compute **J** using $\dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$ (solid) or $\dfrac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ (hollow) | Polar moment in mm⁴ |
| **4** | Apply $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r_{\max}}{J}$ for strength check, or $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ for stiffness check | Required answer |
| **5** | Compare computed $\tau$ with allowable $\tau_{\text{allow}}$ and compute safety factor if required | Design verification |

### Worked Example Walkthrough (Standard KTU 3-Mark Type)

**Problem:** A solid circular shaft of diameter 60 mm transmits 75 kW at 200 rpm. Find the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft material. Take $G = 80$ GPa.

**Step 1 — Compute Torque from Power:**

$$
T = \frac{9550 \times 75}{200} = \frac{716{,}250}{200} = 3581.25 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to N·mm (to match mm units):**

$$
T = 3581.25 \times 10^{3} = 3{,}581{,}250 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (60)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 12{,}960{,}000}{32} = 1{,}272{,}345 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{3{,}581{,}250 \times 30}{1{,}272{,}345} = 84.4 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 84.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Marking Scheme for this problem:** Step 1 = 1 mark, Step 2 = 0.5 mark, Step 3 = 1 mark, Step 4 = 0.5 mark. **Showing the formula before substitution** is mandatory for full marks.

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Stress Distribution in a Solid Circular Shaft

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **3D physical state**, the **2D cross-sectional stress distribution**, and the **stress variation profile** of a solid circular shaft under torsion.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["3D SHAFT UNDER TORQUE T"] --> B["CROSS SECTION VIEW"]
    A --> C["AXIAL VIEW OF TWIST"]
    B --> D["SHEAR STRESSES ON ELEMENT"]
    D --> D1["Tangential direction on outer rim"]
    D --> D2["Zero stress at center r equals 0"]
    D --> D3["Maximum stress at outer fiber r equals R"]
    C --> E["Angle of twist theta between ends"]
    E --> E1["Stiffness G times J"]
    B --> F["LINEAR STRESS VARIATION"]
    F --> F1["Slope equals T over J"]
    F --> F2["Intercept passes through origin"]
    F --> F3["Tau max at r equals R"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** The block `LINEAR STRESS VARIATION` represents the famous result that $\tau$ is **zero at the center** and **increases linearly** to a **maximum at the outer fiber** $R$. The slope of this line in the $r$-$\tau$ plane equals the **torque per unit polar stiffness**, $T/J$.

## 4.2 Shear Stress Distribution in a Hollow Circular Shaft

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["HOLLOW CIRCULAR CROSS SECTION"] --> B["Outer diameter D"]
    A --> C["Inner diameter d"]
    B --> D["Shear stress tau max at outer fiber"]
    C --> E["Shear stress tau min at inner fiber"]
    D --> F["Direction: tangential to circumference"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["BOTH stresses are NON ZERO"]
    G --> H["Linear variation of tau with r"]
    H --> I["NO zero-stress region inside"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference from Solid Shaft:** Unlike the solid shaft where stress is zero at the center, the **hollow shaft has non-zero stress at the inner fiber**. This is because every bit of material in the annulus actively participates in carrying the torque. This is why hollow shafts are more **material-efficient** than solid shafts.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Torsion Problem Solving Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["INPUT: Torque T, Length L, Geometry D and d, Material G"] --> B["COMPUTE J"]
    B --> B1["Solid: pi D4 by 32"]
    B --> B2["Hollow: pi D4 minus d4 by 32"]
    B --> C["DECISION: STRENGTH or STIFFNESS"]
    C -->|Strength| D["COMPUTE tau max"]
    C -->|Stiffness| E["COMPUTE theta"]
    D --> D1["tau equals T times R over J"]
    D --> F["Compare with tau allow"]
    E --> E1["theta equals T L over G J"]
    E --> G["Compare with theta allow"]
    F --> H["DESIGN VERIFIED or REVISE"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["OUTPUT: SAFE DESIGN"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this flow chart in the exam:** Begin every torsion problem by writing the input quantities, computing $J$, then branching to either the strength or stiffness sub-routine based on what the question asks. Examiners love systematic approaches and award bonus method marks.

## 4.4 Angle of Twist Accumulation Diagram (Composite Shaft)

For a shaft with multiple segments (different diameters or different torques), the total angle of twist is the **algebraic sum** of each segment's contribution. This is critical for KTU 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["SEGMENT 1: D1, T1, L1"] -->|"theta 1 equals T1 L1 over G J1"| B["ACCUMULATOR"]
    C["SEGMENT 2: D2, T2, L2"] -->|"theta 2 equals T2 L2 over G J2"| B
    D["SEGMENT 3: D3, T3, L3"] -->|"theta 3 equals T3 L3 over G J3"| B
    B --> E["TOTAL: theta total equals sum of theta i"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT: Total angle of twist"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention in Multi-Segment Problems:** When using the **right-hand rule** (curl fingers in the direction of the applied torque, thumb points in the positive $x$-direction), torques producing twists in the **same sense** are added, and those producing twists in the **opposite sense** are subtracted. KTU examiners often test this with a 14-mark problem featuring 2 or 3 flanges/gears.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

### Question A.1 — 3 Marks

**Q:** State the assumptions made in the theory of pure torsion of a circular shaft.

**Model Answer:**

The theory of pure torsion of a circular shaft is based on the following Saint-Venant assumptions:

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and **linearly elastic**, obeying Hooke's law up to the proportional limit.
2. The cross-section of the shaft is **circular** (solid or hollow) and remains **circular, plane, and undistorted** after the application of torque — i.e., there is **no warping** of the cross-section.
3. Cross-sections **rotate as rigid discs** about the longitudinal axis; the radius of any cross-section remains straight and unchanged in length.
4. The **distance between any two cross-sections remains unchanged**; only relative rotation (twist) occurs along the length.
5. The **twist per unit length is constant** for uniform torsion, and the shaft is initially straight and unloaded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Listing only 3 assumptions earns **partial credit (2 of 3 marks)**. The KTU 2024 marking scheme requires **at least 5 assumptions** for full marks. Use bullet points, not a paragraph.

### Question A.2 — 3 Marks

**Q:** Derive the expression for the polar moment of inertia of a hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$.

**Model Answer:**

The polar moment of inertia is defined as:

$$
J = \int_{A} r^{2} \, dA
$$

For a hollow shaft, considering an elemental ring of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$, the elemental area is $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^{2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^{3} \, dr \\[4pt]
  &= 2\pi \left[\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{d/2}^{D/2} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi}{2}\left[\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{4} - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4}\right] \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the polar moment of inertia of a hollow circular shaft is $\boxed{J = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution:** Statement of integral = 1 mark, Setting up the limits and $dA$ = 1 mark, Final simplification = 1 mark.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

> **[KTU University Exam - 14 Marks Each — Module 3 Choice Pattern]**

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Strength + Stiffness Composite Problem

**Q:** A solid circular shaft of diameter **80 mm** transmits a torque of **12 kN·m** over a length of **2 m**. The shaft is made of steel with $G = 80$ GPa and allowable shear stress $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 60$ MPa.

**(a)** Determine the maximum shear stress induced in the shaft and check the design for safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same shaft is replaced by a **hollow circular shaft** of the **same weight per unit length** and **same material**, having an inner diameter equal to **0.6 times the outer diameter**, determine:
   - The outer diameter of the hollow shaft.
   - The maximum shear stress in the hollow shaft.
   - The percentage reduction in maximum shear stress compared to the solid shaft. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Strength Check on Solid Shaft

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $T = 12$ kN·m $= 12 \times 10^6$ N·mm, $L = 2$ m, $G = 80$ GPa, $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 60$ MPa.

**Step 1: Compute Polar Moment of Inertia**

$$
J = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40{,}960{,}000}{32} = 4{,}021{,}238 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Maximum Shear Stress**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 12 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times (80)^{3}} = \frac{192 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 512{,}000} = 119.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with Allowable Stress**

$$
\tau_{\max} = 119.4 \text{ MPa} > \tau_{\text{allow}} = 60 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft is **NOT safe**. It must be redesigned (e.g., increase diameter or use hollow shaft). This is the **critical flaw in the design** that the hollow replacement aims to fix.

#### Part (b) — Hollow Shaft of Same Weight

**Step 1: Equate Weights (equal cross-sectional area)**

For equal weight per unit length, the cross-sectional area of the hollow shaft must equal that of the solid shaft:

$$
A_{\text{hollow}} = A_{\text{solid}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_{h}^{2} - d^{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}(80)^{2}
$$

With $d = 0.6 D_{h}$:

$$
D_{h}^{2} - (0.6 D_{h})^{2} = 80^{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad D_{h}^{2}(1 - 0.36) = 6400
$$

$$
0.64 \, D_{h}^{2} = 6400 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad D_{h}^{2} = 10{,}000 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad D_{h} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

Therefore, the inner diameter is $d = 0.6 \times 100 = 60$ mm.

**Step 2: Compute Polar Moment of Inertia of Hollow Shaft**

$$
J_h = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D_{h}^{4} - d^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(100^{4} - 60^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{32}(100{,}000{,}000 - 12{,}960{,}000) = \frac{\pi \times 87{,}040{,}000}{32} = 8{,}545{,}910 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Maximum Shear Stress in Hollow Shaft**

$$
\tau_{\max, h} = \frac{T \cdot D_h}{2 J_h} = \frac{12 \times 10^{6} \times 100}{2 \times 8{,}545{,}910} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{17{,}091{,}820} = 70.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the hollow shaft, while **slightly larger** in outer diameter (100 vs. 80 mm), has a **lower maximum shear stress** (70.2 MPa vs. 119.4 MPa) at the **same weight**. This is the textbook example of why hollow shafts dominate engineering design.

**Step 4: Compute Percentage Reduction**

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{\tau_{\max, \text{solid}} - \tau_{\max, \text{hollow}}}{\tau_{\max, \text{solid}}} \times 100 = \frac{119.4 - 70.2}{119.4} \times 100 = 41.2\%
$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Parameter | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Outer Diameter | 80 mm | 100 mm |
| Inner Diameter | 0 (solid) | 60 mm |
| Polar Moment $J$ | 4.02 × 10⁶ mm⁴ | 8.55 × 10⁶ mm⁴ |
| $\tau_{\max}$ | 119.4 MPa | 70.2 MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors (KTU Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting to equate cross-sectional areas** when "same weight" is mentioned. The hollow shaft must have the same material volume per unit length, which means same area.
> 2. **Using $D/2$ instead of $D$ in the shear stress formula** for the hollow shaft: $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r_{\max}}{J} = \dfrac{T \cdot D/2}{J}$. Many students write $\tau = TD/J$ which is dimensionally wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up the units of torque**: $T = 12$ kN·m must be converted to $12 \times 10^6$ N·mm **before** substituting into the formula. Mixing kN·m with mm gives a 1000× error.

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Composite Multi-Segment Shaft

**Q:** A composite shaft consists of two segments rigidly joined at flange B. Segment AB is a **solid steel shaft** of diameter **50 mm** and length **1.5 m**. Segment BC is a **hollow bronze shaft** of outer diameter **60 mm**, inner diameter **40 mm**, and length **2 m**. Torques of **6 kN·m** and **4 kN·m** are applied at the free ends A and C in **opposite rotational senses**. The shaft is fixed at the mid-support. Take $G_{\text{steel}} = 80$ GPa and $G_{\text{bronze}} = 40$ GPa.

**(a)** Draw the torque diagram and find the maximum shear stress in each segment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the angle of twist of end A relative to the fixed support and relative to end C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Torque Diagram and Maximum Shear Stress

**Step 1: Compute Polar Moments of Inertia**

For segment AB (solid steel):
$$
J_{AB} = \frac{\pi (50)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 6{,}250{,}000}{32} = 613{,}592 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

For segment BC (hollow bronze):
$$
J_{BC} = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(60^{4} - 40^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{32}(12{,}960{,}000 - 2{,}560{,}000) = \frac{\pi \times 10{,}400{,}000}{32} = 1{,}021{,}017 \text{ mm}^{4}
$$

**Step 2: Internal Torque Analysis**

Let the fixed support be at the mid-point M. From end A: $T_{AM} = 6$ kN·m. From end C: $T_{MC} = 4$ kN·m in the opposite sense.

Internal torque in segment AB: $T_{AB} = 6$ kN·m
Internal torque in segment BC: $T_{BC} = 4$ kN·m

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Stress in Each Segment**

For AB (steel, $r = 25$ mm):
$$
\tau_{AB} = \frac{T_{AB} \cdot r_{AB}}{J_{AB}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{6} \times 25}{613{,}592} = 244.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

For BC (bronze, $r_{\text{outer}} = 30$ mm):
$$
\tau_{BC} = \frac{T_{BC} \cdot r_{\text{outer}}}{J_{BC}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6} \times 30}{1{,}021{,}017} = 117.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

#### Part (b) — Angle of Twist

**Step 1: Angle of Twist of Segment AB**

$$
\theta_{AB} = \frac{T_{AB} \cdot L_{AB}}{G_{s} \cdot J_{AB}} = \frac{6 \times 10^{6} \times 1500}{80{,}000 \times 613{,}592} = 0.1833 \text{ rad} = 10.5^{\circ}
$$

**Step 2: Angle of Twist of Segment BC**

$$
\theta_{BC} = \frac{T_{BC} \cdot L_{BC}}{G_{b} \cdot J_{BC}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6} \times 2000}{40{,}000 \times 1{,}021{,}017} = 0.1959 \text{ rad} = 11.22^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3: Relative Twists**

- Angle of twist of A relative to fixed support M: $\phi_A = 0.1833$ rad $= 10.5°$
- Angle of twist of C relative to fixed support M: $\phi_C = 0.1959$ rad $= 11.22°$
- Angle of twist of A relative to C: $\phi_{A/C} = \phi_A + \phi_C = 0.3792$ rad $= 21.72°$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Composite Shaft Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing units of G**: $G$ is given as $80$ GPa $= 80{,}000$ N/mm², **not** $80$ N/mm². Converting to consistent N/mm² units is critical.
> 2. **Sign errors in the torque diagram**: When torques are applied in opposite senses, students often forget to assign proper signs using the right-hand rule. This cascades into wrong $\theta$ values.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** (or vice versa) for the final answer. KTU expects answers in **radians** unless explicitly asked otherwise.
> 4. **Adding angles when they should be subtracted**: If the support is at one end (not the middle), the total twist between the two free ends is the algebraic sum with proper sign.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print This and Read Before the Exam!**

- **Master Torsion Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — memorize this as **"T J equals tau r equals G theta L"**.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia:**
  - Solid: $J = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$
  - Hollow: $J = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$
- **Polar Section Modulus:** $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{r_{\max}} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{16}$ (solid) — useful for direct substitution.
- **Maximum Shear Stress Formula (Short Form):** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi D^{3}}$ for solid shafts — memorize this for time-saving.
- **Power–Torque Relationship:** $T = \dfrac{9550 \cdot P\,(\text{kW})}{N\,(\text{rpm})}$ in N·m — or $T = \dfrac{60 P}{2 \pi N}$ in N·m if $P$ is in watts.
- **Hollow Shaft Advantage:** $\tau_{\text{hollow}} = \tau_{\text{solid}} \times (1 - k^4)$ for equal weight, where $k = d/D$. Lower shear stress at the **same weight** = better design.
- **Stiffness Rule:** A shaft is **stiffer** in torsion if $GJ$ is larger. Hollow shafts have a higher $J$ for the same weight, hence stiffer.
- **Multi-Segment Rule:** $\theta_{\text{total}} = \sum_i \dfrac{T_i L_i}{G_i J_i}$ — use **signs** based on right-hand rule.
- **Material Property Table (Standard Values for KTU 2024):**

| Material | $G$ (GPa) | Typical $\tau_{\text{allow}}$ (MPa) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mild Steel | 80 | 60 – 80 |
| Cast Iron | 40 – 50 | 20 – 30 |
| Bronze | 40 – 45 | 30 – 50 |
| Aluminum Alloy | 25 – 28 | 50 – 90 |

- **Validity Check:** The torsion theory is valid only when the **shaft is straight**, **circular**, and the **material is within the elastic limit**. Any deviation (non-circular, plastic deformation) requires a different approach (e.g., membrane analogy, Prandtl's stress function).
- **Strain Energy in Torsion (Bonus):** $U = \dfrac{T^{2} L}{2 G J}$ — useful for impact loading and sudden torque application problems.
- **Common Conversion Factors:** 1 N/mm² = 1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa; 1 kN·m = 10⁶ N·mm; 1° = $\pi/180$ rad ≈ 0.01745 rad.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy for KTU 2024 Torsion Module:**
> 1. **Always draw a free-body diagram and torque diagram** at the start of a 14-mark problem — earns at least 1 mark.
> 2. **Show all unit conversions explicitly** in the first step.
> 3. **Box the final answer** in a clear mathematical expression.
> 4. **State assumptions** if the problem is a "design a shaft" type.
> 5. **Mention the safety factor** at the end of strength-based problems: $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{allow}}}{\tau_{\max}}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Transmitted by a Shaft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Transmitted by a Shaft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaft (KTU Definition):** A shaft is a rotating machine element, usually of circular cross-section, used to transmit **power, torque, and rotational motion** from one component (e.g., a motor or engine) to another (e.g., a gear, pulley, or propeller).

**Power Transmitted by a Shaft** is defined as the rate at which mechanical rotational work is transferred through a rotating member. When a shaft rotates under the action of an externally applied twisting moment (torque, $T$), it performs rotational work at an angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) or rotational speed $N$ (revolutions per minute, **rpm**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Power** is fundamentally a *scalar* product of torque and angular velocity. It is the rotational analogue of the linear relationship $P = F \times v$ (Force × Linear Velocity).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are using a **hand-cranked screwdriver** to drive a screw into wood.

- Your hand applies a **force** at a distance (creating a **torque**).
- The screwdriver rotates at a certain **angular speed**.
- The "work" being done is pushing the screw into the wood.
- The **rate** at which you do this work is the **power transmitted** through the screwdriver (the shaft).

Similarly, in a factory:
- An **electric motor** spins a shaft at a high rotational speed.
- The shaft carries this rotation to a **gearbox** or **pump**.
- The torque multiplied by the rotational speed gives the **power** flowing through the system.

**Real-World Insight:** The same shaft running faster can transmit *more power* even if the torque is small — this is why high-speed turbo shafts in jet engines transmit enormous power with relatively small diameters.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Power ($P$):** Watts ($\text{W}$), Kilowatts ($\text{kW} = 10^3 \text{W}$), or Horsepower ($\text{HP} = 745.7 \text{W}$)
> - **Torque ($T$):** Newton-metre ($\text{N·m}$)
> - **Angular Velocity ($\omega$):** Radians per second ($\text{rad/s}$)
> - **Rotational Speed ($N$):** **Revolutions per minute (rpm)**
> - **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** MPa or $\text{N/mm}^2$ (where $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{N/m}^2 = 1 \text{N/mm}^2$)
> - **Shaft Diameter ($d$ or $D$):** mm or m (consistent throughout a problem)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — How Power Flows Through a Shaft

The transmission of power through a shaft follows a clean logical chain:

1. **An input source** (motor, engine, turbine) applies a **Torque ($T$)** to one end of the shaft.
2. This torque creates a **twisting moment** distributed uniformly along the shaft length (for pure torsion).
3. The shaft rotates at an **angular velocity $\omega$** (or speed $N$ in **rpm**).
4. At every cross-section, the shaft material experiences a **shear stress $\tau$** (torsional shear).
5. The product of the applied torque and the angular velocity gives the **power transmitted**.

**Core Logic:**
- Power = Rate of doing work
- For rotation: $P = \dfrac{\text{Work done per revolution} \times \text{Number of revolutions per second}}{1}$
- Work per revolution = $T \times 2\pi$ (torque × angular distance)
- Revolutions per second = $N/60$
- Therefore: $P = T \times 2\pi \times \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table** — these are the **high-yield formulas** that appear in 80%+ of KTU exam questions on this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|-------|
| 1 | Power (in terms of torque & angular velocity) | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | $T$ = Torque, $\omega$ = angular velocity | $\text{W}$ if $T$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 2 | Power (in terms of torque & rpm) | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | $N$ = speed in rpm, $T$ in N·m | $\text{W}$ |
| 3 | Torque from Power (in SI) | $T = \dfrac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N}$ | $P$ in Watts | N·m |
| 4 | Torque from Power (in kW) | $T = \dfrac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{2 \pi N}$ | $P$ in kW | N·m |
| 5 | Shear Stress (Solid Shaft) | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3}$ | $d$ = shaft diameter | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 6 | Shear Stress (Hollow Shaft) | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T \cdot D}{\pi \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}$ | $D$ = outer dia, $d$ = inner dia | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 7 | Torque capacity (Solid) | $T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot d^3$ | — | N·mm |
| 8 | Torque capacity (Hollow) | $T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot \dfrac{D^4 - d^4}{D}$ | — | N·mm |
| 9 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid) | $J = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32}$ | $d$ = diameter | $\text{mm}^4$ |
| 10 | Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow) | $J = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ | $D$, $d$ = outer, inner | $\text{mm}^4$ |
| 11 | Shaft Design (Solid, for $\tau$ and $P$) | $d^3 = \dfrac{16}{\pi \cdot \tau} \cdot \dfrac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{2 \pi N}$ | derived combined form | $\text{mm}^3$ |
| 12 | Shaft Design (Hollow, for $\tau$ and $P$) | $d^3 = \dfrac{16 \cdot D}{\pi \cdot \tau \cdot (D^4 - d^4)} \cdot \dfrac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{2 \pi N}$ | — | $\text{mm}^3$ |

**Engineering Utility:** This formula system is the backbone of mechanical design for **drive shafts in automobiles, propeller shafts in ships, rotating axles in turbines, and spindle shafts in machine tools**. Every rotating machine is sized using these exact relations.

### 2.3 The Torsion Equation (Foundation of All Shaft Design)

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ (max at outer surface, $r = R$)
- $T$ = Applied torque
- $r$ = Radial distance from shaft centerline
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section

**Why this matters for power transmission:** The torque $T$ in the power equation is the *same torque* used in the torsion equation. So once you know the power and speed (giving you $T$), you can immediately find the required diameter for a given allowable shear stress.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power Transmitted in Terms of Torque and Angular Velocity

**Step 1 — Work Done in One Revolution**

Consider a torque $T$ applied to a shaft. In one complete revolution, the point of application of the torque moves through an angular distance of $2\pi$ radians.

Work done in one revolution:

$$W = T \times 2\pi$$

**Step 2 — Number of Revolutions per Second**

If the shaft makes $N$ revolutions per minute, then in one second it makes:

$$n = \frac{N}{60} \text{ revolutions per second}$$

**Step 3 — Work Done per Second = Power**

$$P = W \times n = T \times 2\pi \times \frac{N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \text{ Watts}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Angular Velocity Form**

Since $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ rad/s, we can substitute:

$$P = T \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = T \cdot \omega$$

$$\boxed{P = T \cdot \omega \text{ Watts}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque Expressed in Terms of Power (Two Useful Forms)

**Form A — When $P$ is in Watts and $T$ is in N·m:**

Starting from $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$:

$$T = \frac{P \cdot 60}{2 \pi N} \text{ N·m}$$

**Form B — When $P$ is in kW (commonly used in KTU problems):**

Since $1 \text{ kW} = 10^3 \text{ W}$:

$$P \text{ (in W)} = P \text{ (in kW)} \times 10^3$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{60 \times P \times 10^3}{2 \pi N} \text{ N·m}$$

**Simplified Numerical Form (Memorize this):**

$$T \approx 9550 \times \frac{P \text{ (kW)}}{N \text{ (rpm)}} \text{ N·m}$$

*(Derivation of 9550: $\dfrac{60 \times 10^3}{2\pi} = \dfrac{60000}{6.2832} \approx 9549.3$)*

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Diameter of a Solid Shaft in Terms of Power and Speed

**Step 1 — Get the Torque from Power and Speed**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{2 \pi N} \text{ N·m} = \frac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^6}{2 \pi N} \text{ N·mm}$$

(Converting N·m to N·mm by multiplying by $10^3$ mm/m.)

**Step 2 — Apply the Torsion Equation for a Solid Shaft**

For a solid circular shaft, the torsion formula is:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r}$$

For a solid shaft of diameter $d$, the polar moment of inertia is:

$$J = \frac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32}$$

and the maximum radius is:

$$r = \frac{d}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{T}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \frac{\tau}{d/2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T$**

$$T = \frac{\pi \cdot d^4}{32} \times \frac{\tau \cdot 2}{d} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^3 \cdot \tau}{16}$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot d^3}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions for $T$**

$$\frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot d^3 = \frac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^6}{2 \pi N}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $d^3$**

$$d^3 = \frac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^6}{2 \pi N} \times \frac{16}{\pi \cdot \tau}$$

$$\boxed{d^3 = \frac{16 \times 60 \cdot P \cdot 10^6}{2 \pi^2 \cdot \tau \cdot N} = \frac{16 \times 60 \cdot P}{2 \pi^2 \cdot \tau \cdot N} \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3}$$

*(with $P$ in kW, $\tau$ in N/mm², $N$ in rpm)*

Or equivalently, keeping $P$ in W:

$$d^3 = \frac{16}{\pi \cdot \tau} \times \frac{60 \cdot P}{2 \pi N} \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^3$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Diameter of a Hollow Shaft

For a hollow shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

**Polar moment of inertia:**

$$J = \frac{\pi \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$$

**Torsion equation:**

$$T = \tau \cdot \frac{J}{r} = \tau \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot (D^4 - d^4)}{32} \times \frac{2}{D} = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D}$$

**Equating with the torque from power:**

$$\frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D} = \frac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^6}{2 \pi N}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{D^4 - d^4}{D} = \frac{16 \times 60 \cdot P \cdot 10^6}{2 \pi^2 \cdot \tau \cdot N}}$$

Solving for $D$ requires either:
- A given ratio $D/d$ (or $d/D$), or
- A given inner diameter $d$, in which case we solve for $D$ directly.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example (Typical KTU-Numerical Type)

> **Problem:** A solid circular shaft transmits **$P = 75 \text{ kW}$** at **$N = 240 \text{ rpm}$**. The allowable shear stress in the material is **$\tau = 60 \text{ MPa}$**. Determine:
> (a) The torque transmitted.
> (b) The required shaft diameter.
> (c) The maximum shear stress if the diameter is reduced to **$d = 45 \text{ mm}$**.

**Solution (a): Torque**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 75 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 240}$$

$$T = \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{1507.96} \approx 2984.2 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T \approx 2.984 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Solution (b): Required Diameter**

$$T = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot d^3$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot \tau} = \frac{16 \times 2.984 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60}$$

$$d^3 = \frac{47.744 \times 10^6}{188.496} = 253{,}280.6 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (253{,}280.6)^{1/3} \approx 63.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $d = 64 \text{ mm}$ (next standard size).**

**Solution (c): Shear Stress at $d = 45 \text{ mm}$**

$$\tau = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2.984 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (45)^3}$$

$$\tau = \frac{47.744 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 91{,}125} = \frac{47.744 \times 10^6}{286{,}278.9}$$

$$\tau \approx 166.76 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 166.76 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Note:** This exceeds the allowable stress of 60 MPa — the shaft would fail. Hence the safe diameter is at least 64 mm.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Transmission Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of how power flows through a shaft system, showing the relationship between the input source, torque, speed, shaft, and the output device.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InputA[INPUT POWER SOURCE\nElectric Motor or Engine] --> TorqueBlock[APPLIED TORQUE T\nN·m]
    TorqueBlock --> ShaftBlock[ROTATING SHAFT\nCircular Cross Section]
    ShaftBlock --> SpeedBlock[ANGULAR VELOCITY\nomega rad per s\nN in rpm]
    SpeedBlock --> OutputBlock[OUTPUT DEVICE\nGearbox, Pump, Propeller]
    ShaftBlock --> StressBlock[TORSIONAL SHEAR STRESS\ntau equals T r over J]
    StressBlock --> SafetyCheck{tau less than tau allowable?}
    SafetyCheck -- YES --> SafeShaft[SAFE DESIGN\nWithin Elastic Limit]
    SafetyCheck -- NO --> FailShaft[DESIGN FAILURE\nShaft Yields or Fractures]
    TorqueBlock --> PowerEq[POWER EQUATION\nP equals 2 pi N T over 60]
    SpeedBlock --> PowerEq
    PowerEq --> ResultPower[TRANSMITTED POWER\nP in Watts or kW]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cross-Sectional Stress Distribution in a Solid Shaft

This diagram shows how the torsional shear stress varies across the cross-section of a solid shaft — zero at the center and maximum at the outer surface.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Center[SHAFT CENTERLINE\nRadius r equals 0\nShear Stress tau equals 0]
    MidRadius[INTERMEDIATE RADIUS\ntau proportional to r\nLinear variation]
    OuterSurf[OUTER SURFACE\nRadius r equals d over 2\nMAXIMUM Shear Stress tau max]
    Center --> MidRadius
    MidRadius --> OuterSurf
    OuterSurf --> NoteBlock[TAKEAWAY\nAll material outside contributes\nto torque capacity\nInner core carries minimal stress]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Design Procedure for a Solid Shaft

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([START\nGiven: P, N, tau allowable]) --> Step1[Step 1\nCompute Torque\nT equals 60 P times 10 cubed over 2 pi N]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2\nApply Torsion Formula\nT equals pi over 16 times tau times d cubed]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3\nSolve for d cubed\nd cubed equals 16 T over pi tau]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4\nTake Cube Root\nd equals d cubed power 1 over 3]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5\nAdopt Standard Size\nd standard greater than d calculated]
    Step5 --> Verify[VERIFY\ntau actual less than tau allowable]
    Verify --> End([END\nShaft Sized Successfully])
```

### 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (for clarity)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Shaft-Pulley Arrangement (described in text form for visualization):**
>
> - A **horizontal cylindrical shaft** of diameter $d$ is supported between two bearings.
> - On one end, a **driving pulley** (or motor coupling) applies torque $T$ in one rotational direction.
> - On the other end, a **driven pulley** (or load) reacts with an equal and opposite torque $-T$ (Newton's third law).
> - Between the two ends, **every cross-section experiences the same torque magnitude** $T$ (pure torsion, no bending if load is purely tangential).
> - The shaft surface shows **torsional shear stress $\tau$** which is maximum at the outer fibers and zero at the centerline.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap
