### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Note to students:** Part A questions in KTU 2024 scheme tests **definition + brief explanation**. Keep answers **3–4 lines** with the **key formula**.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define power transmitted by a shaft. Derive the relationship between power, torque, and rotational speed.**

**Model Answer:**

Power transmitted by a shaft is the rate at which rotational work is transferred through the shaft.

The torque $T$ acting through one revolution does work $W = T \times 2\pi$ joules.

If the shaft makes $N$ revolutions per minute, revolutions per second = $N/60$.

$$\text{Power } P = W \times \dfrac{N}{60} = T \times 2\pi \times \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \text{ Watts}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] State the torsion equation. How is it used in the design of a power transmission shaft?**

**Model Answer:**

The torsion equation is $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$.

For a solid circular shaft, $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ and $r = d/2$, so the equation becomes $T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \tau \cdot d^3$.

**Use in design:** Given the power $P$ and speed $N$, the torque is first computed as $T = \dfrac{60 P \times 10^3}{2\pi N}$. Substituting this into the torsion formula yields the required diameter $d$ for the allowable shear stress.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question is **14 marks**, usually split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE full question** out of an internal choice pair.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **$75 \text{ mm}$** transmits **$90 \text{ kW}$** at **$200 \text{ rpm}$**. Calculate:
- (i) The torque transmitted by the shaft.
- (ii) The torsional shear stress developed in the shaft material.

**(b)** A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of **$60 \text{ mm}$** and inner diameter of **$40 \text{ mm}$**. Compare its torque-transmitting capacity with that of a solid shaft of the same material, weight, and allowable shear stress. Which shaft is more efficient, and by what percentage?

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Part (i) — Torque:**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 90 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 200}$$

$$T = \frac{5{,}400{,}000}{1256.64} \approx 4297.2 \text{ N·m} = 4.297 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Part (ii) — Torsional Shear Stress:**

$$\tau = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^3} = \frac{16 \times 4.297 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (75)^3}$$

$$\tau = \frac{68.755 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 421{,}875} = \frac{68.755 \times 10^6}{1{,}325{,}362.5}$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 51.88 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 51.88 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Areas (Weight Equivalence):**

Solid shaft: $A_s = \dfrac{\pi D_s^2}{4}$

Hollow shaft: $A_h = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (60^2 - 40^2)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (3600 - 1600)}{4} = \dfrac{2000\pi}{4} = 500\pi \text{ mm}^2$

For equal weight (and length): $A_s = A_h$

$$\frac{\pi D_s^2}{4} = 500\pi \implies D_s^2 = 2000 \implies D_s = \sqrt{2000} \approx 44.72 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Torque Capacities (same $\tau$):**

Solid shaft torque:

$$T_s = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot D_s^3 = \frac{\pi}{16} \times \tau \times (44.72)^3$$

Hollow shaft torque:

$$T_h = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D} = \frac{\pi}{16} \times \tau \times \frac{60^4 - 40^4}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Comparison:**

$$T_s \propto (44.72)^3 = 89{,}442.7$$

$$T_h \propto \frac{60^4 - 40^4}{60} = \frac{12{,}960{,}000 - 2{,}560{,}000}{60} = \frac{10{,}400{,}000}{60} = 173{,}333.3$$

**Step 4 — Ratio and Percentage:**

$$\frac{T_h}{T_s} = \frac{173{,}333.3}{89{,}442.7} \approx 1.938$$

**Result:** The hollow shaft transmits **$1.938$ times** (or **93.8% more**) torque than a solid shaft of the **same weight and material**.

$$\boxed{\text{Hollow shaft is more efficient by } 93.8\%}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q-B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** A line shaft running at **$300 \text{ rpm}$** is to transmit **$150 \text{ kW}$**. The allowable shear stress for the shaft material is **$70 \text{ MPa}$**. Determine the required diameter if:
- (i) The shaft is **solid**.
- (ii) The shaft is **hollow with a diameter ratio $D/d = 1.6$**.

**(b)** Define the term **polar moment of inertia**. Why is the **polar section modulus** ($Z_p = J/r$) a more direct design parameter than $J$ alone?

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the Torque:**

$$T = \frac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 150 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 300} = \frac{9{,}000{,}000}{1884.96} \approx 4774.6 \text{ N·m}$$

$$T = 4.7746 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Solid Shaft Diameter:**

$$T = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot d^3$$

$$d^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot \tau} = \frac{16 \times 4.7746 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 70} = \frac{76.39 \times 10^6}{219.91} = 347{,}310 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = (347{,}310)^{1/3} \approx 70.30 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\text{solid}} \approx 70.3 \text{ mm} \implies \text{adopt } 72 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Hollow Shaft Diameter (D/d = 1.6):**

Let $d = x$, $D = 1.6x$.

$$T = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{D^4 - d^4}{D} = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{(1.6x)^4 - x^4}{1.6x}$$

$$T = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{6.5536 x^4 - x^4}{1.6x} = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot \frac{5.5536 x^4}{1.6x} = \frac{\pi}{16} \cdot \tau \cdot 3.471 x^3$$

So:

$$x^3 = \frac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot \tau \cdot 3.471} = \frac{16 \times 4.7746 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 70 \times 3.471}$$

$$x^3 = \frac{76.39 \times 10^6}{763.30} = 100{,}079 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$x = (100{,}079)^{1/3} \approx 46.41 \text{ mm}$$

$$D = 1.6 x \approx 74.26 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{d_{\text{inner}} \approx 46.4 \text{ mm}, \quad D_{\text{outer}} \approx 74.3 \text{ mm}}$$

**Observation:** The hollow shaft has a slightly larger outer diameter but **much less weight** (since material is removed from the low-stress center).

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Polar moment of inertia ($J$):** It is a geometrical property of a cross-section that measures its resistance to twisting (torsional deformation). It is defined as the second moment of area about the polar axis (centerline) of the cross-section.

For a circle: $J = \int r^2 \, dA = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ (solid), $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ (hollow).

**Why $Z_p = J/r$ is more useful:**

The torsion equation is $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$, which can be rewritten as $\tau = \dfrac{T}{J/r} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$.

- $J$ depends only on the cross-section geometry.
- $r$ (the outer radius) also depends on geometry.
- The ratio $Z_p = J/r$ **directly combines both** into a single design parameter.
- Maximum stress = $\dfrac{T}{Z_p}$, so $Z_p$ appears **linearly in the design equation**, making it easier to size shafts.
- For a solid shaft: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$. For a hollow shaft: $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16 D}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–3 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch on $P$:** If $P$ is in **kW**, multiply by $10^3$ when computing $T$. If $P$ is in **HP**, multiply by $745.7$. Mixing units is the **#1 mistake** in KTU papers.
> 2. **Angular velocity vs. rpm:** Using $\omega$ directly with $N$ in rpm without converting to rad/s. Always use $P = \dfrac{2\pi NT}{60}$ with $N$ in **rpm** (this is the *safest* formula in KTU).
> 3. **Diameter vs. radius:** $J$ uses the **fourth power of diameter** ($d^4$), and the torsion formula uses $d^3$. Forgetting to cube-root correctly is a common error.
> 4. **Shear stress units:** If $T$ is in N·mm and $d$ is in mm, then $\tau$ comes out in **N/mm² = MPa**. Do NOT convert $T$ to N·m if diameter is in mm.
> 5. **Hollow shaft formula sign error:** The correct expression is $\dfrac{D^4 - d^4}{D}$, not $\dfrac{D^4 - d^4}{16 D}$ inside the $\pi$ — students often forget to divide by $D$ in the denominator.
> 6. **Standard sizes:** Always adopt a **standard shaft diameter** (e.g., 60, 65, 70, 75, 80 mm) greater than or equal to the calculated value. Not doing so loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **Shaft:** Rotating member of circular cross-section used to transmit power.
- **Core Power Formula:** $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ W (with $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m). Equivalent form: $P = T \omega$ (with $\omega$ in rad/s).
- **Torque from Power:** $T = \dfrac{60 \cdot P \cdot 10^3}{2\pi N}$ N·m (when $P$ in kW); $T \approx 9550 \cdot \dfrac{P_{kW}}{N_{rpm}}$ N·m.
- **Torsion Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r}$, leading to $T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \tau \cdot d^3$ (solid) and $T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \tau \cdot \dfrac{D^4 - d^4}{D}$ (hollow).
- **Polar Moment of Inertia:** $J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ (solid); $J = \dfrac{\pi(D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ (hollow).
- **Polar Section Modulus:** $Z_p = \dfrac{J}{r} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ (solid); $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{16D}$ (hollow).
- **Shear stress varies linearly** with radius — **zero at center, maximum at outer surface.**
- **Hollow shafts are more efficient** than solid shafts of the same weight — they transmit more torque per unit weight because the central core carries very little stress.
- **Combining equations** for direct design: Substituting the torque from power into the torsion formula gives $d^3 = \dfrac{16 \times 60 \times P \times 10^6}{2\pi^2 \cdot \tau \cdot N}$ mm³ (solid, $P$ in kW).
- **Practical applications:** Automobile drive shafts, marine propeller shafts, turbine rotors, machine tool spindles, helicopter rotor shafts.
- **Always adopt standard sizes** (e.g., 60, 65, 70, 75, 80 mm) above the calculated value.
- **Key failure mode:** Torsional yielding of outer fibers; hence design uses **maximum shear stress at the outer radius**.
- **Unit discipline is critical:** $P$ in kW or W, $T$ in N·m or N·mm, $d$ in mm, $\tau$ in MPa — keep them **consistent**.
- **Two formulas to memorize absolutely:** $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ and $T = \dfrac{\pi}{16} \tau d^3$ — every problem on this topic starts from these.
- **Numerical shortcut:** For a **solid shaft**, $d \approx 14.6 \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{P_{kW}}{N_{rpm} \cdot \tau_{MPa}}}$ mm — a quick check formula for verifying your full calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strength of a Solid Shaft and a Hollow Shaft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Strength of a Solid Shaft and a Hollow Shaft # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Torsion of Circular Shafts**, the **Strength** of a shaft refers to its ability to resist failure due to torsional shear stress induced by an applied twisting moment (torque). According to the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory** (Tresca Criterion) and KTU's prescribed strength design methodology, a shaft is considered to have failed in torsion when the maximum shear stress at its outer surface reaches the permissible/proportional limit of the material in shear. For a shaft transmitting torque **T**, the **Strength Criterion** is given by: $$\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{permissible}$$ Where $\tau_{max}$ is the maximum torsional shear stress developed at the outer fiber of the shaft cross-section. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Definition:** The *strength* of a shaft is governed by the **Maximum Shear Stress** it can safely transmit, which depends on the **Polar Section Modulus** ($Z_p$) of its cross-section. Strength is independent of the material's modulus of rigidity (G) — it is a function of geometry and permissible stress only. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Twisting a Rope" Model Imagine you are twisting a **wet towel** to wring water out of it. Your hands apply a *couple* (two equal and opposite forces at a distance), which is essentially a **torque**. The fibers at the outer surface of the towel experience the maximum shear (the most relative sliding), while the core fibers barely slide at all. - A **solid shaft** is like a *solid wooden dowel* — it has material all the way to the center, but that central material carries almost no stress in torsion. It's "wasted mass." - A **hollow shaft** is like a *bicycle axle tube* — the central material is removed, and what remains is precisely the high-stress outer region. Same strength, much less weight. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Lock-in:** Hollow shafts are *stronger per unit weight* than solid shafts because torsion stress is maximum at the outer surface and zero at the center — exactly where the hollow shaft has *no material*. This is why drive shafts in cars, propeller shafts in ships, and bicycle axles are hollow. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants, Notation & Standards (KTU 2024) | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit (SI) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **T** | Applied Torque (Twisting Moment) | **N·m** | | **d** | Diameter of Solid Shaft | **m** (or **mm**) | | **D** | Outer Diameter of Hollow Shaft | **m** (or **mm**) | | **d_i** | Inner Diameter of Hollow Shaft | **m** (or **mm**) | | **k** | Diameter Ratio = $d_i / D$ | Dimensionless | | **J** | Polar Moment of Inertia | **m⁴** (or **mm⁴**) | | **Z_p** | Polar Section Modulus ($J/r_{max}$) | **m³** (or **mm³**) | | **τ** | Shear Stress | **N/m² = Pa** (or **MPa** = **N/mm²**) | | **G** | Modulus of Rigidity | **N/m² = Pa** | | **θ** | Angle of Twist | **radians** (rad) | | **L** | Length of Shaft | **m** (or **mm**) | | **ρ** | Density of Material | **kg/m³** | > [!IMPORTANT] > All derivations in KTU board examinations follow the convention that **1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ N/m²**. When numerical problems mix units, convert **everything to a single unit system** before substituting. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Torsional Stress Distribution — The Foundation When a circular shaft (solid or hollow) is subjected to a pure torque **T** about its longitudinal axis, every cross-section rotates as a rigid disc, and shear stresses develop that vary **linearly with the radial distance from the center**. The general torsion formula (derived from elastic theory of circular shafts) is: $$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$ Where: - $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ from the center - $T$ = Applied torque - $r$ = Radial distance from the center of the cross-section - $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section **Key Insight:** Since $\tau \propto r$, the stress is **zero at the center** ($r = 0$) and **maximum at the outer surface** ($r = r_{max}$). This single fact drives the entire design philosophy of hollow shafts. --- ## 2.2 Polar Moment of Inertia (J) — The Geometric Property The **Polar Moment of Inertia** is the second moment of area about the polar (longitudinal) axis of the shaft cross-section. For a circle: $$J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$ For a **Solid Circular Shaft** of diameter $d$: $$J_{solid} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$ For a **Hollow Circular Shaft** of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d_i$: $$J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}(1 - k^4)$$ Where $k = d_i / D$ is the **diameter ratio**. --- ## 2.3 Polar Section Modulus (Z_p) — The Strength Parameter The **Polar Section Modulus** is defined as: $$Z_p = \frac{J}{r_{max}}$$ | Shaft Type | $r_{max}$ | $Z_p$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Solid** | $d/2$ | $\dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | | **Hollow** | $D/2$ | $\dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}(1 - k^4)$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Trick:** Polar Section Modulus is to torsion what Section Modulus is to bending. Memorize $\pi d^3 / 16$ and the hollow variant — these two formulas appear in **every** KTU board question on this topic. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM GOLD** — The following table is the *single most important* reference for KTU Module 3 problems. Reproduce this structure in your answer sheet whenever possible to score full method marks. | # | Parameter | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Diameter | $d$ | $D$ (outer), $d_i$ (inner) | | 2 | Diameter Ratio | — | $k = d_i / D$ | | 3 | Cross-sectional Area | $A_s = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $A_h = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d_i^2)$ | | 4 | Polar Moment of Inertia | $J_s = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32}$ | $J_h = \dfrac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d_i^4)$ | | 5 | Polar Section Modulus | $Z_{p,s} = \dfrac{\pi d^3}{16}$ | $Z_{p,h} = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}(1 - k^4)$ | | 6 | Max Shear Stress | $\tau_s = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | $\tau_h = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi D^3(1 - k^4)}$ | | 7 | Angle of Twist | $\theta_s = \dfrac{32 TL}{\pi G d^4}$ | $\theta_h = \dfrac{32 TL}{\pi G D^4(1 - k^4)}$ | | 8 | Torsional Rigidity | $GJ_s = \dfrac{\pi G d^4}{32}$ | $GJ_h = \dfrac{\pi G D^4}{32}(1 - k^4)$ | > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE (KTU Examiner's Pet Peeve):** Always write units in **every** step of the calculation. A step missing units is a guaranteed **half-mark deduction** in the KTU board valuation key. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The strength-of-shaft concept directly governs the design of: 1. **Automotive Drive Shafts (Propeller Shafts):** Always *hollow* — they transmit engine torque to the differential with minimum weight (critical for fuel efficiency and unsprung mass). 2. **Aircraft Landing Gear Axles:** Hollow high-strength steel — strength + low weight = critical for takeoff performance. 3. **Marine Propeller Shafts:** Hollow tubes to reduce weight at the stern (which is critical for vessel stability). 4. **Turbo-Generator Shafts:** Solid, but only when torsional vibrations are a concern (a hollow shaft can have undesirable resonance modes). 5. **Bicycle and E-scooter Axles:** Hollow — strength at the rim where it matters, no material at the center. > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb (Industry Standard):** For shaft diameters above **100 mm**, hollow shafts are universally preferred. For diameters below **30 mm**, solid shafts are used (drilling a small hole provides negligible weight saving but adds manufacturing cost). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Shear Stress in a Solid Circular Shaft **Given:** A solid circular shaft of diameter **d** subjected to torque **T**. **Step 1 — Start with the basic torsion formula:** $$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the maximum radius:** For a solid shaft, the stress is maximum at the **outermost fiber**, where $r = r_{max} = d/2$. $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the polar moment of inertia for a solid circular section:** $$J_{solid} = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the equation:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \frac{T \cdot d}{2} \cdot \frac{32}{\pi d^4}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify algebraically:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{32 T d}{2 \pi d^4} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max,solid} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** The maximum shear stress is **inversely proportional to the cube of the diameter**. Doubling the diameter reduces the stress by a factor of **8**. This is why shaft diameter selection is so sensitive to load changes. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Shear Stress in a Hollow Circular Shaft **Given:** A hollow circular shaft with outer diameter **D** and inner diameter **d_i**, subjected to torque **T**. **Step 1 — Start with the basic torsion formula:** $$\tau = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the maximum radius:** For a hollow shaft, the outer surface has the maximum stress: $r = r_{max} = D/2$. $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the polar moment of inertia for a hollow circular section:** $$J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{32}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the equation:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot D / 2}{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4) / 32} = \frac{T \cdot D}{2} \cdot \frac{32}{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify algebraically:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T D}{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}$$ **Step 6 — Introduce the diameter ratio $k = d_i / D$ to get the standard KTU textbook form:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T D}{\pi D^4 (1 - k^4)} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max,hollow} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** As $k \to 0$ (very thin hollow shaft — almost a tube with no wall), the term $(1 - k^4) \to 1$, and the formula reduces to that of a solid shaft with $D = d$. As $k \to 1$ (wall becomes vanishingly thin), the stress becomes **infinite** — this is the failure mode of thin-walled tubes in torsion (they buckle/wrap). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Strength Comparison — Solid vs Hollow (Same Material, Same Length, Same Torque) This is the most frequently asked KTU question type. We compare the **strengths** of a solid shaft of diameter $d_s$ and a hollow shaft of outer diameter $D$ with ratio $k$, both made of the same material and transmitting the same torque **T**. **Step 1 — Maximum stress in solid shaft:** $$\tau_s = \frac{16 T}{\pi d_s^3}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum stress in hollow shaft (same torque T, same outer diameter, i.e., $D = d_s$):** $$\tau_h = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)} = \frac{\tau_s}{1 - k^4}$$ **Step 3 — Strength ratio:** $$\frac{\tau_s}{\tau_h} = 1 - k^4$$ > **Conclusion:** Since $k < 1$, we have $1 - k^4 < 1$, which means $\tau_s < \tau_h$. The **hollow shaft has a higher maximum shear stress** (for the same outer diameter), which means — *for the same permissible stress* — the hollow shaft can carry **more torque**. **Step 4 — Equating strengths (same maximum shear stress), find the diameter ratio $d_s / D$ for equal strength:** Set $\tau_s = \tau_h$: $$\frac{16 T}{\pi d_s^3} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}$$ $$d_s^3 = D^3 (1 - k^4)$$ $$\frac{d_s}{D} = (1 - k^4)^{1/3}$$ > **Insight:** For a hollow shaft with $k = 0.5$, $1 - k^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$. Then $d_s / D = 0.9375^{1/3} \approx 0.979$. So a solid shaft must be **only ~2% larger in diameter** to match the strength of a hollow shaft with $k = 0.5$. But the *weight saving* is enormous (see next derivation). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Weight Ratio (Same Material, Same Length, Same Strength) **Step 1 — Weight of a shaft is proportional to its volume × density:** $$W \propto A \cdot L$$ For the same length L, we compare cross-sectional areas. **Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of solid shaft (equal strength to hollow):** The solid shaft diameter $d_s$ satisfies $d_s^3 = D^3 (1 - k^4)$, so $d_s = D (1 - k^4)^{1/3}$. $$A_s = \frac{\pi d_s^2}{4} = \frac{\pi D^2 (1 - k^4)^{2/3}}{4}$$ **Step 3 — Cross-sectional area of hollow shaft:** $$A_h = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d_i^2) = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}(1 - k^2)$$ **Step 4 — Weight ratio:** $$\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = \frac{A_h}{A_s} = \frac{1 - k^2}{(1 - k^4)^{2/3}}$$ $$\boxed{\frac{W_h}{W_s} = \frac{1 - k^2}{(1 - k^4)^{2/3}}}$$ > **Engineering Interpretation:** For $k = 0.5$, $1 - k^2 = 0.75$ and $(1 - k^4)^{2/3} = 0.9375^{2/3} \approx 0.958$. The weight ratio is $0.75 / 0.958 \approx 0.783$. So the hollow shaft is only **~78% the weight of an equally-strong solid shaft** — a **22% weight saving** for the *same* torsional strength. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Evaluation for Common k Values | $k = d_i / D$ | $1 - k^4$ | $d_s / D$ (equal strength) | $W_h / W_s$ (equal strength) | Weight Saving (%) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 0.0 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 1.000 | 0% (both solid) | | 0.25 | 0.9961 | 0.9987 | 0.970 | 3.0% | | 0.50 | 0.9375 | 0.9787 | 0.783 | 21.7% | | 0.60 | 0.8704 | 0.9557 | 0.668 | 33.2% | | 0.75 | 0.6836 | 0.8813 | 0.460 | 54.0% | | 0.80 | 0.5904 | 0.8381 | 0.367 | 63.3% | | 0.90 | 0.3439 | 0.7016 | 0.187 | 81.3% | | 1.00 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | undefined | hollow tube failure | > [!NOTE] > **Optimal Industrial Range:** Most engineering hollow shafts are designed with **k between 0.6 and 0.8** — this range provides substantial weight savings (33% to 63%) without making the wall too thin for manufacturing or buckling concerns. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Procedure for Solving KTU Numericals > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solve any KTU strength-of-shaft problem using this 5-step methodology:** **Step 1 — Identify the shaft type and given quantities.** List $T$, $d$ (or $D$, $d_i$, $k$), $L$, $G$, $\tau_{permissible}$ (if given). **Step 2 — Determine the design criterion.** - *Strength design:* Use $\tau = T/Z_p \leq \tau_{permissible}$ → solve for required diameter. - *Comparison:* Use the formulas in Section 2.4 directly. **Step 3 — Choose the appropriate formula.** - Solid: $\tau = 16T/(\pi d^3)$ - Hollow: $\tau = 16T/[\pi D^3(1-k^4)]$ **Step 4 — Substitute and solve for the unknown.** Maintain unit consistency. Show every algebraic step. **Step 5 — Verify the result.** - Sanity check: The diameter should be in a reasonable range (a few cm to a few tens of cm for typical torque values). - For hollow shafts, ensure the wall thickness is feasible: $t = (D - d_i)/2 \geq 2$ mm for manufacturability. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Stress Distribution Across Cross-Section This diagram shows the **linear variation** of shear stress across the cross-section of solid and hollow shafts under torsion. Stress is maximum at the outer surface and zero at the center. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Solid_Shaft sA["Outer Surface
tau MAX = 16T over pi d cubed"] --> sB["Quarter Radius
r equals d by 4
tau equals 0.5 tau MAX"] sB --> sC["Center
r equals 0
tau equals 0"] end subgraph Hollow_Shaft hA["Outer Surface
tau MAX = 16T over pi D cubed times 1 by 1 minus k to the 4"] --> hB["Inner Surface
r equals d sub i over 2
tau equals tau MAX times k cubed"] hB --> hC["Hollow Core
No Material
tau undefined"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Visual Takeaway:** In a *solid* shaft, the stress tapers linearly from maximum (outer) to zero (center). In a *hollow* shaft, the inner surface has a *non-zero* stress (proportional to $k^3$), and the center region simply doesn't exist. The hollow shaft uses material only where stress is high. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hollow Shaft System ```mermaid flowchart TB inputA["Input Torque T_in from Engine or Motor"] --> couplingA["Coupling Flange A
Torque Transmission Joint"] couplingA --> hollowShaft["Hollow Shaft Tube
Outer D equals D mm
Inner D equals d sub i mm
Material SS 304 or EN8 Steel"] hollowShaft --> couplingB["Coupling Flange B
Torque Transmission Joint"] couplingB --> outputA["Output Torque T_out to Load"] hollowShaft -.->|"Torsional Shear Stress tau MAX"| stressField["Stress Field
Linear Distribution
tau MAX at Outer Surface"] hollowShaft -.->|"Angle of Twist theta"| twistField["Angular Twist Field
theta equals 32TL over pi G D to the 4 times 1 minus k to the 4"] stressField --> designCheck["Design Check
tau MAX must be less than tau permissible"] twistField --> stiffnessCheck["Stiffness Check
theta must be less than theta permissible"] designCheck --> verified["Verified Safe Design"] stiffnessCheck --> verified ``` > **Diagram Reading Guide:** The two main *functional* paths from the hollow shaft are **(1) the stress path** (governs material failure — the *strength* criterion) and **(2) the twist path** (governs serviceability — the *stiffness* criterion). Both must satisfy their respective limits for the shaft to be acceptable. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Decision Flow for Solid vs Hollow Shaft Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Shaft Design Problem Given Torque T and Material"] --> q1{"Diameter Required More than 100 mm?"} q1 -->|"Yes"| q2{"Weight Critical Application?
Aircraft, Automotive, Marine"} q1 -->|"No"| solid["Use SOLID Shaft
d equals cube root of 16T over pi tau permissible"] q2 -->|"Yes"| hollow["Use HOLLOW Shaft
Choose k between 0.6 and 0.8
D equals cube root of 16T over pi tau permissible times 1 by 1 minus k to the 4"] q2 -->|"No"| q3{"High Torsional Vibration Concern?"} q3 -->|"Yes"| solid q3 -->|"No"| hollow hollow --> verify["Verify Wall Thickness
t equals D minus d sub i over 2 greater than 2 mm"] solid --> done["Design Complete"] verify --> done ``` > **Diagram Reading Guide:** This is the **engineer's decision tree** for choosing between solid and hollow shafts. The two primary drivers are *diameter magnitude* and *weight sensitivity*. A third consideration (torsional vibration) sometimes forces a return to solid construction. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A consists of short-answer questions. Each carries 3 marks. Answers should be **concise (3–5 lines)**, with the key formula and a brief explanation. No lengthy derivations expected. ### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define polar section modulus. Obtain the polar section modulus of a hollow circular shaft of outer diameter D and inner diameter d.** **Model Answer:** The **Polar Section Modulus** ($Z_p$) is defined as the ratio of the polar moment of inertia ($J$) to the maximum radial distance ($r_{max}$) of the cross-section from the centroidal axis: $$Z_p = \frac{J}{r_{max}}$$ For a hollow circular shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$: $$J = \frac{\pi}{32}(D^4 - d^4)$$ $$r_{max} = \frac{D}{2}$$ $$\boxed{Z_p = \frac{\pi D^3}{32}\left(1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^4\right) = \frac{\pi D^3}{16}(1 - k^4)}$$ *(3 marks — full formula and proper substitution required for full credit.)* --- ### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State two advantages of a hollow shaft over a solid shaft of the same material and weight.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Higher strength-to-weight ratio:** A hollow shaft of the *same weight* as a solid shaft has a larger outer diameter, which (since $\tau \propto 1/D^3$) gives it significantly higher torque-transmitting capacity. 2. **Better material utilization:** In torsion, the shear stress varies linearly from zero at the center to maximum at the outer surface. A hollow shaft removes the low-stress central material, putting mass where the stress is highest. *(3 marks — two distinct points with brief justification. KTU board awards full marks for clear engineering reasoning.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks each**, divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full. Each sub-part must show complete working. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]` **Question:** A solid circular shaft of diameter 60 mm is transmitting a torque of 2.5 kN·m. **(a)** Determine the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft. *(7 marks)* **(b)** If this shaft is to be replaced by a hollow shaft of the same material with an outer diameter of 70 mm and an inner diameter of 50 mm, find the maximum torque that the hollow shaft can transmit if the maximum shear stress is limited to the same value. Also, find the percentage saving in weight for the same length. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a): **Step 1 — Given data:** - Solid shaft diameter: $d = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m - Applied torque: $T = 2.5$ kN·m $= 2.5 \times 10^6$ N·mm **Step 2 — Formula for maximum shear stress in a solid shaft:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 2.5 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (60)^3}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the denominator:** $$\pi \times (60)^3 = \pi \times 216000 = 678584.013 \text{ N·mm}^{-1} \text{ (approx.)}$$ **Step 5 — Final computation:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{678584.013} = 58.95 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 58.95 \text{ MPa}}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for calculation, 1 for correct units and box.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Step 1 — Given data for hollow shaft:** - Outer diameter: $D = 70$ mm - Inner diameter: $d_i = 50$ mm - Maximum permissible shear stress: $\tau_{max} = 58.95$ MPa (same as solid) - Diameter ratio: $k = d_i / D = 50/70 = 0.7143$ **Step 2 — Compute $1 - k^4$:** $$k^4 = (0.7143)^4 = 0.2603$$ $$1 - k^4 = 1 - 0.2603 = 0.7397$$ **Step 3 — Formula for maximum torque transmitted by hollow shaft:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T_{hollow}}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $T_{hollow}$:** $$T_{hollow} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot \pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}{16}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute values:** $$T_{hollow} = \frac{58.95 \times \pi \times (70)^3 \times 0.7397}{16}$$ **Step 6 — Compute step by step:** $$(70)^3 = 343000 \text{ mm}^3$$ $$\pi \times 343000 = 1077568.93 \text{ mm}^3$$ $$58.95 \times 1077568.93 = 6.3525 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm}^2$$ $$6.3525 \times 10^7 \times 0.7397 = 4.6987 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm}^2$$ $$T_{hollow} = \frac{4.6987 \times 10^7}{16} = 2.9367 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$ $$\boxed{T_{hollow} \approx 2.937 \text{ kN·m}}$$ **Step 7 — Weight comparison (same length L):** Cross-sectional area of solid shaft (we need to find the equivalent solid shaft for *equal strength*): Wait — the question asks for the **weight saving of the hollow shaft compared to the original solid shaft** (same length, same material). So we compare their actual cross-sectional areas: $$A_{solid} = \frac{\pi (60)^2}{4} = 2827.43 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$A_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{4}(70^2 - 50^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(4900 - 2500) = \frac{\pi \times 2400}{4} = 1884.96 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 8 — Weight ratio:** $$\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = \frac{A_{hollow}}{A_{solid}} = \frac{1884.96}{2827.43} = 0.6667$$ **Step 9 — Percentage weight saving:** $$\text{Weight saving} = (1 - 0.6667) \times 100\% = 33.33\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{Weight saving} \approx 33.33\%}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for k calculation, 2 for T computation, 2 for weight ratio, 1 for final boxed answer.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **Question:** A shaft is required to transmit 200 kW at 300 rpm. The maximum permissible shear stress for the material is 70 MPa. **(a)** Design a suitable solid shaft diameter based on strength. *(7 marks)* **(b)** If a hollow shaft is to be designed with the same material and same strength, with an inner-to-outer diameter ratio of $k = 0.6$, find the required outer diameter. Also, calculate the percentage reduction in weight. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a): **Step 1 — Convert power to torque using the standard relation:** The relationship between power **P**, torque **T**, and angular speed **N** is: $$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$ Solving for **T**: $$T = \frac{60 P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 200 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 300}$$ **Step 2 — Compute T:** $$T = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{2 \pi \times 300} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{1884.96} = 6366.20 \text{ N·m}$$ Converting to N·mm: $T = 6366.20 \times 10^3$ N·mm $= 6.3662 \times 10^6$ N·mm. **Step 3 — Apply the strength formula:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for d:** $$d^3 = \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{max}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute values:** $$d^3 = \frac{16 \times 6.3662 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 70}$$ **Step 6 — Compute:** $$d^3 = \frac{1.0186 \times 10^8}{219.91} = 4.6315 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 7 — Take cube root:** $$d = (4.6315 \times 10^5)^{1/3} \approx 77.39 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 77.4 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{Say } 78 \text{ mm for standard availability})}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for torque formula, 2 for strength formula, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for final answer.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Step 1 — Given data for hollow shaft:** - Same torque: $T = 6.3662 \times 10^6$ N·mm - Same permissible stress: $\tau_{max} = 70$ MPa - Diameter ratio: $k = 0.6$ **Step 2 — Compute $1 - k^4$:** $$k^4 = (0.6)^4 = 0.1296$$ $$1 - k^4 = 1 - 0.1296 = 0.8704$$ **Step 3 — Apply the hollow shaft strength formula:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for D:** $$D^3 = \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{max} (1 - k^4)} = \frac{d_{solid}^3}{1 - k^4}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute:** $$D^3 = \frac{4.6315 \times 10^5}{0.8704} = 5.3213 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 6 — Cube root:** $$D = (5.3213 \times 10^5)^{1/3} \approx 80.92 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{D \approx 81 \text{ mm}}$$ **Step 7 — Inner diameter:** $$d_i = k \cdot D = 0.6 \times 80.92 = 48.55 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 8 — Cross-sectional areas:** $$A_{solid} = \frac{\pi (77.39)^2}{4} = 4704.42 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$A_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{4}((80.92)^2 - (48.55)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(6548.05 - 2357.10) = \frac{\pi \times 4190.95}{4} = 3291.40 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 9 — Weight ratio:** $$\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = \frac{3291.40}{4704.42} = 0.6996$$ **Step 10 — Percentage weight reduction:** $$\text{Reduction} = (1 - 0.6996) \times 100\% = 30.04\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{Weight reduction} \approx 30\%}$$ *(7 marks: 2 for $1-k^4$ computation, 2 for D calculation, 2 for weight ratio, 1 for final boxed answer.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams (Module 3 - Strength of Shafts):** > > 1. **Forgetting the $(1 - k^4)$ term in the hollow shaft formula.** This is the **#1 error** in KTU board exams. Students write $\tau = 16T/(\pi D^3)$ for hollow shafts — this gives the *outer surface stress of a solid shaft of diameter D*, NOT the hollow shaft stress. *Always include $(1-k^4)$ in the denominator.* > > 2. **Mixing up the diameter ratio.** Some students compute $k = D/d_i$ (inverted). The standard KTU convention is $k = d_i/D$ (inner over outer, always less than 1). > > 3. **Unit inconsistency.** The most common numeric error: using torque in N·m and diameter in mm without converting. The shear stress formula $16T/(\pi d^3)$ requires *consistent* units. Use **N·mm and mm** together, or **N·m and m** together — never mix. > > 4. **Not boxing the final answer.** In KTU valuation, the final answer must be **boxed** with a clear unit. An unboxed answer loses 0.5 to 1 mark even if the number is correct. > > 5. **Confusing *strength* with *stiffness*.** Strength uses $\tau_{max} = T/Z_p$ (no G, no L). Stiffness uses $\theta = TL/(GJ)$ (has G and L). KTU board questions on Module 3 strength typically have **no G or L** — if you see those given, it might be a stiffness problem. > > 6. **Arithmetic slip in cube roots.** The cube root of $4.6315 \times 10^5$ is approximately 77.4 — students often mis-extract and write 70 or 80. **Always re-verify cube roots by cubing back.** Use a calculator in the exam hall. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Strength of Solid & Hollow Shafts (Module 3)** > > - [x] **Two key formulas to memorize:** > - Solid: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ > - Hollow: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi D^3 (1 - k^4)}$ > > - [x] **Polar Section Modulus** = $J / r_{max}$ — this is the "torsion equivalent" of section modulus in bending. > - Solid: $Z_p = \pi d^3 / 16$ > - Hollow: $Z_p = \pi D^3 (1 - k^4) / 16$ > > - [x] **Diameter ratio** $k = d_i / D$ is always **less than 1**. Typical industrial range: **0.6 to 0.8**. > > - [x] **Torsion is a strength AND stiffness problem.** This module covers *strength* (stress-based). Stiffness (twist-based) uses $\theta = TL/(GJ)$ — *different formula, different problem type*. > > - [x] **Hollow shaft advantages:** (i) Higher strength per unit weight, (ii) Better material utilization, (iii) Lower bearing loads (less weight), (iv) Can be used to route cables/fluids through the bore. > > - [x] **Hollow shaft disadvantages:** (i) Higher manufacturing cost, (ii) Wall thickness limits, (iii) Corrosion on inner surface is hard to inspect. > > - [x] **Weight saving formula (for equal strength):** $W_h / W_s = (1 - k^2) / (1 - k^4)^{2/3}$. For $k = 0.5$, this gives $\approx 0.78$ (22% saving). For $k = 0.75$, $\approx 0.46$ (54% saving). > > - [x] **Power-Torque relation:** $T = 60P / (2\pi N)$, where P is in watts, T in N·m, and N in rpm. *Memorize this — it appears in nearly every KTU design problem.* > > - [x] **Permissible stress is NOT the same as ultimate shear stress.** Always use $\tau_{permissible} = \tau_{ultimate} / \text{Factor of Safety}$ in design problems. The factor of safety is usually **2 to 4** for shaft design. > > - [x] **Units cheat sheet for the exam:** > - Stress: $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$ > - Torque: $1 \text{ kN·m} = 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$ > - Power: $1 \text{ kW} = 10^3 \text{ W} = 10^3 \text{ N·m/s}$ > > - [x] **Exam mantra:** "Show the formula → substitute with units → compute step-by-step → box the final answer." KTU examiners award *method marks* generously, even when arithmetic has minor errors.

topics.title: Derivation of the Torsional Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of the Torsional Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (typically a cylindrical shaft) about its longitudinal axis when subjected to a **torque** (a moment acting in a plane perpendicular to the axis of the member). The **Torsional Equation** is the fundamental relationship that links the applied torque, the resulting shear stress, the angle of twist, and the geometric and material properties of a circular shaft.

The standard form of the torsional equation is expressed as:

$$
\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G \theta}{L}
$$

Where:
- $T$ = Applied Torque (N·m)
- $J$ = Polar Moment of Inertia of the cross-section (m⁴)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at the outer fibre (N/m²)
- $R$ = Radius of the shaft (m)
- $G$ = Modulus of Rigidity (N/m²)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist (radians)
- $L$ = Length of the shaft over which twist occurs (m)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Wet Towel Twist**
> Imagine wringing water out of a wet towel. You grip both ends and rotate them in opposite directions. The fibres on the outer surface stretch and shear dramatically, while fibres closer to the centre barely move. This non-uniform deformation is exactly what happens in a **circular shaft under torsion**. The outermost fibres experience maximum shear strain, and the centreline fibres experience zero strain. The torsional equation mathematically captures this gradient.

Another mechanical analogy is the **drive shaft of an automobile** — power from the engine is transmitted to the wheels via torque, causing the shaft to twist slightly along its length. Engineers must ensure the shear stress $\tau$ does not exceed the allowable limit, and the angle of twist $\theta$ remains within serviceability bounds.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Units

| Quantity | Standard SI Unit | Typical Value (Steel) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Modulus of Rigidity ($G$) | N/m² or **Pa** | **80 GPa** |
| Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$) | m⁴ | depends on geometry |
| Shear Stress ($\tau$) | N/m² or **Pa** | depends on loading |
| Angle of Twist ($\theta$) | **radians** | dimensionless ratio |
| Torque ($T$) | **N·m** | derived from power |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational Assumptions of the Torsion Theory

The classical derivation of the torsional equation rests on the following **five key assumptions** (these are examinable in KTU):

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite — Assumptions List**
> 1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law in shear).
> 2. The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** throughout its length.
> 3. **Shaft axis remains straight** — there is no bending or warping.
> 4. **Cross-sections plane before twisting remain plane and rotate as rigid discs** after twisting (this is the *kinematic assumption* leading to linear strain distribution).
> 5. **Pure torsion** — the shaft is subjected to torque only, with no bending, axial, or transverse shear loads.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 — Geometric Kinematics:** When a shaft twists, the originally straight longitudinal fibres on the surface deform into helices. The angle of shear $\gamma$ at radius $R$ relates to total angle of twist $\theta$ and length $L$.
- **Step 2 — Strain Distribution:** Because plane sections remain plane, shear strain $\gamma$ varies *linearly* from **zero at the centre** to a **maximum at the outer surface**.
- **Step 3 — Hooke's Law in Shear:** Since material is elastic, $\tau = G \gamma$ applies at every fibre.
- **Step 4 — Static Equilibrium:** The internal torque $T$ must equal the integral of shear stress × area element over the cross-section. This introduces the **polar moment of inertia $J$**.
- **Step 5 — Synthesis:** Combining geometry, elasticity, and equilibrium yields the master torsional equation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application / When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Polar Moment of Inertia (Solid Shaft) | $J = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$ | Standard solid circular shaft |
| Polar Moment of Inertia (Hollow Shaft) | $J = \dfrac{\pi}{32} \left( D^{4} - d^{4} \right)$ | Hollow / tubular shaft |
| Master Torsional Equation | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$ | Universal — must be memorised |
| Shear Stress (Outer Fibre) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi D^{3}}$ | Solid shaft stress check |
| Angle of Twist | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | Total angular displacement |
| Polar Section Modulus | $Z_{p} = \dfrac{J}{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{16}$ | Stress in form $\tau = T / Z_{p}$ |
| Power Transmitted | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m, $P$ in Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM TIP:** When asked to "derive the torsional equation", examiners expect the full logical flow — *Assumptions → Strain Geometry → Hooke's Law → Equilibrium Integration → Final Form*. Missing any one of these will cost **at least 3 marks** out of the 14 allocated.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Propeller shafts, axle shafts, drive trains.
- **Civil Engineering:** Torsion in reinforced concrete beams subjected to eccentric loading (e.g., spandrel beams in buildings, curved bridge girders).
- **Mechanical:** Turbine shafts, drill bits, screws, bolts under tightening torque.
- **Aerospace:** Helicopter rotor drive shafts, where weight optimisation (hollow shafts) is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Classical Derivation of the Torsional Equation

**Setup:** Consider a solid circular shaft of:
- Length $= L$
- Radius $= R$
- Modulus of Rigidity $= G$
- Fixed at one end (say, the left end at $x = 0$)
- Subjected to a torque $T$ at the free (right) end

Under the action of $T$, the free end rotates through an angle $\theta$ relative to the fixed end.

---

**Step 1 — Geometric Picture of Twisting**

Take a small element of length $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the fixed end. When the shaft twists, this element rotates by an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$ about the longitudinal axis.

A radial line on the end face, originally of length $R$, sweeps through the angle $d\theta$. The arc length traced by the outer point is $R \, d\theta$.

The original length of this radial line was $dx$. After twisting, it is inclined at a small shear angle $\gamma$ to its original position. From elementary geometry:

$$
\gamma \approx \tan(\gamma) = \frac{\text{Arc length}}{\text{Original length}} = \frac{R \, d\theta}{dx}
$$

Hence the shear strain at the outer surface is:

$$
\gamma = \frac{R \, d\theta}{dx}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Total Angle of Twist (Integration)**

Integrating along the entire length $L$ of the shaft, assuming $d\theta/dx$ is constant for a uniform shaft under a constant torque $T$:

$$
\int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta = \frac{1}{R} \int_{0}^{L} \gamma \, dx
$$

This gives the relationship between the **total angle of twist** $\theta$ and the shear strain at the outer fibre:

$$
\gamma = \frac{R \, \theta}{L}
$$

> **Logic Note:** This equation is the geometric foundation — it links *deformation* to *twist angle* without invoking any material properties.

---

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear**

Since the shaft material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic, Hooke's Law for shear states:

$$
\tau = G \, \gamma
$$

Substituting the expression for $\gamma$ from Step 2:

$$
\tau = G \cdot \frac{R \, \theta}{L} = \frac{G R \theta}{L}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G \theta}{L}
$$

This is **the second part** of the master torsional equation.

> **Logic Note:** This is where material stiffness (via $G$) enters the relationship. So far, we have linked shear stress $\tau$ to the angle of twist $\theta$.

---

**Step 4 — Static Equilibrium (Internal Torque)**

Now consider the cross-section of the shaft. The internal resisting torque $T$ is generated by the shear stresses $\tau$ distributed over the area. Consider a small elemental ring at radius $r$ with thickness $dr$.

- Area of element: $dA = 2 \pi r \, dr$
- Shear stress at radius $r$ (linearly varying): $\tau_{r} = \tau \cdot (r/R)$, where $\tau$ is the stress at outer radius $R$.
- Force on element: $dF = \tau_{r} \cdot dA = \tau \cdot (r/R) \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr$
- Moment of this force about the axis: $dT = r \cdot dF = \frac{2 \pi \tau}{R} r^{3} \, dr$

Integrating from $r = 0$ to $r = R$:

$$
T = \int_{0}^{R} \frac{2 \pi \tau}{R} r^{3} \, dr = \frac{2 \pi \tau}{R} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\pi \tau R^{3}}{2}
$$

Solving for $\tau$:

$$
\tau = \frac{2T}{\pi R^{3}}
$$

The integral $\int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \cdot 2 \pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2}$ is by definition the **polar moment of inertia**:

$$
J = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tau = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{R}
$$

This is **the first part** of the master torsional equation.

> **Logic Note:** This step introduces the *geometric stiffness* of the cross-section through the polar moment of inertia $J$. It is the equilibrium counterpart of the kinematic derivation in Step 2.

---

**Step 5 — Synthesis: The Master Torsional Equation**

Combining the results of Step 3 and Step 4:

$$
\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G \theta}{L}}
$$

This single equation relates **applied torque, internal shear stress, angle of twist, geometric properties, and material stiffness** — the cornerstone of torsion analysis.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Derivation Logical Map

| Step | Concept Invoked | Equation Obtained | Physical Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Kinematics of twist | $\gamma = R \, d\theta / dx$ | Strain from geometry |
| 2 | Integration over length | $\gamma = R \theta / L$ | Total strain vs total twist |
| 3 | Hooke's Law in shear | $\tau / R = G \theta / L$ | Material stiffness enters |
| 4 | Static equilibrium | $T / J = \tau / R$ | Cross-section resistance |
| 5 | Synthesis | $T / J = \tau / R = G \theta / L$ | **Master Torsional Equation** |

### 3.3 Special Case — Hollow Circular Shaft

For a hollow shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$, the polar moment of inertia becomes:

$$
J = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( D^{4} - d^{4} \right)
$$

The torsional equation retains the **same form**, but $\tau_{max}$ is evaluated at the *outer* radius and $\tau_{min}$ at the *inner* radius:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J}, \quad \tau_{min} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J}
$$

The ratio $\tau_{min} / \tau_{max} = d/D$ is a frequently tested KTU sub-question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architecture of Torsion Derivation

The following Mermaid block depicts the **logical flow of the derivation** as a block-level functional architecture. This serves as a high-yield revision diagram for the KTU board exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assumptions: Homogeneous, Isotropic, Elastic, Circular Shaft] --> B[Step 1: Kinematics - Strain Geometry]
    B --> C[Shear Strain at radius R: gamma = R theta / L]
    C --> D[Step 2: Apply Hookes Law in Shear]
    D --> E[tau = G gamma, gives tau/R = G theta/L]
    E --> F[Step 3: Static Equilibrium of Cross-Section]
    F --> G[Integrate elemental torques: T = integral of tau r dA]
    G --> H[Define Polar Moment J = integral r squared dA]
    H --> I[Obtain T/J = tau/R]
    I --> J[MASTER TORSIONAL EQUATION]
    E --> J
    J --> K[Compute Stress: tau max = T R / J]
    J --> L[Compute Twist: theta = T L / G J]
    J --> M[Power: P = 2 pi N T / 60]
```

### 4.2 Physical Schematic — Torsion of a Circular Shaft

Since a literal cylindrical-shaft diagram is best drawn freehand in the answer script, here is a **Mermaid-based conceptual schematic** of the deformed vs undeformed shaft that students can mentally visualise:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE[UNDEFORMED SHAFT]
        P1[Fixed End A] --- Q1[Straight Longitudinal Line on Surface] --- R1[Free End B]
    end
    subgraph AFTER[TWISTED SHAFT]
        P2[Fixed End A] --- Q2[Helical Line on Surface after Twist] --- R2[Free End B Rotated by theta]
    end
    BEFORE --> APPLY[Apply Torque T at Free End]
    APPLY --> AFTER
```

### 4.3 Cross-Section Stress Distribution (Radial Variation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Centre r=0, tau=0] --> B[r=R/2, tau = tau_max/2]
    B --> C[Outer r=R, tau=tau_max = T R / J]
    style A fill:#e0ffe0
    style C fill:#ffe0e0
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always include a clearly labelled sketch of the twisted shaft showing the helix angle and the radial strain variation. KTU examiners award **2–3 marks** simply for a clean, correctly labelled diagram.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3, Q1]**

**Q1.** State any **three assumptions** made in the derivation of the torsional equation for a circular shaft.

**Model Answer:**
1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**, obeying Hooke's law in shear ($\tau = G\gamma$).
2. The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** throughout its length.
3. **Plane cross-sections remain plane and rotate as rigid discs** after twisting — implying no warping or out-of-plane distortion.

*(Any three correctly stated assumptions earn full 3 marks. Including Hooke's law earns bonus credit.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 3, Q2]**

**Q2.** Define the **polar moment of inertia** $J$ of a circular cross-section. Write its expression for a solid and a hollow shaft of diameters $D$ and $d$.

**Model Answer:**
The **polar moment of inertia** $J$ of a cross-section is the integral $\int r^{2} \, dA$ over the area, where $r$ is the radial distance from the centroidal axis. It represents the **torsional geometric stiffness** of the section.

- Solid shaft: $\; J = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$
- Hollow shaft: $\; J = \dfrac{\pi}{32} \left( D^{4} - d^{4} \right)$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the **torsional equation** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft, clearly stating all assumptions.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A solid steel shaft of diameter **60 mm** transmits a torque of **2 kN·m**. If the modulus of rigidity $G = 80$ GPa, determine:
- (i) The maximum shear stress in the shaft.
- (ii) The angle of twist per metre length.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Derivation:** *(Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Briefly outline):*
1. State the five assumptions clearly.
2. Show the kinematic relation $\gamma = R\theta/L$ from helix geometry.
3. Apply Hooke's law: $\tau = G\gamma \Rightarrow \tau/R = G\theta/L$.
4. Use equilibrium: $T = \int \tau_r \cdot r \, dA$ and define $J = \int r^{2} \, dA$.
5. Conclude: $T/J = \tau/R = G\theta/L$.

*(Full marks for logical flow, labelled diagram, and clear algebra. Missing diagram deducts 2 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $D = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m, $T = 2$ kN·m $= 2000$ N·m, $G = 80$ GPa $= 80 \times 10^{9}$ N/m².

**Step 1 — Compute Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.06)^{4}}{32}
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = \frac{4.0715 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J} = \frac{2000 \times 0.03}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{60}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.717 \times 10^{7} \; \text{N/m}^{2} \approx 47.17 \; \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Angle of Twist per Metre:**

For $L = 1$ m:

$$
\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{2000 \times 1}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\theta = \frac{2000}{1.0176 \times 10^{5}} = 0.01965 \; \text{rad/m}
$$

Converting to degrees: $\theta = 0.01965 \times (180/\pi) \approx 1.126 \; \text{°/m}$.

> **Final Answers:** $\tau_{max} \approx 47.17$ MPa, $\theta \approx 0.01965$ rad/m (or $1.126$ °/m).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **polar moment of inertia** of a hollow circular shaft. Using the torsional equation, show that $\tau_{min} / \tau_{max} = d / D$.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** A hollow shaft with outer diameter **80 mm** and inner diameter **40 mm** is required to transmit **100 kW** at **240 rpm**. Find the maximum shear stress developed in the shaft. Take $G = 80$ GPa.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a)** *(Outline of derivation):*
1. Define $J$ for a hollow circular section: $J = \int_{d/2}^{D/2} r^{2} (2\pi r \, dr) = \dfrac{\pi}{32}(D^{4} - d^{4})$.
2. By linear stress variation, $\tau \propto r$, hence $\tau_{min} / \tau_{max} = (d/2) / (D/2) = d/D$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $D = 0.08$ m, $d = 0.04$ m, $P = 100$ kW $= 100,000$ W, $N = 240$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Compute Torque from Power:**

$$
T = \frac{60 \, P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 100{,}000}{2 \pi \times 240}
$$

$$
T = \frac{6{,}000{,}000}{1507.96} = 3978.87 \; \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Polar Moment of Inertia:**

$$
J = \frac{\pi}{32} (D^{4} - d^{4}) = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( (0.08)^{4} - (0.04)^{4} \right)
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi}{32} (4.096 \times 10^{-5} - 2.56 \times 10^{-6}) = \frac{\pi}{32} \times 3.84 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
J = 3.7699 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (D/2)}{J} = \frac{3978.87 \times 0.04}{3.7699 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{159.155}{3.7699 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.222 \times 10^{7} \; \text{N/m}^{2} \approx 42.22 \; \text{MPa}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\tau_{max} \approx 42.22$ MPa.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with N·m and m with N·m is the **#1 cause of lost marks**. Always convert to a single unit system (preferably SI) before substituting. 1 mm = $10^{-3}$ m.
> 2. **Confusing Solid and Hollow Formulae:** Using $J = \pi D^{4}/32$ for a hollow shaft costs **full 7 marks** in part (b). Always check whether the shaft is solid or hollow.
> 3. **Missing the Diagram:** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly allocates **2 marks** for a labelled figure in 14-mark derivation questions. Omitting the diagram forfeits those marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the Hooke's Law Step:** Many students jump from $\gamma = R\theta/L$ directly to $T/J = \tau/R$ without invoking $\tau = G\gamma$. This breaks the derivation's logic.
> 5. **Angle of Twist Units:** Always state $\theta$ in **radians**. Converting to degrees at the end (if asked) requires the $\times 180/\pi$ factor — don't forget it.
> 6. **Polar Moment vs Area Moment:** $J$ (polar) is NOT the same as $I$ (second moment of area about a diameter). For a solid circle, $I = \pi D^{4}/64$ and $J = \pi D^{4}/32 = 2I$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The **master torsional equation** is $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$ — memorise verbatim.
- ✅ The **five assumptions** (homogeneous, isotropic, linear-elastic material; uniform circular section; straight axis; plane sections remain plane; pure torsion) are compulsory in derivations.
- ✅ **Polar moment of inertia** — Solid: $J = \pi D^{4}/32$. Hollow: $J = \pi(D^{4} - d^{4})/32$.
- ✅ **Shear stress varies linearly** with radius: zero at centre, maximum at outer surface.
- ✅ **Angle of twist** is in **radians**, not degrees, when applying the equation directly.
- ✅ **Power relation:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ ($N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m, $P$ in Watts).
- ✅ The **polar section modulus** $Z_p = J/R = \pi D^{3}/16$ allows compact stress formula: $\tau = T / Z_p$.
- ✅ For a hollow shaft, **stress ratio** $\tau_{min}/\tau_{max} = d/D$.
- ✅ Always include a **labelled diagram** of the twisted shaft in derivations.
- ✅ **Hooke's Law in shear** ($\tau = G\gamma$) is the **bridge** between kinematics and stress in the derivation.
- ✅ **Modulus of Rigidity $G$** is the shear counterpart of Young's Modulus $E$ — they are related by $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$, where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis:** This topic is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in ESE (End Semester Exam) — both the derivation and its numerical application are compulsory practice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assumptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assumptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Assumptions in the Theory of Simple Bending

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Strength of Materials**, the **Assumptions of the Theory of Simple Bending** (also known as the **Bernoulli–Euler Beam Theory**) form the foundational premises upon which the **Bending Equation** 

$$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$

is derived. These assumptions mathematically simplify the complex three-dimensional stress state in a loaded beam into a tractable one-dimensional relationship between bending moment, curvature, and bending stress. Without these assumptions, the partial differential equations governing beam deflection become analytically unsolvable for most practical engineering cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, every derivation involving the **flexure formula**, **moment of resistance**, and **section modulus** is anchored to these assumptions. Examiner questions frequently begin with: *"State and explain the assumptions of the theory of simple bending"* (carry **3 marks** in Part A and up to **7 marks** as part of a 14-mark Part B question).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine bending a long, thin, flexible plastic ruler held firmly between your palms. What happens to the ink line drawn across its width (the cross-section)?
>
> 1. The line **does not curve** — it stays **straight** (planar cross-section).
> 2. It only **rotates** about a horizontal line inside the ruler (the **neutral axis**).
> 3. The ruler is straight *before* you bend it, and its material is uniform (same plastic everywhere).
>
> These real-world observations are exactly what Bernoulli assumed mathematically when he formulated bending theory in 1757. The ruler fibers above the rotation line stretch (tension), those below compress, and exactly at the line, they stay the same length (neutral axis).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Conventions

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Modulus of elasticity of the beam material, measured in **N/mm²** or **GPa** (e.g., Structural Steel $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²**).
- **Radius of Curvature ($R$):** Distance from the beam's center of curvature to the neutral axis, in **mm** or **m**.
- **Moment of Inertia ($I$):** Second moment of area about the neutral axis, in **mm⁴** or **m⁴**.
- **Bending Stress ($\sigma$):** Flexural stress at distance $y$ from the neutral axis, in **N/mm²** or **MPa**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete List of Assumptions (KTU Board-Standard Wording)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 12 Foundational Assumptions** — Memorize using this mnemonic: **"HIPS HEN RES"** (Homogeneous, Isotropic, Prismatic, Straight, Hooke's Law, Elastic limit, Neutral-axis-centroid, Radius large, End-section plane, Self-weight neglected).

### 2.1.1 Material Property Assumptions

| # | Assumption | Engineering "Why" |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Homogeneity** — Material has uniform composition throughout its volume | Guarantees $E$ is constant at every fiber, so stress varies only with $y$ |
| 2 | **Isotropy** — Material has identical elastic properties in all directions | Justifies a single Young's Modulus $E$ governing all fibers |
| 3 | **Hooke's Law Validity** — Stress is proportional to strain within the elastic limit | Permits the linear substitution $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ |

### 2.1.2 Geometric Assumptions

| # | Assumption | Engineering "Why" |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 4 | **Initial Straightness** — Beam axis is perfectly straight before loading | Ensures $y$ is measured from a known straight reference (centroidal axis) |
| 5 | **Uniform Cross-Section** (Prismatic) — Cross-section is constant along the span | $I$ becomes a constant, simplifying integration for slope/deflection |
| 6 | **Plane Sections Remain Plane** (Bernoulli's Hypothesis) — Cross-sections plane before bending remain plane and normal to the deformed axis after bending | Eliminates shear deformation; strain $\varepsilon = y/R$ is purely linear |
| 7 | **Large Radius of Curvature** — $R \gg$ depth of beam | Allows small-slope approximations: $\tan\theta \approx \theta \approx \sin\theta$ |

### 2.1.3 Loading & Boundary Assumptions

| # | Assumption | Engineering "Why" |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 8 | **Self-Weight Negligible** — Beam's own weight is ignored compared to applied loads | Removes distributed body force from the moment equilibrium |
| 9 | **Pure / Symmetric Bending** — Loads act in a plane containing the principal axis (no skew loading) | Bending occurs about one axis only; no twisting |
| 10 | **Small Deflections** — Deflection $\delta$ is tiny compared to span $L$ | Linear superposition principle applies; second-order effects ignored |

### 2.1.4 Stress Distribution Assumptions

| # | Assumption | Engineering "Why" |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 11 | **No Shear Stress due to Bending** — Transverse shear deformation is ignored in pure bending derivation | Derivation focuses purely on flexural (normal) stresses |
| 12 | **No Warping** — Cross-sections do not distort in their own plane | Maintains symmetry; warping torsion is treated separately |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this single table for every derivation in Module 3 (Bending Stresses, Shear Stresses, Combined Stresses).

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Valid Under |
|---|-------------------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Bending strain compatibility | $\dfrac{\text{N/mm}^2}{\text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{N/mm}^2}{\text{mm}}$ | All 12 assumptions |
| 2 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y}$ | Flexure formula | $\dfrac{\text{N·mm}}{\text{mm}^4} = \dfrac{\text{N/mm}^2}{\text{mm}}$ | Symmetric bending |
| 3 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress at fiber $y$ | **N/mm² (MPa)** | Elastic limit |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{E I}{R}$ | Moment–Curvature relationship | **N·mm** | Pure bending |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Curvature equation | **1/mm** | Linear elastic |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$ | Strain at fiber $y$ | Dimensionless | Plane sections |
| 7 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress | **N/mm²** | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{\max}}$ |
| 8 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law | **N/mm²** | Elastic range |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil:** Design of RCC beams, prestressed girders, bridge girders (IRC loading).
- **Mechanical:** Crankshafts, leaf springs, pressure vessel nozzles.
- **Aerospace:** Aircraft wing spars and helicopter rotor blades.
- **Failure Mode:** When assumptions break (e.g., short deep beams), **shear deformation theory** by Timoshenko must replace Euler–Bernoulli.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Strain at a Fiber (Linear Strain Distribution)

**Starting Premise:** A straight beam of length $L$ bends into an arc of radius $R$.

**Step 1 — Initial Geometry (Before Bending):**

All fibers at distance $y$ from the neutral axis have original length:

$$L_0 = R \cdot \theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle subtended at the center of curvature.

**Step 2 — Deformed Geometry (After Bending):**

A fiber at distance $y$ above the neutral axis has new length:

$$L_f = (R + y) \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3 — Change in Length (Elongation):**

$$\Delta L = L_f - L_0 = (R + y)\theta - R\theta = y \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4 — Strain (Definition):**

Strain $\varepsilon$ is change in length per unit original length:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{y \cdot \theta}{R \cdot \theta}$$

**Step 5 — Simplification:**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The angle $\theta$ cancels out, proving that **strain is purely a function of $y$ and $R$** — it is independent of the absolute length of the beam. This is the cornerstone of the "plane sections remain plane" assumption.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stress at a Fiber (Using Hooke's Law)

**Step 1 — Apply Hooke's Law (Assumption #3):**

For a material within the elastic limit:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Strain:**

$$\sigma = E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Form the Curvature Equation:**

$$\boxed{\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This single equation connects **geometry** ($y$, $R$) to **material stiffness** ($E$) and **stress** ($\sigma$). It is the **half-bridge** between the geometry of deformation and the resulting stress field.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Complete Flexure Formula $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y}$

**Step 1 — Consider a Cross-Section Element:**

Take an elemental strip of area $dA$ at distance $y$ from the neutral axis. The elemental force on this strip due to bending stress $\sigma$ is:

$$dF = \sigma \cdot dA$$

**Step 2 — Elemental Moment Contribution:**

The moment of this elemental force about the neutral axis is:

$$dM = y \cdot dF = y \cdot \sigma \cdot dA$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Stress from Previous Derivation:**

$$dM = y \cdot \left( \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R} \right) \cdot dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \cdot y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Over the Entire Cross-Section:**

The total resisting moment $M$ equals the sum of all elemental moments:

$$M = \int_A dM = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the Moment of Inertia:**

By definition, the second moment of area about the neutral axis is:

$$I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

**Step 6 — Form the Moment–Curvature Relation:**

$$M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the Common Ratio:**

Since both $\dfrac{M}{EI}$ and $\dfrac{1}{R}$ are equal to curvature, and earlier $\dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$:

$$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **complete Bending Equation** — universally valid under all 12 assumptions. From this, **every** Module 3 result (section modulus, stress block, neutral axis location) is derived.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Consequence of Violating Each Assumption

> [!WARNING]
> This matrix is **critical** for KTU 14-mark answers where examiners ask: *"Discuss the limitations of the simple bending theory."*

| Assumption Violated | Practical Effect | Corrective Theory |
|---|---|---|
| Plane sections warp | Shear deformation in deep beams | **Timoshenko Beam Theory** |
| Material non-linear | Plastic hinge formation | **Plastic Analysis** |
| Non-prismatic | Variable $I(x)$ complicates integration | **Stepped/tapered beam analysis** |
| Large deflections | Geometric non-linearity | **Von Kármán non-linear theory** |
| Skew loading | Combined bending + torsion | **Biaxial + torsional analysis** |
| Anisotropic material | Direction-dependent $E$ | **Orthotropic plate theory** (e.g., plywood, composites) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of Bending Theory

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the 12 assumptions feed into the Bending Equation, and what downstream outputs they enable.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Loaded Prismatic Beam]) --> geom{Geometry Check}
    geom -->|Straight Axis| a1[Assumption 1: Initial Straightness]
    geom -->|Uniform Section| a2[Assumption 2: Prismatic Section]
    geom -->|Large R| a3[Assumption 3: Large Radius of Curvature]

    a1 --> mat{Material Check}
    a2 --> mat
    a3 --> mat

    mat -->|Uniform Composition| b1[Assumption 4: Homogeneity]
    mat -->|Same E all directions| b2[Assumption 5: Isotropy]
    mat -->|Linear Stress-Strain| b3[Assumption 6: Hooke Law Valid]

    b1 --> load{Loading Check}
    b2 --> load
    b3 --> load

    load -->|In Plane of Bending| c1[Assumption 7: Symmetric Bending]
    load -->|Self Weight Neglected| c2[Assumption 8: No Body Forces]
    load -->|Small Deflection| c3[Assumption 9: Linear Superposition]

    c1 --> str{Stress Field Check}
    c2 --> str
    c3 --> str

    str -->|Plane Sections Remain Plane| d1[Assumption 10: Bernoulli Hypothesis]
    str -->|No Shear in Pure Bending| d2[Assumption 11: Pure Normal Stress]
    str -->|No Warping| d3[Assumption 12: Rigid Cross Section]

    d1 --> eq((Bending Equation))
    d2 --> eq
    d3 --> eq

    eq --> out1[Flexure Formula M by I = sigma by y]
    eq --> out2[Moment Curvature M = EI by R]
    eq --> out3[Section Modulus Z = I by y_max]
    eq --> out4[Maximum Stress sigma_max = M by Z]

    out1 --> exam[KTU 2024 Module 3 Outcomes]
    out2 --> exam
    out3 --> exam
    out4 --> exam

    exam --> end([Engineering Design Decisions])
```

## 4.2 Visualization of Strain Variation Across a Cross-Section

```mermaid
graph LR
    n1[Top Fiber: y = +y_max] -->|Compression or Tension| n2[Strain eps_top = y_max by R]
    n2 --> n3[Stress sigma_top = E times y_max by R]
    n3 --> n4[NA Line: y = 0]
    n4 -->|Zero Strain, Zero Stress| n5[Neutral Axis]
    n5 --> n6[Bottom Fiber: y = -y_max]
    n6 --> n7[Strain eps_bot = -y_max by R]
    n7 --> n8[Stress sigma_bot = -E times y_max by R]

    style n4 fill:#FFD700
    style n5 fill:#FFD700
    style n3 fill:#FFB6C1
    style n8 fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **yellow band** at $y = 0$ is the **Neutral Axis (NA)** — strain and stress both vanish here. Above the NA: one type of stress (say compression); below: opposite (tension). The sign depends on the **sagging** or **hogging** moment convention.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the theory of simple bending.**

**Model Answer:**

The following assumptions are made:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**.
2. The beam is **initially straight** and has a **uniform cross-section** along its length.
3. **Self-weight** of the beam is neglected.
4. The load is applied in the plane of bending — **pure (symmetric) bending** with no twisting.
5. **Plane sections remain plane** and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending (**Bernoulli's hypothesis**).
6. The radius of curvature $R$ is **large** compared to beam depth.
7. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** — stresses remain within the **elastic limit**.
8. **Shear deformation** and **warping** of the cross-section are negligible.
9. **Deflections are small** — linear elastic theory applies.

*(3 marks — full marks awarded for any 6 well-explained assumptions)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Bernoulli's hypothesis? Why is it critical for the bending equation?**

**Model Answer:**

**Bernoulli's hypothesis** states that *plane cross-sections of a beam, initially plane and perpendicular to the longitudinal axis, remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis even after deformation*.

It is **critical** because:

- It allows the **linear strain distribution** $\varepsilon = y/R$ across the depth.
- This linearity, combined with Hooke's Law, yields the **bending stress equation** $\sigma = E \cdot y / R$.
- Without it, shear deformation would dominate and the simple flexure formula would fail (as in **Timoshenko beam theory** for deep beams).

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **internal choice** is mandatory. Both options below carry full 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Model Paper Adaptation]`

**(a)** State and explain in detail all the assumptions used in the **theory of simple bending**. Discuss the consequences if any **two** assumptions are violated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported steel beam of span **4 m** carries a **uniformly distributed load (UDL) of 20 kN/m** over its entire span. The beam has a rectangular cross-section of **breadth $b = 150$ mm** and **depth $d = 250$ mm**. Determine:
- (i) The **maximum bending moment**.
- (ii) The **maximum bending stress** at the extreme fibers.
- (iii) The **radius of curvature** at the section of maximum moment.

Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²**. **(7 marks)**

### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Model Answer (7 marks):**

State all 12 assumptions (use the table from Section 2 above) — give **5 marks**.
Discuss **two** violations — give **2 marks**. Examples:

- **Violation 1: Plane sections warp** → In deep beams (depth > span/10), shear strain becomes significant. The beam behaves as a **Timoshenko beam**, and bending stresses deviate from the linear $\sigma = My/I$ distribution.
- **Violation 2: Material exceeds elastic limit** → Stress is no longer proportional to strain; a **plastic hinge** forms and the section undergoes plastic rotation. The flexure formula is replaced by the **fully plastic moment** $M_p = \sigma_y \cdot Z_p$.

---

**(b) Step-by-Step Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
