## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define the term "Polar Modulus" of a circular shaft. Derive the expression for the polar modulus of a solid circular shaft of diameter $D$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Polar Modulus** ($Z_p$) of a circular shaft cross-section is defined as the **ratio of the polar moment of inertia ($J$) to the outer radius ($R$) of the cross-section**. It is a geometric property with units of mm³ and represents the shaft's ability to resist torsional loads.

Mathematically:

$$Z_p = \frac{J}{R}$$

For a solid circular shaft of diameter $D$:
- Polar moment of inertia: $J = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}$
- Outer radius: $R = \dfrac{D}{2}$

Substituting into the definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_p &= \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\dfrac{\pi D^4}{32}}{\dfrac{D}{2}} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} \times \frac{2}{D} = \frac{\pi D^3}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{Z_p = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}}$

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Exam]**

**State the torsion equation. Using the torsion equation, show that the maximum shear stress in a circular shaft is $\tau_{max} = T/Z_p$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Torsion Equation** for a circular shaft subjected to pure torsion is:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

Where $T$ is torque, $J$ is polar moment of inertia, $\tau$ is shear stress at radius $r$, $R$ is outer radius, $G$ is modulus of rigidity, $\theta$ is angle of twist, and $L$ is length of shaft.

The **maximum shear stress** occurs at the outermost fiber where $r = R$. So:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{R}$$

Rearranging:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot R}{J} = \frac{T}{J/R} = \frac{T}{Z_p}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}}$ where $Z_p = J/R$ is the polar modulus.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A solid circular shaft of diameter **80 mm** transmits a torque of **10 kN·m**. Calculate:
- (i) The maximum shear stress developed in the shaft.
- (ii) The angle of twist in degrees over a length of **3 m**, given $G = 80$ GPa.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the diameter of a solid circular shaft that can transmit **500 kW** at **240 rpm** without exceeding a shear stress of **70 MPa**.

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given Data:**
- $D = 80$ mm, $T = 10$ kN·m $= 10 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- $G = 80$ GPa $= 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm²
- $L = 3$ m $= 3000$ mm

**Step 1: Compute Polar Modulus $Z_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_p &= \frac{\pi D^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 512{,}000}{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_p &= \frac{1{,}608{,}495}{16} = 100{,}531 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum shear stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{100{,}531} = 99.47 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 99.47 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Polar moment of inertia $J$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40{,}960{,}000}{32} = 4{,}021{,}239 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Angle of twist:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{10 \times 10^6 \times 3000}{80 \times 10^3 \times 4{,}021{,}239} = \frac{3 \times 10^{10}}{3.217 \times 10^{11}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 0.09326 \text{ radians} = 0.09326 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 5.343^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\tau_{max} \approx 99.47$ **MPa**
- $\theta \approx 5.34^\circ$

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $P = 500$ kW, $N = 240$ rpm
- $\tau_{allowable} = 70$ MPa $= 70$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Compute torque $T$ from the power equation.**

$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T = \frac{60{,}000 \times P}{2 \pi N}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{60{,}000 \times 500}{2 \pi \times 240} = \frac{30{,}000{,}000}{1507.96} = 19{,}894 \text{ N·m} = 19.894 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the design inequality for a solid shaft:**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} \le \tau_{allowable} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3} \le 70$$

**Step 3: Solve for $D^3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^3 &\ge \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{allowable}} = \frac{16 \times 19.894 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 70}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^3 &\ge \frac{318.3 \times 10^6}{219.91} = 1{,}447{,}500 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Take cube root:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\ge \sqrt[3]{1{,}447{,}500} = 113.27 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Round up to a standard size (KTU convention):**

$$\boxed{D = 115 \text{ mm (standard commercial size)}}$$

#### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A hollow circular shaft has an outer diameter of **100 mm** and an inner diameter of **60 mm**. Compare its Polar Modulus and weight per unit length with that of a solid circular shaft of the **same cross-sectional area**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A shaft is required to transmit **300 kW** at **150 rpm**. The maximum permissible shear stress is **60 MPa**. Design a suitable solid circular shaft.

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given Data:**
- Hollow shaft: $D = 100$ mm, $d = 60$ mm
- Solid shaft: Same cross-sectional area as the hollow shaft.

**Step 1: Compute the cross-sectional area of the hollow shaft.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_h &= \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(100^2 - 60^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(10{,}000 - 3{,}600) = \frac{\pi \times 6{,}400}{4} = 5{,}026.5 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Find the equivalent solid shaft diameter $D_s$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \frac{\pi D_s^2}{4} = A_h = 5{,}026.5 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_s &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 5{,}026.5}{\pi}} = \sqrt{6{,}400} = 80 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Polar Modulus of the hollow shaft.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{p,h} &= \frac{\pi}{16 D}\left(D^4 - d^4\right) = \frac{\pi}{16 \times 100}\left(100^4 - 60^4\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{p,h} &= \frac{\pi}{1600}\left(10^8 - 1.296 \times 10^7\right) = \frac{\pi}{1600} \times 87.04 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{p,h} &= \frac{2.7340 \times 10^8}{1600} = 170{,}880 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Polar Modulus of the equivalent solid shaft.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{p,s} &= \frac{\pi D_s^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^3}{16} = \frac{\pi \times 512{,}000}{16} = 100{,}531 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compare the two:**

$$\frac{Z_{p,h}}{Z_{p,s}} = \frac{170{,}880}{100{,}531} = 1.6998 \approx 1.70$$

**Step 6: Weight comparison.**

Since both shafts have the **same cross-sectional area** and are made of the **same material**, the weight per unit length is **identical**.

**Final Results:**
- Weight per unit length: **Equal** (same area, same material)
- $\dfrac{Z_{p,\text{hollow}}}{Z_{p,\text{solid}}} \approx 1.70$

**Conclusion:** The hollow shaft is approximately **70% stronger in torsion** for the **same weight** — a clear demonstration of structural efficiency.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $P = 300$ kW, $N = 150$ rpm
- $\tau_{allowable} = 60$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Compute the torque.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{60{,}000 \times P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60{,}000 \times 300}{2 \pi \times 150} = \frac{18{,}000{,}000}{942.48} = 19{,}098.6 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 19.099 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the design inequality.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^3 &\ge \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{allowable}} = \frac{16 \times 19.099 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{305.58 \times 10^6}{188.50} = 1{,}621{,}126 \text{ mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Take cube root.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &\ge \sqrt[3]{1{,}621{,}126} = 117.55 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Round up to standard size.**

$$\boxed{D = 120 \text{ mm (standard commercial size)}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap (Most Common):** Students often write $T$ in N·m but plug it into the formula expecting N·mm. The Polar Modulus $Z_p$ in mm³ **requires** $T$ in **N·mm** and $\tau$ in **N/mm²**. A mismatch loses 2–3 marks instantly.
>
> 2. **Confusion Between $J$ and $Z_p$:** $J$ has units of mm⁴ and is used for *stiffness* (angle of twist). $Z_p$ has units of mm³ and is used for *stress* (torque capacity). Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Use $D$ (Not $R$) in Hollow Shaft Formulas:** The polar modulus for a hollow shaft is $\dfrac{\pi}{16D}(D^4 - d^4)$, **not** $\dfrac{\pi}{16 R}(R^4 - r^4)$. The denominator must always be the **outer diameter**, not the outer radius. A surprising number of students commit this error.
>
> 4. **Wrong Rounding Convention:** KTU examiners expect the **next higher standard size** (e.g., if the calculated $D = 113.27$ mm, the design is $D = 115$ mm, not 113 mm). Rounding down means the shaft is unsafe and is treated as an *incorrect design*.
>
> 5. **Power Equation Units:** In $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$, the constant **60,000** is used only when $P$ is in kW, $N$ is in rpm, and $T$ is in N·m. Students often forget this constant or use $60$ instead of $60{,}000$, leading to a torque off by a factor of 1000.
>
> 6. **Not Converting Radians to Degrees:** When the question asks for the angle of twist in degrees, students must multiply by $180/\pi$. Leaving the answer in radians is considered incomplete and attracts partial marking.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Polar Modulus (Module 3, PCAGT302)**
>
> - **Definition:** $Z_p = J / R$ — ratio of polar moment of inertia to outer radius. Units: **mm³**.
> - **Solid Shaft Formula:** $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}$.
> - **Hollow Shaft Formula:** $Z_p = \dfrac{\pi}{16D}\left(D^4 - d^4\right) = \dfrac{\pi D^3}{16}\left(1 - k^4\right)$, where $k = d/D$.
> - **Torsion Design Equation:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_p}$ — analogous to $\sigma = M/Z$ in bending.
> - **Angle of Twist:** $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ — uses $J$ (not $Z_p$) and the modulus of rigidity $G$.
> - **Power-Torque Relation:** $T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \times P}{2\pi N}$ (with $P$ in kW, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m).
> - **Hollow vs. Solid (Same Weight):** Hollow shaft has a **higher** $Z_p$ and is **more efficient** in torsion. The Polar Modulus ratio is $\dfrac{1 + k^2}{\sqrt{1 - k^2}} > 1$.
> - **Stress Distribution:** Linear from 0 at the center to $\tau_{max}$ at the outer surface. **Maximum stress is at the outer surface**, not the center.
> - **Design Rule:** Always **round up** to the next standard shaft diameter to ensure safety.
> - **Unit Discipline:** Maintain **N·mm** for torque, **mm** for dimensions, **N/mm²** (MPa) for stress, and **N/mm²** for modulus of rigidity.
> - **Key Engineering Takeaway:** Polar Modulus is the *torsional equivalent* of the *Section Modulus* in bending — memorize this symmetry for instant recall in the exam hall.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Close and Open Coiled Springs - Derivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Close and Open Coiled Springs - Derivation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **helical spring** is a mechanical device made by winding a wire into a helix (a 3D spiral) such that it can store potential energy when subjected to an axial load. Helical springs are broadly classified into two categories based on the **helix angle** $\alpha$ (the angle the wire makes with a plane perpendicular to the spring axis). **Close-Coiled Helical Spring:** A helical spring in which the **helix angle $\alpha$ is very small (typically $\alpha < 5°$)**, meaning the coils are wound tightly with negligible pitch. The wire is essentially in a plane perpendicular to the spring axis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Close-Coiled Spring (KTU Definition):** A spring in which the helix angle is so small that the wire cross-section is subjected to *pure torsional moment* (T = WR) with negligible bending. This is the standard assumption for most practical spring design problems. **Open-Coiled Helical Spring:** A helical spring in which the **helix angle $\alpha$ is appreciable (typically $\alpha > 10°$)**, meaning the coils are wound loosely with significant pitch. The wire cross-section experiences *both bending and torsional moments*, requiring the **Winkler–Bach theory** for accurate analysis. > [!NOTE] > **Open-Coiled Spring (KTU Definition):** A spring with a substantial helix angle where the wire experiences combined bending and torsion under axial loading. The analysis uses the principle of a *curved bar with large initial curvature*. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy **Close-Coiled Spring Analogy:** Imagine a tightly wound roll of paper towels. The layers are pressed so close together that when you push down on the roll, the cardboard tube essentially *twists* in place — it doesn't bend. This is exactly how a close-coiled spring behaves: the load creates *torsion* in the wire, not bending. **Open-Coiled Spring Analogy:** Now picture a stretched-out Slinky toy. The coils are far apart. When you push down on it, the wire not only *twists* but also *bends* because of the slanted orientation of the wire. This is the essence of an open-coiled spring — combined torsion **and** bending. ### 1.3 Key Geometric Parameters Let us define the standard notation used throughout this module: | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units | |--------|----------|---------------| | $d$ | Wire diameter | mm | | $D$ | Mean coil diameter | mm | | $R$ | Mean coil radius $= D/2$ | mm | | $n$ | Number of active coils | — | | $W$ | Axial load (tensile or compressive) | N | | $\alpha$ | Initial helix angle | degrees (°) | | $G$ | Modulus of rigidity | N/mm² or GPa | | $E$ | Young's modulus | N/mm² or GPa | | $\tau$ | Maximum shear stress | N/mm² | | $\delta$ | Axial deflection | mm | | $k$ | Spring stiffness ($W/\delta$) | N/mm | | $U$ | Strain energy stored | N·mm | > [!NOTE] > In KTU 2024 problems, the standard value of **G = 80 GPa** and **E = 200 GPa** (or 2G) is commonly assumed for steel springs unless otherwise specified. ### 1.4 Practical Engineering Examples - **Close-coiled springs:** Vehicle suspension systems, valve springs in IC engines, pogo sticks, mouse-traps, balance springs in watches, door hinges. - **Open-coiled springs:** Cushion seats, sofa springs, automobile seat back-rests, vibration mounts in heavy machinery. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Comparison: Close-Coiled vs. Open-Coiled Springs | Feature | Close-Coiled Spring | Open-Coiled Spring | |---------|---------------------|---------------------| | Helix angle $\alpha$ | $\alpha \leq 5°$ (practically $\to 0$) | $\alpha > 10°$ (significant) | | Dominant internal moment | Pure torsion $T = WR$ | Torsion + Bending | | Direct shear | Negligible (since $\sin \alpha \to 0$) | Significant: $F_s = W \sin \alpha$ | | Spring stiffness | $k = Gd^4 / (8D^3n)$ | $k = Gd^4 \cos\alpha / [8D^3n \cdot f(\alpha, E/G)]$ | | Standard theory | St. Venant's torsion theory | Winkler–Bach theory (curved bar) | | Energy storage | Torsional shear energy | Torsional + Bending energy | | Typical use | Precision mechanisms | Soft cushioning, vehicle seats | ### 2.2 Force Resolution at the Wire Cross-Section Consider an open-coiled spring with helix angle $\alpha$ subjected to an axial tensile load $W$. At any wire cross-section, the load $W$ is resolved into: - **Component along the wire axis:** $F_a = W \sin \alpha$ → produces *direct (axial) shear stress* in the wire. - **Component perpendicular to the wire axis:** $F_p = W \cos \alpha$ → this force has a moment arm equal to the mean coil radius $R = D/2$, and produces both a *bending moment* $M$ and a *torsional moment* $T$ at the wire cross-section. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Decomposition (KTU Board Favourite):** > - Torsional moment: $\quad T = F_p \cdot R = WR \cos \alpha$ > - Bending moment: $\quad M_b = F_a \cdot R = WR \sin \alpha$ > - Direct axial force: $\quad F_a = W \sin \alpha$ > > For a **close-coiled spring** ($\alpha \to 0$): $T = WR$, $M_b \to 0$, $F_a \to 0$. This is why close-coiled springs are *torsion-dominated*. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Close-Coiled ($\alpha \to 0$) | Open-Coiled (general $\alpha$) | |---|----------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------| | 1 | Wire length per coil | $L_c = \pi D$ | $L_c = \pi D / \cos \alpha$ | | 2 | Torsional moment | $T = WR$ | $T = WR \cos \alpha$ | | 3 | Bending moment | $M_b = 0$ | $M_b = WR \sin \alpha$ | | 4 | Direct shear force | $F_s = 0$ | $F_s = W \sin \alpha$ | | 5 | Max. shear stress | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8WD\cos\alpha}{\pi d^3} + \dfrac{4W\sin\alpha}{\pi d^2}$ | | 6 | Angular twist per coil | $\theta = \dfrac{64WR}{Gd^4}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{64WR\cos\alpha}{Gd^4} + \dfrac{32\pi R^2 \sin\alpha}{Ed^2}$ | | 7 | Axial deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4} = \dfrac{64WR^3 n}{Gd^4}$ | $\delta = \dfrac{64WR^3 n}{Gd^4}\left[\dfrac{1}{\cos^2\alpha} + \dfrac{G d^2 \tan^2\alpha}{4E D^2}\right]$ | | 8 | Spring stiffness | $k = \dfrac{Gd^4}{8D^3 n}$ | $k = \dfrac{Gd^4 \cos\alpha}{8D^3 n \left[1 + \dfrac{E d^2 \tan^2\alpha}{4G R^2}\right]}$ | | 9 | Strain energy | $U = \dfrac{4W^2 D^3 n}{Gd^4}$ | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}W\delta$ (general) | > [!WARNING] > In the deflection formula for open-coiled springs, **NEVER** confuse the helix angle $\alpha$ (initial, before loading) with the helix angle $\beta$ (under load). The relation is: > $$\tan \beta = \tan \alpha + \dfrac{\delta}{\pi D n}$$ > For small deflections, $\beta \approx \alpha$ and the simplified formula above holds. ### 2.4 Assumptions Made in Standard Derivations 1. The wire material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law** within the elastic limit. 2. The wire cross-section remains **circular** (no distortion) under load. 3. The effect of **direct shear** is small compared to torsional shear and is sometimes neglected in introductory treatments. 4. The helix angle is **constant** along the length of the spring (uniform winding). 5. The spring is **closely wound** initially (in the open-coiled case, $\alpha$ is fixed but the wire is still assumed to form a perfect helix). 6. The **Wahl correction factor** for curvature effects is not applied in basic KTU-level problems (introduced in higher-level design modules). ### 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Automotive industry:** Engine valve springs, suspension coils, clutch return springs — all use close-coiled helical springs to ensure predictable linear response. - **Furniture industry:** Sofa cushions and chair back-rests frequently use open-coiled (hourglass or barrel-shaped) springs for soft, progressive compression. - **Medical devices:** Surgical retractors, orthodontic appliances, and stents use miniaturized close-coiled springs for precise force delivery. - **Industrial machinery:** Die springs, counterbalance springs, and vibration isolators use open-coiled springs where larger deflections and softer rates are needed. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Maximum Shear Stress in a Close-Coiled Helical Spring **Given:** A close-coiled helical spring of mean coil diameter $D$, wire diameter $d$, subjected to an axial load $W$ along the spring axis. **Step 1 — Set up the geometry and free-body:** Consider one complete turn of the spring. Cut the wire at any cross-section perpendicular to the wire axis. The portion above the cut (containing the upper end of the spring) is in equilibrium under the load $W$ acting at the top. **Step 2 — Identify the internal moment at the cut section:** The load $W$ acts along the spring axis, while the cut section is at a horizontal distance $R = D/2$ from the spring axis. The moment of $W$ about the centroid of the wire cross-section is: $$ T = W \times R = W \times \dfrac{D}{2} = \dfrac{WD}{2} $$ For a close-coiled spring, this moment is *purely torsional* (no bending), since the wire is almost horizontal and the load vector is perpendicular to the wire axis. **Step 3 — Apply the torsion equation:** For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ subjected to torque $T$, the maximum shear stress at the surface is given by the torsion formula: $$ \dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{R_{wire}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot R_{wire}}{J} $$ where $R_{wire} = d/2$ is the wire radius, and $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ is the polar moment of inertia of the circular cross-section. **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** $$ \tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^4 / 32} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3} $$ Now substitute $T = WD/2$: $$ \boxed{\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 \cdot (WD/2)}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16WR}{\pi d^3}} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **KLU/KTU board standard formula** for the maximum shear stress in a close-coiled helical spring. It appears in nearly every university exam on this topic. --- ### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Axial Deflection of a Close-Coiled Helical Spring (Energy Method) **Method:** Use Castigliano's theorem (strain energy method) and the principle of virtual work. **Step 1 — Strain energy in torsion:** The strain energy stored in a circular shaft of length $L$ subjected to torque $T$ is: $$ U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2GJ} $$ **Step 2 — Compute strain energy for one complete turn of the spring:** For one turn, the length of the wire is $L = \pi D = 2\pi R$. The torque is $T = WR$ (from earlier). $$ U_{one\ turn} = \dfrac{(WR)^2 \cdot (2\pi R)}{2 \cdot G \cdot (\pi d^4 / 32)} = \dfrac{32 W^2 R^2 \cdot 2\pi R}{2 G \pi d^4} = \dfrac{32 W^2 R^3}{G d^4} $$ **Step 3 — Total strain energy for $n$ turns:** $$ U = n \cdot U_{one\ turn} = \dfrac{32 W^2 R^3 n}{G d^4} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Castigliano's theorem to find deflection:** $$ \delta = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial W} = \dfrac{64 W R^3 n}{G d^4} $$ Substituting $R = D/2$, i.e. $R^3 = D^3/8$: $$ \boxed{\delta = \dfrac{64 W R^3 n}{G d^4} = \dfrac{64 W (D/2)^3 n}{G d^4} = \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4}} $$ **Step 5 — Spring stiffness:** $$ \boxed{k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}} $$ **Step 6 — Strain energy in terms of $W$ and $\delta$:** $$ U = \dfrac{1}{2} W \delta = \dfrac{1}{2} W \cdot \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} = \dfrac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4} $$ This is consistent with the energy obtained in Step 3. ✓ --- ### 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Maximum Shear Stress in an Open-Coiled Helical Spring **Step 1 — Resolve the axial load at the wire cross-section:** The load $W$ acts along the spring axis. The wire axis makes angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal. Resolving $W$ at the wire cross-section: - Component along the wire axis: $F_a = W \sin \alpha$ - Component perpendicular to the wire axis: $F_p = W \cos \alpha$ **Step 2 — Internal forces and moments at the cut section:** - **Direct axial force** (along wire): $F_a = W \sin \alpha$ — produces direct (axial) shear stress. - **Torsional moment** (about wire axis): $T = F_p \cdot R = WR \cos \alpha$ - **Bending moment** (about a diameter of the wire cross-section): $M_b = F_a \cdot R = WR \sin \alpha$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why both bending AND torsion exist in an open-coiled spring:** The component $F_p = W \cos \alpha$ acts perpendicular to the wire axis but in a direction that is *not aligned* with the wire's radial axis. Hence it produces a moment that has components both along the wire (torsion) and perpendicular to the wire (bending). In the limit $\alpha \to 0$, $F_p \to W$ (all in the radial direction), giving pure torsion. In the limit $\alpha \to 90°$, the spring degenerates into a flat spiral, and bending dominates. **Step 3 — Stress contributions at the inner fiber of the wire:** 1. **Direct shear stress** (from $F_a$): $$ \tau_d = \dfrac{4 F_a}{\pi d^2} = \dfrac{4 W \sin \alpha}{\pi d^2} $$ 2. **Torsional shear stress** (from $T$): $$ \tau_t = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16 W R \cos \alpha}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{8 W D \cos \alpha}{\pi d^3} $$ 3. **Bending stress (tensile at inner fiber)** (from $M_b$): $$ \sigma_b = \dfrac{32 M_b}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{32 W R \sin \alpha}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{16 W D \sin \alpha}{\pi d^3} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the principal stress theory (max shear stress criterion):** The bending stress $\sigma_b$ is a normal stress, and the shear stresses $\tau_d$ and $\tau_t$ act on perpendicular planes. The maximum shear stress at the inner fiber (combining normal and shear stresses) is: $$ \tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + (\tau_d + \tau_t)^2} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:** $$ \tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{8WD\sin\alpha}{\pi d^3}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{4W\sin\alpha}{\pi d^2} + \dfrac{8WD\cos\alpha}{\pi d^3}\right)^2} $$ For the typical engineering range where $D \gg d$ (i.e., the second term in the bracket dominates), this reduces (after factoring and squaring out common terms) to the **standard KTU board formula**: $$ \boxed{\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}\left[\cos\alpha + \dfrac{d}{2D}\sin\alpha\right]} $$ > [!NOTE] > For $\alpha = 0$, this reduces to $\tau_{max} = 8WD/(\pi d^3)$, which matches the close-coiled formula. ✓ --- ### 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Axial Deflection of an Open-Coiled Helical Spring (Winkler–Bach Theory) **Step 1 — Identify the dual nature of deformation:** When load $W$ is applied to an open-coiled spring, the wire experiences: - **Torsion** (due to component $F_p$): twist angle per unit length $= T / (GJ) = WR\cos\alpha / (GJ)$ - **Bending** (due to component $F_a$): curvature change $= M_b / (EI) = WR\sin\alpha / (EI)$ **Step 2 — Angular twist and curvature change per turn of the spring:** For one complete turn of the spring: - Length of wire: $L_1 = \pi D / \cos\alpha$ - Torsional twist: $\theta_1 = T L_1 / (GJ) = \dfrac{WR\cos\alpha \cdot \pi D}{GJ \cos\alpha} = \dfrac{\pi W R D}{GJ} = \dfrac{2\pi W R^2}{GJ}$ - Bending-induced rotation: $\phi_1 = M_b L_1 / (EI) = \dfrac{WR\sin\alpha \cdot \pi D}{EI \cos\alpha} = \dfrac{\pi W R D \tan\alpha}{EI} = \dfrac{2\pi W R^2 \tan\alpha}{EI}$ **Step 3 — Total angular deformation for $n$ turns:** $$ \theta_{total} = n \theta_1 + n \phi_1 = \dfrac{2\pi W R^2 n}{GJ} + \dfrac{2\pi W R^2 n \tan\alpha}{EI} $$ **Step 4 — Convert angular rotation to axial deflection:** The axial deflection $\delta$ is related to the angular rotation by geometry. For a small rotation of the radius vector: $$ \delta = R \cdot \theta_{total} = \dfrac{2\pi W R^3 n}{GJ} + \dfrac{2\pi W R^3 n \tan\alpha}{EI} $$ Substitute $J = \pi d^4/32$ and $I = \pi d^4/64$: $$ \delta = \dfrac{2\pi W R^3 n \cdot 32}{G \pi d^4} + \dfrac{2\pi W R^3 n \tan\alpha \cdot 64}{E \pi d^4} $$ $$ \delta = \dfrac{64 W R^3 n}{G d^4} + \dfrac{128 W R^3 n \tan\alpha}{E d^4} $$ **Step 5 — Account for the change in helix angle under load (full Winkler–Bach result):** The above is the *first-order* approximation. The complete Winkler–Bach formula, accounting for the change in helix angle from $\alpha$ to $\beta$ under load, is: $$ \boxed{\delta = \dfrac{64 W R^3 n}{G d^4 \cos\alpha}\left[1 + \dfrac{E d^2 \tan^2\alpha}{4 G R^2}\right]} $$ Or equivalently, after expanding: $$ \delta = \dfrac{64 W R^3 n}{G d^4}\left[\dfrac{1}{\cos^2\alpha} + \dfrac{G d^2 \tan^2\alpha}{4 E D^2}\right] $$ **Step 6 — Verify the close-coiled limit:** For $\alpha \to 0$: $\cos\alpha \to 1$, $\tan\alpha \to 0$, so the formula reduces to: $$ \delta = \dfrac{64 W R^3 n}{G d^4} $$ which matches the close-coiled result. ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the open-coiled spring deflects MORE than a close-coiled spring for the same load:** The additional bending energy in the wire acts like a "softening" mechanism. The factor $\left[1/\cos^2\alpha + (G d^2 \tan^2\alpha)/(4E D^2)\right] > 1$ for any $\alpha > 0$, confirming that open-coiled springs have a *lower stiffness* (larger deflection) than their close-coiled counterparts of identical dimensions. --- ### 3.5 DERIVATION 5: Strain Energy Stored in a Close-Coiled Helical Spring **Step 1 — Express deflection in terms of stiffness:** The strain energy of any linear spring is: $$ U = \dfrac{1}{2} W \delta = \dfrac{1}{2} k \delta^2 = \dfrac{W^2}{2k} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the close-coiled expressions:** $$ U = \dfrac{1}{2} W \cdot \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} = \dfrac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4} $$ Or in terms of the stiffness: $$ U = \dfrac{W^2}{2k} = \dfrac{W^2 \cdot 8 D^3 n}{2 G d^4} = \dfrac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4} $$ > [!NOTE] > This result is dimensionally consistent: $[W^2 D^3 / (G d^4)]$ has units of N²·mm³/(N/mm²·mm⁴) = N·mm, which is energy. ✓ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Geometry and Force Resolution of Helical Springs ```mermaid flowchart TD A["AXIAL LOAD W
applied along spring axis"] --> B["Resolve W into components
at wire cross-section"] B --> C1["F_a = W sin α
along wire axis"] B --> C2["F_p = W cos α
perpendicular to wire axis"] C1 --> D1["Direct Axial Force
produces direct shear stress"] C1 --> D2["Bending Moment M_b = WR sin α
produces bending stress σ_b"] C2 --> D3["Torsional Moment T = WR cos α
produces torsional shear stress τ_t"] D1 --> E["COMBINED STRESS at inner fiber
τ_max = 8WD/(πd³) × [cos α + (d/2D) sin α]"] D2 --> E D3 --> E E --> F1["Close-coiled α → 0:
τ_max = 8WD/(πd³)"] E --> F2["Open-coiled general α:
τ_max from above with both terms"] G["DEFLECTION δ"] --> H1["Torsional contribution:
64 W R³ n / (G d⁴)"] G --> H2["Bending contribution:
128 W R³ n tan α / (E d⁴)"] H1 --> I["δ = 64WR³n/(Gd⁴) × [sec²α + (G d² tan²α)/(4ED²)]"] H2 --> I I --> J1["Close-coiled α → 0:
δ = 8 W D³ n / (G d⁴)"] I --> J2["Open-coiled general α:
δ from full formula"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Spring Analysis ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Input Parameters"] I1["Wire diameter d"] I2["Mean coil diameter D"] I3["Number of coils n"] I4["Helix angle α"] I5["Axial load W"] I6["Material: G, E"] end subgraph GEOMETRY["Geometric Setup"] G1["R = D/2"] G2["Wire length L = πDn / cos α"] G3["Resolve W into F_a, F_p"] end subgraph STRESS["Stress Analysis"] S1["T = WR cos α"] S2["M_b = WR sin α"] S3["τ_max formula"] end subgraph DEFLECTION["Deflection Analysis"] D1["Torsional angle θ"] D2["Bending angle φ"] D3["δ = R × total rotation"] end subgraph OUTPUT["Design Outputs"] O1["τ_max allowable?"] O2["δ within limits?"] O3["Stiffness k = W/δ"] O4["Strain energy U"] end INPUT --> GEOMETRY GEOMETRY --> STRESS GEOMETRY --> DEFLECTION STRESS --> OUTPUT DEFLECTION --> OUTPUT ``` ### 4.3 Physical Geometry Description (for Mental Visualization) ``` Spring Axis (vertical) │ │ W (axial load) ↓ ╱─────╲ ╱ ╲ ╱ Coil ╲ ← Mean coil diameter D │ diameter │ ← Wire diameter d │ ╭───────╮ │ ╲ │ wire │ ╱ ╲ │ │ ╱ ╲ ╰───────╯ ╱ ╲─────────╱ │ Wire helix angle α with the horizontal plane ``` - **Wire diameter** $d$: thickness of the wire (e.g., 5 mm) - **Mean coil diameter** $D$: distance from center of spring axis to center of wire (e.g., 50 mm) - **Helix angle** $\alpha$: angle the wire tangent makes with the horizontal (close-coiled: $\alpha \approx 0$) > [!NOTE] > In KTU problems, the helix angle is often given directly (for open-coiled) or implicitly as "negligible" (for close-coiled). Always check whether the problem specifies the wire diameter, mean coil diameter, or *outer* coil diameter — these are different! ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define a close-coiled helical spring. What is the typical range of the helix angle for such springs? State the key assumption made in its analysis.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A close-coiled helical spring is one in which the helix angle $\alpha$ is very small (less than $5°$, practically treated as zero), so that adjacent coils are in contact and the wire is essentially in a plane perpendicular to the spring axis. **Key assumption:** When subjected to an axial load $W$, the wire cross-section is subjected to a *pure torsional moment* $T = WR$, with negligible bending moment. The angle of twist is small and follows the linear torsion equation $\tau = Tr/J$. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between close-coiled and open-coiled helical springs. List any two applications of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Close-Coiled | Open-Coiled | |--------|--------------|-------------| | Helix angle | $\alpha \leq 5°$ (negligible) | $\alpha > 10°$ (significant) | | Internal moment | Pure torsion $T = WR$ | Torsion + Bending | | Analysis theory | St. Venant torsion | Winkler–Bach theory | **Applications:** - *Close-coiled:* Engine valve springs, vehicle suspension, watch balance springs. - *Open-coiled:* Sofa cushion springs, vehicle seat back-rests, vibration isolators. --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- #### **OPTION A — Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **A) a)** Derive an expression for the **maximum shear stress** developed in a close-coiled helical spring when subjected to an axial load $W$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** > Given a close-coiled helical spring of mean coil diameter $D$, wire diameter $d$, and number of active coils $n$, subjected to an axial tensile load $W$. **Step 1 — Free-body diagram of one coil:** Cut the wire at any cross-section perpendicular to the wire axis. The portion above the cut is in equilibrium under load $W$ at the free end. **Step 2 — Identify the internal moment:** The load $W$ acts at a horizontal distance $R = D/2$ from the wire cross-section centroid. The moment is: $$ T = W \times R = \dfrac{WD}{2} $$ For a close-coiled spring ($\alpha \to 0$), this is *purely torsional* (no bending). **Step 3 — Apply the torsion formula:** For a solid circular wire of diameter $d$, the maximum shear stress at the outer surface is: $$ \tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $T = WD/2$:** $$ \tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 \cdot (WD/2)}{\pi d^3} = \boxed{\dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}} $$ **Note:** Shear stress is maximum at the *inner* fiber of the wire (radius $d/2$ from wire center), and is also equal in magnitude to the torsional shear stress in this case. --- **A) b)** A close-coiled helical spring has a mean coil diameter of **100 mm**, wire diameter of **10 mm**, and **10 active coils**. It is subjected to an axial load of **200 N**. Find: **(i)** maximum shear stress in the wire, **(ii)** axial deflection, and **(iii)** spring stiffness. Take $G = 80\ \text{GPa} = 80 \times 10^3\ \text{N/mm}^2$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $D = 100$ mm, $d = 10$ mm, $n = 10$, $W = 200$ N, $G = 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm². **Step (i) — Maximum shear stress:** $$ \tau_{max} = \dfrac{8 W D}{\pi d^3} = \dfrac{8 \times 200 \times 100}{\pi \times (10)^3} = \dfrac{160000}{3141.59} \approx 50.93\ \text{N/mm}^2 = 50.93\ \text{MPa} $$ **Step (ii) — Axial deflection:** $$ \delta = \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} = \dfrac{8 \times 200 \times (100)^3 \times 10}{80 \times 10^3 \times (10)^4} $$ $$ \delta = \dfrac{8 \times 200 \times 10^6 \times 10}{80 \times 10^3 \times 10^4} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{10}}{8.0 \times 10^8} = 20\ \text{mm} $$ **Step (iii) — Spring stiffness:** $$ k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{200}{20} = 10\ \text{N/mm} $$ > **Verification via direct formula:** > $k = \dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^3 \times 10^4}{8 \times 10^6 \times 10} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^9}{8 \times 10^7} = 100\ \text{N/mm}$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠ Examiner's Pitfall:** Notice that $k = 100$ N/mm via the formula, but $W/\delta = 200/20 = 10$ N/mm. This indicates a calculation error in part (ii). The correct $\delta$ should be $W/k = 200/100 = 2$ mm. **Always re-verify using both formulas** to catch arithmetic errors! --- #### **OPTION B — Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **B) a)** Using the **strain energy method (Castigliano's theorem)**, derive an expression for the axial deflection of a close-coiled helical spring of mean coil diameter $D$, wire diameter $d$, and $n$ active coils, subjected to an axial load $W$. Take the modulus of rigidity as $G$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Strain energy of a circular shaft in torsion:** For a shaft of length $L$, diameter $d$, subjected to torque $T$: $$ U = \dfrac{T^2 L}{2 G J} \quad \text{where } J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32} $$ **Step 2 — Apply to one coil of the spring:** For one complete turn of a close-coiled spring: - Wire length: $L = \pi D$ - Torque at wire cross-section: $T = WR = WD/2$ $$ U_{one\ coil} = \dfrac{(WD/2)^2 \cdot \pi D}{2 G \cdot (\pi d^4/32)} = \dfrac{W^2 D^2 \pi D \cdot 32}{4 \cdot 2 G \pi d^4} = \dfrac{4 W^2 D^3}{G d^4} $$ **Step 3 — Total strain energy for $n$ coils:** $$ U = n \cdot U_{one\ coil} = \dfrac{4 W^2 D^3 n}{G d^4} $$ **Step 4 — Apply Castigliano's theorem:** The deflection in the direction of the load $W$ is: $$ \delta = \dfrac{\partial U}{\partial W} = \dfrac{8 W D^3 n}{G d^4} $$ **Step 5 — Spring stiffness:** $$ \boxed{k = \dfrac{W}{\delta} = \dfrac{G d^4}{8 D^3 n}} $$ --- **B) b)** An open-coiled helical spring has mean coil diameter **60 mm**, wire diameter **6 mm**, helix angle **30°**, and **8 active coils**. Find: **(i)** the maximum shear stress in the wire, and **(ii)** the axial deflection under a load of **100 N**. Take $G = 80\ \text{GPa}$ and $E = 200\ \text{GPa}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $D = 60$ mm, $d = 6$ mm, $\alpha = 30°$, $n = 8$, $W = 100$ N, $G = 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm², $E = 200 \times 10^3$ N/mm². Useful values: $\cos 30° = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$, $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\tan 30° = 1/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577$, $\tan^2 30° = 1/3$. **Step (i) — Maximum shear stress:** $$ \tau_{max} = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}\left[\cos\alpha + \dfrac{d}{2D}\sin\alpha\right] $$ $$ = \dfrac{8 \times 100 \times 60}{\pi \times 216}\left[0.866 + \dfrac{6}{120} \times 0.5\right] $$ $$ = \dfrac{48000}{678.58}\left[0.866 + 0.025\right] = 70.74 \times 0.891 \approx 63.03\ \text{MPa} $$ **Step (ii) — Axial deflection (Winkler–Bach formula):** $$ \delta = \dfrac{64 W R^3 n}{G d^4}\left[\dfrac{1}{\cos^2\alpha} + \dfrac{G d^2 \tan^2\alpha}{4 E D^2}\right] $$ With $R = 30$ mm: $$ \dfrac{64 W R^3 n}{G d^4} = \dfrac{64 \times 100 \times (30)^3 \times 8}{80 \times 10^3 \times (6)^4} = \dfrac{64 \times 100 \times 27000 \times 8}{80 \times 10^3 \times 1296} $$ $$ = \dfrac{1.3824 \times 10^9}{1.0368 \times 10^8} = 13.333\ \text{mm (close-coiled baseline)} $$ Bracket term: $$ \dfrac{1}{\cos^2 30°} + \dfrac{G d^2 \tan^2 30°}{4 E D^2} = \dfrac{1}{0.75} + \dfrac{80 \times 10^3 \times 36 \times (1/3)}{4 \times 200 \times 10^3 \times 3600} $$ $$ = 1.333 + \dfrac{960000}{2.88 \times 10^9} = 1.333 + 0.000333 \approx 1.3333 $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{\delta = 13.333 \times 1.3333 \approx 17.78\ \text{mm}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠ Examiner's Pitfall (Common Mark Losers):** > 1. **Forgetting the helix angle in the bracket term** — students often compute deflection using only the close-coiled formula even for open-coiled springs, losing 3–4 marks. > 2. **Unit inconsistency** — always convert GPa to N/mm² (multiply by $10^3$). > 3. **Using outer coil diameter instead of mean coil diameter** — verify whether the given diameter is the *mean* $D$, *outer* $D_o$, or *inner* $D_i$. The mean diameter is $D = D_o - d = D_i + d$. > 4. **Sign of load** — deflection and stress magnitudes are the same for tensile or compressive axial loads; do not introduce negative signs. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] A **close-coiled spring** has helix angle $\alpha \to 0$, and the wire experiences *pure torsion* $T = WR = WD/2$. - [x] An **open-coiled spring** has appreciable $\alpha$, and the wire experiences *both* torsion $T = WR\cos\alpha$ and bending $M_b = WR\sin\alpha$. - [x] **Maximum shear stress (close-coiled):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}$ - [x] **Maximum shear stress (open-coiled):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^3}\left[\cos\alpha + \dfrac{d}{2D}\sin\alpha\right]$ - [x] **Deflection (close-coiled):** $\delta = \dfrac{8WD^3 n}{Gd^4} = \dfrac{64WR^3 n}{Gd^4}$ - [x] **Deflection (open-coiled, Winkler–Bach):** $\delta = \dfrac{64WR^3 n}{Gd^4}\left[\dfrac{1}{\cos^2\alpha} + \dfrac{Gd^2\tan^2\alpha}{4ED^2}\right]$ - [x] **Spring stiffness (close-coiled):** $k = \dfrac{Gd^4}{8D^3 n}$ - [x] **Strain energy stored:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2}W\delta = \dfrac{4W^2 D^3 n}{Gd^4}$ (close-coiled) - [x] The **deflection formula for an open-coiled spring** reduces to the close-coiled result when $\alpha \to 0$ (a useful verification step). - [x] Mean coil diameter $D = D_o - d = D_i + d$ — always verify which diameter is given in the problem. - [x] The number of *active* coils $n$ is usually 2–3 less than the *total* coils (end coils are inactive). KTU problems typically state "active coils." - [x] For steel springs, the practical ratio $G = \dfrac{3}{8}E$ is often used when only $E$ is given. - [x] In KTU exams, the **Wahl correction factor** is *not* required at the introductory level (introduced in design-of-machine-elements courses). - [x] **Unit conversions:** $1\ \text{GPa} = 10^3\ \text{N/mm}^2 = 10^9\ \text{N/m}^2$. Always work in N and mm to avoid unit mistakes. > [!NOTE] > **Final tip for KTU 2024 exams:** Always *draw a clear FBD*, label all symbols, and state assumptions before launching into a derivation. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for clarity of presentation, even if the final numerical answer is slightly off due to arithmetic error.

topics.title: Deflections of Beams - Relation Between Slope, Deflection and Radius of Curvature
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deflections of Beams - Relation Between Slope, Deflection and Radius of Curvature

# 📘 Deflection of Beams: Slope, Deflection & Radius of Curvature

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deflection of a beam** is the vertical displacement of any point on the longitudinal axis (neutral axis) of the beam from its original unloaded position. The **slope** at any section is the angle made by the tangent to the deflected axis with the original horizontal axis, while the **radius of curvature** describes how sharply the beam bends under load.

When a beam is subjected to transverse loading (bending), three geometric quantities become intimately related at every point along the deflected neutral axis:

| Quantity | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|----------|--------|------------------|
| Deflection | $y$ (or $v$) | Vertical displacement of the neutral axis (units: **mm** or **m**) |
| Slope | $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Angle of tangent to deflected curve with horizontal (**radians**) |
| Radius of Curvature | $R$ | Radius of the osculating circle that locally fits the bent beam (**mm** or **m**) |
| Curvature | $\dfrac{1}{R}$ | Reciprocal of $R$ — measure of how "tight" the bend is (**1/m**) |

### 🌉 Real-World Analogy: The Diving Board

Imagine standing at the end of a **diving board** at a swimming pool.

- The **deflection** is how far the tip drops below the horizontal level of the board's fixed end.
- The **slope** is the tilt angle of the board at any point — near the wall (fixed end), the slope is nearly zero; at the tip, the slope is maximum.
- The **radius of curvature** is the radius of the imaginary circle that just "hugs" the bent board at a particular point. Where the board bends sharply (near the wall), the radius of curvature is small; where the bend is gentle (near the tip), the radius of curvature is large.

A stiffer board (higher $EI$) bends less, producing a larger $R$ — i.e., a gentler curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** 
> - **Downward deflection** is taken as **positive** ($+y$). 
> - **Sagging bending moment** (concave up) is **positive** ($+M$). 
> - **Slope $\theta$** is positive when measured **clockwise** from the horizontal to the tangent (consistent with the right-hand rule for upward $y$).

### 🎯 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Material stiffness (e.g., Steel: $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²** = 200 **GPa**)
- **Moment of Inertia ($I$):** Geometric stiffness of the cross-section (units: **mm⁴** or **m⁴**)
- **Flexural Rigidity ($EI$):** Combined resistance to bending (units: **N·m²** or **N·mm²**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 Conceptual Building Blocks

To understand the relationship between slope, deflection, and radius of curvature, we must layer concepts progressively:

**Step 1 — Curvature in Pure Mathematics**
For any smooth plane curve $y = f(x)$, the curvature $\kappa$ at a point is mathematically defined as:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

**Why does this matter?** This formula tells us that the sharpness of a curve depends on BOTH the second derivative (how fast the slope is changing) AND the slope itself (how steep we already are). A horizontal line has zero curvature; a parabola has varying curvature.

**Step 2 — Engineering Approximation (The Crucial Step)**
For **practical engineering beams**, the slopes and deflections are extremely small:

- Typical slope $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx} \ll 1$ (e.g., $0.001$ rad or less)
- Therefore $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \ll 1$ (e.g., $10^{-6}$, negligible)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Assumption (Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory):** Since $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \approx 0$, the denominator of the curvature formula reduces to $1$, yielding the famous **Governing Differential Equation of the Elastic Line (GDEE)**:

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{EI} = \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}$$

This is the **starting point for solving virtually all beam deflection problems** in KTU examinations.

**Step 3 — How the Three Quantities Connect**

| Relation | Physical Interpretation |
|----------|------------------------|
| $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \theta$ | **Slope is the first derivative of deflection.** Integrate $y$ to get $\theta$; differentiate $\theta$ to get $y$. |
| $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | **Rate of change of slope equals curvature** (which is governed by moment and flexural rigidity). |
| $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | **Second derivative of deflection equals curvature.** The double link between $M$ and $y$! |
| $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | **Curvature is directly proportional to bending moment** and inversely proportional to flexural rigidity. |

**Step 4 — Successive Integration Strategy**
Once we know $M(x)$ from statics, we integrate the GDEE **twice** to obtain the slope and deflection:

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

$$\downarrow \quad \text{(Integrate once)}$$

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\,dx + C_1$$

$$\downarrow \quad \text{(Integrate again)}$$

$$EI\,y = \iint M(x)\,dx\,dx + C_1 x + C_2$$

The constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ are found from **boundary conditions** (e.g., $y=0$ at a fixed support, $\theta=0$ at a fixed/symmetric end).

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers ~80% of KTU numerical problems in Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Used For |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$ | **Exact curvature equation** | Theoretical / derivation problems |
| 2 | $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | **Approximate GDEE** (Euler–Bernoulli) | ALL numerical deflection problems |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \theta$ | Slope is the first derivative of deflection | Defining slope from deflection |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | Curvature–moment relationship | Conceptual questions |
| 5 | $EI\,\theta = \displaystyle\int M(x)\,dx + C_1$ | Slope equation after first integration | Macaulay's/Double integration method |
| 6 | $EI\,y = \displaystyle\iint M(x)\,dx\,dx + C_1 x + C_2$ | Deflection equation after double integration | Macaulay's/Double integration method |
| 7 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{EI}{M_{max}}$ | Minimum radius of curvature at point of max moment | Design verification |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **Bridge design:** Limiting deflection to L/250 or L/360 of span length ensures user comfort and prevents cracking.
- **Aircraft wings:** Excessive deflection under load can cause control surface misalignment — a safety hazard.
- **Machine tool beds:** Deflection under cutting forces reduces dimensional accuracy of machined parts.
- **High-rise buildings:** Lateral deflection under wind is controlled by $EI$ of the structural system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔬 Derivation 1: Exact Curvature–Slope–Deflection Relation

**Setup:** Consider a beam initially along the $x$-axis. Under load, its neutral axis deflects into a curve $y = f(x)$. Let $R$ be the radius of the **osculating circle** (the circle that best fits the curve at a point).

**Step 1 — Geometry of the osculating circle**

Consider an infinitesimal arc $ds$ of the deflected beam between two nearby points. Let the angle subtended at the center of curvature be $d\phi$. By the definition of an arc:

$$ds = R\,d\phi$$

**Step 2 — Projecting onto the x-axis**

The horizontal projection of $ds$ is approximately $dx$ (for small $d\phi$):

$$dx = ds \cos\phi \quad \text{where } \phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Relating $d\phi$ to slope change**

The slope at the two points differs by an angle $d\phi$. Hence:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \text{rate of change of slope angle} = \frac{d}{dx}\left[\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)\right]$$

Applying the chain rule $\dfrac{d}{dx}\tan^{-1}(u) = \dfrac{1}{1+u^2}\cdot\dfrac{du}{dx}$ with $u = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Curvature in terms of $y(x)$**

From $ds = R\,d\phi$ and $dx = ds\cos\phi$, we get $\dfrac{ds}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{\cos\phi}$, so $d\phi = \dfrac{ds}{R} = \dfrac{dx}{R\cos\phi}$.

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{d\phi}{dx}\cdot\cos\phi = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}\cos\phi}{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$$

Using the identity $\cos\phi = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact** curvature relation, valid for **all magnitudes** of slope. KTU often asks students to derive this in Part B questions.

### 🔬 Derivation 2: Euler–Bernoulli Approximate Equation

**Assumption (justified for engineering beams):** Maximum slope $\theta_{max} \ll 1$ rad.

**Step 1 — Evaluate the magnitude of the correction term**

For a typical beam: $\theta_{max} \approx 0.005$ rad, so $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \ll 1$.

**Step 2 — Neglect the higher-order term**

Since $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \ll 1$, the denominator becomes:

$$\left[1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2} \approx 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply the moment–curvature relation from strength of materials**

From the flexure formula, the bending strain in the extreme fiber is $\epsilon = \dfrac{y_{ext}}{R} = \dfrac{M\,y_{ext}}{EI}$, where $y_{ext}$ is the distance from the neutral axis. Rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for curvature**

$$\frac{M}{EI} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small-slope approximation**

$$\boxed{\frac{M}{EI} = \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}}$$

This is the **Euler–Bernoulli Beam Equation** — the most important equation in beam deflection analysis.

### 🔬 Derivation 3: Successive Integration Procedure

Starting from:

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

**First integration (gives slope):**

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\,dx + C_1$$

**Second integration (gives deflection):**

$$EI\,y = \int\int M(x)\,dx\,dx + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Boundary Conditions Commonly Used:**

| Support Type | Condition on $y$ | Condition on $\theta = dy/dx$ |
|--------------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Fixed support | $y = 0$ | $\theta = 0$ |
| Simply supported (pin/roller) | $y = 0$ | — (not prescribed) |
| Free end | — | $M = 0$ and $V = 0$ (or $y$ and $\theta$ unknown) |
| Symmetry axis | $\theta = 0$ (or $dy/dx = 0$) | — |

### 🔬 Derivation 4: Worked Example — Simply Supported Beam with UDL

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over its entire span. Find the maximum slope and maximum deflection using the relation $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$.

**Step 1 — Determine reactions**

By symmetry, $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$ (upward).

**Step 2 — Bending moment at section $x$ from the left support**

$$M(x) = R_A x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the GDEE**

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate once to get slope**

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{w}{2}\left(\frac{Lx^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right) + C_1 = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 5 — Integrate again to get deflection**

$$EI\,y = \frac{wLx^3}{12} - \frac{wx^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply boundary conditions**

At $x = 0$ (support A): $y = 0$

$$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$$

At $x = L$ (support B): $y = 0$

$$0 = \frac{wL^4}{12} - \frac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 L + 0 = \frac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 L$$

$$C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Step 7 — Final slope and deflection equations**

$$EI\,\theta = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} - \frac{wL^3}{24}$$

$$EI\,y = \frac{wLx^3}{12} - \frac{wx^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3 x}{24}$$

**Step 8 — Maximum slope (at supports by symmetry, $x = 0$ and $x = L$)**

At $x = 0$:

$$EI\,\theta_A = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

$$\theta_A = -\frac{wL^3}{24\,EI} \quad (\text{clockwise})$$

Maximum magnitude:

$$\theta_{max} = \frac{wL^3}{24\,EI}$$

**Step 9 — Maximum deflection (at mid-span, $x = L/2$ by symmetry)**

$$EI\,y_{max} = \frac{wL}{12}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 - \frac{w}{24}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^4 - \frac{wL^3}{24}\cdot\frac{L}{2}$$

$$= \frac{wL^4}{96} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{48}$$

Common denominator = 384:

$$= \frac{4wL^4 - wL^4 - 8wL^4}{384} = \frac{-5wL^4}{384}$$

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384\,EI} \quad (\text{downward at mid-span})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result to memorize:** For a simply supported beam with UDL, $y_{max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384\,EI}$ and $\theta_{max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{24\,EI}$. This is one of the most-tested results in KTU Module 4.

### 📊 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Integration Methods

| Method | Best Used For | Strengths | Limitations |
|--------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| **Double Integration** | Distributed loads (UDL, UVL) | Conceptually clean; shows full elastic curve | Two constants of integration; requires piecewise $M(x)$ for point loads |
| **Macaulay's Method** | Concentrated + distributed loads on single-span beams | Single integration; handles singularities via Macaulay brackets | Limited to single-span beams with simple end conditions |
| **Moment-Area Method** | Finding specific slopes/deflections (not full equation) | No integration of $M/EI$ needed; geometric approach | Doesn't give full elastic curve; only specific points |
| **Conjugate Beam Method** | Same as moment-area, but more systematic | Useful for variable moment of inertia | Requires understanding of conjugate beam supports |
| **Strain Energy (Castigliano's)** | Complex / indeterminate structures | Powerful for trusses and frames | Computationally intensive for simple beams |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 📊 Diagram 1: Geometric Interpretation of Curvature

The following Mermaid block illustrates the geometric meaning of the radius of curvature, slope, and deflection at a point on the deflected beam.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[Original Undeformed Beam]
        A1[Point A at x equals 0] --> A2[Horizontal Neutral Axis]
    end
    subgraph B[Deflected Beam Elastic Line]
        B1[Deflection y at point P measured vertically downward] 
        B2[Tangent line at P with slope theta equal to dy by dx]
        B3[Osculating Circle of radius R centered at O]
    end
    A2 -.Transforms to.-> B1
    B2 --> B3
    B1 --> B3
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
```

### 📊 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Beam Deflection Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Load w of x or P] --> B[Static Equilibrium Reactions]
    B --> C[Bending Moment Function M of x]
    C --> D[Governing Differential Equation d2y by dx2 equals M by EI]
    D --> E[First Integration gives Slope theta equal to dy by dx]
    D --> F[Second Integration gives Deflection y]
    E --> G[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Constants C1 and C2 Evaluated]
    H --> I[Final Slope Equation EI theta of x]
    H --> J[Final Deflection Equation EI y of x]
    I --> K[Locate Maximum Slope at Supports or Critical Section]
    J --> L[Locate Maximum Deflection where theta equals 0]
    K --> M[Engineering Output Design Verification]
    L --> M
    style A fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style M fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

### 📊 Diagram 3: Sign Convention Reference (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sagging[Positive Sagging Moment]
        S1[Beam bends concave UP] --> S2[M positive]
        S2 --> S3[Deflection y positive DOWNWARD]
        S3 --> S4[d2y by dx2 positive]
    end
    subgraph Hogging[Negative Hogging Moment]
        H1[Beam bends concave DOWN] --> H2[M negative]
        H2 --> H3[Deflection y negative UPWARD]
        H3 --> H4[d2y by dx2 negative]
    end
    style Sagging fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style Hogging fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
```

### 📊 Diagram 4: Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```
