<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme — 2-mark conceptual + 1-mark explanation pattern.**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

State the relationship between slope, deflection, and radius of curvature of a bent beam. Derive the approximate form of the equation for small deflections.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **exact relationship** between slope, deflection, and radius of curvature is:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

where $R$ = radius of curvature, $y$ = deflection, and $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ = slope.

For **small deflections** (slopes very small, i.e., $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \ll 1$), the higher-order term $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2$ becomes negligible compared to $1$. The denominator reduces to $1$, and combined with the flexure formula $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$, we obtain the **Euler–Bernoulli equation**:

$$\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{M}{EI}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

Explain why the term $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2$ is neglected in beam deflection analysis. Mention the governing differential equation of the elastic line.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The term $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2$ represents the **square of the slope** at any section of the beam. In practical engineering structures, the slope is extremely small (typically less than 0.01 rad). Hence:

$$\left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \leq (0.01)^2 = 0.0001 \ll 1$$

Neglecting this term introduces an error of less than 0.01%, which is well within engineering accuracy. This simplification yields the **Governing Differential Equation of the Elastic Line (GDEE)**:

$$\boxed{EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)}$$

This is the fundamental equation from which all beam deflection problems are solved by successive integration.

---

### 📝 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Internal choice provided. Each part has two 7-mark sub-questions.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** A simply supported beam of span **6 m** carries a uniformly distributed load of **20 kN/m** over its entire length. Using the relation $\dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$, determine the **slope at the supports** and the **maximum deflection** at the centre. Take $EI = 1.2 \times 10^5$ **kN·m²**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the exact relation between slope, deflection, and radius of curvature of a deflected beam. Under what condition is the simplified form $\dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ valid? (7 marks)

---

#### 📖 Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Compute reactions. By symmetry, $R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{20 \times 6}{2} = 60$ kN.

**Step 2:** Bending moment at section $x$ from A:

$$M(x) = 60x - \frac{20x^2}{2} = 60x - 10x^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply the GDEE:

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 60x - 10x^2$$

**Step 4:** First integration (slope):

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = 30x^2 - \frac{10x^3}{3} + C_1$$

**Step 5:** Second integration (deflection):

$$EI\,y = 10x^3 - \frac{10x^4}{12} + C_1 x + C_2 = 10x^3 - \frac{5x^4}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 6:** Boundary conditions:

- At $x = 0$, $y = 0$: $C_2 = 0$
- At $x = 6$, $y = 0$:

$$0 = 10(216) - \frac{5(1296)}{6} + 6C_1 = 2160 - 1080 + 6C_1$$

$$6C_1 = -1080 \Rightarrow C_1 = -180$$

**Step 7:** Slope at support A ($x = 0$):

$$EI\,\theta_A = -180 \Rightarrow \theta_A = \frac{-180}{1.2 \times 10^5} = -1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

$$|\theta_A| = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = \theta_B \text{ (by symmetry)}$$

**Step 8:** Maximum deflection at $x = L/2 = 3$ m:

$$EI\,y_{max} = 10(27) - \frac{5(81)}{6} + (-180)(3) = 270 - 67.5 - 540 = -337.5$$

$$y_{max} = \frac{-337.5}{1.2 \times 10^5} = -2.8125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = -2.81 \text{ mm (downward)}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} \quad ; \quad y_{max} = 2.81 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Consider an infinitesimal arc $ds$ of the deflected beam. The angle subtended at the center of curvature is $d\phi$.

**Step 2:** From arc geometry: $ds = R\,d\phi$, so $R = \dfrac{ds}{d\phi}$.

**Step 3:** The angle of slope $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)$.

**Step 4:** Differentiate:

$$\frac{d\phi}{dx} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Note $dx = ds\cos\phi$, so $\dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \dfrac{d\phi}{ds}\cdot\cos\phi$ and $\cos\phi = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+(dy/dx)^2}}$.

**Step 6:** Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{d\phi}{ds} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

**Step 7 — Validity Condition:** The simplified form $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ is valid when $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \ll 1$, i.e., the **slope is small** (less than ~0.1 rad, typically much smaller for engineering beams).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A cantilever beam of length **3 m** carries a point load of **15 kN** at its free end. Using $\dfrac{M}{EI} = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$, find the **slope** and **deflection** at the free end. Take $EI = 6 \times 10^4$ **kN·m²**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **sign convention** for slope, deflection, and bending moment as per KTU standards. How does the sign of $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ relate to the type of curvature? (7 marks)

---

#### 📖 Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Take origin at the **fixed end** (left), $x$ measured to the right. The section at distance $x$ from the fixed end has bending moment:

$$M(x) = -15(3 - x) = -45 + 15x \text{ kN·m (hogging, negative)}$$

**Step 2:** Apply GDEE:

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -45 + 15x$$

**Step 3:** First integration:

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = -45x + \frac{15x^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 4:** Second integration:

$$EI\,y = -\frac{45x^2}{2} + \frac{15x^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2 = -22.5x^2 + 2.5x^3 + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5:** Boundary conditions at fixed end ($x = 0$):

- $y = 0$: $\Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
- $\theta = 0$: $\Rightarrow C_1 = 0$

**Step 6:** Slope at free end ($x = 3$):

$$EI\,\theta_B = -45(3) + \frac{15(9)}{2} = -135 + 67.5 = -67.5$$

$$\theta_B = \frac{-67.5}{6 \times 10^4} = -1.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

$$|\theta_B| = 1.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad (clockwise)}$$

**Step 7:** Deflection at free end ($x = 3$):

$$EI\,y_B = -22.5(9) + 2.5(27) = -202.5 + 67.5 = -135$$

$$y_B = \frac{-135}{6 \times 10^4} = -2.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = -2.25 \text{ mm (downward)}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_B = 1.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} \quad ; \quad y_B = 2.25 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$

**Verification with standard formula:** For cantilever with point load at free end:

$$\theta_B = \frac{PL^2}{2EI} = \frac{15 \times 9}{2 \times 6 \times 10^4} = \frac{135}{120000} = 1.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} \checkmark$$

$$y_B = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} = \frac{15 \times 27}{3 \times 6 \times 10^4} = \frac{405}{180000} = 2.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \checkmark$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Sign Convention Table (KTU Standard):**

| Quantity | Positive Sign | Negative Sign |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| Deflection $y$ | **Downward** from original axis | Upward (rises above original axis) |
| Slope $\theta = dy/dx$ | **Clockwise rotation** of tangent | Counter-clockwise rotation |
| Bending Moment $M$ | **Sagging** (concave up, smile-shaped) | **Hogging** (concave down, frown-shaped) |
| $d^2y/dx^2$ | **Concave up** (curve opens upward) | **Concave down** (curve opens downward) |

**Relationship between sign of $d^2y/dx^2$ and curvature type:**

- If $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} > 0$ (positive): The elastic curve is **concave upward** (sagging region). The beam curves like a smile — characteristic of **positive bending moment** regions. Example: middle of a simply supported beam under gravity load.

- If $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} < 0$ (negative): The elastic curve is **concave downward** (hogging region). The beam curves like a frown — characteristic of **negative bending moment** regions. Example: cantilever beam near the fixed end under tip load.

- If $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = 0$: The curve has an **inflection point** where it transitions between sagging and hogging — a critical location in **continuous beams**.

This sign linkage is precisely what the equation $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ encodes: the sign of $M$ directly determines the sign of the second derivative of the deflected shape.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Module 4 (Slope–Deflection–Curvature Problems):**
> 
> 1. **Sign error in $M(x)$:** Students often forget the sign of the bending moment (sagging vs. hogging). Always draw a free-body diagram and use the **statics convention** (sagging positive) consistently. (1–2 marks lost)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting constants of integration:** Both $C_1$ and $C_2$ MUST be evaluated using **two** boundary conditions. Many students use only one BC and proceed — full marks are not awarded. (2–3 marks lost)
> 
> 3. **Wrong boundary conditions for cantilever:** At the **fixed end**, BOTH $y = 0$ AND $\theta = 0$. At the **free end**, the moment is zero (which gives an additional equation if needed). Confusing these is a common error. (2 marks lost)
> 
> 4. **Unit mismatch:** $EI$ must be expressed in **N·m²** (not N/mm²) if the load and span are in **kN and m**. A common mistake is mixing units. Always state units clearly. (1 mark lost)
> 
> 5. **Sign of final answer:** Report both magnitude and direction. Writing "$y = -2.25$ mm" with the comment "downward" gets full credit. Writing just "$y = 2.25$ mm" without sign may lose **0.5–1 mark**.
> 
> 6. **Misapplication of the exact curvature formula:** In Part B derivation questions, students often write $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ directly without showing the small-slope approximation. KTU examiners look for the **complete justification** of why the higher-order term is dropped. (2 marks lost)

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — go through this before the exam!**

- ✅ The **exact curvature equation** is $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1+\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$.

- ✅ The **approximate GDEE** (Euler–Bernoulli) is $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$, valid only when slope is very small ($\theta \ll 1$ rad).

- ✅ **Slope = first derivative** of deflection: $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$.

- ✅ **Curvature = second derivative** of deflection: $\kappa = \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} \approx \dfrac{M}{EI}$.

- ✅ Integrating $M/EI$ **twice** gives the slope and deflection; **two constants of integration** are needed.

- ✅ **Boundary conditions:**
  - Fixed end: $y = 0$ and $\theta = 0$
  - Simply supported end: $y = 0$ only
  - Free end: $M = 0$ and $V = 0$ (or unconstrained $y$, $\theta$)
  - Symmetry line: $\theta = 0$

- ✅ **Sign convention:** Sagging moment positive; downward deflection positive; clockwise slope positive.

- ✅ **Key standard results to memorize:**
  - SS beam + UDL: $y_{max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384\,EI}$ at centre; $\theta_{max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{24\,EI}$ at supports.
  - SS beam + Central point load: $y_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48\,EI}$ at centre; $\theta_{max} = \dfrac{PL^2}{16\,EI}$ at supports.
  - Cantilever + UDL: $y_{max} = \dfrac{wL^4}{8\,EI}$ at free end; $\theta_{max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{6\,EI}$ at free end.
  - Cantilever + Point load at tip: $y_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3\,EI}$ at free end; $\theta_{max} = \dfrac{PL^2}{2\,EI}$ at free end.

- ✅ **Units checklist:** $E$ in N/m² (or N/mm²), $I$ in m⁴ (or mm⁴), $M$ in N·m, $w$ in N/m, $L$ in m, $y$ in m, $\theta$ in rad.

- ✅ **Relation to other methods:** Double Integration → Macaulay's Method → Moment-Area → Conjugate Beam → Strain Energy are all derived from or consistent with the GDEE.

- ✅ **Physical meaning of $EI$:** It is the **flexural rigidity** — the beam's resistance to bending. Doubling $I$ (e.g., deeper section) halves the deflection — but doubling $L$ increases deflection by $L^3$ (cubic!) or $L^4$ (quartic!) for distributed loads.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deflection of Cantilever and Simply Supported Beams by Double Integration Method, Macaulay's Method, Moment Area Method and Conjugate Beam Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deflection of Cantilever and Simply Supported Beams by Double Integration Method, Macaulay's Method, Moment Area Method and Conjugate Beam Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Beam Deflection** is the vertical displacement of a point on the axis of a beam from its original unloaded position, caused by the bending of the beam under applied transverse loads. The **elastic curve** (or *deflected shape*) of a beam is the locus of deflected positions of all points along its centroidal axis.

Mathematically, the deflection $y$ at a distance $x$ along the beam is governed by the **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation**:

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the material (**N/m²**)
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area of the cross-section about the neutral axis (**m⁴**)
- $M(x)$ = Bending moment at section $x$ (**N·m**)
- $y$ = Vertical deflection (**m**)
- $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ = Slope (dimensionless, in **radians**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This module demands mastery of **four** classical analytical methods to evaluate slopes and deflections of **cantilever** and **simply supported** beams. Choosing the *right* method for a given loading is the most critical examiner skill.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board Intuition

Imagine standing at the end of a **flexible diving board** (cantilever fixed at one end, free at the other):

- When you step on it, the board **bends downward** — this bending is the *deflection*.
- The board **tilts** at an angle from its original horizontal — this tilt is the *slope*.
- The *stiffest* the board (large $EI$), the less it bends — this is why diving boards are designed with specific stiffness.
- For a **simply supported** plank resting on two logs (a footbridge), when you walk to the center, it sags the most at the middle, but the ends do not displace (they are pinned).

This everyday intuition captures exactly what we will calculate using mathematics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $EI$ Matters:** The product $EI$ is called the **flexural rigidity** of the beam. It is the beam's resistance to bending. Doubling $E$ or $I$ halves the deflection.

---

## 1.3 The Four Methods — A Bird's-Eye View

| Method | Core Idea | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Double Integration** | Integrate $EI\,y''=M(x)$ twice | Continuous moment expressions |
| **Macaulay's Method** | Use singularity functions to handle discontinuous loads | Beams with multiple point loads / UDLs starting at offsets |
| **Moment Area Method** | Two geometric theorems by Mohr | Beams with concentrated loads, finding tangents |
| **Conjugate Beam Method** | Treat the $M/EI$ diagram as a fictitious load on a *fictitious* beam | Cantilevers and simply supported beams, graphical clarity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All four methods must yield the **same numerical answer** for slope and deflection at any point — they are merely different mathematical pathways to the same physical truth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Differential Equation (Derivation of the Master Equation)

Consider an elemental length $dx$ of a beam that bends into an arc of radius $R$. From the **theory of simple bending**:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

The radius of curvature is related to the deflection $y$ by:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

For **small deflections** (slopes $\ll 1$, which is the KTU assumption), $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \approx 0$, so:

$$\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

Combining:

$$\boxed{\;EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)\;}$$

- First integration $\Rightarrow$ Slope equation: $EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\,dx + C_1$
- Second integration $\Rightarrow$ Deflection equation: $EI\,y = \iint M(x)\,dx\,dx + C_1 x + C_2$

---

## 2.2 METHOD 1 — Double Integration Method (DIM)

### Procedure

1. **Fix origin** at the left support (or fixed end for cantilever).
2. Compute $M(x)$ as a function of $x$ over the full length (valid for the **entire span**, no discontinuities allowed).
3. Substitute $M(x)$ into $EI\,y'' = M(x)$ and integrate **twice**.
4. Apply **boundary conditions** to determine $C_1$ and $C_2$.

### Boundary Conditions

| Beam Type | At $x = 0$ | At $x = L$ |
|---|---|---|
| Cantilever (fixed at $x=0$) | $y = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ | Free end — no condition |
| Simply Supported (pin at both ends) | $y = 0$ | $y = 0$ |

---

## 2.3 METHOD 2 — Macaulay's Method (Singularity Functions)

### The Macaulay Bracket

$$\langle x - a \rangle^n = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } x < a \\ (x-a)^n & \text{if } x \geq a \end{cases}$$

**Key Rule:** In Macaulay's method, **integration of a bracket preserves the bracket but increases its power by 1** — and terms that evaluate to zero at the lower limit are simply dropped.

$$\int \langle x - a \rangle^n \, dx = \frac{\langle x - a \rangle^{n+1}}{n+1} + C$$

### Standard Singularity Functions for Common Loads

| Load Type | Bending Moment Expression $M(x)$ |
|---|---|
| Point load $W$ at $x = a$ | $-W\,\langle x - a \rangle$ |
| UDL of intensity $w$ from $x = a$ to $x = b$ | $-\dfrac{w}{2}\,\langle x - a \rangle^2 + \dfrac{w}{2}\,\langle x - b \rangle^2$ |
| Point moment $M_0$ at $x = a$ | $-M_0\,\langle x - a \rangle^0$ |

### Procedure

1. Write $M(x)$ in a **single expression** using Macaulay brackets (covering the full span).
2. Integrate twice using the bracket rule.
3. Apply boundary conditions to find constants.

---

## 2.4 METHOD 3 — Moment Area Method (Mohr's Theorems)

### Mohr's First Theorem (Slope)
> The change in slope between any two points $A$ and $B$ on the elastic curve equals the **area of the $M/EI$ diagram** between those two points:

$$\theta_{B/A} = \theta_B - \theta_A = \int_A^B \frac{M}{EI}\,dx = \text{Area of } \frac{M}{EI} \text{ diagram between } A \text{ and } B$$

### Mohr's Second Theorem (Deflection)
> The vertical deviation of point $B$ from the tangent drawn at point $A$ equals the **moment of the $M/EI$ area** between $A$ and $B$, taken about the vertical line through $B$:

$$t_{B/A} = \int_A^B \frac{M}{EI}\,\bar{x}\,dA = \text{Moment of } \frac{M}{EI} \text{ area about } B$$

where $\bar{x}$ is the distance from $B$ to the centroid of the area.

### Procedure

1. Draw the **Bending Moment Diagram (BMD)** and divide by $EI$ to get the $M/EI$ diagram.
2. Compute areas and centroids of the various regions of the $M/EI$ diagram.
3. For a cantilever: use the fixed end as the reference point (slope and deflection = 0 there).
4. For a simply supported beam: use the **tangent at one support** as the reference and find the deviation at the other support to set it equal to zero (this fixes the reference tangent).

---

## 2.5 METHOD 4 — Conjugate Beam Method

### The Concept
The **conjugate beam** is a fictitious beam of the **same length** as the real beam, but loaded with the $M/EI$ diagram of the real beam (treated as a distributed load). The **shear force** in the conjugate beam at any section = **slope** of the real beam; the **bending moment** in the conjugate beam at any section = **deflection** of the real beam.

### Support Transformations

| Real Beam Support | Conjugate Beam Support |
|---|---|
| Fixed end | Free end |
| Free end | Fixed end |
| Simple support (pin/roller) | Simple support (pin/roller) |
| Internal hinge | Internal hinge (with shear release) |

### Procedure

1. Draw the $M/EI$ diagram of the real beam.
2. Treat this diagram as a **load** on the conjugate beam.
3. Compute the **bending moment** in the conjugate beam using standard equilibrium → this gives deflection in the real beam.
4. Compute the **shear force** in the conjugate beam → this gives slope in the real beam.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

| # | Beam & Loading | Max Slope $\theta_{max}$ | Max Deflection $\delta_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cantilever, point load $W$ at free end | $\dfrac{WL^2}{2EI}$ at free end | $\dfrac{WL^3}{3EI}$ at free end |
| 2 | Cantilever, UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$ at free end | $\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ at free end |
| 3 | Cantilever, point load $W$ at distance $a$ from fixed end | $\dfrac{Wa^2}{2EI}$ at free end | $\dfrac{Wa^2(3L - a)}{6EI}$ at free end |
| 4 | Cantilever, moment $M_0$ at free end | $\dfrac{M_0 L}{EI}$ | $\dfrac{M_0 L^2}{2EI}$ |
| 5 | Simply supported, central point load $W$ | $\dfrac{WL^2}{16EI}$ at supports | $\dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$ at centre |
| 6 | Simply supported, UDL $w$ over full span | $\dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ at supports | $\dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ at centre |
| 7 | Simply supported, moment $M_0$ at centre | $\dfrac{M_0 L}{16EI}$ | $\dfrac{M_0 L^2}{16EI}$ near load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** You are *not* expected to memorize all seven — you must know the **derivation procedure** for any new combination. The table above is for **verification only**.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge design** (steel/concrete girders) — limiting deflection to $L/325$ per IRC/IS codes.
- **Machine tool beds** — minimal deflection ensures machining accuracy.
- **Aircraft wings** — deflection governs aerodynamic profile and structural fatigue.
- **High-rise floor systems** — excessive deflection causes partition cracks and user discomfort.
- **Railway sleepers and rails** — elastic curve analysis prevents dynamic resonance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Cantilever with Point Load at Free End (Double Integration)

**Problem:** A cantilever of length $L$ is fixed at $A$ ($x = 0$) and carries a point load $W$ at the free end $B$ ($x = L$). Find slope and deflection at $B$.

**Step 1 — Bending moment expression.** Taking a section at distance $x$ from the fixed end, and considering the right portion (from $x$ to $L$, which carries the load $W$ at distance $(L-x)$):

$$M(x) = -W(L - x) = -WL + Wx$$

The negative sign indicates a hogging (concave-downward) moment — this is the standard convention for cantilevers.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the master equation.**

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -WL + Wx$$

**Step 3 — First integration (Slope Equation).**

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = -WLx + \frac{Wx^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second integration (Deflection Equation).**

$$EI\,y = -\frac{WLx^2}{2} + \frac{Wx^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions.**
At fixed end $A$ ($x = 0$): slope $= 0$ and deflection $= 0$.

From $y(0) = 0$:

$$0 = 0 + 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$

From $y'(0) = 0$:

$$0 = 0 + 0 + C_1 \implies C_1 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Final Equations.**

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = -WLx + \frac{Wx^2}{2}$$

$$EI\,y = -\frac{WLx^2}{2} + \frac{Wx^3}{6}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate at free end $B$ ($x = L$).**

Slope at $B$:

$$EI\,\theta_B = -WL(L) + \frac{W(L)^2}{2} = -WL^2 + \frac{WL^2}{2} = -\frac{WL^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta_B = -\frac{WL^2}{2EI}\;}$$

The negative sign indicates the slope is clockwise (downward tilt toward the load).

Deflection at $B$:

$$EI\,y_B = -\frac{WL(L)^2}{2} + \frac{W(L)^3}{6} = -\frac{WL^3}{2} + \frac{WL^3}{6} = -\frac{3WL^3 - WL^3}{6} = -\frac{WL^3}{3}$$

$$\boxed{\;y_B = -\frac{WL^3}{3EI}\;}$$

The magnitude is the classic textbook result. The negative sign simply indicates *downward* deflection under the standard sign convention.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load (Macaulay's Method)

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a point load $W$ at the centre ($x = L/2$). Determine the slope at the supports and the deflection at the centre using Macaulay's method.

**Step 1 — Reactions.** By symmetry:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{W}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Bending moment using Macaulay brackets.** Consider a section at distance $x$ from $A$. The load $W$ at $x = L/2$ contributes a term only when $x \geq L/2$:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - W \left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle^1 = \frac{W}{2}\,x - W\left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle$$

**Step 3 — Apply master equation and integrate once.**

$$EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{W}{2}\,x - W\left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle$$

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{W}{2}\,\frac{x^2}{2} - W\frac{\left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle^2}{2} + C_1$$

$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{Wx^2}{4} - \frac{W}{2}\left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle^2 + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Integrate again (Deflection).**

$$EI\,y = \frac{Wx^3}{12} - \frac{W}{2} \cdot \frac{\left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle^3}{3} + C_1 x + C_2$$

$$EI\,y = \frac{Wx^3}{12} - \frac{W}{6}\left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle^3 + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary conditions.**

At $x = 0$: $y = 0$:

$$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$

At $x = L$: $y = 0$. At this point, the bracket term is non-zero: $\langle L - L/2 \rangle^3 = (L/2)^3 = L^3/8$:

$$0 = \frac{WL^3}{12} - \frac{W}{6}\cdot\frac{L^3}{8} + C_1 L$$

$$0 = \frac{WL^3}{12} - \frac{WL^3}{48} + C_1 L$$

Compute the common denominator (48):

$$0 = \frac{4WL^3}{48} - \frac{WL^3}{48} + C_1 L = \frac{3WL^3}{48} + C_1 L = \frac{WL^3}{16} + C_1 L$$

$$C_1 = -\frac{WL^2}{16}$$

**Step 6 — Final slope and deflection equations.**

$$EI\,\theta = \frac{Wx^2}{4} - \frac{W}{2}\left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle^2 - \frac{WL^2}{16}$$

$$EI\,y = \frac{Wx^3}{12} - \frac{W}{6}\left\langle x - \frac{L}{2} \right\rangle^3 - \frac{WL^2}{16}\,x$$

**Step 7 — Slope at support $A$ ($x = 0$).**

$$EI\,\theta_A = 0 - 0 - \frac{WL^2}{16} = -\frac{WL^2}{16}$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta_A = -\frac{WL^2}{16EI}\;}$$

By symmetry, $\theta_B = +\dfrac{WL^2}{16EI}$.

**Step 8 — Deflection at centre ($x = L/2$).** The bracket term $\langle L/2 - L/2 \rangle^3 = 0$:

$$EI\,y_{mid} = \frac{W(L/2)^3}{12} - 0 - \frac{WL^2}{16}\cdot\frac{L}{2}$$

$$EI\,y_{mid} = \frac{WL^3}{96} - \frac{WL^3}{32} = \frac{WL^3}{96} - \frac{3WL^3}{96} = -\frac{2WL^3}{96} = -\frac{WL^3}{48}$$

$$\boxed{\;y_{mid} = -\frac{WL^3}{48EI}\;}$$

The magnitudes match the standard results from Row 5 of the formula table. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cantilever with UDL (Moment Area Method)

**Problem:** A cantilever of length $L$ is fixed at $A$ ($x=0$) and carries a UDL of intensity $w$ over the full span. Find the slope and deflection at the free end $B$ using the **Moment Area Method**.

**Step 1 — Bending Moment Diagram (BMD).** The bending moment at distance $x$ from the fixed end:

$$M(x) = -\frac{w(L-x)^2}{2}$$

At $A$ ($x=0$): $M_A = -\dfrac{wL^2}{2}$ (maximum hogging).
At $B$ ($x=L$): $M_B = 0$.

The BMD is a **parabolic** (second-degree) curve from $0$ at $B$ to $-wL^2/2$ at $A$.

**Step 2 — $M/EI$ diagram.** Dividing by $EI$ gives a similar parabolic shape scaled by $1/EI$.

**Step 3 — Area of $M/EI$ diagram between $A$ and $B$.** The parabolic area from $x = 0$ (where $M/EI = -wL^2/2EI$) to $x = L$ (where $M/EI = 0$):

$$\text{Area} = \frac{1}{3} \times \text{base} \times \text{height} = \frac{1}{3} \times L \times \left(-\frac{wL^2}{2EI}\right) = -\frac{wL^3}{6EI}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Mohr's First Theorem (Slope at $B$).** Since slope at $A$ is zero (fixed):

$$\theta_B - \theta_A = \text{Area between } A \text{ and } B$$

$$\theta_B - 0 = -\frac{wL^3}{6EI}$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta_B = -\frac{wL^3}{6EI}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Centroid of the parabolic area.** For a parabola with vertex at the free end ($x = L$) and base at the fixed end, the centroid lies at $\dfrac{3L}{4}$ from the *fixed* end (i.e., at $x = 3L/4$ from $A$). Equivalently, the centroid is at a distance of $L/4$ from the free end $B$.

**Step 6 — Apply Mohr's Second Theorem (Deflection at $B$).** The deviation of $B$ from the tangent at $A$ (which is horizontal at the fixed end):

$$t_{B/A} = \text{Area} \times \bar{x}_{B}$$

where $\bar{x}_{B}$ is the distance from $B$ to the centroid = $L/4$.

$$t_{B/A} = \left(-\frac{wL^3}{6EI}\right) \times \left(\frac{L}{4}\right) = -\frac{wL^4}{24EI}$$

$$\boxed{\;y_B = -\frac{wL^4}{24EI}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Correction:** The classical textbook result is $-\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$, which represents the *total* deflection. The Moment Area theorem gives the deviation from the tangent at $A$, which is *not* the actual deflection when the tangent is not horizontal. For a cantilever, however, the tangent at the fixed end *is* horizontal (slope = 0), so the deviation from the tangent at $A$ **is** the actual deflection. The result $\dfrac{wL^4}{24EI}$ must therefore be checked.
>
> **Re-examination:** The parabolic area for $M/EI$ between $A$ and $B$ is actually $\dfrac{1}{3} \times L \times \dfrac{wL^2}{2EI} = \dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$. The centroid from $B$ is at $\dfrac{3L}{4}$ (because for a standard second-degree parabola, the centroid of the area from vertex to base is at $\dfrac{3}{4}$ of the base from the vertex).
>
> **Corrected calculation:**
> $$y_B = \text{Area} \times \bar{x}_B = \frac{wL^3}{6EI} \times \frac{3L}{4} = \frac{wL^4}{8EI}$$
>
> This matches the standard textbook result. ✓

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Simply Supported Beam with UDL (Conjugate Beam Method)

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a UDL $w$ over the full span. Find the slope at the supports and maximum deflection at the centre using the **Conjugate Beam Method**.

**Step 1 — Real BMD.** The bending moment at distance $x$:

$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{w}{2}\,x(L - x)$$

This is a parabola with maximum at the centre: $M_{max} = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$.

**Step 2 — Conjugate beam loading.** The $M/EI$ diagram (a parabola with max $wL^2/8EI$) is applied as a **load** on the conjugate beam. Since the real beam has simple supports at both ends, the conjugate beam also has simple supports at both ends.

**Step 3 — Conjugate beam reactions.** The total load (area of parabolic $M/EI$ diagram):

$$W_{conj} = \frac{2}{3} \times L \times \frac{wL^2}{8EI} = \frac{wL^3}{12EI}$$

By symmetry, each reaction: $R'_A = R'_B = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$.

**Step 4 — Slope at supports (shear in conjugate beam).** The shear at $A$ in the conjugate beam equals the slope at $A$ in the real beam:

$$V'_{A} = R'_A = \frac{wL^3}{24EI}$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta_A = -\frac{wL^3}{24EI}\;}$$

(Negative sign: counter-clockwise in the real beam depending on convention.)

By symmetry, $\theta_B = +\dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$.

**Step 5 — Maximum deflection (bending moment in conjugate beam at centre).** The bending moment in the conjugate beam at $x = L/2$:

$$M'_{L/2} = R'_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \text{(moment of half the parabolic load about centre)}$$

The half-parabolic load has area $= \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{wL^3}{12EI} = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ and its centroid lies at $\dfrac{3L}{8}$ from the centre (or $\dfrac{5L}{8}$ from $A$).

Distance from centre to centroid of left half = $\dfrac{L}{2} - \dfrac{3L}{8} = \dfrac{L}{8}$? Let me redo this carefully.

**Recalculation:** The full parabolic $M/EI$ load can be thought of as two halves. Consider the left half (from $x = 0$ to $x = L/2$). Its area is $\dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$, and its centroid is located at $\dfrac{3L/2}{4} = \dfrac{3L}{8}$ from $A$ — which is $\dfrac{3L}{8} - \dfrac{L}{2} = -\dfrac{L}{8}$ from the centre (i.e., to the left of centre).

For the conjugate beam bending moment at $x = L/2$:

$$M'_{L/2} = R'_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - (\text{area of left half}) \times (\text{lever arm to centroid})$$

$$M'_{L/2} = \frac{wL^3}{24EI} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{wL^3}{24EI} \cdot \frac{L}{8}$$

$$M'_{L/2} = \frac{wL^4}{48EI} - \frac{wL^4}{192EI}$$

Common denominator 192:

$$M'_{L/2} = \frac{4wL^4}{192EI} - \frac{wL^4}{192EI} = \frac{3wL^4}{192EI} = \frac{wL^4}{64EI}$$

Hmm, this does not match the classical $5wL^4/384EI$. Let me re-examine.

**Careful Redo:** The full parabolic $M/EI$ load has total area $\dfrac{wL^3}{12EI}$, with reactions $R'_A = R'_B = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ at the supports. The shape of the load on the conjugate beam is a parabola (same as the real BMD, which is a second-degree parabola in $x$).

The bending moment at midspan of a simply supported beam under a parabolic UDL is computed as:

$$M'_{L/2} = R'_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - (\text{moment of load on left half about midspan})$$

The left half of the parabolic load: intensity at $x$ is $q(x) = \dfrac{w}{2EI}\cdot x(L-x)$.

Total load on left half:

$$W_{left} = \int_0^{L/2} \frac{w}{2EI}x(L-x)\,dx = \frac{w}{2EI}\left[\frac{Lx^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^{L/2}$$

$$= \frac{w}{2EI}\left[\frac{L \cdot L^2/4}{2} - \frac{L^3/8}{3}\right] = \frac{w}{2EI}\left[\frac{L^3}{8} - \frac{L^3}{24}\right] = \frac{w}{2EI} \cdot \frac{3L^3 - L^3}{24} = \frac{w}{2EI} \cdot \frac{2L^3}{24}$$

$$= \frac{wL^3}{24EI}$$

Moment about midspan of this left-half load (taking moment of each element about $x = L/2$):

$$\bar{x}_{L/2 \to \text{centroid of left half}} = \frac{1}{W_{left}} \int_0^{L/2} \frac{w}{2EI}x(L-x)\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right)dx$$

Let $u = x$, expand:

$$(L-x)\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right) = \frac{L^2}{2} - Lx - Lx + 2x^2 = \frac{L^2}{2} - 2Lx + 2x^2$$

Wait: $(L-x)\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right) = \frac{L^2}{2} - Lx - \frac{Lx}{2} + x^2 = \frac{L^2}{2} - \frac{3Lx}{2} + x^2$

So the moment integral becomes:

$$\int_0^{L/2} x\left(\frac{L^2}{2} - \frac{3Lx}{2} + x^2\right)dx = \int_0^{L/2} \left(\frac{L^2 x}{2} - \frac{3Lx^2}{2} + x^3\right)dx$$

$$= \left[\frac{L^2 x^2}{4} - \frac{Lx^3}{2} + \frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^{L/2}$$

$$= \frac{L^2 \cdot L^2/4}{4} - \frac{L \cdot L^3/8}{2} + \frac{L^4/16}{4}$$

$$= \frac{L^4}{16} - \frac{L^4}{16} + \frac{L^4}{64} = \frac{L^4}{64}$$

So the lever arm of the left-half load about the centre:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{w/(2EI) \cdot L^4/64}{wL^3/(24EI)} = \frac{L^4/64 \cdot 1}{2L^3/24} = \frac{L^4}{64} \cdot \frac{24}{2L^3} = \frac{24L}{128} = \frac{3L}{16}$$

The moment of the left-half load about the midspan:

$$M_{left} = W_{left} \cdot \bar{x} = \frac{wL^3}{24EI} \cdot \frac{3L}{16} = \frac{3wL^4}{384EI} = \frac{wL^4}{128EI}$$

Now the bending moment in the conjugate beam at midspan:

$$M'_{L/2} = R'_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - M_{left} = \frac{wL^3}{24EI} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{wL^4}{128EI}$$

$$= \frac{wL^4}{48EI} - \frac{wL^4}{128EI}$$

Common denominator 384:

$$= \frac{8wL^4}{384EI} - \frac{3wL^4}{384EI} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$$

$$\boxed{\;y_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}\;}$$

This matches the classical result from Row 6 of the formula table. ✓

---

## 3.5 Comparison of All Four Methods — Analytical Matrix

| Aspect | Double Integration | Macaulay's | Moment Area | Conjugate Beam |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Setup effort | Low (for simple loads) | Medium | High (geometry) | High (geometry) |
| Best for | Continuous UDL | Multiple discrete loads | Point loads | Graphical clarity |
| Handles discontinuities | Poorly (needs piecewise) | Excellent | Good | Good |
| Computes slope | Yes (1st integral) | Yes (1st integral) | Yes (Mohr-I) | Yes (shear) |
| Computes deflection | Yes (2nd integral) | Yes (2nd integral) | Yes (Mohr-II) | Yes (BM) |
| Requires integration | Yes (twice) | Yes (twice) | No | No |
| Requires area/centroid knowledge | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| KTU 14-mark weightage | Most common | Most common | Common | Common |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Methodology Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the decision logic for choosing the right method for a given KTU problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Beam + Loading] --> B{Beam Type}
    B -->|Cantilever| C{Loading Type}
    B -->|Simply Supported| C
    C -->|Single UDL| D[Double Integration]
    C -->|Multiple Point Loads / UDLs at offsets| E[Macaulay's Method]
    C -->|Few concentrated loads| F[Moment Area Method]
    C -->|Need graphical insight| G[Conjugate Beam Method]
    D --> H[Integrate EI.y'' = M twice]
    E --> I[Integrate EI.y'' = M with brackets]
    F --> J[Draw BMD, compute M/EI areas]
    G --> K[Treat M/EI as load on conjugate beam]
    H --> L[Apply boundary conditions]
    I --> L
    J --> M[Apply Mohr Theorems I and II]
    K --> N[Find shear and moment in conj. beam]
    L --> O[Slope and Deflection values]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Deflection Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Beam Geometry: L, E, I] --> A2[Loading: W, w, M0]
        A2 --> A3[Support Conditions]
    end
    INPUT --> B[Compute Reactions]
    B --> C[Write M of x expression]
    C --> D[Apply Master Equation: EI y'' = M]
    D --> E[Choose Method]
    E --> F1[Double Integration]
    E --> F2[Macaulay Brackets]
    E --> F3[Mohr Theorems]
    E --> F4[Conjugate Beam]
    F1 --> G[Integrate and Apply BCs]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Output: Slope and Deflection]
    H --> I[Sanity Check vs Standard Results]
```

## 4.3 Conjugate Beam Transformation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REAL[Real Beam]
        R1[Fixed End] 
        R2[Free End]
        R3[Pin / Roller Support]
        R4[Internal Hinge]
    end
    subgraph CONJ[Conjugate Beam]
        C1[Free End]
        C2[Fixed End]
        C3[Pin / Roller Support]
        C4[Internal Hinge with Shear Release]
    end
    R1 -.->|Transform| C1
    R2 -.->|Transform| C2
    R3 -.->|Same| C3
    R4 -.->|Transform| C4
    REAL --> BM[Compute BMD]
    BM --> LOAD[Load the Conjugate Beam with M over EI]
    LOAD --> SOLVE[Solve for Shear and Moment]
    SOLVE --> RESULT[Slope = Shear, Deflection = Moment]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Macaulay Bracket Mechanics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify All Loads and Positions] --> B[Write M of x as single expression with Macaulay brackets]
    B --> C[Substitute into EI y'' = M of x]
    C --> D[First Integration: slope equation with brackets]
    D --> E[Second Integration: deflection equation with brackets]
    E --> F[Apply Boundary Conditions: y=0 and y'=0 at supports]
    F --> G[Solve for constants C1 and C2]
    G --> H[Evaluate slope and deflection at desired x]
    H --> I{Section before or after load point}
    I -->|x less than a| J[Bracket terms vanish, use simple part]
    I -->|x greater than or equal to a| K[Bracket terms active, full expression]
    J --> L[Final answer]
    K --> L
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Moment Area Method Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Draw BMD of Real Beam] --> B[Divide by EI to get M over EI diagram]
    B --> C{Choose Reference Point}
    C -->|Cantilever| D[Fixed end: slope = 0, deflection = 0]
    C -->|Simply Supported| E[Choose one support, find tangent at other]
    D --> F[Apply Mohr Theorem 1: theta change = area under M over EI]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply Mohr Theorem 2: deviation = moment of M over EI area about point]
    G --> H[If simply supported, set deviation at other support to 0]
    H --> I[Back-substitute to find true reference slope and deflection]
    I --> J[Final slope and deflection at required point]
```

## 4.6 Sign Convention Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cartesian Coordinates: x right, y up] --> B[Bending Moment M positive if sagging]
    B --> C[Master Equation: EI y'' = M]
    C --> D[Deflection y positive upward]
    D --> E[Slope dy by dx positive counter clockwise]
    E --> F[For Cantilever with downward load, expect negative slope and negative y]
    F --> G[Magnitude is the actual physical displacement]
```
