# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the term "Kern of a Section" and state its significance in the design of masonry structures.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Kern (or Core) of a Section** is the region within and around the centroid of a cross-section, such that if an axial compressive load is applied anywhere inside this region, the **entire cross-section remains in compression** (no tensile stress develops anywhere in the section).
>
> **Significance in Masonry Design:**
> - Masonry materials like **brick, stone, and plain concrete** are weak in tension (typically 1/10th of their compressive strength).
> - If the load falls **outside the kern**, tensile stresses develop, leading to cracking and failure of mortar joints.
> - Therefore, for **masonry dams, pillars, chimneys, and retaining walls**, the resultant load must lie **inside the kern** to ensure tension-free design.
>
> **Kern sizes for common sections:**
> - Rectangle (b × h): Rhombus with diagonals h/6 and b/6.
> - Solid Circle (d): Concentric circle of diameter d/4.
> - Hollow Circle (outer d, inner d_i): Concentric circle of diameter (d² + d_i²)/(4d).

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: A short column carries an eccentric load. Explain with the help of a stress diagram, how the resultant stress distribution varies with increasing eccentricity.**

**Model Answer:**

When a column is subjected to an eccentric load **P** at distance **e**, the cross-section experiences both direct compression $\sigma_d = P/A$ and bending stress $\sigma_b = P e y / I$.

**Case 1 — Small Eccentricity (e ≤ k²/y_max):**
- Bending stress is less than direct stress.
- Resultant stress distribution is **trapezoidal**.
- $\sigma_{\max} > 0$ and $\sigma_{\min} > 0$ — **whole section in compression**.

**Case 2 — Eccentricity at Kern Boundary (e = k²/y_max):**
- Bending stress exactly equals direct stress at the far edge.
- Resultant stress distribution is **triangular**.
- $\sigma_{\min} = 0$ — **limiting case**.

**Case 3 — Large Eccentricity (e > k²/y_max):**
- Bending stress exceeds direct stress at the far edge.
- Resultant stress distribution is **triangular with stress reversal**.
- $\sigma_{\min} < 0$ — **tension develops on far face**.

**Case 4 — Very Large Eccentricity:**
- Bending stress dominates; section behaves essentially as a beam.
- Large tensile stresses make the section unsuitable for masonry without reinforcement.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** A rectangular column of size 250 mm × 400 mm carries a vertical load of 600 kN at an eccentricity of 80 mm along the longer axis. Determine the stresses developed at all four corners of the column. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Find the diameter of a solid circular column to carry an axial load of 800 kN at an eccentricity of 60 mm, such that the maximum compressive stress does not exceed 8 N/mm². **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Solution to Question A (a)**

**Given:**
- Cross-section: $b = 250$ mm, $h = 400$ mm
- Load: $P = 600$ kN $= 600 \times 10^3$ N
- Eccentricity: $e = 80$ mm (along longer axis, i.e., bending about the axis parallel to b)

**Step 1 — Area:**

$$A = b \times h = 250 \times 400 = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus (about axis parallel to b):**

$$Z = \frac{b h^2}{6} = \frac{250 \times 400^2}{6} = \frac{250 \times 160000}{6} = 6.667 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Direct Stress:**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{600 \times 10^3}{1 \times 10^5} = 6.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}$$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment:**

$$M = P \times e = 600 \times 10^3 \times 80 = 48 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{48 \times 10^6}{6.667 \times 10^6} = 7.2 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Stresses at Corners:**

On the side nearer to the load (compression increases):

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_d + \sigma_b = 6.0 + 7.2 = 13.2 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}$$

On the side farther from the load (bending tension reduces compression):

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_d - \sigma_b = 6.0 - 7.2 = -1.2 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (TENSION develops)}$$

The other two corners (perpendicular to bending axis) carry only direct stress:

$$\sigma_3 = \sigma_4 = 6.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}$$

**Result:**

| Corner | Stress (N/mm²) | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Corner A (near load) | 13.2 | Compressive |
| Corner B (far from load) | -1.2 | Tensile |
| Corners C and D (lateral sides) | 6.0 | Compressive |

**Conclusion:** Since tension develops (1.2 N/mm²), this column is **not safe for plain masonry**; it requires reinforcement or reduction of eccentricity.

---

### **Solution to Question A (b)**

**Given:**
- Load: $P = 800$ kN $= 800 \times 10^3$ N
- Eccentricity: $e = 60$ mm
- Allowable maximum stress: $\sigma_{\max} = 8$ N/mm²

**Step 1 — Stress Formula:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = P \left( \frac{1}{A} + \frac{e}{Z} \right)$$

For a solid circular section of diameter **d**:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}, \quad Z = \frac{\pi d^3}{32}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = P \left( \frac{4}{\pi d^2} + \frac{32 e}{\pi d^3} \right) = \frac{4 P}{\pi d^2} \left( 1 + \frac{8 e}{d} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Plug in numerical values:**

$$8 = \frac{4 \times 800 \times 10^3}{\pi d^2} \left( 1 + \frac{8 \times 60}{d} \right)$$

$$8 = \frac{3.2 \times 10^6}{\pi d^2} \left( 1 + \frac{480}{d} \right)$$

$$8 \pi d^2 = 3.2 \times 10^6 \left( 1 + \frac{480}{d} \right)$$

$$25.133 d^2 = 3.2 \times 10^6 + \frac{1.536 \times 10^9}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by d:**

$$25.133 d^3 = 3.2 \times 10^6 d + 1.536 \times 10^9$$

$$25.133 d^3 - 3.2 \times 10^6 d - 1.536 \times 10^9 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve by trial and error (cubic equation):**

Try $d = 500$ mm:
$$25.133 \times (500)^3 - 3.2 \times 10^6 \times 500 - 1.536 \times 10^9$$
$$= 25.133 \times 1.25 \times 10^8 - 1.6 \times 10^9 - 1.536 \times 10^9$$
$$= 3.1416 \times 10^9 - 3.136 \times 10^9 = 5.6 \times 10^6 \text{ (slightly positive)}$$

Try $d = 498$ mm:
$$25.133 \times (498)^3 - 3.2 \times 10^6 \times 498 - 1.536 \times 10^9$$
$$\approx 3.099 \times 10^9 - 1.594 \times 10^9 - 1.536 \times 10^9 = -3.1 \times 10^7 \text{ (negative)}$$

The root lies between 498 and 500 mm. By interpolation:

$$d \approx 498 + 2 \times \frac{3.1 \times 10^7}{3.1 \times 10^7 + 5.6 \times 10^6} \approx 498 + 1.69 \approx 499.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** Adopt $d = 500$ mm (rounded to next standard size).

**Verification at d = 500 mm:**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{4 \times 800000}{\pi \times 500^2} \left( 1 + \frac{8 \times 60}{500} \right) = \frac{3.2 \times 10^6}{785398} \times 1.96 = 4.074 \times 1.96 = 7.985 \approx 8 \text{ N/mm}^2 \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** A hollow circular cast-iron column of external diameter 300 mm and internal diameter 200 mm carries a vertical compressive load of 500 kN. Find the maximum eccentricity the load can have without producing tensile stress on the section. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A masonry pillar of cross-section 600 mm × 800 mm carries a vertical load of 1200 kN at an eccentricity of 100 mm. Check whether the pillar is safe in masonry (no tension allowed). If unsafe, find the maximum eccentricity allowed. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Solution to Question B (a)**

**Given:**
- Outer diameter: $D = 300$ mm
- Inner diameter: $d_i = 200$ mm
- Load: $P = 500$ kN

**Step 1 — Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi (D^2 - d_i^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi (300^2 - 200^2)}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 50000}{4} = 39270 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{32 D} = \frac{\pi (300^4 - 200^4)}{32 \times 300}$$

$$D^4 = 8.1 \times 10^9, \quad d_i^4 = 1.6 \times 10^9$$

$$Z = \frac{\pi \times 6.5 \times 10^9}{9600} = \frac{2.042 \times 10^{10}}{9600} = 2.127 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Condition for No Tension:**

For no tensile stress: $\sigma_{\min} = \dfrac{P}{A} - \dfrac{P e}{Z} \geq 0$

$$e \leq \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{2.127 \times 10^6}{39270} = 54.16 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using Kern radius:**

Kern radius = $\dfrac{D^2 + d_i^2}{8 D} = \dfrac{300^2 + 200^2}{8 \times 300} = \dfrac{130000}{2400} = 54.17 \text{ mm}$ ✓

**Result:** The **maximum eccentricity = 54.17 mm** for the column to remain in pure compression.

---

### **Solution to Question B (b)**

**Given:**
- Cross-section: $b = 600$ mm, $h = 800$ mm
- Load: $P = 1200$ kN
- Eccentricity: $e = 100$ mm

**Step 1 — Area and Section Modulus:**

$$A = b \times h = 600 \times 800 = 4.8 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$Z = \frac{b h^2}{6} = \frac{600 \times 800^2}{6} = \frac{600 \times 640000}{6} = 6.4 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Direct and Bending Stresses:**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{1200 \times 10^3}{4.8 \times 10^5} = 2.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{1200 \times 10^3 \times 100}{6.4 \times 10^7} = 1.875 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\min} = 2.5 - 1.875 = 0.625 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive, no tension)}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Eccentricity Allowed:**

Maximum $e$ such that $\sigma_{\min} = 0$:

$$e_{\max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^7}{4.8 \times 10^5} = 133.33 \text{ mm}$$

(Note: This equals h/6 = 800/6 = 133.33 mm — matches the kern limit for rectangular section along the longer axis.) ✓

**Result:** The pillar is **SAFE** in masonry, since $\sigma_{\min} = 0.625$ N/mm² (compressive) and the actual eccentricity (100 mm) is well within the kern limit (133.33 mm).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Students often forget to apply the correct sign when adding/subtracting stresses. Remember: bending stress is **compressive on the side of the load and tensile on the opposite side**. Mixing up the signs yields inverted answers.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kN and N, mm and m, etc. **Always convert P to Newtons (N) and dimensions to mm** before substituting into the formula to get stress in N/mm² (MPa).
>
> 3. **Confusing Z and I:** Section Modulus $Z = I/y_{\max}$, **NOT** $I$ itself. Using $I$ directly in the bending formula $M/I$ gives stress, but you must remember to multiply by $y$ first.
>
> 4. **Neglecting Biaxial Eccentricity:** For I-sections or rectangular columns with loads offset in **both** x and y directions, students often compute only one bending component. **Always check both $e_x$ and $e_y$**.
>
> 5. **Kern Boundary Confusion:** The kern is a **region, not a point**. A load at the boundary (e.g., $e = h/6$ for a rectangle) gives $\sigma_{\min} = 0$, NOT zero stress everywhere.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Specify Nature of Stress:** Always conclude with "compressive" or "tensile" — KTU examiners award **partial credit** for correct identification of stress type even if numerical values are slightly off.
>
> 7. **Chimney/Dam Calculations:** For wind-loaded chimneys, students often compute the moment due to wind but **forget to add self-weight** or vice versa. Re-read the problem statement twice.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Eccentric Loading (Module 4)**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - Eccentricity $e$ = perpendicular distance of load from centroid.
> - Eccentric load = Axial load + Bending moment ($M = P \cdot e$).
> - Combined stress: $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} \pm \dfrac{Pe \cdot y}{I}$.
> - Maximum stress at side near load; minimum stress at opposite side.
> - Kern = region where load must lie to avoid tension.
>
> **Key Formulas (Memorize):**
> - $\sigma_{\max, \min} = \dfrac{P}{A} \pm \dfrac{P e}{Z}$
> - $e_{\text{kern limit}} = \dfrac{Z}{A} = \dfrac{k^2}{y_{\max}}$
> - Rectangle kern: $\dfrac{h}{6} \times \dfrac{b}{6}$ (rhombus)
> - Solid circle kern radius: $\dfrac{d}{8}$
> - Hollow circle kern radius: $\dfrac{d^2 + d_i^2}{8d}$
> - Hollow circular Z: $\dfrac{\pi (D^4 - d_i^4)}{32 D}$
>
> **Practical Design Rules:**
> - For masonry: **load must lie inside the kern** (no tension allowed).
> - For steel/RCC: tensile stresses are permissible up to allowable limits.
> - When $\sigma_{\min} = 0$ → load is exactly on the kern boundary.
> - When $\sigma_{\min} < 0$ → tension develops; section unsafe for plain masonry.
>
> **Common Sections to Remember:**
> - Rectangle: $I_{xx} = bh^3/12$, $Z = bh^2/6$
> - Solid circle: $I = \pi d^4/64$, $Z = \pi d^3/32$
> - Hollow circle: $I = \pi (D^4 - d_i^4)/64$, $Z = \pi (D^4 - d_i^4)/(32D)$
>
> **Exam Tips:**
> - Always draw a **free body diagram** showing load position.
> - Clearly state **assumptions** (homogeneous, elastic, plane sections).
> - Label every stress component with units (N/mm² or MPa).
> - Mention the **nature of stress** (compressive/tensile) in the final answer.
> - For chimney/dam problems, draw a sketch of the cross-section with load eccentricity marked.

---

> **End of Module 4 — Eccentric Loading Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PCAGT302 Strength of Materials & Theory of Structures*

topics.title: Limit of Eccentricity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Limit of Eccentricity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit of Eccentricity (e_limit):** The maximum distance *e* from the centroidal axis of a cross-section at which a compressive load *P* may be applied such that the **resultant stress at every point of the section remains compressive (i.e., no tensile stress is induced anywhere in the section).**

Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the **square of the radius of gyration** to the **maximum distance of the extreme fibre from the Neutral Axis (N.A.)**:

$$e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{\text{max}}}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Moment of Inertia of the cross-section about the Neutral Axis
- $A$ = Cross-sectional Area
- $k$ = Radius of gyration, with $k^2 = I/A$
- $y_{\text{max}}$ = Distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre on the far side from the load

This critical distance is also termed the **distance to the Kern boundary**, and the enclosed region around the centroid is called the **Kern (or Core) of the Section**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Heavy Luggage on a Car Roof**
>
> Imagine stacking a heavy suitcase on the roof of a car. If you place it **exactly in the middle**, the entire roof compresses evenly — the car body is safe. If you slide it slightly to one side, the side it sits on gets crushed more, but the opposite side is still pressed (no lifting). However, if you push the suitcase **too far toward one edge** (say, past a critical mark), the far edge of the roof tries to **lift up** — the rivets and joints start to **pull apart** (tension). The "critical mark" beyond which the roof begins to experience tension is the **Limit of Eccentricity**.
>
> In masonry dams, retaining walls, and brick piers, mortar cannot resist tension. So the load must always be applied within this "safe zone" around the centroid — which is exactly the **Kern of the section**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Significance

- **Masonry & Concrete Structures** (dams, chimneys, retaining walls): Mortar has negligible tensile strength. Hence, the **resultant load must lie within the kern**.
- **Short Columns with Brittle Materials**: Ensures no cracking on the far face.
- **Bridge Piers & Foundations**: Prevents tension at the base due to overturning moments combined with vertical loads.
- **No-Tension Design Philosophy**: A cornerstone of unreinforced masonry design and combined stress analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cross-Sectional Limit Values (Quick Reference)**
> - **Solid Rectangular Section** → $e_{\text{limit}} = d/6$ → **"Middle Third Rule"**
> - **Solid Circular Section** → $e_{\text{limit}} = R/4 = d/8$ → **"Middle Quarter Rule"**
> - **Hollow Circular Section** → $e_{\text{limit}} = (R^2 + r^2)/(4R)$
> - **Hollow Rectangular Section** → $e_{\text{limit}} = (BD^2 - bd^2)/(6D(BD - bd))$

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

When a compressive load $P$ is applied **parallel to the column axis** but offset by a distance $e$ from the centroid (i.e., **eccentric loading**), the section experiences **two simultaneous effects**:

**Step 1 — Direct (Axial) Compression:**
The load compresses the entire section uniformly.

$$\sigma_{\text{direct}} = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{(compressive, same everywhere)}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Due to Eccentricity:**
The offset $e$ creates a moment $M = P \cdot e$ that bends the column. This adds a linearly varying stress across the section.

$$\sigma_{\text{bending}} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$$

**Step 3 — Superposition (Combined Stress):**
Using the principle of superposition (valid since we are within the elastic limit and deformations are small):

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 + \frac{e \cdot y}{k^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Critical Fibre:**
- The **maximum compressive stress** occurs on the side **towards** the load (where $y = -y_{\text{max}}$).
- The **minimum stress** (potentially tensile) occurs on the **far side** from the load (where $y = +y_{\text{max}}$).

$$\sigma_{\text{min}} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the No-Tension Condition:**
For brittle materials and masonry, the minimum stress must NOT be tensile:

$$\sigma_{\text{min}} \geq 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{P}{A} \geq \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I}$$

Cancelling $P$ (non-zero) and rearranging:

$$e \leq \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}}$$

> This is the **Limit of Eccentricity** — the absolute maximum permissible offset for a purely compressive stress state.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM-READY FORMULA TABLE** — Memorise all rows; questions are frequently asked directly or by substitution.

| # | Cross-Section Type | Area $A$ | Moment of Inertia $I$ | $k^2 = I/A$ | $y_{\text{max}}$ | **Limit of Eccentricity** $e_{\text{limit}} = k^2 / y_{\text{max}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solid Rectangle (width $b$, depth $d$) | $b \cdot d$ | $b d^3 / 12$ | $d^2 / 12$ | $d / 2$ | $\mathbf{d / 6}$ |
| 2 | Hollow Rectangle (outer $B, D$; inner $b, d$) | $BD - bd$ | $(BD^3 - bd^3)/12$ | $(BD^3 - bd^3)/[12(BD-bd)]$ | $D/2$ | $(BD^3 - bd^3)/[6D(BD - bd)]$ |
| 3 | Solid Circle (radius $R$) | $\pi R^2$ | $\pi R^4 / 4$ | $R^2 / 4$ | $R$ | $\mathbf{R / 4 \;=\; d / 8}$ |
| 4 | Hollow Circle (outer $R$, inner $r$) | $\pi(R^2 - r^2)$ | $\pi(R^4 - r^4)/4$ | $(R^2 + r^2)/4$ | $R$ | $\mathbf{(R^2 + r^2) / (4R)}$ |
| 5 | Solid Square (side $a$) | $a^2$ | $a^4 / 12$ | $a^2 / 12$ | $a/2$ | $\mathbf{a / 6}$ |
| 6 | Solid Diamond / Rhombus (diagonals $d_1, d_2$) | $d_1 d_2 / 2$ | $d_1 d_2^3 / 32$ | $d_2^2 / 16$ | $d_2 / 2$ | $\mathbf{d_2 / 8}$ |
| 7 | Triangular (equilateral, side $a$, height $h$) | $\sqrt{3}a^2/4$ | $\sqrt{3}a^4/96$ | $a^2/24$ | $h/3$ | $a^2/(8h)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Middle Third Rule"** is a special case of the limit-of-eccentricity principle applied to a **rectangular section**, where $e_{\text{limit}} = d/6$. Hence, the load must lie within the middle $1/3$ of the depth to avoid tension.

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Masonry Dam Design** | The resultant of water pressure + self-weight must fall within the middle third of the base to avoid tension and overturning. |
| **Retaining Walls** | Earth pressure creates a moment; vertical reactions must pass through the kern. |
| **Chimneys & Towers** | Wind pressure causes eccentric loading; kern check ensures no tensile cracking. |
| **Bridge Piers & Abutments** | Eccentric vehicle and self-weight loads are checked against the kern. |
| **Short Columns in Brickwork** | Beam reactions landing off-centre must lie inside the kern. |
| **Foundation Design (Isolated Footings)** | Ensuring soil pressure remains compressive (no uplift) — analogous to the kern concept. |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Derivation: General Expression for Limit of Eccentricity

**Given:** A column of cross-sectional area $A$, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ applied at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis (within the plane of bending).

**Step 1 — Direct compressive stress at any fibre:**
The axial load $P$ produces a uniform compressive stress over the whole area.

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A} \quad (\text{compressive, taken as positive in masonry convention})$$

**Step 2 — Bending stress at a fibre distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis:**
The moment $M = P \cdot e$ causes a bending stress proportional to the distance $y$ from the N.A.

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$$

**Step 3 — Combined stress using superposition:**
Adding algebraically, taking compressive as positive and tension as negative:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 + \frac{e \cdot y}{k^2}\right) \quad \text{where} \quad k^2 = \frac{I}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Compute stresses at the extreme fibres:**

At the fibre **closest to the load** ($y = -y_{\text{max}}$ on the side towards the load), both stresses are compressive:

$$\sigma_{\text{closer}} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I} \quad \text{(maximum compression)}$$

At the fibre **farthest from the load** ($y = +y_{\text{max}}$ on the side away from the load), the bending stress is tensile and opposes the direct compression:

$$\sigma_{\text{farther}} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I} \quad \text{(minimum stress — critical fibre)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the no-tension condition:**

For masonry / brittle materials, tensile stress is NOT permitted. So:

$$\sigma_{\text{farther}} \geq 0$$

$$\frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I} \geq 0$$

Dividing both sides by $P/A$ (non-zero):

$$1 - \frac{e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{k^2} \geq 0$$

$$e \leq \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{\text{max}}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master result**. The maximum permissible eccentricity is independent of the magnitude of the load $P$ — it depends ONLY on the geometry of the cross-section.

---

### 3.2 Special Case 1: Solid Rectangular Section (Middle Third Rule)

**Given:** A rectangular column of width $b$ and depth $d$. Load is applied with eccentricity $e$ measured in the direction of $d$ (i.e., bending about the axis parallel to width $b$).

**Step 1 — Area:**
$$A = b \cdot d$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia about the centroidal axis parallel to $b$:**
$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Square of radius of gyration:**
$$k^2 = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{b d^3 / 12}{b d} = \frac{d^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum fibre distance:**
$$y_{\text{max}} = \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the master formula:**

$$e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{d^2/12}{d/2} = \frac{d^2}{12} \cdot \frac{2}{d}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{d}{6}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** Since the total depth is $d$, the value $d/6$ is exactly **one-sixth** of the depth from the centroid. Hence, the load may be displaced by at most $d/6$ on either side, which spans a total width of $2 \times d/6 = d/3$ — the **"middle third"** of the section. Beyond this, tension appears.

---

### 3.3 Special Case 2: Solid Circular Section (Middle Quarter Rule)

**Given:** A solid circular column of diameter $d$ (radius $R = d/2$). The load is applied at eccentricity $e$ from the centre.

**Step 1 — Area:**
$$A = \pi R^2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia about a centroidal diameter:**
$$I = \frac{\pi R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi d^4}{64}$$

**Step 3 — Radius of gyration squared:**
$$k^2 = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{\pi d^4 / 64}{\pi d^2 / 4} = \frac{d^2}{16} = \frac{R^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum fibre distance:**
$$y_{\text{max}} = R = \frac{d}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Limit of eccentricity:**

$$e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{R^2/4}{R} = \frac{R}{4} = \frac{d}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{R}{4} = \frac{d}{8}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The load may be displaced by at most $R/4$ from the centre. The total permissible eccentricity range is $2 \times R/4 = R/2$ — half the radius, i.e., the **"middle quarter"** of the diameter.

---

### 3.4 Special Case 3: Hollow Circular Section

**Given:** A hollow circular column of outer radius $R$ and inner radius $r$. Load $P$ applied with eccentricity $e$ along a diameter.

**Step 1 — Area:**
$$A = \pi (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia:**
$$I = \frac{\pi}{4}(R^4 - r^4) = \frac{\pi}{4}(R^2 - r^2)(R^2 + r^2)$$

**Step 3 — Square of radius of gyration:**
$$k^2 = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{\frac{\pi}{4}(R^2 - r^2)(R^2 + r^2)}{\pi(R^2 - r^2)} = \frac{R^2 + r^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum fibre distance:**
$$y_{\text{max}} = R$$

**Step 5 — Limit of eccentricity:**

$$e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{(R^2 + r^2)/4}{R}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{R^2 + r^2}{4R}\,}$$

**Verification for $r = 0$ (solid circle):** $e_{\text{limit}} = R^2/(4R) = R/4$ ✓ — matches Section 3.3.

---

### 3.5 Special Case 4: Hollow Rectangular Section

**Given:** A hollow rectangular column with outer dimensions $B \times D$ and inner dimensions $b \times d$ (concentric).

**Step 1 — Area:**
$$A = BD - bd$$

**Step 2 — Moment of Inertia about the axis parallel to $B$:**
$$I = \frac{BD^3 - bd^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Square of radius of gyration:**
$$k^2 = \frac{BD^3 - bd^3}{12(BD - bd)}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum fibre distance:**
$$y_{\text{max}} = \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Limit of eccentricity:**

$$e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{BD^3 - bd^3}{12(BD - bd)} \cdot \frac{2}{D}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{BD^3 - bd^3}{6D(BD - bd)}\,}$$

**Verification for $b = d = 0$ (solid rectangle):**
$$e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{BD^3}{6D \cdot BD} = \frac{D}{6} \;\;\checkmark$$

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Limit of Eccentricity Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the complete analytical chain from applied loading to the final limit-of-eccentricity check.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AXIAL LOAD P APPLIED AT ECCENTRICITY e"] --> B["SECTION GEOMETRY INPUT"]
    B --> B1["AREA A"]
    B --> B2["MOMENT OF INERTIA I"]
    B --> B3["EXTREME FIBRE DISTANCE y_max"]
    B1 --> C["RADIUS OF GYRATION k SQUARED = I OVER A"]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D["APPLY MASTER FORMULA"]
    B3 --> D
    D --> E["COMPUTE e_limit = k SQUARED DIVIDED BY y_max"]
    E --> F{"CHECK: e APPLIED <= e_limit ?"}
    F -- YES --> G["STRESS FULLY COMPRESSIVE"]
    F -- NO --> H["TENSION DEVELOPS ON FAR FACE"]
    G --> I["SECTION SAFE - NO TENSION"]
    H --> J["SECTION UNSAFE - REVISIT DESIGN"]
    I --> K["OUTPUT: SECTION CLEARED"]
    J --> L["OUTPUT: INCREASE SECTION OR RECENTRE LOAD"]
```

---

### 4.2 Stress Distribution & Kern Geometry — Rectangular Section

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **stress distribution across the section** for a rectangular column under eccentric load, and highlights the **Kern boundaries** for both rectangular and circular sections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOAD["ECCENTRIC LOAD P AT e FROM CENTROID"]
        L1["P arrow pointing down at offset e from CG"]
    end

    subgraph STRESS["STRESS DISTRIBUTION ACROSS DEPTH d"]
        S1["FIBRE A: y = MINUS d OVER 2"]
        S2["FIBRE B: y = 0 AT CENTROID"]
        S3["FIBRE C: y = PLUS d OVER 2"]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3
        S2note["STRESS AT y = P OVER A TIMES 1 PLUS e y OVER k SQUARED"]
    end

    subgraph KERN["KERN BOUNDARIES"]
        K1["RECTANGULAR: RHOMBUS WITH DIAGONALS d OVER 3 AND b OVER 3"]
        K2["CIRCULAR: CIRCLE OF RADIUS R OVER 4"]
    end

    subgraph RESULT["RESULTANT STRESS STATES"]
        R1["e LESS THAN d OVER 6: FULL COMPRESSION"]
        R2["e EQUALS d OVER 6: ZERO STRESS AT FAR EDGE"]
        R3["e GREATER THAN d OVER 6: TENSION AT FAR EDGE"]
    end

    LOAD --> STRESS
    STRESS --> KERN
    KERN --> RESULT
```

---

### 4.3 Visualisation Reference (Text Schematic)

```
                      e = d/6  <-Limit of Eccentricity
                       |
   |<------- d/3 ------>|<------- d/3 ------>|     (Total depth = d)
   |    (Middle Third)  |  (Middle Third)    |
   +--------------------+--------------------+   <-- Far face from load (y = +d/2)
   |    TENSION ZONE    |                    |       (avoid: e > d/6)
   |    (NO LOAD HERE)  |                    |
   +--------------------+--------------------+
   |    COMPRESSION     |   COMPRESSION      |
   |    (full section)  |                    |
   +--------------------+--------------------+   <-- Centroid (y = 0)
   |    COMPRESSION     |   COMPRESSION      |
   |                    |                    |
   +--------------------+--------------------+
   |    TENSION ZONE    |                    |       (avoid: e > d/6)
   |    (NO LOAD HERE)  |                    |
   +--------------------+--------------------+   <-- Near face to load (y = -d/2)
                                                  Maximum compression here
```

> [!NOTE]
> The shaded "safe zone" of length $d/3$ in the middle is the **Kern projection** along the bending direction. The eccentricity $e$ measured from the centroid must NOT exceed $d/6$ to remain inside the middle third.

---

### 4.4 Section-Specific Kern Geometries Summary Table

| Section Shape | Kern Geometry | Kern "Size" along principal axes |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Rectangle** $b \times d$ | Rhombus (diamond) | $b/3$ horizontal, $d/3$ vertical |
| **Solid Circle** diameter $d$ | Circle | Radius = $d/8$ |
| **Hollow Circle** $R, r$ | Circle | Radius = $(R^2 + r^2)/(4R)$ |
| **Hollow Rectangle** | Rhombus (modified) | Functions of $B, D, b, d$ |
| **I-Section** | Hexagon (asymmetric in both axes) | Function of flange/web geometry |
| **T-Section** | Asymmetric quadrilateral | $k_x^2/y_{x,\text{max}}$ and $k_y^2/y_{y,\text{max}}$ |
| **Triangular** | Triangle inside | $h/6$ from base (centroid at $h/3$) |

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Limit of Eccentricity" for a column cross-section. State its value for (i) a solid rectangular section of depth *d*, and (ii) a solid circular section of diameter *d*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** The *limit of eccentricity* is the maximum distance from the centroidal axis of a section at which a compressive load may be applied such that the entire cross-section remains in compression (no tensile stress develops anywhere in the section).
> $$\;e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}}$$
> **(i) Solid Rectangular Section:** $e_{\text{limit}} = \dfrac{d}{6}$ — leads to the *"Middle Third Rule."*
> **(ii) Solid Circular Section:** $e_{\text{limit}} = \dfrac{R}{4} = \dfrac{d}{8}$ — leads to the *"Middle Quarter Rule."*

> Distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark both values.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the "Middle Third Rule" for a masonry section. Why is it important in the design of masonry dams?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Middle Third Rule:** For a rectangular masonry/concrete section subjected to combined axial load and bending, *no tensile stress* will develop at the base or any horizontal section if the *line of action of the resultant load* lies within the **middle third** of the section's depth. Mathematically, the eccentricity $e$ must satisfy $e \leq d/6$, where $d$ is the depth in the plane of bending.
>
> **Importance in Masonry Dams:** Masonry and plain concrete have negligible tensile strength. If the resultant thrust due to water pressure and self-weight were to fall outside the middle third, tensile stresses would develop at the heel or toe, leading to cracking and possible failure. Hence the rule guarantees that the dam base is entirely in compression, ensuring safety against overturning and tensile cracking.

> Distribution: 1.5 marks rule + 1.5 marks importance.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**Q3.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

A **hollow rectangular cast-iron column** of outer dimensions **300 mm × 200 mm** and inner dimensions **200 mm × 100 mm** carries an axial compressive load of **500 kN** with an eccentricity of **30 mm** in the plane parallel to the longer outer dimension.

**(a)** Determine the **stresses at the four corners** of the column section. (7 marks)

**(b)** Find the **maximum permissible eccentricity** for this section so that no tensile stress is induced. Compare it with the applied eccentricity and comment on safety. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Corner Stresses (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Section Properties:**

$$A = BD - bd = (300 \times 200) - (200 \times 100) = 60{,}000 - 20{,}000 = 40{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I_{xx} = \frac{BD^3 - bd^3}{12} = \frac{(300)(200)^3 - (200)(100)^3}{12}$$

$$I_{xx} = \frac{(300)(8 \times 10^6) - (200)(1 \times 10^6)}{12} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^9 - 0.2 \times 10^9}{12} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^9}{12}$$

$$I_{xx} = 1.833 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I_{xx}}{D/2} = \frac{1.833 \times 10^8}{100} = 1.833 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Direct (Axial) Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\text{direct}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{40{,}000} = 12.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\; (\text{compressive})$$

**Step 4 — Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{\text{bending,max}} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{(500 \times 10^3)(30)}{1.833 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_{\text{bending,max}} = \frac{15 \times 10^6}{1.833 \times 10^6} = 8.182 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Stresses at Four Corners:**

The eccentricity is in the plane of the **300 mm** direction (bending about the axis parallel to the 200 mm side). The far and near faces are at $y = \pm 100$ mm.

| Corner | Direct Stress | Bending Stress | **Total Stress (N/mm²)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Near face (closer to load), $y = -100$** | $+12.5$ | $+8.182$ | $\mathbf{+20.682 \text{ (compression)}}$ |
| **Far face (away from load), $y = +100$** | $+12.5$ | $-8.182$ | $\mathbf{+4.318 \text{ (compression)}}$ |
| **Other two corners** (at $y = \pm 100$ but on neutral axis of bending about orthogonal axis) | $+12.5$ | $\pm$ varies | Intermediate values |

**Stresses at all four corners** (using superposition in both directions, since only eccentricity in one direction is given):

| Corner | $y$ (mm) | $x$ (mm) | $\sigma_x$ bending | $\sigma_y$ bending | **Total Stress (N/mm²)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Top-Left (far-near) | $+100$ | $-150$ | $-8.182$ | $0$ | $+4.318$ |
| Top-Right (far-far) | $+100$ | $+150$ | $-8.182$ | $0$ | $+4.318$ |
| Bottom-Left (near-near) | $-100$ | $-150$ | $+8.182$ | $0$ | $+20.682$ |
| Bottom-Right (near-far) | $-100$ | $+150$ | $+8.182$ | $0$ | $+20.682$ |

> All corners remain in **compression** — minimum stress = $4.318$ N/mm² (compressive, no tension).

---

#### Part (b) — Limit of Eccentricity and Safety Check (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $k^2$ and $y_{\text{max}}$ for this section:**

$$k^2 = \frac{I_{xx}}{A} = \frac{1.833 \times 10^8}{40{,}000} = 4583.33 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$y_{\text{max}} = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{200}{2} = 100 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Limit of eccentricity:**

$$e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{4583.33}{100} = 45.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Safety Comment:**

$$e_{\text{applied}} = 30 \text{ mm} \;<\; e_{\text{limit}} = 45.83 \text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark$$

> **Comment:** Since the applied eccentricity (30 mm) is well within the limit (45.83 mm), the section is **safe** and the resultant stress across the entire column remains *compressive*. The minimum stress at the far face is $+4.318$ N/mm² (compressive), confirming the absence of tension. The design has a safety margin of $(45.83 - 30)/45.83 \approx 34.5\%$.

---

### **Question B** (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A **solid circular masonry pillar** of diameter **300 mm** is subjected to an axial compressive load of **250 kN**.

**(a)** Find the eccentricity at which the **maximum compressive stress is 1.5 times the minimum compressive stress**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the **maximum load** the pillar can carry without causing tension if the eccentricity is fixed at **40 mm**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Eccentricity for given stress ratio (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Section Properties (Solid Circle):**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (300)^2}{4} = 70{,}685.83 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (300)^4}{64} = 3.976 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$k^2 = \frac{I}{A} = \frac{d^2}{16} = \frac{(300)^2}{16} = 5625 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$y_{\text{max}} = R = 150 \text{ mm}$$

$$e_{\text{limit}} = \frac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \frac{5625}{150} = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Stress expressions:**

$$\sigma_{\text{max}} = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 + \frac{e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{k^2}\right) = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 + \frac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}}\right)$$

$$\sigma_{\text{min}} = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 - \frac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ratio condition:**

$$\sigma_{\text{max}} = 1.5 \cdot \sigma_{\text{min}}$$

$$1 + \frac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}} = 1.5 \left(1 - \frac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}}\right)$$

$$1 + \frac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}} = 1.5 - 1.5 \cdot \frac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}}$$

$$2.5 \cdot \frac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}} = 0.5$$

$$\frac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}} = \frac{0.5}{2.5} = 0.2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $e$:**

$$e = 0.2 \times e_{\text{limit}} = 0.2 \times 37.5 = 7.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\,e = 7.5 \text{ mm}\,}$$

> **Sanity check:** $e < e_{\text{limit}}$, so no tension. $\sigma_{\text{max}}/\sigma_{\text{min}} = 1.5$ ✓

---

#### Part (b) — Maximum load at given eccentricity (7 marks)

**Given:** $e = 40$ mm, $d = 300$ mm, $A = 70{,}685.83$ mm²

**Step 1 — Check the eccentricity against the limit:**

$$e_{\text{limit}} = 37.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$e = 40 \text{ mm} \;>\; e_{\text{limit}} = 37.5 \text{ mm} \;\; \rightarrow \text{Tension will develop!}$$

**Step 2 — Strategy:**

We need to **reduce the load** so that the *net tensile stress* equals zero at the extreme fibre. The condition for zero tension at the far face is:

$$\sigma_{\text{min}} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I} = 0$$

$$P\left(\frac{1}{A} - \frac{e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I}\right) = 0$$

Since $P \neq 0$:

$$\frac{1}{A} = \frac{e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I}$$

$$P = \frac{I}{A \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}} \cdot A = \frac{I}{e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}$$

Wait — re-derive carefully:

$$\frac{P}{A} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I}$$

$$1 = \frac{A \cdot e \cdot y_{\text{max}}}{I}$$

This gives a *condition on e* only — which is the limit of eccentricity! So with $e > e_{\text{limit}}$, no positive $P$ can keep the section fully compressive.

**Step 3 — Practical interpretation:**

If the eccentricity must be **40 mm**, the load must be **reduced** such that the maximum tensile stress is acceptable (e.g., within allowable tensile stress of masonry). However, since the problem says "without causing tension" and $e > e_{\text{limit}}$, we report:

> **No positive value of P can keep the section fully in compression when $e = 40$ mm**, because $e > e_{\text{limit}} = 37.5$ mm. The eccentricity itself violates the no-tension condition. The section must be **redesigned** (e.g., increased diameter, or load recentred) so that the eccentricity is reduced to $e \leq 37.5$ mm.

**Step 4 — If the question is interpreted as "maximum P such that the section just satisfies no-tension at the redesigned eccentricity":**

Suppose the eccentricity is interpreted as the *minimum* value that triggers tension — i.e., the **limit value** of $e = 40$ mm is to be matched with a **redesigned diameter**. Solving for $d$ such that $e_{\text{limit}} = 40$ mm:

$$\frac{d}{8} = 40 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; d = 320 \text{ mm}$$

Then with $d = 320$ mm:

$$A = \frac{\pi (320)^2}{4} = 80{,}424.77 \text{ mm}^2$$

For any $P$, the section remains fully compressive (with zero stress at the far face when the limit is exactly reached). The **maximum P** is governed by the **crushing strength** of the material, which is not given.

**Step 5 — Final answer based on standard interpretation:**

If the column must accommodate $e = 40$ mm, the **minimum safe diameter** is:

$$d_{\text{min}} = 8 \times e = 8 \times 40 = 320 \text{ mm}$$

> **Conclusion:** The pillar, as designed (diameter 300 mm), is **unsafe** at $e = 40$ mm. It must be enlarged to at least **320 mm diameter** to maintain the no-tension condition.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign convention:** Bending stress is *tensile* on the far side and *compressive* on the near side. Students often subtract the bending stress from the direct stress on BOTH sides, which is wrong. Always use $\sigma = P/A \pm (Pe \cdot y)/I$ with the **+** sign on the side *towards* the load and **–** on the side *away* from the load.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong $y_{\text{max}}$:** For a hollow rectangle, $y_{\text{max}}$ is the **outer half-depth** $D/2$, NOT the inner half-depth $d/2$. A common error is to use $d/2$, leading to an overestimated $e_{\text{limit}}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing "limit of eccentricity" with "eccentricity":** The limit of eccentricity is a **property of the section geometry** — independent of load magnitude. Some students wrongly substitute $P$ values into the limit calculation.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "Middle Third Rule" comes from $d/6$:** This is by far the most-tested sub-question. Memorise: Rectangular → $d/6$, Circular → $d/8$.
>
> 5. **Mixed unit errors:** $I$ in mm⁴, $A$ in mm², $y$ in mm, then $e$ in mm. Don't mix cm and mm in the same calculation.
>
> 6. **For the hollow circular section**, students forget to factor $R^2 - r^2$ correctly and end up with the solid-circle formula — losing 2–3 marks.
>
> 7. **In drawing stress diagrams**, examiners award 1–2 marks for a correctly drawn trapezoidal/rectangular stress block. Always sketch and label the diagram.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Limit of Eccentricity**
>
> - **Master Formula:** $e_{\text{limit}} = \dfrac{k^2}{y_{\text{max}}} = \dfrac{I}{A \cdot y_{\text{max}}}$ — geometry-dependent only, **not** load-dependent.
>
> - **Combined Stress Equation:** $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 \pm \dfrac{e \cdot y}{k^2}\right)$; the $\pm$ sign gives max and min stresses at $y = \pm y_{\text{max}}$.
>
> - **No-Tension Condition:** $\sigma_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 - \dfrac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}}\right) \geq 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e \leq e_{\text{limit}}$.
>
> - **Solid Rectangle (depth $d$):** $e_{\text{limit}} = d/6$ — **Middle Third Rule**.
>
> - **Solid Circle (diameter $d$):** $e_{\text{limit}} = d/8$ — **Middle Quarter Rule**.
>
> - **Hollow Circle ($R, r$):** $e_{\text{limit}} = (R^2 + r^2)/(4R)$ — verify with $r=0 \to R/4$.
>
> - **Hollow Rectangle ($B, D, b, d$):** $e_{\text{limit}} = (BD^3 - bd^3)/[6D(BD - bd)]$.
>
> - **Kern of Section:** The closed region around the centroid inside which the load must lie to keep the entire section in compression. Rectangular kern = rhombus; circular kern = circle.
>
> - **Stress at near face (towards load):** $\sigma_{\text{max comp}} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 + \dfrac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}}\right)$.
>
> - **Stress at far face (away from load):** $\sigma_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 - \dfrac{e}{e_{\text{limit}}}\right)$.
>
> - **Applications:** Masonry dams, retaining walls, chimneys, bridge piers, unreinforced brick columns.
>
> - **Key Insight:** $e_{\text{limit}}$ is a **dimensionless ratio** when divided by the section's relevant dimension; e.g., for a rectangle, $e_{\text{limit}}/d = 1/6$ regardless of size.
>
> - **Sign Convention Tip:** Take *compression as positive* in masonry problems; this avoids sign confusion in superposition.
>
> - **Quick Diagnostic:** If $e > e_{\text{limit}}$ → tension appears; redesign (larger section, recentre load, add reinforcement, or accept limited tension if material allows).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Middle Third Rule for Rectangular and Other Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Middle Third Rule for Rectangular and Other Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MIDDLE THIRD RULE (KTU 2024 Official Terminology):**
> The *Middle Third Rule* is a fundamental design criterion in the strength of materials and masonry/footing design which states that **for a cross-section subjected to an axial compressive load with eccentricity, the resultant load must act within the middle third of the section's depth (or relevant dimension) in order to ensure that the entire cross-section remains in compression — i.e., no tensile stress develops anywhere in the section.**

Mathematically, for a rectangular section of width $b$ and depth $d$, this translates to the eccentricity $e$ of the load from the centroidal axis satisfying:

$$e \le \frac{d}{6}$$

Equivalently, the **kern (core) of the section** — the region within which the load must lie to produce compression over the whole section — is the *middle third* zone of the depth for rectangles. The rule generalizes to other section geometries (circular, hollow circular, I, T, etc.) with their own characteristic limits.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine balancing a stack of books on a table.**
> If you place the stack perfectly in the middle of the table, every part of the table pushes *up* (compression) on the books. Now slide the stack gradually toward the edge — the table still holds it, but the contact stress is no longer uniform. Just before the stack tips off, one edge of the table has *zero* contact (zero stress) while the opposite edge carries all the load. If you push the stack any further, part of the table would have to *pull* (tension) on the books to hold them — but a table (or brick wall, or soil) cannot pull. So the books fall.
> 
> **The "middle third" is the safe zone** inside which the load can sit without ever demanding a "pull" from the supporting material. This is exactly why the rule governs the design of **brick walls, masonry dams, footings, and chimneys** — materials that are excellent in compression but fail in tension.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical KTU Units |
|---|---|---|
| Eccentricity of load | $e$ | **mm** or **m** |
| Section depth / diameter | $d$ or $D$ | **mm** or **m** |
| Section width | $b$ | **mm** or **m** |
| Cross-sectional area | $A$ | **mm²** or **m²** |
| Section modulus | $Z$ | **mm³** or **m³** |
| Axial compressive load | $P$ | **kN** or **N** |
| Bending moment (M = P·e) | $M$ | **kN·m** or **N·mm** |
| Stress (σ) | $\sigma$ | **N/mm² (MPa)** or **kN/m² (kPa)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this rule is critical in Kerala Engineering Practice:**
> Kerala's traditional laterite-stone masonry walls, foundation footings on soft alluvial soil, and small masonry dams in the Western Ghats all rely on this rule. Materials like laterite, brick, dry stone, and soil exhibit **near-zero tensile strength**, so a designer must *guarantee* pure compression across every horizontal joint.

---

## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Combined Axial + Bending Stress

When a vertical (or axial) load $P$ acts on a column/wall section at an **eccentricity** $e$ from the centroidal axis, it is statically equivalent to:
- An axial compressive force $P$ acting at the centroid
- A bending moment $M = P \cdot e$ about the centroidal axis

The **combined stress** at any fibre located at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis (centroid) is given by the **superposition principle**:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{P \cdot e}{Z}$$

where the sign $\pm$ indicates the two extreme fibres.

### 2.2 The "No-Tension" Boundary Condition

For a material that **cannot resist tension** (e.g., brick, stone, plain concrete in early age, soil), the minimum stress on the section must be greater than or equal to zero:

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} \ge 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{P}{A} \ge \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \le \frac{Z}{A}$$

This is the **general kern/core limit**. The quantity $\dfrac{Z}{A} = \dfrac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}}$ is the *radius of the kern* (or *core radius*) for bending about the relevant axis.

### 2.3 Application to a Rectangular Section

For a rectangle of width $b$ and depth $d$:

- Area: $A = b \cdot d$
- Moment of inertia about centroidal axis: $I = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{3}}{12}$
- Distance to extreme fibre: $y_{max} = \dfrac{d}{2}$
- Section modulus: $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}} = \dfrac{b \cdot d^{2}}{6}$

Therefore:

$$e_{max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{b \cdot d^{2}/6}{b \cdot d} = \frac{d}{6}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The middle third of the depth $d$ extends from $d/6$ to $5d/6$ — a total width of $d/3$ on each side of the centroid… wait, the rule actually defines the safe zone as the central one-third of the depth (i.e., from $d/3$ to $2d/3$ measured from one edge), within which the eccentricity $e$ from the centroid must lie. This central $d/3$ band has half-width $d/6$ on either side of the centroid, matching the derived limit.**

### 2.4 Generalization to Other Sections

The kern is **not always a "middle third"**; the term is a historical shorthand for rectangles. For other sections, the *no-tension limit* is computed using the same formula $e_{max} = Z/A$, but yields different geometric expressions.

### 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are exam-critical. Memorize the derivations, not just the results.**

| # | Section Geometry | Section Properties | Maximum Permissible Eccentricity $e_{max}$ | "Safe Zone" Geometric Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rectangle** $b \times d$ | $A=bd$, $Z=\dfrac{bd^{2}}{6}$ | $e_{max} = \dfrac{d}{6}$ | **Middle third** of depth |
| 2 | **Solid Circle** diameter $D$ | $A=\dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$, $Z=\dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{32}$ | $e_{max} = \dfrac{D}{8}$ | **Middle quarter** of diameter (i.e., a central core of width $D/4$) |
| 3 | **Hollow Circle** $D$ outer, $d$ inner | $A=\dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2}-d^{2})$, $Z=\dfrac{\pi}{32}\cdot\dfrac{D^{4}-d^{4}}{D}$ | $e_{max} = \dfrac{D^{4}-d^{4}}{8D\cdot(D^{2}-d^{2})}$ | **Concentric central core** |
| 4 | **Solid Square** $a \times a$ | $A=a^{2}$, $Z=\dfrac{a^{3}}{6}$ | $e_{max} = \dfrac{a}{6}$ | Middle third of side |
| 5 | **Hollow Rectangle** $B \times D$ outer, $b \times d$ inner | $A=BD-bd$, $I=\dfrac{BD^{3}-bd^{3}}{12}$ | $e_{max} = \dfrac{BD^{3}-bd^{3}}{6D(BD-bd)}$ | Modified central band |
| 6 | **I-Section** (standard) | $I_{xx}, Z_{xx}$ from tables | $e_{max} = \dfrac{Z_{xx}}{A}$ | Asymmetric — depends on flange/centroid position |
| 7 | **T-Section** | $I_{xx}, Z_{top}, Z_{bot}$ | $e_{max} = \dfrac{Z_{top}}{A}$ and $\dfrac{Z_{bot}}{A}$ (two limits, kern is a **diamond**) | Non-rectangular kern shape |

> [!WARNING]
> **For non-symmetric sections like T or unequal I, the kern is bounded by TWO different limits on the two sides of the centroid. The kern region is therefore a quadrilateral (rhombus-like), NOT a simple band. Always compute both $e_{top}$ and $e_{bot}$.**

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Masonry Wall Design (KTU Civil Context):** Brick/stone walls under eccentric vertical loads from roof beams — must satisfy middle third rule to prevent joint cracking.
- **Combined Footings:** When a column is placed at the property line, the footing becomes eccentric; the soil pressure must remain compressive everywhere → kern condition.
- **Retaining Walls & Dams:** Check overturning stability and ensure base pressure is wholly compressive → direct application of the kern (middle third for rectangular base).
- **Chimneys & Towers:** Wind load creates eccentricity at the base → apply kern check.
- **Nuclear & Pressure Vessel Codes (Advanced):** Use of *core* concept in plasticity analysis and limit-state design.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Middle Third Rule for a Solid Rectangular Section

**Given:** A rectangular column section of width $b$ and depth $d$, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ acting at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis (measured in the plane of bending, i.e., perpendicular to the neutral axis).

**Step 1 — Free Body Conceptualization:**
Replace the eccentric load with its static equivalent at the centroid:

$$P \text{ at eccentricity } e \equiv P \text{ (axial)} + M = P \cdot e \text{ (bending moment)}$$

> *Conversion logic:* This equivalence holds because forces and moments are vector-additive; moving a force parallel to its line of action by a perpendicular distance $e$ introduces a couple of magnitude $P \cdot e$ with no change in net force or overall equilibrium.

**Step 2 — Direct Stress (Axial Component):**

$$\sigma_{axial} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{b \cdot d}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Uniform stress across the section since the force passes through the centroid.

**Step 3 — Bending Stress (Linear Distribution):**

$$\sigma_{bending} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$$

At the extreme fibre, $y = \dfrac{d}{2}$, so:

$$\sigma_{bending,max} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot (d/2)}{bd^{3}/12} = \frac{P \cdot e \cdot (d/2) \cdot 12}{b \cdot d^{3}} = \frac{6 P e}{b d^{2}}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Substitute $I = bd^{3}/12$ for a rectangle and $y_{max} = d/2$. The $d$ in the denominator cancels one power from the numerator's $d^{3}$, leaving $d^{2}$.

**Step 4 — Superposition of Stresses (Combined Stress Equation):**

$$\sigma_{combined} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P}{bd} + \frac{6Pe}{bd^{2}}$$

Extracting common terms:

$$\sigma_{combined} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{d}\right) \quad \text{(on the compression side, near the load)}$$

$$\sigma_{combined} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right) \quad \text{(on the far side, opposite the load)}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The "+" sign applies to the fibre on the same side as the eccentricity (more compressed); the "−" sign applies to the opposite fibre (less compressed or potentially tensile).

**Step 5 — Applying the No-Tension Condition:**

For zero tension on the far-side fibre:

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right) \ge 0$$

Since $P > 0$ (compressive) and $b, d > 0$ (geometric), the inequality reduces to:

$$1 - \frac{6e}{d} \ge 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{6e}{d} \le 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e \le \frac{d}{6}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Dividing both sides by $P/bd$ (a strictly positive quantity) preserves the inequality direction.

**Step 6 — Geometric Interpretation:**

The maximum permissible eccentricity is $d/6$ on either side of the centroidal axis. The total "safe zone" for the load's line of action is therefore a central strip of width $2 \cdot (d/6) = d/3$ centered on the centroid. This is the **middle third** of the section's depth.

$$\boxed{\,e \le \frac{d}{6} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Load lies in the middle third of the depth}\,}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: No-Tension Limit for a Solid Circular Section

**Given:** A solid circular section of diameter $D$, loaded by $P$ at eccentricity $e$.

**Step 1 — Section Properties:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}, \qquad I = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64}, \qquad y_{max} = \frac{D}{2}, \qquad Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\pi D^{3}}{32}$$

**Step 2 — General Kern Limit:**

$$e_{max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\pi D^{3}/32}{\pi D^{2}/4} = \frac{\pi D^{3}}{32} \cdot \frac{4}{\pi D^{2}} = \frac{4 D}{32} = \frac{D}{8}$$

> *Conversion logic:* The $\pi$ cancels, $D^{3}/D^{2} = D$, and $4/32 = 1/8$.

$$\boxed{\,e_{max} = \frac{D}{8} \quad \text{for a solid circle}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** For a circular section, the load must lie within a central core of *diameter* $D/4$ (radius $D/8$ from centroid) to maintain pure compression.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: No-Tension Limit for a Hollow Circular Section

**Given:** Outer diameter $D$, inner diameter $d$.

**Step 1 — Section Properties:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^{4} - d^{4})$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32 D}$$

**Step 2 — Kern Limit:**

$$e_{max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\pi(D^{4}-d^{4})/(32D)}{\pi(D^{2}-d^{2})/4} = \frac{D^{4}-d^{4}}{32D} \cdot \frac{4}{D^{2}-d^{2}} = \frac{D^{4}-d^{4}}{8D(D^{2}-d^{2})}$$

> *Conversion logic:* Factor $D^{4}-d^{4} = (D^{2}-d^{2})(D^{2}+d^{2})$:

$$e_{max} = \frac{(D^{2}-d^{2})(D^{2}+d^{2})}{8D(D^{2}-d^{2})} = \frac{D^{2}+d^{2}}{8D}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{max} = \frac{D^{2}+d^{2}}{8D} \quad \text{for a hollow circular section}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity Check:** For $d \to 0$ (solid case), $e_{max} \to D^{2}/(8D) = D/8$ ✓. Consistent with Section 3.2.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Hollow Rectangular Section

**Given:** Outer dimensions $B \times D$, inner cavity $b \times d$, with the cavity centered (so the section remains doubly symmetric).

**Step 1 — Section Properties:**

$$A = BD - b \cdot d$$

$$I = \frac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{12}$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{BD^{3} - b d^{3}}{6D}$$

**Step 2 — Kern Limit:**

$$e_{max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{BD^{3} - b d^{3}}{6D \cdot (BD - bd)} = \frac{BD^{3} - b d^{3}}{6(BD^{2} - bdD)}$$

$$\boxed{\,e_{max} = \frac{BD^{3} - b d^{3}}{6D(BD - bd)} \quad \text{for a centered hollow rectangle}\,}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Stress Distribution at the Kern Boundary

When the load acts **exactly at the edge of the middle third** ($e = d/6$ for a rectangle), the stress at the far edge becomes zero, and the stress distribution is triangular:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 + \frac{6 \cdot d/6}{d}\right) = \frac{P}{bd}(1 + 1) = \frac{2P}{bd} = \frac{2P}{A}$$

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6 \cdot d/6}{d}\right) = \frac{P}{bd}(1 - 1) = 0$$

> *Conversion logic:* At the kern boundary, the maximum stress on the compression side **doubles** compared to the centric case. Designers should note this — even though the tension is zero, the peak compressive stress can govern material strength.

**Comparison Table for Different Eccentricities (Rectangular Section):**

| Eccentricity $e$ | $\sigma_{max}$ (compression side) | $\sigma_{min}$ (far side) | Nature of Stress |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ (centric) | $\dfrac{P}{A}$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}$ | Uniform compression |
| $\dfrac{d}{12}$ | $\dfrac{3P}{2A}$ | $\dfrac{P}{2A}$ | Reduced compression, still fully compressive |
| $\dfrac{d}{6}$ (kern edge) | $\dfrac{2P}{A}$ | $0$ | Zero stress at far edge |
| $>\dfrac{d}{6}$ (outside kern) | $>\dfrac{2P}{A}$ | **Tensile** | Part of section in tension — **NOT allowed** for masonry |

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Is My Section Safe?"

> [!TIP]
> **Use this matrix as a mental checklist during the KTU exam:**

| Step | Action | Decision Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the cross-section geometry (Rect / Circle / Hollow / I / T) | Choose correct $A$, $Z$ formulas |
| 2 | Compute the eccentricity $e = M/P$ of the applied load | $e$ is known from problem data |
| 3 | Compute $e_{max} = Z/A$ for the section | Compare with $e$ |
| 4 | If $e \le e_{max}$ | ✅ **SAFE** — no tension develops |
| 5 | If $e > e_{max}$ | ❌ **UNSAFE** — redesign: enlarge section, shift load, or add reinforcement |

## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram A — Concept Flow: From Eccentric Load to Stress Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axial Load P with Eccentricity e] --> B[Equivalent Static System at Centroid]
    B --> B1[Axial Force P at Centroid]
    B --> B2[Bending Moment M = P times e]
    B1 --> C[Uniform Stress P over A]
    B2 --> D[Linear Bending Stress P times e times y over I]
    C --> E[Superpose Stresses]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Compare e with Z over A}
    F -->|e is less than or equal to Z over A| G[SAFE: Pure Compression Across Section]
    F -->|e is greater than Z over A| H[UNSAFE: Tension Develops on Far Side]
    G --> I[Apply Middle Third Rule for Rectangle: e is less than or equal to d over 6]
    H --> J[Redesign: Increase Section or Shift Load]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram B — Kern Geometry Comparison Across Sections

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RECT[RECTANGULAR SECTION]
        R1[Full Depth d] --> R2[Safe Kern is middle third, width d over 3]
        R2 --> R3[Half width on each side: d over 6]
    end
    subgraph CIRC[SOLID CIRCULAR SECTION]
        C1[Diameter D] --> C2[Safe Kern is central core, diameter D over 4]
        C2 --> C3[Radius from center: D over 8]
    end
    subgraph HOLL[HOLLOW CIRCULAR SECTION]
        H1[Outer D, Inner d] --> H2[Kern Radius: D squared plus d squared over 8D]
    end
    subgraph IBEAM[I OR T SECTION]
        I1[Asymmetric geometry] --> I2[Kern is a Diamond shaped Quadrilateral]
        I2 --> I3[Two distinct limits: Z top over A and Z bot over A]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram C — Stress Distribution at the Three Critical Eccentricities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CASE1[Case 1: e equals 0, CENTRIC LOAD]
        S1A[Stress at all fibres equals P over A] --> S1B[Rectangular stress block]
    end
    subgraph CASE2[Case 2: e equals d over 12, INSIDE KERN]
        S2A[Far fibre stress equals P over 2A] --> S2B[Near fibre stress equals 3P over 2A]
        S2B --> S2C[Trapezoidal block, no tension]
    end
    subgraph CASE3[Case 3: e equals d over 6, KERN EDGE]
        S3A[Far fibre stress equals 0] --> S3B[Near fibre stress equals 2P over A]
        S3B --> S3C[Triangular block, zero at far edge]
    end
    subgraph CASE4[Case 4: e greater than d over 6, OUTSIDE KERN]
        S4A[Far fibre has TENSION] --> S4B[Section partially in tension]
        S4B --> S4C[UNSAFE for masonry materials]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Receive Applied Load P and Moment M] --> P2[Step 2: Compute Eccentricity e equals M divided by P]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Identify Section Geometry]

    P3 --> P4b[If Solid Circle: e max equals D over 8]

    P4b --> P5
    P4c --> P5
    P4d --> P5
    P5 --> P6{Condition Satisfied?}

    P6 -->|No| P8[Step 5: Redesign - Increase Section, Shift Load, or Add Tie Rods or Reinforcement]
    P8 --> P2
```
