## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*State and prove the "Middle Third Rule" for a rectangular masonry section. Why is this rule essential in the design of brick and stone masonry walls?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** For a rectangular section of depth $d$ subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ acting at eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis, the section will be subjected to *pure compression* (i.e., no tensile stress at any fibre) **if and only if** the load line of action lies within the **middle third** of the depth. Mathematically: $e \le d/6$.

**Proof (1.5 marks):** Under combined axial and bending, the minimum stress on the far fibre is:

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P}{bd} - \frac{6Pe}{bd^{2}} = \frac{P}{bd}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$

For no tension: $\sigma_{min} \ge 0 \Rightarrow e \le d/6$. The safe zone is the central band of width $d/3$ (i.e., the middle third) within which $e$ must lie.

**Engineering Justification (0.5 mark):** Brick, stone, and laterite have negligible tensile strength. The rule guarantees that horizontal joints experience pure compression, preventing cracking and water ingress — essential for Kerala's monsoon-exposed masonry.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*A solid circular masonry column of diameter $400 \text{ mm}$ carries an axial load of $500 \text{ kN}$ with an eccentricity of $60 \text{ mm}$. Check whether the section is safe against tension using the kern concept.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 400 \text{ mm}$, $P = 500 \text{ kN}$, $e = 60 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the maximum permissible eccentricity for a solid circle:

$$e_{max} = \frac{D}{8} = \frac{400}{8} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compare with actual eccentricity:

$$e = 60 \text{ mm} \;>\; e_{max} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Conclusion: **The section is NOT safe** — the load lies outside the kern. Tension will develop on the far side. **Remedial measure:** Either reduce the eccentricity (reposition load) or enlarge the section to a diameter $D' \ge 8e = 480 \text{ mm}$ so that $e_{max} = D'/8 \ge 60 \text{ mm}$.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, Part B Module questions carry 14 marks and offer internal choice. Students must attempt ONE full question (with both sub-parts).**

---

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q.A (a)** [7 Marks] *Derive an expression for the maximum eccentricity of an axial compressive load on a hollow rectangular masonry section of outer dimensions $B \times D$ and inner cavity $b \times d$ (cavity centered) such that no tensile stress develops in the section. Hence state the geometric "safe zone" for the load.*

**Q.A (b)** [7 Marks] *A masonry wall of cross-section $600 \text{ mm} \times 400 \text{ mm}$ (outer) has a rectangular cavity of $300 \text{ mm} \times 200 \text{ mm}$ (centered). An axial load of $800 \text{ kN}$ is applied with an eccentricity of $40 \text{ mm}$ about the axis of depth $400 \text{ mm}$. Check whether the section is safe under the middle third rule. If not, suggest a redesign.*

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Section Properties:**

For a centered hollow rectangle (outer $B \times D$, inner $b \times d$):

$$A = BD - b \cdot d$$

$$I_{xx} = \frac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{12}$$

$$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{D/2} = \frac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{6 D}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Combined Stress Equation:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P}{BD-bd} \pm \frac{6Pe}{BD^{3}-bd^{3}/(6D)} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

For brevity, write the maximum eccentricity (no-tension limit):

$$e_{max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{(B D^{3} - b d^{3})/(6D)}{BD - bd} = \frac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{6 D (BD - bd)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Simplification:**

Expanding numerator factoring: the result is non-trivial; presenting in the standard form:

$$\boxed{\,e_{max} = \frac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{6 D (BD - bd)}\,}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Geometric Safe Zone:**

The load's line of action must lie within a central band of total width $2 \cdot e_{max} = \dfrac{B D^{3} - b d^{3}}{3 D (BD - bd)}$ centered on the centroid. Outside this band, tension develops on the far fibre.

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $B = 600 \text{ mm}$, $D = 400 \text{ mm}$, $b = 300 \text{ mm}$, $d = 200 \text{ mm}$, $P = 800 \text{ kN}$, $e = 40 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Compute Section Properties:**

$$A = (600)(400) - (300)(200) = 240000 - 60000 = 180000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$BD^{3} = 600 \cdot (400)^{3} = 600 \cdot 64 \times 10^{6} = 38400 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$bd^{3} = 300 \cdot (200)^{3} = 300 \cdot 8 \times 10^{6} = 2400 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$I = \frac{38400 \times 10^{6} - 2400 \times 10^{6}}{12} = \frac{36000 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 3000 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{3000 \times 10^{6}}{200} = 15 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute Maximum Permissible Eccentricity:**

$$e_{max} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{15 \times 10^{6}}{180000} = 83.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Compare with Actual Eccentricity:**

$$e = 40 \text{ mm} \;<\; e_{max} = 83.33 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SAFE} \;✅$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Optional Stress Verification:**

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{Pe}{Z} = \frac{800 \times 10^{3}}{180000} - \frac{800 \times 10^{3} \cdot 40}{15 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$= 4.444 - 2.133 = 2.311 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = 4.444 + 2.133 = 6.577 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

Both stresses are compressive, confirming the section is safe.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q.B (a)** [7 Marks] *A solid circular masonry chimney of outer diameter $1.2 \text{ m}$ and inner diameter $0.8 \text{ m}$ (hollow circular) is subjected to a vertical load of $600 \text{ kN}$ together with a bending moment of $45 \text{ kN·m}$ due to wind. Determine whether tension will develop in the section. If yes, calculate the maximum tensile stress.*

**Q.B (b)** [7 Marks] *Compare the "middle third" limits for: (i) a $400 \text{ mm}$ square section, and (ii) a $400 \text{ mm}$ diameter solid circular section, when both are subjected to the same axial load with eccentricity. Which section permits a larger eccentricity before developing tension, and by how much?*

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $D = 1.2 \text{ m} = 1200 \text{ mm}$, $d = 0.8 \text{ m} = 800 \text{ mm}$, $P = 600 \text{ kN}$, $M = 45 \text{ kN·m}$.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Compute Eccentricity:**

$$e = \frac{M}{P} = \frac{45}{600} = 0.075 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute Section Properties:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1200^{2} - 800^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4}(1440000 - 640000) = \frac{\pi}{4}(800000)$$

$$A = 628318.5 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(D^{4} - d^{4}) = \frac{\pi}{64}(1200^{4} - 800^{4}) = \frac{\pi}{64}(2.0736 \times 10^{12} - 4.096 \times 10^{11})$$

$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}(1.664 \times 10^{12}) = 8.168 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{8.168 \times 10^{10}}{600} = 1.361 \times 10^{8} \text{ mm}^{3}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Compute Maximum Permissible Eccentricity (Hollow Circle Formula):**

$$e_{max} = \frac{D^{2} + d^{2}}{8D} = \frac{1200^{2} + 800^{2}}{8 \times 1200} = \frac{1440000 + 640000}{9600} = \frac{2080000}{9600} = 216.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compare:**

$$e = 75 \text{ mm} \;<\; e_{max} = 216.67 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No tension develops} \;✅$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Optional: Maximum Stresses:**

$$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{Pe}{Z} = \frac{600 \times 10^{3}}{628318.5} - \frac{600 \times 10^{3} \cdot 75}{1.361 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$= 0.955 - 0.331 = 0.624 \text{ MPa (compressive)} \;✅$$

> *No tension develops — the section is safe under the given wind load.*

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Square $a = 400 \text{ mm}$ and Solid Circle $D = 400 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Square Section (Middle Third Limit):**

$$e_{max,square} = \frac{a}{6} = \frac{400}{6} = 66.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Solid Circle Section Limit:**

$$e_{max,circle} = \frac{D}{8} = \frac{400}{8} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Comparison:**

$$e_{max,square} = 66.67 \text{ mm} \;>\; e_{max,circle} = 50 \text{ mm}$$

**The square section permits a larger eccentricity** before tension develops.

**Difference:** $66.67 - 50 = 16.67 \text{ mm}$.

**Percentage greater:** $\dfrac{16.67}{50} \times 100 = 33.33\%$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Engineering Interpretation:**

For the same cross-sectional *area*, a square (with a = 400 mm, A = 160,000 mm²) has a *larger kern* than a circle of equal *characteristic dimension*. This is because the square distributes material away from the centroid more effectively, increasing the section modulus $Z$ relative to area $A$. **However**, for equal *area*, a circle of diameter $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi} = 451 \text{ mm}$ would have $e_{max} = 451/8 = 56.4 \text{ mm}$ — still less than the square. **Conclusion:** Square sections are more "kern-efficient" per unit area when eccentricity is a primary design concern.

---

### 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS — KTU 2024 SCHEME VALUATION KEY ANALYSIS:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/A$ factor:** Many students write $\sigma = P/A \pm M/Z$ and forget to combine units consistently. *Always express $P$ in N, $A$ in mm², $M$ in N·mm, $Z$ in mm³, and $\sigma$ in MPa.*
> 
> 2. **Sign convention confusion:** The kern condition is $\sigma_{min} \ge 0$ (no tension), NOT $\sigma_{max} \le \sigma_{allowable}$ (no overstress). Examiners specifically test this distinction. *Mentioning both checks scores full marks.*
> 
> 3. **Using the solid circle formula $D/8$ for a hollow circle:** This is a *frequent* error. Always use $e_{max} = (D^2 + d^2)/(8D)$ for hollow circular sections.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring units in $(D^2 + d^2)/(8D)$:** The numerator has mm² and the denominator has mm, so the result is in mm. Show this conversion explicitly.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the geometric "safe zone":** KTU answers that only give a numerical comparison (e.g., "$e < e_{max}$") *without* describing the middle third geometry often lose 1–2 marks. Always state: *"The safe zone is the central band of width $d/3$ within the depth."*
> 
> 6. **For non-symmetric sections (I, T):** Many students assume a single "middle third" applies. State explicitly that *the kern is bounded by two different limits* and may form a non-rectangular (rhombus) shape.
> 
> 7. **No partial calculation marks:** Show every substitution step. If your final answer is wrong, well-documented intermediate steps still earn 50–70% partial credit per the KTU marking scheme.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Middle Third Rule**

- ✅ **Core principle:** Axial load + eccentricity = combined axial + bending stress. For no tension, the load must lie within the **kern** (core) of the section.

- ✅ **General formula (works for ALL sections):** $\;e_{max} = \dfrac{Z}{A}\;$ where $Z$ is section modulus and $A$ is cross-sectional area, both computed about the relevant bending axis.

- ✅ **Rectangle $b \times d$:** $\;e_{max} = d/6\;$ → safe zone is the **middle third** of the depth.

- ✅ **Solid Circle (D):** $\;e_{max} = D/8\;$ → safe zone is a central **core of diameter $D/4$** (NOT a "middle third").

- ✅ **Hollow Circle (D, d):** $\;e_{max} = (D^{2} + d^{2})/(8D)\;$ — reduces to $D/8$ when $d = 0$.

- ✅ **Hollow Rectangle (B, D, b, d):** $\;e_{max} = (BD^{3} - bd^{3})/[6D(BD - bd)]\;$.

- ✅ **I/T sections:** Compute $e_{top} = Z_{top}/A$ and $e_{bot} = Z_{bot}/A$ separately; the kern is a quadrilateral.

- ✅ **Stress at kern edge ($e = d/6$ for rectangle):** $\sigma_{max} = 2P/A$ (compressive, doubles) and $\sigma_{min} = 0$.

- ✅ **Stress at centric loading ($e = 0$):** $\sigma = P/A$ (uniform compression) — the most efficient state.

- ✅ **Three stress regimes:** (i) $e = 0$ → uniform block; (ii) $0 < e < d/6$ → trapezoidal block; (iii) $e = d/6$ → triangular block with zero at far edge; (iv) $e > d/6$ → tension — **REJECT**.

- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Masonry walls, brick columns, stone dams, footings, chimneys, retaining walls, soil-foundation interfaces.

- ✅ **Why it matters in Kerala:** Laterite masonry, brick walls, and soil footings all rely on this rule. KTU examiners expect students to *connect theory to local construction practice*.

- ✅ **Kern check vs. Strength check:** The kern (no-tension) check is a **serviceability / cracking** criterion distinct from the **ultimate strength** check. Both must be satisfied.

- ✅ **Quick reference order of magnitudes:** $D/8$ < $a/6$ < $BD^{3}-bd^{3}/[6D(BD-bd)]$ < ... (general comparison is section-specific).

- ✅ **Always show units explicitly** in KTU answers: mm, mm², mm³, MPa — this is a free mark differentiator.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condition for No Tension in the Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Condition for No Tension in the Sections # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Condition for No Tension in a Section** is a fundamental stability and safety criterion in **Strength of Materials** (and **Design of Masonry & Concrete Structures**) that defines the geometric and loading limits required to ensure that **no tensile stress** develops at any point within a cross-section subjected to a combination of an **axial compressive load (P)** and a **bending moment (M)**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this condition is most commonly applied to: - **Masonry dams** (gravity dams, earth dams) - **Retaining walls** - **Chimneys and tall piers** - **Columns and pillars** built of brittle materials (brick, stone, plain concrete) For a **rectangular section** of width $b$ and depth $d$, subjected to an axial load $P$ acting with an eccentricity $e$ from the centroidal axis, the condition for no tension to develop anywhere in the section is mathematically expressed as: $$e \leq \frac{d}{6}$$ This is famously known as the **"Middle Third Rule"** (for rectangular sections) or the **"Middle Fourth Rule"** (for circular sections), and is a direct consequence of the **Kern / Core of the Section** concept. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > The condition for no tension is derived under the assumption of **elastic stress distribution** (linear Navier's bending theory) and is a mandatory check while designing all unreinforced or lightly reinforced masonry/RCC sections carrying eccentric loads. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are **standing on a soft beach sand with a heavy backpack**. If you stand perfectly upright with the backpack on your **center of gravity** (above your navel), your weight passes through the center of your feet — the pressure on the sand is uniform, and the sand doesn't crack or pull apart anywhere. Now, if you lean forward or backward, your weight shifts to one edge of your feet. The sand under the **front edge** gets compressed more (high stress), while the sand under the **back edge** is no longer being pressed — and may even try to lift up (tension!). If your backpack is heavy enough and you lean too far, the sand at the back edge actually **cracks and separates** (tensile failure). **Brittle materials behave exactly like this beach sand:** They are excellent at resisting **compression** but extremely poor at resisting **tension**. Stone, brick, and plain concrete have tensile strength only about **1/10th** of their compressive strength. Therefore, the engineer must ensure that the load line (line of action of the resultant compressive force) passes **close enough** to the geometric center of the section so that the **entire base remains in compression** — no point is ever pulled apart. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** > "Keep the load near the middle. If the load is too far to one side, the other side will try to fly off, and the brittle material will crack. The safe zone is the **middle third** for rectangles." --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Units | Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | KTU Notation | |----------|--------|------------------|--------------| | Axial Compressive Load | $P$ | Newton (N) or kN | $P$ | | Eccentricity of Load | $e$ | metre (m) or mm | $e$ or $x$ | | Bending Moment | $M = P \cdot e$ | N·m or kN·m | $M$ | | Breadth of Section | $b$ | m or mm | $b$ | | Depth of Section | $d$ | m or mm | $d$ or $D$ | | Cross-sectional Area | $A$ | m² or mm² | $A$ | | Moment of Inertia | $I$ | m⁴ or mm⁴ | $I$ | | Section Modulus | $Z$ | m³ or mm³ | $Z = I/y$ | | Extreme Fibre Distance | $y$ | m or mm | $d/2$ | | Stress | $\sigma$ | N/m² (Pa) or N/mm² (MPa) | $\sigma$ | > [!TIP] > KTU board examiners expect students to maintain unit consistency throughout the derivation. A common mistake is mixing $mm$ for $b$ and $m$ for $e$, which produces an answer off by a factor of $10^6$. --- ## 1.4 Physical Significance in Engineering Practice | Real-World Structure | Why "No Tension" Condition is Applied | |----------------------|----------------------------------------| | **Gravity Dam** (e.g., Idukki Dam, Kerala) | Water pressure creates overturning moment; base must remain fully compressed. | | **Retaining Wall** | Lateral earth pressure creates eccentric vertical loads from the retained soil. | | **Brick Chimney** | Wind pressure on tall chimneys causes large bending moments. | | **Stone Arch Bridge Abutment** | Arch thrust is delivered at an angle, causing eccentric load on the abutment. | | **Plain Concrete Column** | Until rebar takes over, plain concrete cannot be trusted in tension. | > [!WARNING] > KTU board examinations frequently test this concept in the context of **stability of masonry dams and retaining walls**. Students must derive the middle-third rule from **first principles** and apply it to numerical problems. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Stress Distribution in an Eccentrically Loaded Section When a short column (or any prismatic member) is loaded with a compressive force $P$ acting at an **eccentricity $e$** from the centroidal axis, the load is statically equivalent to: 1. An **axial compressive force** $P$ acting at the centroid. 2. A **bending moment** $M = P \cdot e$ about the centroidal axis. By the **principle of superposition** (valid because stress is linear in both load and moment for elastic materials), the total stress at any point in the section located at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis is given by the **Navier's bending equation extended for axial load**: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M}{Z}$$ The **'+'** sign is for the side **away** from the load (the side where the moment causes additional tension if any), and the **'−'** sign is for the side **near** the load (where compression is increased). For a **rectangular section** of dimensions $b \times d$: - Area: $A = b \cdot d$ - Section Modulus: $Z = \dfrac{b \cdot d^2}{6}$ - Extreme fibre distance: $y = d/2$ Therefore the stresses at the two extreme fibres become: $$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P}{b \cdot d} + \frac{6 M}{b \cdot d^2}$$ $$\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{P}{b \cdot d} - \frac{6 M}{b \cdot d^2}$$ ## 2.2 The No-Tension Condition: Derivation Logic Step-by-Step **Step 1 — Identify the Critical Fibre** The **minimum stress** occurs at the fibre **opposite** to the side of the load (since the moment tries to lift that side up, potentially producing tension). **Step 2 — Set the Minimum Stress to Zero (or Just Non-Negative)** For the section to remain entirely in compression with **no tension anywhere**: $$\sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{M}{Z} \geq 0$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $M = P \cdot e$** Since the load is eccentric by $e$: $$\frac{P}{A} \geq \frac{P \cdot e}{Z}$$ **Step 4 — Cancel $P$ (assuming compressive load $P > 0$)** $$\frac{1}{A} \geq \frac{e}{Z}$$ $$e \leq \frac{Z}{A}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute for Rectangular Section** For a rectangle: $Z/A = (b d^2/6) / (b d) = d/6$. $$\boxed{e \leq \frac{d}{6}}$$ This is the **Middle Third Rule**: the load must act within the **middle third** of the depth $d$. **Step 6 — Geometric Interpretation** The middle third of a rectangle is a central strip of width $d/3$ (i.e., from $d/3$ to $2d/3$ measured from one edge). As long as the load $P$ is applied anywhere **within this strip**, the stress distribution at the base is either: - **Uniformly compressive** (when $e = 0$), OR - **Triangularly compressive with the smaller edge having zero stress** (when $e = d/6$), OR - **Fully compressive with linearly varying intensity** (when $0 < e < d/6$). If $e > d/6$, a portion of the section experiences **tensile stress**, leading to cracking in brittle materials. ## 2.3 The Kern (Core) of a Section The **Kern** (also called the **Core** or **Heart**) of a section is the region **inside the section** within which the resultant compressive load must act in order to produce **compression throughout the section** (i.e., no tension anywhere). For any arbitrary section, the kern boundary is constructed by drawing **tangents** to the elastic core of the section rotated by 90°, or equivalently, by applying the formula: $$\text{Kern Limit} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}}$$ | Section Shape | Kern Shape | Half-Kern Limit | |---------------|------------|----------------| | **Rectangle** ($b \times d$) | **Rhombus (Diamond)** | $d/6$ on major axis, $b/6$ on minor axis | | **Circle** (diameter $D$) | **Circle** | $D/8$ (Middle Fourth Rule) | | **Solid Square** ($a \times a$) | **Square rotated 45°** | $a/6$ | | **Hollow Rectangle** | **Rhombus** | Depends on $b_1, b_2, d_1, d_2$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Insight:** When asked "What is the kern of a section?", a complete answer requires both the **definition** and a **sketch** showing the kern boundary superimposed on the cross-section. Marks are often lost if the student forgets to label the dimensions. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A} \pm \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Combined axial + bending stress (Navier's extended equation) | N/mm² or MPa | | 2 | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{d}\right)$ | Stress at the **far face** of rectangular section | MPa | | 3 | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 - \dfrac{6e}{d}\right)$ | Stress at the **near face** of rectangular section | MPa | | 4 | $e \leq \dfrac{d}{6}$ | No-tension condition for **rectangle** (Middle Third Rule) | mm or m | | 5 | $e \leq \dfrac{D}{8}$ | No-tension condition for **circle** (Middle Fourth Rule) | mm or m | | 6 | $e \leq \dfrac{Z}{A}$ | General no-tension condition for any section | mm or m | | 7 | $M = P \cdot e$ | Moment-equivalent of eccentric load | kN·m | | 8 | $A = b \cdot d$ | Area of rectangle | mm² or m² | | 9 | $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$ | Section modulus of rectangle | mm³ or m³ | | 10 | $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of rectangle | mm⁴ or m⁴ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Sign Convention:** Compression is taken as **positive** and tension as **negative** in most masonry/strength of materials problems at KTU. A negative $\sigma$ value in a no-tension problem is **always wrong** and signals an eccentricity violation. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the No-Tension Concept 1. **Prevents Cracking of Masonry:** Stone and brick joints open up under tension, leading to water ingress and structural failure. 2. **Ensures Stability of Dams:** A dam whose base develops tension will leak and overturn. The middle-third rule guarantees a **factor of safety against overturning** of approximately 1.5–2.0. 3. **Allows Safe Design of Footings:** Isolated footings under columns must be checked for no-tension to prevent soil-punching and uneven settlement. 4. **Governs Reinforcement Design in RCC:** Although reinforced concrete can resist tension via steel, the **cracking moment** is still a critical serviceability limit state. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the No-Tension Condition (Rectangular Section) ### Statement of the Problem A **rectangular masonry column** of width $b$ and depth $d$ carries an **axial compressive load** $P$ that acts at an **eccentricity $e$** from the centroidal axis (taken along the depth $d$ direction). Derive the condition for the section to remain free from tensile stresses. ### Given: - Cross-section: $b$ (width) $\times$ $d$ (depth) - Axial compressive load: $P$ - Eccentricity of load from centroid: $e$ (measured along depth direction) ### To Prove: The condition for no tension is $e \leq d/6$. ### Full Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Replace the eccentric load with a statically equivalent system at the centroid.** An eccentric load $P$ at distance $e$ from the centroid is equivalent to: - A concentric (axial) load $P$ at the centroid, PLUS - A couple (bending moment) $M = P \cdot e$ about the centroidal axis perpendicular to the direction of eccentricity. This step is justified by the **principle of moments** (Varignon's theorem). The net force and net moment about any point remain unchanged. **Step 2 — Compute the cross-sectional properties.** For a rectangle of width $b$ and depth $d$: $$A = b \cdot d$$ $$I_{xx} = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$$ $$Z_{xx} = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{b d^3 / 12}{d/2} = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the combined stress formula (Navier's extended equation).** The stress at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis (centroidal axis for pure bending) is: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$ Substituting $M = P \cdot e$ and $I = Z \cdot (d/2) = (b d^2/6) \cdot (d/2) = b d^3/12$: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{b d} \pm \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{b d^3 / 12} = \frac{P}{b d} \pm \frac{12 \cdot P \cdot e \cdot y}{b d^3}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate stresses at the two extreme fibres.** At the fibre **near the load** (compression increased by bending), $y = -d/2$: $$\sigma_{near} = \frac{P}{b d} + \frac{12 P e (d/2)}{b d^3} = \frac{P}{b d} + \frac{6 P e}{b d^2} = \frac{P}{b d}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$ At the fibre **far from the load** (compression reduced by bending), $y = +d/2$: $$\sigma_{far} = \frac{P}{b d} - \frac{12 P e (d/2)}{b d^3} = \frac{P}{b d} - \frac{6 P e}{b d^2} = \frac{P}{b d}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$ **Step 5 — State the no-tension criterion.** For no tensile stress to develop anywhere in the section, the **minimum stress** must not be negative (i.e., must be $\geq 0$). The minimum stress occurs at the **far face**: $$\sigma_{far} \geq 0$$ $$\frac{P}{b d}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right) \geq 0$$ **Step 6 — Simplify the inequality.** Since $P > 0$, $b > 0$, and $d > 0$, we can divide both sides by $\frac{P}{b d}$ (a positive quantity): $$1 - \frac{6e}{d} \geq 0$$ $$1 \geq \frac{6e}{d}$$ $$\frac{d}{6} \geq e$$ **Step 7 — State the final condition (boxed).** $$\boxed{e \leq \frac{d}{6}}$$ **Step 8 — Geometric interpretation.** The depth of the section is $d$. The permissible region for the line of action of the load is a central strip of width $d/3$ located at the centre, i.e., from a distance $d/3$ to $2d/3$ from either edge. This is called the **middle third** of the section. > [!NOTE] > When $e = 0$: $\sigma_{far} = \sigma_{near} = P/(b d)$ → **uniform compression**. > When $e = d/6$: $\sigma_{far} = 0$, $\sigma_{near} = 2P/(b d)$ → **triangular stress block, zero at far edge**. > When $e > d/6$: $\sigma_{far} < 0$ → **tension develops** (unacceptable for masonry/brittle materials). ### Q.E.D. --- ## 3.2 Derivation for Circular Section (Middle Fourth Rule) ### Setup Consider a **circular column** of diameter $D$ carrying an axial load $P$ at eccentricity $e$. The section properties are: $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$ $$I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64}$$ $$Z = \frac{I}{D/2} = \frac{\pi D^3}{32}$$ ### Combined Stress $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{4P}{\pi D^2} \pm \frac{32 P e}{\pi D^3} = \frac{4P}{\pi D^2}\left(1 \pm \frac{8e}{D}\right)$$ ### No-Tension Condition Minimum stress at far face must be $\geq 0$: $$\frac{4P}{\pi D^2}\left(1 - \frac{8e}{D}\right) \geq 0$$ Since $4P/(\pi D^2) > 0$: $$1 - \frac{8e}{D} \geq 0$$ $$\boxed{e \leq \frac{D}{8}}$$ This is the **Middle Fourth Rule** for circular sections. The kern of a circle is a concentric circle of diameter $D/4$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stress Distribution at Different Eccentricity Ratios | Case | Eccentricity Ratio $e/d$ | $\sigma_{near}$ (Max) | $\sigma_{far}$ (Min) | Nature of Stress Block | Engineering Verdict | |------|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|-------------------------|---------------------| | 1 | $0$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}$ | Uniform rectangle | **Ideal** — pure compression | | 2 | $0 < e < d/6$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{d}\right)$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 - \dfrac{6e}{d}\right) > 0$ | Trapezoidal, all compression | **Safe** | | 3 | $e = d/6$ (limit) | $\dfrac{2P}{A}$ | $0$ | Triangular, zero at far edge | **Limiting safe case** | | 4 | $d/6 < e < d/2$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{d}\right)$ | $\dfrac{P}{A}\left(1 - \dfrac{6e}{d}\right) < 0$ | Part compression, part tension | **Unsafe** for brittle materials | | 5 | $e = d/2$ | $\dfrac{4P}{A}$ | $-\dfrac{2P}{A}$ | Tension present | **Unsafe** | | 6 | $e > d/2$ | Large compression | Large tension | Unsuitable | **Reject** | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Recall Trick:** "e = d/6 is the threshold. Below = SAFE. Above = NO!" Memorize this for two-mark conceptual questions. --- ## 3.4 Maximum and Minimum Eccentricity for Different Loading Conditions For combined loading scenarios commonly tested at KTU: ### Case A: Axial Load + Moment (general) $$e_{allow} = \frac{Z}{A} = \frac{I}{A \cdot y_{max}}$$ ### Case B: Axial Load + Moment + Lateral Force The total moment $M_{total} = P \cdot e + F \cdot h$, where $F$ is lateral force and $h$ is its lever arm. The effective eccentricity is: $$e_{eff} = \frac{M_{total}}{P} = e + \frac{F \cdot h}{P}$$ This effective eccentricity must satisfy $e_{eff} \leq d/6$ for no tension. ### Case C: Self-Weight + Horizontal Force (e.g., Dam) - Vertical force $W$ (self-weight) acts at center of base. - Horizontal force $H$ (water pressure) acts at height $h/3$ from base (for triangular pressure distribution). - Overturning moment: $M_{OT} = H \cdot h/3$. - Restoring moment about toe: $M_R = W \cdot b/2$ (where $b$ is base width). - Eccentricity of resultant: $e = \dfrac{M_{OT} - M_{R_{net}}}{W}$ → must be within middle third. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that **horizontal forces** produce a moment that effectively shifts the line of action of the vertical load. The eccentricity $e$ is computed **about the base center**, not from the load point on the wall. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Stress Block Evolution with Increasing Eccentricity ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Rectangular Section b x d] --> B[Apply Eccentric Load P at distance e from centroid] B --> C{Is e within d/6?} C -->|Yes, e = 0| D[Case 1: Uniform Compression
sigma_near = sigma_far = P / A] C -->|Yes, 0 less than e less than d/6| E[Case 2: Trapezoidal Compression
Both fibres in compression] C -->|Yes, e = d/6 Limit| F[Case 3: Triangular Stress Block
sigma_far = 0, sigma_near = 2P/A] C -->|No, e greater than d/6| G[Case 4: Partial Tension
UNSAFE for masonry] C -->|No, e much greater than d/6| H[Case 5: Major Tension
REJECT design] D --> I[Safe Design: Conventional Centric Load] E --> J[Safe Design: Eccentric within Kern] F --> K[Limiting Safe Design: Load on Kern Boundary] G --> L[Redesign: Increase depth d or relocate load] H --> M[Redesign: Add reinforcement or change geometry] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 style C fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000 style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style F fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style G fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315,color:#000 style H fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style I fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style J fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style K fill:#dce775,stroke:#827717,color:#000 style L fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style M fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Kern (Core) of Common Sections ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RectangularKern R1[Rectangle b x d] --> R2[Kern: Rhombus with vertices] R2 --> R3[Vertex 1: d/6 above centroid on depth axis] R2 --> R4[Vertex 2: d/6 below centroid on depth axis] R2 --> R5[Vertex 3: b/6 left of centroid on width axis] R2 --> R6[Vertex 4: b/6 right of centroid on width axis] end subgraph CircularKern C1[Circle Diameter D] --> C2[Kern: Concentric Circle] C2 --> C3[Kern diameter: D by 4] C2 --> C4[Allowable eccentricity: D by 8] end subgraph SquareKern S1[Square side a] --> S2[Kern: Square rotated 45 degrees] S2 --> S3[Diagonal vertices at a by 6 from center] end style R1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 style R2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 style C1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style C2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 style S1 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b,color:#000 style S2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a No-Tension Design Check ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Inputs: Load P, Eccentricity e, Section b x d] --> B[Compute Section Properties
A = b d, Z = b d^2 / 6] B --> C[Compute Maximum Permissible Eccentricity
e_allow = d / 6] C --> D{Compare e with e_allow} D -->|e less than d/6| E[Safe: Compute sigma_near and sigma_far] D -->|e equals d/6| F[Limiting: sigma_far = 0, sigma_near = 2P / A] D -->|e greater than d/6| G[Unsafe: Tension develops at far face] E --> H[Output: Both stresses positive compressive] F --> I[Output: Triangular stress block, all compression] G --> J[Output: Provide Reinforcement or Redesign Section] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style D fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style E fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style G fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style H fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style I fill:#dce775,stroke:#827717,color:#000 style J fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 ASCII Schematic of Stress Block (For Quick Visualization in Exam) ``` e = 0 0 < e < d/6 e = d/6 (Limit) ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ │░░░░░░░░░░░░░│ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│ │████████████▓│ │░░░░░░░░░░░░░│ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│ │████████████▓│ │░░░░░░░░░░░░░│ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓│ │██████████ │ │░░░░░░░░░░░░░│ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ │████████ │ │░░░░░░░░░░░░░│ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ │██████ │ │░░░░░░░░░░░░░│ │▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ │████ │ │░░░░░░░░░░░░░│ │▓▓▓▓▓▓ │ │██ │ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ Uniform (P/A) Trapezoidal Triangular ALL COMPRESSION ALL COMPRESSION 0 at far edge Legend: ░ = uniform σ ▓ = trapezoidal σ (varying) = zero stress ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: State the "Middle Third Rule" for a rectangular masonry section. Why is it important in the design of masonry dams and retaining walls?** #### Model Answer: The **Middle Third Rule** states that for a **rectangular cross-section** to remain free from tensile stresses when subjected to an eccentric compressive load, the **line of action of the resultant load must lie within the middle third of the cross-section**. Mathematically: $e \leq \dfrac{d}{6}$, where $e$ is the eccentricity of the load from the centroid and $d$ is the depth of the section in the plane of bending. **Importance in Masonry Design:** 1. Masonry materials (stone, brick, plain concrete) are **brittle** and have very low tensile strength (about 1/10th of compressive strength). 2. In **gravity dams**, water pressure creates a large overturning moment; the resultant vertical force shifts toward the toe. If the resultant moves outside the middle third, the heel of the dam develops tension, leading to **cracking and water seepage**, which can cause catastrophic failure. 3. In **retaining walls**, lateral earth pressure produces a similar moment effect, and the base must remain fully in compression to prevent the wall from overturning or sliding on a cracked base. 4. The rule provides a **simple geometric design check** that gives an implicit factor of safety of about 1.5 to 2.0 against overturning, without requiring detailed stress analysis at every point. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Expectation:** A clear statement of the rule, the formula, and **at least two** practical applications. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: Define the term "Kern of a Section." Sketch the kern of (i) a rectangular section, and (ii) a circular section.** #### Model Answer: **Definition:** The **Kern** (or **Core**) of a cross-section is the region within and around the centroid such that if the **resultant compressive load** acts at any point within this region, the entire section experiences **only compressive stresses** (i.e., no tension develops anywhere in the section). **Kern Dimensions for Common Sections:** (i) **Rectangular Section** ($b \times d$): - The kern is a **rhombus (diamond shape)**. - The distance from the centroid to the kern boundary along the depth axis is $d/6$. - The distance along the width axis is $b/6$. (ii) **Circular Section** (diameter $D$): - The kern is a **concentric circle** of diameter $D/4$. - The maximum allowable eccentricity is $D/8$. ``` Rectangular Kern (Rhombus) Circular Kern ___________ ___ / | \ / \ / | \ / . \ / <--d/6--d/6-->\ | / \ | \ | / | | • | | \ | / | \ / | \_____|_____/ \_______/ (• = centroid) ``` > [!TIP] > **Remember:** For square sections, the kern is a **square rotated 45°** with its vertices at a distance of $a/6$ from the center, where $a$ is the side of the square. --- ## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) ### 🎯 Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]** **Q: A rectangular masonry column of size 600 mm × 400 mm (depth × width) carries an axial load of 400 kN at an eccentricity of 80 mm along the depth direction. Check whether the section develops any tensile stress. If tension develops, determine the maximum tensile stress and suggest a suitable redesign such that no tension occurs.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Check for Tension **Given:** - Section: $b = 400$ mm, $d = 600$ mm - Axial load: $P = 400$ kN $= 400 \times 10^3$ N - Eccentricity: $e = 80$ mm (along depth) **Step 1: Compute Section Properties** $$A = b \times d = 400 \times 600 = 240{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$Z = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6} = \frac{400 \times 600^2}{6} = \frac{400 \times 360{,}000}{6} = 24 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 2: Check the Middle Third Limit** $$e_{allow} = \frac{d}{6} = \frac{600}{6} = 100 \text{ mm}$$ Compare with actual eccentricity: $e = 80$ mm. Since $e = 80$ mm $< e_{allow} = 100$ mm, the section lies **within the middle third**. **No tension develops.** **Step 3: Compute the Actual Stresses for Verification** $$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{400 \times 10^3}{240{,}000} + \frac{400 \times 10^3 \times 80}{24 \times 10^6}$$ $$\sigma_1 = 1.667 + 1.333 = 3.000 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 3.0 \text{ MPa (Compression, near face)}$$ $$\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = 1.667 - 1.333 = 0.333 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 0.333 \text{ MPa (Compression, far face)}$$ **Step 4: Conclusion** Both stresses are **compressive and positive**. The stress distribution is **trapezoidal** with minimum stress $0.333$ MPa (compression) and maximum stress $3.0$ MPa (compression). **The section is SAFE — no tension develops.** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Redesign if Tension Occurred (Hypothetical Redesign Problem) > **Note to student:** The original problem shows no tension. The redesign part is included to demonstrate the procedure should $e > d/6$ occur. **Hypothetical Scenario:** If the eccentricity were $e = 120$ mm (which is $> d/6 = 100$ mm), tension would develop. To redesign: **Step 1: Determine the required depth $d_{new}$ such that $e \leq d_{new}/6$:** $$d_{new} = 6 \times e = 6 \times 120 = 720 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2: Verify the new stresses:** $$A_{new} = 400 \times 720 = 288{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$Z_{new} = \frac{400 \times 720^2}{6} = 34.56 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$ $$\sigma_{near} = \frac{400 \times 10^3}{288{,}000} + \frac{400 \times 10^3 \times 120}{34.56 \times 10^6} = 1.389 + 1.389 = 2.778 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_{far} = 1.389 - 1.389 = 0.0 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3: Alternative Redesign — Reduce Eccentricity** Alternatively, keep $d = 600$ mm and shift the load to $e \leq 100$ mm. This can be achieved by: - Adding a **bracket or corbel** to relocate the load closer to the centroid. - Using a **haunched (wider) base** at the foundation level. - Introducing a **steel reinforcement** if the slight tension is acceptable (limit-state design). **Final Conclusion:** The original section (with $e = 80$ mm) is safe under the given load. No redesign is necessary. --- ### 🎯 Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]** **Q: A solid circular masonry column of diameter 500 mm carries an axial load of 600 kN with an eccentricity of 50 mm. Determine the maximum and minimum stresses at the base. State the condition for no tension in such circular sections and verify whether the given column satisfies it.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compute Section Properties and Stresses **Given:** - Diameter: $D = 500$ mm - Axial load: $P = 600$ kN $= 600 \times 10^3$ N - Eccentricity: $e = 50$ mm **Step 1: Section Properties for Circle** $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 500^2}{4} = 196{,}349.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$Z = \frac{\pi D^3}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 500^3}{32} = 12.272 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 2: Compute the Stresses** $$\sigma_{near} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{600 \times 10^3}{196{,}349.5} + \frac{600 \times 10^3 \times 50}{12.272 \times 10^6}$$ $$\sigma_{near} = 3.056 + 2.445 = 5.501 \text{ MPa (Compression, near face)}$$ $$\sigma_{far} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = 3.056 - 2.445 = 0.611 \text{ MPa (Compression, far face)}$$ **Step 3: Conclusion on Stress State** Both stresses are **compressive**. The stress distribution is **trapezoidal** with values from $0.611$ MPa to $5.501$ MPa. The column is **safe** with respect to the no-tension condition. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: State the No-Tension Condition and Verify **Statement of the Middle Fourth Rule:** For a **circular section** of diameter $D$, the line of action of the compressive load must lie within a concentric inner circle of diameter $D/4$. This is equivalent to requiring the **eccentricity** $e$ to satisfy: $$\boxed{e \leq \frac{D}{8}}$$ This is known as the **"Middle Fourth Rule"** for circular sections. **Derivation Sketch:** For a circular section, the extreme fibre distance is $y_{max} = D/2$ and the section modulus is $Z = \pi D^3/32$. The general no-tension condition $e \leq Z/A$ yields: $$\frac{Z}{A} = \frac{\pi D^3 / 32}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{D}{8}$$ Hence $e_{max} = D/8$. **Verification for the Given Column:** $$e_{allow} = \frac{D}{8} = \frac{500}{8} = 62.5 \text{ mm}$$ Compare with actual eccentricity: $e = 50$ mm. Since $e = 50$ mm $< e_{allow} = 62.5$ mm, **the column lies within the kern**. **The column satisfies the no-tension condition.** ✓ > [!TIP] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The actual eccentricity (50 mm) is 80% of the allowable limit (62.5 mm), giving a safety margin of 20%. The minimum stress is still positive (0.611 MPa compression), confirming the safety of the design. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 marks):** Students often forget that $\sigma_{far} = P/A - M/Z$. If the formula is written in the wrong order, the computed minimum stress can become negative even when the design is safe, or vice versa. > > 2. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Mixing $mm$ and $m$ in the same calculation. KTU expects consistent units — if $b$ and $d$ are in mm, then $P$ must be in N, $A$ in mm², and $\sigma$ in N/mm² (MPa). > > 3. **Forgetting the Direction of Eccentricity (–1 to –3 marks):** The problem must clearly state whether $e$ is along the depth or width. The critical eccentricity limit is $d/6$ for bending about the depth axis. If $e$ is along the width, the limit becomes $b/6$ instead. > > 4. **Not Stating the "No Tension" Conclusion (–1 mark):** Even if the math is correct, KTU examiners deduct marks if the student does not explicitly write **"The section is safe from tension"** or **"No tensile stress develops"**. > > 5. **Confusing Rectangular and Circular Rules (–3 marks):** Writing $e \leq d/6$ for a circular section. The correct rule is $e \leq D/8$. > > 6. **Skipping the Limiting Case Discussion (–2 marks):** When asked to "verify the no-tension condition," the answer should explicitly state: (i) the allowable limit, (ii) the comparison, and (iii) the computed minimum stress (showing it is $\geq 0$). --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Core Formula:** Combined stress in an eccentrically loaded section is $\sigma = P/A \pm M/Z$, where $M = P \cdot e$. - **Middle Third Rule (Rectangle):** $e \leq d/6$. The kern is a rhombus with half-diagonals $d/6$ and $b/6$ along the two principal axes. - **Middle Fourth Rule (Circle):** $e \leq D/8$. The kern is a concentric circle of diameter $D/4$. - **General No-Tension Condition:** $e \leq Z/A = I/(A \cdot y_{max})$ for any cross-section. - **Physical Meaning:** Masonry and plain concrete are weak in tension. The no-tension condition ensures the entire cross-section remains in compression, preventing cracks. - **Stress Distribution Cases:** - $e = 0$ → Uniform compression. - $0 < e < d/6$ → Trapezoidal compression. - $e = d/6$ → Triangular, zero at far edge (limiting case). - $e > d/6$ → Tension develops (UNSAFE for masonry). - **Maximum Eccentricity Ratios (Quick Memory Aid):** - Rectangle: $e_{max}/d = 1/6 \approx 0.1667$ - Circle: $e_{max}/D = 1/8 = 0.125$ - Square: $e_{max}/a = 1/6 \approx 0.1667$ - **Sign Convention:** Compression is **positive**, tension is **negative** in no-tension problems. KTU follows this convention. - **Real-World Applications:** Gravity dams, retaining walls, brick chimneys, stone columns, arch bridge abutments. - **Design Strategy when $e > d/6$:** 1. Increase the depth $d$ such that $d_{new} = 6e$. 2. Reduce the eccentricity (relocate the load). 3. Provide steel reinforcement to resist the small tensile stress (limit-state design). 4. Change the cross-section shape (e.g., to I-section or hollow section with a larger kern). - **Formula Variants to Memorize (Direct Examination Use):** $$\sigma_{max} = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{d}\right), \quad \sigma_{min} = \frac{P}{A}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{d}\right)$$ - **KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** Always draw the stress block diagram as part of your answer. Examiners reward **visual clarity** with 1–2 extra marks, especially in 14-mark questions. - **Maximum Marks Strategy in KTU Board Exams:** 1. State given data and assumptions (1 mark). 2. Show the full derivation/formula selection (2–3 marks). 3. Substitute values with units (2 marks). 4. Compute final stresses (2 marks). 5. State the no-tension condition explicitly (1 mark). 6. Compare and conclude (1–2 marks). 7. Draw a stress block diagram (1 mark).

topics.title: Stability of Masonry Dams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stability of Masonry Dams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Masonry Dam** is a massive hydraulic structure constructed using stone masonry, brick masonry, or plain concrete, designed to impound water and resist the horizontal thrust exerted by the reservoir through its **self-weight (gravity action)**. Under the KTU 2024 syllabus, the analysis is restricted to the **Gravity Dam**, which derives its stability entirely from the dead weight of the masonry resisting the destabilizing forces (water pressure, uplift, silt, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAGT302, Module 4):**
> A gravity dam is a solid masonry structure, generally triangular in cross-section, whose stability depends solely on its self-weight resisting the external forces. The dam must satisfy four cardinal conditions: (1) No overturning, (2) No sliding, (3) No tensile stress at the base, and (4) No crushing of masonry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Leaning Bookshelf"**
> Imagine a heavy bookshelf standing on a carpet. The bookshelf tries to topple forward (overturn) if you push the top of it. To prevent this, we make the base very wide and the body very heavy. The wider the base, the more "grip" it has on the carpet (preventing sliding), and the heavier the bookshelf, the harder it is to tip over. A **masonry dam behaves identically** — the reservoir water pushes the dam horizontally (like your hand), while the dam's enormous self-weight and broad base resist the push. If the dam is too light or too narrow, it will tip; if it has poor grip on the foundation, it will slide.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w$ | **9.81 kN/m³** (often **10 kN/m³** for KTU problems) | Used in water pressure & uplift |
| Density of water | $\rho_w$ | **1000 kg/m³** | SI unit reference |
| Specific weight of masonry | $\gamma_m$ | **22 to 24 kN/m³** | Typical: **22.4 kN/m³** (granite), **23.0 kN/m³** (concrete) |
| Coefficient of friction (concrete on rock) | $\mu$ | **0.6 to 0.75** | KTU typical: **0.6** |
| Safe compressive strength of masonry | $\sigma_c$ | **800 to 1500 kN/m²** | As per IS 6512 |

## 1.4 Cross-Section of a Typical Gravity Dam

A gravity dam has a **triangular or trapezoidal cross-section** with:
- **Heel** — the upstream edge of the base (in contact with water).
- **Toe** — the downstream edge of the base.
- **Top width (free board + roadway)** — usually **1.5 m to 6 m**.
- **Base width (B)** — depends on height and water head.
- **Vertical upstream face** (typical) or slightly battered.
- **Downstream face** — battered (sloped) at about **0.7H : 1V to 0.8H : 1V**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forces Acting on a Masonry Dam

The following external forces (per metre run of dam length) act on the dam. Each contributes either a **destabilizing (overturning) moment** or a **stabilizing (restoring) moment** about the toe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - All moments are taken about the **Toe** (downstream edge of base).
> - **Restoring Moment ($M_R$)** — caused by self-weight (acts downward, lever arm from toe).
> - **Overturning Moment ($M_O$)** — caused by horizontal water pressure, uplift, silt pressure, ice pressure.
> - Consider a strip of **1 m length** of the dam perpendicular to the page.

### A. Self-Weight of the Dam (W)
Acts vertically downward through the centroid of the cross-section.
$$W = \sum (\text{Area}_i \times \gamma_m)$$

### B. Horizontal Water Pressure (P)
Acts horizontally on the upstream face. Magnitude (per metre run):
$$P = \frac{1}{2}\,\gamma_w\,H^2$$
This force acts at a height of **H/3** from the base.

### C. Uplift Pressure (U)
Water seeps under the dam and exerts an upward pressure on the base. Two standard assumptions:

- **Case 1 — Full Uplift (Triangular):** Zero at toe, full hydrostatic head $\gamma_w H$ at heel.
$$U = \frac{1}{2}\,\gamma_w\,H\,B \quad ; \quad \text{acts at } \frac{B}{3} \text{ from heel } \left(=\frac{2B}{3} \text{ from toe}\right)$$

- **Case 2 — Reduced / Rectangular Uplift:** Uniform pressure = $\frac{1}{3}\gamma_w H$ across base.
$$U = \frac{1}{3}\,\gamma_w\,H\,B \quad ; \quad \text{acts at } \frac{B}{2} \text{ from heel}$$

### D. Silt Pressure ($P_s$)
When reservoir water carries silt and deposits it against the dam, an equivalent fluid pressure of $\gamma_s = (1 - \sin\phi)/(1 + \sin\phi) \cdot \gamma_w$ acts.
$$P_s = \frac{1}{2}\,\gamma_s\,h_s^2$$
where $h_s$ = height of silt (commonly taken as $H/2$ to $H$ in KTU problems), $\phi$ = angle of internal friction of silt (often 30°).

### E. Wind Pressure, Wave Pressure, Ice Pressure, Seismic Forces
Secondary; often neglected in elementary KTU numericals but mentioned in theory.

## 2.2 The Four Conditions of Stability

| # | Condition | KTU Requirement | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **No Overturning** | FOS $\geq 1.5$ (some texts $\geq 1.2$) | $\text{FOS}_O = \Sigma M_R \,/\, \Sigma M_O \geq 1.5$ |
| 2 | **No Sliding** | FOS $\geq 1.0$ (often $\geq 1.5$ for safety) | $\text{FOS}_S = \mu \Sigma V \,/\, \Sigma H \geq 1.0$ |
| 3 | **No Tension (Middle Third Rule)** | Resultant must lie within middle third of base | $e \leq B/6$ |
| 4 | **No Crushing** | Max base pressure $\leq$ safe strength of masonry | $p_{\max} \leq \sigma_{\text{allowable}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Middle Third Rule (Middle Half Rule for Full Uplift):**
> For a **rectangular base**, when uplift is neglected, the resultant of all vertical and horizontal forces must intersect the base within the **middle third** to ensure the entire base remains in compression (no tension). The eccentricity $e$ measured from the centroid of the base must satisfy $e \leq B/6$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Lever Arm (from toe) | Moment Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Self-weight (rectangular portion) | $W_1 = a \times H \times \gamma_m$ | $B - a/2$ | Restoring |
| 2 | Self-weight (triangular portion) | $W_2 = \frac{1}{2}(B-a)H \gamma_m$ | $B - a - (B-a)/3$ | Restoring |
| 3 | Horizontal water pressure | $P = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H^2$ | $H/3$ | Overturning |
| 4 | Uplift (full triangular) | $U = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H B$ | $2B/3$ | Overturning |
| 5 | Uplift (reduced rectangular) | $U = \frac{1}{3}\gamma_w H B$ | $B/2$ | Overturning |
| 6 | Silt pressure | $P_s = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_s h_s^2$ | $h_s / 3$ | Overturning |
| 7 | Eccentricity (from centre) | $e = \dfrac{B}{2} - \bar{x}$ | — | — |
| 8 | Distance of resultant from toe | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\Sigma M_R - \Sigma M_O}{\Sigma V_{\text{net}}}$ | — | — |
| 9 | Net vertical force | $V = W_{\text{total}} - U$ | — | — |
| 10 | Max base pressure | $p_{\max} = \dfrac{V}{B}\left(1 + \dfrac{6e}{B}\right)$ | — | — |
| 11 | Min base pressure | $p_{\min} = \dfrac{V}{B}\left(1 - \dfrac{6e}{B}\right)$ | — | — |
| 12 | FOS against overturning | $\text{FOS}_O = \dfrac{\Sigma M_R}{\Sigma M_O}$ | — | — |
| 13 | FOS against sliding | $\text{FOS}_S = \dfrac{\mu \cdot V}{P}$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Sign Convention:** Since uplift acts **upward**, it both (i) **reduces the net vertical force** $V$ and (ii) **adds to the overturning moment** $M_O$ about the toe. Both effects are **destabilizing**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of large reservoirs** in India (e.g., Bhakra, Hirakud, Idukki, Mullaperiyar) — all follow these stability principles.
- **Tailings dams** in mining industries use the same overturning/sliding checks.
- **Spillway stability** during partial submergence scenarios.
- **Irrigation weirs and barrages** are designed using identical principles.
- **Foundation engineering** — informs allowable bearing pressure calculations for any massive concrete structure on rock.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Base Pressure under an Eccentric Vertical Load (Middle Third Rule)

**Statement to derive:** For a rectangular base of width $B$ subjected to a vertical load $V$ acting at an eccentricity $e$ from the centroid, the normal stress distribution at the base is **trapezoidal (or triangular)** given by the classic flexure formula:
$$p = \frac{V}{B}\left(1 \pm \frac{6e}{B}\right)$$

### Step 1: Set up the geometry

Consider a rectangular base of width $B$ and length (perpendicular to page) $= 1$ m. Let the resultant vertical force be $V$, acting at a distance $\bar{x}$ from the toe. Define the **eccentricity** $e$ as the deviation of the line of action of $V$ from the **centroid of the base**:
$$e = \frac{B}{2} - \bar{x}$$

### Step 2: Decompose the eccentric load

An eccentric axial load is statically equivalent to a **concentric axial load + a moment**. Place $V$ at the centroid and add a moment $M = V \cdot e$.

### Step 3: Stress due to concentric load (uniform part)

The uniform stress across the base is:
$$p_1 = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{V}{B \times 1} = \frac{V}{B}$$

### Step 4: Stress due to the moment (bending part)

Using the **flexure formula** $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ where $y$ is the distance from the neutral axis (centroid) and $I$ is the second moment of area.

For a rectangular base of width $B$ and depth 1 m:
$$I = \frac{1 \times B^3}{12}, \qquad y_{\max} = \frac{B}{2}$$

The maximum bending stress is:
$$p_2 = \frac{M \cdot (B/2)}{I} = \frac{V \cdot e \cdot (B/2)}{B^3 / 12} = \frac{6 V e}{B^2}$$

### Step 5: Superpose the two stress components

At the **heel** (upstream edge, $y = -B/2$, same side as load shift):
$$p_{\max} = p_1 + p_2 = \frac{V}{B} + \frac{6 V e}{B^2} = \frac{V}{B}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{B}\right)$$

At the **toe** (downstream edge, $y = +B/2$):
$$p_{\min} = p_1 - p_2 = \frac{V}{B} - \frac{6 V e}{B^2} = \frac{V}{B}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{B}\right)$$

### Step 6: Apply the "no tension" condition

For $p_{\min} \geq 0$, we require:
$$\frac{V}{B}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{B}\right) \geq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 1 - \frac{6e}{B} \geq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad e \leq \frac{B}{6}$$

This is the **Middle Third Rule** — the resultant must lie within the middle third of the base.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "middle third"?** If $e \leq B/6$, then $\bar{x}$ (from toe) is between $B/3$ and $2B/3$, i.e. the resultant lies within the **middle third** of the base. The "kern" of a rectangle of width $B$ is exactly the middle third region.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Forces, Lever Arms, and Moments

| Force Symbol | Magnitude (per m run) | Point of Action (Lever arm from toe) | Moment About Toe | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $W_1$ (rectangle of dam) | $a \cdot H \cdot \gamma_m$ | $B - a/2$ | $W_1 \cdot (B - a/2)$ | Restoring |
| $W_2$ (triangle of dam) | $\frac{1}{2}(B-a) H \gamma_m$ | $a + (B-a)/3$ | $W_2 \cdot [a + (B-a)/3]$ | Restoring |
| $P$ (water thrust) | $\frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H^2$ | $H/3$ (above base) | $P \cdot H/3$ | Overturning |
| $U$ (uplift, full tri.) | $\frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H B$ | $2B/3$ | $U \cdot 2B/3$ | Overturning |
| $P_s$ (silt) | $\frac{1}{2}\gamma_s h_s^2$ | $h_s/3$ | $P_s \cdot h_s/3$ | Overturning |

## 3.3 Derivation: Eccentricity and Resultant Location

The resultant of all vertical forces (after subtracting uplift) is:
$$V_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{total}} - U$$

The **net moment** about the toe:
$$M_{\text{net}} = M_R - M_O = \left(\sum W_i \cdot x_i\right) - \left(\sum F_{\text{horiz}} \cdot y_i\right) - \left(U \cdot \bar{x}_U\right)$$

The distance of the resultant from the toe:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{M_{\text{net}}}{V_{\text{net}}}$$

Eccentricity from the centre of the base:
$$e = \frac{B}{2} - \bar{x}$$

A **positive eccentricity** means the resultant is shifted towards the **heel** (upstream). A **negative eccentricity** means it has shifted past the centre towards the **toe** (downstream).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In nearly all KTU numericals, the heel is on the upstream side and the resultant falls on the upstream side of the centre (positive $e$). This is safe because it produces higher compressive stress at the heel where the foundation rock is usually strongest.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Forces Acting on a Masonry Gravity Dam

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Since the dam is a physical 2D cross-section, the Mermaid block diagram below maps the **functional interaction** of each force with the dam body, its point of application, and its effect on stability.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ENV[ENVIRONMENTAL LOADS]
        nodeA[Reservoir Water]
        nodeB[Silt Deposit]
        nodeC[Seismic / Wind]
    end

    subgraph FORCES[FORCES ON DAM PER METRE RUN]
        nodeD[Self Weight W — Vertical, Downward]
        nodeE[Horizontal Water Pressure P — Push from upstream]
        nodeF[Uplift Pressure U — Vertical, Upward at base]
        nodeG[Silt Pressure Ps — Horizontal, on upstream face]
    end

    subgraph LOC[LOCATION OF APPLICATION]
        nodeH[Centroid of cross-section]
        nodeI[At depth H from top, lever H/3 from base]
        nodeJ[Triangular distribution across base, peak at heel]
        nodeK[At depth hs from silt top, lever hs/3 from base]
    end

    subgraph EFF[STABILITY EFFECTS]
        nodeL[Restoring Moment MR — Prevents overturning]
        nodeM[Overturning Moment MO — Tends to tip dam downstream]
        nodeN[Net Vertical Force V = W - U — Provides sliding resistance]
    end

    subgraph CHK[STABILITY CHECKS]
        nodeO[FOS Overturning = MR / MO — should be greater than 1.5]
        nodeP[FOS Sliding = mu times V / P — should be greater than 1.0]
        nodeQ[Base Pressure Distribution — middle third rule]
        nodeR[Max Base Stress less than allowable strength]
    end

    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeM

    nodeD --> nodeH --> nodeL
    nodeE --> nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeF --> nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeF --> nodeN
    nodeG --> nodeK --> nodeM

    nodeL --> nodeO
    nodeM --> nodeO
    nodeL --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeL --> nodeQ
    nodeN --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Stability Check Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[STEP 1: Define dam geometry and material properties] --> nodeB[STEP 2: Identify all external forces and their lever arms]
    nodeB --> nodeC[STEP 3: Compute vertical forces W and U]
    nodeC --> nodeD[STEP 4: Compute horizontal forces P and Ps]
    nodeD --> nodeE[STEP 5: Calculate restoring moment MR about toe]
    nodeE --> nodeF[STEP 6: Calculate overturning moment MO about toe]
    nodeF --> nodeG[STEP 7: Compute FOS over = MR divided by MO]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Is FOS over greater than 1.5?}
    nodeH -- No --> nodeI[REVISE SECTION — increase base width]
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeJ[STEP 8: Compute net vertical V and resultant position xbar]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[STEP 9: Compute eccentricity e = B/2 minus xbar]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Is e less than or equal to B/6?}
    nodeL -- No --> nodeI
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeM[STEP 10: Compute pmax and pmin at base]
    nodeM --> nodeN[STEP 11: Check pmax less than allowable strength]
    nodeN --> nodeO[STEP 12: Compute FOS slide = mu times V divided by P]
    nodeO --> nodeP{Is FOS slide greater than 1.0?}
    nodeP -- No --> nodeI
    nodeP -- Yes --> nodeQ[DESIGN IS SAFE]
```

        │ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓  │

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the various forces acting on a masonry gravity dam. State the conditions that a gravity dam must satisfy for stability.**

**Model Answer:**

A masonry gravity dam is subjected to the following forces (per metre length):
1. **Self-weight of the dam ($W$)** — vertical, downward, acts through the centroid.
2. **Horizontal water pressure ($P$)** — horizontal, acting on the upstream face at $H/3$ from base.
3. **Uplift pressure ($U$)** — vertical, upward, acting on the base (triangular or rectangular distribution).
4. **Silt pressure ($P_s$)** — horizontal, on upstream face above bed level.
5. **Wind/seismic forces, ice pressure, wave pressure** — secondary loads.

**Conditions of Stability:**
1. The dam must be safe against **overturning** (FOS $\geq 1.5$).
2. The dam must be safe against **sliding** (FOS $\geq 1.0$).
3. There must be **no tension** at the base (resultant within middle third, $e \leq B/6$).
4. The **maximum compressive stress** at the base or any section must not exceed the safe strength of masonry.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the "middle third rule" for a masonry dam. Why is this rule important?**

**Model Answer:**

The **middle third rule** states that for a gravity dam with a horizontal base and negligible uplift, the **resultant of all forces** (self-weight, water pressure, uplift, etc.) must intersect the base **within the middle third** of the base width to ensure the entire base remains in compression.

**Mathematically:** $e \leq B/6$, where $e$ is the eccentricity of the resultant from the centre of the base.

**Importance:**
- If the resultant passes outside the middle third, the **minimum base pressure $p_{\min}$ becomes negative** (i.e. tension develops at the base).
- Masonry and the underlying rock foundation are **weak in tension** and will crack, allowing water seepage which can lead to uplift increase and progressive failure.
- Hence, by keeping $p_{\min} \geq 0$, the entire base remains in compression — a safe design principle.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** A masonry gravity dam is of trapezoidal section with top width **1.5 m**, bottom width **6.0 m**, and height **6.0 m**. The upstream face is vertical. The water level is at the top of the dam. Take specific weight of masonry $\gamma_m = 22.4$ kN/m³, specific weight of water $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³, and assume full triangular uplift pressure acts at the base. Calculate:
- (i) Self-weight of the dam.
- (ii) Horizontal water pressure on the dam.
- (iii) Uplift pressure at the base. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Using the values obtained in part (a), and taking the coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.6$, determine:
- (i) The eccentricity of the resultant at the base.
- (ii) The maximum and minimum stresses at the base.
- (iii) The factors of safety against overturning and sliding.
- (iv) State whether the dam is safe in all respects. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a): Calculation of Forces**

**Step 1 — Self-weight of the dam:**
Split the cross-section into a **rectangle** (top portion) and a **triangle** (side batter).

- Rectangle: width $a = 1.5$ m, height $H = 6$ m
$$W_1 = 1.5 \times 6 \times 22.4 = 201.6 \text{ kN}$$

- Triangle: base $= B - a = 6.0 - 1.5 = 4.5$ m, height $= 6$ m
$$W_2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 4.5 \times 6 \times 22.4 = 302.4 \text{ kN}$$

- Total self-weight:
$$W = W_1 + W_2 = 201.6 + 302.4 = 504.0 \text{ kN (per m run)}$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal water pressure:**
$$P = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 6^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 36 = 176.58 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Uplift pressure (full triangular distribution):**
$$U = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H B = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 6 \times 6 = 176.58 \text{ kN}$$

---

**Part (b): Stability Analysis**

**Step 4 — Lever arms (measured from the toe):**

- $W_1$ (rectangle, centroid at $a/2 = 0.75$ m from heel): distance from toe $= B - 0.75 = 5.25$ m
- $W_2$ (triangle, centroid at $\frac{1}{3} \times 4.5 = 1.5$ m from heel): distance from toe $= 1.5 + 1.5 = 3.0$ m
- $P$ (water): acts at $H/3 = 6/3 = 2.0$ m above base (lever arm for moment about toe = 2.0 m)
- $U$ (uplift, triangular, peak at heel): centroid at $B/3 = 6/3 = 2.0$ m from heel; distance from toe $= B - 2.0 = 4.0$ m

**Step 5 — Restoring moment about the toe:**
$$M_R = W_1 \times 5.25 + W_2 \times 3.0$$
$$M_R = 201.6 \times 5.25 + 302.4 \times 3.0 = 1058.4 + 907.2 = 1965.6 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 6 — Overturning moment about the toe:**
$$M_O = P \times 2.0 + U \times 4.0$$
$$M_O = 176.58 \times 2.0 + 176.58 \times 4.0 = 353.16 + 706.32 = 1059.48 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 7 — Factor of safety against overturning:**
$$\text{FOS}_O = \frac{M_R}{M_O} = \frac{1965.6}{1059.48} = 1.855$$

Since $\text{FOS}_O > 1.5$, the dam is **safe against overturning**. ✓

**Step 8 — Net vertical force at the base:**
$$V = W - U = 504.0 - 176.58 = 327.42 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 9 — Position of resultant from the toe:**
$$\bar{x} = \frac{M_R - M_O}{V} = \frac{1965.6 - 1059.48}{327.42} = \frac{906.12}{327.42} = 2.768 \text{ m}$$

**Step 10 — Eccentricity from the centre of base:**
$$e = \frac{B}{2} - \bar{x} = \frac{6.0}{2} - 2.768 = 3.0 - 2.768 = 0.232 \text{ m}$$

Check middle third rule: $B/6 = 6/6 = 1.0$ m. Since $e = 0.232 \leq 1.0$, **no tension at base**. ✓

**Step 11 — Maximum and minimum stresses at base:**
$$p_{\max} = \frac{V}{B}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{B}\right) = \frac{327.42}{6.0}\left(1 + \frac{6 \times 0.232}{6.0}\right)$$
$$p_{\max} = 54.57 \times (1 + 0.232) = 54.57 \times 1.232 = 67.23 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$p_{\min} = \frac{V}{B}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{B}\right) = 54.57 \times (1 - 0.232) = 54.57 \times 0.768 = 41.91 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Both are **compressive**; $p_{\min} = 41.91$ kN/m² is well below the safe strength of masonry (~800 kN/m²). ✓

**Step 12 — Factor of safety against sliding:**
$$\text{FOS}_S = \frac{\mu \cdot V}{P} = \frac{0.6 \times 327.42}{176.58} = \frac{196.45}{176.58} = 1.113$$

Since $\text{FOS}_S > 1.0$, the dam is **safe against sliding**. ✓

**Step 13 — Final Verdict:**
All four conditions are satisfied. **The dam is SAFE.**

| Condition | Computed | Required | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| FOS Overturning | 1.855 | $\geq 1.5$ | ✓ Safe |
| FOS Sliding | 1.113 | $\geq 1.0$ | ✓ Safe |
| Eccentricity | 0.232 m | $\leq 1.0$ m | ✓ No tension |
| Max base stress | 67.23 kN/m² | $\leq 800$ kN/m² | ✓ Safe |

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the term "uplift pressure" as applied to a masonry dam. Sketch the standard pressure diagrams used for (i) full triangular uplift and (ii) reduced rectangular uplift. State the assumption regarding the intensity of uplift at the heel and toe in each case. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A masonry gravity dam has a triangular cross-section with top width **2.0 m** and height **8.0 m**, with a vertical upstream face and a downstream batter. The coefficient of friction between dam and foundation is **0.7**. Take $\gamma_m = 23.0$ kN/m³ and $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. Assume **no uplift** acts. Determine:
- (i) The base width required so that the resultant passes through the **middle third** limit (i.e. eccentricity $= B/6$).
- (ii) The factor of safety against sliding with this base width.
- (iii) The factor of safety against overturning. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a): Uplift Pressure — Theory**

**Definition:** Uplift pressure is the upward water pressure that acts on the base of the dam due to the head of water seeping under the foundation and through the porous concrete/masonry. It reduces the effective weight of the dam and also produces an overturning effect about the toe.

**Standard Pressure Diagrams:**

- **Case (i) — Full Triangular Uplift:** Pressure is zero at the toe and maximum hydrostatic pressure $\gamma_w H$ at the heel. The distribution forms a **right triangle** with its right angle at the toe and maximum ordinate at the heel.
- **Case (ii) — Reduced Rectangular Uplift (or Trapezoidal):** A uniform pressure of magnitude $\frac{1}{3}\gamma_w H$ (per IS 6512) acts across the entire base, assuming drainage arrangements. The diagram is a **rectangle** of height $\frac{1}{3}\gamma_w H$ over the full base width $B$.

**Assumptions at the boundaries:**

| Case | Pressure at Heel | Pressure at Toe |
|---|---|---|
| Full Triangular | $\gamma_w H$ (full hydrostatic) | $0$ |
| Reduced Rectangular | $\frac{1}{3}\gamma_w H$ (uniform) | $\frac{1}{3}\gamma_w H$ (uniform) |

---

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Define geometry:**
Top width $a = 2.0$ m, height $H = 8.0$ m. Let the base width be $B$ (unknown). Upstream face vertical; downstream face sloped.

**Step 2 — Self-weight components:**
- Rectangle (top): $W_1 = a \cdot H \cdot \gamma_m = 2.0 \times 8.0 \times 23.0 = 368.0$ kN
- Triangle: base $= (B - 2.0)$, height $= 8.0$
$$W_2 = \frac{1}{2}(B - 2.0) \times 8.0 \times 23.0 = 92.0(B - 2.0) \text{ kN}$$

- Total weight:
$$W = 368.0 + 92.0(B - 2.0) = 368.0 + 92B - 184.0 = 184.0 + 92.0B \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Lever arms from the toe:**
- $W_1$ centroid at $a/2 = 1.0$ m from heel $\Rightarrow$ distance from toe $= B - 1.0$
- $W_2$ centroid at $\frac{1}{3}(B - 2.0)$ from heel $\Rightarrow$ distance from toe $= 2.0 + \frac{1}{3}(B - 2.0) = 2.0 + \frac{B-2.0}{3} = \frac{B + 4.0}{3}$

**Step 4 — Horizontal water pressure:**
$$P = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 8.0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.81 \times 64.0 = 313.92 \text{ kN}$$

Lever arm from toe: $H/3 = 8/3 = 2.667$ m

**Step 5 — Restoring moment (no uplift):**
$$M_R = W_1 \times (B - 1.0) + W_2 \times \frac{B + 4.0}{3}$$
$$M_R = 368.0(B - 1.0) + 92.0(B - 2.0) \times \frac{B + 4.0}{3}$$

**Step 6 — Overturning moment:**
$$M_O = P \times \frac{H}{3} = 313.92 \times 2.667 = 837.12 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the condition that the resultant lies at the middle third limit (eccentricity $e = B/6$):**

The distance of the resultant from the toe $\bar{x}$ at the middle third limit is:
$$\bar{x} = \frac{B}{2} - \frac{B}{6} = \frac{B}{3}$$

This means the resultant must pass through a point at $B/3$ from the toe. Equivalently, the **moment of the vertical forces about the toe must equal the moment of the horizontal force about the toe**, with the vertical force acting at $B/3$:
$$W \times \frac{B}{3} = P \times \frac{H}{3}$$
$$W \times B = P \times H$$

**Step 8 — Substitute and solve for $B$:**
$$(184.0 + 92.0B) \times B = 313.92 \times 8.0$$
$$184.0B + 92.0B^2 = 2511.36$$
$$92.0B^2 + 184.0B - 2511.36 = 0$$

Divide throughout by 92:
$$B^2 + 2.0B - 27.297 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:
$$B = \frac{-2.0 + \sqrt{4.0 + 4 \times 27.297}}{2} = \frac{-2.0 + \sqrt{113.188}}{2} = \frac{-2.0 + 10.640}{2} = 4.320 \text{ m}$$

**Required base width $B \approx 4.32$ m.**

**Step 9 — Factor of safety against sliding:**
- Total weight: $W = 184.0 + 92.0 \times 4.32 = 184.0 + 397.44 = 581.44$ kN
- Since no uplift: $V = W = 581.44$ kN
$$\text{FOS}_S = \frac{\mu V}{P} = \frac{0.7 \times 581.44}{313.92} = \frac{407.01}{313.92} = 1.297$$

Since $\text{FOS}_S > 1.0$, the dam is **safe against sliding**. ✓

**Step 10 — Factor of safety against overturning:**
Compute $M_R$ with $B = 4.32$ m:
$$M_R = 368.0(4.32 - 1.0) + 92.0(4.32 - 2.0) \times \frac{4.32 + 4.0}{3}$$
$$M_R = 368.0 \times 3.32 + 92.0 \times 2.32 \times \frac{8.32}{3}$$
$$M_R = 1221.76 + 213.44 \times 2.773 = 1221.76 + 591.94 = 1813.70 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$\text{FOS}_O = \frac{M_R}{M_O} = \frac{1813.70}{837.12} = 2.166$$

Since $\text{FOS}_O > 1.5$, the dam is **safe against overturning**. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the uplift lever arm:** Many students take uplift's lever arm as $B/2$ always, but for the **full triangular** case it acts at $B/3$ from heel (or $\mathbf{2B/3}$ from toe). Confusing these leads to wrong overturning moments and a flawed stability check.
> 2. **Sign of eccentricity:** If the resultant is at the centre of the base, $e = 0$ and the stress is uniform. A **positive $e$** (resultant shifted towards heel) gives $p_{\max}$ at the heel; do not interchange.
> 3. **Using the wrong arm for triangular weight:** The centroid of a triangle (dam batter) is at **one-third** of its base from the wider end (heel), not the centre. This single mistake causes a cascading error in $M_R$.
> 4. **Forgetting to check the middle third rule:** Even if FOS are okay, the dam fails the "no tension" criterion if $e > B/6$. Always check all four conditions, not just two.
> 5. **Units of $\gamma_w$:** KTU sometimes uses $\gamma_w = 10$ kN/m³ (not 9.81). Read the question carefully and follow the value given; do not mix.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM**

- [ ] **Gravity dam stability** = Self-weight vs. horizontal water thrust + uplift.
- [ ] **Four checks:** No overturning, no sliding, no tension, no crushing.
- [ ] **Horizontal water pressure:** $P = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H^2$ at $H/3$ from base. **Always overturning.**
- [ ] **Uplift (full triangular):** $U = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_w H B$ at $B/3$ from heel ($2B/3$ from toe). **Always destabilizing — reduces $V$ and increases $M_O$.**
- [ ] **Uplift (reduced rectangular):** $U = \frac{1}{3}\gamma_w H B$ at $B/2$ from heel.
- [ ] **Silt pressure:** $P_s = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_s h_s^2$ with $\gamma_s = \frac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi}\gamma_w$ at $h_s/3$ from base.
- [ ] **Middle third rule:** $e \leq B/6 \Rightarrow p_{\min} \geq 0$ (no tension).
- [ ] **Base pressure formula:** $p_{\max}, p_{\min} = \dfrac{V}{B}\left(1 \pm \dfrac{6e}{B}\right)$.
- [ ] **Eccentricity:** $e = \dfrac{B}{2} - \bar{x}$, where $\bar{x} = \dfrac{M_R - M_O}{V_{\text{net}}}$.
- [ ] **FOS Overturning:** $\Sigma M_R / \Sigma M_O \geq 1.5$ (KTU board preference).
- [ ] **FOS Sliding:** $\mu \cdot V / \Sigma H \geq 1.0$ (some books require $\geq 1.5$).
- [ ] **Standard constants to remember:** $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (or 10), $\gamma_m = 22\text{–}24$ kN/m³, $\mu = 0.6$ to 0.75.
- [ ] **Worked example for quick memory:** A dam with $H = 6$ m, $B = 6$ m typically gives FOS$_{sliding} \approx 1.1$ to $1.3$ and FOS$_{overturning} \approx 1.8$ to $2.2$ under full uplift and $\mu = 0.6$ — these are the typical **order-of-magnitude** values for a moderately proportioned gravity dam.
- [ ] **Convention:** All moments about the **toe**. All forces per **metre run** of dam length.
- [ ] **Free body of dam = vertical slice of 1 m length** perpendicular to the dam axis.
- [ ] **Why middle third?** Masonry is weak in tension; a tension crack at the heel would propagate water seepage → increased uplift → progressive failure (catastrophic).
- [ ] **Top width** is provided for roadway + freeboard (usually 1.5 m minimum, 5–6 m for major dams).
- [ ] **Practical downstream batter** = 0.7H : 1V to 0.8H : 1V (0.7 to 0.8 horizontal per 1 vertical).
- [ ] **Drainage galleries** in real dams reduce uplift from "full triangular" to "reduced rectangular" by intercepting seepage water.
