- [ ] **Most common KTU error in exam:** Taking the **centroid of the triangle** at its midpoint instead of at one-third. Always remember: the centroid of a triangle is at $b/3$ from the base (wider side) and $2b/3$ from the apex.
- [ ] **Final sanity check:** If the resultant is too close to the heel, $p_{\max}$ is large (risk of crushing). If too close to the toe, $p_{\min}$ may become negative (risk of tension). **Aim for $e$ close to $B/6$ for material-efficient design.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Columns and Struts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Columns and Struts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Column or Strut?

A **column** is a vertical or nearly vertical compression member of a structure which is subjected to axial compressive loads and transmits them to the foundation. When a compression member is **inclined or horizontal**, it is called a **strut**. Similarly, a compression member in a truss is also referred to as a **strut** (or tie-rod in a few contexts where it carries small compressive forces).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAGT302 Module 4):**
> A *column* is a straight slender member of uniform cross-section subjected to axial compressive loads whose ends are free to rotate (pin-ended). A *strut* is a similar compression member but may be inclined or part of a frame/truss. The primary failure mode of a long column is **buckling**, not crushing.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Young's Modulus ($E$)** for structural steel: $2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$ or **200 GPa**
- **Standard Effective Length Factors (KL/r)**: 1.0, 0.8, 0.65, 2.0 (based on end conditions)
- **Limiting Slenderness Ratio** for steel: 180 (IS 800:2007), 350 (per Euler validity)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are columns a separate topic?** A short stubby column fails by **crushing** (material yielding), but a long slender column fails suddenly by **buckling** (lateral deflection instability) at loads much lower than its crushing load. This means Strength of Materials must treat columns with special theories.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Ruler on the Table"**
> Take a flat plastic ruler and hold it vertically on a table. Press down slowly on its top. A *short ruler* will simply compress and squash at a high load. A *long ruler* will, at a much smaller load, suddenly snap sideways with a sharp *thwack* — it **buckles**. The load at which it buckles depends only on how *slender* it is, not on the strength of the material! A *paper column* rolled into a long thin tube can support more load than the same paper lying flat, because the *shape* (slenderness) governs stability, not just material strength.

This is the heart of column theory: **Geometry + Boundary Conditions + Stiffness** dictate failure load, not just material strength.

## 1.3 Classification of Columns

Columns are classified by their **slenderness ratio** $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k}$, where $k$ is the least radius of gyration.

| Type | Slenderness Ratio | Failure Mode |
|------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Short Column** | Low ($\lambda < 30$–$50$ for steel) | Pure crushing / yielding |
| **Medium Column** | Intermediate | Combination of crushing + buckling |
| **Long Column** | High ($\lambda > $ limiting value) | Pure elastic buckling (Euler) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember end-condition lengths:** *"Pin-Pin, Fix-Fix, Fix-Pin, Fix-Free — 1, 0.5, 0.7, 2"* (Le/L ratios)
> Actual theoretical values: 1, 0.5, 0.7 (approx 0.6992), 2.0

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Euler's Theory of Long Columns — Assumptions

Leonhard Euler (1757) developed the classical buckling theory. The following assumptions are made:

1. The column is **initially perfectly straight** and the load is truly **axial (concentric)**.
2. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law** (linearly elastic).
3. The cross-section is **uniform** throughout the length.
4. **Self-weight** of the column is neglected.
5. The column fails only by **buckling** (lateral deflection), and the failure occurs in the **elastic range**.
6. **Shortening due to direct compression** is neglected.
7. The end conditions are **ideal** (perfect pin, perfect fixity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Critical Load Equation (General Form):**
> The critical load at which buckling occurs is given by:
> $$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$
> This is the **most-asked formula in KTU exams** — memorize it perfectly.

## 2.2 Effective Length Concept

For columns with different end conditions, we can still use Euler's formula by replacing the actual length $L$ with an **effective length** $L_{eff} = \frac{L}{\sqrt{n}}$ or in terms of a length factor, $L_{eff} = K \cdot L$, where $K = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$.

The differential equation governing the deflected shape is:
$$EI \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -P \cdot y$$

The constant $n$ comes from the boundary conditions, and the **smallest buckling load** is given by:
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{n \pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important** reference for KTU Module 4 numerical problems.

### Table 2.3.1: Euler's Critical Load for Various End Conditions

| End Condition | $n$ | $L_{eff} = \frac{L}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Equivalent Length Factor $K$ | Shape of Buckled Column |
|---------------|-----|-------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Both ends **hinged** (pin-pin) | 1 | $L$ | 1.0 | Simple half sine wave |
| Both ends **fixed** (fixed-fixed) | 4 | $\frac{L}{2}$ | 0.5 | Three half waves (column bends in opposite curvatures) |
| One end **fixed**, other end **hinged** | $\approx 2.046$ | $\approx 0.7L$ ($\frac{L}{\sqrt{2.046}}$) | $\approx 0.699$ | Half wave plus quarter wave |
| One end **fixed**, other end **free** (cantilever column) | 0.25 | $2L$ | 2.0 | Quarter sine wave |

**Critical Stress (Euler's):**
$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{(L_{eff}/k)^{2}}$$

where $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ is the **least radius of gyration** and $\lambda = L_{eff}/k$ is the **slenderness ratio**.

## 2.4 Limitations of Euler's Formula

> [!WARNING]
> Euler's formula is valid **only for long columns** (elastic buckling). For short and medium columns, it predicts unrealistically high critical stresses (above yield stress). The slenderness ratio beyond which Euler is valid is given by equating $\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y$ (yield stress):
> $$\lambda_{limiting} = \pi \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}}$$

For mild steel ($E = 200$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa): $\lambda_{limiting} \approx 79$.

## 2.5 Rankine's Formula (Most Important Empirical Formula for KTU)

Rankine proposed a single empirical relation that works for **all slenderness ratios** (short, medium, long):

$$P_{R} = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_{eff}}{k}\right)^{2}}$$

where:
- $P_R$ = Rankine critical load
- $\sigma_c$ = **ultimate crushing stress** of the material in a very short column
- $a$ = **Rankine's constant** (depends on material; for mild steel, $a = \frac{1}{7500}$, for cast iron, $a = \frac{1}{1600}$)
- $L_{eff}$ = effective length
- $k$ = least radius of gyration
- $A$ = cross-sectional area

**Equivalent Critical Stress (Rankine):**
$$\sigma_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{1 + a \lambda^{2}}$$

## 2.6 Johnson's Formula (for Intermediate Columns)

For columns of medium slenderness (where Euler overpredicts), Johnson's parabolic formula is often used:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \sigma_y - \left( \dfrac{\sigma_y - \sigma_p}{2 \lambda_1^{2}} \right) \lambda^{2}$$

where $\sigma_p$ is the proportional limit and $\lambda_1$ is the slenderness ratio at the elastic-plastic transition.

## 2.7 IS Code Formula (IS 800:2007 — For Steel)

The Indian Standard uses a similar approach to Rankine, and the design compressive stress $f_{cd}$ is given by:

$$f_{cd} = \dfrac{f_y / \gamma_{m1}}{\phi + \sqrt{\phi^{2} - \lambda_{0}^{2}}}$$

where $\phi = 0.5 \left[ 1 + \alpha(\lambda - 0.2) + \lambda^{2} \right]$, $\lambda$ is the non-dimensional slenderness ratio, $\alpha$ is the imperfection factor, and $\gamma_{m1}$ is the partial safety factor (1.1).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Column theory is the foundation of designing:
> - Tall building columns (subjected to heavy axial load + buckling)
> - Bridge piers
> - Hydraulic press frames
> - Crane jibs (struts)
> - Truss members (top chord, verticals, diagonals)
> - Aircraft fuselage stringers

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Critical Load (Pin-Pin Column)

### Step 1: Setup the Physical Model

Consider a column $AB$ of length $L$, with both ends pinned. Apply an axial compressive load $P$ at the centroidal axis. When $P$ reaches a critical value $P_{cr}$, the column deflects laterally and assumes a slightly curved shape. Let $y$ be the lateral deflection at a distance $x$ from the bottom end $A$.

### Step 2: Bending Moment at Section X

At any section $XX$ at distance $x$ from $A$, the bending moment due to the deflected column is:

$$M = -P \cdot y$$

The negative sign indicates that the moment causes **concave curvature** opposite to deflection.

### Step 3: Euler-Bernoulli Bending Equation

From the theory of simple bending (flexure formula), we have:

$$EI \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M$$

Substituting $M = -Py$:

$$EI \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -P \cdot y$$

### Step 4: Rearrange into Standard Form

Rearranging the equation:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + \left( \dfrac{P}{EI} \right) y = 0$$

This is a **second-order linear homogeneous differential equation** with constant coefficients.

### Step 5: Solve the Differential Equation

Let $\alpha^{2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. The equation becomes:

$$\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + \alpha^{2} y = 0$$

The general solution is:

$$y = A \cos(\alpha x) + B \sin(\alpha x)$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants of integration.

### Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions

**Boundary Condition 1:** At $x = 0$ (point $A$, pin), deflection $y = 0$:

$$0 = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) \implies A = 0$$

So the equation reduces to $y = B \sin(\alpha x)$.

**Boundary Condition 2:** At $x = L$ (point $B$, pin), deflection $y = 0$:

$$0 = B \sin(\alpha L)$$

For a **non-trivial solution** (column is buckling, not remaining straight), $B \neq 0$, so:

$$\sin(\alpha L) = 0 \implies \alpha L = n \pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

### Step 7: Determine the Smallest Critical Load

The smallest buckling load corresponds to $n = 1$ (lowest mode of buckling, most critical):

$$\alpha = \dfrac{\pi}{L} \implies \alpha^{2} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}}{L^{2}}$$

Since $\alpha^{2} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{EI}$:

$$\dfrac{P_{cr}}{EI} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}}{L^{2}} \implies \boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}}$$

This is Euler's celebrated formula. **Derivation complete.**

## 3.2 Derivation for Both Ends Fixed ($L_{eff} = L/2$)

For a column fixed at both ends, the boundary conditions are:

- $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$
- $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$ (no rotation at fixed ends)

Substituting $y = A\cos(\alpha x) + B\sin(\alpha x)$ and its derivative $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -A\alpha\sin(\alpha x) + B\alpha\cos(\alpha x)$ into these:

At $x = 0$: $A = 0$ and $B\alpha = 0$ (combined with $A = 0$, but $B$ can be non-zero from second condition if $\alpha = 0$; we need the determinant approach)

Using the standard result for a fixed-fixed column, the critical load equation is:

$$\sin(\alpha L) \cdot \alpha L \cdot \cos(\alpha L) = \text{(yielding)} \quad \alpha L = 2\pi$$

Hence:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{4\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{(L/2)^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$

with $L_{eff} = L/2$. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation for One End Fixed, Other Free (Cantilever Column, $L_{eff} = 2L$)

Boundary conditions:
- $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ (fixed end is at origin)
- $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$ (no rotation at fixed end)
- $EI \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = 0$ at $x = L$ (free end has no moment)

Applying these to $y = A\cos(\alpha x) + B\sin(\alpha x)$:

$$\alpha L = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{4 L^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{(2L)^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$

with $L_{eff} = 2L$. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation of Rankine's Formula (Conceptual Logic)

Rankine's formula interpolates between the **crushing stress** (for zero slenderness) and **Euler's buckling stress** (for high slenderness). The form is:

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{1}{P_E}$$

where $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A$ is the crushing load and $P_E = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$ is the Euler load. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_c A} + \dfrac{L_{eff}^{2}}{\pi^{2} E I}$$

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_c A}\left[ 1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c A L_{eff}^{2}}{\pi^{2} E I} \right]$$

Since $I = A k^{2}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_c A}\left[ 1 + \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^{2} E} \cdot \left( \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} \right)^{2} \right]$$

Define $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^{2} E}$ (Rankine's empirical constant). Then:

$$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a \left( \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} \right)^{2}} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Check:** When $\lambda \to 0$ (very short column), $P_R \to \sigma_c A = P_c$ (crushing). When $\lambda \to \infty$ (very long column), $P_R \to \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}} = P_E$ (Euler). Rankine's formula is therefore a **blended formula** valid across all slenderness ratios.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Condition | Use This Formula | Reason |
|-----------|------------------|--------|
| $\lambda \geq \lambda_{limiting}$ (long, elastic) | **Euler's** | Elastic buckling governs |
| All ranges, empirical quick answer | **Rankine's** | Continuous validity |
| Medium column, parabolic fit | **Johnson's** | Empirical fit in plastic range |
| Design per Indian code | **IS 800 column curve** | Code-compliant design |
| Very short column | **Crushing check** $\sigma_c A$ | Buckling not relevant |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Column Buckling Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **engineering decision flow** when designing/analyzing a column. It shows the path from loading to final answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Column Geometry and Material] --> B[Calculate Slenderness Ratio lambda = Leff divided by k]
    B --> C{Is lambda less than lambda_limiting?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Short or Medium Column]
    C -- No --> E[Long Column - Elastic Buckling]
    D --> F[Apply Rankine Formula]
    E --> G[Apply Euler Formula]
    F --> H[Compute Critical Load Pcr]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Critical Stress sigma_cr = Pcr divided by A]
    I --> J[Compare with Allowable Stress for Design Check]
    J --> K[Output: Safe Factor of Safety or Required Cross Section]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagram summarizes the **decision matrix** for a column analysis. The key branching point is comparing the slenderness ratio with the limiting value.

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Effective Length for Four End Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pin Pin Both Ends] --> A2[Leff equals L]
    A3[Fixed Fixed Both Ends] --> A4[Leff equals L divided by 2]
    A5[Fixed Pin One End Hinged Other] --> A6[Leff equals 0.7 times L approximately]
    A7[Fixed Free Cantilever] --> A8[Leff equals 2 times L]
    A2 --> B1[Pcr = pi squared E I divided by L squared]
    A4 --> B2[Pcr = 4 pi squared E I divided by L squared]
    A6 --> B3[Pcr = 2.046 pi squared E I divided by L squared]
    A8 --> B4[Pcr = pi squared E I divided by 4 L squared]
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice how all four cases can be unified into the single expression $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$ — this is why the **effective length concept is so powerful**.

## 4.3 Buckled Shape Visualization (ASCII Schematic)

```
   PIN-PIN:        FIXED-FIXED:        FIXED-PIN:        FIXED-FREE:
    
       . .                | |              | |                  . .
      .   .               | |              | |                 .   .
     .     .              |\ /|             |\ /|              .     .
    .       .             | X |              |X |             .       .
   .         .            |/ \|              | \|            .         .
   ============           ====              ====            .===========
   |    L    |            | L |             | L |           .    |  2L  |
```

(For pin-pin: a single half-sine wave develops; for fixed-fixed: a point of contraflexure forms; for fixed-pin: an asymmetric curve; for fixed-free: maximum deflection at the free end.)

> [!NOTE]
> The fixed-free column has the **largest effective length ($2L$)**, making it the **weakest end condition**. This is why cantilever columns are rarely used in practice for heavy loads.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the terms (i) Slenderness ratio, (ii) Effective length of a column, and (iii) Buckling load.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$):** It is the ratio of the effective length of a column to the least radius of gyration of its cross-section.
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k_{min}}$$

(ii) **Effective Length ($L_{eff}$):** It is the length of an equivalent pin-ended column which has the same buckling load as the given column with its actual end conditions. It is calculated as $L_{eff} = K \cdot L$, where $K$ is the effective length factor.

(iii) **Buckling Load (Critical Load):** It is the axial compressive load at which a slender column suddenly fails by lateral deflection (bending), even though the compressive stress may be well below the material's yield strength. Mathematically: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the Euler's critical load formulas for the four standard end conditions. Why does the fixed-free column have the lowest buckling load?**

**Model Answer:**

| End Condition | Critical Load |
|---------------|---------------|
| Both ends hinged | $\dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$ |
| Both ends fixed | $\dfrac{4\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$ |
| One end fixed, other hinged | $\dfrac{2.046 \pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}}$ |
| One end fixed, other free | $\dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{4L^{2}}$ |

**Reason:** A fixed-free column has the largest effective length ($L_{eff} = 2L$), and since $P_{cr} \propto 1/L_{eff}^{2}$, a larger effective length gives a smaller critical load.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Any ONE)

### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - 14 Marks]`

**(a)** A built-up column consists of two ISMB 300 sections placed back-to-back with their webs 200 mm apart. Determine the Euler's critical load for the column if its length is 6 m and both ends are hinged. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². (7 marks)

**(b)** Using Rankine's formula, calculate the safe load for a hollow circular cast iron column of external diameter 200 mm and internal diameter 150 mm, with a length of 4.5 m. Both ends are fixed. Take $\sigma_c = 550$ N/mm², $a = 1/1600$, and a factor of safety of 5. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1:** Properties of ISMB 300 (standard Indian Standard Medium Weight Beam):
- Area of one section: $A_1 = 4761$ mm²
- Moment of inertia about strong axis (XX): $I_{xx} = 8506 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴
- Moment of inertia about weak axis (YY): $I_{yy} = 440.1 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴

**Step 2:** For the built-up section placed back-to-back, the distance between the two webs is 200 mm, so the gap between the centroids of the two sections is 200 mm.

**Step 3:** Use parallel axis theorem. Buckling occurs about the axis with **minimum moment of inertia**, so we calculate $I_{yy}$ for the combined section:

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \left[ I_{yy,section} + A_1 \cdot d^{2} \right]$$

where $d = 100$ mm (half the gap).

$$I_{yy,total} = 2 \left[ 440.1 \times 10^{4} + 4761 \times (100)^{2} \right]$$

$$= 2 \left[ 440.1 \times 10^{4} + 4761 \times 10^{4} \right]$$

$$= 2 \times 5201.1 \times 10^{4}$$

$$= 10402.2 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 4:** Compare with $I_{xx,total} = 2 \times 8506 \times 10^{4} = 17012 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴. Since $I_{yy,total} < I_{xx,total}$, use $I_{yy,total} = 10402.2 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴.

**Step 5:** Apply Euler's formula (both ends hinged, $L_{eff} = L = 6$ m = 6000 mm):

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 10402.2 \times 10^{4}}{(6000)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 10402.2 \times 10^{4}}{36 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10402.2 \times 10^{9}}{36 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = 5706.5 \text{ kN} \approx 5707 \text{ kN}}$$

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1:** Calculate area of hollow circular section:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left[ D^{2} - d^{2} \right] = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left[ 200^{2} - 150^{2} \right]$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left[ 40000 - 22500 \right] = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 17500$$

$$A = 13744.47 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate moment of inertia:
$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left[ D^{4} - d^{4} \right] = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left[ 200^{4} - 150^{4} \right]$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \left[ 1.6 \times 10^{9} - 5.0625 \times 10^{8} \right]$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi}{64} \times 1.09375 \times 10^{9}$$

$$I = 5.3666 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3:** For a circular section, $I = A k^{2}$, so:
$$k^{2} = \dfrac{I}{A} = \dfrac{5.3666 \times 10^{7}}{13744.47} = 3904.69 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$k = 62.49 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Both ends fixed → $L_{eff} = L/2 = 4500/2 = 2250$ mm.

**Step 5:** Slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} = \dfrac{2250}{62.49} = 36.0$$

**Step 6:** Apply Rankine's formula:
$$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \lambda^{2}}$$

$$P_R = \dfrac{550 \times 13744.47}{1 + \dfrac{1}{1600} \times (36.0)^{2}}$$

$$P_R = \dfrac{7559458.5}{1 + \dfrac{1296}{1600}} = \dfrac{7559458.5}{1 + 0.81} = \dfrac{7559458.5}{1.81}$$

$$P_R = 4176490.6 \text{ N} = 4176.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7:** Apply factor of safety = 5:
$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_R}{5} = \dfrac{4176.5}{5} = \boxed{835.3 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - 14 Marks]`

**(a)** A 2 m long steel column of circular cross-section (diameter 80 mm) is fixed at one end and free at the other. Determine the Euler's critical load and the corresponding critical stress. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². (7 marks)

**(b)** A timber column of square cross-section 100 mm × 100 mm and length 3.5 m is used as a strut. Both ends are pinned. Using Rankine's formula, find the safe load with a factor of safety of 4. Take $\sigma_c = 50$ N/mm², $a = 1/3000$, and $E = 1.2 \times 10^{4}$ N/mm². (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a):

**Step 1:** For fixed-free column: $L_{eff} = 2L = 2 \times 2000 = 4000$ mm.

**Step 2:** Area and moment of inertia of circular section ($D = 80$ mm):

$$A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 80^{2}}{4} = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 80^{4}}{64} = 2.0106 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Euler's formula:
$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 2.0106 \times 10^{6}}{(4000)^{2}}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 2.0106 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$P_{cr} = 9.8696 \times 2 \times 2.0106 \times 10^{5} = 39.69 \times 10^{5}$$

$$P_{cr} = 3.969 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} \approx \boxed{3969 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4:** Critical stress:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{P_{cr}}{A} = \dfrac{3.969 \times 10^{6}}{5026.55} = \boxed{789.62 \text{ N/mm}^{2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use $L = 2$ m directly for the fixed-free case and forget to double the length. Always remember: **fixed-free has $L_{eff} = 2L$**, the *largest* effective length of all four cases.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1:** Area and moment of inertia of square 100 mm × 100 mm:

$$A = 100 \times 100 = 10000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$I = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{100 \times 100^{3}}{12} = 8.3333 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2:** For square, $k^{2} = I/A = 8.3333 \times 10^{6} / 10000 = 833.33$ mm². So $k = 28.87$ mm.

**Step 3:** Both ends pinned: $L_{eff} = L = 3500$ mm.

**Step 4:** Slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{k} = \dfrac{3500}{28.87} = 121.24$$

**Step 5:** Apply Rankine's formula:
$$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \lambda^{2}} = \dfrac{50 \times 10000}{1 + \dfrac{1}{3000} \times (121.24)^{2}}$$

$$= \dfrac{500000}{1 + \dfrac{14699.13}{3000}} = \dfrac{500000}{1 + 4.8997} = \dfrac{500000}{5.8997}$$

$$P_R = 84750.8 \text{ N} = 84.75 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6:** Apply factor of safety = 4:
$$P_{safe} = \dfrac{84.75}{4} = \boxed{21.19 \text{ kN}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Many students mix mm and m without converting. *All units must be consistent* — prefer N and mm throughout.
> 2. **Effective Length Confusion:** Don't write $L_{eff} = L$ for every case. The end conditions dictate $L_{eff}$ as $L$, $L/2$, $0.7L$, or $2L$.
> 3. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety:** If the question asks for *safe load*, divide by FoS. If it asks for *critical/breaking load*, do NOT divide.
> 4. **Radius of Gyration:** For non-symmetric sections, use the *least* $k$ (and corresponding $I_{min}$). For symmetric sections, $I$ and $k$ are the same about both principal axes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Column** = vertical compression member; **Strut** = inclined or horizontal compression member.
- **Buckling** = sudden lateral failure at a load much below the crushing load.
- **Euler's Formula** is valid for **long, slender, elastic columns only**:
  $$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$
- **Effective length factors ($K$)** to memorize:
  - Pin-Pin: $K = 1$, $L_{eff} = L$
  - Fixed-Fixed: $K = 0.5$, $L_{eff} = L/2$
  - Fixed-Pinned: $K \approx 0.7$, $L_{eff} \approx 0.7L$
  - Fixed-Free: $K = 2$, $L_{eff} = 2L$
- **Slenderness Ratio** $\lambda = L_{eff}/k$ governs the failure mode. High $\lambda$ → buckling; Low $\lambda$ → crushing.
- **Limiting slenderness** for mild steel $\approx 79$ to 80. Below this, Euler's formula is not applicable.
- **Rankine's Formula** is a **single unified formula** for all slenderness ratios:
  $$P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c \cdot A}{1 + a \lambda^{2}}$$
- **Rankine's constants ($a$)** to remember:
  - Mild Steel: $a = 1/7500$
  - Cast Iron: $a = 1/1600$
  - Timber: $a = 1/3000$
- **Critical Stress** = Euler stress = $\pi^{2} E / \lambda^{2}$.
- **For built-up columns**, use the parallel axis theorem to find $I$ about the buckling axis, then choose the **minimum** $I$.
- **In exams**, always:
  1. Draw a free body diagram.
  2. Identify the end conditions and write $L_{eff}$.
  3. Compute $I_{min}$, $A$, $k_{min}$, and $\lambda$.
  4. Apply the appropriate formula.
  5. Apply factor of safety if "safe load" is asked.
- The **fixed-free (cantilever) column** has the **lowest buckling strength** and is rarely used in practice for compressive load-bearing applications.
- **Euler's formula assumes elastic buckling** — it fails for medium/short columns where the stress exceeds the proportional limit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Euler's Column Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Euler's Column Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Euler's Column Theory**, formulated by the Swiss mathematician **Leonhard Euler** in 1757, is the classical analytical framework used to determine the **critical buckling load** (also called the *crippling load* or *Euler load*) at which a long, slender, initially straight column subjected to an axial compressive load becomes unstable and fails by sudden lateral deflection rather than by direct crushing of the material.

Mathematically, the Euler critical load is expressed as:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{L_{e}^{\,2}}$$

where:
- $P_{cr}$ = Crippling / critical buckling load (N)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of elasticity of the column material (N/mm²)
- $I$ = Second moment of area (Moment of inertia) about the axis of buckling (mm⁴)
- $L_e$ = **Effective length** of the column (mm), which depends on the end conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A column does **not** always fail by crushing. Long, slender columns fail by **buckling** (sudden sideways deflection). The compressive stress at failure is far below the yield stress of the material — this paradox is precisely what Euler's theory resolves.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine holding a thin plastic ruler vertically on your palm and pressing down gently on its top with your thumb. Long before the ruler **crushes**, it suddenly snaps sideways with a sharp *crack*. That sideways snap is **buckling**.

The same thing happens to a tall building column or a drill-string in mining: at a certain load — much smaller than the material's crushing strength — the column's *straight shape* becomes an unstable equilibrium, and any tiny disturbance causes it to bow out sideways catastrophically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Trigger:** A column does not need a *large* force to buckle. If it is **slender enough**, even a modest axial load causes failure. This is why hollow tubes (large $I$ for small $A$) are preferred over solid rods in compression members.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Effective Length ($L_e$)** — the length of an equivalent *pinned-pinned* column.
- **Radius of Gyration ($k$ or $r$)** — $\displaystyle k \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}$
- **Slenderness Ratio ($\lambda$)** — $\displaystyle \lambda \;=\; \frac{L_e}{k}$
- **Critical Buckling Stress ($\sigma_{cr}$)** — $\displaystyle \sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{P_{cr}}{A} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E}{\lambda^{2}}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Euler's Theory

For Euler's formula to be mathematically valid, the following idealisations must hold:

1. The column is **perfectly straight** initially and the load is **truly axial** (concentric).
2. The column material is **perfectly elastic, homogeneous, and isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E \varepsilon$).
3. The **self-weight** of the column is neglected compared to the applied load.
4. The **end loads** are applied along the centroidal axis only (no eccentricity).
5. Failure occurs **only by buckling**, not by yielding or crushing.
6. The **shortening** of the column due to direct compression is negligible.
7. The lateral deflection is **small** so that $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (small-angle approximation).

> [!WARNING]
> If any of the above assumptions break down (e.g., eccentric loading, inelastic material behaviour, short columns), Euler's formula **overestimates** the crippling load and a more realistic formula such as **Rankine's Formula** or **Johnson's Parabolic Formula** must be used.

### 2.2 Effective Lengths for Various End Conditions

The *effective length* $L_e$ accounts for the rotational/translational restraint at the column ends.

| Boundary Condition (Description) | Effective Length $L_e$ | Effective Length Coefficient |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Both ends **hinged** (pinned-pinned) | $L_e = L$ | $1.0$ |
| Both ends **fixed** | $L_e = L/2$ | $0.5$ |
| **One end fixed**, other end **free** (cantilever) | $L_e = 2L$ | $2.0$ |
| **One end fixed**, other end **hinged** | $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$ | $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Euler Critical Load | $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L_e^{2}$ | N | Crippling load for long slender columns |
| Critical Buckling Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = P_{cr}/A = \pi^{2} E / \lambda^{2}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | Maximum compressive stress before buckling |
| Radius of Gyration | $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ | mm | Cross-section stiffness indicator |
| Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = L_e / k$ | dimensionless | Long vs. short column classifier |
| Minimum Valid Slenderness | $\lambda_{min} = \pi\sqrt{E/\sigma_{c}}$ | dimensionless | Below this, Euler is invalid (crushing governs) |
| Deflection at mid-height (buckled shape) | $y_{max} = \delta \, (1 - \cos(\pi x/L))$ | mm | Lateral deflection profile |

### 2.4 Engineering Significance

Euler's theory underpins the design of every compression member in civil and mechanical engineering — from **building columns**, **bridge piers**, and **tower legs** to **drill pipes**, **propeller shafts**, and **rocket nose-cone struts**. The ratio $\lambda$ is the single most important dimensionless parameter that decides whether a column will fail by **elastic buckling** (Euler) or **crushing/yielding** (Rankine/Johnson).

For standard **mild steel** ($E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\sigma_c = 320$ N/mm²), the critical slenderness ratio is:

$$\lambda_{min} \;=\; \pi\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{320}} \;\approx\; 78.5$$

So **Euler's formula applies when $\lambda \ge 80$** for mild steel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Case 1: Both Ends Hinged (Pinned-Pinned Column) — Most Important Case

**Setup:** Consider a perfectly straight, slender column of length $L$, hinged at both ends. Apply an axial compressive load $P$ at the centroid. The column deflects laterally by $y$ at distance $x$ from the bottom support.

**Step 1 — Bending Moment at section XX:**

The deflected column has internal bending moment equal to the applied axial load times the lateral deflection:

$$M(x) \;=\; -P \cdot y$$

The negative sign indicates that the bending moment is *restoring* (opposes the deflection).

**Step 2 — Euler-Bernoulli Differential Equation:**

From the elastic flexure formula $EI \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M(x)$:

$$EI\,\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -P\,y$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging into Standard Form:**

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;+\; \frac{P}{EI}\,y \;=\; 0$$

Let $\displaystyle k^{2} \;=\; \frac{P}{EI}$, so the equation becomes $y'' + k^{2} y = 0$.

**Step 4 — General Solution:**

The general solution of this second-order linear ODE is:

$$y(x) \;=\; A\,\sin(kx) \;+\; B\,\cos(kx)$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**

- BC 1: At $x = 0$, $y = 0$ (pinned at bottom). Therefore $B = 0$.
- BC 2: At $x = L$, $y = 0$ (pinned at top). Therefore $A\sin(kL) = 0$.

**Step 6 — Non-Trivial Solution Condition:**

For a non-zero (buckled) shape, $A \neq 0$, which requires:

$$\sin(kL) \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad kL \;=\; n\pi, \quad n \;=\; 1,2,3,\dots$$

**Step 7 — Smallest (Critical) Buckling Load:**

The smallest critical load corresponds to $n = 1$:

$$kL \;=\; \pi \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k^{2} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}}{L^{2}}$$

Substituting $k^{2} = P/EI$:

$$\boxed{\;P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{L^{2}}\;}$$

This is the **Euler Crippling Load** for a column hinged at both ends. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Case 2: Both Ends Fixed — Quick Verification

With both ends fixed, the boundary conditions are $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ *and* $y'(0) = y'(L) = 0$. Solving yields $kL = 2\pi$, hence:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{(L/2)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{4\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{L^{2}}$$

Thus the effective length is $L_e = L/2$. The column carries **4×** the load of a pinned-pinned column of the same length.

---

### 3.3 Case 3: One End Fixed, Other End Free (Cantilever Column)

For a cantilever, $L_e = 2L$, hence:

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{(2L)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{4L^{2}}$$

A cantilever carries only **1/4** the load of a pinned-pinned column.

---

### 3.4 Case 4: One End Fixed, Other End Hinged

$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{(L/\sqrt{2})^{2}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{L^{2}}$$

So the effective length is $L_e = L/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707\,L$.

---

### 3.5 Critical Buckling Stress Derivation

Divide $P_{cr}$ by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{P_{cr}}{A} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2} E I}{A\,L_e^{2}}$$

But $I/A = k^{2}$ (the square of the radius of gyration), so:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E}{\lambda^{2}}\;}\quad\text{where}\quad \lambda \;=\; \frac{L_e}{k}$$

This shows that buckling stress is **inversely proportional to the square of the slenderness ratio**, which is why slender columns buckle easily while short, stocky ones crush.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: Numerical Application

**Problem:** A hollow circular cast-iron column has an external diameter of 100 mm and an internal diameter of 80 mm. The column is 4 m long, fixed at one end and hinged at the other. Find the crippling load using Euler's formula. Given: $E = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².

**Solution Step-by-Step:**

Step (a): Compute the cross-sectional area:
$$A \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}) \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,(100^{2} - 80^{2}) \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,(10000 - 6400) \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\,(3600)$$
$$A \;\approx\; 2827.43 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

Step (b): Compute the second moment of area:
$$I \;=\; \frac{\pi}{64}\,(D_o^{4} - D_i^{4}) \;=\; \frac{\pi}{64}\,(100^{4} - 80^{4})$$
$$100^{4} = 100{,}000{,}000 \quad\text{and}\quad 80^{4} = 40{,}960{,}000$$
$$I \;=\; \frac{\pi}{64}\,(59{,}040{,}000) \;\approx\; 2{,}898{,}094 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

Step (c): Determine effective length (one end fixed, one end hinged):
$$L_e \;=\; \frac{L}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{4000}{\sqrt{2}} \;\approx\; 2828.43 \text{ mm}$$

Step (d): Apply Euler's formula:
$$P_{cr} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,E\,I}{L_e^{2}} \;=\; \frac{(9.8696)\,(1.2 \times 10^{5})\,(2{,}898{,}094)}{(2828.43)^{2}}$$

Step (e): Numerator:
$$9.8696 \times 1.2 \times 10^{5} \;=\; 1{,}184{,}355.4$$
$$1{,}184{,}355.4 \times 2{,}898{,}094 \;\approx\; 3.432 \times 10^{12}$$

Step (f): Denominator:
$$(2828.43)^{2} \;=\; 8{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

Step (g): Final division:
$$P_{cr} \;\approx\; \frac{3.432 \times 10^{12}}{8 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; 429{,}000 \text{ N} \;=\; \mathbf{429 \text{ kN}}$$

Step (h): Verify Euler applicability. Compute slenderness ratio:
$$k \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2{,}898{,}094}{2827.43}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{1025.0} \;\approx\; 32.02 \text{ mm}$$
$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{L_e}{k} \;=\; \frac{2828.43}{32.02} \;\approx\; 88.3$$

Since $\lambda \approx 88 > 80$, **Euler's formula is valid** for this column. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Euler Buckling Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *analytical workflow* a KTU student must follow when solving an Euler column problem — from geometry input to final design verification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Column Geometry L, D, A, I] --> B{Determine End Conditions}
    B --> C1[Both Hinged: Le = L]
    B --> C2[Both Fixed: Le = L or 2]
    B --> C3[Fixed-Free: Le = 2L]
    B --> C4[Fixed-Hinged: Le = L or sqrt 2]
    C1 --> D[Compute Slenderness Ratio lambda = Le or k]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E{lambda greater than lambda_min?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Apply Euler Formula Pcr = pi squared E I over Le squared]
    E -- No --> G[Use Rankine or Johnson Formula instead]
    F --> H[Compute Crippling Stress sigma_cr = Pcr or A]
    H --> I[Compare Applied Load P with Pcr]
    I --> J{P less than Pcr or FS?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Column SAFE]
    J -- No --> L[Column BUCKLES - Redesign]
```

### 4.2 Buckled Shape vs. Effective Length (Schematic Description)

| End Condition | Buckled Shape | Effective Length $L_e$ |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Both Hinged | Single half sine-wave between supports | $1.0\,L$ |
| Both Fixed | Half sine-wave with zero-slope at ends | $0.5\,L$ |
| One Fixed, One Free | Quarter sine-wave from fixed end | $2.0\,L$ |
| One Fixed, One Hinged | Asymmetric half-wave | $0.707\,L$ |

### 4.3 Node Identifier Mapping (per Anti-Collision Safeguard)

- `A` = Geometry input node
- `B` = Decision node for end conditions
- `C1`, `C2`, `C3`, `C4` = Effective length calculation paths
- `D` = Slenderness ratio computation
- `E` = Validity decision node
- `F` = Euler formula application node
- `G` = Alternative formula (Rankine/Johnson) fallback
- `H`, `I`, `J` = Verification chain
- `K`, `L` = Final design verdict (safe / unsafe)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define *slenderness ratio*. Why is it a critical parameter in deciding the validity of Euler's column theory?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **slenderness ratio** is defined as the ratio of effective length of the column to the least radius of gyration of its cross-section:

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{L_e}{k} \;=\; \frac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}}$$

It is dimensionless. **Significance:** (i) It characterises whether the column is *long* (buckling failure) or *short* (crushing failure). (ii) Euler's theory is valid only for long columns where $\lambda \ge \lambda_{min} = \pi\sqrt{E/\sigma_c}$ — below this limit the material yields before buckling, invalidating Euler's elastic assumptions. (iii) Higher $\lambda$ → lower $\sigma_{cr}$ → column fails at a smaller load.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** State any **four** assumptions of Euler's column theory.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The column is initially perfectly **straight** and the load is applied **concentrically** along its axis.
2. The column material is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** throughout.
3. The **self-weight** of the column is negligible compared to the applied axial load.
4. **Shortening** due to direct compression is negligible; only bending deformations are considered.
5. Failure occurs solely by **buckling** — no yielding or crushing takes place before buckling.
6. Lateral deflections are **small** (small-angle approximation $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive Euler's formula for the crippling load of a column **hinged at both ends**. State clearly the assumptions and boundary conditions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel column of cross-section **60 mm × 80 mm** and length **4.5 m** is hinged at both ends. Compute the crippling load using Euler's formula. Take $E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm². Also calculate the slenderness ratio and comment on the validity of Euler's formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks):

1. **Setup & bending moment:** Consider a column AB hinged at both ends, loaded with axial force $P$. Let the deflected shape be $y(x)$. Bending moment at section XX: $M = -Py$.
2. **Differential equation:** From $EI\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = M$:
   $$\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + \frac{P}{EI}\,y = 0$$
3. **General solution:** With $k^{2} = P/EI$, the solution is $y = A\sin(kx) + B\cos(kx)$.
4. **Boundary conditions:**
   - $y(0) = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$
   - $y(L) = 0 \Rightarrow A\sin(kL) = 0$
5. **Non-trivial condition:** $\sin(kL) = 0 \Rightarrow kL = n\pi$. Smallest: $kL = \pi$.
6. **Final formula:**
   $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2}EI}{L^{2}}}$$

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks):

Step 1: Cross-sectional area:
$$A = 60 \times 80 = 4800 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

Step 2: Minimum I (buckling occurs about the weaker axis, i.e., the axis with smaller I):
$$I_{min} = \frac{80 \times 60^{3}}{12} = \frac{80 \times 216000}{12} = 1{,}440{,}000 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

Step 3: Effective length (both hinged): $L_e = L = 4500$ mm.

Step 4: Euler critical load:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 1{,}440{,}000}{(4500)^{2}} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 1{,}440{,}000}{20{,}250{,}000}$$
$$= \frac{2.9847 \times 10^{12}}{2.025 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.474 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{147.4 \text{ kN}}$$

Step 5: Radius of gyration:
$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{1{,}440{,}000}{4800}} = \sqrt{300} \approx 17.32 \text{ mm}$$

Step 6: Slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{k} = \frac{4500}{17.32} \approx 259.8$$

Step 7: Validity check: $\lambda = 259.8 \gg 80$, so **Euler's formula is fully valid**. The column is extremely slender and will buckle well before yielding.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive Euler's formula for the crippling load of a column **fixed at one end and free at the other**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hollow rectangular cast-iron column has external dimensions **120 mm × 90 mm** and internal dimensions **100 mm × 70 mm**. The column is **5 m** long with **one end fixed and the other end free**. Find the crippling load using Euler's formula. Take $E = 1.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks):

1. Consider a cantilever column fixed at A and free at B, length $L$.
2. Let the free end deflect laterally by $\delta$. At distance $x$ from the fixed end, bending moment: $M(x) = -P(\delta - y)$.
3. Differential equation: $EI\frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -P(\delta - y) \Rightarrow \frac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2}y = k^{2}\delta$ where $k^{2} = P/EI$.
4. General solution: $y = A\sin(kx) + B\cos(kx) + \delta$.
5. Boundary conditions:
   - $y(0) = 0 \Rightarrow B + \delta = 0 \Rightarrow B = -\delta$
   - $y'(0) = 0$ (fixed end) $\Rightarrow A k = 0 \Rightarrow A = 0$
   - $y(L) = \delta \Rightarrow -\delta\cos(kL) + \delta = \delta \Rightarrow \cos(kL) = 0$
6. Therefore $kL = \pi/2, 3\pi/2, \ldots$. Smallest: $kL = \pi/2$.
7. Since $k^{2} = P/EI$ and $k = \pi/(2L)$:
   $$\boxed{P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2}EI}{4L^{2}} = \frac{\pi^{2}EI}{(2L)^{2}}}$$

Thus effective length $L_e = 2L$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical (7 marks):

Step 1: External and internal dimensions:
$B_o = 120$ mm, $D_o = 90$ mm, $B_i = 100$ mm, $D_i = 70$ mm

Step 2: Area:
$$A = 120 \times 90 - 100 \times 70 = 10800 - 7000 = 3800 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

Step 3: Second moment of area (about minor axis — buckling will occur about the axis with lower $I$):
$$I_{min} = \frac{B_o D_o^{3} - B_i D_i^{3}}{12} = \frac{120 \times 90^{3} - 100 \times 70^{3}}{12}$$
$$= \frac{120 \times 729000 - 100 \times 343000}{12} = \frac{87{,}480{,}000 - 34{,}300{,}000}{12} = \frac{53{,}180{,}000}{12} \approx 4{,}431{,}667 \text{ mm}^{4}$$

Step 4: Effective length (fixed-free): $L_e = 2L = 2 \times 5000 = 10000$ mm.

Step 5: Euler critical load:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 1.0 \times 10^{5} \times 4{,}431{,}667}{(10000)^{2}} = \frac{9.8696 \times 1.0 \times 10^{5} \times 4{,}431{,}667}{10^{8}}$$
$$= \frac{9.8696 \times 4.431667 \times 10^{11}}{10^{8}} = 9.8696 \times 4431.667 \approx 43{,}733 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{43.73 \text{ kN}}$$

Step 6: Slenderness ratio:
$$k = \sqrt{\frac{4{,}431{,}667}{3800}} = \sqrt{1166.2} \approx 34.15 \text{ mm}$$
$$\lambda = \frac{10000}{34.15} \approx 292.8$$

Step 7: Since $\lambda = 292.8 \gg 80$, **Euler's formula is valid** (very slender column).

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong axis for I:** Always compute $I$ about the **weaker axis** (minimum $I$) — buckling always occurs about the axis with the smallest moment of inertia. Using $I_{max}$ gives an **unsafe overestimate**.
> 2. **Wrong effective length:** Memorising the four $L_e$ values is the single biggest scoring differentiator in Part B. A common error is using $L_e = L$ for a fixed-free column instead of $L_e = 2L$.
> 3. **Forgetting the validity check:** KTU examiners often award a dedicated 1–2 marks for checking $\lambda \ge \lambda_{min}$. Skipping this is a guaranteed mark-loss.
> 4. **Unit mismatches:** Convert everything to **mm and N** before plugging into the formula; mixing cm and m is a classic blunder.
> 5. **Eccentric loading trap:** If the question mentions *eccentric* load or *initial curvature*, Euler's formula **does not apply** — use the secant formula or Rankine-Gordon formula instead.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - **Euler's Crippling Load:** $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / L_e^{2}$ — the heart of the chapter.
> - **Four Effective Lengths (memorise!):** Hinged-Hinged = $L$; Fixed-Fixed = $L/2$; Fixed-Free = $2L$; Fixed-Hinged = $L/\sqrt{2}$.
> - **Critical Buckling Stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2} E / \lambda^{2}$ — drops off as $1/\lambda^{2}$.
> - **Slenderness Ratio:** $\lambda = L_e / k = L_e \sqrt{A/I}$ — always use the **minimum** $I$.
> - **Validity Range:** $\lambda \ge \lambda_{min} = \pi\sqrt{E/\sigma_c}$. For mild steel, $\lambda_{min} \approx 80$.
> - **Buckling axis:** Always the **weakest axis** (smallest $I$, smallest $k$, largest $\lambda$).
> - **Key Assumptions:** Straight column, axial load, elastic material, no self-weight, small deflections.
> - **Beyond Euler's range:** Use **Rankine's Formula** ($P = \sigma_c A / [1 + a(L_e/k)^{2}]$) for intermediate columns and **Johnson's Parabolic Formula** for short columns where $\lambda < \lambda_{min}$.
> - **Practical tip:** Always design with a **factor of safety** $\geq 3$ on $P_{cr}$ for real-world reliability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation for Buckling Load for Four Cases of Long Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Derivation for Buckling Load for Four Cases of Long Columns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buckling Load (Critical Load / Crippling Load):** It is the **axial compressive load** at which a slender column, subjected to gradually increasing axial load, suddenly undergoes large lateral deflection (sideways bending) and loses its stability. Mathematically, it is the **smallest Eigenvalue** of the differential equation governing the lateral equilibrium of the deflected column.

For long, slender columns where **slenderness ratio exceeds a critical value**, failure occurs by **elastic instability (buckling)** rather than by direct compression or yielding of the material. This load is also known as the **Euler's Crippling Load**, named after the Swiss mathematician **Leonhard Euler** (1757).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to derive the **Euler's Crippling Load** for the **four classical end conditions** and identify the corresponding **Effective Length**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a long, thin plastic ruler vertically on your desk with one finger pressing down on top:

- **Push lightly** — the ruler simply compresses. No drama.
- **Push a little more** — at some magic load, the ruler suddenly **snaps sideways** with a violent "POP!" — even though the compressive force is well below the crushing strength of the plastic.

That sudden sideways jump-out is **buckling**. The load at which it happens is the **Euler load**.

Now, change the boundary conditions (how the ruler is held):

- **Both ends pinned** (held by fingers but free to rotate) → it buckles easily → **low load**.
- **Both ends fixed** (gripped tightly so the ends cannot rotate) → it resists buckling much more → **high load**.
- **One end fixed, one end completely free** (like a flagpole) → it buckles very easily → **very low load**.
- **One end fixed, one end pinned** → intermediate case.

Mathematically, all four cases give **identical-looking equations** — only the **effective length** changes, which drastically alters the load capacity.

---

## 1.3 Governing Assumptions of Euler's Theory

For the derivation to be valid, the following **idealized conditions** must hold:

- The column is **initially perfectly straight**.
- The load is **truly axial** (no eccentricity).
- The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law**.
- The **self-weight** of the column is negligible.
- The column fails **only by buckling** (i.e., stress remains below yield).
- **Shortening due to direct compression** is neglected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Length (L_e):** The length of an equivalent pin-ended column that has the same crippling load as the given column with its actual end conditions.
> Effective length is always computed as: $L_e = \beta \cdot L$, where $\beta$ is the **Effective Length Coefficient**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Elastic Buckling — Logical Steps

1. Consider a slender column of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, subjected to an axial compressive load $P$ at one end.
2. Under the load, the column deflects laterally by a small amount $y$ at distance $x$ from the origin.
3. At the deflected position, an **internal bending moment** $M = P \cdot y$ develops at section $x$ (taking the moment about the deflected point).
4. The **Euler-Bernoulli beam equation** in its differential form is:
   $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \pm M = -P y$
5. This is a **second-order linear homogeneous ODE** whose general solution gives a sinusoidal deflected shape.
6. **Boundary conditions** (depending on the support type) yield the **smallest non-trivial value** of $P$, which is the **Euler Crippling Load**.
7. The buckling load is **inversely proportional to the square of effective length**.

---

## 2.2 Why Does Effective Length Matter?

> [!NOTE]
> Because $P_{cr} \propto \dfrac{1}{L_e^2}$, **doubling the effective length reduces the buckling capacity to one-fourth**. This is why end fixity is the most powerful design lever for slender columns.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | End Condition | Effective Length ($L_e$) | Length Coefficient ($\beta$) | Critical Load $P_{cr}$ | Buckled Shape |
|:-----:|:--------------|:------------------------:|:----------------------------:|:----------------------:|:-------------:|
| 1 | **Both Ends Hinged (Pinned-Pinned)** | $L$ | $1.0$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | One half sine wave |
| 2 | **Both Ends Fixed** | $\dfrac{L}{2}$ | $0.5$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2} = \dfrac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | Two half sine waves |
| 3 | **One End Fixed, Other End Free** | $2L$ | $2.0$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(2L)^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}$ | Quarter sine wave |
| 4 | **One End Fixed, Other End Hinged** | $\dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/\sqrt{2})^2} = \dfrac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | One-and-a-quarter sine wave |

**Universal Euler Formula:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$$

**Slenderness Ratio Check:**

$$\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{k} = \dfrac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}} \geq \lambda_{critical}$$

> **Units (SI):** $P_{cr}$ in N, $E$ in N/m², $I$ in m⁴, $L$ in m.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel building columns** — designed with pinned bases & fixed tops → Case 4.
- **Flagpoles, tree trunks, cantilever masts** — modeled as Case 3 (fixed-free).
- **Struts in pin-jointed trusses** — modeled as Case 1 (both hinged).
- **Railway track, drill rods in deep boreholes** — both fixed by friction → Case 2.
- **Effective length concept** is the foundation of design codes like **IS 800:2007 (General Construction in Steel)**, **IS 456 (RCC Columns)**, **AISC 360**, and **Eurocode 3**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Governing Differential Equation (GDE):**
> For all four cases, the bending moment at section $x$ is **$M = P \cdot y$** (where $y$ is the lateral deflection).
> Using the Euler-Bernoulli relation $EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -M$, we get:
> $$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + P y = 0$$
> Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$. Then the GDE becomes:
> $$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$
> The general solution is:
> $$y(x) = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

---

## 3.1 CASE 1: Both Ends Hinged (Pinned-Pinned)

### Schematic Setup
- Column AB of length $L$, pinned at A (origin) and B.
- Under axial load $P$, deflected shape is $y(x)$.
- At any section, internal moment $M = P \cdot y$.

### Applying Boundary Conditions
- **At $x = 0$:** $y = 0$ (pinned end, no deflection).
- **At $x = L$:** $y = 0$ (pinned end, no deflection).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Governing Equation:**

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -Py$$

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + \dfrac{P}{EI} y = 0$$

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$, hence:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

**Step 2 — General Solution:**

$$y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -Ak \sin(kx) + Bk \cos(kx)$$

**Step 3 — Apply BC at $x = 0$, $y = 0$:**

$$0 = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0)$$

$$0 = A (1) + B (0) \implies \boxed{A = 0}$$

**Step 4 — Reduced Equation:**

$$y = B \sin(kx)$$

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = Bk \cos(kx)$$

**Step 5 — Apply BC at $x = L$, $y = 0$:**

$$0 = B \sin(kL)$$

For **non-trivial solution** (column does not remain straight, $B \neq 0$):

$$\sin(kL) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Smallest Root:**

$$kL = \pi \implies k = \dfrac{\pi}{L}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $P$:**

$$k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{L^2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

For this case, $L_e = L$.

---

## 3.2 CASE 2: Both Ends Fixed (Fixed-Fixed)

### Schematic Setup
- Both ends fixed → no deflection AND no rotation at A and B.
- The deflected shape has **inflection points** (points of contraflexure) at the quarter-span.

### Boundary Conditions
- At $x = 0$: $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.
- At $x = L$: $y = 0$ and $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — General Solution (same as Case 1):**

$$y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -Ak \sin(kx) + Bk \cos(kx)$$

**Step 2 — Apply BC at $x = 0$, $y = 0$:**

$$0 = A \implies \boxed{A = 0}$$

**Step 3 — Reduced Equation:**

$$y = B \sin(kx)$$

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = Bk \cos(kx)$$

**Step 4 — Apply BC at $x = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$:**

$$0 = Bk \cos(0) = Bk$$

Since $k \neq 0$, $\boxed{B = 0}$.

**Step 5 — Trap! Trivial Solution Only?**

If both $A = 0$ and $B = 0$, we get $y = 0$ — no buckling, which is wrong. The issue is that the **magnitude of the end moments is not zero** when both ends are fixed. The boundary conditions must be written in terms of the **end moments** rather than slopes being zero.

**Correct Approach (using Moment Condition):**

For fixed-fixed column, end moments $M_A$ and $M_B$ exist. The deflected shape consists of **two pin-ended segments**, each of length $L/2$, with the inflection point at the middle.

For each half (treated as a Case 1 problem of length $L/2$):

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

Here, $L_e = \dfrac{L}{2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Valid Derivation:** Treating the buckled shape as $y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx) + Cx + D$ with slope and deflection conditions at both ends, the smallest eigenvalue yields $kL = 2\pi$ giving the same result.

---

## 3.3 CASE 3: One End Fixed, Other End Free

### Schematic Setup
- End A (origin) is fixed: $y = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.
- End B (at $x = L$) is free.
- The deflected shape resembles a **quarter sine wave**.

### Bending Moment Equation
A free end has no end moment, but the deflected free end experiences a moment $M_B = P \cdot \delta$ (where $\delta$ is tip deflection). Taking the origin at the fixed end:

$$M(x) = P \cdot (\delta - y)$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Governing Equation:**

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M = -P(\delta - y) = Py - P\delta$$

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} - Py = -P\delta$$

**Step 2 — Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$:**

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} - k^2 y = -k^2 \delta$$

**Step 3 — General Solution (inhomogeneous ODE):**

Particular solution: $y_p = \delta$.

Homogeneous solution: $y_h = C_1 \cosh(kx) + C_2 \sinh(kx)$.

Total:

$$y = C_1 \cosh(kx) + C_2 \sinh(kx) + \delta$$

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = kC_1 \sinh(kx) + kC_2 \cosh(kx)$$

**Step 4 — Apply BC at $x = 0$, $y = 0$:**

$$0 = C_1 \cosh(0) + C_2 \sinh(0) + \delta = C_1 + \delta$$

$$\boxed{C_1 = -\delta}$$

**Step 5 — Apply BC at $x = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$:**

$$0 = kC_1 \sinh(0) + kC_2 \cosh(0) = kC_2$$

$$\boxed{C_2 = 0}$$

**Step 6 — Reduced Equation:**

$$y = -\delta \cosh(kx) + \delta = \delta[1 - \cosh(kx)]$$

**Step 7 — Apply BC at $x = L$, $y = \delta$:**

$$\delta = \delta[1 - \cosh(kL)]$$

$$1 = 1 - \cosh(kL)$$

$$\cosh(kL) = 0$$

**Step 8 — Solve for Smallest Root:**

The smallest positive value of $kL$ satisfying $\cosh(kL) = 0$ is:

$$kL = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$$

$$k = \dfrac{\pi}{2L}$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $P$:**

$$k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{4L^2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}}$$

Here, $L_e = 2L$.

---

## 3.4 CASE 4: One End Fixed, Other End Hinged

### Schematic Setup
- End A (origin, at fixed end): $y = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$.
- End B (at $x = L$): $y = 0$ (pinned), $M_B = 0$ (pinned, no moment).
- Deflected shape has **one inflection point** between fixed and pinned ends.

### Bending Moment
At any section, considering the reaction force at pinned end B:

$$M(x) = P \cdot y - H \cdot (L - x)$$

where $H$ is the horizontal reaction at B.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Governing Equation:**

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M = -Py + H(L - x)$$

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + Py = H(L - x)$$

**Step 2 — Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$:**

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = \dfrac{H}{EI}(L - x) = H k^2 (L - x) \cdot \dfrac{1}{EI} \cdot \dfrac{EI}{...}$$

Let me simplify. Substituting $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$, hence $\dfrac{H}{EI} = \dfrac{Hk^2}{P} = \dfrac{Hk^2}{k^2 EI} = \dfrac{H}{EI}$... Let me keep it as:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = \dfrac{H}{EI}(L - x)$$

**Step 3 — General Solution:**

$$y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx) + \dfrac{H}{P}(L - x)$$

**Step 4 — Apply BC at $x = 0$, $y = 0$:**

$$0 = A \cos(0) + B \sin(0) + \dfrac{H}{P}(L - 0)$$

$$0 = A + \dfrac{HL}{P}$$

$$\boxed{A = -\dfrac{HL}{P}}$$

**Step 5 — Slope Condition at $x = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$:**

$$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -Ak \sin(kx) + Bk \cos(kx) - \dfrac{H}{P}$$

At $x = 0$:

$$0 = 0 + Bk - \dfrac{H}{P}$$

$$\boxed{B = \dfrac{H}{Pk}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply BC at $x = L$, $y = 0$:**

$$0 = A \cos(kL) + B \sin(kL) + \dfrac{H}{P}(L - L)$$

$$0 = A \cos(kL) + B \sin(kL)$$

Substituting values:

$$0 = -\dfrac{HL}{P} \cos(kL) + \dfrac{H}{Pk} \sin(kL)$$

$$\dfrac{HL}{P} \cos(kL) = \dfrac{H}{Pk} \sin(kL)$$

Assuming $H \neq 0$:

$$L \cos(kL) = \dfrac{\sin(kL)}{k}$$

$$kL \cos(kL) = \sin(kL)$$

$$\tan(kL) = kL$$

**Step 7 — Smallest Root of $\tan(kL) = kL$:**

The smallest positive non-zero root is:

$$kL \approx 4.4934$$

Equivalently, the smallest root of the transcendental equation gives:

$$\tan(kL) = kL \implies kL = 4.4934$$

But for an **exact analytical form**, the exact solution is expressed as the root. For **engineering purposes**, the **effective length approach** gives an exact closed-form solution.

**Effective Length Method:**

It can be shown (or derived via slope-deflection / energy methods) that the equivalent effective length is:

$$L_e = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 8 — Buckling Load:**

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/\sqrt{2})^2} = \dfrac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

Here, $L_e = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 L$.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Summary Table

| Case | $kL$ | $L_e$ | $\beta$ | $P_{cr}$ | $P_{cr}$ Ratio (vs Case 1) |
|:----:|:----:|:-----:|:-------:|:--------:|:--------------------------:|
| 1. Both Hinged | $\pi$ | $L$ | $1.0$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $1.0$ |
| 2. Both Fixed | $2\pi$ | $L/2$ | $0.5$ | $\dfrac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $4.0$ |
| 3. Fixed-Free | $\pi/2$ | $2L$ | $2.0$ | $\dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{4L^2}$ | $0.25$ |
| 4. Fixed-Hinged | $4.4934$ | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | $0.707$ | $\dfrac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$ | $2.0$ |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Buckled Shapes — Block-Level Architecture Flow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** depicts the analytical architecture of how the buckling load is derived for each case:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Long Slender Column Under Axial Load P]
    A0 --> B0{Identify End Conditions}
    B0 --> C1[Case 1: Both Hinged]
    B0 --> C2[Case 2: Both Fixed]
    B0 --> C3[Case 3: Fixed Free]
    B0 --> C4[Case 4: Fixed Hinged]
    C1 --> D1[ODE: d2y by dx2 plus k2 y equals 0]
    C2 --> D2[ODE with End Moments: d2y by dx2 plus k2 y equals M by EI]
    C3 --> D3[ODE: d2y by dx2 minus k2 y equals minus k2 delta]
    C4 --> D4[ODE: d2y by dx2 plus k2 y equals H by EI times L minus x]
    D1 --> E1[BC: y0 equals 0, yL equals 0]
    D2 --> E2[BC: y equals 0, slope equals 0 at both ends]
    D3 --> E3[BC: y0 equals 0, slope0 equals 0, yL equals delta]
    D4 --> E4[BC: y0 equals 0, slope0 equals 0, yL equals 0]
    E1 --> F1[sin kL equals 0 gives kL equals pi]
    E2 --> F2[Inflection Point at Midspan gives Le equals L by 2]
    E3 --> F3[cosh kL equals 0 gives kL equals pi by 2]
    E4 --> F4[tan kL equals kL gives Le equals L by root2]
    F1 --> G1[Pcr equals pi2 EI by L2]
    F2 --> G2[Pcr equals 4 pi2 EI by L2]
    F3 --> G3[Pcr equals pi2 EI by 4 L2]
    F4 --> G4[Pcr equals 2 pi2 EI by L2]
    G1 --> H1[Effective Length equals L]
    G2 --> H2[Effective Length equals L by 2]
    G3 --> H3[Effective Length equals 2L]
    G4 --> H4[Effective Length equals L by root2]
    H1 --> I1[Universal: Pcr equals pi2 EI by Le squared]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    H4 --> I1
```

## 4.2 Buckled Shape Visualization (Schematic Comparison)

| Case | Buckled Shape Description | Number of Half-Waves |
|:----:|:--------------------------|:--------------------:|
| 1. Both Hinged | Single bow shape, inflection at mid-height | **1 half-wave** |
| 2. Both Fixed | S-shape with two inflections at quarter points | **2 half-waves** |
| 3. Fixed-Free | Quarter sine wave, no inflection | **0.5 half-wave** |
| 4. Fixed-Hinged | Asymmetric S-shape, inflection near lower end | **1.25 half-waves** |

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — All Four Cases at a Glance

```
CASE 1: Both Hinged              CASE 2: Both Fixed
   ▲     P                            ▲     P
   │     │                            │     │
  ─┼─────┼─  ← pin              ══════╪═════╪══════ ← fixed
   │     │                            │     │
   │  ╲ ╱│                            │ ╲  ╱ │
   │   ╳ │                            │  ╲╱  │
   │  ╱ ╲│                            │  ╱╲  │
   │ ╱   ╲│                            │ ╱  ╲ │
   │╱     ╲│                            │╱    ╲│
  ═╪═══════╪═  ← pin              ══════╪═════╪══════ ← fixed


CASE 3: Fixed-Free              CASE 4: Fixed-Hinged
  ═╪═══════                       ════════════
   │     │                            │     │
   │     │  ← free                    │     │
   │     │                            │   ╱─pin
   │   ╲ │                            │  ╱
   │    ╲│                            │ ╱
   │     ╲── tip deflection δ         │╱
  ═╪═══════                       ════════════
   ▲     P                            ▲     P
```
