<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Euler's Crippling Load. State Euler's formula for a column hinged at both ends. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Euler's Crippling Load** is the axial compressive load at which a long, slender column suddenly fails by lateral buckling (sideways deflection) instead of by direct crushing. It is the smallest load at which the deflected equilibrium shape of the column is non-trivial.

For a **both-ends-hinged** column of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

This formula is applicable only when the **slenderness ratio is greater than the critical slenderness ratio** (i.e., the column is "long").

---

### Q2. What is meant by effective length of a column? Write its value for all four end conditions. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Effective Length ($L_e$):** It is the length of an equivalent pin-ended column that has the same crippling load as the actual column with its given end conditions. It is expressed as $L_e = \beta L$ where $\beta$ is the effective length coefficient.

| End Condition | Effective Length ($L_e$) |
|:--------------|:-----------------------:|
| Both Ends Hinged | $L$ |
| Both Ends Fixed | $L/2$ |
| One End Fixed, Other Free | $2L$ |
| One End Fixed, Other Hinged | $L/\sqrt{2}$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state the GDE clearly** — students lose 2 marks if they skip writing $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M$.
> 2. **Mention assumptions** of Euler's theory at the start — examiners specifically look for **slenderness ratio condition**.
> 3. **Show all boundary conditions explicitly** with $x = 0$ and $x = L$.
> 4. **For Case 4 (fixed-hinged)**, do NOT confuse $\tan(kL) = kL$ with Euler's simple formula. Either derive the transcendental equation OR quote the effective length result and clearly mark it.
> 5. **Mention the unit** of $P_{cr}$ in the final answer (N or kN).
> 6. Many students **forget the Effective Length concept** — KTU loves asking "Why is the effective length for a fixed-free column equal to $2L$?" — explain intuitively in 2 lines.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Derive the Euler's crippling load for the following end conditions:**

**(a)** Both ends of the column are **fixed**.

**(b)** One end is **fixed** and the other end is **hinged**.

#### Part (a) Solution — Both Ends Fixed (7 Marks)

**Given:** Column of length $L$, flexural rigidity $EI$, both ends fixed.

**Assumptions:**
- Column is long, slender, initially straight.
- Material obeys Hooke's Law (elastic buckling).
- Self-weight is negligible.

**Step 1 — Buckled Shape:**
When the column buckles, it deflects into an S-shape with an **inflection point at midspan**. At this point, $M = 0$, and the curvature changes sign. The shape consists of two pin-ended segments of length $L/2$ each.

**Step 2 — Equivalent Problem:**
Each half acts as a pin-ended column of length $L/2$. Applying Euler's formula for Case 1:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplification:**

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{4\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

**Effective Length:**

$$L_e = \dfrac{L}{2}$$

**Buckled Shape:** Two half sine waves (S-shape).

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Fixed-Hinged Column (7 Marks)

**Given:** Column of length $L$, fixed at one end, hinged at the other.

**Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation:**
The bending moment at section $x$ (taking origin at fixed end) due to axial load $P$ and horizontal reaction $H$ at the hinged end:

$$M = P y - H(L - x)$$

Therefore:

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M = -Py + H(L-x)$$

**Step 2 — Standard Form:**
Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = \dfrac{H}{EI}(L - x)$$

**Step 3 — General Solution:**

$$y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx) + \dfrac{H}{P}(L - x)$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Conditions:**

(i) At $x = 0$, $y = 0$:

$$0 = A + \dfrac{HL}{P} \implies A = -\dfrac{HL}{P}$$

(ii) At $x = 0$, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$:

$$0 = Bk - \dfrac{H}{P} \implies B = \dfrac{H}{Pk}$$

(iii) At $x = L$, $y = 0$:

$$0 = A \cos(kL) + B \sin(kL) + 0$$

Substituting:

$$0 = -\dfrac{HL}{P}\cos(kL) + \dfrac{H}{Pk}\sin(kL)$$

$$kL \cos(kL) = \sin(kL)$$

$$\boxed{\tan(kL) = kL}$$

**Step 5 — Smallest Non-Zero Root:**
The smallest positive root is $kL \approx 4.4934$.

Therefore: $k = \dfrac{4.4934}{L}$, and $k^2 = \dfrac{20.19}{L^2}$.

**Step 6 — Euler's Crippling Load (using Effective Length):**
For this case, the equivalent effective length is:

$$L_e = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 L$$

Therefore:

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/\sqrt{2})^2} = \dfrac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{2\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Euler's crippling load for a **column hinged at both ends**.

**(b)** A **mild steel column** of length 6 m, with both ends fixed, has a **circular cross-section** of diameter 80 mm. Find the **Euler's crippling load**. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$.

#### Part (a) Solution — Both Ends Hinged (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Setup:**
Column of length $L$ pinned at both ends A and B, with axial load $P$ causing small lateral deflection $y(x)$.

**Step 2 — Governing Equation:**

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -Py$$

Let $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} + k^2 y = 0$$

**Step 3 — General Solution:**

$$y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$$

**Step 4 — Boundary Conditions:**

(i) At $x = 0$, $y = 0$ → $A = 0$.

(ii) At $x = L$, $y = 0$ → $B \sin(kL) = 0$.

For non-trivial solution ($B \neq 0$): $\sin(kL) = 0$.

**Step 5 — Smallest Root:**
$kL = \pi$ → $k = \dfrac{\pi}{L}$.

**Step 6 — Crippling Load:**

$$k^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2}{L^2} = \dfrac{P}{EI}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L^2}}$$

Effective Length $L_e = L$.

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Length $L = 6 \text{ m} = 6000 \text{ mm}$
- Both ends **fixed** → Effective Length $L_e = \dfrac{L}{2} = 3000 \text{ mm}$
- Diameter $d = 80 \text{ mm}$
- Young's Modulus $E = 2 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**Step 1 — Moment of Inertia (Circular Section):**

$$I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi (80)^4}{64}$$

$$I = \dfrac{\pi \times 40960000}{64} = \dfrac{128679635.1}{64}$$

$$I \approx 2010619.3 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler's Formula (Both Ends Fixed):**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{4 \pi^2 EI}{L^2} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(L/2)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times 2010619.3}{(3000)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$\pi^2 \approx 9.8696$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 2010619.3$$

$$= 9.8696 \times 4.0212 \times 10^{11}$$

$$= 3.9687 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$(3000)^2 = 9 \times 10^6$$

**Step 5 — Final Calculation:**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{3.9687 \times 10^{12}}{9 \times 10^6} = 4.41 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 441 \text{ kN}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Buckling of Long Columns**

- **Euler's Crippling Load** applies **only** to long, slender columns where slenderness ratio $\lambda > \lambda_{critical}$ (the column fails by buckling, not by crushing).

- **Universal Formula (valid for ALL four cases):**

$$P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$$

- **Effective Length Values (Memorize!):**
  - Both Hinged → $L_e = L$
  - Both Fixed → $L_e = L/2$
  - Fixed-Free → $L_e = 2L$ (weakest)
  - Fixed-Hinged → $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$

- **Load Capacity Ranking (Strongest to Weakest):**
  Both Fixed $>$ Fixed-Hinged $>$ Both Hinged $>$ Fixed-Free
  - Ratio: $4 : 2 : 1 : 0.25$

- **GDE to Remember:** $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -M = -Py$ (basic case).

- **General solution form:** $y = A \cos(kx) + B \sin(kx)$ where $k^2 = \dfrac{P}{EI}$.

- **For Case 4 (Fixed-Hinged):** Transcendental equation $\tan(kL) = kL$ gives $kL \approx 4.4934$, which translates to the closed-form effective length $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$.

- **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
  - Using $L$ instead of $L_e$ in Euler's formula.
  - Forgetting to convert units (mm vs m) when substituting $E$ and $I$.
  - Applying Euler's formula to **short columns** (use Johnson's formula or Rankine's formula instead).
  - Confusing **buckling load** with **crushing load**.

- **Units to remember:** $P$ in N, $E$ in N/mm², $I$ in mm⁴, $L$ in mm → $P$ in N.
  - OR: $P$ in kN, $E$ in kN/m², $I$ in m⁴, $L$ in m → $P$ in kN.

- **Real-World Examples:**
  - **Building columns** — mostly Fixed-Hinged or Both Fixed.
  - **Flagpole** — Fixed-Free.
  - **Truss members** — Both Hinged.
  - **Rail tracks** — Both Fixed (friction).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine's Formula for Long Columns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Rankine's Formula for Long Columns # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **RANKINE'S FORMULA (RANKINE–GORDON FORMULA):** > A semi-empirical, unified column formula that predicts the **Crippling (Buckling) Load** of a *long* column by interpolating between the two limiting cases — the **crushing failure** of a short column and the **elastic buckling failure** of a long column. Mathematically, for a column of effective length $L_e$, cross-sectional area $A$, second moment of area $I$, radius of gyration $k$, and material crushing stress $\sigma_c$: $$ P_R \;=\; \dfrac{P_c \, P_e}{P_c + a \, P_e} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c \, A}{1 + a \left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^{2}} $$ where: - $P_c = \sigma_c \, A$ → **Crushing Load** (short-column failure) - $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{L_e^{2}}$ → **Euler's Buckling Load** (long-column failure) - $a$ → **Rankine's Constant** (empirical, depends on material and end conditions) - $L_e$ → **Effective Length** of the column based on end-fixity ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Imperfect Straw" > [!NOTE] > **Imagine trying to crush a slightly bent drinking straw** versus a perfectly straight one. A perfectly straight straw will only fail by sudden sideways buckling once the Euler load is reached. A *slightly bent* straw, however, will start bending more and more as you compress it, and it will give way *much earlier* — long before reaching Euler's ideal load. Real columns are never perfectly straight; they always have tiny imperfections, eccentric loading, and material inhomogeneities. **Rankine's formula mathematically accounts for this real-world imperfection by combining the crushing strength and the Euler buckling strength into a single, conservative formula.** ## 1.3 Why Two Separate Formulas Are Insufficient | Column Type | Governing Failure | Valid Formula | |:---:|:---|:---| | **Short (Stocky)** | Direct crushing of material | $P_c = \sigma_c A$ | | **Long (Slender)** | Elastic lateral buckling | $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_e^{2}}$ | | **Intermediate** | **Combined crushing + buckling** | **Rankine's Formula** | A pure Euler formula is unsafe for medium-length columns (it over-predicts strength). A pure crushing formula is unsafe for slender columns. Rankine's formula bridges this gap. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logic Behind the Combination The genius of Rankine's formula lies in a single, elegant reciprocal addition: $$ \boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{P_R} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{P_c} \;+\; \dfrac{a}{P_e}\;} $$ **Why this form?** Consider the deflection of an *imperfect* column: - When $P \to 0$: $1/P_R \to 0$, so $P_R \to \infty$ (no load → no stress — correct). - When $P \to P_c$ (short column): crushing governs, and the formula degenerates toward $P_R \to P_c$. - When $P \to P_e$ (slender column): elastic buckling dominates, and $P_R$ degenerates toward $P_e/a$. The dimensionless **Rankine's constant $a$** acts as a "weight" that controls how rapidly the formula transitions from crushing-dominated to buckling-dominated behaviour. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $P_c = \sigma_c A$ | Crushing load of short column | Stocky column | | 2 | $P_e = \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_e^{2}}$ | Euler's critical load | Long column | | 3 | $P_R = \dfrac{P_c \, P_e}{P_c + a\,P_e}$ | Rankine's Crippling Load | **General-purpose** | | 4 | $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a (L_e/k)^{2}}$ | Rankine in direct form (most used) | Direct substitution | | 5 | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Radius of gyration | Section property | | 6 | $L_e = \beta L$ | Effective length; $\beta$ depends on end conditions | Buckling analysis | | 7 | $P_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{P_R}{\text{FOS}}$ | Safe working load | Design | ### Effective Length Factor $\beta$ (KTU Standard) | End Conditions | $\beta = L_e/L$ | Effective Length $L_e$ | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Both ends **hinged** | 1.0 | $L$ | | Both ends **fixed** | 0.5 | $L/2$ | | One end **fixed**, other **free** | 2.0 | $2L$ | | One end **fixed**, other **hinged** | $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | ### Typical Values of Rankine's Constant $a$ (for Mild Steel) | End Conditions | Rankine's Constant $a$ | |:---|:---:| | Both ends hinged | $\dfrac{1}{7500}$ | | Both ends fixed | $\dfrac{1}{7500}$ | | One end fixed, other free | $\dfrac{1}{7500}$ | | One end fixed, other hinged | $\dfrac{1}{7500}$ | > [!NOTE] > The empirical constant $a$ is calibrated against experimental data. For KTU 2024 Scheme problems, **mild steel with $a = 1/7500$** is the default unless stated otherwise. For other materials, problem data will be given. ### Typical Crushing Stress $\sigma_c$ Values | Material | $\sigma_c$ (MPa) | |:---|:---:| | Mild Steel | 320 | | Cast Iron | 550 | | Wrought Iron | 250 | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Civil Engineering**: Design of building columns, bridge piers, and trestles (intermediate slenderness). - **Mechanical Engineering**: Piston rods, screw jack columns, hydraulic press frames. - **Aerospace & Marine**: Compression members in trusses and frames where weight and slenderness matter. - **Structural Steel Design**: IS 800:2007 uses similar concepts (with Perry-Robertson as a more refined form) for all practical column design. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Rankine's Formula ### 3.1.1 Initial Setup Consider a long column of length $L$, **hinged at both ends**, possessing a small initial curvature (imperfection). Let the initial central deflection be $\delta_0$. The initial deflected shape is assumed sinusoidal: $$ y_0(x) \;=\; \delta_0 \, \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) $$ When a compressive axial load $P$ is applied, an *additional* deflection $y$ develops. The total deflection at any section is $y + y_0$. ### 3.1.2 Governing Differential Equation The bending moment at section $x$ is $M = P(y + y_0)$. By Euler–Bernoulli beam theory: $$ EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; -\,M \;=\; -P\,(y + y_0) $$ Rearranging: $$ EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + P\,y \;=\; -P\,\delta_0\,\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) $$ ### 3.1.3 Solving the Differential Equation Let $k^{2} = P/(EI)$. The equation becomes: $$ \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + k^{2} y \;=\; -k^{2}\,\delta_0\,\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) $$ **Complementary (homogeneous) solution:** $$ y_c \;=\; A\cos(kx) + B\sin(kx) $$ **Particular solution** (try $y_p = C\sin(\pi x/L)$): $$ -C\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{L}\right)^{2}\!\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) + k^{2} C \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) \;=\; -k^{2}\,\delta_0\,\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) $$ Equating coefficients: $$ C\Big[k^{2} - \left(\tfrac{\pi}{L}\right)^{2}\Big] \;=\; -k^{2}\,\delta_0 $$ $$ C \;=\; \dfrac{-k^{2}\,\delta_0}{k^{2} - (\pi/L)^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{-\delta_0 \cdot P/EI}{P/EI - \pi^{2}/L^{2}} $$ Using $P_e = \pi^{2}EI/L^{2}$, i.e. $\pi^{2}/L^{2} = P_e/(EI)$: $$ C \;=\; \dfrac{-\delta_0 \, P}{P - P_e} \;=\; \dfrac{\delta_0 \, P}{P_e - P} $$ **General solution:** $$ y(x) \;=\; A\cos(kx) + B\sin(kx) + \dfrac{\delta_0\,P}{P_e - P}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) $$ ### 3.1.4 Applying Boundary Conditions For both ends hinged: $y(0) = 0$ and $y(L) = 0$. - At $x = 0$: $A = 0$ (since $\cos 0 = 1$ and $\sin 0 = 0$). - At $x = L$: $B\sin(kL) + \dfrac{\delta_0\,P}{P_e - P}\sin(\pi) = B\sin(kL) = 0$. Since $\sin(kL) \neq 0$ (the column has not yet buckled), $B = 0$. **Final deflection profile:** $$ \boxed{\;y(x) \;=\; \dfrac{\delta_0\,P}{P_e - P}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right)\;} $$ ### 3.1.5 Maximum Deflection & Bending Moment The additional deflection is maximum at $x = L/2$: $$ y_{\max}^{\text{add}} \;=\; \dfrac{\delta_0\,P}{P_e - P} $$ **Total central deflection** (initial + additional): $$ \delta \;=\; \delta_0 + y_{\max}^{\text{add}} \;=\; \delta_0 + \dfrac{\delta_0\,P}{P_e - P} \;=\; \delta_0\left(1 + \dfrac{P}{P_e - P}\right) $$ $$ \delta \;=\; \delta_0 \cdot \dfrac{P_e - P + P}{P_e - P} \;=\; \dfrac{\delta_0\,P_e}{P_e - P} $$ **Maximum bending moment** at the centre: $$ M_{\max} \;=\; P\,\delta \;=\; \dfrac{P\,\delta_0\,P_e}{P_e - P} $$ ### 3.1.6 Maximum Compressive Stress At the extreme fibre (distance $c$ from the neutral axis), using $I = A k^{2}$: $$ \sigma_{\max} \;=\; \underbrace{\dfrac{P}{A}}_{\text{direct}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{M_{\max}\cdot c}{I}}_{\text{bending}} $$ $$ \sigma_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{P\,\delta_0\,P_e\,c}{I\,(P_e - P)} $$ ### 3.1.7 Introducing Rankine's Empirical Constant Define the empirical Rankine constant $a$ by the relation: $$ \delta_0\,c \;=\; a\,L^{2} $$ This single equation captures the combined effect of initial curvature, eccentric loading, and material inhomogeneity in one experimentally calibrated constant. Substituting: $$ \sigma_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{P \cdot a\,L^{2} \cdot P_e}{I\,(P_e - P)} $$ Using $I = A k^{2}$ and $P_e = \pi^{2}EI/L^{2} = \pi^{2}EAk^{2}/L^{2}$: $$ \sigma_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{P \cdot a\,L^{2} \cdot \pi^{2}EAk^{2}}{A k^{2}\,L^{2}\,(P_e - P)} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,a\,P}{P_e - P} $$ ### 3.1.8 Failure Condition: $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_c$ Setting $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_c$ and multiplying throughout by $A$: $$ \sigma_c\,A \;=\; P + \dfrac{\pi^{2}\,a\,A\,P}{P_e - P} $$ Multiplying both sides by $(P_e - P)$: $$ \sigma_c\,A\,(P_e - P) \;=\; P\,(P_e - P) + \pi^{2}\,a\,A\,P $$ Expanding: $$ \sigma_c\,A\,P_e - \sigma_c\,A\,P \;=\; P\,P_e - P^{2} + \pi^{2}\,a\,A\,P $$ For slender columns where $P \ll P_e$, the term $P^{2}$ is small compared to $P_e \cdot P$, so we approximate by dropping $P^{2}$ in a first-order Rankine treatment. To retain the exact empirical form, the **empirical constant $a$** is chosen such that the terms involving $P^{2}$ are absorbed. Rearranging with this convention: $$ \sigma_c\,A\,P_e \;=\; P\,P_e + \pi^{2}\,a\,A\,P + \sigma_c\,A\,P $$ Factoring $P$ on the right: $$ \sigma_c\,A\,P_e \;=\; P\left[P_e + \pi^{2}\,a\,A + \sigma_c\,A\right] $$ For slender columns, the **empirical relation** $P_e \gg \pi^{2}aA$ leads (after dropping the small $\pi^{2}aA$ term, which is absorbed into the constant $a$ already calibrated against experiments) to: $$ \sigma_c\,A\,P_e \;\approx\; P\,(P_e + \sigma_c\,A) $$ Solving for $P$: $$ P \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c\,A\,P_e}{\sigma_c\,A + P_e} \;=\; \dfrac{P_c\,P_e}{P_c + P_e} $$ In its **general Rankine form**, the constant $a$ is retained to capture end-condition and slenderness effects (e.g., for one end fixed and the other free, the formula becomes $P_R = P_cP_e/(P_c + aP_e)$ with the same empirical $a$): $$ \boxed{\;P_R \;=\; \dfrac{P_c\,P_e}{P_c + a\,P_e} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c\,A}{1 + a\left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^{2}}\;} $$ ### 3.1.9 Verification of Limiting Cases **Case 1: Short column** ($L_e \to 0$ or $k \to \infty$, i.e., $L_e/k \to 0$): $$ P_R \;\to\; \dfrac{\sigma_c\,A}{1 + 0} \;=\; \sigma_c\,A \;=\; P_c \quad \checkmark $$ **Case 2: Very long column** ($L_e/k \to \infty$): $$ P_R \;\to\; \dfrac{\sigma_c\,A}{a\,(L_e/k)^{2}} \;\to\; \dfrac{\sigma_c\,A\,k^{2}}{a\,L_e^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c\,I}{a\,L_e^{2}} $$ This is proportional to $1/L_e^{2}$, matching Euler's $1/L_e^{2}$ dependence (with the empirical factor $1/a$ and the use of crushing-related $\sigma_c$). ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A hollow circular cast-iron column, 150 mm external diameter and 100 mm internal diameter, is 4 m long and effectively pinned at both ends. Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $\sigma_c = 550$ MPa, $a = 1/1600$. Find the crippling load and safe load (FOS = 5). **Solution:** **Step 1 — Section Properties:** $$ A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(150^{2} - 100^{2}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}(22500 - 10000) \;=\; 9817.5 \,\text{mm}^{2} $$ $$ I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}\left(150^{4} - 100^{4}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}(506250000 - 100000000) \;=\; 1.99 \times 10^{7} \,\text{mm}^{4} $$ **Step 2 — Radius of Gyration:** $$ k^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{I}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{1.99 \times 10^{7}}{9817.5} \;=\; 2026.85 \,\text{mm}^{2} \;\Rightarrow\; k = 45.02 \,\text{mm} $$ **Step 3 — Slenderness Ratio** (both ends hinged, $L_e = L = 4000$ mm): $$ \dfrac{L_e}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{4000}{45.02} \;=\; 88.85 $$ **Step 4 — Crushing and Euler Loads:** $$ P_c \;=\; \sigma_c\,A \;=\; 550 \times 9817.5 \;=\; 5.40 \times 10^{6} \,\text{N} \;=\; 5400 \,\text{kN} $$ $$ P_e \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_e^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 1.2 \times 10^{5} \times 1.99 \times 10^{7}}{(4000)^{2}} \;=\; 1.473 \times 10^{6} \,\text{N} \;=\; 1473 \,\text{kN} $$ **Step 5 — Rankine's Crippling Load:** $$ P_R \;=\; \dfrac{P_c\,P_e}{P_c + a\,P_e} \;=\; \dfrac{5400 \times 1473}{5400 + (1473/1600)} $$ $$ P_R \;=\; \dfrac{7.954 \times 10^{6}}{5400.92} \;=\; 1472.7 \,\text{kN} \;\approx\; 1473 \,\text{kN} $$ > [!NOTE] > Here, since $P_e \ll P_c$, the column is **slender-dominated**; Euler buckling almost entirely controls. The crushing term barely enters. **Step 6 — Safe Load:** $$ P_{\text{safe}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_R}{\text{FOS}} \;=\; \dfrac{1473}{5} \;=\; 294.6 \,\text{kN} $$ --- ## 3.3 Direct Formula Substitutions (Crippling Stress Form) The maximum compressive stress at crippling is: $$ \sigma_c \;=\; \dfrac{P_R}{A}\left[1 + a\left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^{2}\right] $$ **Equivalently**, the safe load using the FOS is: $$ \sigma_{\text{working}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{safe}}}{A}\left[1 + a\left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^{2}\right] \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\text{FOS}} $$ These are the workhorse equations for **direct numerical substitution** in KTU problems. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Derivation Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Imperfect Long Column
(Initial Central Deflection delta_0)"] --> B["Apply Axial Load P"] B --> C["Governing DE:
EI y'' + Py = -P delta_0 sin(pi x / L)"] C --> D["Solve: y = delta_0 P sin(pi x / L) / (Pe - P)"] D --> E["Max Deflection at Centre:
delta = delta_0 Pe / (Pe - P)"] E --> F["Max Bending Moment:
M = P delta = P delta_0 Pe / (Pe - P)"] F --> G["Max Stress:
sigma_max = P/A + M c / I"] G --> H["Empirical Relation:
delta_0 c = a L^2"] H --> I["Set sigma_max = sigma_c"] I --> J["RANKINE FORMULA:
P_R = Pc Pe / Pc + a Pe"] J --> K{"Column Type?"} K -->|Short| L["P_R approx Pc
(Crushing Dominates)"] K -->|Long| M["P_R approx Pe / a
(Euler Dominates)"] K -->|Intermediate| N["P_R from Full Formula"] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style H fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a style J fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:3px style L fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style M fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828 style N fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Slenderness-Regime Classification ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SHORT["SHORT COLUMN REGIME"] S1["L_e / k is small"] --> S2["Crushing governs"] S2 --> S3["P_R approx Pc = sigma_c A"] end subgraph INTER["INTERMEDIATE COLUMN REGIME"] I1["L_e / k moderate"] --> I2["Both crushing and buckling act"] I2 --> I3["RANKINE FORMULA applies"] I3 --> I4["P_R = Pc Pe / Pc + a Pe"] end subgraph LONG["LONG COLUMN REGIME"] L1["L_e / k large"] --> L2["Euler buckling governs"] L2 --> L3["P_R approx Pe / a"] end SHORT ==>|"Transition"| INTER INTER ==>|"Transition"| LONG style S3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c style I4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px style L3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 ``` ## 4.3 Effective Length Table (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TD EC{"End Conditions"} EC -->|Both Hinged| A["beta = 1.0
Le = L"] EC -->|Both Fixed| B["beta = 0.5
Le = L/2"] EC -->|Fixed - Free| C["beta = 2.0
Le = 2L"] EC -->|Fixed - Hinged| D["beta = 1 / sqrt 2
Le = L / sqrt 2"] style EC fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style B fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style C fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style D fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **State Rankine's formula for the crippling load of a column and explain the significance of Rankine's constant.** **Model Answer:** Rankine's formula for the crippling load of a column is: $$ P_R \;=\; \dfrac{P_c\,P_e}{P_c + a\,P_e} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c\,A}{1 + a\left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^{2}} $$ **Significance of Rankine's constant $a$:** - It is an **empirical constant** that accounts for the initial imperfections (initial curvature, eccentric loading, material inhomogeneity) present in real columns. - Its value is determined by fitting the formula to experimental test data on actual columns. - For **mild steel** with both ends hinged, $a = 1/7500$. - It essentially acts as a **weighting factor** for the Euler buckling term, controlling the transition between short-column and long-column behaviour. - It is **dimensionless** and depends on the material and end conditions of the column. *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for significance)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Compare Euler's formula and Rankine's formula for long columns. Why is Rankine's formula preferred for design of intermediate columns?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Euler's Formula | Rankine's Formula | |:---|:---|:---| | **Applicability** | Only for long (slender) columns | General (short, long, and intermediate) | | **Failure type** | Pure elastic buckling | Combined crushing + buckling | | **Empirical basis** | Purely theoretical | Semi-empirical (uses constant $a$) | | **Initial imperfections** | Not considered | Implicitly considered via $a$ | | **Safe vs. unsafe** | Overestimates for medium columns | Conservative, accurate across regimes | **Why Rankine is preferred for intermediate columns:** - Euler's formula assumes the column is perfectly straight, axially loaded, and uses ideal material — which overestimates the load-carrying capacity of intermediate columns. - Real columns always have imperfections that reduce the actual load capacity. - Rankine's formula, being calibrated against experimental data, gives a more **realistic and safe** crippling load for columns of intermediate slenderness. *(3 marks: 2 for comparison table, 1 for explanation)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT confuse effective length $L_e$ with actual length $L$.** Many students lose 2–3 marks by directly using $L$ instead of $L_e = \beta L$. > - **Units must be consistent.** Convert all dimensions to **mm** and stresses to **MPa (N/mm²)** before substitution; mixing units is the #1 cause of numerical errors. > - **Always state Rankine's constant $a$ explicitly** with reference to material and end conditions — don't assume it is $1/7500$ unless told. > - **Use $I = Ak^{2}$** in the direct formula $P_R = \sigma_c A / [1 + a(L_e/k)^2]$; this avoids the need to compute $P_e$ separately. > - In derivation-type questions, **show the differential equation, boundary conditions, and the empirical substitution $\delta_0 c = aL^{2}$** clearly — partial credit is heavily dependent on these steps. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive Rankine's formula for the crippling load of a long column, clearly stating the assumptions involved. (7 marks) **(b)** A solid mild steel column of circular cross-section, 50 mm diameter and 3 m long, has both ends fixed. Using Rankine's formula, determine: 1. The crippling load, given $\sigma_c = 320$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $a = 1/7500$. 2. The safe load with a factor of safety of 4. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (a) — Derivation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Assumption:** A long column with both ends hinged has a small initial curvature. The initial deflected shape is assumed sinusoidal: $$ y_0(x) \;=\; \delta_0 \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) $$ **Step 2 — Differential equation under axial load $P$:** $$ EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} + P\,y \;=\; -P\,\delta_0\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Solving with $k^{2} = P/EI$** and boundary conditions $y(0) = 0$, $y(L) = 0$: $$ y(x) \;=\; \dfrac{\delta_0\,P}{P_e - P}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi x}{L}\right), \quad P_e = \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum deflection at $x = L/2$:** $$ \delta \;=\; \dfrac{\delta_0\,P_e}{P_e - P} $$ **Step 5 — Maximum bending moment and stress:** $$ M_{\max} = P\delta, \quad \sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{P}{A} + \dfrac{M_{\max}\,c}{I} $$ **Step 6 — Empirical substitution $\delta_0 c = a L^{2}$ and failure condition $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_c$:** $$ P_R \;=\; \dfrac{P_c\,P_e}{P_c + a\,P_e} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_c\,A}{1 + a\left(\dfrac{L_e}{k}\right)^{2}} \qquad \blacksquare $$ *(Marks split: 1 each for assumptions, DE, solution, max deflection, max stress, empirical relation, final formula — total 7)* --- #### Model Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks) **Step 1 — Section Properties:** $$ A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}(50)^{2} \;=\; 1963.5 \,\text{mm}^{2} $$ $$ I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}(50)^{4} \;=\; 3.068 \times 10^{5} \,\text{mm}^{4} $$ $$ k^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{I}{A} \;=\; \dfrac{3.068 \times 10^{5}}{1963.5} \;=\; 156.25 \,\text{mm}^{2} \;\Rightarrow\; k = 12.5 \,\text{mm} $$ **Step 2 — Effective Length** (both ends fixed: $L_e = L/2$): $$ L_e \;=\; \dfrac{3000}{2} \;=\; 1500 \,\text{mm} $$ **Step 3 — Slenderness Ratio:** $$ \dfrac{L_e}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{1500}{12.5} \;=\; 120 $$ **Step 4 — Crushing and Euler Loads:** $$ P_c = \sigma_c A = 320 \times 1963.5 = 6.283 \times 10^{5} \,\text{N} = 628.3 \,\text{kN} $$ $$ P_e = \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_e^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 3.068 \times 10^{5}}{(1500)^{2}} = 2.694 \times 10^{5} \,\text{N} = 269.4 \,\text{kN} $$ **Step 5 — Rankine's Crippling Load:** $$ P_R = \dfrac{P_c P_e}{P_c + a P_e} = \dfrac{628.3 \times 269.4}{628.3 + (269.4/7500)} $$ $$ P_R = \dfrac{1.693 \times 10^{5}}{628.34} = 269.4 \,\text{kN (approx.)} $$ Or via the direct form: $$ P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a(L_e/k)^{2}} = \dfrac{320 \times 1963.5}{1 + (1/7500)(120)^{2}} = \dfrac{6.283 \times 10^{5}}{1 + 1.92} $$ $$ P_R = \dfrac{6.283 \times 10^{5}}{2.92} = 2.152 \times 10^{5} \,\text{N} = 215.2 \,\text{kN} $$ **Step 6 — Safe Load (FOS = 4):** $$ P_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{P_R}{4} = \dfrac{215.2}{4} = 53.8 \,\text{kN} $$ *(Marks split: 1.5 for section properties, 1 for effective length, 1 for slenderness ratio, 1.5 for Pc and Pe, 1 for Rankine application, 1 for safe load — total 7)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (Alternative Choice) **(a)** A hollow rectangular cast-iron column, 200 mm × 150 mm external and 150 mm × 100 mm internal, is 6 m long, with one end fixed and the other hinged. Using Rankine's formula, find the crippling load. Given: $\sigma_c = 550$ MPa, $E = 1.2 \times 10^{5}$ MPa, $a = 1/1600$. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the concept of effective length. List the effective lengths for all four standard end conditions and explain why Euler's formula alone is insufficient for design. (7 marks) #### Model Solution to (a) — Numerical (7 marks) **Step 1 — Section Properties:** External: $200 \times 150 = 30000 \,\text{mm}^{2}$ Internal: $150 \times 100 = 15000 \,\text{mm}^{2}$ $$ A = 30000 - 15000 = 15000 \,\text{mm}^{2} $$ For a rectangular section, $I$ about the weaker axis (smaller dimension, 150 mm) governs: $$ I_{\min} = \dfrac{200 \times 150^{3} - 150 \times 100^{3}}{12} $$ $$ I_{\min} = \dfrac{200 \times 3.375 \times 10^{6} - 150 \times 10^{6}}{12} = \dfrac{6.75 \times 10^{8} - 1.5 \times 10^{8}}{12} = 4.375 \times 10^{7} \,\text{mm}^{4} $$ **Step 2 — Radius of Gyration (using $I_{\min}$):** $$ k^{2} = \dfrac{I_{\min}}{A} = \dfrac{4.375 \times 10^{7}}{15000} = 2916.67 \,\text{mm}^{2} \;\Rightarrow\; k = 54.0 \,\text{mm} $$ **Step 3 — Effective Length** (one end fixed, other hinged: $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$): $$ L_e = \dfrac{6000}{\sqrt{2}} = 4242.6 \,\text{mm} $$ **Step 4 — Slenderness Ratio:** $$ \dfrac{L_e}{k} = \dfrac{4242.6}{54.0} = 78.6 $$ **Step 5 — Crushing and Euler Loads:** $$ P_c = 550 \times 15000 = 8.25 \times 10^{6} \,\text{N} = 8250 \,\text{kN} $$ $$ P_e = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \times 1.2 \times 10^{5} \times 4.375 \times 10^{7}}{(4242.6)^{2}} = \dfrac{5.18 \times 10^{13}}{1.80 \times 10^{7}} = 2.876 \times 10^{6} \,\text{N} = 2876 \,\text{kN} $$ **Step 6 — Rankine's Crippling Load:** $$ P_R = \dfrac{P_c P_e}{P_c + a P_e} = \dfrac{8250 \times 2876}{8250 + (2876/1600)} $$ $$ P_R = \dfrac{2.373 \times 10^{7}}{8251.8} = 2875.6 \,\text{kN} \;\approx\; 2876 \,\text{kN} $$ *(Marks split similar to Question A(b))* --- #### Model Solution to (b) — Conceptual (7 marks) **Effective Length Concept:** The **effective length $L_e$** of a column is the length of an equivalent *pin-ended* column that has the same crippling (buckling) load as the actual column with its given end conditions. Mathematically: $$ L_e = \beta\,L $$ where $\beta$ is a dimensionless factor depending on the rotational and translational restraints at the column ends. **Table of Effective Lengths:** | End Conditions | $\beta$ | $L_e$ | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Both ends hinged | 1.0 | $L$ | | Both ends fixed | 0.5 | $L/2$ | | One end fixed, other free | 2.0 | $2L$ | | One end fixed, other hinged | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | **Why Euler's Formula Alone is Insufficient:** 1. **Limitation to slender columns:** Euler's formula $P_e = \pi^{2}EI/L_e^{2}$ is mathematically valid only when the column is sufficiently slender that it buckles *elastically* before the material reaches its crushing stress. For short or intermediate columns, the material yields (crushes) before buckling — Euler's formula overestimates the load. 2. **Overestimation of strength:** In the medium-slenderness range, Euler's predicted load is unconservatively high, leading to unsafe designs. 3. **No consideration of imperfections:** Real columns have initial curvature, eccentric loading, and residual stresses — none of which Euler's ideal formula accounts for. 4. **Rankine's advantage:** Rankine's formula combines the crushing strength (via $P_c$) and Euler's buckling strength (via $P_e$) with an empirical constant $a$, giving accurate predictions across the **entire slenderness spectrum**. The formula naturally reduces to $P_c$ for short columns and to a form proportional to $1/L_e^{2}$ for long columns. *(Marks split: 2 for effective length definition, 2 for the table, 3 for Euler's limitations and Rankine's advantage)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — RANKINE'S FORMULA** - ✅ **Core Formula:** $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a(L_e/k)^{2}}$ — the *workhorse* form for all numerical KTU problems. - ✅ **Reciprocal Form:** $\dfrac{1}{P_R} = \dfrac{1}{P_c} + \dfrac{a}{P_e}$ — the form that reveals the formula's physical meaning (resistance is the series combination of crushing and buckling). - ✅ **Three Key Quantities:** - $P_c = \sigma_c A$ (crushing load) - $P_e = \pi^{2}EI/L_e^{2}$ (Euler's load) - $a$ (Rankine's empirical constant) - ✅ **Effective Length Rules** — memorize all four: - Hinged–Hinged: $L$ - Fixed–Fixed: $L/2$ - Fixed–Free: $2L$ - Fixed–Hinged: $L/\sqrt{2}$ - ✅ **For Mild Steel (default):** $a = 1/7500$, $\sigma_c = 320$ MPa, $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ MPa. - ✅ **Limiting Case Check:** As $L_e \to 0$ → $P_R \to P_c$. As $L_e \to \infty$ → $P_R$ is Euler-dominated. - ✅ **Radius of Gyration:** Always use the **smaller** $k$ (weaker axis) for safe design of unsymmetric sections. - ✅ **Units Discipline:** Length in **mm**, area in **mm²**, stress in **MPa (= N/mm²)**, force in **N**. Convert to **kN** only at the final reporting stage. - ✅ **Safety Factor:** $P_{\text{safe}} = P_R / \text{FOS}$. KTU 2024 Scheme typically uses FOS in the range 3–5. - ✅ **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to use $L_e$ (effective length) instead of $L$ (actual length) — this alone can cause 50%–100% error in the final answer. - ✅ **Direct vs. Reciprocal Form:** The direct form $\sigma_c A / [1 + a(L_e/k)^2]$ is **faster** in numerical problems because you avoid computing $P_e$ separately. Use it whenever the problem gives $k$ or section dimensions.

topics.title: Slenderness Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Slenderness Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Slenderness Ratio (λ)** is a dimensionless geometric parameter of a compression member defined as the ratio of its **effective length ($L_{eff}$)** to the **least radius of gyration ($r_{min}$)** of its cross-section.
> $$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$$

It is a **pure numerical ratio** (no units) that quantifies how "slender" a column is in proportion to its cross-sectional geometry. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4 — Columns and Struts), this single parameter acts as the **master switch** that decides whether the column fails by *crushing* (material yielding) or by *buckling* (geometric instability).

> [!NOTE]
> The associated auxiliary parameter — the **Least Radius of Gyration** — is itself defined as:
> $$r_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{min}}{A}}$$
> where $I_{min}$ is the **minimum second moment of area** about the weakest axis and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine two pencils standing on a table:

- **Pencil A**: Short and thick (stubby) — you can press down on it with enormous force before it bends sideways.
- **Pencil B**: Long and thin (like a chopstick) — even a small downward push makes it snap sideways long before the wood itself is crushed.

Both pencils are made of the **same material**, but **Pencil B collapses earlier** because it is *slender*. The "slenderness" is captured mathematically by $\lambda = L/r$. The larger the ratio, the more the column behaves like a long spaghetti strand — buckling governs. The smaller the ratio, the more it behaves like a short metal post — crushing governs.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU exams**: A high slenderness ratio → low strength. A low slenderness ratio → high strength. This inverse relationship lies at the heart of column design.

## 1.3 Standard End-Conditions and Effective Lengths

The effective length $L_{eff}$ depends on how the column is held at its ends. The four canonical KTU cases are:

| # | End Condition (Both Ends) | Effective Length $L_{eff}$ | Effective Length Factor $K$ |
|:-:|:--|:--:|:--:|
| 1 | **Both Ends Hinged (Pinned-Pinned)** | $L_{eff} = L$ | $K = 1.0$ |
| 2 | **Both Ends Fixed** | $L_{eff} = \dfrac{L}{2}$ | $K = 0.5$ |
| 3 | **One End Fixed, Other End Free (Cantilever)** | $L_{eff} = 2L$ | $K = 2.0$ |
| 4 | **One End Fixed, Other End Hinged** | $L_{eff} = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $K = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ |

In every case, $L_{eff} = K \cdot L$, where $K$ is the **effective length factor**.

## 1.4 Classification of Columns Based on Slenderness Ratio

According to **IS 800:2007** (referenced under KTU 2024 Module 4):

- **Short Column** : $\lambda \leq 12$ — Failure by **crushing/yielding**
- **Medium / Intermediate Column** : $12 < \lambda \leq 180$ — Failure by **combined crushing + buckling**
- **Long / Slender Column** : $\lambda \geq 180$ (theoretically) — Failure by **pure elastic buckling** (Euler's regime)

> [!WARNING]
> As per **IS 800**, the **maximum permissible slenderness ratio** for a compression member is **180**, and for a member acting solely as a brace it is **250 to 300**. Per **IS 456:2000** for RCC columns, the maximum $\lambda$ is typically **60** for unswayed frames.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1 → Establish Geometry
A column of actual length $L$, supported by different end conditions, deflects laterally in its buckled shape. The distance between consecutive **points of contraflexure (zero bending moment)** equals the effective length $L_{eff}$.

### Step 2 → Identify the Weak Axis
Every cross-section has two principal centroidal axes. The column buckles about the axis with the **smallest moment of inertia**, because that axis offers the **least resistance** to lateral deflection. Hence we use $I_{min}$ and $r_{min}$.

### Step 3 → Compute $r_{min}$
$$r_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{min}}{A}}$$
This "radius of gyration" has the physical meaning of the **distance from the centroid at which the entire area could be concentrated to produce the same moment of inertia**.

### Step 4 → Form the Slenderness Ratio
$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$$
Higher $\lambda$ → more slender → more susceptible to buckling.

### Step 5 → Predict Failure Mode
- If $\lambda$ is small → $P_{crushing}$ governs → use strength formulas (IS 800 clause on compressive strength).
- If $\lambda$ is large → $P_{Euler}$ governs → use Euler's buckling formula.
- If $\lambda$ is intermediate → use **Rankine–Gordon formula** or **IS 800 buckling curves**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Reference Table

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--:|
| 1 | $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Radius of gyration about a given axis | m |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$ | **Slenderness Ratio** (master parameter) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $L_{eff} = K \cdot L$ | Effective length with end-condition factor $K$ | m |
| 4 | $P_{Euler} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I_{min}}{(L_{eff})^{2}}$ | Euler's crippling (buckling) load | N or kN |
| 5 | $\sigma_{Euler} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$ | Euler's critical stress (in terms of $\lambda$) | N/m² or MPa |
| 6 | $P_{Rankine} = \dfrac{\sigma_{c} \cdot A}{1 + a\left(\dfrac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}\right)^{2}}$ | Rankine–Gordon load (for intermediate columns) | N or kN |
| 7 | $\lambda_{max} = 180$ | IS 800 limit for compression members | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\lambda_{max} = 60$ | IS 456 limit for RCC columns (approx.) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\lambda \leq 12$ | Short-column regime (crushing governs) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $K = 1.0,\ 0.5,\ 2.0,\ \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Effective length factors for 4 end conditions | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation**: Equation 5 — $\sigma_{Euler} = \pi^{2}E/\lambda^{2}$ — explicitly shows that buckling stress is **inversely proportional to the square of the slenderness ratio**. Doubling $\lambda$ quarters the buckling stress. This is the single most powerful formula for KTU derivations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Steel Building Frames** — IS 800 mandates $\lambda \leq 180$ for design of all compression members; this ensures serviceable buckling safety.
2. **Transmission Towers** — Slender leg members are designed with specific $\lambda$ ranges to balance strength vs. weight.
3. **Bridges (Truss Piers)** — Top chords and end posts rely on $\lambda$-based buckling class selection.
4. **Reinforced Concrete Columns** — IS 456 controls slenderness to prevent second-order (P-Δ) effects.
5. **Machine Tool Columns** — Drill press and hydraulic press columns use $\lambda$ to avoid deflection-induced inaccuracies.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Radius of Gyration from First Principles

**Conceptual Setup**: Suppose the entire cross-sectional area $A$ is replaced by an equivalent thin ring of radius $r$, such that this fictitious ring has the **same moment of inertia** as the actual section.

**Step 1**: Moment of inertia of a thin ring of radius $r$ and area $A$ (all concentrated at radius $r$):
$$I_{ring} = A \cdot r^{2}$$

**Step 2**: Equate this to the actual moment of inertia of the cross-section about the same centroidal axis:
$$I = A \cdot r^{2}$$

**Step 3**: Solve for $r$ (radius of gyration):
$$r^{2} = \frac{I}{A}$$

$$\boxed{r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}}$$

**Step 4**: For the column problem, select the minimum value over the two principal axes:
$$r_{min} = \min\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{I_{xx}}{A}}, \sqrt{\frac{I_{yy}}{A}}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Expression of Euler Stress in Terms of Slenderness Ratio

**Step 1**: Start from Euler's crippling load formula:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I_{min}}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$

**Step 2**: Divide both sides by the area $A$ to get critical (crippling) stress:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I_{min}}{A \cdot L_{eff}^{2}}$$

**Step 3**: Substitute $I_{min} = A \cdot r_{min}^{2}$ (from the radius of gyration definition):
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E \cdot A \cdot r_{min}^{2}}{A \cdot L_{eff}^{2}}$$

**Step 4**: Cancel the area $A$ from numerator and denominator:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E \cdot r_{min}^{2}}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$

**Step 5**: Recognize $\lambda = L_{eff}/r_{min}$, so $r_{min}^{2}/L_{eff}^{2} = 1/\lambda^{2}$:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{Euler} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation**: This compact equation shows that for a given material (fixed $E$), the **buckling stress depends ONLY on the slenderness ratio**. It contains no explicit length, area, or moment of inertia — they are all bundled inside $\lambda$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Slenderness Ratio at the Transition Point (Short ↔ Long)

**Step 1**: For a short column, the failure stress equals the **yield stress** in compression:
$$\sigma_{short} = \sigma_{y} = f_{y}$$

**Step 2**: For a long column, the failure stress equals the **Euler critical stress**:
$$\sigma_{Euler} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Step 3**: At the **transition** slenderness ratio $\lambda_{tr}$, both stresses are equal:
$$\frac{\pi^{2} E}{\lambda_{tr}^{2}} = f_{y}$$

**Step 4**: Solve for the transition slenderness ratio:
$$\lambda_{tr}^{2} = \frac{\pi^{2} E}{f_{y}}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{tr} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{f_{y}}}}$$

**Numerical Check (Mild Steel)**: Taking $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² and $f_{y} = 250$ N/mm²:
$$\lambda_{tr} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{5}}{250}} = \pi \sqrt{800} \approx \pi \times 28.28 \approx 88.9$$

For mild steel, columns with $\lambda > 88.9$ are in the **Euler regime**; below this, the **Rankine regime** applies.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Short Column ($\lambda \leq 12$) | Intermediate Column ($12 < \lambda \leq 180$) | Long Column ($\lambda > 180$) |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Governing Mechanism** | Material yielding | Combined yielding + buckling | Pure elastic buckling |
| **Design Formula** | $\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Rankine–Gordon equation | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}E}{\lambda^{2}}$ |
| **Key Property** | $f_{y}$ | $\sigma_{c},\ a$ | $E$ |
| **Failure Mode** | Sudden crushing | Gradual lateral deflection | Sudden sideways snap |
| **Design Code Clause** | IS 800 Cl. 7.1 (cl. 7.1.1) | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2 buckling curves | IS 800 Cl. 7.1.2 (Euler) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Effective Length Factor Diagram (Four Canonical End Conditions)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1["Both Ends Hinged. K equals 1.0. Leff equals L"]
    A2["Both Ends Fixed. K equals 0.5. Leff equals L divided by 2"]
    A3["One End Fixed, Other Free. K equals 2.0. Leff equals 2L"]
    A4["One End Fixed, Other Hinged. K equals 1 divided by root 2. Leff equals L divided by root 2"]
    A5["Effective Length Leff equals K times L"]
    A6["Slenderness Ratio lambda equals Leff divided by rmin"]

    A1 --> A5
    A2 --> A5
    A3 --> A5
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6

    A6 --> B1["Small lambda. Short Column. Crushing governs"]
    A6 --> B2["Large lambda. Long Column. Buckling governs"]

    B1 --> C1["Use Strength Formulas IS 800"]
    B2 --> C2["Use Euler Formula. Pi squared E I over Leff squared"]
```

## 4.2 Column Behaviour Map — Slenderness vs. Failure Mode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1["Lambda equals 0"] --> N2["Lambda equals 12"]
    N2 --> N3["Lambda equals 88.9 for mild steel"]
    N3 --> N4["Lambda equals 180"]

    N1 --> R1["SHORT COLUMN. Pure compression. Failure by crushing. Governing stress fy"]
    N2 --> R2["INTERMEDIATE COLUMN. Combined action. Rankine Gordon formula applies"]
    N3 --> R3["TRANSITION ZONE. Euler stress equals yield stress"]
    N4 --> R4["LONG COLUMN. Pure buckling. Euler regime. Sigma cr equals pi squared E over lambda squared"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Workflow Using Slenderness Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["Step 1. Given column length L and end conditions"] --> S2["Step 2. Determine K from end condition chart"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3. Compute Leff equals K times L"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4. Compute Imin and A of cross section"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5. Compute rmin equals root Imin over A"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6. Compute lambda equals Leff divided by rmin"]

    S6 --> S7{"Lambda less than or equal to 12"}
    S7 --> |Yes| S8["Short Column. Use direct compression formula"]
    S7 --> |No| S9{"Lambda less than or equal to 180"}
    S9 --> |Yes| S10["Use Rankine Gordon OR IS 800 buckling curve"]
    S9 --> |No| S11["Design rejected. Redesign section to reduce lambda"]
```

## 4.4 Stress vs. Slenderness — Qualitative Plot (Schematic Description)

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1["Stress axis vertical. Lambda axis horizontal"]
    P2["Curve A. Yield Stress fy is horizontal line"]
    P3["Curve B. Euler Stress sigma equals pi squared E over lambda squared is hyperbolic decreasing curve"]
    P4["Intersection Point. Lambda transition where Euler equals fy"]
    P5["Region left of intersection. Short column regime. Stress is fy"]
    P6["Region right of intersection. Long column regime. Stress follows hyperbola"]

    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P4 --> P6
```
