<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Q: Define slenderness ratio. Mention the IS 800:2007 limit on slenderness ratio for a compression member and a bracing member.**

**Model Answer** (3 marks — 1 mark each for definition, IS 800 column limit, IS 800 brace limit):

- **Definition**: The slenderness ratio ($\lambda$) of a compression member is defined as the ratio of its effective length ($L_{eff}$) to the least radius of gyration ($r_{min}$) of its cross-section.
$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}}$$

- **IS 800 Limit for Compression Member**: $\lambda_{max} = 180$

- **IS 800 Limit for Bracing Member**: $\lambda_{max}$ ranges from **250 to 300** depending on bracing type.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Q: A hollow circular column has outer diameter 120 mm and inner diameter 80 mm. Its actual length is 4 m with both ends hinged. Compute the slenderness ratio. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

**Step 1 — Effective Length**: Both ends hinged → $L_{eff} = L = 4000$ mm.

**Step 2 — Area of Hollow Circular Section**:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\!\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}\!\left(120^{2} - 80^{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 8000 = 2000\pi \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Moment of Inertia** (circular sections have $I_{xx} = I_{yy}$, so $I_{min}$ is the same):
$$I_{min} = \frac{\pi}{64}\!\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}\!\left(120^{4} - 80^{4}\right)$$
$$I_{min} = \frac{\pi}{64}\!\left(2.0736 \times 10^{8} - 4.096 \times 10^{7}\right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 1.664 \times 10^{8}$$
$$I_{min} \approx 8.168 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Radius of Gyration**:
$$r_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{min}}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{8.168 \times 10^{6}}{2000\pi}} \approx \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Slenderness Ratio**:
$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} = \frac{4000}{36.06} \approx 110.94$$

Since $\lambda > 12$, this is a **long column**, and Euler's buckling formula applies.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**Q: A steel column of length 5 m is effectively held in position at both ends but not restrained against rotation. It has to carry an axial load of 400 kN. Using Euler's formula, design a hollow circular cross-section. Take $f_{y} = 250$ N/mm² and $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm². Assume factor of safety = 3. Also calculate the slenderness ratio and classify the column.**

**Sub-Part (a) — Design of Cross-Section** (7 marks)

**Given**: $L = 5$ m = 5000 mm, $P = 400$ kN = $400 \times 10^{3}$ N, FOS = 3, $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².

**Step 1 — Effective Length**: Both ends hinged in position only (rotationally free) → $L_{eff} = L = 5000$ mm.

**Step 2 — Crippling Load with FOS**:
$$P_{cr} = P \times \text{FOS} = 400 \times 10^{3} \times 3 = 1200 \times 10^{3} \text{ N} = 1200 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's Formula**:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I_{min}}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$
$$1200 \times 10^{3} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times I_{min}}{5000^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $I_{min}$**:
$$I_{min} = \frac{1200 \times 10^{3} \times 5000^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{1200 \times 10^{3} \times 25 \times 10^{6}}{1.9739 \times 10^{6}}$$
$$I_{min} = \frac{3 \times 10^{13}}{1.9739 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.520 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 5 — Design Hollow Circular Section**: Assume diameter ratio $d/D = 0.6$.
$$I = \frac{\pi}{64}\!\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64}\!\left(1 - 0.6^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{64} \times 0.8704$$
$$1.520 \times 10^{7} = \frac{\pi \times 0.8704}{64} D^{4}$$
$$D^{4} = \frac{1.520 \times 10^{7} \times 64}{\pi \times 0.8704} \approx \frac{9.728 \times 10^{8}}{2.7342} \approx 3.557 \times 10^{8}$$
$$D \approx \left(3.557 \times 10^{8}\right)^{1/4} \approx 137.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $D = 140$ mm, $d = 0.6 \times 140 = 84$ mm** (rounded to nearest even).

**Sub-Part (b) — Slenderness Ratio and Classification** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Actual $I_{min}$ with adopted section**:
$$I_{min} = \frac{\pi}{64}\!\left(140^{4} - 84^{4}\right) = \frac{\pi}{64}\!\left(3.8416 \times 10^{8} - 4.978 \times 10^{7}\right)$$
$$I_{min} = \frac{\pi}{64} \times 3.3438 \times 10^{8} \approx 1.642 \times 10^{7} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Area**:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}\!\left(140^{2} - 84^{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4}(19600 - 7056) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 12544 \approx 9852 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Radius of Gyration**:
$$r_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{1.642 \times 10^{7}}{9852}} \approx \sqrt{1667} \approx 40.83 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Slenderness Ratio**:
$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} = \frac{5000}{40.83} \approx 122.5$$

**Step 5 — Classification**: Since $12 < \lambda = 122.5 \leq 180$, the column is a **long (slender) column** — failure by **Euler buckling** is the governing mechanism.

**Step 6 — Verification of Crippling Load**:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 1.642 \times 10^{7}}{5000^{2}} \approx 1297 \text{ kN} > 1200 \text{ kN} \;\; \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-A)**: Students often forget that **"held in position but not restrained against rotation"** means both ends are *hinged*, giving $K = 1.0$. Many wrongly assume fixed conditions and halve the effective length, producing a dangerously oversized section. Also, **do NOT confuse** $r_{min}$ (radius of gyration) with the inner radius $r$ of the hollow pipe — they are completely different quantities!

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` — 14 Marks

**Q: An I-section column ISMB 300 has the following properties: Area $A = 4766$ mm², $I_{xx} = 8603.4 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴, $I_{yy} = 453.9 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴. The column has length 6 m and is fixed at one end and hinged at the other. Determine:**
**(a) The Euler's crippling load, and**
**(b) The slenderness ratio. State whether the column is short, medium, or long per IS 800:2007. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².**

**Sub-Part (a) — Euler's Crippling Load** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Effective Length**: One end fixed, other hinged → 
$$L_{eff} = \frac{L}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{6000}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 4242.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Identify Weak Axis**: Compare moments of inertia:
- $I_{xx} = 8603.4 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴
- $I_{yy} = 453.9 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴

Clearly $I_{yy} < I_{xx}$, so the column will buckle about the **y-y axis (weak axis)**.
$$I_{min} = 453.9 \times 10^{4} \text{ mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's Formula**:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} E I_{min}}{L_{eff}^{2}}$$
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 453.9 \times 10^{4}}{(4242.6)^{2}}$$
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 4.539 \times 10^{6}}{1.8002 \times 10^{7}}$$
$$P_{cr} = \frac{8.957 \times 10^{12}}{1.8002 \times 10^{7}} \approx 4.976 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}$$
$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 497.6 \text{ kN}}$$

**Sub-Part (b) — Slenderness Ratio and Classification** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Radius of Gyration about Weak Axis**:
$$r_{min} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{yy}}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{453.9 \times 10^{4}}{4766}}$$
$$r_{min} = \sqrt{952.4} \approx 30.86 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness Ratio**:
$$\lambda = \frac{L_{eff}}{r_{min}} = \frac{4242.6}{30.86} \approx 137.5$$

**Step 3 — Classification per IS 800:2007**:
Since $12 < \lambda = 137.5 \leq 180$, the column is in the **long column regime** (intermediate-to-long transition), and the failure will be governed by **Euler's elastic buckling**.

**Step 4 — Critical Stress**:
$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{4.976 \times 10^{5}}{4766} \approx 104.4 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

Since $\sigma_{cr} = 104.4$ N/mm² $< f_y = 250$ N/mm², the column will **NOT crush** before buckling — buckling is the critical failure mode, confirming the long-column classification.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q-B)**: Many students mistakenly use $I_{xx}$ (the larger moment of inertia) in Euler's formula, leading to an **inflated crippling load** (around 9.4 times too large). **Always use $I_{min}$** — the column buckles about its weakest axis, not its strongest. This is the **#1 most common mark-losing mistake** in Module 4 KTU exams. Also, students frequently forget to take $L/\sqrt{2}$ for the fixed-hinged case and use $L = 6000$ mm directly, which underestimates the crippling load by a factor of 2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Slenderness Ratio** = $L_{eff} / r_{min}$ — *dimensionless*, governs failure mode.
- [x] **Effective Length** depends on end conditions: $L_{eff} = KL$ with $K = 1.0, 0.5, 2.0, 1/\sqrt{2}$ for the four standard cases.
- [x] **Radius of Gyration**: $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ — always use $I_{min}$ and corresponding $r_{min}$.
- [x] **Euler's Critical Stress**: $\sigma_{cr} = \pi^{2}E/\lambda^{2}$ — inverse-square law in slenderness.
- [x] **Short Column** ($\lambda \leq 12$): Crushing/yielding governs; use direct compression.
- [x] **Long Column** ($\lambda > 12$, up to 180): Euler buckling governs.
- [x] **IS 800:2007 Limits**: 180 for compression members; 250–300 for bracings.
- [x] **IS 456:2000 Limit**: ~60 for RCC columns (much stricter due to concrete brittleness).
- [x] **Transition Slenderness**: $\lambda_{tr} = \pi\sqrt{E/f_y}$ — where Euler stress equals yield stress.
- [x] **Weakest-Axis Rule**: Always use the smaller of $I_{xx}$ and $I_{yy}$ when computing $r_{min}$.
- [x] **Rankine Formula**: For intermediate columns, $P = \sigma_c A / [1 + a(L_{eff}/r_{min})^{2}]$.
- [x] **Engineering Intuition**: Higher $\lambda$ → lower strength → more slender → buckle-prone.
- [x] **Exam Tip**: Always state end condition → write $K$ → write $L_{eff}$ → compute $r_{min}$ → compute $\lambda$ → classify column → apply correct formula. This 6-step pipeline catches every KTU Module 4 question.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Word for KTU 2024 Students**: Slenderness ratio is the **gateway concept** to all column design — Euler, Rankine, IS 800 buckling curves, and IS 456 short-column clauses ALL begin from computing $\lambda$. Master the 6-step pipeline above and you master Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limitation of Euler's Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Limitation of Euler's Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Euler's Formula for Column Buckling** gives the critical (crippling) load at which a slender, long, pin-ended column buckles due to elastic instability:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$$

where:
- $P_{cr}$ = Critical buckling load (**N**)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the material (**N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $I$ = Minimum moment of inertia of the cross-section (**m⁴** or **mm⁴**)
- $L_e$ = Effective length of the column (**m** or **mm**), depending on end conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The topic *"Limitation of Euler's Formula"* asks the student to identify the conditions under which this formula **fails to predict** the true crippling load of a column. Euler's formula is **not a universal column formula** — it is valid **only for long, slender columns** within the elastic range.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a long, thin plastic ruler held vertically between your palms. If you press your palms together gently (small axial load), nothing happens. Increase the pressure slightly, and suddenly the ruler snaps sideways with a *crack* — even though the material is perfectly fine and the force is small. This sideways *snap* is **buckling**, not crushing.

Now, the same experiment with a short, stubby wooden block of the same material and same cross-section: it never buckles sideways — it gets crushed under load. Why?

- The **ruler is long and slender** → Euler's formula works perfectly.
- The **block is short and stocky** → Euler's formula **over-predicts** the failure load; the material actually **yields/crushes** before buckling can occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **The One-Line Intuition:** Euler's formula is like a *weather prediction model* that works perfectly for hurricanes (long columns) but is useless for predicting drizzle (short columns). The boundary between the two is governed by the **Slenderness Ratio**.

---

## 1.3 Why Limitations Exist — The Core Reason

Euler's formula is derived from the **elastic bending equation** (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory), which assumes:
1. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** (perfectly linear elastic).
2. The column is **initially perfectly straight** (no imperfections).
3. The load is applied **perfectly along the centroidal axis** (no eccentricity).
4. The column is **sufficiently slender** so that buckling occurs at a stress *below* the proportional limit of the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The primary limitation tested in KTU is the **validity range of slenderness ratio** — beyond which Euler's formula is invalid and empirical formulas (Rankine, Johnson's, IS 800) must be used instead.

---

## 1.4 The Most Important Limitation — Slenderness Ratio Criterion

The **Slenderness Ratio** is defined as:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r}$$

where $r = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ is the **radius of gyration** of the cross-section.

**Euler's formula is valid ONLY when:**

$$\lambda \geq \lambda_{critical} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}}$$

where $\sigma_p$ is the **proportional limit** (or yield stress, conservatively) of the material.

For **Mild Steel**:
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²**
- $\sigma_p \approx 200$ **N/mm²**

$$\lambda_{critical} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{200}} = \pi \times \sqrt{1000} \approx 99.3 \approx 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** For Mild Steel, **Euler's formula is valid when $\lambda \geq 100$**. For $\lambda < 100$, Euler's formula **overestimates** the crippling load and is **unsafe** to use.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed List of Limitations

### **Limitation 1 — Valid Only for Long (Slender) Columns**

The critical stress predicted by Euler's formula is:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

- As $\lambda$ **decreases**, $\sigma_{cr}$ **increases rapidly**.
- When $\lambda$ becomes small enough that $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_p$ (proportional limit), the material has **already yielded** before buckling — so the elastic theory is **invalid**.

**Graphical Concept (very important for KTU):**

- **Euler's curve** is a hyperbola, dropping steeply as $\lambda$ increases.
- The **yield line** (horizontal line at $\sigma_y$) intersects the Euler curve at $\lambda = \lambda_{critical}$.
- For $\lambda < \lambda_{critical}$: Use **Empirical formulas** (Johnson, Rankine, IS 800).
- For $\lambda \geq \lambda_{critical}$: Use **Euler's formula**.

---

### **Limitation 2 — Assumes Perfectly Elastic Material**

- Euler's derivation uses the relation $M = EI \dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$ (purely elastic bending).
- The moment-curvature relationship is **linear** — true only up to the **proportional limit** $\sigma_p$.
- If $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_p$, the column yields and the modulus $E$ is no longer constant. Euler's formula **ceases to apply**.

---

### **Limitation 3 — Assumes the Column is Initially Perfectly Straight**

- Real columns have **initial crookedness**, **eccentric loading**, or **material inhomogeneities**.
- Such imperfections cause **bending stresses from the very start of loading**, reducing the actual failure load below Euler's predicted $P_{cr}$.
- Euler's formula gives the **ideal (theoretical maximum) load**, not the actual capacity.

---

### **Limitation 4 — Assumes Load is Truly Axial (Concentric)**

- Any **load eccentricity** $e$ produces a bending moment $M = P \cdot e$ in addition to axial compression.
- This causes **combined bending + compression** failure (described by the **secant formula**), not pure buckling.
- Euler's formula ignores eccentricity entirely.

---

### **Limitation 5 — Assumes Constant Cross-Section Along the Length**

- The formula uses a **single value of $I$** for the entire length.
- **Tapered columns** (with varying $I$) or stepped columns require modified analysis.
- Euler's formula is **not directly applicable** to non-prismatic columns.

---

### **Limitation 6 — Assumes Ideal End Conditions**

- Effective length $L_e$ is based on ideal pin, fixed, or free conditions.
- In practice, end conditions involve **partial fixity** that is neither perfectly pinned nor perfectly fixed.
- The actual $L_e$ lies somewhere between theoretical extremes, but Euler's formula offers **no intermediate values**.

---

### **Limitation 7 — Neglects Self-Weight of the Column**

- The classical Euler derivation assumes the column's **own weight is negligible** compared to the applied load.
- For very long columns (e.g., tall chimneys, masts), self-weight cannot be ignored.

---

### **Limitation 8 — Neglects Shear Deformation**

- Euler-Bernoulli beam theory assumes **plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** — i.e., shear deformation is ignored.
- For very short columns, **shear effects dominate** and Euler's formula is inaccurate.

---

### **Limitation 9 — Gives Only the Elastic Buckling Load, Not the Ultimate Failure Load**

- $P_{cr}$ is the load at which the column becomes **unstable** (buckles).
- The actual **failure load** may be lower due to material yielding, initial imperfections, or eccentric loading.
- Euler's formula is a **stability criterion**, not a **strength criterion**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Meaning / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Euler's Critical Load | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$ | Buckling load for long, slender, pin-ended columns |
| Euler's Critical Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$ | Stress at the onset of buckling |
| Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r} = \dfrac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}}$ | Geometric property of column |
| Critical Slenderness Ratio | $\lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_p}}$ | Lower bound of validity for Euler's formula |
| Validity Condition | $\lambda \geq \lambda_{cr}$ | Required for Euler's formula to apply |
| Effective Length (Pinned-Pinned) | $L_e = L$ | Both ends pinned |
| Effective Length (Fixed-Fixed) | $L_e = L/2$ | Both ends fixed |
| Effective Length (Fixed-Pinned) | $L_e = L/\sqrt{2}$ | One end fixed, one end pinned |
| Effective Length (Fixed-Free) | $L_e = 2L$ | One end fixed, one end free |
| Mild Steel Critical $\lambda$ | $\lambda_{cr} \approx 100$ | Approximate boundary for mild steel |
| Cast Iron Critical $\lambda$ | $\lambda_{cr} \approx 80$ | Approximate boundary for cast iron |
| Timber Critical $\lambda$ | $\lambda_{cr} \approx 100$ | Approximate boundary for timber |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **critical slenderness ratio for mild steel ($\approx 100$)** — it is the most frequently tested value in Part A questions (3-mark questions).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding Euler's limitations is **critical in practical column design** because:

1. **Bridge Piers & Building Columns:** Most practical columns are *intermediate slenderness* ($\lambda = 30$ to $100$) — Euler's formula is **invalid**, and codes like **IS 800:2007** or **AISC 360** use empirical curves.
2. **Tall Transmission Towers & Masts:** These are *very slender* ($\lambda > 200$) — Euler's formula is **highly accurate**.
3. **Ship Masts, Drill Strings, Antenna Poles:** Long, slender members where Euler's formula is the **primary design tool**.
4. **Design Codes (IS 800, AISC, Eurocode 3)** transition smoothly from **Euler's elastic curve** (for long columns) to **yield-line** (for short columns) via transition curves (Johnson parabolic, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Slenderness Ratio (The Key Result)

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU on this topic.

### **Step 1: Start with Euler's Critical Stress**

The critical (crippling) load from Euler's formula is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$$

Divide both sides by the cross-sectional area $A$ to get the **average critical stress**:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{A \cdot L_e^2}$$

### **Step 2: Introduce Radius of Gyration**

By definition, the radius of gyration is:

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad I = A r^2$$

Substitute this into the expression for $\sigma_{cr}$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E \cdot (A r^2)}{A \cdot L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E \cdot r^2}{L_e^2}$$

### **Step 3: Express in Terms of Slenderness Ratio**

Rearrange by grouping $\dfrac{r^2}{L_e^2} = \dfrac{1}{(L_e/r)^2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$$

This is the **Euler's critical stress equation**.

### **Step 4: Apply the Validity Condition**

For Euler's formula to be valid, the critical stress must **not exceed the proportional limit** $\sigma_p$ (or yield stress $\sigma_y$ conservatively):

$$\sigma_{cr} \leq \sigma_p$$

Substituting $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2}$:

$$\frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} \leq \sigma_p$$

### **Step 5: Solve for the Limiting Slenderness Ratio**

Rearrange to isolate $\lambda$:

$$\lambda^2 \geq \frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_p}$$

$$\lambda \geq \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}}$$

Therefore, the **critical (limiting) slenderness ratio** is:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}}}$$

### **Step 6: Numerical Evaluation for Mild Steel**

For mild steel, using standard values:
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²**
- $\sigma_p = 200$ **N/mm²** (proportional limit)

$$\lambda_{cr} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{200}} = \pi \sqrt{1000} = \pi \times 31.62 \approx 99.35 \approx 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For all practical KTU problems involving mild steel, take $\lambda_{cr} = 100$.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| **Slenderness Range** | **Column Type** | **Failure Mode** | **Applicable Formula** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\lambda \geq \lambda_{cr}$ (≥ 100 for steel) | Long / Slender column | Elastic buckling | **Euler's formula** |
| $\lambda_{cr} > \lambda \geq \lambda_{min}$ | Intermediate / Medium column | Inelastic buckling (partial yielding) | **Johnson's parabolic formula** or **IS 800 curves** |
| $\lambda < \lambda_{min}$ (≈ 20 for steel) | Short / Stocky column | Pure crushing / yielding | **Direct compressive stress** (no buckling check needed) |

where $\lambda_{min}$ is the slenderness ratio below which the column behaves as a **short column** and only crushing needs to be checked (typically $\lambda_{min} \approx 20$ to $30$ for steel as per IS 800).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Identifying Validity

**Problem:** A steel column has length 4 m, both ends pinned, cross-section 80 mm × 60 mm. Given $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²** and $\sigma_p = 200$ **N/mm²**. Check if Euler's formula is applicable.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute cross-section properties:**
- $A = 80 \times 60 = 4800$ **mm²**
- $I_{min} = \dfrac{60 \times 80^3}{12} = 2,560,000$ **mm⁴** (bending about the weaker axis)
- $r_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{min}}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2,560,000}{4800}} = \sqrt{533.33} = 23.09$ **mm**

**Step 2 — Compute slenderness ratio:**
- $L_e = L = 4000$ **mm** (pinned-pinned)

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r_{min}} = \frac{4000}{23.09} \approx 173.2$$

**Step 3 — Compute critical slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{200}} \approx 100$$

**Step 4 — Apply the validity condition:**

Since $\lambda = 173.2 > \lambda_{cr} = 100$:

> **Conclusion:** Euler's formula **IS applicable** for this column. The column is a *long, slender column*.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example — When Euler's Formula is NOT Valid

**Problem:** Same cross-section (80 mm × 60 mm) and material, but length is reduced to 1.0 m. Check validity.

**Step 1 — Recompute slenderness ratio:**
- $L_e = 1000$ **mm**

$$\lambda = \frac{1000}{23.09} \approx 43.3$$

**Step 2 — Compare with $\lambda_{cr} = 100$:**

Since $\lambda = 43.3 < \lambda_{cr} = 100$:

> **Conclusion:** Euler's formula is **NOT applicable**. The column is an *intermediate column*; the failure is by **inelastic buckling / crushing**. Use **Johnson's formula**, **Rankine's formula**, or **IS 800** curves instead.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Warning:** If a student applies Euler's formula directly when $\lambda < \lambda_{cr}$, **full marks are deducted** and the answer is marked unsafe. Always begin any column problem by computing $\lambda$ and checking the validity condition first.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Validity Flowchart — Decision Tree for Column Analysis

The following Mermaid flowchart guides a student through the *correct* decision logic when solving a column problem in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given Column Problem"] --> B["Compute Slenderness Ratio: lambda = Le divided by rmin"]
    B --> C{"Is lambda greater than or equal to lambda_cr equals 100 for mild steel?"}
    C -- YES --> D["Column is LONG and SLENDER"]
    D --> E["Apply EULER FORMULA: Pcr = pi squared E I divided by Le squared"]
    E --> F["Compute Pcr and sigma_cr"]
    C -- NO --> G{"Is lambda between 30 and 100?"}
    G -- YES --> H["Column is INTERMEDIATE"]
    H --> I["Apply JOHNSON or RANKINE FORMULA or IS 800 curves"]
    I --> J["Failure is by INELASTIC BUCKLING"]
    G -- NO --> K["Column is SHORT and STOCKY"]
    K --> L["Apply direct STRESS check: sigma = P divided by A"]
    L --> M["Failure is by CRUSHING or YIELDING"]
    F --> N["End: Safe Design Verified"]
    J --> N
    M --> N
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Euler's Formula Concept

The following diagram maps the **interrelationship between parameters, assumptions, validity, and limitations** of Euler's formula — a typical question students are asked to draw in KTU 2-mark/3-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["INPUTS: E, I, Le, A, L, Material"] --> P2["EULER CORE EQUATION: Pcr = pi squared E I divided by Le squared"]
    P2 --> P3["VALIDITY GATE: lambda greater than or equal to lambda_cr"]
    P3 -- "PASS" --> P4["SAFE DESIGN: Buckling Load is Valid"]
    P3 -- "FAIL" --> P5["UNSAFE: Use Empirical Formulas Johnson or Rankine"]
    P2 --> A1["ASSUMPTION 1: Perfectly Straight Column"]
    P2 --> A2["ASSUMPTION 2: Perfectly Axial Load"]
    P2 --> A3["ASSUMPTION 3: Linear Elastic Material"]
    P2 --> A4["ASSUMPTION 4: Constant Cross Section"]
    P2 --> A5["ASSUMPTION 5: Ideal End Conditions"]
    A1 --> L1["LIMITATION 1: Real columns have initial crookedness"]
    A2 --> L2["LIMITATION 2: Real loads have eccentricity"]
    A3 --> L3["LIMITATION 3: Material yields beyond sigma_p"]
    A4 --> L4["LIMITATION 4: Real columns may be tapered"]
    A5 --> L5["LIMITATION 5: End fixity is rarely ideal"]
    L3 --> P3
```

---

## 4.3 Euler Stress Curve — Conceptual Schematic

This diagram represents the **stress vs. slenderness ratio curve** that students are expected to understand and draw:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Yield Stress Line: horizontal at sigma_y"] --> C{"Intersection Point at lambda = lambda_cr"}
    B["Euler Hyperbola: sigma_cr = pi squared E divided by lambda squared"] --> C
    C --> D["Region 1: lambda greater than lambda_cr: EULER ZONE: Elastic Buckling governs"]
    C --> E["Region 2: lambda less than lambda_cr: EMPIRICAL ZONE: Inelastic Buckling or Crushing"]
    D --> F["Valid for LONG columns"]
    E --> G["Valid for SHORT and INTERMEDIATE columns"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Tip:** The Euler curve is a **hyperbola** that **falls steeply** as $\lambda$ increases. The yield line is **horizontal** at $\sigma_y$. The two curves **intersect at $\lambda_{cr}$**, which is the boundary of validity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**State any three limitations of Euler's formula for columns.**

**Model Answer:**

The limitations of Euler's formula are:

1. **Valid only for long columns:** Euler's formula is applicable only when the slenderness ratio $\lambda \geq \lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{E/\sigma_p}$. For mild steel, $\lambda_{cr} \approx 100$. For short and intermediate columns, it is invalid.

2. **Assumes perfectly elastic material:** The derivation assumes the material obeys Hooke's law throughout. When $\sigma_{cr}$ exceeds the proportional limit $\sigma_p$, the formula fails.

3. **Assumes perfectly straight column with concentric axial load:** Real columns have initial crookedness and load eccentricity, which cause additional bending and reduce the actual crippling load below Euler's predicted value.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define critical slenderness ratio and obtain its value for mild steel.**

**Model Answer:**

The **critical slenderness ratio** $\lambda_{cr}$ is the minimum slenderness ratio above which Euler's formula is valid. It is the value of $\lambda$ at which the Euler's critical stress equals the proportional limit of the material.

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} = \sigma_p \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}}$$

For **mild steel**, taking $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²** and $\sigma_p = 200$ **N/mm²**:

$$\lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{200}} = \pi \times \sqrt{1000} \approx 99.3 \approx 100$$

---

## **PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the critical slenderness ratio of a column. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** A hollow circular cast iron column has an external diameter of 200 mm and internal diameter of 150 mm. It is 4 m long and is fixed at both ends. Determine whether Euler's formula is valid. Take $E = 1.2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²** and $\sigma_p = 160$ **N/mm²**. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Derivation of Critical Slenderness Ratio:**

**Step 1:** Euler's critical load is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$$

**Step 2:** Dividing by area $A$ to obtain critical stress:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{A \cdot L_e^2}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $I = A r^2$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E \cdot r^2}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} \quad \text{where } \lambda = \frac{L_e}{r}$$

**Step 4:** For Euler's formula to be valid, $\sigma_{cr}$ must be less than or equal to the proportional limit $\sigma_p$:

$$\frac{\pi^2 E}{\lambda^2} \leq \sigma_p$$

**Step 5:** Solving for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda^2 \geq \frac{\pi^2 E}{\sigma_p} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda \geq \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, the critical slenderness ratio is:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}}}$$

**Step 7:** Significance — for $\lambda < \lambda_{cr}$, Euler's formula overestimates $P_{cr}$, and empirical formulas must be used.

---

### **Part (b) — Validity Check for Hollow Circular Cast Iron Column:**

**Step 1 — Cross-section properties:**

- Outer diameter $D = 200$ **mm**, Inner diameter $d = 150$ **mm**
- Area: $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(200^2 - 150^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(40000 - 22500) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(17500) \approx 13744.5$ **mm²**
- Moment of inertia: $I = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - d^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(200^4 - 150^4) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(1.6 \times 10^9 - 5.0625 \times 10^8) = \dfrac{\pi}{64}(1.09375 \times 10^9)$
- $I \approx 53.69 \times 10^6$ **mm⁴**

**Step 2 — Radius of gyration:**

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{53.69 \times 10^6}{13744.5}} = \sqrt{3906.2} \approx 62.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Effective length (Fixed-Fixed):**

$$L_e = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{4000}{2} = 2000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{2000}{62.5} = 32$$

**Step 5 — Critical slenderness ratio for cast iron:**

$$\lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{E}{\sigma_p}} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{160}} = \pi \times \sqrt{750} = \pi \times 27.39 \approx 86$$

**Step 6 — Decision:**

Since $\lambda = 32 < \lambda_{cr} = 86$:

> **Euler's formula is NOT valid.** The column is an *intermediate column*; Johnson's or Rankine's empirical formula must be used.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam Pattern]**

**(a)** List and explain the various assumptions and limitations of Euler's formula for long columns. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** A mild steel column of square cross-section 50 mm × 50 mm is 2.5 m long and is hinged at both ends. Calculate the Euler's crippling load. Also, state whether Euler's formula is applicable. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ **N/mm²** and $\sigma_p = 200$ **N/mm²**. **[(7 Marks)]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Assumptions and Limitations (Detailed List):**

1. **Initial straightness:** The column is assumed to be perfectly straight initially. Real columns have initial crookedness which reduces the actual load capacity.

2. **Concentric loading:** The load is assumed to be applied exactly at the centroid, producing pure compression. Any eccentricity creates additional bending.

3. **Linear elastic material:** The material is assumed to obey Hooke's law. Beyond the proportional limit, the modulus $E$ is no longer constant, and the formula is invalid.

4. **Long and slender column:** The formula is valid only for long columns with $\lambda \geq \lambda_{cr} \approx 100$ for mild steel. For short and intermediate columns, empirical formulas apply.

5. **Constant cross-section:** The column has uniform $I$ along its length. Tapered or stepped columns require modified analysis.

6. **Ideal end conditions:** End conditions are idealized (perfect pin or perfect fix). Real connections have partial fixity not captured by Euler's formula.

7. **Negligible self-weight:** Self-weight of the column is ignored. Important for very tall, slender columns.

8. **Negligible shear deformation:** The Euler-Bernoulli theory ignores shear, which is significant for very short columns.

---

### **Part (b) — Euler's Crippling Load Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Cross-section properties:**

- Square: 50 mm × 50 mm
- $A = 50 \times 50 = 2500$ **mm²**
- $I_{min} = \dfrac{50^4}{12} = \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^6}{12} = 520,833$ **mm⁴**
- $r_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{520,833}{2500}} = \sqrt{208.33} = 14.43$ **mm**

**Step 2 — Effective length (Hinged-Hinged):**

$$L_e = L = 2500 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{2500}{14.43} = 173.2$$

**Step 4 — Validity check:**

$$\lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{200}} \approx 100$$

Since $\lambda = 173.2 > \lambda_{cr} = 100$, **Euler's formula IS applicable**.

**Step 5 — Euler's crippling load:**

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times 520,833}{(2500)^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 520,833}{6.25 \times 10^6}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \times 1.0417 \times 10^{11}}{6.25 \times 10^6}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{1.0282 \times 10^{12}}{6.25 \times 10^6} = 1.645 \times 10^5 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cr} \approx 164.5 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 6 — Verification via critical stress:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{P_{cr}}{A} = \frac{1.645 \times 10^5}{2500} = 65.8 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Since $\sigma_{cr} = 65.8 < \sigma_p = 200$ **N/mm²** ✓, the elastic assumption is valid. **Euler's formula is applicable.**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check validity first:** Many students directly plug values into $P_{cr} = \pi^2 EI/L_e^2$ without computing $\lambda$ and comparing with $\lambda_{cr}$. The examiner will deduct **up to 3 marks** for skipping the validity check.
>
> 2. **Using yield stress instead of proportional limit:** Always use $\sigma_p$ (proportional limit) in the validity equation, not $\sigma_y$ (yield stress). For mild steel, $\sigma_p \approx 200$ **N/mm²** and $\sigma_y \approx 250$ **N/mm²** — these are different.
>
> 3. **Confusing effective length with actual length:** For fixed-fixed columns, $L_e = L/2$, not $L$. For fixed-free columns, $L_e = 2L$. Mixing up end conditions is a **frequent error** worth 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Not stating the units explicitly:** Always write **N/mm²** or **MPa** for stress and **N** or **kN** for load. The examiner deducts marks for ambiguous units.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the "minimum" moment of inertia:** For non-symmetric cross-sections (e.g., rectangular), $I_{min}$ governs buckling. Using $I_{max}$ is incorrect.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Limitations of Euler's Formula**

- ✅ **Euler's formula** is the **elastic buckling load** for **long, slender, pin-ended columns**: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{L_e^2}$.

- ✅ **Slenderness ratio** is the key parameter: $\lambda = \dfrac{L_e}{r} = \dfrac{L_e}{\sqrt{I/A}}$.

- ✅ **Critical slenderness ratio** is the lower limit of Euler's validity: $\lambda_{cr} = \pi\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_p}}$.

- ✅ **For mild steel, $\lambda_{cr} \approx 100$** — this is the most-tested value in KTU exams.

- ✅ **If $\lambda \geq 100$** → **Euler's formula applies** (elastic buckling).

- ✅ **If $\lambda < 100$** → **Euler's formula does NOT apply** (use Johnson / Rankine / IS 800).

- ✅ **Euler's formula assumes:** (i) perfectly straight column, (ii) concentric load, (iii) elastic material, (iv) constant cross-section, (v) ideal end conditions, (vi) negligible self-weight, (vii) negligible shear deformation.

- ✅ **Effective length values (must memorize):**
  - Pinned–Pinned: $L_e = L$
  - Fixed–Fixed: $L_e = L/2$
  - Fixed–Pinned: $L_e = L/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707 L$
  - Fixed–Free: $L_e = 2L$

- ✅ **Euler's formula is a stability criterion, not a strength criterion** — it predicts buckling load, not the actual crushing load.

- ✅ **For cast iron, $\lambda_{cr} \approx 80$**; **for timber, $\lambda_{cr} \approx 100$**.

- ✅ **Always check validity FIRST** in any KTU column problem before applying any formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safe Load Calculation Using Rankine's Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Safe Load Calculation Using Rankine's Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rankine's Formula** is a semi-empirical, unified column-strength relationship that predicts the *crippling (ultimate) load* of a structural column by mathematically interpolating between the two limiting failure modes — **crushing (compressive yielding)** for very short stocky columns and **elastic flexural buckling (Euler instability)** for very long slender columns. It is the only classical column formula that is valid across the *entire* spectrum of column slenderness, and it is extensively adopted in the design of intermediate (medium) columns used in trusses, building frames, and industrial structures.

Mathematically, the formula is expressed as:

$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 \;+\; a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}}$$

where every term has a precise physical meaning (detailed in §2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAGT302 – Module 4):**
> Students are expected to (i) state Rankine's formula, (ii) derive it from first principles, (iii) apply it to columns of *circular, rectangular, hollow rectangular, I-section, and built-up geometry*, (iv) account for **all four classical end conditions** via the effective length concept, and (v) compute the **Safe Load** by dividing the crippling load by an appropriate Factor of Safety (FOS), typically **FOS = 3 to 5** for column design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Springy Brick Tower" Intuition

Imagine stacking a tower of bricks. If the tower is **very short (e.g., 3 bricks high)**, the failure mode is straightforward: the bricks simply *crush* at the bottom because the applied load exceeds the brick's compressive strength. This is the **crushing regime**.

Now imagine the *same* bricks stacked to form a tower 100 bricks high. The tower will **not** crush — instead, it will **buckle sideways** like a snapped spaghetti strand, even though the compressive stress in each brick is far below its crushing strength. This is the **buckling regime**.

But what about a tower of, say, 25 bricks? It is *neither* a pure crushing problem *nor* a pure buckling problem. The tower exhibits a **hybrid failure**: it deflects sideways *and* the material begins to yield simultaneously. This intermediate regime is precisely what Rankine's formula was engineered to capture. It gracefully *blends* the crushing strength with the Euler buckling strength through a harmonic-mean-style interpolation, smoothly transitioning from one limit to the other as the slenderness ratio changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Summary:**
> - **Short column** → failure dominated by **material crushing** (strength problem).
> - **Long column** → failure dominated by **geometric instability** (stiffness problem).
> - **Medium column** → failure is a *combination* of both → **Rankine's formula** is the design tool.

## 1.3 The Three Defining Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Crushing Stress** | $\sigma_{c}$ | Compressive stress at which the column material yields/crushes | **N/mm² (MPa)** |
| **Cross-sectional Area** | $A$ | Total load-bearing area of the column cross-section | **mm²** |
| **Rankine's Empirical Constant** | $a$ | Material-specific constant that quantifies the relative importance of buckling vs. crushing | **dimensionless** |

> [!TIP]
> Standard textbook values of **$a$** that KTU examiners love to test:
> - **Mild Steel:** $a = \dfrac{1}{7500}$
> - **Wrought Iron:** $a = \dfrac{1}{9000}$
> - **Cast Iron:** $a = \dfrac{1}{1600}$
> - **Timber:** $a = \dfrac{1}{3000}$

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why Three Different Column Regimes Exist

The structural behaviour of a column under axial compression is governed by a single dimensionless parameter — the **slenderness ratio** $L_{e}/k$ — which compares the column's effective length to its least radius of gyration. The classification of columns is:

- **Short columns** ($L_{e}/k < 20$ for mild steel): The member fails by **direct compressive crushing** of the material. The Euler buckling load is so astronomically large that yielding always pre-empts instability.
- **Long columns** ($L_{e}/k > 120$ for mild steel): The member fails by **elastic flexural buckling** at a load far below the crushing load. The Euler formula gives highly accurate predictions.
- **Medium (Intermediate) columns** ($20 \le L_{e}/k \le 120$): The failure is *coupled* — partial yielding *and* partial elastic deflection occur simultaneously. Neither the crushing equation nor the pure Euler equation is valid. **This is the regime where Rankine's formula is the standard design tool.**

> [!NOTE]
> The thresholds (20 and 120) are not universal. They depend on the material's crushing stress $\sigma_{c}$ and Young's modulus $E$. They are conventionally derived from the intersection of the Rankine curve with the crushing line and the Euler curve.

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation — The Two Limiting Cases

### Case A: Pure Crushing (Short Column Limit)
The column behaves as a direct compression member. The crippling load equals:

$$P_{c} \;=\; \sigma_{c}\cdot A$$

### Case B: Pure Elastic Buckling (Long Column Limit)
The column behaves as an Euler column. The crippling load is:

$$P_{E} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_{e}^{2}}$$

The deflection of the column is governed by the differential equation $EI\,\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} = -M$ subject to the appropriate boundary conditions. The resulting Euler load formula accounts for the four end-condition cases (see §2.4).

### Case C: The Rankine Interpolation
Rankine (1857) proposed that for **any** slenderness ratio, the actual crippling load $P_{R}$ should be obtained by the **reciprocal (harmonic) interpolation**:

$$\dfrac{1}{P_{R}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{P_{c}} \;+\; \dfrac{1}{P_{E}}$$

This elegant expression ensures that:
- When $L_{e}/k \to 0$ (very short), $P_{E} \to \infty$, so $\frac{1}{P_{E}} \to 0$, giving $P_{R} \approx P_{c}$. ✓
- When $L_{e}/k \to \infty$ (very long), $P_{c} \ll P_{E}$ in the harmonic sense, so $P_{R} \to P_{E}$. ✓

## 2.3 Master Derivation — From Reciprocal Interpolation to the Standard Form

Starting from:
$$\dfrac{1}{P_{R}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{c}\,A} \;+\; \dfrac{L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}EI}$$

Taking the common denominator and inverting:
$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot \pi^{2}EI}{\pi^{2}EI \;+\; \sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot L_{e}^{2}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\pi^{2}EI$:
$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 \;+\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}EI}}$$

Recognising the radius of gyration identity $k^{2} = I/A$, hence $I = Ak^{2}$:
$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 \;+\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}E\,k^{2}}} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 \;+\; a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}}$$

where Rankine's empirical constant is defined as:

$$a \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}}{\pi^{2}E}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Key Insight:** Although the formula contains the empirical constant $a$, it is **not a free parameter** — it is mathematically determined by the two fundamental material properties $\sigma_{c}$ and $E$. Different textbook values (e.g., $1/7500$ for mild steel) arise from the choice of allowable crushing stress *or* from a built-in safety factor embedded in older code recommendations.

## 2.4 End Conditions and the Effective Length $L_{e}$

| End Condition (one end / other end) | Effective Length $L_{e}$ | Ratio $L_{e}/L$ | Physical Situation |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Hinged / Hinged | $L$ | 1.00 | Pin-supported truss chord |
| Fixed / Fixed | $L/2$ | 0.50 | Built-in column in a rigid frame |
| Fixed / Hinged | $L/\sqrt{2}$ | 0.707 | Column rigidly attached to a foundation with a pin at top |
| Fixed / Free (Cantilever) | $2L$ | 2.00 | Flagpole, free-standing strut |

> [!NOTE]
> The effective length represents the *equivalent length of a pin-ended column* that would buckle at the same load as the actual column with its given end restraints.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Applicable Regime |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{c} = \sigma_{c}\cdot A$ | Crushing crippling load | Short columns ($L_{e}/k$ very small) |
| 2 | $P_{E} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_{e}^{2}}$ | Euler elastic buckling load | Long columns ($L_{e}/k$ very large) |
| 3 | $P_{R} = \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 + a(L_{e}/k)^{2}}$ | **Rankine crippling load** | **All slenderness ratios** |
| 4 | $a = \dfrac{\sigma_{c}}{\pi^{2}E}$ | Rankine's material constant | Definition |
| 5 | $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ | Least radius of gyration | Geometric property |
| 6 | $L_{e} = $ tabulated value | Effective length | Depends on end conditions |
| 7 | $P_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{P_{R}}{\text{FOS}}$ | **Safe allowable load** | **Design stage** |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{induced}} = \dfrac{P}{A} \le \sigma_{\text{allowable}}$ | Direct stress check | Service load verification |
| 9 | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress (if eccentric load) | Combined loading |
| 10 | $I_{\text{rect}} = \dfrac{bd^{3}}{12}$ | M.O.I. of a solid rectangle | Cross-section property |
| 11 | $I_{\text{hollow rect}} = \dfrac{BD^{3} - bd^{3}}{12}$ | M.O.I. of a hollow rectangle | Cross-section property |
| 12 | $I_{\text{circular}} = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{64}$ | M.O.I. of a solid circle | Cross-section property |
| 13 | $I_{\text{hollow circular}} = \dfrac{\pi(D^{4} - d^{4})}{64}$ | M.O.I. of a hollow circle | Cross-section property |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rankine's formula is the workhorse of **traditional design offices** for the following applications:
- **Roof trusses and bridge girders** — top chords that behave as columns.
- **Industrial column design** in single-storey workshops where the slenderness ratio is in the medium range.
- **Struts in bracing systems** of buildings and towers.
- **Pedestals and short piers** in civil infrastructure.

In modern limit-state design, IS 800 (for steel) and IS 456 (for concrete) use more refined multiple-column curves, but the **conceptual underpinning** is the same — and KTU examiners heavily test the Rankine approach.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Rankine's Formula

**Statement to Prove:** For a column of cross-sectional area $A$, crushing stress $\sigma_{c}$, Young's modulus $E$, effective length $L_{e}$, and least radius of gyration $k$, the crippling load is given by:
$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 \;+\; a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}} \quad \text{where} \quad a = \dfrac{\sigma_{c}}{\pi^{2}E}$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Define the two limiting crippling loads.**

For a *very short* column, the failure load is governed purely by material crushing:
$$P_{c} \;=\; \sigma_{c}\cdot A \tag{i}$$

For a *very long* column, the failure load is governed purely by elastic flexural buckling (Euler):
$$P_{E} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_{e}^{2}} \tag{ii}$$

**Step 2 — Propose the harmonic-mean interpolation.**

Rankine postulated that the reciprocal of the actual crippling load is the *sum* of the reciprocals of the two limiting loads:
$$\dfrac{1}{P_{R}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{P_{c}} \;+\; \dfrac{1}{P_{E}} \tag{iii}$$

*Justification:* This reciprocal interpolation has the dual property that the smaller of the two limiting loads dominates the prediction (whichever is smaller, $P_{c}$ or $P_{E}$, is the bottleneck), which matches physical intuition.

**Step 3 — Substitute equations (i) and (ii) into (iii).**

$$\dfrac{1}{P_{R}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{c}\cdot A} \;+\; \dfrac{L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}EI}$$

**Step 4 — Take the common denominator.**

The common denominator is $\sigma_{c}\cdot A \cdot \pi^{2}EI$:
$$\dfrac{1}{P_{R}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI \;+\; \sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot L_{e}^{2}}{\sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot \pi^{2}EI}$$

**Step 5 — Invert the expression to isolate $P_{R}$.**

$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot \pi^{2}EI}{\pi^{2}EI \;+\; \sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot L_{e}^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Divide numerator and denominator by $\pi^{2}EI$.**

$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 \;+\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}EI}}$$

**Step 7 — Introduce the radius of gyration.**

By definition, $k^{2} = I/A$, hence $I = A\,k^{2}$. Substituting in the denominator:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}EI} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A\cdot L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}E\cdot A k^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}E\cdot k^{2}}$$

**Step 8 — Define the Rankine constant.**

$$a \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}}{\pi^{2}E}$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot L_{e}^{2}}{\pi^{2}E\cdot k^{2}} \;=\; a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 9 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{\,P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 \;+\; a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}}\,}$$

**Q.E.D.**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Hollow Circular Cast-Iron Column

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem Statement:**
> *A hollow circular cast-iron column has an external diameter of 200 mm and an internal diameter of 150 mm. Its effective length is 4.5 m. Determine the crippling load using Rankine's formula. Take $\sigma_{c} = 550\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$, $a = 1/1600$, and FOS = 5. Also compute the safe load.*

### Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area $A$.

For a hollow circular section:
$$A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\!\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)$$

Substituting $D = 200\,\text{mm}$ and $d = 150\,\text{mm}$:
$$A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\!\left(200^{2} - 150^{2}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\!\left(40000 - 22500\right) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\times 17500$$

$$A \;=\; 13744.47\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the moment of inertia $I$.

For a hollow circular section:
$$I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}\!\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$$

Computing the fourth powers:
$$D^{4} = 200^{4} = 1.6 \times 10^{9}\,\text{mm}^{4}$$
$$d^{4} = 150^{4} = 5.0625 \times 10^{8}\,\text{mm}^{4}$$

$$I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}\!\left(1.6 \times 10^{9} - 5.0625 \times 10^{8}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}\times 1.09375 \times 10^{9}$$

$$I \;=\; 5.3675 \times 10^{7}\,\text{mm}^{4}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the radius of gyration $k$.

$$k \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{5.3675 \times 10^{7}}{13744.47}} \;=\; \sqrt{3904.92}$$

$$k \;=\; 62.49\,\text{mm}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the slenderness ratio $L_{e}/k$.

The effective length is given directly as $L_{e} = 4.5\,\text{m} = 4500\,\text{mm}$:
$$\dfrac{L_{e}}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{4500}{62.49} \;=\; 72.01$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Rankine crippling load $P_{R}$.

$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 + a(L_{e}/k)^{2}}$$

Compute the denominator term:
$$a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1600}\times (72.01)^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{5185.4}{1600} \;=\; 3.2409$$

Denominator:
$$1 + 3.2409 \;=\; 4.2409$$

Numerator:
$$\sigma_{c}\cdot A \;=\; 550 \times 13744.47 \;=\; 7{,}559{,}460\,\text{N} \;=\; 7559.46\,\text{kN}$$

Final crippling load:
$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{7{,}559{,}460}{4.2409} \;=\; 1{,}782{,}442\,\text{N} \;\approx\; 1782.44\,\text{kN}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the safe load $P_{\text{safe}}$.

$$P_{\text{safe}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{R}}{\text{FOS}} \;=\; \dfrac{1782.44}{5} \;=\; 356.49\,\text{kN}$$

### Final Answer

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :---: |
| Area $A$ | $13744.47\,\text{mm}^{2}$ |
| M.O.I. $I$ | $5.3675 \times 10^{7}\,\text{mm}^{4}$ |
| Radius of gyration $k$ | $62.49\,\text{mm}$ |
| Slenderness ratio $L_{e}/k$ | $72.01$ |
| **Crippling load $P_{R}$** | **$\mathbf{1782.44\,\text{kN}}$** |
| **Safe load $P_{\text{safe}}$** | **$\mathbf{356.49\,\text{kN}}$** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Check:** Since $L_{e}/k = 72.01$ lies in the medium-column range ($20 < L_{e}/k < 120$ for cast iron), Rankine's formula is the **correct** design tool. Pure Euler or pure crushing would both give misleading results.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Column Regimes

| Parameter | Short Column | Medium Column | Long Column |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Typical $L_{e}/k$ range | $< 20$ | $20$ to $120$ | $> 120$ |
| Dominant failure mode | Crushing | Combined | Buckling |
| Applicable formula | $P_{c} = \sigma_{c}A$ | $\mathbf{P_{R} = \dfrac{\sigma_{c}A}{1 + a(L_{e}/k)^{2}}}$ | $P_{E} = \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_{e}^{2}}$ |
| Sensitivity to $E$ | None | Moderate | Very high |
| Sensitivity to $\sigma_{c}$ | Very high | Moderate | None |
| Stress distribution | Uniform | Non-uniform | Non-uniform (bending-dominated) |
| Deflection before failure | Negligible | Moderate | Large |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Rankine's Formula

The following diagram maps the **logical flow** of how Rankine's formula is constructed by combining the crushing and Euler contributions. This is the safe Mermaid-rendered equivalent of a hand-drawn free-body / conceptual sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Axially Loaded Column] --> B{Identify End Conditions}
    B -->|Hinged-Hinged| C1[Le = L]
    B -->|Fixed-Fixed| C2[Le = L slash 2]
    B -->|Fixed-Hinged| C3[Le = L over sqrt 2]
    B -->|Fixed-Free| C4[Le = 2L]

    C1 --> D[Compute Cross-section Properties]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> D1[Area A]
    D --> D2[Moment of Inertia I]
    D --> D3[Radius of Gyration k]

    D1 --> E[Material Properties]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Crushing Stress sigma-c]
    E --> E2[Young Modulus E]
    E --> E3[Rankine Constant a]

    E1 --> F1[Crushing Load Pc = sigma-c times A]
    E2 --> F2[Euler Load Pe = pi squared E I over Le squared]
    E3 --> G[Combine via Reciprocal Sum]

    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Rankine Crippling Load PR]
    H --> I[Apply Factor of Safety]
    I --> J[Safe Load P_safe]

    H --> K[Compare with Pure Crushing Limit]
    H --> L[Compare with Pure Euler Limit]
    K --> M[Select Governing Failure Mode]
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Column Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Given Column Dimensions] --> T[Compute Slenderness Ratio Le over k]
    T --> U{Le/k Comparison}

    U -->|Le/k less than 20| V1[SHORT COLUMN]
    U -->|Le/k between 20 and 120| V2[MEDIUM COLUMN]
    U -->|Le/k greater than 120| V3[LONG COLUMN]

    V1 --> W1[Use Crushing Formula: Pc = sigma-c times A]
    V2 --> W2[Use RANKINE FORMULA: PR = sigma-c A over 1 + a times Le slash k squared]
    V3 --> W3[Use Euler Formula: PE = pi squared E I over Le squared]

    W1 --> X[Compute Safe Load by dividing by FOS]
    W2 --> X
    W3 --> X
    X --> Y[Final Design Load]
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description of the Rankine Interpolation

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Picture for Examiners:**
> Imagine a graph with $L_{e}/k$ on the horizontal axis and crippling load $P$ on the vertical axis. The **crushing line** is a *horizontal* line at $P = \sigma_{c}A$. The **Euler curve** is a *hyperbola* that diverges to infinity as $L_{e}/k \to 0$ and decays to zero as $L_{e}/k \to \infty$. The **Rankine curve** is a *smooth, monotonically decreasing curve* that is tangent to (or closely follows) the crushing line for small $L_{e}/k$ and asymptotes to the Euler curve for large $L_{e}/k$. The transition is graceful and continuous — this is the graphical signature of the harmonic-mean interpolation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Rankine's formula for the crippling load of a column. What is the significance of the constant $a$ in the formula?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rankine's formula for the crippling load of a column is:
$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 \;+\; a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2}}$$

where $\sigma_{c}$ = crushing stress of the material, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $L_{e}$ = effective length of the column, and $k$ = least radius of gyration. **(1.5 marks)**

The constant $a = \sigma_{c}/(\pi^{2}E)$ is called **Rankine's empirical constant** and depends only on the material properties (crushing stress and Young's modulus). It is dimensionless and quantifies the **relative importance of buckling versus crushing** for that particular material. For example, $a = 1/7500$ for mild steel, $1/1600$ for cast iron, and $1/3000$ for timber. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by "effective length" of a column? Write the effective length for the four standard end conditions.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **effective length $L_{e}$** of a column is the length of an *equivalent pin-ended column* that would buckle at the same crippling load as the actual column with its given end restraints. It is obtained by multiplying the actual length $L$ by a factor that depends on the end-fixity conditions. **(1.5 marks)**

The four standard cases are:

| End Conditions | Effective Length $L_{e}$ |
| :--- | :---: |
| Both ends hinged | $L$ |
| Both ends fixed | $L/2$ |
| One end fixed, other hinged | $L/\sqrt{2}$ |
| One end fixed, other free | $2L$ | **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 (Choice Option A)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

A hollow rectangular cast-iron column has external dimensions $120\,\text{mm} \times 100\,\text{mm}$ and internal dimensions $80\,\text{mm} \times 60\,\text{mm}$. It is $3.6\,\text{m}$ long and is fixed at both ends. Determine the **crippling load** using Rankine's formula. Also find the **safe load** with a factor of safety of 6. Take $\sigma_{c} = 550\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$ and $a = 1/1600$. **(14 marks)**

#### Part (a): Compute the Crippling Load $P_{R}$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area $A$.**
$$A \;=\; BD - bd \;=\; (120 \times 100) - (80 \times 60) \;=\; 12000 - 4800 \;=\; 7200\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia about the weaker axis.**

For a rectangular section, the moment of inertia about an axis parallel to side $D$ is $\dfrac{B D^{3}}{12}$. The weaker axis corresponds to the **smaller dimension**, i.e., the 100 mm side. Hence:
$$I_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{(120)(100)^{3} - (80)(60)^{3}}{12}$$
$$I_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{120 \times 1{,}000{,}000 - 80 \times 216{,}000}{12} \;=\; \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{8} - 1.728 \times 10^{7}}{12}$$
$$I_{\min} \;=\; \dfrac{1.0272 \times 10^{8}}{12} \;=\; 8.56 \times 10^{6}\,\text{mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Least radius of gyration $k_{\min}$.**
$$k_{\min} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{\min}}{A}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{8.56 \times 10^{6}}{7200}} \;=\; \sqrt{1188.89} \;=\; 34.48\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Effective length $L_{e}$.**

Both ends fixed $\Rightarrow$ $L_{e} = L/2 = 3600/2 = 1800\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 5 — Slenderness ratio.**
$$\dfrac{L_{e}}{k_{\min}} \;=\; \dfrac{1800}{34.48} \;=\; 52.20$$

**Step 6 — Crippling load via Rankine's formula.**
$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 + a(L_{e}/k_{\min})^{2}}$$

$$a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k_{\min}}\right)^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1600}\times (52.20)^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{2724.84}{1600} \;=\; 1.7030$$

$$1 + 1.7030 \;=\; 2.7030$$

$$\sigma_{c}\cdot A \;=\; 550 \times 7200 \;=\; 3{,}960{,}000\,\text{N} \;=\; 3960\,\text{kN}$$

$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{3{,}960{,}000}{2.7030} \;=\; 1{,}465{,}038\,\text{N} \;\approx\; 1465.04\,\text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{R} \;\approx\; 1465\,\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

#### Part (b): Compute the Safe Load $P_{\text{safe}}$ (7 marks)

$$P_{\text{safe}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{R}}{\text{FOS}} \;=\; \dfrac{1465.04}{6} \;=\; 244.17\,\text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{safe}} \;\approx\; 244.17\,\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to take the weaker axis:** Students often compute $I$ about the larger dimension (120 mm side) by mistake. Since buckling always occurs about the *weaker* (smaller $I$) axis, the smaller dimension must be cubed. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Wrong end-condition multiplier:** Both ends fixed gives $L/2$, not $L/\sqrt{2}$. Mixing these up is the most frequent error. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** $L_{e}$ and $k$ must both be in the **same units** (e.g., both in mm) before forming the ratio. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Forgetting to invert:** Some students substitute into $\frac{1}{P_R} = \cdots$ and forget to invert at the end, leaving $P_R$ as a tiny number. **(−2 marks)**
> 5. **Skipping the validity check:** Always state whether the slenderness ratio lies in the short, medium, or long regime. This earns an extra half-mark in many KTU papers. **(−0.5 mark)**

---

### Question B2 (Choice Option B)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

A solid circular mild-steel column has a diameter of $150\,\text{mm}$ and a length of $5\,\text{m}$. It is effectively pin-ended. Using Rankine's formula, find the **crippling load** and the **safe load** with FOS = 4. Take $\sigma_{c} = 320\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$, $E = 2 \times 10^{5}\,\text{N/mm}^{2}$, and $a = 1/7500$. Also compute the **Euler buckling load** for comparison and comment on the result. **(14 marks)**

#### Part (a): Crippling Load via Rankine's Formula and Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area $A$.**
$$A \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\,D^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\times 150^{2} \;=\; 17671.46\,\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia $I$.**
$$I \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}\,D^{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}\times 150^{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{64}\times 5.0625 \times 10^{8} \;=\; 2.4850 \times 10^{7}\,\text{mm}^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Radius of gyration $k$.**
$$k \;=\; \dfrac{D}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{150}{4} \;=\; 37.5\,\text{mm}$$
*(Equivalently, $k = \sqrt{I/A} = \sqrt{2.4850 \times 10^{7}/17671.46} = 37.5\,\text{mm}$.)* ✓

**Step 4 — Effective length $L_{e}$.**

Both ends hinged $\Rightarrow$ $L_{e} = L = 5000\,\text{mm}$.

**Step 5 — Slenderness ratio.**
$$\dfrac{L_{e}}{k} \;=\; \dfrac{5000}{37.5} \;=\; 133.33$$

**Step 6 — Rankine's crippling load.**
$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{c}\cdot A}{1 + a(L_{e}/k)^{2}}$$

$$a\!\left(\dfrac{L_{e}}{k}\right)^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{7500}\times (133.33)^{2} \;=\; \dfrac{17777.78}{7500} \;=\; 2.3704$$

$$1 + 2.3704 \;=\; 3.3704$$

$$\sigma_{c}\cdot A \;=\; 320 \times 17671.46 \;=\; 5{,}654{,}867\,\text{N} \;=\; 5654.87\,\text{kN}$$

$$P_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{5{,}654{,}867}{3.3704} \;=\; 1{,}677{,}793\,\text{N} \;\approx\; 1677.79\,\text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{R} \;\approx\; 1677.79\,\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{(3.5 marks)}$$

**Step 7 — Euler's buckling load for comparison.**
$$P_{E} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}EI}{L_{e}^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi^{2}\times 2 \times 10^{5}\times 2.4850 \times 10^{7}}{(5000)^{2}}$$

$$P_{E} \;=\; \dfrac{9.8696 \times 4.97 \times 10^{12}}{2.5 \times 10^{7}} \;=\; \dfrac{4.905 \times 10^{13}}{2.5 \times 10^{7}} \;=\; 1{,}962{,}000\,\text{N} \;\approx\; 1962\,\text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{E} \;\approx\; 1962\,\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 8 — Comparison comment.**

Since $L_{e}/k = 133.33 > 120$, this is **borderline long column** behaviour. The Rankine load ($1678\,\text{kN}$) is reasonably close to but slightly less than the Euler load ($1962\,\text{kN}$), which is expected because Rankine accounts for the *initial* material yielding that Euler neglects. The difference is about $14.5\%$, indicating that buckling (not crushing) is the dominant failure mode, but the material's compressive strength is not entirely negligible. **(1.5 marks)**

#### Part (b): Safe Load Computation (7 marks)

$$P_{\text{safe}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{R}}{\text{FOS}} \;=\; \dfrac{1677.79}{4} \;=\; 419.45\,\text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{safe}} \;\approx\; 419.45\,\text{kN}} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B2:**
> 1. **Missing the "comment" section:** Part (a) explicitly asks for a *comment* on the result. Skipping this loses 1.5 marks — a steep penalty in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Forgetting the solid-circle identity $k = D/4$:** This shortcut saves time. Using $k = \sqrt{I/A}$ from first principles is also acceptable, but you must show all four powers correctly.
> 3. **Misinterpreting "pin-ended":** This is *not* the same as "fixed-free". Pin-ended means both ends hinged, so $L_{e} = L$, not $2L$.
> 4. **Missing the regime classification:** Always state whether the column is short, medium, or long *before* selecting the formula. KTU examiners look for this as a marker of conceptual clarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation):**

- [x] **Rankine's formula** gives the crippling load of a column of *any* slenderness: $P_R = \dfrac{\sigma_c A}{1 + a(L_e/k)^2}$.
- [x] **Rankine's constant** $a = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\pi^2 E}$ — *not* a free parameter, fully determined by material properties.
- [x] **Standard values to memorise:** Mild Steel $1/7500$, Wrought Iron $1/9000$, Cast Iron $1/1600$, Timber $1/3000$.
- [x] **Effective length** $L_e$ is the equivalent pin-ended length: hinged-hinged ($L$), fixed-fixed ($L/2$), fixed-hinged ($L/\sqrt{2}$), fixed-free ($2L$).
- [x] **Always use the *least* radius of gyration** $k_{\min}$ (buckling occurs about the weaker axis). For a solid circle, $k = D/4$. For a solid rectangle, $k_{\min} = d/\sqrt{12}$ where $d$ is the smaller side.
- [x] **Safe load** = Crippling load ÷ Factor of Safety. Typical FOS for column design: **3 to 5** (steel), **4 to 6** (cast iron), **6 to 8** (timber).
- [x] **Classification of columns by slenderness ratio:** Short ($< 20$), Medium ($20$–$120$), Long ($> 120$) for mild steel. *Thresholds are material-dependent.*
- [x] **Rankine's interpolation is *reciprocal***: $\frac{1}{P_R} = \frac{1}{P_c} + \frac{1}{P_E}$ — this is the formula's core mathematical identity.
- [x] **Units check:** $A$ in $\text{mm}^2$, $\sigma_c$ in $\text{N/mm}^2$ (MPa), $I$ in $\text{mm}^4$, $L_e$ and $k$ in $\text{mm}$ — keep all length units consistent.
- [x] **Validity regime:** Rankine's formula is the *only* classical formula valid across the entire slenderness spectrum, but it is the *primary* design tool only in the medium-column range.
- [x] **Comparison with Euler:** For $L_e/k > 120$, $P_R < P_E$ but they are close; for $L_e/k < 20$, $P_R \approx P_c$ and Euler gives absurdly large values (so don't use Euler for short columns).
- [x] **Always state the failure regime** (short/medium/long) at the start of your solution — this earns easy partial credit.
- [x] **Cross-section property formulas to remember cold:**
  - Solid circle: $A = \pi D^2/4$, $I = \pi D^4/64$, $k = D/4$.
  - Hollow circle: $A = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$, $I = \pi(D^4 - d^4)/64$.
  - Solid rectangle: $A = b \cdot d$, $I = b d^3/12$ (about the axis parallel to $b$).
  - Hollow rectangle: $A = BD - bd$, $I = (BD^3 - bd^3)/12$.
- [x] **Examiner mindset:** KTU markers allocate marks for: (i) the correct formula statement, (ii) the correct $k_{\min}$ identification, (iii) the correct $L_e$ for the given end conditions, (iv) clean arithmetic with units, and (v) a final conclusion sentence linking the result to the failure regime.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statically Indeterminate Beams
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT302 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS & THEORY OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Statically Indeterminate Beams

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **statically indeterminate beam** is a continuous flexural member in which the number of unknown reaction components (vertical forces, horizontal forces, and moment reactions) exceeds the number of independent equilibrium equations available in **two-dimensional statics** (i.e., $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, and $\sum M = 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DSI):** 
> $$\text{DSI} = R - 3$$ 
> where $R$ is the total number of unknown reaction components at the supports. A beam with $\text{DSI} \geq 1$ is statically indeterminate. 

## 1.2 Common Beam Configurations in KTU 2024 Scheme

| Beam Type | Unknowns (R) | Equilibrium Eqs. | DSI |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Simply Supported Beam | 3 | 3 | 0 (Determinate) |
| **Propped Cantilever** (Fixed + Roller) | 4 | 3 | **1** |
| **Fixed Beam** (Fixed + Fixed) | 6 | 3 | **3** |
| **Continuous Beam** (2 spans, 3 supports) | 5 | 3 | **2** |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, the primary focus is the analysis of **Propped Cantilevers**, **Fixed Beams**, and the application of the **Three-Moment Theorem (Clapeyron's Theorem)** to two-span continuous beams.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are trying to lift a heavy wooden plank resting on **two stools** versus a plank **bolted firmly at one wall** and resting on a single stool at the other end. 

- In the **simply supported case**, the plank can rock freely on the stools—its reactions are completely defined by simple weight-balance (determinate).
- In the **bolted case**, the wall grip introduces a *hidden internal moment* that cannot be measured by a simple weighing scale. The beam "fights back" against the bolt, creating extra unknowns. 

This extra "hidden grip" is exactly what we mean by **static indeterminacy**. To solve for these extra unknowns, we must use **compatibility equations** (deflections = 0 at fixed points) drawn from the **theory of bending (flexural rigidity, $EI$)**—this is the heart of Module 4.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
> - $I$ = Area Moment of Inertia of the cross-section (in **m⁴** or **mm⁴**)
> - $EI$ = Flexural Rigidity (assumed **constant** throughout the beam unless specified otherwise)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Indeterminate Beams Exist: The "Why" Behind the Extra Unknowns

The classical **three equations of equilibrium** ($\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$) are the only tools provided by statics. When a beam has more supports or fixed-end conditions than the minimum required for stability, the **additional constraints** generate extra reaction components. Since we have no more equations to solve them, we must bring in **geometry of deformation** (compatibility) and the **constitutive law of materials** ($M = EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$).

## 2.2 Method of Superposition (Consistency of Deformations)

The most direct approach tested in KTU 2024 exams is the **Method of Superposition**:

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. **Identify redundants:** Choose the unknown reaction(s) exceeding 3 as the *primary redundant force(s)*.
2. **Release the structure:** Remove the redundant supports to obtain a *primary (determinate) structure*.
3. **Apply load on primary structure:** Compute the deflection ($\Delta_{L0}$) at the released point in the direction of the redundant.
4. **Apply unit redundant:** Compute the deflection ($\delta_{LL}$) at the released point due to a *unit value* of the redundant.
5. **Superpose and enforce compatibility:** 
   $$\Delta_{L0} + X \cdot \delta_{LL} = 0$$
6. Solve for $X$ (the redundant reaction) and use equilibrium to find the remaining reactions.

## 2.3 Three-Moment Theorem (Clapeyron's Theorem) for Continuous Beams

For two adjacent spans $AB$ and $BC$ of a continuous beam on simple supports at $A$, $B$, and $C$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clapeyron's Three-Moment Equation:**
> $$M_A L_1 + 2M_B(L_1 + L_2) + M_C L_2 = -6\left[\frac{A_1 \bar{a}_1}{L_1} + \frac{A_2 \bar{b}_2}{L_2}\right]$$
> 
> where $A_1$ and $A_2$ are the **areas of the free bending moment diagrams** of spans 1 and 2 treated as simply supported, and $\bar{a}_1$, $\bar{b}_2$ are the centroidal distances of these areas measured from the **outer supports** $A$ and $C$ respectively.

For **uniformly distributed loads (UDL)** on both spans:
$$\frac{A_1 \bar{a}_1}{L_1} = \frac{w_1 L_1^3}{24}, \quad \frac{A_2 \bar{b}_2}{L_2} = \frac{w_2 L_2^3}{24}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Beam Type & Loading | Redundant Reaction | Fixed-End Moment | Central Deflection / Moment |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | **Propped Cantilever, UDL** $w$ on span $L$ | $R_B = \frac{3wL}{8}$ | $M_A = \frac{wL^2}{8}$ | Max BM at $x = \frac{3L}{8}$ from fixed end = $\frac{9wL^2}{128}$ |
| 2 | **Propped Cantilever, Point Load** $P$ at distance $a$ from fixed end ($b = L-a$) | $R_B = \frac{Pb^2(3a+b)}{2L^3}$ | $M_A = \frac{Pab(L+a)}{2L^2}$ | – |
| 3 | **Fixed Beam, UDL** $w$ on span $L$ | $R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$ | $M_A = M_B = \frac{wL^2}{12}$ | Central BM = $\frac{wL^2}{24}$ (sagging) |
| 4 | **Fixed Beam, Central Point Load** $P$ | $R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$ | $M_A = M_B = \frac{PL}{8}$ | Central BM = $\frac{PL}{8}$ (sagging) |
| 5 | **Two-Span Continuous, UDL** $w$ on both equal spans $L$ | $R_A = R_C = \frac{3wL}{8}$ | $M_B = -\frac{wL^2}{8}$ (hogging) | Central BM of each span = $\frac{wL^2}{16}$ (sagging) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Sign Convention:** In the KTU 2024 valuation key, **sagging moments are POSITIVE** and **hogging moments are NEGATIVE**. A continuous beam at an interior support always has a **hogging (negative)** moment.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Propped Cantilevers:** Used in design of *reinforced concrete balconies, aircraft wings* (where the fuselage acts as the prop), and *highway bridge deck overhangs*.
- **Fixed Beams:** Forms the analysis basis for *rigid frame corners, ship hulls, and portal frames*.
- **Three-Moment Theorem:** Crucial for designing *multi-span bridges and continuous building floor beams* in reinforced concrete and steel structures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Propped Cantilever with UDL over the Entire Span

**Setup:** A beam of span $L$ is fixed at end $A$ and simply supported (propped) at end $B$. A uniformly distributed load of intensity $w$ acts over the entire span.

**Step 1 — Identify the redundant.**
Total unknowns: $H_A$ (zero by symmetry), $V_A$, $M_A$ (fixed-end moment), and $R_B$ (vertical reaction at prop) $\rightarrow$ $R = 4$. 
Equilibrium equations available = 3. 
So **DSI = 1**, and the redundant chosen is $R_B$.

**Step 2 — Release the support.**
The primary structure is a **cantilever** fixed at $A$ and free at $B$. The redundant $R_B$ acts upward at the free end.

**Step 3 — Compute the downward deflection at B due to UDL on the cantilever (from the right).**
For a cantilever of span $L$ with UDL $w$ over the whole span, the deflection at the free end is:
$$\Delta_{B0} = \frac{w L^4}{8 EI} \quad (\downarrow)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the upward deflection at B due to the redundant $R_B$ acting alone.**
For a cantilever with a point load at the free end:
$$\delta_{BB} = \frac{R_B L^3}{3 EI} \quad (\uparrow)$$

**Step 5 — Apply compatibility (deflection at B = 0 since there is a real support).**
$$\Delta_{B0} - \delta_{BB} = 0$$
$$\frac{w L^4}{8 EI} = \frac{R_B L^3}{3 EI}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the redundant.**
$$R_B = \frac{3 w L}{8}$$

**Step 7 — Use equilibrium to find fixed-end moment.**
Taking moments about $A$ for the full beam:
$$\sum M_A = 0: \quad M_A + R_B L - w L \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0$$
$$M_A = \frac{w L^2}{2} - \frac{3 w L}{8} \cdot L = \frac{4 w L^2 - 3 w L^2}{8} = \frac{w L^2}{8}$$
Thus, the **fixed-end moment** is $M_A = \dfrac{w L^2}{8}$ (hogging).

**Step 8 — Verify the vertical reaction at A.**
$$\sum F_y = 0: \quad V_A = w L - R_B = wL - \frac{3wL}{8} = \frac{5wL}{8}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fixed Beam with UDL over the Entire Span

**Setup:** A beam of span $L$ is fixed at both ends $A$ and $B$ under a UDL of intensity $w$. By symmetry of geometry and loading:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}, \quad M_A = M_B$$

**Step 1 — Identify redundants.** DSI = 3, but by symmetry, $M_A = M_B$ reduces the problem to **one redundant**: the end moment $M_A$ (with $M_B$ being symmetric).

**Step 2 — Primary structure.** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carrying UDL $w$ and two end moments $M_A$ applied at $A$ and $M_B = M_A$ at $B$.

**Step 3 — Compatibility condition.** The slope at the fixed end $A$ must be **zero**.
- Slope at $A$ due to UDL on simply supported beam (anticlockwise positive):
$$\theta_{A, \text{load}} = \frac{w L^3}{24 EI}$$
- Slope at $A$ due to end moments $M_A$ at $A$ and $M_B = M_A$ at $B$ (clockwise from sagging convention):
$$\theta_{A, \text{mom}} = \frac{M_A L}{2 EI} + \frac{M_B L}{2 EI} = \frac{M_A L}{EI}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve.**
